response: The expected output is a successful DNS resolution with the IP address 10.0.0.1.
title: Debugging DNS Resolution — Create a simple Pod to use as a test environment

instruction: What is the default container registry to pull control plane images from?
response: The default container registry to pull control plane images from is 'registry.k8s.io'.
title: — Synopsis

instruction: What output formats are supported by the kubeadm config images list command?
response: The supported output formats are: text, json, yaml, kyaml, go-template, go-template-file, template, templatefile, jsonpath, jsonpath-as-json, and jsonpath-file.
title: — Synopsis

instruction: What is the structure of the .spec.template in a ReplicationController?
response: The .spec.template is a pod template with the same schema as a Pod, except it does not have an apiVersion or kind.
title: ReplicationController — Pod Template

instruction: What is the default restart policy for a pod template in a ReplicationController?
response: The default restart policy is Always, which can be overridden by specifying a different restart policy.
title: ReplicationController — Pod Template

instruction: How does a ReplicationController handle local container restarts?
response: ReplicationControllers delegate local container restarts to an agent on the node, for example the Kubelet.
title: ReplicationController — Pod Template

instruction: What is the default value for the 'pretty' parameter in the GET /apis/internal.apiserver.k8s.io/v1alpha1/storageversions request?
response: The default value for the 'pretty' parameter is 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: StorageVersion — getList

instruction: What is the effect of setting the 'limit' parameter to a non-zero value in the GET /apis/internal.apiserver.k8s.io/v1alpha1/storageversions request?
response: Setting a limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items (up to zero items) in the event all requested objects are filtered out, and clients should only use the presence of the continue field to determine whether more results are available.
title: StorageVersion — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'sendInitialEvents' parameter in the GET /apis/internal.apiserver.k8s.io/v1alpha1/storageversions request?
response: The 'sendInitialEvents' parameter may be set together with 'watch=true' to produce the current state of objects in the collection at the start of the watch stream.
title: StorageVersion — getList

instruction: What are the requirements for a sysctl to be considered safe in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: A safe sysctl must be properly namespaced and isolated between pods on the same node, meaning it must not have any influence on any other pod on the node, must not allow to harm the node's health, and must not allow to gain CPU or memory resources outside of the resource limits of a pod.
title: Using sysctls in a Kubernetes Cluster — Safe and Unsafe Sysctls

instruction: What are some examples of sysctls that are supported in the safe set?
response: The following sysctls are supported in the safe set: kernel.shm_rmid_forced, net.ipv4.ip_local_port_range, net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies, net.ipv4.ping_group_range, net.ipv4.ip_unprivileged_port_start, net.ipv4.ip_local_reserved_ports, net.ipv4.tcp_keepalive_time, net.ipv4.tcp_fin_timeout, net.ipv4.tcp_keepalive_intvl, net.ipv4.tcp_keepalive_probes, net.ipv4.tcp_rmem, and net.ipv4.tcp_wmem.
title: Using sysctls in a Kubernetes Cluster — Safe and Unsafe Sysctls

instruction: Are all namespaced sysctls considered safe?
response: No, by far most of the namespaced sysctls are not necessarily considered safe.
title: Using sysctls in a Kubernetes Cluster — Safe and Unsafe Sysctls

instruction: How can you roll back to the previous deployment of a Kubernetes deployment?
response: You can use the command `kubectl rollout undo deployment/frontend` to roll back to the previous deployment.
title: kubectl Quick Reference — Updating resources

instruction: How can you roll back to a specific revision of a Kubernetes deployment?
response: You can use the command `kubectl rollout undo deployment/frontend --to-revision=2` to roll back to a specific revision.
title: kubectl Quick Reference — Updating resources

instruction: How can you watch the rolling update status of a Kubernetes deployment until completion?
response: You can use the command `kubectl rollout status -w deployment/frontend` to watch the rolling update status of a deployment until completion.
title: kubectl Quick Reference — Updating resources

instruction: How can you add a label to a Kubernetes pod?
response: You can use the command `kubectl label pods my-pod new-label=awesome` to add a label to a pod.
title: kubectl Quick Reference — Updating resources

instruction: How can you overwrite an existing label value for a Kubernetes pod?
response: You can use the command `kubectl label pods my-pod new-label=new-value --overwrite` to overwrite an existing label value for a pod.
title: kubectl Quick Reference — Updating resources

instruction: How can you auto scale a Kubernetes deployment?
response: You can use the command `kubectl autoscale deployment foo --min=2 --max=10` to auto scale a deployment.
title: kubectl Quick Reference — Updating resources

instruction: What is the default behavior for certificate renewal when upgrading the control plane?
response: The default behavior is to perform the renewal of certificates used by component changed during upgrades.
title: — Synopsis

instruction: What is the purpose of the --patches flag when upgrading the control plane?
response: The --patches flag is used to specify a directory containing files with patches to apply during the upgrade process. The patches can be in JSON or YAML format and match the patch formats supported by kubectl.
title: — Synopsis

instruction: What is the default location of the kubeconfig file used when talking to the cluster?
response: The default location is /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf, but if the flag is not set, a set of standard locations can be searched for an existing kubeconfig file.
title: — Synopsis

instruction: What is the API group used by RBAC authorization in Kubernetes?
response: The rbac.authorization.k8s.io API group is used by RBAC authorization in Kubernetes to drive authorization decisions.
title: Using RBAC Authorization

instruction: How can you enable RBAC authorization in the Kubernetes API server?
response: You can enable RBAC authorization by starting the API server with the --authorization-config flag set to a file that includes the RBAC authorizer, or by starting the API server with the --authorization-mode flag set to a comma-separated list that includes RBAC.
title: Using RBAC Authorization

instruction: What is the format of the file that includes the RBAC authorizer for enabling RBAC authorization?
response: The file should be in the format of apiVersion: apiserver.config.k8s.io/v1, kind: AuthorizationConfiguration, and include the RBAC authorizer in the authorizers list.
title: Using RBAC Authorization

instruction: What is the default behavior when creating a Pod without specifying a ServiceAccount?
response: Kubernetes automatically assigns the ServiceAccount named 'default' in that namespace.
title: Configure Service Accounts for Pods — Use the default service account to access the API server

instruction: How can you verify the ServiceAccount assigned to a Pod?
response: You can fetch the details for a Pod using 'kubectl get pods/<podname> -o yaml' and look for the 'spec.serviceAccountName' field in the output.
title: Configure Service Accounts for Pods — Use the default service account to access the API server

instruction: What happens to the API credentials when a Pod is deleted?
response: The API credentials are automatically revoked 60 seconds beyond the Pod's deletion timestamp, even if finalizers are in place.
title: Configure Service Accounts for Pods — Use the default service account to access the API server

instruction: Why is it recommended to replace failed etcd members immediately, even if the cluster can tolerate minor member failures?
response: Replacing failed members immediately improves the overall health of the cluster.
title: Operating etcd clusters for Kubernetes — Replacing a failed etcd member

instruction: How do you get the member ID of a failed etcd member?
response: Use the command `etcdctl --endpoints=http://<other-member-urls> member list` to display the member list.
title: Operating etcd clusters for Kubernetes — Replacing a failed etcd member

instruction: What should you do if each Kubernetes API server is configured to communicate with all etcd members after replacing a failed member?
response: Remove the failed member from the `--etcd-servers` flag and restart each Kubernetes API server.
title: Operating etcd clusters for Kubernetes — Replacing a failed etcd member

instruction: What is the purpose of stopping the etcd server on the broken node before removing the failed member?
response: Stopping the etcd server prevents writes to the data directory and helps to prevent data corruption.
title: Operating etcd clusters for Kubernetes — Replacing a failed etcd member

instruction: What is the purpose of the --dry-run flag when using kubeadm reset phase remove-etcd-member?
response: The --dry-run flag is used to prevent any changes from being applied; instead, it will output what would be done.
title: — Synopsis

instruction: What is the default location of the kubeconfig file used by kubeadm reset phase remove-etcd-member?
response: The default location is /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf, but if the flag is not set, a set of standard locations can be searched for an existing kubeconfig file.
title: — Synopsis

instruction: What is the purpose of CSI plugins in Kubernetes?
response: CSI plugins provide a way to extend Kubernetes with supports for new kinds of volumes, which can be backed by durable external storage, provide ephemeral storage, or offer a read-only interface to information using a filesystem paradigm.
title: Compute, Storage, and Networking Extensions

instruction: What is the difference between CSI and FlexVolume plugins?
response: CSI plugins are the recommended replacement for FlexVolume plugins, which are deprecated since Kubernetes v1.23. FlexVolume plugins allow users to mount volume types that aren't natively supported by Kubernetes, but CSI plugins offer more features and are the preferred choice for new deployments.
title: Compute, Storage, and Networking Extensions

instruction: What is the purpose of Device plugins in Kubernetes?
response: Device plugins allow a node to discover new Node facilities and provide these custom node-local facilities to Pods that request them, in addition to the built-in node resources such as cpu and memory.
title: Compute, Storage, and Networking Extensions

instruction: What type of plugins are required for a working Pod network in Kubernetes?
response: A network plugin is required for a working Pod network and to support other aspects of the Kubernetes network model. Kubernetes 1.36 is compatible with CNI network plugins.
title: Compute, Storage, and Networking Extensions

instruction: What is required to use seccomp profile defaulting in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: You must run the kubelet with the --seccomp-default command line flag enabled for each node where you want to use it.
title: Restrict a Container's Syscalls with seccomp — Enable the use ofRuntimeDefaultas the default seccomp profile for all workloads

instruction: What is the purpose of the RuntimeDefault seccomp profile?
response: The RuntimeDefault seccomp profile is defined by the container runtime and aims to provide a strong set of security defaults while preserving the functionality of the workload.
title: Restrict a Container's Syscalls with seccomp — Enable the use ofRuntimeDefaultas the default seccomp profile for all workloads

instruction: What are some possible mitigation strategies for workloads that fail during runtime with the RuntimeDefault profile?
response: You can run the workload explicitly as Unconfined, disable the SeccompDefault feature for the nodes, or create a custom seccomp profile for the workload.
title: Restrict a Container's Syscalls with seccomp — Enable the use ofRuntimeDefaultas the default seccomp profile for all workloads

instruction: How can you enable the SeccompDefault feature gate in a kind cluster?
response: You can enable the SeccompDefault feature gate in kind by ensuring that kind provides the minimum required Kubernetes version and enables the SeccompDefault feature in the kind configuration.
title: Restrict a Container's Syscalls with seccomp — Enable the use ofRuntimeDefaultas the default seccomp profile for all workloads

instruction: What is the basic syntax to create and run a particular image in a pod using kubectl run?
response: The basic syntax is `kubectl run NAME --image=image [--env="key=value"] [--port=port] [--dry-run=server|client] [--overrides=inline-json] [--command] -- [COMMAND] [args...]`.
title: kubectl run — Synopsis

instruction: How can you start a pod with a specific image and expose a particular port?
response: You can start a pod with a specific image and expose a particular port by using the `--port` flag, for example, `kubectl run hazelcast --image=hazelcast/hazelcast --port=5701`.
title: kubectl run — Synopsis

instruction: What is the purpose of the `--dry-run` flag in the kubectl run command?
response: The `--dry-run` flag is used to print the corresponding API objects without creating them, allowing you to see what would be created without actually creating it, for example, `kubectl run nginx --image=nginx --dry-run=client`.
title: kubectl run — Synopsis

instruction: How can you start a pod with a custom command and arguments?
response: You can start a pod with a custom command and arguments by using the `--command` flag followed by the command and its arguments, for example, `kubectl run nginx --image=nginx --command -- <cmd> <arg1> ... <argN>`.
title: kubectl run — Synopsis

instruction: What types of volumes can be projected into a Pod using a projected Volume?
response: Currently, secret, configMap, downwardAPI, and serviceAccountToken volumes can be projected.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a Projected Volume for Storage

instruction: What is the recommended environment for running this tutorial?
response: It is recommended to run this tutorial on a cluster with at least two nodes that are not acting as control plane hosts.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a Projected Volume for Storage

instruction: How can you check the version of the kubectl command-line tool?
response: To check the version, enter kubectl version.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a Projected Volume for Storage

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the `GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/watch/resourceslices` API call?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type `BOOKMARK` from the server. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag, and clients should not assume bookmarks are returned at any specific interval.
title: ResourceSlice — getWatch List

instruction: What happens when a client uses the `continue` field to retrieve more results from the server, but the specified continue value is no longer valid?
response: The server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token. The client may send another list request with the token received with the 410 error, and the server will respond with a list starting from the next key, but from the latest snapshot, which is inconsistent from the previous list results.
title: ResourceSlice — getWatch List

instruction: What is the purpose of the `sendInitialEvents` field in the `GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/watch/resourceslices` API call?
response: The `sendInitialEvents` field may be set together with `watch=true` to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all synthetic events have been sent, a synthetic `Bookmark` event will be sent, and the watch stream will proceed as usual, sending watch events corresponding to changes to objects watched.
title: ResourceSlice — getWatch List

instruction: What are the requirements for running this tutorial?
response: You need to have a Kubernetes cluster, and the kubectl command-line tool must be configured to communicate with your cluster. It is recommended to run this tutorial on a cluster with at least two nodes that are not acting as control plane hosts.
title: Example: Deploying WordPress and MySQL with Persistent Volumes — Objectives

instruction: What are the alternatives for creating a Kubernetes cluster for this tutorial?
response: You can create a cluster using minikube or use one of these Kubernetes playgrounds: iximiuz Labs, Killercoda, or KodeKloud.
title: Example: Deploying WordPress and MySQL with Persistent Volumes — Objectives

instruction: What is the minimum version of kubectl required to run this tutorial?
response: The example works with kubectl 1.27 and above.
title: Example: Deploying WordPress and MySQL with Persistent Volumes — Objectives

instruction: What is the purpose of the --dry-run flag when upgrading with kubeadm?
response: The --dry-run flag is used to output the actions that would be performed without changing any state.
title: kubeadm upgrade phases — Options

instruction: What is the format of the files in the directory specified by the --patches flag?
response: The files in the directory specified by the --patches flag must be named in the format 'target[suffix][+patchtype].extension', where 'target' can be one of the specified components, 'patchtype' can be 'strategic', 'merge', or 'json', and 'extension' must be either 'json' or 'yaml'.
title: kubeadm upgrade phases — Options

instruction: What is the purpose of the `continue` field in the response of a list call?
response: The `continue` field is used to continue retrieving more results from the server. It should be set when retrieving more results and should be used with identical query parameters (except for the value of `continue`) from a previous query result.
title: Job — getList All Namespaces

instruction: What happens if the client needs a consistent list and the `continue` value is no longer valid?
response: The server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token. The client must restart their list without the `continue` field to get a consistent list.
title: Job — getList All Namespaces

instruction: What is the semantic of the watch request when `sendInitialEvents` option is set?
response: When `sendInitialEvents` option is set, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. The bookmark will report the ResourceVersion (RV) corresponding to the set of objects, and be marked with "k8s.io/initial-events-end": "true" annotation.
title: Job — getList All Namespaces

instruction: What feature gate enables monitoring of Kubelet metrics for resource managers?
response: The feature gate is called 'PodLevelResourceManagers'.
title: Resource managers — Observability and metrics

instruction: What label distinguishes between resource allocations drawn from the node-level pool versus a pre-allocated pod-level pool?
response: The 'source' label, which can be either 'pod' or 'node'.
title: Resource managers — Observability and metrics

instruction: What metric tracks the cumulative number of containers granted a specific type of resource assignment?
response: The 'resource_manager_container_assignments' metric, which includes the 'assignment_type' label to provide visibility into different types of resource assignments.
title: Resource managers — Observability and metrics

instruction: What is the default value for the certificateRenewal field in UpgradeNodeConfiguration?
response: The default value for the certificateRenewal field is true.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta4) — UpgradeNodeConfiguration

instruction: What is the effect of setting dryRun to true in UpgradeNodeConfiguration?
response: If dryRun is set to true, kubeadm will not apply any changes and will only output what would be done.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta4) — UpgradeNodeConfiguration

instruction: What are the valid values for the imagePullPolicy field in UpgradeNodeConfiguration?
response: The valid values for the imagePullPolicy field are 'Always', 'IfNotPresent', or 'Never'. If this field is unset, kubeadm will default it to 'IfNotPresent' or pull the required images if not present on the host.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta4) — UpgradeNodeConfiguration

instruction: What is the default value for the imagePullSerial field in UpgradeNodeConfiguration?
response: The default value for the imagePullSerial field is true.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta4) — UpgradeNodeConfiguration

instruction: What is the effect of setting --aggregator-reject-forwarding-redirect to false?
response: Aggregator will not reject forwarding redirect response back to client.
title: kube-apiserver — Options

instruction: How does the --allow-metric-labels flag work?
response: The --allow-metric-labels flag allows you to specify a map from metric-label to value allow-list. The key's format is <MetricName>,<LabelName> and the value's format is <allowed_value>,<allowed_value>...
title: kube-apiserver — Options

instruction: What is the default value of --anonymous-auth?
response: The default value of --anonymous-auth is true, which enables anonymous requests to the secure port of the API server.
title: kube-apiserver — Options

instruction: What is the purpose of the --audit-log-batch-throttle-qps flag?
response: The --audit-log-batch-throttle-qps flag sets the maximum average number of batches per second for batching throttling in batch mode.
title: kube-apiserver — Options

instruction: What is the default value of --audit-log-format?
response: The default value of --audit-log-format is 'json', which indicates structured json format for saved audits.
title: kube-apiserver — Options

instruction: What is the effect of setting --audit-log-maxsize to 0?
response: Setting --audit-log-maxsize to 0 disables rotation of the audit log file (not recommended).
title: kube-apiserver — Options

instruction: What is the purpose of the --audit-log-truncate-enabled flag?
response: The --audit-log-truncate-enabled flag enables or disables event and batch truncating.
title: kube-apiserver — Options

instruction: What is the default value of --audit-log-truncate-max-batch-size?
response: The default value of --audit-log-truncate-max-batch-size is 10485760, which is the maximum size of the batch sent to the underlying backend.
title: kube-apiserver — Options

instruction: What is the default value of --audit-log-truncate-max-event-size?
response: The default value of --audit-log-truncate-max-event-size is 102400, which is the maximum size of the audit event sent to the underlying backend.
title: kube-apiserver — Options

instruction: What is the default value of --audit-policy-file?
response: The default value of --audit-policy-file is not specified, implying that it must be set manually.
title: kube-apiserver — Options

instruction: What happens when you run kubeadm certs renew all without specifying any flags?
response: Renewals are run unconditionally, regardless of expiration date.

instruction: How can you determine which container runtime endpoint your nodes use?
response: You can check the kubelet configuration on your nodes by reading the starting commands for the kubelet process using the command `tr \0 ' ' < /proc/$(pgrep kubelet)/cmdline` and looking for the `--container-runtime` flag and the `--container-runtime-endpoint` flag.
title: Find Out What Container Runtime is Used on a Node — Find out what container runtime endpoint you use

instruction: What does the presence of the `--container-runtime-endpoint` flag indicate?
response: The presence of the `--container-runtime-endpoint` flag indicates that you are using a container runtime other than Docker Engine, and you can check the socket name to find out which runtime you use.
title: Find Out What Container Runtime is Used on a Node — Find out what container runtime endpoint you use

instruction: What should you do if you want to change the Container Runtime on a Node from Docker Engine to containerd?
response: You can find out more information on migrating from Docker Engine to containerd.
title: Find Out What Container Runtime is Used on a Node — Find out what container runtime endpoint you use

instruction: What are the two ways cloud providers can fulfill a load balancer request?
response: Cloud providers can fulfill a load balancer request either by using a proxy that terminates the client connection and opens a new connection to your nodes/endpoints, or by using a packet forwarder that sends requests from the client to the node with the source IP of the client.
title: Using Source IP — Cross-platform support

instruction: What is required for load balancers in the first category to communicate the true client IP?
response: Load balancers in the first category must use an agreed upon protocol between the load balancer and backend to communicate the true client IP, such as the HTTP Forwarded or X-FORWARDED-FOR headers, or the proxy protocol.
title: Using Source IP — Cross-platform support

instruction: How can load balancers in the second category leverage the feature described above?
response: Load balancers in the second category can leverage the feature described above by creating an HTTP health check pointing at the port stored in the service.spec.healthCheckNodePort field on the Service.
title: Using Source IP — Cross-platform support

instruction: What is the difference between a JSON merge patch and a strategic merge patch in kubectl patch?
response: A JSON merge patch requires specifying the entire new list when updating a list, which completely replaces the existing list, whereas a strategic merge patch allows for more flexible updates.
title: Update API Objects in Place Using kubectl patch — Use a JSON merge patch to update a Deployment

instruction: What is the default value for the type parameter in the kubectl patch command?
response: The default value for the type parameter is strategic, which means that a strategic merge patch is performed by default.
title: Update API Objects in Place Using kubectl patch — Use a JSON merge patch to update a Deployment

instruction: How does a JSON merge patch update a list of containers in a Deployment?
response: A JSON merge patch replaces the entire existing list of containers with the new list specified in the patch file, as shown in the example where the containers list is updated to contain only one Container.
title: Update API Objects in Place Using kubectl patch — Use a JSON merge patch to update a Deployment

instruction: What is the system:masters group in Kubernetes?
response: The system:masters group is a built-in Kubernetes group that grants unrestricted access to the API server.
title: Authorization — The system:masters group

instruction: What are the implications of adding a user to the system:masters group?
response: Adding a user to this group grants them full cluster administrator privileges, bypassing any authorization restrictions imposed by the RBAC or Webhook mechanisms.
title: Authorization — The system:masters group

instruction: What is the recommended alternative to granting a user cluster-admin rights?
response: You can create a ClusterRoleBinding to the built-in cluster-admin ClusterRole instead of adding the user to the system:masters group.
title: Authorization — The system:masters group

instruction: How do you create a ConfigMap with an empty configuration block?
response: You can create a ConfigMap with an empty configuration block by running the command `cat <<EOF >./example-redis-config.yaml ...` and applying it with `kubectl apply -f example-redis-config.yaml`.
title: Configuring Redis using a ConfigMap — Real World Example: Configuring Redis using a ConfigMap

instruction: Why doesn't the Redis Pod pick up the updated configuration values from the ConfigMap immediately after applying the updated ConfigMap?
response: The Pod needs to be restarted to grab updated values from associated ConfigMaps.
title: Configuring Redis using a ConfigMap — Real World Example: Configuring Redis using a ConfigMap

instruction: How do you confirm that the ConfigMap was updated with the new configuration values?
response: You can confirm that the ConfigMap was updated by running `kubectl describe configmap/example-redis-config` and checking the updated configuration values.
title: Configuring Redis using a ConfigMap — Real World Example: Configuring Redis using a ConfigMap

instruction: How do you access the logs of an Init Container in a Pod?
response: You can access the logs of an Init Container in a Pod by running the command `kubectl logs <pod-name> -c <init-container-name>`, where `<pod-name>` is the name of the Pod and `<init-container-name>` is the name of the Init Container.
title: Debug Init Containers — Accessing logs from Init Containers

instruction: What is the significance of the 'Init:' status in a Pod's status?
response: The 'Init:' status in a Pod's status summarizes the status of Init Container execution. It indicates the number of Init Containers that have been executed and the number that have completed so far.
title: Debug Init Containers — Accessing logs from Init Containers

instruction: What does an 'Init:Error' status in a Pod's status indicate?
response: An 'Init:Error' status in a Pod's status indicates that an Init Container has failed to execute.
title: Debug Init Containers — Accessing logs from Init Containers

instruction: What does a 'PodInitializing' status in a Pod's status indicate?
response: A 'PodInitializing' status in a Pod's status indicates that the Pod has already finished executing Init Containers.
title: Debug Init Containers — Accessing logs from Init Containers

instruction: How does the metadata path for a Pod created from a ResourceClaimTemplate relate to the Pod's claim reference name?
response: The metadata path uses the Pod's claim reference name instead of a predictable claim name.
title: Access DRA Device Metadata — Access device metadata with a ResourceClaimTemplate

instruction: What is the purpose of the podClaimName field in the JSON metadata?
response: The podClaimName field records the mapping between the Pod's claim reference name and the generated claim name.
title: Access DRA Device Metadata — Access device metadata with a ResourceClaimTemplate

instruction: What is the default container registry used to pull control plane images by kubeadm?
response: The default container registry used to pull control plane images by kubeadm is 'registry.k8s.io'.
title: — Synopsis

instruction: How can you specify a custom Kubernetes version for the control plane when using kubeadm?
response: You can specify a custom Kubernetes version for the control plane when using kubeadm by using the '--kubernetes-version' flag followed by the desired version, for example '--kubernetes-version stable-1'.
title: — Synopsis

instruction: What is the purpose of the '--cri-socket' flag in kubeadm?
response: The '--cri-socket' flag in kubeadm is used to specify the path to the CRI socket to connect to, and can be used when there are multiple CRI installations or a non-standard CRI socket.
title: — Synopsis

instruction: What command should be run on the Windows node to sync new kubelet configuration?
response: Run the command `kubeadm upgrade node` from the Windows node to sync new kubelet configuration.
title: Upgrading Windows nodes — Upgrade the kubelet configuration

instruction: How do you upgrade and restart the kubelet on a Windows node?
response: To upgrade and restart the kubelet, stop the service with `stop-service kubelet`, download the new kubelet executable with `curl https://dl.k8s.io/v1.36.0/bin/windows/amd64/kubelet.exe -Lo <path-to-kubelet.exe>`, and then restart the service with `restart-service kubelet`.
title: Upgrading Windows nodes — Upgrade the kubelet configuration

instruction: How do you upgrade and restart the kube-proxy on a Windows node?
response: To upgrade and restart the kube-proxy, stop the service with `stop-service kube-proxy`, download the new kube-proxy executable with `curl https://dl.k8s.io/v1.36.0/bin/windows/amd64/kube-proxy.exe -Lo <path-to-kube-proxy.exe>`, and then restart the service with `restart-service kube-proxy`.
title: Upgrading Windows nodes — Upgrade the kubelet configuration

instruction: What is the default behavior for the 'pretty' parameter in the DELETE /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/endpoints endpoint?
response: The default behavior for the 'pretty' parameter is 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: Endpoints — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting the 'continue' parameter in the DELETE /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/endpoints endpoint?
response: The 'continue' parameter should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It allows clients to retrieve the next set of results, but the server may reject a continue value it does not recognize, and the client may need to restart their list without the continue field.
title: Endpoints — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting the 'ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential' parameter to true in the DELETE /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/endpoints endpoint?
response: Setting 'ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential' to true will trigger an unsafe deletion of the resource in case the normal deletion flow fails with a corrupt object error. This may potentially break the cluster if the workload associated with the resource being unsafe-deleted relies on normal deletion flow.
title: Endpoints — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting the 'limit' parameter in the DELETE /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/endpoints endpoint?
response: The 'limit' parameter sets a maximum number of responses to return for a list call. If more items exist, the server will set the 'continue' field on the list metadata to a value that can be used with the same initial query to retrieve the next set of results.
title: Endpoints — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting the 'orphanDependents' parameter to true in the DELETE /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/endpoints endpoint?
response: Setting 'orphanDependents' to true will add the 'orphan' finalizer to the object's finalizers list, which will orphan the dependent objects.
title: Endpoints — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting the 'propagationPolicy' parameter in the DELETE /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/endpoints endpoint?
response: The 'propagationPolicy' parameter determines whether and how garbage collection will be performed. It can be set to 'Orphan', 'Background', or 'Foreground' to specify the policy.
title: Endpoints — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the `autoscaling/v2` API version in HorizontalPodAutoscaler (HPA)?
response: The `autoscaling/v2` API version allows you to introduce additional metrics to use when autoscaling a Deployment, such as CPU and memory utilization.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler Walkthrough — Autoscaling on multiple metrics and custom metrics

instruction: How do you specify a custom metric in the HPA YAML file?
response: You can specify a custom metric using a `type` field with one of the following values: `Resource`, `Pods`, or `Object`. For example, to specify a pod metric, you would use `type: Pods` and provide a `metric` block with the desired metric name and target value.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler Walkthrough — Autoscaling on multiple metrics and custom metrics

instruction: How does the HPA calculate proposed replica counts when multiple metrics are provided?
response: The HPA calculates proposed replica counts for each metric and then chooses the one with the highest replica count. This allows you to define multiple metrics and have the HPA choose the best replica count based on the metrics that are being tracked.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler Walkthrough — Autoscaling on multiple metrics and custom metrics

instruction: What is the recommended approach to testing or developing an API extension in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: It is not recommended to do testing/development/debug of an API extension in a live cluster, as API extensions affect the entire cluster.
title: Set up an Extension API Server — Set up an extension api-server to work with the aggregation layer

instruction: What is the purpose of the CA cert used to sign the server cert for the extension api-server?
response: The CA cert is used to sign the server cert for the extension api-server, and it should also have a CN of the Kube DNS name, derived from the Kubernetes service and be of the form <service name>.<service name namespace>.svc.
title: Set up an Extension API Server — Set up an extension api-server to work with the aggregation layer

instruction: What is the expected output when running `kubectl get` after creating an API service?
response: The expected output is 'No resources found.', which indicates that everything worked but you currently have no objects of that resource type created.
title: Set up an Extension API Server — Set up an extension api-server to work with the aggregation layer

instruction: What is the purpose of the `extension-apiserver-authentication-reader` role?
response: The `extension-apiserver-authentication-reader` role is used to allow the extension api-server to access the `extension-apiserver-authentication` configmap.
title: Set up an Extension API Server — Set up an extension api-server to work with the aggregation layer

instruction: What is the primary purpose of a StatefulSet in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: A StatefulSet runs a group of Pods, and maintains a sticky identity for each of those Pods. This is useful for managing applications that need persistent storage or a stable, unique network identity.
title: StatefulSet

instruction: How does a StatefulSet guarantee network identity consistency?
response: The StatefulSet guarantees that a given network identity will always map to the same storage identity.
title: StatefulSet

instruction: What type of applications benefit from using a StatefulSet?
response: Applications that need persistent storage or a stable, unique network identity benefit from using a StatefulSet.
title: StatefulSet

instruction: What information does the kubelet report in the .status.declaredFeatures field of the Node?
response: The kubelet reports which managed Kubernetes features are currently enabled on the node, specifically only features under active development.
title: Node Declared Features — How it Works

instruction: How does the default kube-scheduler use the NodeDeclaredFeatures plugin?
response: The plugin checks the PodSpec to infer the set of node features required by the pod in the PreFilter stage, and then checks if the features listed in the node's .status.declaredFeatures satisfy the requirements in the Filter stage.
title: Node Declared Features — How it Works

instruction: What is the purpose of the nodedeclaredfeaturevalidator admission controller?
response: The admission controller can reject Pods that require features not declared by the node they are bound to, preventing issues during pod updates.
title: Node Declared Features — How it Works

instruction: What happens when `fsGroup` is unset in a PodSecurityContext?
response: The Kubelet will not modify the ownership and permissions of any volume.
title: Pod — PodSecurityContext

instruction: What is the default behavior for `fsGroupChangePolicy` when it is not specified?
response: The default behavior is to always change the ownership and permissions of the volume whenever it is mounted inside a Pod.
title: Pod — PodSecurityContext

instruction: What happens when `runAsNonRoot` is set to `true` and the container runs as UID 0 (root)?
response: The Kubelet will fail to start the container.
title: Pod — PodSecurityContext

instruction: What is the effect of setting `seLinuxChangePolicy` to `Recursive`?
response: It relabels all files on all Pod volumes by the container runtime, which may be slow for large volumes but allows mixing privileged and unprivileged Pods sharing the same volume on the same node.
title: Pod — PodSecurityContext

instruction: How does a Kubernetes cluster determine the default memory limit for a namespace?
response: A Kubernetes cluster assigns the default memory limit to a namespace, which is then applied to a Pod's container if it does not specify its own memory limit.
title: Configure Default Memory Requests and Limits for a Namespace

instruction: What happens when a Pod's container does not specify its own memory limit?
response: The control plane assigns the default memory limit to that container.
title: Configure Default Memory Requests and Limits for a Namespace

instruction: Under what conditions does Kubernetes assign a default memory request for a namespace?
response: The conditions for assigning a default memory request are explained later in this topic.
title: Configure Default Memory Requests and Limits for a Namespace

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'allowWatchBookmarks' field in the GET /apis/scheduling.k8s.io/v1/watch/priorityclasses request?
response: The 'allowWatchBookmarks' field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK' from the server. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag, and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: PriorityClass — getWatch List

instruction: What happens when a client uses the 'continue' field to retrieve more results from the server, but the specified continue value is no longer valid?
response: The server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token. The client must restart their list without the continue field to receive a consistent list.
title: PriorityClass — getWatch List

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'sendInitialEvents' field when set together with 'watch=true'?
response: The 'sendInitialEvents' field may be set together with 'watch=true' to start the watch stream with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. This is useful for clients that need to know the current state of objects before receiving watch events.
title: PriorityClass — getWatch List

instruction: What is the semantic of the watch request when 'resourceVersionMatch' is set to 'NotOlderThan'?
response: When 'resourceVersionMatch' is set to 'NotOlderThan', the watch request is interpreted as 'data at least as new as the provided resourceVersion', and the bookmark event is sent when the state is synced to a resourceVersion at least as fresh as the one provided by the ListOptions.
title: PriorityClass — getWatch List

instruction: What happens to a node when it experiences an out-of-memory (OOM) event before the kubelet can reclaim memory?
response: The node depends on the oom_killer to respond.
title: Node-pressure Eviction — Node out of memory behavior

instruction: How does the kubelet set the oom_score_adj value for each container?
response: The kubelet sets an oom_score_adj value for each container based on the Quality of Service (QoS) for the pod.
title: Node-pressure Eviction — Node out of memory behavior

instruction: What determines the oom_score_adj value for a container with a Burstable QoS?
response: The oom_score_adj value is determined by the formula: min(max(2, 1000 - (1000 × memoryRequestBytes) / machineMemoryCapacityBytes), 999)
title: Node-pressure Eviction — Node out of memory behavior

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' field in the DeviceTaintRule watch API?
response: The 'continue' field is used to retrieve more results from the server when the initial query returns a limited number of items. It should be set to the value received in the previous query result with identical query parameters, and the server may reject a continue value it does not recognize.
title: DeviceTaintRule — getWatch

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'limit' to a non-zero value when making a list call to the DeviceTaintRule API?
response: Setting a limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items in the event all requested objects are filtered out, and clients should only use the presence of the continue field to determine whether more results are available.
title: DeviceTaintRule — getWatch

instruction: What is the semantic of the watch request when 'sendInitialEvents' is set to true?
response: When 'sendInitialEvents' is set to true, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event that reports the ResourceVersion corresponding to the set of objects. Afterwards, the watch stream will proceed as usual, sending watch events corresponding to changes to objects watched.
title: DeviceTaintRule — getWatch

instruction: What type of volume is used in the Redis Pod example?
response: The Redis Pod example uses an emptyDir volume, which lasts for the life of the Pod, even if the Container terminates and restarts.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a Volume for Storage — Configure a volume for a Pod

instruction: What happens to the test-file created in the /data/redis directory when the Container terminates and restarts?
response: The test-file is still present in the /data/redis directory after the Container terminates and restarts, because the emptyDir volume persists even after the Container restarts.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a Volume for Storage — Configure a volume for a Pod

instruction: What is the restart policy of the Redis Pod?
response: The Redis Pod has a restartPolicy of Always, which means that the Container will be restarted if it terminates.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a Volume for Storage — Configure a volume for a Pod

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubectl create rolebinding command?
response: The kubectl create rolebinding command is used to create a role binding for a particular role or cluster role.
title: kubectl create rolebinding — Synopsis

instruction: How can you specify a cluster role when creating a role binding?
response: You can specify a cluster role by using the --clusterrole option followed by the name of the cluster role, for example, --clusterrole=admin.
title: kubectl create rolebinding — Synopsis

instruction: What is the difference between the --user and --serviceaccount options when creating a role binding?
response: The --user option is used to specify a user, while the --serviceaccount option is used to specify a service account, and both options are used in conjunction with the --namespace option to specify the namespace where the service account is located.
title: kubectl create rolebinding — Synopsis

instruction: What happens when both SecurityContext and PodSecurityContext have the same field set?
response: When both are set, the values in SecurityContext take precedence.
title: Pod — SecurityContext

instruction: Can the `allowPrivilegeEscalation` field be set when the container is running on Windows?
response: No, this field cannot be set when `spec.os.name` is Windows.
title: Pod — SecurityContext

instruction: What is the default behavior for the `procMount` field when `spec.os.name` is not Windows?
response: The default value is `Default` which uses the container runtime defaults for readonly paths and masked paths.
title: Pod — SecurityContext

instruction: What is the purpose of the `runAsNonRoot` field?
response: It indicates that the container must run as a non-root user, and the Kubelet will validate the image at runtime to ensure that it does not run as UID 0 (root).
title: Pod — SecurityContext

instruction: What is the default value for the 'pretty' parameter in the DELETE /apis/flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1/prioritylevelconfigurations request?
response: The default value for the 'pretty' parameter is 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: PriorityLevelConfiguration — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What happens when a client uses a continue value from a previous query result with different query parameters?
response: The server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token.
title: PriorityLevelConfiguration — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting the 'limit' parameter in the DELETE /apis/flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1/prioritylevelconfigurations request?
response: Setting a limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items (up to zero items) in the event all requested objects are filtered out.
title: PriorityLevelConfiguration — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the default policy for garbage collection when the 'PropagationPolicy' field is not set?
response: The default policy is decided by the existing finalizer set in the metadata.finalizers and the resource-specific default policy.
title: PriorityLevelConfiguration — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential' to true?
response: It will trigger an unsafe deletion of the resource in case the normal deletion flow fails with a corrupt object error.
title: PriorityLevelConfiguration — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the ObjectMetricStatus in a Kubernetes Horizontal Pod Autoscaler?
response: ObjectMetricStatus indicates the current value of a metric describing a Kubernetes object, such as hits-per-second on an Ingress object.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler — ObjectMetricStatus

instruction: What does the 'current' field in ObjectMetricStatus represent?
response: The 'current' field contains the current value for the given metric.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler — ObjectMetricStatus

instruction: What is the 'metric' field in ObjectMetricStatus used for?
response: The 'metric' field identifies the target metric by name and selector.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler — ObjectMetricStatus

instruction: What is the recommended approach for setting kubelet's eviction thresholds when swap is enabled?
response: It is recommended to set the kubelet's eviction thresholds to be slightly lower than the vm.min_free_kbytes value.
title: Swap memory management — Evictions

instruction: Why is it not ideal to set the eviction thresholds too high when swap is enabled?
response: Setting the eviction thresholds too high could result in the node running out of memory and invoking the OOM killer, which is not ideal.
title: Swap memory management — Evictions

instruction: How can you determine the value of vm.min_free_kbytes on a node?
response: You can determine the value of vm.min_free_kbytes by running the command cat /proc/sys/vm/min_free_kbytes on the node.
title: Swap memory management — Evictions

instruction: What is the minimum duration for which a GA flag in a user-facing component must function after deprecation?
response: 12 months or 2 releases, whichever is longer.
title: Kubernetes Deprecation Policy — Deprecating a flag or CLI

instruction: What is the minimum duration for which a GA flag in an admin-facing component must function after deprecation?
response: 6 months or 1 release, whichever is longer.
title: Kubernetes Deprecation Policy — Deprecating a flag or CLI

instruction: Can a CLI element be deprecated in favor of a less stable alternative?
response: No, CLI elements cannot be deprecated in favor of less stable CLI elements. The recommended alternative must be of the same or higher stability level.
title: Kubernetes Deprecation Policy — Deprecating a flag or CLI

instruction: What is the recommended approach for obtaining ServiceAccount tokens in Kubernetes v1.22 and later?
response: The recommended approach is to obtain a short-lived, automatically rotating ServiceAccount token by using the TokenRequest API instead of using the kubernetes.io/service-account-token type of Secret.
title: Secrets — ServiceAccount token Secrets

instruction: How can you ensure that the kubernetes.io/service-account.name annotation is set to an existing ServiceAccount name?
response: You should create the ServiceAccount object first if you are creating both the ServiceAccount and the Secret objects. Alternatively, you can set the annotation to an existing ServiceAccount name when creating the Secret object.
title: Secrets — ServiceAccount token Secrets

instruction: How does a Kubernetes controller fill in some other fields in the Secret object?
response: After the Secret is created, a Kubernetes controller fills in some other fields such as the kubernetes.io/service-account.uid annotation, and the token key in the data field, which is populated with an authentication token.
title: Secrets — ServiceAccount token Secrets

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client signer?
response: The kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client signer signs certificates that will be honored as client certificates by the API server.
title: Certificates and Certificate Signing Requests — Kubernetes signers

instruction: What is the recommended maximum lifetime for certificates signed by the kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client signer?
response: The recommended maximum lifetime is 30 days.
title: Certificates and Certificate Signing Requests — Kubernetes signers

instruction: What is the purpose of the CertificateSubjectRestriction admission plugin?
response: The CertificateSubjectRestriction admission plugin is enabled by default to restrict system:masters, but it is often not the only cluster-admin subject in a cluster.
title: Certificates and Certificate Signing Requests — Kubernetes signers

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-serving signer?
response: The kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-serving signer signs certificates that can be used to verify kube-apiserver serving certificates.
title: Certificates and Certificate Signing Requests — Kubernetes signers

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubernetes.io/legacy-unknown signer?
response: The kubernetes.io/legacy-unknown signer has no guarantees for trust at all, and some third-party distributions of Kubernetes may honor client certificates signed by it.
title: Certificates and Certificate Signing Requests — Kubernetes signers

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'dryRun' field in a PATCH request to a PersistentVolumeClaim?
response: When present, 'dryRun' indicates that modifications should not be persisted. An invalid or unrecognized 'dryRun' directive will result in an error response and no further processing of the request.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — patchPatch

instruction: What is the default behavior for unknown or duplicate fields in a PATCH request to a PersistentVolumeClaim, prior to v1.23?
response: The default behavior prior to v1.23 is to ignore any unknown fields that are silently dropped from the object, and will ignore all but the last duplicate field that the decoder encounters.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — patchPatch

instruction: What is the effect of setting the 'force' field to true in an Apply request to a PersistentVolumeClaim?
response: Setting 'force' to true will cause the user to re-acquire conflicting fields owned by other people.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — patchPatch

instruction: What is the primary purpose of a RoleBinding in Kubernetes?
response: A RoleBinding grants the permissions defined in a role to a user or set of users, holding a list of subjects and a reference to the role being granted.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — RoleBinding and ClusterRoleBinding

instruction: How does a RoleBinding differ from a ClusterRoleBinding in terms of scope?
response: A RoleBinding grants permissions within a specific namespace, whereas a ClusterRoleBinding grants that access cluster-wide.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — RoleBinding and ClusterRoleBinding

instruction: What happens when you try to change the Role or ClusterRole that a binding refers to?
response: You get a validation error, and if you want to change the roleRef, you need to remove the binding object and create a replacement.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — RoleBinding and ClusterRoleBinding

instruction: What is the purpose of the --dry-run flag when upgrading the CoreDNS addon?
response: The --dry-run flag is used to output the actions that would be performed without actually changing any state.
title: — Synopsis

instruction: What is the default path to the kubeconfig file when using the kubeadm upgrade node phase addon coredns command?
response: The default path to the kubeconfig file is /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf. If the flag is not set, kubeadm will search for an existing kubeconfig file in a set of standard locations.
title: — Synopsis

instruction: How can you specify a custom kubeconfig file when upgrading the CoreDNS addon?
response: You can specify a custom kubeconfig file using the --kubeconfig flag, followed by the path to the file.
title: — Synopsis

instruction: What is the format of the files in the directory specified by the --patches flag?
response: The files in the directory specified by the --patches flag must be named in the format target[suffix][+patchtype].extension, where target can be one of kube-apiserver, kube-controller-manager, kube-scheduler, etcd, kubeletconfiguration, or corednsdeployment, patchtype can be one of strategic, merge, or json, and extension must be either json or yaml.
title: — Synopsis

instruction: What is the purpose of the --recursive or -R argument when using kubectl?
response: The --recursive or -R argument is used to recursively perform operations on local files, including subdirectories, when using kubectl.
title: Managing Workloads — Recursive operations on local files

instruction: How can you specify the --recursive argument when performing a bulk operation on a directory?
response: You can specify the --recursive argument by adding it alongside the --filename or -f argument, like this: kubectl apply -f project/k8s/development --recursive.
title: Managing Workloads — Recursive operations on local files

instruction: Can the --recursive argument be used with other kubectl operations, such as kubectl create, kubectl get, and kubectl delete?
response: Yes, the --recursive argument works with any operation that accepts the --filename or -f argument, including kubectl create, kubectl get, kubectl delete, kubectl describe, and kubectl rollout.
title: Managing Workloads — Recursive operations on local files

instruction: Can the --recursive argument be used with multiple -f arguments?
response: Yes, the --recursive argument can be used with multiple -f arguments, like this: kubectl apply -f project/k8s/namespaces -f project/k8s/development --recursive.
title: Managing Workloads — Recursive operations on local files

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'dryRun' field in a ClusterRoleBinding patch request?
response: The 'dryRun' field indicates that modifications should not be persisted. An invalid or unrecognized 'dryRun' directive will result in an error response and no further processing of the request.
title: ClusterRoleBinding — patchPatch

instruction: What is the default behavior for handling unknown or duplicate fields in a ClusterRoleBinding patch request prior to v1.23?
response: The default behavior prior to v1.23 is to ignore any unknown fields that are silently dropped from the object, and to ignore all but the last duplicate field that the decoder encounters.
title: ClusterRoleBinding — patchPatch

instruction: What is the effect of setting the 'force' field to true in a ClusterRoleBinding patch request?
response: Setting the 'force' field to true will force Apply requests, allowing the user to re-acquire conflicting fields owned by other people.
title: ClusterRoleBinding — patchPatch

instruction: How can you access the Kubernetes API from a Pod without using a proxy?
response: You can pass the authentication token directly to the API server, which is secured by an internal certificate.
title: Accessing the Kubernetes API from a Pod — Without using a proxy

instruction: What is the path to the ServiceAccount token in a Pod?
response: The path to the ServiceAccount token is /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount.
title: Accessing the Kubernetes API from a Pod — Without using a proxy

instruction: How do you read the ServiceAccount bearer token from the Pod's ServiceAccount file?
response: You can read the ServiceAccount bearer token by running the command cat ${SERVICEACCOUNT}/token.
title: Accessing the Kubernetes API from a Pod — Without using a proxy

instruction: What is the format of the curl command used to explore the API with the bearer token?
response: The curl command is in the format: curl --cacert ${CACERT} --header "Authorization: Bearer ${TOKEN}" -X GET ${APISERVER}/api.
title: Accessing the Kubernetes API from a Pod — Without using a proxy

instruction: What is the recommended approach for scheduling Windows containers in Kubernetes 1.36?
response: The best practice for Kubernetes 1.36 is to use a nodeSelector.
title: Guide for Running Windows Containers in Kubernetes — Ensuring OS-specific workloads land on the appropriate container host

instruction: What is the alternative to using nodeSelector for scheduling Windows containers?
response: The alternative is to use taints, where taints can be added to all Windows nodes during registration.
title: Guide for Running Windows Containers in Kubernetes — Ensuring OS-specific workloads land on the appropriate container host

instruction: What is required for a Windows Pod to be scheduled on a Windows node?
response: A Windows Pod requires both a nodeSelector with kubernetes.io/os: windows and a matching toleration with key: os, operator: Equal, value: windows, and effect: NoSchedule.
title: Guide for Running Windows Containers in Kubernetes — Ensuring OS-specific workloads land on the appropriate container host

instruction: What are the different phases of the kubeadm upgrade apply workflow?
response: The different phases of the kubeadm upgrade apply workflow are preflight, control-plane, upload-config, kubelet-config, bootstrap-token, addon, and post-upgrade.
title: kubeadm upgrade phases — kubeadm upgrade apply phase

instruction: How do you invoke a single phase of the kubeadm upgrade apply workflow?
response: You can invoke a single phase of the kubeadm upgrade apply workflow using the command kubeadm upgrade apply phase [flags].
title: kubeadm upgrade phases — kubeadm upgrade apply phase

instruction: What is the format of the value for the node.kubernetes.io/windows-build label?
response: The format is 'MajorVersion.MinorVersion.BuildNumber'.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — node.kubernetes.io/windows-build

instruction: What is the purpose of the storage.alpha.kubernetes.io/migrated-plugins annotation?
response: This annotation shows the in-tree plugin name of the migrated plugin, and its value depends on the cluster's in-tree cloud provider storage type.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — node.kubernetes.io/windows-build

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' field in the Ingress getList API?
response: The 'continue' field is used to retrieve more results from the server when the initial list call returns less than the requested amount of items. It should be set to the value returned by the server in the list metadata to retrieve the next set of results.
title: Ingress — getList

instruction: What is the effect of setting the 'limit' field in the Ingress getList API?
response: Setting the 'limit' field returns a maximum number of responses, but the server may return fewer items if all requested objects are filtered out. The presence of the 'continue' field indicates whether more results are available.
title: Ingress — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'resourceVersionMatch' field in the Ingress getList API?
response: The 'resourceVersionMatch' field determines how the 'resourceVersion' field is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended to set this field for list calls where 'resourceVersion' is set.
title: Ingress — getList

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'sendInitialEvents' to true in the Ingress getList API?
response: When 'sendInitialEvents' is set to true, the watch stream begins with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event with the corresponding ResourceVersion (RV).
title: Ingress — getList

instruction: What is the effect of setting `dryRun` to `All` in a DELETE request to a ClusterRoleBinding?
response: When present, indicates that modifications should not be persisted. An invalid or unrecognized dryRun directive will result in an error response and no further processing of the request.
title: ClusterRoleBinding — deleteDelete

instruction: What is the default value of `ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential`?
response: The default value is false, and the user must opt in to enable it.
title: ClusterRoleBinding — deleteDelete

instruction: What is the relationship between `orphanDependents` and `PropagationPolicy` in a DELETE request to a ClusterRoleBinding?
response: Either this field or PropagationPolicy may be set, but not both. `orphanDependents` is deprecated and will be removed in 1.7.
title: ClusterRoleBinding — deleteDelete

instruction: What is the effect of setting `gracePeriodSeconds` to 0 in a DELETE request to a ClusterRoleBinding?
response: The object should be deleted immediately.
title: ClusterRoleBinding — deleteDelete

instruction: What are the Prometheus metrics exposed by the kube-apiserver for constrained impersonation?
response: The kube-apiserver exposes the following metrics: apiserver_impersonation_attempts_total, apiserver_impersonation_attempts_duration_seconds, apiserver_impersonation_authorization_attempts_total, and apiserver_impersonation_authorization_attempts_duration_seconds.
title: User Impersonation — Metrics

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'mode' label in the impersonation metrics?
response: The 'mode' label indicates the type of impersonation attempt, which can be one of associated-node, arbitrary-node, serviceaccount, user-info, or legacy.
title: User Impersonation — Metrics

instruction: How does the 'decision' label relate to the impersonation metrics?
response: The 'decision' label indicates whether the impersonation attempt was allowed or denied.
title: User Impersonation — Metrics

instruction: What type of load balancer is used for external load balancing in Kubernetes?
response: The load balancer is external to the Kubernetes cluster.
title: Create an External Load Balancer — External load balancer providers

instruction: How does Kubernetes automate the creation of the external load balancer?
response: The Kubernetes control plane automates the creation of the external load balancer, health checks (if needed), and packet filtering rules (if needed).
title: Create an External Load Balancer — External load balancer providers

instruction: What happens once the cloud provider allocates an IP address for the load balancer?
response: The control plane looks up that external IP address and populates it into the Service object.
title: Create an External Load Balancer — External load balancer providers

instruction: What are the security risks associated with using a hostPath volume?
response: Using a hostPath volume presents many security risks, including access to privileged system credentials and APIs, and the potential for container escape or to attack other parts of the cluster.
title: Volumes — hostPath

instruction: What are some alternative approaches to using a hostPath volume?
response: You can define a local PersistentVolume and use that instead. Additionally, if you are restricting access to specific directories on the node using admission-time validation, you can require that any mounts of that hostPath volume are read-only.
title: Volumes — hostPath

instruction: What are some valid values for the type field in a hostPath volume?
response: The available values for the type field are: "", "DirectoryOrCreate", "Directory", "FileOrCreate", "File", "Socket", "CharDevice", and "BlockDevice".
title: Volumes — hostPath

instruction: How does the kubelet handle the creation of directories and files for a hostPath volume?
response: If nothing exists at the given path, the kubelet creates an empty directory or file with the specified permissions and ownership.
title: Volumes — hostPath

instruction: What is the behavior if a file or directory created on the underlying host is only accessible by root?
response: You need to run your process as root in a privileged container or modify the file permissions on the host to read from or write to a hostPath volume.
title: Volumes — hostPath

instruction: How does the kubelet handle the creation of a directory or file if it already exists but is not a directory/file?
response: The Pod fails if the path already exists but is not a directory/file.
title: Volumes — hostPath

instruction: How can you specify CPU and memory resources for a Pod in Kubernetes?
response: You can specify them at both the pod level and the container level by including the resources field in the Pod spec and within the container's specification in the Pod's manifest.
title: Assign Pod-level CPU and memory resources — Create a pod with resource requests and limits at both pod-level and container-level

instruction: What happens when a container in a Pod has its own explicit resource requests and limits, and the Pod itself has defined resource requests and limits?
response: The container's request guarantees will be equal to the pod-level requests, and the container will inherit the resource constraints from the pod-level settings.
title: Assign Pod-level CPU and memory resources — Create a pod with resource requests and limits at both pod-level and container-level

instruction: What ensures that containers in a Pod cannot exceed the combined total of pod-level limits?
response: The pod-level limits, which prevent both containers together from using more resources than specified in the pod-level limits.
title: Assign Pod-level CPU and memory resources — Create a pod with resource requests and limits at both pod-level and container-level

instruction: What line should be added to a Service manifest to create an external load balancer?
response: The line 'type: LoadBalancer' should be added to the Service manifest.
title: Create an External Load Balancer — Create a Service from a manifest

instruction: How can you create a service with an external load balancer using the kubectl expose command?
response: You can create a service with an external load balancer using the kubectl expose command with the flag '--type=LoadBalancer'. For example, the command 'kubectl expose deployment example --port=8765 --target-port=9376 --name=example-service --type=LoadBalancer' creates a new Service using the same selectors as the referenced resource.
title: Create an External Load Balancer — Create a Service from a manifest

instruction: How do you grant minimum permissions to a service account using Kubernetes RBAC?
response: You create a role, which grants access, and then bind the role to your ServiceAccount.
title: Service Accounts — Grant permissions to a ServiceAccount

instruction: What is the principle of least privilege in service account permissions?
response: The principle of least privilege states that service account permissions should follow the minimum set of permissions required for a Pod to function correctly.
title: Service Accounts — Grant permissions to a ServiceAccount

instruction: Can you use RBAC to allow service accounts in one namespace to perform actions on resources in a different namespace?
response: Yes, you can use RBAC to allow service accounts in one namespace to perform actions on resources in a different namespace in the cluster.
title: Service Accounts — Grant permissions to a ServiceAccount

instruction: How do you allow a Pod in one namespace to see resources in another namespace using RBAC?
response: You create a Role object that grants permissions to the resources, then create a RoleBinding object in the namespace where the resources are located to bind the Role to the ServiceAccount object.
title: Service Accounts — Grant permissions to a ServiceAccount

instruction: What is the purpose of the `kubeadm init phase addon coredns` command?
response: The `kubeadm init phase addon coredns` command is used to install the CoreDNS addon components via the API server.

instruction: What is the condition for the CoreDNS server to be scheduled in the cluster?
response: The CoreDNS server will not be scheduled until CNI (Container Network Interface) is installed.

instruction: What is the default value for the `--image-repository` flag?
response: The default value for the `--image-repository` flag is `registry.k8s.io`.

instruction: What is user impersonation in Kubernetes?
response: User impersonation is a method of allowing authenticated users to act as another user, group, or service account through HTTP headers.
title: User Impersonation

instruction: What are the HTTP headers used for user impersonation?
response: The following HTTP headers can be used to perform an impersonation request: Impersonate-User, Impersonate-Uid, Impersonate-Group, and Impersonate-Extra-(extra name).
title: User Impersonation

instruction: What is the purpose of the impersonate verb in RBAC authorization?
response: The impersonate verb is used to allow a user to impersonate another user, group, or service account, and is required for impersonation requests.
title: User Impersonation

instruction: How do you configure impersonation headers using kubectl?
response: You can configure impersonation headers using the --as, --as-group, --as-uid, and --as-user-extra flags with kubectl.
title: User Impersonation

instruction: What is the difference between impersonating a user and impersonating a ServiceAccount?
response: Impersonating a user is not namespace scoped, while impersonating a ServiceAccount is namespace scoped, but the impersonated ServiceAccount could perform actions outside of the namespace.
title: User Impersonation

instruction: What is the ClusterRole required for user impersonation in RBAC authorization?
response: The ClusterRole required for user impersonation is the impersonator ClusterRole, which encompasses the rules needed to set user and group impersonation headers.
title: User Impersonation

instruction: What is required to secure the communication channel for the nginx server before exposing it to the internet?
response: Self-signed certificates for https and an nginx server configured to use the certificates, along with a secret that makes the certificates accessible to pods.
title: Connecting Applications with Services — Securing the Service

instruction: How can you create a secret for the certificates if you don't want to use the 'make' tool?
response: You can use the manual steps, which involve creating a public private key pair using OpenSSL, converting the keys to base64 encoding, and then creating a YAML file to create the secret.
title: Connecting Applications with Services — Securing the Service

instruction: How can you modify the nginx replicas to start an https server using the certificate in the secret, and the Service to expose both ports (80 and 443)?
response: You can modify the Deployment and Service specification in the same file to include the necessary configuration for the nginx server to serve HTTP traffic on port 80 and HTTPS traffic on 443, and to expose both ports through the Service.
title: Connecting Applications with Services — Securing the Service

instruction: What are the requirements for enabling recursive read-only mounts in a Pod?
response: The requirements are: readOnly is set to true, mountPropagation is unset or set to None, the host is running with Linux kernel v5.12 or later, and the CRI-level or OCI-level container runtime supports recursive read-only mounts.
title: Volumes — Recursive read-only mounts

instruction: What happens if any of the requirements for enabling recursive read-only mounts are not met?
response: The mount will fail if any of the requirements are not true.
title: Volumes — Recursive read-only mounts

instruction: How does the kubelet and kube-apiserver handle the recursiveReadOnly field if the feature is not supported by the kernel or the runtime class?
response: If the feature is not supported, the kubelet and kube-apiserver will fall back to Disabled if the value is set to IfPossible.
title: Volumes — Recursive read-only mounts

instruction: Which container runtimes are known to support recursive read-only mounts?
response: The following container runtimes are known to support recursive read-only mounts: CRI-level containerd (since v2.0), CRI-O (since v1.30), OCI-level runc (since v1.1), and crun (since v1.8.6).
title: Volumes — Recursive read-only mounts

instruction: What is the minimum Linux kernel version required for Memory QoS?
response: Linux with cgroup v2 is required, and kernel 5.9 or higher is recommended.
title: Pod Quality of Service Classes — System requirements

instruction: What happens to a Container that exceeds its resource limit?
response: The Container is killed and restarted by the kubelet without affecting other Containers in the Pod.
title: Pod Quality of Service Classes — System requirements

instruction: Can the kube-scheduler consider QoS class when selecting which Pods to preempt?
response: No, the kube-scheduler does not consider QoS class when selecting which Pods to preempt.
title: Pod Quality of Service Classes — System requirements

instruction: Can the QoS class of a Pod be changed after it is created?
response: No, if you later attempt an in-place resize that would result in a different QoS class, the resize is rejected by admission.
title: Pod Quality of Service Classes — System requirements

instruction: What is the purpose of the VolumeAttributesClass in Kubernetes?
response: The VolumeAttributesClass represents a specification of mutable volume attributes defined by the CSI driver, which can be specified during dynamic provisioning of PersistentVolumeClaims or changed in the PersistentVolumeClaim spec after provisioning.
title: VolumeAttributesClass

instruction: How are the parameters in a VolumeAttributesClass handled by Kubernetes?
response: The parameters in a VolumeAttributesClass are opaque to Kubernetes and are passed directly to the CSI driver. These parameters are immutable, but the underlying storage provider supports changing them on an existing volume by creating a new VolumeAttributesClass with new parameters and updating the PersistentVolumeClaim to reference the new class.
title: VolumeAttributesClass

instruction: What happens if the CSI driver rejects invalid parameters in a VolumeAttributesClass?
response: If the CSI driver rejects invalid parameters, the target PersistentVolumeClaim will be set to an 'Infeasible' state in the modifyVolumeStatus field.
title: VolumeAttributesClass

instruction: What is used to detect memory pressure on a Node?
