title: Validating Admission Policy — Matching requests:matchConditions

instruction: What happens if there is an error evaluating a match condition in a ValidatingAdmissionPolicy?
response: In the event of an error evaluating a match condition, the policy is not evaluated. Whether to reject the request is determined by the failurePolicy.
title: Validating Admission Policy — Matching requests:matchConditions

instruction: How can you create multiple Jobs from manifests using a single kubectl command?
response: You can use the command `kubectl create -f ./jobs` to create all Jobs from the manifests in the current directory.
title: Parallel Processing using Expansions — Create Jobs from the manifests

instruction: How can you filter the output of kubectl get jobs to show only a specific group of Jobs?
response: You can use the `-l` option with a label selector, for example, `kubectl get jobs -l jobgroup=jobexample`, to show only the Jobs that are part of the specified group.
title: Parallel Processing using Expansions — Create Jobs from the manifests

instruction: How can you check the output of all Jobs in a group at once?
response: You can use the command `kubectl logs -f -l jobgroup=jobexample` to check the output of all Jobs in the specified group at once.
title: Parallel Processing using Expansions — Create Jobs from the manifests

instruction: What is the first step to add a new Linux worker node to a cluster?
response: Connect to the machine by using SSH or another method.
title: Adding Linux worker nodes

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'dryRun' parameter in the PUT /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/roles/{name} request?
response: The 'dryRun' parameter indicates that modifications should not be persisted. An invalid or unrecognized 'dryRun' directive will result in an error response and no further processing of the request.
title: Role — putReplace

instruction: What are the valid values for the 'fieldValidation' parameter in the PUT /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/roles/{name} request?
response: The valid values for 'fieldValidation' are 'Ignore', 'Warn', and 'Strict'. 'Ignore' will silently drop unknown fields, 'Warn' will send a warning for unknown fields and duplicate fields, and 'Strict' will fail the request with a BadRequest error for unknown or duplicate fields.
title: Role — putReplace

instruction: What is the default behavior for the 'fieldValidation' parameter in Kubernetes versions prior to v1.23?
response: The default behavior for 'fieldValidation' in Kubernetes versions prior to v1.23 is to 'Ignore' any unknown fields that are silently dropped from the object.
title: Role — putReplace

instruction: How can you print the logs for a container in a pod or specified resource if the pod has only one container?
response: You can omit the container name, and kubectl will automatically use the first container in the pod.
title: kubectl logs — Synopsis

instruction: What is the purpose of the ingressclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class annotation on an IngressClass resource?
response: When this annotation is set to 'true' on an IngressClass resource, new Ingress resources without a specified class will be assigned this default class.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — ingressclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class

instruction: What is the effect of setting the kubernetes.io/cluster-service label on a Service to 'true'?
response: This label indicates that the Service provides a service to the cluster, and running kubectl cluster-info will query for Services with this label set to true.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — ingressclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class

instruction: Is it recommended to set the kubernetes.io/cluster-service label on any Service?
response: No, setting this label on any Service is deprecated.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — ingressclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class

instruction: Where can I find more information about Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) in Kubernetes?
response: You can find more information about RBAC in the RBAC documentation.
title: Role Based Access Control Good Practices — What's next

instruction: What is the recommended platform for asking specific, answerable questions about Kubernetes?
response: The recommended platform for asking specific, answerable questions about Kubernetes is Stack Overflow.
title: Role Based Access Control Good Practices — What's next

instruction: How can I report a problem or suggest an improvement for the Kubernetes documentation?
response: You can report a problem or suggest an improvement by opening an issue in the GitHub Repository.
title: Role Based Access Control Good Practices — What's next

instruction: What is the effect of setting topologyManagerScope to pod in the kubelet configuration file?
response: Setting topologyManagerScope to pod allows the Topology Manager to group all containers in a pod to a common set of NUMA nodes, treating the pod as a whole and attempting to allocate the entire pod to either a single NUMA node or a common set of NUMA nodes.
title: Control Topology Management Policies on a node — podscope

instruction: What is the benefit of using the pod scope in tandem with single-numa-node Topology Manager policy?
response: Using the pod scope with single-numa-node policy allows all containers in a pod to be placed onto a single NUMA node, eliminating inter-NUMA communication overhead for that pod, which is particularly valuable for latency-sensitive workloads or high-throughput applications that perform IPC.
title: Control Topology Management Policies on a node — podscope

instruction: What happens when a pod is submitted with single-numa-node policy and a set of NUMA nodes is not present among possible allocations?
response: In this case, the pod is rejected because the Topology Manager policy requires a suitable set of NUMA nodes to satisfy the allocation, and the pod is not admitted.
title: Control Topology Management Policies on a node — podscope

instruction: How can you disable native histograms for Prometheus 3.x without restarting Kubernetes?
response: You can disable native histograms by setting `scrape_native_histograms: false` per scrape job in Prometheus.
title: Native Histogram Support for Kubernetes Metrics — Disabling native histograms

instruction: What happens to the metrics endpoint when native histograms are disabled?
response: When native histograms are disabled, the metrics endpoint reverts to classic histogram format only.
title: Native Histogram Support for Kubernetes Metrics — Disabling native histograms

instruction: What happens to historical native histogram data in Prometheus when native histograms are disabled?
response: Historical native histogram data in Prometheus remains queryable.
title: Native Histogram Support for Kubernetes Metrics — Disabling native histograms

instruction: How can you disable native histograms using the Kubernetes feature gate?
response: You can disable native histograms by restarting the component with `--feature-gates=NativeHistograms=false`.
title: Native Histogram Support for Kubernetes Metrics — Disabling native histograms

instruction: What is the purpose of the patches directory in Kubeadm?
response: The patches directory is used to pass a directory with patch files to InitConfiguration, JoinConfiguration, and UpgradeConfiguration on individual nodes. These patches can be used as the last customization step before component configuration is written to disk.
title: Customizing components with the kubeadm API — Etcd flags

instruction: What is the correct format for a patch file in the patches directory?
response: The correct format for a patch file is target[suffix][+patchtype].extension, where target can be one of kube-apiserver, kube-controller-manager, kube-scheduler, etcd, kubeletconfiguration, and corednsdeployment, suffix is an optional string, patchtype can be one of strategic, merge, or json, and extension must be either json or yaml.
title: Customizing components with the kubeadm API — Etcd flags

instruction: How do you specify the patches directory when running kubeadm init or kubeadm join?
response: You can specify the patches directory by passing the --config <YOUR CONFIG YAML> flag with the directory path, for example, apiVersion: kubeadm.k8s.io/v1beta4, kind: InitConfiguration, patches: directory: /home/user/somedir.
title: Customizing components with the kubeadm API — Etcd flags

instruction: What is the API version and kind of the object sent in the request body when the API Server makes an authorization decision?
response: The API version is 'authorization.k8s.io/v1beta1' and the kind is 'SubjectAccessReview'.
title: Webhook Mode — Request Payloads

instruction: How does the API Server indicate that the authorization webhook should not allow, or has 'no opinion' about the request, but other authorizers are given a chance to allow the request?
response: The API Server returns a response with 'allowed' set to 'false' and omits the 'denied' field, along with a reason for the denial.
title: Webhook Mode — Request Payloads

instruction: What are some examples of non-resource paths that are sent as SubjectAccessReview objects with nonResourceAttributes?
response: Examples of non-resource paths include '/debug', '/metrics', '/logs', '/healthz', '/livez', '/openapi/v2', '/readyz', and '/version'.
title: Webhook Mode — Request Payloads

instruction: How should authorization webhooks handle label and field selectors in the request?
response: Authorization webhooks should interpret and handle label and field selectors according to the guidelines in the SubjectAccessReview API documentation, specifically using the parsed requirements rather than the raw selector strings, and handling unrecognized operators safely.
title: Webhook Mode — Request Payloads

instruction: What happens to a static pod running on a node under resource pressure?
response: The kubelet may evict the static pod and then tries to create a replacement.
title: Node-pressure Eviction — Self healing for static pods

instruction: How does the kubelet prioritize replacement of a static pod that was evicted due to resource pressure?
response: The kubelet takes the priority of the static pod into account when creating a replacement.
title: Node-pressure Eviction — Self healing for static pods

instruction: Can a static pod with low priority be replaced on a node under resource pressure if there are higher-priority pods in the cluster?
response: If the static pod manifest specifies a low priority and there are higher-priority Pods defined within the cluster's control plane, the kubelet may not be able to make room for that static pod.
title: Node-pressure Eviction — Self healing for static pods

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/watch/clusterrolebindings request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: ClusterRoleBinding — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/watch/clusterrolebindings request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is server-defined and clients may only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: ClusterRoleBinding — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/watch/clusterrolebindings request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event. The bookmark will report the ResourceVersion corresponding to the set of objects.
title: ClusterRoleBinding — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `resourceVersionMatch` field interact with `resourceVersion` in list calls?
response: It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set. The `resourceVersionMatch` determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls.
title: ClusterRoleBinding — getWatch List

instruction: What is the path and port used by the liveness probe in the provided configuration file?
response: The liveness probe uses the path '/healthz' and port 8080.
title: Configure Liveness, Readiness and Startup Probes — Define a liveness HTTP request

instruction: How often does the kubelet perform a liveness probe and what is the initial delay?
response: The kubelet performs a liveness probe every 3 seconds, as specified by the periodSeconds field, and waits 3 seconds before performing the first probe, as specified by the initialDelaySeconds field.
title: Configure Liveness, Readiness and Startup Probes — Define a liveness HTTP request

instruction: What HTTP status codes indicate success for the liveness probe?
response: Any HTTP status code greater than or equal to 200 and less than 400 indicates success for the liveness probe.
title: Configure Liveness, Readiness and Startup Probes — Define a liveness HTTP request

instruction: What happens to the container if the liveness probe fails?
response: If the liveness probe fails, the kubelet kills the container and restarts it.
title: Configure Liveness, Readiness and Startup Probes — Define a liveness HTTP request

instruction: How can I verify if a Deployment rollout is stuck?
response: You can verify if a Deployment rollout is stuck by checking the rollout status using the command 'kubectl rollout status deployment/<deployment-name>'.
title: Deployments — Rolling Back a Deployment

instruction: What happens to a Deployment rollout if a Pod created by the new ReplicaSet is stuck in an image pull loop?
response: The Deployment controller stops the bad rollout automatically and stops scaling up the new ReplicaSet, depending on the rollingUpdate parameters (maxUnavailable specifically) that you have specified.
title: Deployments — Rolling Back a Deployment

instruction: How can I rollback to a previous revision of a Deployment that is stable?
response: To rollback to a previous revision of a Deployment that is stable, you need to use the Deployment's rollout history, which is kept in the system by default. You can change this by modifying the revision history limit.
title: Deployments — Rolling Back a Deployment

instruction: What information can I find in the Deployment description?
response: In the Deployment description, you can find information such as the Deployment's name, namespace, creation timestamp, labels, selector, replicas, strategy type, rolling update strategy, and events.
title: Deployments — Rolling Back a Deployment

instruction: What is the purpose of a startup probe in a Kubernetes pod?
response: A startup probe verifies whether the application within a container is started, allowing it time to finish its initialization before liveness or readiness probes are executed.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Startup probe

instruction: What happens if a startup probe fails in a Kubernetes pod?
response: If the startup probe fails, the kubelet kills the container, and the container is subjected to its restart policy.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Startup probe

instruction: When are liveness and readiness probes executed in relation to a startup probe?
response: Kubernetes does not execute liveness or readiness probes until the startup probe succeeds.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Startup probe

instruction: What is the appropriate RestartPolicy for Pods in a StatefulSet?
response: The only appropriate RestartPolicy for Pods in a StatefulSet is Always, and this is the default value. You should never override the default policy for stateful applications.
title: Running ZooKeeper, A Distributed System Coordinator — Handling process failure

instruction: What happens when the ZooKeeper process in a Pod is terminated, and what is the effect of the RestartPolicy being set to Always?
response: When the ZooKeeper process in a Pod is terminated, its parent process also terminates. Because the RestartPolicy of the container is Always, Kubernetes restarts the parent process, which in turn restarts the ZooKeeper process.
title: Running ZooKeeper, A Distributed System Coordinator — Handling process failure

instruction: Why must a script used to launch an application's business logic process terminate with the child process?
response: The script must terminate with the child process to ensure that Kubernetes will restart the application's container when the process implementing the application's business logic fails.
title: Running ZooKeeper, A Distributed System Coordinator — Handling process failure

instruction: How can you use a ConfigMap to configure a container inside a Pod?
response: There are four different ways to use a ConfigMap to configure a container inside a Pod: 1) inside a container command and args, 2) environment variables for a container, 3) add a file in read-only volume, and 4) write code to run inside the Pod that uses the Kubernetes API to read a ConfigMap.
title: ConfigMaps — ConfigMaps and Pods

instruction: What happens when you mount a ConfigMap as a volume in a Pod, and how do you control which keys become files?
response: When you mount a ConfigMap as a volume in a Pod, the keys in the ConfigMap become files. You can control which keys become files by specifying an 'items' array in the 'volumes' section of the Pod definition. If you omit the 'items' array, every key in the ConfigMap becomes a file with the same name as the key.
title: ConfigMaps — ConfigMaps and Pods

instruction: Can you access a ConfigMap from a Pod in a different namespace?
response: No, by default, a Pod and the ConfigMap it references must be in the same namespace. However, you can write code to read a ConfigMap using the Kubernetes API, which allows you to access a ConfigMap in a different namespace and also subscribe to updates when the ConfigMap changes.
title: ConfigMaps — ConfigMaps and Pods

instruction: What is the purpose of the SelfSubjectReview API in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The SelfSubjectReview API allows a client to find out how the Kubernetes cluster maps its authentication information to identify the client, whether it is authenticating as a user or a ServiceAccount.
title: Authenticating — API access to authentication information for a client

instruction: Who can create SelfSubjectReviews in a typical Kubernetes cluster?
response: In a typical Kubernetes cluster, all authenticated users can create SelfSubjectReviews, and this access is allowed by the built-in system:basic-user ClusterRole.
title: Authenticating — API access to authentication information for a client

instruction: What are some scenarios where the ability to learn a client's own identity is useful?
response: The ability for a client to learn its own identity is extremely useful when troubleshooting a complicated authentication flow, such as when using webhook token authentication or an authenticating proxy.
title: Authenticating — API access to authentication information for a client

instruction: What is the purpose of the `pretty` parameter in the CSIDriver delete collection request?
response: The `pretty` parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If set to `true`, the output will be pretty-printed. Defaults to `false` unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: CSIDriver — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: How does the `continue` parameter work in the CSIDriver delete collection request?
response: The `continue` parameter should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is a server-defined value that clients may only use with identical query parameters (except for the value of continue). If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: CSIDriver — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting `dryRun` to `All` in the CSIDriver delete collection request?
response: When `dryRun` is set to `All`, all dry run stages will be processed. This indicates that modifications should not be persisted.
title: CSIDriver — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the `propagationPolicy` parameter in the CSIDriver delete collection request?
response: The `propagationPolicy` parameter determines whether and how garbage collection will be performed. Acceptable values are: 'Orphan', 'Background', and 'Foreground'. This field or `OrphanDependents` may be set, but not both.
title: CSIDriver — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: How can I manage ServiceAccounts as a cluster administrator?
response: You can learn how to manage your ServiceAccounts as a cluster administrator by following the relevant Kubernetes documentation.
title: Service Accounts — What's next

instruction: How do I assign a ServiceAccount to a Pod?
response: You can learn how to assign a ServiceAccount to a Pod by following the relevant Kubernetes documentation.
title: Service Accounts — What's next

response: You can find detailed information about the ServiceAccount API by reading the ServiceAccount API reference.
title: Service Accounts — What's next

instruction: How do I update the Kubernetes package repository to access packages for a new minor version on Ubuntu, Debian, or HypriotOS?
response: Open the file /etc/apt/sources.list.d/kubernetes.list in a text editor, change the version in the URL to the next available minor release, save the file, and exit the text editor.
title: Changing The Kubernetes Package Repository — Switching to another Kubernetes package repository

instruction: How do I update the Kubernetes package repository to access packages for a new minor version on CentOS, RHEL, or Fedora?
response: Open the file /etc/yum.repos.d/kubernetes.repo in a text editor, change the version in the baseurl and gpgkey URLs to the next available minor release, save the file, and exit the text editor.
title: Changing The Kubernetes Package Repository — Switching to another Kubernetes package repository

instruction: How does the cluster-proportional-autoscaler application obtain the number of nodes and cores in the cluster?
response: The autoscaler Pod runs a client that polls the Kubernetes API server for the number of nodes and cores in the cluster.
title: Autoscale the DNS Service in a Cluster — Understanding how DNS horizontal autoscaling works

instruction: How are scaling parameters provided to the autoscaler and how often are they refreshed?
response: The scaling parameters and data points are provided via a ConfigMap to the autoscaler, and it refreshes its parameters table every poll interval to be up to date with the latest desired scaling parameters.
title: Autoscale the DNS Service in a Cluster — Understanding how DNS horizontal autoscaling works

instruction: What are the two control patterns supported by the autoscaler?
response: The autoscaler provides a controller interface to support two control patterns: linear and ladder.
title: Autoscale the DNS Service in a Cluster — Understanding how DNS horizontal autoscaling works

instruction: Can changes to the scaling parameters be made without rebuilding or restarting the autoscaler Pod?
response: Yes, changes to the scaling parameters are allowed without rebuilding or restarting the autoscaler Pod.
title: Autoscale the DNS Service in a Cluster — Understanding how DNS horizontal autoscaling works

instruction: Why is using a static token file for loading credentials not recommended for production Kubernetes servers?
response: Using a static token file for loading credentials is not recommended for production Kubernetes servers because credentials are stored in clear text on control plane node disks, which can be a security risk. Additionally, changing credentials requires a restart of the API server process, which can impact availability, and there is no mechanism for users to rotate their credentials or prevent brute-force attacks.
title: Hardening Guide - Authentication Mechanisms — Static token file

instruction: What are the implications of changing a credential stored in a static token file?
response: Changing any credential stored in a static token file requires a restart of the API server process to take effect, which can impact availability.
title: Hardening Guide - Authentication Mechanisms — Static token file

instruction: How must a cluster administrator rotate a credential stored in a static token file?
response: To rotate a credential stored in a static token file, a cluster administrator must modify the token on disk and distribute it to the users.
title: Hardening Guide - Authentication Mechanisms — Static token file

instruction: What is the purpose of the `pretty` parameter in the GET /apis/scheduling.k8s.io/v1alpha2/namespaces/{namespace}/workloads endpoint?
response: The `pretty` parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If set to 'true', the output will be pretty-printed. Defaults to 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: Workload — getList

instruction: How does the `continue` parameter work when retrieving more results from the server?
response: The `continue` option should be set when retrieving more results from the server. The value is server-defined, and clients may only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: Workload — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersion` and `resourceVersionMatch` parameters in the GET /apis/scheduling.k8s.io/v1alpha2/namespaces/{namespace}/workloads endpoint?
response: The `resourceVersion` parameter sets a constraint on what resource versions a request may be served from. The `resourceVersionMatch` parameter determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: Workload — getList

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /apis/scheduling.k8s.io/v1alpha2/namespaces/{namespace}/workloads endpoint?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` are set, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent, reporting the ResourceVersion (RV) corresponding to the set of objects.
title: Workload — getList

instruction: What is the default behavior when deploying a Service of type: LoadBalancer on AWS without setting the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type annotation?
response: The AWS integration deploys a classic Elastic Load Balancer.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type (beta)

instruction: What happens when setting the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type annotation to 'external' on a Service of type: LoadBalancer?
response: The AWS Load Balancer controller attempts to deploy a load balancer based on the Service specification, provided that the cluster has a working deployment of the AWS Load Balancer controller.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type (beta)

instruction: What are the permitted values for the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type annotation?
response: There are two permitted values: 'external' and the default (no annotation present), which deploys a classic Elastic Load Balancer.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type (beta)

instruction: What is the purpose of the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-internal annotation in a Kubernetes Service?
response: The service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-internal annotation is used to configure an internal load balancer when integrated with AWS elastic load balancing. When set to "true", it configures the load balancer as internal.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-internal (beta)

instruction: How does the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-internal annotation interact with the AWS load balancer controller?
response: If you are using the AWS load balancer controller, you should refer to the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-scheme annotation instead of service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-internal.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-internal (beta)

instruction: What does the adminAccess field in DeviceRequestAllocationResult indicate?
response: AdminAccess indicates that this device was allocated for administrative access. Admin access is disabled if this field is unset or set to false, otherwise it is enabled.
title: ResourceClaim — DeviceRequestAllocationResult

instruction: What is the purpose of the consumedCapacity field in DeviceRequestAllocationResult?
response: ConsumedCapacity tracks the amount of capacity consumed per device as part of the claim request. The consumed amount may differ from the requested amount: it is rounded up to the nearest valid value based on the device's requestPolicy if applicable.
title: ResourceClaim — DeviceRequestAllocationResult

instruction: What is the significance of the ShareID field in DeviceRequestAllocationResult?
response: ShareID uniquely identifies an individual allocation share of the device, used when the device supports multiple simultaneous allocations. It serves as an additional map key to differentiate concurrent shares of the same device.
title: ResourceClaim — DeviceRequestAllocationResult

instruction: What conditions are copied from the ResourceSlice to the BindingConditions and BindingFailureConditions fields in DeviceRequestAllocationResult?
response: BindingConditions and BindingFailureConditions contain a copy of the BindingConditions and BindingFailureConditions from the corresponding ResourceSlice at the time of allocation, respectively. These are beta fields and require enabling the DRADeviceBindingConditions and DRAResourceClaimDeviceStatus feature gates.
title: ResourceClaim — DeviceRequestAllocationResult

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/watch/resourceclaimtemplates request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/watch/resourceclaimtemplates request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is server-defined and clients may only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: ResourceClaimTemplate — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/watch/resourceclaimtemplates request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event.
title: ResourceClaimTemplate — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersionMatch` field in the GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/watch/resourceclaimtemplates request?
response: The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: ResourceClaimTemplate — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: How can I schedule GPU resources in Kubernetes?
response: You can schedule GPU resources using device plugins.
title: Device Plugins — What's next

instruction: What is the Topology Manager in Kubernetes?
response: The Topology Manager is a feature in Kubernetes, details about which can be found in the related documentation.
title: Device Plugins — What's next

instruction: How can I use hardware acceleration for TLS ingress with Kubernetes?
response: You can use hardware acceleration for TLS ingress with Kubernetes by leveraging specific plugins or tools that provide this functionality.
title: Device Plugins — What's next

instruction: What is Extended Resource allocation by DRA in Kubernetes?
response: Extended Resource allocation by DRA refers to a feature or mechanism in Kubernetes that allows for the allocation of extended resources, more information can be found in the related documentation.
title: Device Plugins — What's next

instruction: What fine-grained authorization checks does Kubelet perform before falling back to the proxy subresource?
response: Kubelet performs a fine-grained check before falling back to the proxy subresource for the /pods, /runningPods, /configz, and /healthz endpoints.
title: Kubelet authentication/authorization — Fine-grained authorization

instruction: What are the required verb, resource, and subresource attributes for a user to be authorized when the KubeletFineGrainedAuthz feature-gate is enabled?
response: When the feature-gate KubeletFineGrainedAuthz is enabled, the user must be authorized for the following attributes: verb=*, resource=nodes, subresource=proxy; verb=*, resource=nodes, subresource=stats; verb=*, resource=nodes, subresource=log; verb=*, resource=nodes, subresource=metrics; verb=*, resource=nodes, subresource=configz; verb=*, resource=nodes, subresource=healthz; verb=*, resource=nodes, subresource=pods.
title: Kubelet authentication/authorization — Fine-grained authorization

instruction: What happens to the builtin system:kubelet-api-admin ClusterRole when the KubeletFineGrainedAuthz feature-gate is enabled and RBAC authorization is used?
response: Enabling the KubeletFineGrainedAuthz feature-gate and using RBAC authorization ensures that the builtin system:kubelet-api-admin ClusterRole is updated with permissions to access all the mentioned subresources.
title: Kubelet authentication/authorization — Fine-grained authorization

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'automountServiceAccountToken' field in a ServiceAccount?
response: The 'automountServiceAccountToken' field indicates whether pods running as this service account should have an API token automatically mounted. This setting can be overridden at the pod level.
title: ServiceAccount

instruction: What is the difference between ImagePullSecrets and Secrets in a ServiceAccount?
response: ImagePullSecrets are references to secrets used for pulling images in pods, and are only accessed by the kubelet. Secrets, on the other hand, can be mounted in the pod. ImagePullSecrets are distinct from Secrets for this reason.
title: ServiceAccount

instruction: How can you request an API token for a ServiceAccount to use outside of a pod?
response: You can request a token directly using the TokenRequest API, or manually create a service account token secret. The 'secrets' field in a ServiceAccount should not be used to find auto-generated service account token secrets for use outside of pods.
title: ServiceAccount

instruction: What is the default reclaim policy for dynamically provisioned PersistentVolumes?
response: The default reclaim policy for dynamically provisioned PersistentVolumes is 'Delete'.
title: Change the Reclaim Policy of a PersistentVolume — Why change reclaim policy of a PersistentVolume

instruction: What happens to a PersistentVolume with a 'Delete' reclaim policy when the corresponding PersistentVolumeClaim is deleted?
response: When a PersistentVolumeClaim is deleted, the corresponding PersistentVolume with a 'Delete' reclaim policy is automatically deleted.
title: Change the Reclaim Policy of a PersistentVolume — Why change reclaim policy of a PersistentVolume

instruction: What reclaim policy should be used if a PersistentVolume contains precious data?
response: The 'Retain' policy should be used if a PersistentVolume contains precious data. With this policy, if a user deletes a PersistentVolumeClaim, the corresponding PersistentVolume will not be deleted but moved to the Released phase, allowing for manual data recovery.
title: Change the Reclaim Policy of a PersistentVolume — Why change reclaim policy of a PersistentVolume

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /apis/storage.k8s.io/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/csistoragecapacities/{name} request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /apis/storage.k8s.io/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/csistoragecapacities/{name} request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is a server-defined value that clients may only use with identical query parameters. If the continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /apis/storage.k8s.io/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/csistoragecapacities/{name} request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event.
title: CSIStorageCapacity — getWatch

instruction: How do you enable a feature gate for a Kubernetes component?
response: To enable a feature gate for a Kubernetes component, use the --feature-gates flag followed by the feature gate name and set its value to true. For example, kube-apiserver --feature-gates=FeatureName1=true.
title: Feature Gates — How to enable Feature Gates

instruction: How can you list available feature gates for a specific Kubernetes component?
response: You can list available feature gates for a specific Kubernetes component by running the command <component> -h.
title: Feature Gates — How to enable Feature Gates

instruction: What is the format for specifying feature gates using the --feature-gates flag?
response: The --feature-gates flag accepts a comma-separated list of key=value pairs, where each key is a feature gate name and each value is either true (enable) or false (disable).
title: Feature Gates — How to enable Feature Gates

instruction: What are the valid values for the cacheKeyType field in CredentialProviderResponse?
response: The valid values for the cacheKeyType field are Image, Registry, and Global.
title: Kubelet CredentialProvider (v1) — CredentialProviderResponse

instruction: How does the kubelet match images against the keys in the auth map?
response: The kubelet matches images against the keys in the auth map when both contain the same number of domain parts and each part matches, the URL path of an imageMatch is a prefix of the target image URL path, and if the imageMatch contains a port, then the port must match in the image as well.
title: Kubelet CredentialProvider (v1) — CredentialProviderResponse

instruction: What is the effect of setting cacheDuration to 0 in CredentialProviderResponse?
response: If cacheDuration is set to 0, the kubelet will not cache the provided AuthConfig.
title: Kubelet CredentialProvider (v1) — CredentialProviderResponse

instruction: How are keys in the auth map ordered when multiple keys are returned?
response: When multiple keys are returned, the kubelet will traverse all keys in reverse order so that longer keys come before shorter keys with the same prefix, and non-wildcard keys come before wildcard keys with the same prefix.
title: Kubelet CredentialProvider (v1) — CredentialProviderResponse

instruction: What is the primary responsibility of the ReplicationController?
response: The ReplicationController ensures that the desired number of pods matches its label selector and are operational, currently excluding only terminated pods from its count.
title: ReplicationController — Responsibilities of the ReplicationController

instruction: Will the ReplicationController perform readiness or liveness probes?
response: No, the ReplicationController will not perform readiness nor liveness probes. These responsibilities are intended to be handled elsewhere.
title: ReplicationController — Responsibilities of the ReplicationController

instruction: Is the ReplicationController intended to handle auto-scaling?
response: No, the ReplicationController is not intended to handle auto-scaling. Instead, it is designed to be controlled by an external auto-scaler, which would modify its 'replicas' field.
title: ReplicationController — Responsibilities of the ReplicationController

instruction: How can I verify and extract private claims from a token in Kubernetes?
response: You can use the TokenReview API to verify and extract private claims from a token. First, create a token bound to a Pod using `kubectl create token`. Then, create a TokenReview resource with the token and submit it to the apiserver for review using `kubectl create -o yaml`.
title: Managing Service Accounts — Verifying and inspecting private claims

instruction: What is the purpose of the TokenReview API in Kubernetes?
response: The TokenReview API is used to verify and expand the embedded claims of a token. It allows clients to present a token to the kube-apiserver for verification, ensuring that the token's bound claims are still valid.
title: Managing Service Accounts — Verifying and inspecting private claims

instruction: How can I inspect a JWT token in Kubernetes?
response: You can inspect a JWT token using standard JWT decoding tools. The token contains Kubernetes-specific claims, such as namespace, node, pod, and serviceaccount information, which can be decoded and verified.
title: Managing Service Accounts — Verifying and inspecting private claims

instruction: What is the difference between verifying a token using the TokenReview API and offline validation of JWTs?
response: When verifying a token using the TokenReview API, the kube-apiserver checks the token's bound claims and ensures they are still valid. In contrast, offline validation of JWTs using a compliant JWT validator does not verify the claims' current validity, and the token may still be considered valid even if the bound object no longer exists.
title: Managing Service Accounts — Verifying and inspecting private claims

instruction: What happens to the ownership of projected files in a Linux pod with a projected volume and RunAsUser set in the Pod SecurityContext?
response: The projected files have the correct ownership set, including container user ownership.
title: Projected Volumes — SecurityContext interactions

instruction: Under what condition does the kubelet ensure that the contents of the serviceAccountToken volume are owned by a specific user and the token file has its permission mode set to 0600?
response: When all containers in a pod have the same runAsUser set in their PodSecurityContext or container SecurityContext.
title: Projected Volumes — SecurityContext interactions

instruction: Can ephemeral containers added to a Pod after it is created change volume permissions that were set when the pod was created?
response: No, ephemeral containers added to a Pod after it is created do not change volume permissions that were set when the pod was created.
title: Projected Volumes — SecurityContext interactions

instruction: What requirement must ephemeral containers meet to read the serviceAccountToken volume when its permissions were set to 0600 due to uniform runAsUser across other containers in the Pod?
response: Ephemeral containers must use the same runAsUser as the other containers in the Pod to be able to read the token.
title: Projected Volumes — SecurityContext interactions

instruction: How do you create a cluster role with an aggregation rule?
response: You can create a cluster role with an aggregation rule using the --aggregation-rule flag. For example, to create a cluster role named 'monitoring' with an aggregation rule, you can use the command: kubectl create clusterrole monitoring --aggregation-rule="rbac.example.com/aggregate-to-monitoring=true"
title: GETTING STARTED — clusterrole

instruction: At what level is Pressure Stall Information (PSI) collected in Kubernetes?
response: PSI information is collected at node, pod, and container levels.
title: Node metrics data — Pressure Stall Information (PSI)

instruction: Can the KubeletPSI feature gate be disabled in Kubernetes version 1.36 and later?
response: No, starting with Kubernetes v1.36, the KubeletPSI feature gate is locked to true and cannot be disabled.
title: Node metrics data — Pressure Stall Information (PSI)

instruction: Where can I find detailed information on how to interpret PSI metrics in Kubernetes?
response: You can learn how to interpret the PSI metrics in 'Understand PSI Metrics'.
title: Node metrics data — Pressure Stall Information (PSI)

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in a GET request to `/apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/resourceclaims`?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers may ignore this flag if they do not implement bookmarks. Clients should not assume bookmarks are returned at any specific interval.
title: ResourceClaim — getList All Namespaces

instruction: How does the `continue` field work when retrieving more results from the server?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is server-defined and clients may only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters. If the continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: ResourceClaim — getList All Namespaces

instruction: What is the effect of setting `limit` in a list call to `/apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/resourceclaims`?
response: Setting a limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items. If more items exist, the server will set the `continue` field on the list metadata to a value that can be used to retrieve the next set of results. The server guarantees that the objects returned when using continue will be identical to issuing a single list call without a limit.
title: ResourceClaim — getList All Namespaces

instruction: How does the `resourceVersion` and `resourceVersionMatch` fields interact in a list call?
response: The `resourceVersion` field sets a constraint on what resource versions a request may be served from. The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: ResourceClaim — getList All Namespaces

instruction: What types of ephemeral volumes are managed by kubelet on each node?
response: emptyDir, configMap, downwardAPI, and secret are provided as local ephemeral storage and are managed by kubelet on each node.
title: Ephemeral Volumes — Types of ephemeral volumes

instruction: What is the primary difference between CSI ephemeral volumes and generic ephemeral volumes in terms of providers?
response: CSI ephemeral volumes must be provided by third-party CSI storage drivers, while generic ephemeral volumes can be provided by third-party CSI storage drivers or any other storage driver that supports dynamic provisioning.
title: Ephemeral Volumes — Types of ephemeral volumes

instruction: What advantage do third-party drivers offer for ephemeral volumes?
response: Third-party drivers can offer functionality that Kubernetes itself does not support, such as storage with different performance characteristics or injecting different data.
title: Ephemeral Volumes — Types of ephemeral volumes

instruction: How do you create a Pod that runs two Containers which share a Volume to communicate?
response: You create a Pod with a Volume, and then mount that Volume to different paths in each Container. The configuration file for the Pod should include the Volume and the Containers with their respective volume mounts.
title: Communicate Between Containers in the Same Pod Using a Shared Volume — Creating a Pod that runs two Containers

instruction: What command does the debian Container run, and what does it do?
response: The debian Container runs the command 'echo Hello from the debian container > /pod-data/index.html' which creates an index.html file in the /pod-data directory. This directory is the mount path for the shared Volume in the debian Container.
title: Communicate Between Containers in the Same Pod Using a Shared Volume — Creating a Pod that runs two Containers

instruction: How can you verify that the nginx server is serving the web page written by the debian Container?
response: You can get a shell to the nginx Container using 'kubectl exec', then use 'curl' to send a GET request to the nginx server. The output should show the web page written by the debian Container, which in this case is 'Hello from the debian container'.
title: Communicate Between Containers in the Same Pod Using a Shared Volume — Creating a Pod that runs two Containers

instruction: What is the initial status of a certificate signing request from a kubelet on a node?
response: The initial status of a certificate signing request from a kubelet on a node is 'Pending'.
title: Configure Certificate Rotation for the Kubelet — Understanding the certificate rotation configuration

instruction: How does the kubelet obtain a signed certificate from the Kubernetes API?
response: The kubelet uses its initial certificate to connect to the Kubernetes API and issue a certificate signing request. The controller manager auto-approves the request, signs a certificate, and attaches it to the request. The kubelet then retrieves the signed certificate from the Kubernetes API and writes it to disk.
title: Configure Certificate Rotation for the Kubelet — Understanding the certificate rotation configuration

instruction: When does the kubelet automatically issue a new certificate signing request?
response: The kubelet automatically issues a new certificate signing request at any point between 30% and 10% of the time remaining on the certificate, as its expiration approaches.
title: Configure Certificate Rotation for the Kubelet — Understanding the certificate rotation configuration

instruction: How can I check the status of dynamically allocated resources in Kubernetes?
response: You can check the status of dynamically allocated resources by using kubelet device metrics, ResourceClaim status, or Device health monitoring.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — Observability of dynamic resources

instruction: What information does the DynamicResource message provide?
response: The DynamicResource message provides information specific to dynamic resource allocation, such as the device name and the claim name.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — Observability of dynamic resources

instruction: How can I monitor in-use devices in Kubernetes?
response: The PodResourcesLister kubelet gRPC service lets you monitor in-use devices.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — Observability of dynamic resources

instruction: What is the role of the xtrabackup sidecar in a MySQL Pod?
response: The xtrabackup sidecar looks at the cloned data files and determines if it's necessary to initialize MySQL replication on the replica. If so, it waits for mysqld to be ready and then executes the CHANGE MASTER TO and START SLAVE commands with replication parameters extracted from the XtraBackup clone files.
title: Run a Replicated Stateful Application — Starting replication

instruction: How do replicas find the primary MySQL server after starting replication?
response: Replicas look for the primary server at its stable DNS name (mysql-0.mysql), which allows them to automatically find the primary server even if it gets a new Pod IP due to being rescheduled.
title: Run a Replicated Stateful Application — Starting replication

instruction: What happens to the xtrabackup container after starting replication?
response: After starting replication, the xtrabackup container listens for connections from other Pods requesting a data clone. This server remains up indefinitely in case the StatefulSet scales up, or in case the next Pod loses its PersistentVolumeClaim and needs to redo the clone.
title: Run a Replicated Stateful Application — Starting replication

instruction: How is the class of a Persistent Volume (PV) specified?
response: The class of a PV is specified by setting the storageClassName attribute to the name of a StorageClass.
title: Persistent Volumes — Class

instruction: Can a PV with a specific class be bound to a PVC that requests no particular class?
response: No, a PV of a particular class can only be bound to PVCs requesting that class.
title: Persistent Volumes — Class

instruction: What happens to a PV with no storageClassName attribute?
response: A PV with no storageClassName has no class and can only be bound to PVCs that request no particular class.
title: Persistent Volumes — Class

instruction: Is the annotation volume.beta.kubernetes.io/storage-class still supported?
response: Yes, the annotation volume.beta.kubernetes.io/storage-class is still working, but it will become fully deprecated in a future Kubernetes release.
title: Persistent Volumes — Class

instruction: What is the primary risk associated with static Pods in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: An attacker with write access to the location where static Pod manifests are stored or fetched from could modify the configuration of static pods or introduce new static Pods.
title: Kubernetes API Server Bypass Risks — Static Pods

instruction: Can static Pods access other objects in the Kubernetes API?
response: No, static Pods are restricted from accessing other objects in the Kubernetes API, such as mounting Secrets from the cluster.
title: Kubernetes API Server Bypass Risks — Static Pods

instruction: What happens to a static Pod if it fails admission control?
response: If a static Pod fails admission control, the kubelet won't register the Pod with the API server, but the Pod still runs on the node.
title: Kubernetes API Server Bypass Risks — Static Pods

instruction: Are static Pods visible in the Kubernetes API by default?
response: By default, the kubelet creates a mirror pod so that static Pods are visible in the Kubernetes API, but an invalid namespace name can make them invisible.
title: Kubernetes API Server Bypass Risks — Static Pods

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in a GET request to watch CustomResourceDefinitions?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: CustomResourceDefinition — getWatch

instruction: How does the `continue` field work when retrieving more results from the server?
response: The `continue` option should be set when retrieving more results from the server. The server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error if the continue value is no longer valid. Clients may use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters to retrieve the next set of results.
title: CustomResourceDefinition — getWatch

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in a GET request to watch CustomResourceDefinitions?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event.
title: CustomResourceDefinition — getWatch

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersionMatch` field in a GET request to watch CustomResourceDefinitions?
response: The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: CustomResourceDefinition — getWatch

instruction: How do I configure the Memory Manager policy on a Kubernetes node?
response: To configure the Memory Manager policy, set the `memoryManagerPolicy` configuration field within the kubelet configuration to the name of your chosen policy.
title: Control Memory Management Policies on a Node — Memory Manager configuration

instruction: What additional step can be taken to increase node stability when configuring Memory Manager policies?
response: Optionally, some amount of memory can be reserved for system or kubelet processes to increase node stability, as described in the Reserved memory configuration section.
title: Control Memory Management Policies on a Node — Memory Manager configuration

instruction: What is the purpose of the ServiceAccountNodeAudienceRestriction feature gate when used with the NodeRestriction admission plugin?
response: The ServiceAccountNodeAudienceRestriction feature gate, when enabled and used with the NodeRestriction admission plugin, restricts the kubelet to request service account tokens for audiences that are already referenced by pods running on that node, preventing a compromised node from obtaining tokens for arbitrary audiences.
title: Using Node Authorization — Service account token audience restriction

instruction: How are allowed audiences for service account tokens determined for a node?
response: The allowed audiences are determined from the pod spec and include: the default API server audience, audiences set in projected service account token volume sources, and audiences configured in CSI driver spec.tokenRequests for any CSI driver used by the pod.
title: Using Node Authorization — Service account token audience restriction

instruction: Why is the ServiceAccountNodeAudienceRestriction feature particularly relevant when using service account tokens for image credential providers?
response: The feature is particularly relevant when using service account tokens for image credential providers because the kubelet requests tokens with a registry-specific audience on behalf of pods, and this feature prevents a compromised node from obtaining tokens for arbitrary registry audiences.
title: Using Node Authorization — Service account token audience restriction

instruction: How can I verify the deployment status of the Cassandra StatefulSet?
response: You can use the command `kubectl get statefulset cassandra` to verify the deployment status of the Cassandra StatefulSet. The response will show the desired and current number of replicas, as well as the age of the StatefulSet.
title: Example: Deploying Cassandra with a StatefulSet — Validating the Cassandra StatefulSet

instruction: Why do Pods in a StatefulSet deploy sequentially?
response: The StatefulSet resource deploys Pods sequentially. This can be observed by running `kubectl get pods -l app=cassandra`, which shows the ordered creation status of the Pods.
title: Example: Deploying Cassandra with a StatefulSet — Validating the Cassandra StatefulSet

instruction: How can I check the status of the Cassandra ring?
response: You can run the Cassandra `nodetool` inside the first Pod to display the status of the ring. Use the command `kubectl exec -it cassandra-0 -- nodetool status` to achieve this.
title: Example: Deploying Cassandra with a StatefulSet — Validating the Cassandra StatefulSet

instruction: How long does it typically take for all Pods in the Cassandra StatefulSet to deploy?
response: It can take several minutes for all three Pods to deploy. You can check the deployment status using `kubectl get pods -l app=cassandra`.
title: Example: Deploying Cassandra with a StatefulSet — Validating the Cassandra StatefulSet

instruction: What is the default restart policy for a container when its resources are resized?
response: The default restart policy is 'NotRequired', which means the resource change is applied to the running container without restarting it.
title: Resize CPU and Memory Resources assigned to Containers — Container resize policies

instruction: When should a container be restarted after changing its memory resources?
response: A container should be restarted after changing its memory resources because many applications and runtimes cannot adjust their memory allocation dynamically. This can be achieved by setting 'restartPolicy' to 'RestartContainer' for memory resources.
title: Resize CPU and Memory Resources assigned to Containers — Container resize policies

instruction: What happens if both CPU and memory resources are changed simultaneously for a container with 'NotRequired' CPU restart policy and 'RestartContainer' memory restart policy?
response: If both CPU and memory resources are changed simultaneously, the container is restarted due to the memory policy.
title: Resize CPU and Memory Resources assigned to Containers — Container resize policies

instruction: What is the valid range of values for the percentageOfNodesToScore option in kube-scheduler configuration?
response: The valid range of values for the percentageOfNodesToScore option is whole numeric values between 0 and 100.
title: Scheduler Performance Tuning — Setting the threshold

instruction: What happens if the percentageOfNodesToScore option is set above 100 in kube-scheduler configuration?
response: If the percentageOfNodesToScore option is set above 100, kube-scheduler acts as if the value was set to 100.
title: Scheduler Performance Tuning — Setting the threshold

instruction: How can I verify that the kube-scheduler component is healthy after making changes to its configuration?
response: You can run the command 'kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep kube-scheduler' to verify that the kube-scheduler component is healthy.
title: Scheduler Performance Tuning — Setting the threshold

instruction: Where is the kube-scheduler configuration file typically located?
response: The kube-scheduler configuration file can typically be found at /etc/kubernetes/config/kube-scheduler.yaml.
title: Scheduler Performance Tuning — Setting the threshold

instruction: What is the purpose of the feature gate that allows kubelet to request a certificate without any Node IP available?
response: The feature gate allows kubelet to request a certificate without any Node IP available, only with DNS names.
title: Feature Gates — List of feature gates

instruction: How does the AuthorizePodWebsocketUpgradeCreatePermission feature gate affect the creation of Pod subresources?
response: When this feature gate is true, clients must be authorized to create Pod subresources even when triggering their creation using a WebSocket. This ensures that WebSocket upgrades strictly enforce the create permission, matching the existing SPDY security model.
title: Feature Gates — List of feature gates

instruction: What is the purpose of the feature gate that enables the use of CBOR as a supported encoding for requests and responses?
response: The feature gate enables CBOR as a supported encoding for requests and responses, and as the preferred storage encoding for custom resources.
title: Feature Gates — List of feature gates

instruction: How does the ConsistentListFromCache feature gate improve Kubernetes API server performance?
response: The feature gate enhances Kubernetes API server performance by serving consistent list requests directly from its watch cache, improving scalability and response times.
title: Feature Gates — List of feature gates

instruction: How do you create a namespace to isolate resources for an exercise?
response: You can create a namespace using the command `kubectl create namespace <namespace-name>`. For example, `kubectl create namespace default-mem-example`.
title: Configure Default Memory Requests and Limits for a Namespace — Create a namespace

instruction: What happens if a Pod is created in a namespace with a LimitRange that specifies default memory requests and limits, and the Pod's container does not specify its own values?
response: The control plane applies the default values specified by the LimitRange. In this case, a memory request of 256MiB and a memory limit of 512MiB are applied.
title: Configure Default Memory Requests and Limits for a Namespace — Create a namespace

instruction: How do you verify that a Pod's container has been assigned default memory requests and limits?
response: You can use the command `kubectl get pod <pod-name> --output=yaml --namespace=<namespace-name>` to view detailed information about the Pod. The output will show the assigned memory requests and limits.
title: Configure Default Memory Requests and Limits for a Namespace — Create a namespace

instruction: What command can be used to delete a Pod in a specific namespace?
response: You can delete a Pod using the command `kubectl delete pod <pod-name> --namespace=<namespace-name>`. For example, `kubectl delete pod default-mem-demo --namespace=default-mem-example`.
title: Configure Default Memory Requests and Limits for a Namespace — Create a namespace

instruction: What happens to filesystem changes when a Container terminates and restarts?
response: Filesystem changes are lost.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a Volume for Storage

instruction: Why is using a Volume important for stateful applications?
response: Using a Volume provides more consistent storage that is independent of the Container, which is especially important for stateful applications, such as key-value stores (like Redis) and databases.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a Volume for Storage

instruction: What is a primary use case for Volumes in Kubernetes?
response: A primary use case for Volumes is to provide consistent storage that persists even when a Container terminates and restarts, ensuring data is not lost.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a Volume for Storage

instruction: What happens to a Pod in a StatefulSet if it is deleted using kubectl?
response: The StatefulSet controller recreates the Pod with the same name and linked to the same PersistentVolumeClaim.
title: Run a Replicated Stateful Application — Delete Pods

instruction: Does a StatefulSet behave similarly to a ReplicaSet in terms of Pod recreation?
response: Yes, a StatefulSet recreates Pods if they're deleted, similar to what a ReplicaSet does for stateless Pods.
title: Run a Replicated Stateful Application — Delete Pods

instruction: What is the effect of deleting a Pod managed by a StatefulSet on the PersistentVolumeClaim?
response: The new Pod created by the StatefulSet controller is linked to the same PersistentVolumeClaim as the original Pod.
title: Run a Replicated Stateful Application — Delete Pods

instruction: Why are ServiceAccount secret tokens being replaced with TokenRequest API tokens in Kubernetes?
response: ServiceAccount secret tokens are being replaced because they have limitations, such as not being able to be set with an expiry, being visible to any cluster user who can read secrets in the namespace, and complicating RBAC management.
title: Hardening Guide - Authentication Mechanisms — ServiceAccount secret tokens

instruction: What are the limitations of using ServiceAccount secret tokens for authentication in Kubernetes?
response: ServiceAccount secret tokens have several limitations: they cannot be set with an expiry and remain valid until the associated service account is deleted, the authentication tokens are visible to any cluster user who can read secrets in the namespace, and service accounts cannot be added to arbitrary groups.
title: Hardening Guide - Authentication Mechanisms — ServiceAccount secret tokens

instruction: In which Kubernetes versions were ServiceAccount secret tokens the default option for authentication?
response: ServiceAccount secret tokens were the default option for authentication in Kubernetes versions prior to 1.23.
title: Hardening Guide - Authentication Mechanisms — ServiceAccount secret tokens

instruction: What is the purpose of the `continue` option in the GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/services request?

response: The resourceVersion sets a constraint on what resource versions a request may be served from. It determines the version of the objects returned, ensuring a consistent snapshot of the objects.

response: The limit field is a maximum number of responses to return for a list call. If more items exist, the server will set the `continue` field on the list metadata to a value that can be used to retrieve the next set of results.
title: Service — getList

instruction: Can gRPC probes use a named port in Kubernetes?
response: No, gRPC probes do not support named ports.
title: Configure Liveness, Readiness and Startup Probes — Use a named port

instruction: What is the purpose of a startup probe in Kubernetes, and how does it relate to a liveness probe?
response: A startup probe is used to allow an application additional startup time on its first initialization. It is configured with the same command, HTTP or TCP check as the liveness probe, but with a failureThreshold * periodSeconds long enough to cover the worst-case startup time. Once the startup probe succeeds, the liveness probe takes over to provide a fast response to container deadlocks.
title: Configure Liveness, Readiness and Startup Probes — Use a named port

instruction: What happens if a startup probe never succeeds in Kubernetes?
response: If the startup probe never succeeds, the container is killed after the failureThreshold * periodSeconds and is subject to the pod's restartPolicy.
title: Configure Liveness, Readiness and Startup Probes — Use a named port

instruction: What is the recommended setting for the endpoint in TracingConfiguration?
response: The recommended setting for the endpoint is unset, which defaults to localhost:4317 for otlp grpc.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — TracingConfiguration

instruction: What is the effect of setting samplingRatePerMillion to unset in TracingConfiguration?
response: If samplingRatePerMillion is unset, the sampler respects its parent span's sampling rate, but otherwise never samples.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — TracingConfiguration

instruction: Is the connection to the tracing collector endpoint secure?
response: No, the connection to the tracing collector endpoint is insecure and does not currently support TLS.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — TracingConfiguration

instruction: What is the purpose of the `pretty` parameter in the GET /api/v1/namespaces request?
response: The `pretty` parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If set to 'true', the output will be pretty-printed. Defaults to 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).

instruction: What tool is required for some steps in this page?
response: The jq tool is required for some steps in this page. If you don't have jq, you can install it via your operating system's software sources or fetch it from https://jqlang.github.io/jq/.
title: Set Kubelet Parameters Via A Configuration File — Before you begin

response: You can install curl via your operating system's software sources.
title: Set Kubelet Parameters Via A Configuration File — Before you begin

instruction: What is the recommended approach for setting kubelet configuration parameters?
response: Providing parameters via a config file is the recommended approach because it simplifies node deployment and configuration management.

instruction: What type of node affinity is specified in the provided Pod manifest?
response: The Pod manifest specifies a requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution node affinity.
title: Assign Pods to Nodes using Node Affinity — Schedule a Pod using required node affinity

instruction: On what condition will the Pod get scheduled according to the node affinity rule?
response: The Pod will get scheduled only on a node that has a disktype=ssd label.
title: Assign Pods to Nodes using Node Affinity — Schedule a Pod using required node affinity

instruction: How can you verify that the Pod is running on the chosen node after applying the manifest?
response: You can verify that the Pod is running on the chosen node by running the command 'kubectl get pods --output=wide'.
title: Assign Pods to Nodes using Node Affinity — Schedule a Pod using required node affinity

instruction: What happens to a container in a Pod when it exits due to an error, according to the restartPolicy defined in the Pod spec?
response: Kubernetes attempts an immediate restart based on the Pod restartPolicy. If the container crashes repeatedly, Kubernetes applies an exponential backoff delay for subsequent restarts.
title: Pod Lifecycle — How Pods handle problems with containers

instruction: What is a CrashLoopBackOff state in a Kubernetes Pod, and what causes it?
response: A CrashLoopBackOff state indicates that the backoff delay mechanism is currently in effect for a given container that is in a crash loop, failing and restarting repeatedly. It can be caused by application errors, configuration errors, resource constraints, health checks failing, or container liveness probes or startup probes returning a Failure result.
title: Pod Lifecycle — How Pods handle problems with containers

instruction: How can a user investigate the root cause of a CrashLoopBackOff issue in a Kubernetes Pod?
response: A user can check logs using kubectl logs <name-of-pod>, inspect events using kubectl describe pod <name-of-pod>, review configuration to ensure it is correct, check resource limits to ensure the container has enough CPU and memory allocated, and debug the application by running the container image locally or in a development environment.
title: Pod Lifecycle — How Pods handle problems with containers

instruction: What happens to the backoff delay when a container runs successfully for a certain duration after a CrashLoopBackOff state?
response: If a container runs successfully for a certain duration (e.g., 10 minutes), Kubernetes resets the backoff delay, treating any new crash as the first one.
title: Pod Lifecycle — How Pods handle problems with containers

instruction: What are the two recommended authentication strategies for a kubelet's initial bootstrap credentials?
response: The two recommended authentication strategies for a kubelet's initial bootstrap credentials are Bootstrap Tokens and Token authentication file.
title: TLS bootstrapping — Initial bootstrap authentication

instruction: What are the requirements for the kubelet to authenticate as a user during the initial bootstrap process?
response: The kubelet must be able to authenticate as a user with the rights to create and retrieve CSRs and be automatically approved to request node client certificates, if automatic approval is enabled.
title: TLS bootstrapping — Initial bootstrap authentication

instruction: How does kube-apiserver recognize a bootstrap token and what rights does it grant?
response: kube-apiserver recognizes a bootstrap token due to its type, namespace, and name, and grants anyone authenticating with that token special bootstrap rights, notably treating them as a member of the system:bootstrappers group.
title: TLS bootstrapping — Initial bootstrap authentication

instruction: What is the command to generate a token with at least 128 bits of entropy on a Linux system?
response: head -c 16 /dev/urandom | od -An -t x | tr -d ' '
title: TLS bootstrapping — Initial bootstrap authentication

instruction: How do I configure a load balancer for an etcd cluster in a Kubernetes setup?
response: To configure a load balancer for an etcd cluster, first set up an etcd cluster, then configure a load balancer in front of the etcd cluster. The address of the load balancer should be used as the value for the --etcd-servers flag when starting Kubernetes API Servers, for example, --etcd-servers=$LB:2379.
title: Operating etcd clusters for Kubernetes — Multi-node etcd cluster with load balancer

instruction: Why is it recommended to restrict access to etcd clusters in a Kubernetes setup?
response: Access to etcd is equivalent to root permission in the cluster, so it is recommended to grant permission to only those nodes that require access to etcd clusters. This is because etcd stores sensitive data, and unrestricted access can pose a security risk.
title: Operating etcd clusters for Kubernetes — Multi-node etcd cluster with load balancer

instruction: How can I secure communication between etcd members and clients in a Kubernetes setup?
response: To secure etcd, you can either set up firewall rules or use the security features provided by etcd, which depend on x509 Public Key Infrastructure (PKI). This involves generating key and certificate pairs, such as peer.key and peer.cert for etcd members, and client.key and client.cert for clients.
title: Operating etcd clusters for Kubernetes — Multi-node etcd cluster with load balancer

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /api/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/resourcequotas request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: ResourceQuota — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /api/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/resourcequotas request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. The server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error if the continue value is no longer valid. Clients may use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters to retrieve the next set of results.
title: ResourceQuota — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /api/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/resourcequotas request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event.
title: ResourceQuota — getWatch List

instruction: What is the purpose of the `pretty` parameter in the GET /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/roles request?
response: The `pretty` parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If set to 'true', the output will be pretty printed. Defaults to 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: Role — getList

instruction: How does the `continue` parameter work in the GET /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/roles request?
