response: The `continue` parameter should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is a server-defined value that clients may only use with identical query parameters (except for the value of continue). If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error. Clients may then send another list request with the token received with the 410 error to retrieve the next set of results.
title: Role — getList

instruction: What is the effect of setting `limit` in the GET /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/roles request?
response: Setting `limit` will return a maximum number of responses for a list call. If more items exist, the server will set the `continue` field on the list metadata to a value that can be used to retrieve the next set of results. The server guarantees that the objects returned when using continue will be identical to issuing a single list call without a limit.
title: Role — getList

instruction: How does the `resourceVersion` and `resourceVersionMatch` parameters interact in the GET /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/roles request?
response: The `resourceVersion` parameter sets a constraint on what resource versions a request may be served from. The `resourceVersionMatch` parameter determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set. The `resourceVersionMatch` value of NotOlderThan is interpreted as 'data at least as new as the provided `resourceVersion`'.
title: Role — getList

instruction: What is the command line option to enable static bearer token file integration for the API server?
response: The API server reads static bearer tokens from a file when given the --token-auth-file=<SOMEFILE> option on the command line.
title: Authenticating — Static token file integration

instruction: What are the required columns in the static token file?
response: The token file is a CSV file with a minimum of 3 columns: token, user name, user uid, followed by a comma-separated list of optional group names.
title: Authenticating — Static token file integration

instruction: When is using a static token file appropriate?
response: Using a static token file is appropriate for tokens that by their nature are long-lived, static, and perhaps may never be rotated. It is also useful when the client is local to a particular API server within the control plane, such as a monitoring agent.
title: Authenticating — Static token file integration

instruction: What is recommended for circumstances where very prompt token rotation is important?
response: For other circumstances, and especially where very prompt token rotation is important, the Kubernetes project recommends using a webhook token authenticator instead of this mechanism.
title: Authenticating — Static token file integration

instruction: What events does the service controller watch for in Service objects?
response: The service controller watches for Service object create, update, and delete events.
title: Cloud Controller Manager — Service controller

instruction: What access does the service controller require to access Services?
response: To access Services, the service controller requires list and watch access.
title: Cloud Controller Manager — Service controller

instruction: What access does the service controller require to update Services?
response: To update Services, the service controller requires patch and update access to the status subresource.
title: Cloud Controller Manager — Service controller

instruction: What are the allowed verbs for the v1/Service resource?
response: The allowed verbs for the v1/Service resource are list, get, watch, patch, and update.
title: Cloud Controller Manager — Service controller

instruction: When should I use a readiness probe to control traffic to a Pod?
response: You should use a readiness probe to control traffic to a Pod when you want to start sending traffic to the Pod only after a specific condition is met, such as when a certain endpoint is responding successfully.
title: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes — When should you use a readiness probe?

instruction: Can I use a readiness probe that is different from the liveness probe?
response: Yes, you can use a readiness probe that is different from the liveness probe. This allows you to check an endpoint specific to readiness that is not used for liveness checks.
title: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes — When should you use a readiness probe?

instruction: How can I use readiness and liveness probes together to handle dependencies on back-end services?
response: You can implement both a liveness and a readiness probe. The liveness probe can pass when the app itself is healthy, while the readiness probe checks that each required back-end service is available, helping to avoid directing traffic to Pods that can only respond with error messages.
title: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes — When should you use a readiness probe?

instruction: What happens to an ephemeral container if it causes a Pod to exceed its resource allocation?
response: The kubelet may evict a Pod if an ephemeral container causes the Pod to exceed its resource allocation.
title: Pod — EphemeralContainer

instruction: Can you remove or restart an ephemeral container?
response: No, ephemeral containers may not be removed or restarted.
title: Pod — EphemeralContainer

instruction: What is the purpose of the targetContainerName field in an ephemeral container?
response: If set, the name of the container from PodSpec that this ephemeral container targets. The ephemeral container will be run in the namespaces (IPC, PID, etc) of this container.
title: Pod — EphemeralContainer

instruction: Are liveness probes, readiness probes, and startup probes allowed for ephemeral containers?
response: No, probes are not allowed for ephemeral containers.
title: Pod — EphemeralContainer

instruction: What is the order of precedence for merging kubelet configuration?
response: The kubelet merges configuration from: 1) Feature gates specified over the command line (lowest precedence), 2) The kubelet configuration, 3) Drop-in configuration files, according to sort order, and 4) Command line arguments excluding feature gates (highest precedence).
title: Set Kubelet Parameters Via A Configuration File — Kubelet configuration merging order

instruction: How can you inspect the final actuated kubelet configuration?
response: To inspect the final actuated kubelet configuration, start a proxy server using 'kubectl proxy' and then use 'curl' to fetch the kubelet configuration from 'http://127.0.0.1:8001/api/v1/nodes/<node-name>/proxy/configz'.
title: Set Kubelet Parameters Via A Configuration File — Kubelet configuration merging order

instruction: What command can be used to start a proxy server to access kubelet configuration?
response: The command to start a proxy server is 'kubectl proxy'.
title: Set Kubelet Parameters Via A Configuration File — Kubelet configuration merging order

instruction: How do you fetch the kubelet configuration using 'curl'?
response: Use 'curl -X GET http://127.0.0.1:8001/api/v1/nodes/<node-name>/proxy/configz | jq .' to fetch and display the kubelet configuration.
title: Set Kubelet Parameters Via A Configuration File — Kubelet configuration merging order

instruction: What are the supported memory policies on Windows nodes?
response: Only the 'None' and 'BestEffort' policies are supported on Windows.
title: Control Memory Management Policies on a Node — Windows support

instruction: How can the Memory Manager be activated on a node?
response: The Memory Manager activates during kubelet startup if a node administrator configures 'reservedMemory' for the kubelet.
title: Control Memory Management Policies on a Node — Windows support

instruction: What is required when the 'Static' policy is configured for the Memory Manager?
response: When the 'Static' policy is configured, you must configure reserved memory for the node, for example, with the 'reservedMemory' configuration field in the kubelet configuration.
title: Control Memory Management Policies on a Node — Windows support

instruction: What happens when a pod's memory request exceeds the capacity of a single NUMA node?
response: The Memory Manager attempts to create a group that comprises several NUMA nodes and features extended memory capacity.
title: Control Memory Management Policies on a Node — Windows support

instruction: How can you confirm that the Pods created from the 'hostnames' deployment are running?
response: You can confirm that the Pods are running by executing the command 'kubectl get pods -l app=hostnames'.
title: Debug Services — Setup

instruction: What command can be used to get the list of Pod IP addresses for the 'hostnames' deployment?
response: You can get the list of Pod IP addresses by executing the command 'kubectl get pods -l app=hostnames -o go-template='{{range .items}}{{.status.podIP}}{{"\n"}}{{end}}''.
title: Debug Services — Setup

instruction: How can you troubleshoot issues with your Pods if they are not responding as expected?
response: If your Pods are not responding as expected, you can use 'kubectl logs' to see what is happening, or 'kubectl exec' directly into your Pods to debug from there.
title: Debug Services — Setup

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in a GET /apis/internal.apiserver.k8s.io/v1alpha1/watch/storageversions request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: StorageVersion — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in a list call, and what is its relationship with the `limit` field?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. The server will set the `continue` field on the list metadata to a value that can be used with the same initial query to retrieve the next set of results. The `limit` field is a maximum number of responses to return for a list call. If more items exist, the server will set the `continue` field. Clients may assume that no more results are available if the `continue` field is empty.
title: StorageVersion — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in a GET /apis/internal.apiserver.k8s.io/v1alpha1/watch/storageversions request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent.
title: StorageVersion — getWatch List

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersionMatch` field, and how does it interact with `resourceVersion`?
response: The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set. The `resourceVersionMatch` field has specific semantics when used with `sendInitialEvents`.
title: StorageVersion — getWatch List

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in a Kubernetes watch request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers may ignore this flag if they do not implement bookmarks, and clients should not assume bookmarks are returned at any specific interval.
title: Workload — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in a Kubernetes list request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is a server-defined value that clients may use to retrieve the next set of results. If the continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: Workload — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` in a Kubernetes watch request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event.
title: Workload — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `resourceVersionMatch` field interact with `resourceVersion` in a Kubernetes list request?
response: The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended to set `resourceVersionMatch` for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: Workload — getWatch List

instruction: How do you manually renew certificates in a Kubernetes cluster using kubeadm?
response: You can renew your certificates manually at any time with the `kubeadm certs renew` command, with the appropriate command line options. If you are running a cluster with a replicated control plane, this command needs to be executed on all the control-plane nodes.
title: Certificate Management with kubeadm — Manual certificate renewal

instruction: What needs to be done after running `kubeadm certs renew` to complete the certificate renewal process?
response: After running the command, you should restart the control plane Pods. This is required since dynamic certificate reload is currently not supported for all components and certificates.
title: Certificate Management with kubeadm — Manual certificate renewal

instruction: How do you restart a static Pod after renewing certificates using kubeadm?
response: To restart a static Pod, you can temporarily remove its manifest file from `/etc/kubernetes/manifests/` and wait for 20 seconds. The kubelet will terminate the Pod if it's no longer in the manifest directory. You can then move the file back and after another fileCheckFrequency period, the kubelet will recreate the Pod and the certificate renewal for the component can complete.
title: Certificate Management with kubeadm — Manual certificate renewal

instruction: Can `kubeadm certs renew` renew specific certificates or all certificates at once?
response: Yes, `kubeadm certs renew` can renew any specific certificate or, with the subcommand `all`, it can renew all of them.
title: Certificate Management with kubeadm — Manual certificate renewal

instruction: What is the purpose of ServiceAccountTokenCacheType in Kubernetes?
response: ServiceAccountTokenCacheType is the type of cache key used for caching credentials returned by the plugin when the service account token is used.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1) — ServiceAccountTokenCacheType

instruction: In which Kubernetes object does ServiceAccountTokenCacheType appear?
response: ServiceAccountTokenCacheType appears in ServiceAccountTokenAttributes.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1) — ServiceAccountTokenCacheType

instruction: What annotation do mirror Pods have in Kubernetes?
response: Mirror Pods have the kubernetes.io/config.mirror annotation.
title: Admission Webhook Good Practices — Don't change immutable objects

instruction: Why shouldn't you attempt to mutate mirror Pods during admission in Kubernetes?
response: You shouldn't attempt to mutate mirror Pods during admission because changes to the mirror Pod don't propagate to the static Pod it mirrors.
title: Admission Webhook Good Practices — Don't change immutable objects

instruction: How can you reduce the security risk of ignoring the kubernetes.io/config.mirror annotation while excluding mirror Pods?
response: You can reduce the security risk by allowing static Pods to only run in specific namespaces.
title: Admission Webhook Good Practices — Don't change immutable objects

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' option in the Kubernetes API for listing jobs?
response: The continue option should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It allows clients to retrieve the next set of results from a previous query, provided the query parameters are identical except for the continue value.
title: Job — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: How does the 'gracePeriodSeconds' parameter affect the deletion of a job in Kubernetes?
response: The 'gracePeriodSeconds' parameter specifies the duration in seconds before the object should be deleted. A non-negative integer value can be provided, with zero indicating immediate deletion. If nil, the default grace period for the specified type will be used.
title: Job — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the difference between 'orphanDependents' and 'propagationPolicy' in the Kubernetes API for deleting jobs?
response: The 'orphanDependents' field is deprecated and should be replaced with 'propagationPolicy'. 'propagationPolicy' allows specifying whether and how garbage collection will be performed, with acceptable values being 'Orphan', 'Background', or 'Foreground'.
title: Job — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: How do you create a Role in Kubernetes that allows a user to perform create, get, list, update, and delete operations on pods?
response:
You can create a Role in Kubernetes using the command `kubectl create role developer --verb=create --verb=get --verb=list --verb=update --verb=delete --resource=pods`. The equivalent YAML for this role is:
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: developer
rules:
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["pods"]
    verbs: ["create", "get", "list", "update", "delete"]
title: Issue a Certificate for a Kubernetes API Client Using A CertificateSigningRequest — Create Role and RoleBinding

instruction: What action does the kubelet take to reclaim node-level resources when disk resources are starved?
response: The kubelet removes unused container images.
title: Node-pressure Eviction — Self healing behavior

instruction: What happens to evicted pods if they are managed by a workload management object like StatefulSet or Deployment?
response: The control plane (kube-controller-manager) creates new pods in place of the evicted pods.
title: Node-pressure Eviction — Self healing behavior

instruction: What flag must be added to the kube-apiserver command to enable client certificate authentication?
response: The --client-ca-file=FILENAME flag must be added to the kube-apiserver command to enable client certificate authentication, referencing a certificate authority bundle containing the signing certificate.
title: TLS bootstrapping — kube-apiserver configuration

instruction: What are the requirements for TLS bootstrapping in kube-apiserver?
response: The kube-apiserver has several requirements to enable TLS bootstrapping: recognizing CA that signs the client certificate, authenticating the bootstrapping kubelet to the system:bootstrappers group, and authorizing the bootstrapping kubelet to create a certificate signing request (CSR).
title: TLS bootstrapping — kube-apiserver configuration

instruction: How do you specify the certificate authority bundle for client certificate authentication?
response: You specify the certificate authority bundle for client certificate authentication by referencing a file containing the signing certificate, for example --client-ca-file=/var/lib/kubernetes/ca.pem.
title: TLS bootstrapping — kube-apiserver configuration

instruction: What is the difference between Docker and Kubernetes interpretation of config.json in terms of auths keys?
response: In Docker, the auths keys can only specify root URLs, whereas Kubernetes allows glob URLs as well as prefix-matched paths.
title: Images — Interpretation of config.json

instruction: What is the limitation for using glob patterns in Kubernetes config.json?
response: The only limitation is that glob patterns (*) have to include the dot (.) for each subdomain, and the amount of matched subdomains has to be equal to the amount of glob patterns (*.).
title: Images — Interpretation of config.json

instruction: How does the kubelet handle multiple entries in config.json for different paths during image pull operations?
response: The kubelet performs image pulls sequentially for every found credential, and if one authentication source fails, it will try to download the image using the next authentication source.
title: Images — Interpretation of config.json

instruction: Can a config.json have multiple auths entries for different paths, and what happens during image pull?
response: Yes, a config.json can have multiple auths entries for different paths. During image pull, the kubelet will try to download the image using both authentication sources if one of them fails.
title: Images — Interpretation of config.json

instruction: How do you add a taint to a node in Kubernetes?
response: You add a taint to a node using the kubectl taint command. For example, kubectl taint nodes node1 key1=value1:NoSchedule places a taint on node node1 with key key1, value value1, and taint effect NoSchedule.
title: Taints and Tolerations — Concepts

instruction: What happens if a pod does not have a matching toleration for a node's taint with effect NoSchedule?
response: If a pod does not have a matching toleration for a node's taint with effect NoSchedule, the pod will not be able to schedule onto that node.
title: Taints and Tolerations — Concepts

instruction: How does a pod's toleration match a taint?
response: A toleration matches a taint if the keys are the same and the effects are the same, and either the operator is Exists (in which case no value should be specified), or the operator is Equal and the values should be equal.
title: Taints and Tolerations — Concepts

instruction: What is the effect of a taint with effect NoExecute on a pod that does not tolerate it?
response: If a pod does not tolerate a taint with effect NoExecute, the pod will be evicted immediately if it is already running on the node, and will not be scheduled onto the node if it is not yet running on the node.
title: Taints and Tolerations — Concepts

instruction: What are the node-aware verbs required for a node-local driver to update the status of a ResourceClaim?
response: The node-aware verbs required are 'get', 'patch', and 'update' for the 'resourceclaims/status' resource in the 'resource.k8s.io' API group.
title: Harden Dynamic Resource Allocation in Your Cluster — Grant node-local driver permissions

instruction: How do you grant a node-local driver permission to update its own ResourceClaim status?
response: You can grant permission by creating a ClusterRole with rules that include 'get', 'patch', and 'update' verbs for 'resourceclaims/status' and 'associated-node:patch' and 'associated-node:update' verbs for 'resourceclaims/driver' with a specific resourceName.
title: Harden Dynamic Resource Allocation in Your Cluster — Grant node-local driver permissions

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'associated-node:patch' and 'associated-node:update' verbs in the context of node-local drivers?
response: These verbs are used to authorize a node-local driver to patch and update the 'resourceclaims/driver' resource for a specific resourceName, allowing the driver to manage its own resource claims.
title: Harden Dynamic Resource Allocation in Your Cluster — Grant node-local driver permissions

instruction: What does the top-level status.observedGeneration field in a Kubernetes pod represent?
response: The top-level status.observedGeneration field shows the metadata.generation corresponding to the latest pod specification that the kubelet has acknowledged.
title: Resize CPU and Memory Resources assigned to Containers — LeveragingobservedGenerationFields

instruction: How can you determine the most recent resize request processed by the kubelet?
response: You can use the top-level status.observedGeneration field to determine the most recent resize request the kubelet has processed.
title: Resize CPU and Memory Resources assigned to Containers — LeveragingobservedGenerationFields

instruction: What does the conditions[].observedGeneration field indicate in the PodResizeInProgress condition?
response: In the PodResizeInProgress condition, the conditions[].observedGeneration field indicates the metadata.generation of the podSpec when the current in-progress resize was initiated.
title: Resize CPU and Memory Resources assigned to Containers — LeveragingobservedGenerationFields

instruction: What does the conditions[].observedGeneration field indicate in the PodResizePending condition?
response: In the PodResizePending condition, the conditions[].observedGeneration field indicates the metadata.generation of the podSpec when the pending resize's allocation was last attempted.
title: Resize CPU and Memory Resources assigned to Containers — LeveragingobservedGenerationFields

instruction: How many control plane instances should be run per failure zone in a large Kubernetes cluster?
response: At least one instance per failure zone should be run to provide fault-tolerance.
title: Considerations for large clusters — Control plane components

instruction: How should control plane instances be scaled in a large Kubernetes cluster?
response: Typically, you would scale control plane instances vertically first, and then scale horizontally after reaching the point of falling returns to (vertical) scale.
title: Considerations for large clusters — Control plane components

instruction: Does Kubernetes automatically steer traffic towards control-plane endpoints in the same failure zone?
response: No, Kubernetes nodes do not automatically steer traffic towards control-plane endpoints that are in the same failure zone.
title: Considerations for large clusters — Control plane components

instruction: How can traffic be directed to control plane hosts in the same failure zone using a managed load balancer?
response: You can configure the load balancer to send traffic that originates from the kubelet and Pods in a specific failure zone, and direct that traffic only to the control plane hosts that are also in that zone.
title: Considerations for large clusters — Control plane components

instruction: What happens to existing Leases when a new instance of kube-apiserver uses the same hostname?
response: When a new instance of kube-apiserver uses the same hostname, it will take over existing Leases using a new holder identity, as opposed to instantiating new Lease objects.
title: Leases — API server identity

instruction: How are expired leases from kube-apiservers that no longer exist handled?
response: Expired leases from kube-apiservers that no longer exist are garbage collected by new kube-apiservers after 1 hour.
title: Leases — API server identity

instruction: How can you disable API server identity leases?
response: You can disable API server identity leases by disabling the APIServerIdentity feature gate.
title: Leases — API server identity

instruction: What is the cause of the fatal forwarding loop when resolving names in upstream servers on some Linux distributions?
response: The cause is systemd-resolved moving and replacing /etc/resolv.conf with a stub file.
title: Debugging DNS Resolution — Known issues

instruction: How can the fatal forwarding loop be fixed manually?
response: The loop can be fixed by using kubelet's --resolv-conf flag to point to the correct resolv.conf, which with systemd-resolved is /run/systemd/resolve/resolv.conf.
title: Debugging DNS Resolution — Known issues

instruction: Why does Kubernetes not configure nodes' resolv.conf files to use the cluster DNS by default?
response: Kubernetes does not configure nodes' resolv.conf files to use the cluster DNS by default because the process is inherently distribution-specific.
title: Debugging DNS Resolution — Known issues

instruction: What is a workaround for the libc limit of 3 DNS nameserver records when a local installation already uses 3 nameservers?
response: A workaround is to run dnsmasq on the node, which will provide more nameserver entries, or use kubelet's --resolv-conf flag.
title: Debugging DNS Resolution — Known issues

instruction: How do you delete a StatefulSet and its associated pods in Kubernetes?
response: To delete everything in a StatefulSet, including the associated pods, you can run a series of commands. First, retrieve the termination grace period seconds of the pod using kubectl get pods <stateful-set-pod> --template '{{.spec.terminationGracePeriodSeconds}}'. Then, delete the StatefulSet using kubectl delete statefulset -l app.kubernetes.io/name=<label>, wait for the retrieved grace period, and finally delete the PVCs using kubectl delete pvc -l app.kubernetes.io/name=<label>.
title: Delete a StatefulSet — Complete deletion of a StatefulSet

instruction: What is the purpose of retrieving the terminationGracePeriodSeconds of a pod during StatefulSet deletion?
response: The terminationGracePeriodSeconds is used to determine the number of seconds to wait after deleting the StatefulSet before deleting the PVCs. This allows the pods to terminate gracefully.
title: Delete a StatefulSet — Complete deletion of a StatefulSet

instruction: How do you specify the label for the StatefulSet and PVCs to be deleted?
response: You can specify the label using the -l flag followed by the label key and value, for example: -l app.kubernetes.io/name=MyApp. You should substitute MyApp with your own label as appropriate.
title: Delete a StatefulSet — Complete deletion of a StatefulSet

instruction: How does kubelet verify if a Linux node supports the noswap option for tmpfs?
response: The kubelet checks the Linux kernel version. If it's above 6.3, noswap is assumed to be supported. Otherwise, kubelet attempts to mount a dummy tmpfs with the noswap option at startup to verify support.
title: Swap memory management — Memory-backed volumes

instruction: What happens if the noswap option is not supported by the Linux node?
response: If the noswap option is not supported, kubelet emits a warning log entry indicating that memory-backed volumes might swap to disk, and then continues its execution.
title: Swap memory management — Memory-backed volumes

instruction: Is handling encrypted swap within the scope of kubelet?
response: No, handling encrypted swap is not within the scope of kubelet. It is a general OS configuration concern and should be addressed at the OS level by the administrator.
title: Swap memory management — Memory-backed volumes

instruction: What are the requirements for the trustBundle field in a ClusterTrustBundle object?
response: The trustBundle field must contain one or more X.509 certificates, DER-serialized, each wrapped in a PEM CERTIFICATE block. The certificates must parse as valid X.509 certificates.
title: Certificates and Certificate Signing Requests — Common properties and validation

instruction: How are ClusterTrustBundle objects secured within a cluster?
response: ClusterTrustBundle objects should be considered world-readable within the cluster. If the cluster uses RBAC authorization, all ServiceAccounts have a default grant that allows them to get, list, and watch all ClusterTrustBundle objects.
title: Certificates and Certificate Signing Requests — Common properties and validation

instruction: How does the kubelet gather metric statistics?
response: The kubelet gathers metric statistics at the node, volume, pod, and container level.
title: Node metrics data

instruction: What API does the kubelet use to emit metric statistics?
response: The kubelet emits metric statistics in the Summary API.
title: Node metrics data

instruction: How can you send a request to the stats summary API for a specific node?
response: You can send a proxied request to the stats summary API via the Kubernetes API server using a command like 'kubectl get --raw /api/v1/nodes/minikube/proxy/stats/summary' or using curl after running 'kubectl proxy'.
title: Node metrics data

instruction: What needs to be done before using curl to call the Summary API?
response: You need to run 'kubectl proxy' before using curl to call the Summary API, and you should change the port in the curl command to the port assigned by 'kubectl proxy'.
title: Node metrics data

instruction: What volume types are permitted by the Restricted policy in Kubernetes?
response: The Restricted policy only permits the following volume types: configMap, csi, downwardAPI, emptyDir, ephemeral, persistentVolumeClaim, projected, and secret.
title: Pod Security Standards — Restricted

instruction: What is the requirement for privilege escalation in containers under the Restricted policy?
response: Privilege escalation should not be allowed. The fields spec.containers[*].securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation, spec.initContainers[*].securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation, and spec.ephemeralContainers[*].securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation must be set to false.
title: Pod Security Standards — Restricted

instruction: What are the allowed values for the seccompProfile.type field under the Restricted policy?
response: The seccomp profile must be explicitly set to one of the allowed values: RuntimeDefault or Localhost. Both the Unconfined profile and the absence of a profile are prohibited.
title: Pod Security Standards — Restricted

instruction: What are the requirements for running containers as non-root users under the Restricted policy?
response: Containers must be required to run as non-root users. The fields spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot, spec.containers[*].securityContext.runAsNonRoot, spec.initContainers[*].securityContext.runAsNonRoot, and spec.ephemeralContainers[*].securityContext.runAsNonRoot must be set to true. Additionally, containers must not set runAsUser to 0.
title: Pod Security Standards — Restricted

instruction: What happens to existing clusters without manifest configuration when the manifest-based admission control feature is enabled?
response: Existing clusters without manifest configuration see no behavioral change.
title: Manifest-Based Admission Control — Upgrade and downgrade

instruction: What steps must be taken before downgrading to a version without manifest-based admission control?
response: Before downgrading, remove staticManifestsDir entries from the AdmissionConfiguration file. If relying on manifest-based policies, recreate them as API objects where possible, and then restart the kube-apiserver.
title: Manifest-Based Admission Control — Upgrade and downgrade

instruction: What is the purpose of init containers in a Pod?
response: Init containers are used to perform initialization tasks before the main app container starts. Examples of tasks include waiting for a Service to be created, registering the Pod with a remote server, cloning a Git repository, and generating a configuration file for the main app container.
title: Init Containers — Examples

instruction: How can I view the logs for init containers in a Pod?
response: You can view the logs for init containers using the command: kubectl logs <pod-name> -c <init-container-name>. For example: kubectl logs myapp-pod -c init-myservice or kubectl logs myapp-pod -c init-mydb.
title: Init Containers — Examples

instruction: What happens to a Pod when its init containers complete?
response: Once all init containers complete, the Pod runs the main app container from its spec section. In the example provided, after both init containers complete, the myapp-pod Pod moves into the Running state.
title: Init Containers — Examples

instruction: What happens when the imagePullPolicy property of a container is set to IfNotPresent or Never?
response: A local image is used, preferentially or exclusively, respectively.
title: Images — Pre-pulled images

instruction: What is a use case for pre-pulled images in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Pre-pulled images can be used to preload certain images for speed or as an alternative to authenticating to a private registry.
title: Images — Pre-pulled images

instruction: What is required to use pre-pulled images as a substitute for registry authentication in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: All nodes in the cluster must have the same pre-pulled images.
title: Images — Pre-pulled images

instruction: What is the purpose of the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-target-group-attributes annotation in a Kubernetes Service?
response: The AWS load balancer controller uses this annotation to configure target group attributes, such as enabling stickiness and specifying the type.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-target-group-attributes (beta)

instruction: How do you specify multiple target group attributes using the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-target-group-attributes annotation?
response: You can specify multiple target group attributes by separating them with commas, for example: "stickiness.enabled=true,stickiness.type=source_ip".
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-target-group-attributes (beta)

instruction: What is the purpose of the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-target-node-labels annotation in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Kubernetes' official integration with AWS uses this annotation to determine which nodes in your cluster should be considered as valid targets for the load balancer, based on node labels.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-target-group-attributes (beta)

instruction: What is the purpose of the `continue` option when retrieving a list of ResourceQuotas?
response: The continue option should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It allows clients to retrieve the next set of results from a previous query with identical query parameters.
title: ResourceQuota — getList

instruction: How does the `resourceVersion` field affect the results of a list call?
response: The resourceVersion field sets a constraint on what resource versions a request may be served from. It determines the consistency of the results, ensuring that the client sees all possible objects.
title: ResourceQuota — getList

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` with `watch=true`?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic Bookmark event.
title: ResourceQuota — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the `limit` field when retrieving a list of ResourceQuotas?
response: The limit field is a maximum number of responses to return for a list call. If more items exist, the server will set the continue field on the list metadata to a value that can be used to retrieve the next set of results.
title: ResourceQuota — getList

instruction: What happens to a pod if the Topology Manager determines that single NUMA Node affinity is not possible with the single-numa-node topology management policy?
response: The Topology Manager will reject the pod from the node, resulting in a pod in a Terminated state with a pod admission failure.
title: Control Topology Management Policies on a node — single-numa-nodepolicy

instruction: What is the recommended approach to reschedule a pod that has encountered a Topology Affinity error and is in a Terminated state?
response: It is recommended to use a Deployment with replicas to trigger a redeployment of the Pod. Alternatively, an external control loop could be implemented to trigger a redeployment of pods that have the Topology Affinity error.
title: Control Topology Management Policies on a node — single-numa-nodepolicy

instruction: How does the Topology Manager use information from Hint Providers when making resource allocation decisions with the single-numa-node topology management policy?
response: The kubelet calls each Hint Provider to discover their resource availability. Using this information, the Topology Manager determines if a single NUMA Node affinity is possible and stores this information. The Hint Providers can then use this information when making the resource allocation decision.
title: Control Topology Management Policies on a node — single-numa-nodepolicy

instruction: What is the default authorization mode for the kubelet API?
response: The default authorization mode is AlwaysAllow, which allows all requests.
title: Kubelet authentication/authorization — Kubelet authorization

instruction: How can you subdivide access to the kubelet API?
response: To subdivide access to the kubelet API, delegate authorization to the API server by ensuring the authorization.k8s.io/v1 API group is enabled, starting the kubelet with the --authorization-mode=Webhook and --kubeconfig flags, and having the kubelet call the SubjectAccessReview API on the configured API server.
title: Kubelet authentication/authorization — Kubelet authorization

instruction: What are the implications of granting nodes/proxy permission?
response: Granting nodes/proxy permission grants access to all other kubelet APIs, including APIs that can be used to execute commands in any container running on the node. This is not a read-only permission, even though it is authorized with the get verb.
title: Kubelet authentication/authorization — Kubelet authorization

instruction: What attributes are used to authorize API requests to the kubelet?
response: The kubelet authorizes API requests using the same request attributes approach as the apiserver, with the verb determined from the HTTP verb, and the resource and subresource determined from the incoming request's path.
title: Kubelet authentication/authorization — Kubelet authorization

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in a GET request to `/apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/resourceclaims`?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: ResourceClaim — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in a list call, and what is its relationship with the `limit` field?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is used with the same initial query to retrieve the next set of results. The `limit` field sets a maximum number of responses to return for a list call. If more items exist, the server will set the `continue` field on the list metadata. The `continue` field is not supported when watch is true.
title: ResourceClaim — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` with `watch=true` in a GET request to `/apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/resourceclaims`?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent.
title: ResourceClaim — getWatch List

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersionMatch` field, and how does it relate to `resourceVersion` and `sendInitialEvents`?
response: The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set. When `sendInitialEvents` option is set, we require `resourceVersionMatch` option to also be set.
title: ResourceClaim — getWatch List

instruction: How do you use kubectl auth reconcile to reconcile RBAC resources from a file named my-rbac-rules.yaml?
response: You can use the command kubectl auth reconcile -f my-rbac-rules.yaml to reconcile RBAC resources from a file.
title: kubectl auth reconcile — Examples

instruction: What is the purpose of the --remove-extra-permissions flag in kubectl auth reconcile?
response: The --remove-extra-permissions flag removes extra permissions added to roles if set to true.
title: kubectl auth reconcile — Examples

instruction: Can you process a kustomization directory with kubectl auth reconcile, and if so, how?
response: Yes, you can process a kustomization directory using the -k or --kustomize flag followed by the directory path. This flag cannot be used together with -f or -R.
title: kubectl auth reconcile — Examples

instruction: How do you specify the output format when using kubectl auth reconcile?
response: You can specify the output format using the -o or --output flag followed by the desired format, such as json, yaml, or go-template.
title: kubectl auth reconcile — Examples

instruction: What happens to BestEffort Pods when a node comes under resource pressure?
response: The kubelet prefers to evict BestEffort Pods.
title: Pod Quality of Service Classes — BestEffort

instruction: How are CPU and memory resources allocated to BestEffort Pods?
response: BestEffort Pods can use node resources that aren't specifically assigned to Pods in other QoS classes. For example, if 4 CPU cores are assigned to a Guaranteed Pod on a node with 16 CPU cores, a BestEffort Pod can try to use any amount of the remaining 12 CPU cores.
title: Pod Quality of Service Classes — BestEffort

instruction: What are the criteria for a Pod to be classified as BestEffort?
response: A Pod is BestEffort if none of its Containers have a memory limit or request, CPU limit or request, and the Pod does not have any Pod-level memory or CPU limits or requests.
title: Pod Quality of Service Classes — BestEffort

instruction: What are the default credentials for unauthenticated requests to the kubelet's HTTPS endpoint?
response: By default, requests to the kubelet's HTTPS endpoint that are not rejected by other configured authentication methods are treated as anonymous requests, and given a username of system:anonymous and a group of system:unauthenticated.
title: Kubelet authentication/authorization — Overview

instruction: How can you disable anonymous access to the kubelet's HTTPS endpoint?
response: To disable anonymous access and send 401 Unauthorized responses to unauthenticated requests, start the kubelet with the --anonymous-auth=false flag.
title: Kubelet authentication/authorization — Overview

instruction: What flags are required to enable X509 client certificate authentication to the kubelet's HTTPS endpoint?
response: To enable X509 client certificate authentication, start the kubelet with the --client-ca-file flag, providing a CA bundle to verify client certificates with. Additionally, start the apiserver with --kubelet-client-certificate and --kubelet-client-key flags.
title: Kubelet authentication/authorization — Overview

instruction: How can you enable API bearer tokens to be used to authenticate to the kubelet's HTTPS endpoint?
response: To enable API bearer tokens, ensure the authentication.k8s.io/v1 API group is enabled in the API server, then start the kubelet with the --authentication-token-webhook and --kubeconfig flags. The kubelet calls the TokenReview API on the configured API server to determine user information from bearer tokens.
title: Kubelet authentication/authorization — Overview

instruction: What type of volume plugins support volume cloning in Kubernetes?
response: Volume cloning is only available for CSI volume plugins.
title: Persistent Volumes — Volume Cloning

instruction: How do you specify the source of a cloned Persistent Volume Claim (PVC) in Kubernetes?
response: You specify the source of a cloned PVC by setting the `dataSource` field in the PVC specification, including the `name` and `kind` of the source PVC.
title: Persistent Volumes — Volume Cloning

instruction: What is the purpose of the `storageClassName` field when creating a cloned PVC?
response: The `storageClassName` field specifies the StorageClass to be used for the cloned PVC, which in this case should match the CSI plugin being used, such as `my-csi-plugin`.
title: Persistent Volumes — Volume Cloning

instruction: What happens when a request is denied by earlier authorizers, such as Node, in the authorization chain?
response: If earlier authorizers, such as Node, deny a request, then the RBAC authorizer attempts to authorize the API request. If RBAC also denies that API request, the ABAC authorizer is then run.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Parallel authorizers

instruction: How can you determine which roles need to be granted to which users, groups, or service accounts when using RBAC?
response: When the kube-apiserver is run with a log level of 5 or higher for the RBAC component, you can see RBAC denials in the API server log (prefixed with RBAC). You can use that information to determine which roles need to be granted to which users, groups, or service accounts.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Parallel authorizers

instruction: When can you remove the ABAC authorizer after using RBAC?
response: Once you have granted roles to service accounts and workloads are running with no RBAC denial messages in the server logs, you can remove the ABAC authorizer.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Parallel authorizers

instruction: What is the purpose of the minReadySeconds field in a DeploymentSpec?
response: The minReadySeconds field specifies the minimum number of seconds for which a newly created pod should be ready without any of its container crashing, for it to be considered available. If not specified, it defaults to 0, meaning the pod will be considered available as soon as it is ready.
title: Deployment — DeploymentSpec

instruction: How does the progressDeadlineSeconds field affect a Deployment?
response: The progressDeadlineSeconds field sets the maximum time in seconds for a deployment to make progress before it is considered to be failed. If a deployment does not make progress within this time frame, a condition with a ProgressDeadlineExceeded reason will be surfaced in the deployment status. The default value is 600 seconds.
title: Deployment — DeploymentSpec

instruction: What are the key settings for a RollingUpdate deployment strategy?
response: For a RollingUpdate deployment strategy, key settings include maxSurge and maxUnavailable. maxSurge specifies the maximum number of pods that can be scheduled above the desired number of pods, and maxUnavailable specifies the maximum number of pods that can be unavailable during the update. Both can be specified as absolute numbers or percentages of desired pods.
title: Deployment — DeploymentSpec

instruction: What is the difference between Recreate and RollingUpdate deployment strategies?
response: The Recreate strategy kills all existing pods before creating new ones, while the RollingUpdate strategy replaces the old ReplicaSets with new ones using a rolling update, gradually scaling down the old ReplicaSets and scaling up the new one. The default strategy is RollingUpdate.
title: Deployment — DeploymentSpec

instruction: What are the prerequisites for issuing a certificate for a Kubernetes API client using a CertificateSigningRequest?
response: You need to have a Kubernetes cluster, the kubectl command-line tool configured to communicate with your cluster, and the kubectl, openssl, and base64 utilities.
title: Issue a Certificate for a Kubernetes API Client Using A CertificateSigningRequest — Before you begin

instruction: What is the recommended configuration for the Kubernetes cluster to run this tutorial?
response: It is recommended to run this tutorial on a cluster with at least two nodes that are not acting as control plane hosts.
title: Issue a Certificate for a Kubernetes API Client Using A CertificateSigningRequest — Before you begin

instruction: What alternatives are available if you don't have a Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can create one by using minikube or use one of these Kubernetes playgrounds: iximiuz Labs, Killercoda, KodeKloud.
title: Issue a Certificate for a Kubernetes API Client Using A CertificateSigningRequest — Before you begin

instruction: What authorization mechanisms are assumed to be in use for this tutorial?
response: This page assumes you are using Kubernetes role-based access control (RBAC). If you have alternative or additional security mechanisms around authorization, you need to account for those as well.
title: Issue a Certificate for a Kubernetes API Client Using A CertificateSigningRequest — Before you begin

instruction: What is the purpose of the `kubectl auth reconcile -f` command?
response: The `kubectl auth reconcile -f` command reconciles rules for RBAC role, role binding, cluster role, and cluster role binding objects from a file.
title: GETTING STARTED — reconcile

instruction: What happens to existing roles and bindings when using `kubectl auth reconcile -f`?
response: Existing roles are updated to include the permissions in the input objects and remove extra permissions if `--remove-extra-permissions` is specified. Existing bindings are updated to include the subjects in the input objects and remove extra subjects if `--remove-extra-subjects` is specified.
title: GETTING STARTED — reconcile

instruction: Why is `kubectl auth reconcile -f` preferred over 'apply' for RBAC resources?
response: It is preferred because semantically-aware merging of rules and subjects is done.
title: GETTING STARTED — reconcile

instruction: What are the status conditions used by the kubelet to communicate the state of a Pod resize request?
response: The kubelet uses PodResizePending (with reasons like Infeasible or Deferred) and PodResizeInProgress to communicate the state of the request.
title: Resize CPU and Memory Resources assigned to Pods — Pod Resize Status and Retry Logic

instruction: How are deferred resizes retried?
response: Deferred resizes are retried based on PriorityClass, then QoS class (Guaranteed over Burstable), and finally by the duration they have been deferred.
title: Resize CPU and Memory Resources assigned to Pods — Pod Resize Status and Retry Logic

instruction: How can you track which Pod specification corresponds to the status of the latest processed resize request?
response: You can use the observedGeneration fields to track which Pod specification (metadata.generation) corresponds to the status of the latest processed resize request.
title: Resize CPU and Memory Resources assigned to Pods — Pod Resize Status and Retry Logic

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the CSIStorageCapacity watch API?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: CSIStorageCapacity — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the CSIStorageCapacity list API?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is a server-defined value that clients may use to retrieve the next set of results. If the continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: CSIStorageCapacity — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` with `watch=true` in the CSIStorageCapacity watch API?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event.
title: CSIStorageCapacity — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `resourceVersion` and `resourceVersionMatch` fields interact in the CSIStorageCapacity list API?
response: The `resourceVersion` field sets a constraint on what resource versions a request may be served from. The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls, and it is highly recommended to set `resourceVersionMatch` for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: CSIStorageCapacity — getWatch List

instruction: What happens to default cluster roles and role bindings at API server start-up?
response: At start-up, the API server updates default cluster roles with any missing permissions, and updates default cluster role bindings with any missing subjects.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Auto-reconciliation

instruction: How can I opt out of auto-reconciliation for a default cluster role or role binding?
response: To opt out of auto-reconciliation, set the rbac.authorization.kubernetes.io/autoupdate annotation on a default cluster role or default cluster RoleBinding to false.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Auto-reconciliation

instruction: What are the potential consequences of missing default permissions and subjects in a cluster?
response: Missing default permissions and subjects can result in non-functional clusters.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Auto-reconciliation

instruction: What are the required claims in a JSON Web Token (JWT) payload for Kubernetes authentication?
response: The minimum valid JWT payload must contain the following claims: 'iss' (issuer URL), 'aud' (at least one of the entries in issuer.audiences), 'exp' (token expiration as Unix time), and a username claim (configured in claimMappings.username.claim or claimMappings.username.expression).
title: Authenticating — JSON Web Token authentication

instruction: How does the API server validate an id_token received from an identity provider?
response: The API server validates the id_token by checking if the JWT signature is valid, ensuring the token hasn't expired, performing claim and/or user validation if CEL expressions are configured with AuthenticationConfiguration, and verifying that the user is authorized.
title: Authenticating — JSON Web Token authentication

instruction: What are the required command line arguments to enable JSON Web Token authentication for the API server?
response: The required command line arguments are: --oidc-issuer-url (URL of the provider), --oidc-client-id (a client id that all tokens must be issued for). The --oidc-username-claim and --oidc-username-prefix arguments are optional.
title: Authenticating — JSON Web Token authentication

instruction: How does kubectl send the id_token to the API server for authentication?
response: kubectl sends the id_token in a header called Authorization to the API server, either by using the --token command line argument or by adding it directly to the kubeconfig file.
title: Authenticating — JSON Web Token authentication

instruction: What happens to a Container if it allocates more memory than its limit?
response: The Container becomes a candidate for termination. If it continues to consume memory beyond its limit, it is terminated.
title: Assign Memory Resources to Containers and Pods — Exceed a Container's memory limit

instruction: Why is the Container in the example repeatedly killed and restarted?
response: The Container is killed because it exceeds its memory limit, but it is restarted by the kubelet because it can be restarted.
title: Assign Memory Resources to Containers and Pods — Exceed a Container's memory limit

instruction: How can you verify that a Container was killed due to an out-of-memory condition?
response: You can use the `kubectl get pod` command with the `--output=yaml` option to view detailed information about the Container status, which will show the reason for termination as 'OOMKilled'. Additionally, you can use `kubectl describe nodes` to view a record of the Container being killed due to an out-of-memory condition.
title: Assign Memory Resources to Containers and Pods — Exceed a Container's memory limit

instruction: What is the minimum valid value for the expirationSeconds field in a CertificateSigningRequestSpec?
response: The minimum valid value for expirationSeconds is 600, which corresponds to 10 minutes.
title: CertificateSigningRequest — CertificateSigningRequestSpec

instruction: How are the extra, groups, uid, and username fields in a CertificateSigningRequestSpec populated?
response: The extra, groups, uid, and username fields are populated by the API server on creation and are immutable.
title: CertificateSigningRequest — CertificateSigningRequestSpec

instruction: What are the well-known Kubernetes signers for CertificateSigningRequests?
response: The well-known Kubernetes signers are: 'kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client', 'kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client-kubelet', and 'kubernetes.io/kubelet-serving'.
title: CertificateSigningRequest — CertificateSigningRequestSpec

instruction: What are the typical usages requested for TLS client and serving certificates in a CertificateSigningRequestSpec?
response: Requests for TLS client certificates typically request: 'digital signature', 'key encipherment', 'client auth'. Requests for TLS serving certificates typically request: 'key encipherment', 'digital signature', 'server auth'.
title: CertificateSigningRequest — CertificateSigningRequestSpec

instruction: What is the default behavior of the kubelet regarding registration with the API server?
response: When the kubelet flag --register-node is true (the default), the kubelet will attempt to register itself with the API server.
title: Nodes — Self-registration of Nodes

instruction: How can you specify labels to add when registering a node in the cluster?
response: You can specify labels to add when registering a node in the cluster using the --node-labels flag, which allows you to add labels when registering the node, subject to label restrictions enforced by the NodeRestriction admission plugin.
title: Nodes — Self-registration of Nodes

instruction: What happens if a kubelet is restarted with a new set of --node-labels but the same Node name is used?
response: If a kubelet is restarted with a new set of --node-labels but the same Node name is used, the change will not take effect, as labels are only set (or modified) upon Node registration with the API server. To ensure the change takes effect, the node should be re-registered with the API server.
title: Nodes — Self-registration of Nodes

instruction: How often does kubelet post its node status to the API server?
response: The frequency at which kubelet posts its node status to the API server can be specified using the --node-status-update-frequency flag.
title: Nodes — Self-registration of Nodes

instruction: At which endpoint are PSI metrics exposed as cumulative counters in Prometheus?
response: The PSI metrics are exposed at the /metrics/cadvisor endpoint.
title: Metrics For Kubernetes System Components — kubelet Pressure Stall Information (PSI) metrics

instruction: What do the 'some' and 'full' values in PSI metrics indicate?
response: The 'some' value indicates that at least one task was stalled on a resource, while the 'full' value indicates that all non-idle tasks were stalled simultaneously.
title: Metrics For Kubernetes System Components — kubelet Pressure Stall Information (PSI) metrics

instruction: How can you interpret a high 'some' PSI value alongside a low 'full' PSI value for CPU?
response: A high 'some' value alongside a low 'full' value indicates that while some processes were delayed waiting for CPU time, the container as a whole was still making progress.
title: Metrics For Kubernetes System Components — kubelet Pressure Stall Information (PSI) metrics

instruction: What does a significant difference between 'avg10' and 'avg300' PSI values for a resource indicate?
response: A significant difference between 'avg10' and 'avg300' values suggests a recent surge in resource contention rather than a sustained long-term bottleneck.
title: Metrics For Kubernetes System Components — kubelet Pressure Stall Information (PSI) metrics

instruction: What phases does the kubeadm init command execute to set up the Kubernetes control plane?
response: The kubeadm init command executes the following phases: preflight, certs, kubeconfig, etcd, control-plane, kubelet-start, wait-control-plane, upload-config, upload-certs, mark-control-plane, bootstrap-token, kubelet-finalize, addon, and show-join-command.
title: kubeadm init

instruction: What certificates are generated during the certs phase of kubeadm init?
response: During the certs phase, kubeadm init generates the following certificates: /ca, /apiserver, /apiserver-kubelet-client, /front-proxy-ca, /front-proxy-client, /etcd-ca, /etcd-server, /etcd-peer, /etcd-healthcheck-client, /apiserver-etcd-client, and /sa.
title: kubeadm init

instruction: What kubeconfig files are generated during the kubeconfig phase of kubeadm init?
response: During the kubeconfig phase, kubeadm init generates the following kubeconfig files: /admin, /super-admin, /kubelet, /controller-manager, and /scheduler.
title: kubeadm init

instruction: What is the purpose of the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-eip-allocations annotation in a Kubernetes Service?
response: The service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-eip-allocations annotation is used to configure an AWS Network Load Balancer with a specific set of elastic IP address allocation IDs for a Service of type LoadBalancer.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-eip-allocations (beta)

instruction: What type of Services is the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-eip-allocations annotation relevant for?
response: This annotation is only relevant for Services of type LoadBalancer, where the load balancer is an AWS Network Load Balancer.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-eip-allocations (beta)

instruction: How should the value of the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-eip-allocations annotation be formatted?
response: The value should be a comma-separated list of elastic IP address allocation IDs, for example: "eipalloc-01bcdef23bcdef456,eipalloc-def1234abc4567890"
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-eip-allocations (beta)

instruction: What is the purpose of the `continue` option when deleting a collection of ResourceClaims?
response: The continue option should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It allows clients to retrieve the next set of results from a previous query with identical query parameters.
title: ResourceClaim — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting `dryRun` to `All` when deleting a ResourceClaim?
response: When `dryRun` is set to `All`, all dry run stages will be processed, and modifications will not be persisted.
title: ResourceClaim — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the difference between `orphanDependents` and `propagationPolicy` when deleting a ResourceClaim?
response: Both fields control the deletion of dependent objects. `orphanDependents` is deprecated and should be replaced with `propagationPolicy`. `propagationPolicy` allows for more control over garbage collection, with acceptable values being 'Orphan', 'Background', and 'Foreground'.
title: ResourceClaim — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What happens when `ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential` is set to `true` when deleting a ResourceClaim?
response: When set to `true`, it triggers an unsafe deletion of the resource in case the normal deletion flow fails with a corrupt object error. This may potentially break the cluster if the workload associated with the resource being unsafe-deleted relies on normal deletion flow.
title: ResourceClaim — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is a sidecar container in Kubernetes and how does it differ from a regular init container?
response: A sidecar container in Kubernetes is a special case of init containers that remains running after Pod startup. Unlike regular init containers that only run during Pod startup, sidecar containers continue to run and can be restarted independently.
title: Sidecar Containers — Sidecar containers in Kubernetes

instruction: What is required for a cluster to support sidecar containers with a restartPolicy?
response: The cluster must have the SidecarContainers feature gate enabled. This feature is active by default since Kubernetes v1.29.
title: Sidecar Containers — Sidecar containers in Kubernetes

instruction: Can sidecar containers be restarted without affecting the main application container and other init containers in the same Pod?
response: Yes, sidecar containers are independent from other init containers and from the main application container(s) within the same Pod. They can be started, stopped, or restarted without affecting the main application container and other init containers.
title: Sidecar Containers — Sidecar containers in Kubernetes

instruction: What are the three options for a kubelet to secure its https endpoint for certain features?
response: The kubelet can use: 1) a provided key and certificate via the --tls-private-key-file and --tls-cert-file flags, 2) create a self-signed key and certificate if not provided, or 3) request serving certificates from the cluster server via the CSR API.
title: TLS bootstrapping — Client and serving certificates

instruction: Why can't a client certificate provided by TLS bootstrapping be used as a serving certificate?
response: The client certificate provided by TLS bootstrapping is signed for client auth only, and thus cannot be used as a serving certificate or for server auth.
title: TLS bootstrapping — Client and serving certificates

instruction: How can the server certificate usage of a client certificate provided by TLS bootstrapping be enabled?
response: You can enable its server certificate, at least partially, via certificate rotation.
title: TLS bootstrapping — Client and serving certificates

instruction: How do you apply a custom profile to an ephemeral container when debugging a Pod?
response: You can apply a custom profile to an ephemeral container using the --custom flag with the kubectl debug command.
title: Debug Running Pods — Applying Custom Profile

instruction: What is the purpose of the custom-profile.yaml file in debugging a Pod?
response: The custom-profile.yaml file defines a partial container spec for debugging, including environment variables and security context settings.
title: Debug Running Pods — Applying Custom Profile

instruction: How can you verify that the custom profile has been applied to the ephemeral container?
response: You can use kubectl get pod with jsonpath to verify the environment variables and security context settings of the ephemeral container, for example: kubectl get pod myapp -o jsonpath='{.spec.ephemeralContainers[0].env}' and kubectl get pod myapp -o jsonpath='{.spec.ephemeralContainers[0].securityContext}'
title: Debug Running Pods — Applying Custom Profile

instruction: When should the kubelet static Pod manifest functionality be enabled?
response: Only if required by the node.
title: Kubernetes API Server Bypass Risks — Mitigations

instruction: How should access to the static Pod manifest directory or URL be controlled on a node that uses static Pod functionality?
response: Restrict filesystem access to users who need the access.
title: Kubernetes API Server Bypass Risks — Mitigations

instruction: Why is it important to restrict access to kubelet configuration parameters and files?
response: To prevent an attacker from setting a static Pod path or URL.
title: Kubernetes API Server Bypass Risks — Mitigations

instruction: What should be done with access to directories or web storage locations that host static Pod manifests and kubelet configuration files?
response: Regularly audit and centrally report all access to these locations.
title: Kubernetes API Server Bypass Risks — Mitigations

instruction: How did Kubernetes versions before v1.22 manage credentials for accessing the Kubernetes API?
response: Kubernetes versions before v1.22 automatically created credentials for accessing the Kubernetes API by creating token Secrets that could then be mounted into running Pods.
title: Managing Service Accounts — Manual Secret management for ServiceAccounts

instruction: What is the current method for obtaining API credentials in recent Kubernetes versions, including v1.36?
response: In recent Kubernetes versions, including v1.36, API credentials are obtained directly using the TokenRequest API and are mounted into Pods using a projected volume.
title: Managing Service Accounts — Manual Secret management for ServiceAccounts

instruction: Why might you manually create a Secret to hold a service account token?
response: You might manually create a Secret to hold a service account token if you need a token that never expires.
title: Managing Service Accounts — Manual Secret management for ServiceAccounts

instruction: What is the name of the Deployment created for DNS horizontal autoscaling?
response: The Deployment created for DNS horizontal autoscaling is named 'kube-dns-autoscaler'.
title: Autoscale the DNS Service in a Cluster — Enable DNS horizontal autoscaling

instruction: What image is used for the container in the kube-dns-autoscaler Deployment?
response: The container in the kube-dns-autoscaler Deployment uses the 'registry.k8s.io/cpa/cluster-proportional-autoscaler:1.8.4' image.
title: Autoscale the DNS Service in a Cluster — Enable DNS horizontal autoscaling

instruction: How do you create the Deployment for DNS horizontal autoscaling?
response: To create the Deployment for DNS horizontal autoscaling, you create a file named 'dns-horizontal-autoscaler.yaml' with the specified content and then run the command 'kubectl apply -f dns-horizontal-autoscaler.yaml' in the directory containing the configuration file.
title: Autoscale the DNS Service in a Cluster — Enable DNS horizontal autoscaling

instruction: What needs to be replaced in the dns-horizontal-autoscaler.yaml file before applying it?
response: You need to replace '<SCALE_TARGET>' with your scale target in the dns-horizontal-autoscaler.yaml file before applying it.
title: Autoscale the DNS Service in a Cluster — Enable DNS horizontal autoscaling

instruction: What is the purpose of the `continue` option in the GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/persistentvolumeclaims request?
response: The continue option should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It allows clients to retrieve the next set of results from a previous query with identical query parameters.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — getList

instruction: How does the `resourceVersion` field affect the list of returned objects in the GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/persistentvolumeclaims request?
response: The resourceVersion field sets a constraint on what resource versions a request may be served from. It allows clients to request objects at a specific version or newer. The resourceVersionMatch field determines how resourceVersion is applied to list calls.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — getList

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/persistentvolumeclaims request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` are set, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the `limit` field in the GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/persistentvolumeclaims request?
response: The limit field is a maximum number of responses to return for a list call. If more items exist, the server will set the `continue` field on the list metadata to a value that can be used to retrieve the next set of results.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — getList

instruction: What is the HTTP method and endpoint to retrieve a Role in Kubernetes?
response: The HTTP method to retrieve a Role is GET, and the endpoint is /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/roles/{name}.
title: Role — getRead

instruction: What query parameter can be used to pretty-print the output when retrieving a Role?
response: The query parameter to pretty-print the output is 'pretty'. If set to 'true', the output will be pretty printed. It defaults to 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool like curl or wget.
title: Role — getRead

instruction: What is the expected HTTP status code when successfully retrieving a Role?
response: The expected HTTP status code when successfully retrieving a Role is 200, with a status description of 'OK'.
title: Role — getRead

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'beginRefreshAt' field in PodCertificateRequestStatus?
response: The 'beginRefreshAt' field is the time at which the kubelet should begin trying to refresh the certificate. This field is only a hint and kubelet may start refreshing before or after this time if necessary.
title: PodCertificateRequest — PodCertificateRequestStatus

instruction: What are the validation requirements for the 'certificateChain' field?
response: The 'certificateChain' field must consist of one or more PEM-formatted certificates. Each entry must be a valid PEM-wrapped, DER-encoded ASN.1 Certificate as described in section 4 of RFC5280.
title: PodCertificateRequest — PodCertificateRequestStatus

instruction: How is the 'conditions' field used in PodCertificateRequestStatus?
response: The 'conditions' field is used to apply conditions to the request. The types 'Issued', 'Denied', and 'Failed' have special handling. At most one of these conditions may be present, and they must have status 'True'.
title: PodCertificateRequest — PodCertificateRequestStatus

instruction: What is the relationship between 'notAfter', 'notBefore', and the leaf certificate in 'certificateChain'?
response: The 'notAfter' and 'notBefore' fields must be the same as the notAfter and notBefore values in the leaf certificate in 'certificateChain'. The signer must set these fields at the same time it sets 'certificateChain'.
title: PodCertificateRequest — PodCertificateRequestStatus

instruction: What happens to the /var/lib/kubelet directory when performing a kubeadm reset if it is being mounted?
response: Performing a kubeadm reset will effectively unmount the /var/lib/kubelet directory.
title: Troubleshooting kubeadm — kubeadm resetunmounts/var/lib/kubelet

instruction: How can you resolve the issue with /var/lib/kubelet being unmounted after a kubeadm reset?
response: To workaround the issue, re-mount the /var/lib/kubelet directory after performing the kubeadm reset operation.
title: Troubleshooting kubeadm — kubeadm resetunmounts/var/lib/kubelet

instruction: In what version of kubeadm was the issue with kubeadm reset unmounting /var/lib/kubelet introduced and fixed?
response: The issue was introduced in kubeadm 1.15 and fixed in kubeadm 1.20.
title: Troubleshooting kubeadm — kubeadm resetunmounts/var/lib/kubelet

instruction: Why does the metrics-server fail to use TLS with the kubelet in a kubeadm cluster by default?
response: The metrics-server fails to use TLS with the kubelet because kubeadm deploys a self-signed serving certificate for the kubelet, causing errors such as 'x509: certificate signed by unknown authority' or 'x509: certificate is valid for IP-foo not IP-bar'.
title: Troubleshooting kubeadm — kubeadm resetunmounts/var/lib/kubelet

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubeadm init phase upload-config command?
response: The purpose of the kubeadm init phase upload-config command is to upload the kubeadm configuration to your cluster.
title: kubeadm init phase — kubeadm init phase upload-config

instruction: What alternative command can be used to upload the kubeadm configuration?
