response: Kubernetes creates DNS records for Services, allowing clients to contact Services with consistent DNS names instead of IP addresses.
title: DNS for Services and Pods

instruction: What does kubelet configure on Pods to enable DNS lookups?
response: kubelet configures Pods' DNS so that running containers can look up Services by name rather than IP.
title: DNS for Services and Pods

instruction: What is included in a client Pod's DNS search list by default?
response: By default, a client Pod's DNS search list includes the Pod's own namespace and the cluster's default domain.
title: DNS for Services and Pods

instruction: What is the purpose of the init container 'init-mysql' in the StatefulSet?
response: The init container 'init-mysql' generates the MySQL server-id from the pod ordinal index, creates a server-id.cnf file, and copies the appropriate conf.d files from the config map to the emptyDir.
title: Run a Replicated Stateful Application — Create the StatefulSet

instruction: What is the role of the 'xtrabackup' container in the StatefulSet?
response: The 'xtrabackup' container is used to create backups of the MySQL data. It listens on port 3307 and sends backups when requested by peers.
title: Run a Replicated Stateful Application — Create the StatefulSet

instruction: What is the purpose of the `pretty` parameter in the CertificateSigningRequest getList API?
response: The `pretty` parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If set to `true`, the output will be pretty-printed. Defaults to `false` unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: CertificateSigningRequest — getList

instruction: How does the `continue` parameter work in the CertificateSigningRequest getList API?
response: The `continue` parameter should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is a server-defined value that clients may only use with identical query parameters (except for the value of continue). If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: CertificateSigningRequest — getList

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the CertificateSigningRequest getList API?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent.
title: CertificateSigningRequest — getList

instruction: How does the `resourceVersion` and `resourceVersionMatch` parameters interact in the CertificateSigningRequest getList API?
response: The `resourceVersion` parameter sets a constraint on what resource versions a request may be served from. The `resourceVersionMatch` parameter determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: CertificateSigningRequest — getList

instruction: How can I configure kubectl to access the Kubernetes API server through a SOCKS5 proxy?
response: You can configure kubectl to access the Kubernetes API server through a SOCKS5 proxy by setting the HTTPS_PROXY environment variable or by specifying the proxy-url attribute in the kubeconfig file.
title: Use a SOCKS5 Proxy to Access the Kubernetes API — Client configuration

instruction: What is the difference between setting the HTTPS_PROXY environment variable and specifying the proxy-url attribute in the kubeconfig file?
response: When using proxy-url, the proxy is used only for the relevant kubectl context, whereas the environment variable will affect all contexts.
title: Use a SOCKS5 Proxy to Access the Kubernetes API — Client configuration

instruction: How can I prevent DNS leakage when using a SOCKS5 proxy to access the Kubernetes API server?
response: You can prevent DNS leakage by using the socks5h protocol name instead of socks5. This will ask the proxy server to resolve the Kubernetes API server domain name instead of resolving it on the system running kubectl.
title: Use a SOCKS5 Proxy to Access the Kubernetes API — Client configuration

instruction: Can I use the HTTPS_PROXY environment variable with kubectl exec?
response: Before kubectl 1.24, kubectl exec did not work when using a SOCKS proxy, but most other kubectl commands did. However, it is recommended to use the HTTPS_PROXY environment variable with kubectl, as it is also used by other programs that support SOCKS, such as curl.
title: Use a SOCKS5 Proxy to Access the Kubernetes API — Client configuration

instruction: How can I verify that newly written data is encrypted in etcd?
response: You can use the etcdctl command line program to retrieve the contents of your secret data and verify that it is encrypted. Specifically, look for the prefix 'k8s:enc:aescbc:v1:' which indicates that the aescbc provider has encrypted the resulting data.
title: Encrypting Confidential Data at Rest — Verify that newly written data is encrypted

instruction: What command can I use to retrieve a Secret from etcd and verify its encryption?
response: Use the command 'ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl get /registry/secrets/default/secret1 [...] | hexdump -C' where [...] must be the additional arguments for connecting to the etcd server.
title: Encrypting Confidential Data at Rest — Verify that newly written data is encrypted

instruction: How can I verify that a Secret is correctly decrypted when retrieved via the API?
response: Use the command 'kubectl get secret secret1 -n default -o yaml' and verify that the output contains the expected data, such as 'mykey: bXlkYXRh', with contents encoded using base64.
title: Encrypting Confidential Data at Rest — Verify that newly written data is encrypted

instruction: What is the purpose of the CredentialProviderConfig in Kubelet configuration?
response: The CredentialProviderConfig contains information about each exec credential provider, which Kubelet reads from disk to enable each provider as specified by the CredentialProvider type.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1alpha1) — Resource Types

instruction: How does Kubelet handle multiple credential providers matching against a single image?
response: If multiple providers match against a single image, credentials from all providers will be returned to the kubelet. The results are combined, and if providers return overlapping auth keys, the value from the provider earlier in the list is attempted first.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1alpha1) — Resource Types

instruction: What is the significance of the order of credential providers in the CredentialProviderConfig?
response: The order of credential providers in the list matters because if multiple providers return overlapping auth keys, the value from the provider earlier in the list is attempted first.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1alpha1) — Resource Types

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'items' field in a ClusterRoleList object?
response: The 'items' field in a ClusterRoleList object is a list of ClusterRoles.
title: ClusterRole — ClusterRoleList

instruction: What is the significance of the 'apiVersion' field in a ClusterRoleList object?
response: The 'apiVersion' field in a ClusterRoleList object defines the versioned schema of this representation of an object. Servers should convert recognized schemas to the latest internal value, and may reject unrecognized values.
title: ClusterRole — ClusterRoleList

instruction: What is the 'kind' field in a ClusterRoleList object used for?
response: The 'kind' field in a ClusterRoleList object is a string value representing the REST resource this object represents. Servers may infer this from the endpoint the client submits requests to. It cannot be updated and is in CamelCase.
title: ClusterRole — ClusterRoleList

instruction: What are the Kubernetes components that support enabling native histograms via the NativeHistograms feature gate?
response: The components that support enabling native histograms are kube-apiserver, kube-controller-manager, kube-scheduler, kubelet, and kube-proxy.
title: Native Histogram Support for Kubernetes Metrics — Enabling native histograms

instruction: How do you enable native histograms on a Kubernetes component?
response: To enable native histograms on a Kubernetes component, you need to use the --feature-gates flag with NativeHistograms=true. For example: --feature-gates=NativeHistograms=true.
title: Native Histogram Support for Kubernetes Metrics — Enabling native histograms

instruction: Is it possible to enable or disable the NativeHistograms feature gate on a per-component basis?
response: Yes, each component's metrics are independent, and you can enable or disable the feature gate per component.
title: Native Histogram Support for Kubernetes Metrics — Enabling native histograms

instruction: Why is external traffic not equally load balanced across different Pods when using an external load balancer?
response: The external load balancer is unaware of the number of Pods on each node that are used as a target, and load balancing services from some cloud providers do not let you configure different weights for each target.
title: Create an External Load Balancer — Caveats and limitations when preserving source IPs

instruction: Under what conditions can a fairly close-to-equal distribution of external traffic be expected across Pods?
response: When NumServicePods << NumNodes or NumServicePods >> NumNodes, a fairly close-to-equal distribution will be seen, even without weights.
title: Create an External Load Balancer — Caveats and limitations when preserving source IPs

instruction: How does internal pod-to-pod traffic behave when using an external load balancer?
response: Internal pod to pod traffic should behave similar to ClusterIP services, with equal probability across all pods.
title: Create an External Load Balancer — Caveats and limitations when preserving source IPs

instruction: What is the purpose of the ClusterRole named 'dra-multinode-status-updater'?
response: The ClusterRole 'dra-multinode-status-updater' is used to grant permissions for updating the status of resource claims and drivers in a multi-node environment, specifically for Dynamic Resource Allocation.
title: Hardening Guide - Dynamic Resource Allocation — Multi-node status controller permissions

instruction: What resources and verbs are allowed by the 'dra-multinode-status-updater' ClusterRole for the 'resource.k8s.io' API group?
response: For the 'resource.k8s.io' API group, the ClusterRole allows 'get', 'patch', and 'update' verbs on 'resourceclaims/status' resources, and 'arbitrary-node:patch' and 'arbitrary-node:update' verbs on 'resourceclaims/driver' resources with the specific resource name 'dra.example.com'.
title: Hardening Guide - Dynamic Resource Allocation — Multi-node status controller permissions

instruction: How do I stop the mysql-client-loop pod?
response: You can stop the mysql-client-loop pod by pressing Ctrl+C in its terminal, or by running kubectl delete pod mysql-client-loop --now from another terminal.
title: Run a Replicated Stateful Application — Cleaning up

instruction: What happens when I delete a StatefulSet?
response: When you delete a StatefulSet, it begins terminating the Pods associated with it.
title: Run a Replicated Stateful Application — Cleaning up

instruction: How do I verify that Pods have terminated?
response: You can verify that Pods have terminated by running kubectl get pods -l app=mysql. The Pods have terminated when this command returns 'No resources found'.
title: Run a Replicated Stateful Application — Cleaning up

instruction: What happens to PersistentVolumes when I delete PersistentVolumeClaims?
response: If you used a dynamic provisioner, it automatically deletes the PersistentVolumes when it sees that you deleted the PersistentVolumeClaims. Some dynamic provisioners also release the underlying resources upon deleting the PersistentVolumes.
title: Run a Replicated Stateful Application — Cleaning up

instruction: What is the purpose of the --allow-missing-template-keys option in kubectl set serviceaccount?
response: The --allow-missing-template-keys option allows ignoring any errors in templates when a field or map key is missing in the template. This option only applies to golang and jsonpath output formats.
title: kubectl set serviceaccount — Options

instruction: What are the possible values for the --dry-run option in kubectl set serviceaccount?
response: The --dry-run option can take one of three values: 'none', 'server', or 'client'. The 'client' strategy only prints the object that would be sent without sending it, while the 'server' strategy submits a server-side request without persisting the resource.
title: kubectl set serviceaccount — Options

instruction: How can you specify the output format when using kubectl set serviceaccount?
response: You can specify the output format using the -o or --output option, followed by one of the supported formats: json, yaml, kyaml, name, go-template, go-template-file, template, templatefile, jsonpath, jsonpath-as-json, or jsonpath-file.
title: kubectl set serviceaccount — Options

instruction: What is the effect of using the --local option with kubectl set serviceaccount?
response: When the --local option is set to true, the set serviceaccount command will not contact the api-server but instead run locally.
title: kubectl set serviceaccount — Options

instruction: What is the default path where Kubernetes retrieves termination messages from a container?
response: The default path is /dev/termination-log.
title: Determine the Reason for Pod Failure — Customizing the termination message

instruction: Can the termination message path be changed after a Pod is launched?
response: No, you cannot set the termination message path after a Pod is launched.
title: Determine the Reason for Pod Failure — Customizing the termination message

instruction: What happens if the termination message file is empty and the container exited with an error, when terminationMessagePolicy is set to FallbackToLogsOnError?
response: Kubernetes uses the last chunk of container log output, limited to 2048 bytes or 80 lines, whichever is smaller.
title: Determine the Reason for Pod Failure — Customizing the termination message

instruction: What is the maximum length of a termination message that the kubelet can handle?
response: The kubelet truncates messages that are longer than 4096 bytes.
title: Determine the Reason for Pod Failure — Customizing the termination message

instruction: What happens to a PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) when a Pod with ephemeral volume storage is deleted?
response: When a Pod with ephemeral volume storage is deleted, the Kubernetes garbage collector deletes the PVC, which usually triggers deletion of the volume because the default reclaim policy of storage classes is to delete volumes.
title: Ephemeral Volumes — Lifecycle and PersistentVolumeClaim

instruction: What is the recommended volume binding mode for generic ephemeral volumes and why?
response: The recommended volume binding mode for generic ephemeral volumes is WaitForFirstConsumer. This is because with immediate binding, the scheduler is forced to select a node that has access to the volume once it is available, whereas with WaitForFirstConsumer, the scheduler is free to choose a suitable node for the Pod.
title: Ephemeral Volumes — Lifecycle and PersistentVolumeClaim

instruction: Can PVCs created for ephemeral volume storage be used like regular PVCs?
response: Yes, while these PVCs exist, they can be used like any other PVC. In particular, they can be referenced as a data source in volume cloning or snapshotting, and the PVC object holds the current status of the volume.
title: Ephemeral Volumes — Lifecycle and PersistentVolumeClaim

instruction: What is the primary responsibility of the Control Plane in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The Control Plane is responsible for managing the cluster, coordinating all activities such as scheduling applications, maintaining applications' desired state, scaling applications, and rolling out new updates.
title: Using Minikube to Create a Cluster — Cluster Diagram

instruction: What is the minimum number of nodes recommended for a Kubernetes cluster handling production traffic?
response: A Kubernetes cluster that handles production traffic should have a minimum of three nodes to ensure redundancy and prevent loss of an etcd member and a control plane instance if one node goes down.
title: Using Minikube to Create a Cluster — Cluster Diagram

instruction: What is Minikube and what is its purpose in Kubernetes development?
response: Minikube is a lightweight Kubernetes implementation that creates a VM on your local machine and deploys a simple cluster containing only one node, allowing you to get started with Kubernetes development.
title: Using Minikube to Create a Cluster — Cluster Diagram

instruction: How do node-level components, such as the kubelet, communicate with the control plane in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Node-level components, such as the kubelet, communicate with the control plane using the Kubernetes API, which the control plane exposes.
title: Using Minikube to Create a Cluster — Cluster Diagram

instruction: What is the purpose of the CSIStorageCapacity object in Kubernetes?
response: CSIStorageCapacity stores the result of one CSI GetCapacity call, describing the available capacity in a particular topology segment for a given StorageClass.
title: CSIStorageCapacity

instruction: How does the kube-scheduler use CSIStorageCapacity objects when a CSI driver opts into capacity-aware scheduling?
response: The scheduler compares the MaximumVolumeSize against the requested size of pending volumes to filter out unsuitable nodes. If MaximumVolumeSize is unset, it falls back to a comparison against the less precise Capacity. If that is also unset, the scheduler assumes that capacity is insufficient and tries some other node.
title: CSIStorageCapacity

instruction: What are the implications of an empty or unset capacity field in a CSIStorageCapacity object?
response: The following three cases all imply that no capacity is available for a certain combination: no object exists with suitable topology and storage class name, such an object exists but the capacity is unset, or such an object exists but the capacity is zero.
title: CSIStorageCapacity

instruction: What are the requirements for the storageClassName field in a CSIStorageCapacity object?
response: storageClassName represents the name of the StorageClass that the reported capacity applies to. It must meet the same requirements as the name of a StorageClass object (non-empty, DNS subdomain).
title: CSIStorageCapacity

instruction: What is required for a StorageClass to support volume expansion?
response: The StorageClass must have the field 'allowVolumeExpansion' set to true.
title: Storage Classes — Volume expansion

instruction: Which types of volumes support volume expansion in Kubernetes, and what are the required Kubernetes versions?
response: The following types of volumes support volume expansion: Azure File (1.11), CSI (1.24), FlexVolume (1.13), Portworx (1.11), and rbd (1.11).
title: Storage Classes — Volume expansion

instruction: How do you resize a volume in Kubernetes?
response: You can resize a volume by editing the corresponding PVC object and requesting a new, larger amount of storage.
title: Storage Classes — Volume expansion

instruction: What happens when a user tries to delete a PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) that is actively being used by a Pod?
response: The PVC is not removed immediately. Its removal is postponed until it is no longer actively used by any Pods.
title: Persistent Volumes — Storage Object in Use Protection

instruction: How can you verify that a PVC or PV is protected from immediate deletion?
response: You can verify that a PVC or PV is protected by checking its status and Finalizers list. A protected PVC will have a status of 'Terminating' and include 'kubernetes.io/pvc-protection' in its Finalizers list. A protected PV will have a status of 'Terminating' and include 'kubernetes.io/pv-protection' in its Finalizers list.
title: Persistent Volumes — Storage Object in Use Protection

instruction: What is the purpose of the Storage Object in Use Protection feature in Kubernetes?
response: The purpose of the Storage Object in Use Protection feature is to ensure that PersistentVolumeClaims (PVCs) in active use by a Pod and PersistentVolume (PVs) that are bound to PVCs are not removed from the system, which could result in data loss.
title: Persistent Volumes — Storage Object in Use Protection

instruction: What is the purpose of a PersistentVolumeClaimVolumeSource in a Kubernetes pod?
response: A PersistentVolumeClaimVolumeSource references the user's PVC in the same namespace, finds the bound PV, and mounts that volume for the pod.
title: Pod — PersistentVolumeClaimVolumeSource

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'readOnly' to true in a PersistentVolumeClaimVolumeSource?
response: Setting 'readOnly' to true will force the ReadOnly setting in VolumeMounts, making the volume read-only for the pod.
title: Pod — PersistentVolumeClaimVolumeSource

instruction: What information is required to reference a PersistentVolumeClaim in a PersistentVolumeClaimVolumeSource?
response: The 'claimName' field, which is the name of a PersistentVolumeClaim in the same namespace as the pod using this volume.
title: Pod — PersistentVolumeClaimVolumeSource

instruction: How can I debug Kubernetes nodes?
response: You can debug Kubernetes nodes using crictl.
title: Create static Pods — What's next

instruction: What is crictl and where can I learn more about it?
response: crictl is a tool for debugging Kubernetes nodes. You can learn more about crictl by visiting the relevant documentation.
title: Create static Pods — What's next

instruction: How can I report a problem or suggest an improvement for Kubernetes documentation?
response: You can report a problem or suggest an improvement by opening an issue in the GitHub Repository.
title: Create static Pods — What's next

instruction: Where can I ask specific, answerable questions about using Kubernetes?
response: You can ask specific, answerable questions about using Kubernetes on Stack Overflow.
title: Create static Pods — What's next

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /apis/certificates.k8s.io/v1beta1/watch/clustertrustbundles request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: ClusterTrustBundle — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /apis/certificates.k8s.io/v1beta1/watch/clustertrustbundles request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. The server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error if the continue value is no longer valid. Clients may use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters to retrieve the next set of results.
title: ClusterTrustBundle — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /apis/certificates.k8s.io/v1beta1/watch/clustertrustbundles request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event.
title: ClusterTrustBundle — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `resourceVersionMatch` field interact with `resourceVersion` in the GET /apis/certificates.k8s.io/v1beta1/watch/clustertrustbundles request?
response: When `resourceVersionMatch` is set, it determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: ClusterTrustBundle — getWatch List

instruction: How does Kubernetes distribute Pods for workload resources across nodes in a cluster?
response: Kubernetes automatically spreads the Pods for workload resources, such as Deployment or StatefulSet, across different nodes in a cluster to reduce the impact of failures.
title: Running in multiple zones — Node behavior

instruction: How do node labels help in controlling Pod distribution across a cluster?
response: Node labels, which include zone information, can be used in conjunction with Pod topology spread constraints to control how Pods are spread across a cluster among fault domains like regions, zones, and specific nodes.
title: Running in multiple zones — Node behavior

instruction: What is the purpose of using Pod topology spread constraints?
response: Pod topology spread constraints provide hints to the scheduler to place Pods for better expected availability, reducing the risk that a correlated failure affects the whole workload.
title: Running in multiple zones — Node behavior

instruction: Can you declaratively define constraints for spreading Pods across zones?
response: Yes, you can declaratively define constraints, for example, to ensure that 3 replicas of a StatefulSet are all running in different zones to each other, without explicitly defining which availability zones are in use for each workload.
title: Running in multiple zones — Node behavior

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /apis/storage.k8s.io/v1/watch/volumeattributesclasses request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: VolumeAttributesClass — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /apis/storage.k8s.io/v1/watch/volumeattributesclasses request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. The server may reject a continue value it does not recognize. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token.
title: VolumeAttributesClass — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /apis/storage.k8s.io/v1/watch/volumeattributesclasses request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent.
title: VolumeAttributesClass — getWatch List

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersionMatch` field in the GET /apis/storage.k8s.io/v1/watch/volumeattributesclasses request?
response: The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: VolumeAttributesClass — getWatch List

instruction: How can I create a secret for a private Docker registry?
response: You can create a secret for a private Docker registry using the command `kubectl create secret docker-registry`.
title: Pull an Image from a Private Registry — What's next

instruction: Where can I find more information about adding image pull secrets to a service account?
response: You can learn more about adding image pull secrets to a service account by reading the relevant documentation.
title: Pull an Image from a Private Registry — What's next

instruction: What field in a Pod's container definitions is used for image pull secrets?
response: The field used for image pull secrets in a Pod's container definitions is `imagePullSecrets`.
title: Pull an Image from a Private Registry — What's next

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in a GET request to watch a CronJob?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers may ignore this flag if they do not implement bookmarks, and clients should not assume bookmarks are returned at any specific interval.
title: CronJob — getWatch

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in a GET request to watch a CronJob?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` are set, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event.
title: CronJob — getWatch

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersionMatch` field in a GET request to list or watch a CronJob?
response: The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended to set `resourceVersionMatch` for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: CronJob — getWatch

instruction: What conditions must a Pod meet to be assigned a QoS class of Burstable?
response: A Pod is given a QoS class of Burstable if it does not meet the criteria for QoS class Guaranteed and at least one Container in the Pod has a memory or CPU request or limit.
title: Configure Quality of Service for Pods — Create a Pod that gets assigned a QoS class of Burstable

instruction: How can I verify that a Pod has been assigned a QoS class of Burstable?
response: You can verify that a Pod has been assigned a QoS class of Burstable by viewing detailed information about the Pod using the command `kubectl get pod <pod-name> --namespace <namespace> --output=yaml`, and checking the `status.qosClass` field in the output.
title: Configure Quality of Service for Pods — Create a Pod that gets assigned a QoS class of Burstable

instruction: What is the effect of creating a Pod with a Container that has both a memory limit and a memory request on the QoS class of the Pod?
response: If a Pod has a Container with both a memory limit and a memory request, and it does not meet the criteria for QoS class Guaranteed, Kubernetes will assign the Pod a QoS class of Burstable.
title: Configure Quality of Service for Pods — Create a Pod that gets assigned a QoS class of Burstable

instruction: What is the role of a Container Runtime in the networking context of Kubernetes?
response: A Container Runtime, in the networking context, is a daemon on a node configured to provide CRI Services for kubelet. It must be configured to load the CNI plugins required to implement the Kubernetes network model.
title: Network Plugins — Installation

instruction: What changes were made to kubelet's management of CNI plugins in Kubernetes 1.24?
response: Prior to Kubernetes 1.24, kubelet could manage CNI plugins using the cni-bin-dir and network-plugin command-line parameters. These parameters were removed in Kubernetes 1.24, and management of CNI plugins is no longer in scope for kubelet.
title: Network Plugins — Installation

instruction: Where can I find specific information about managing CNI plugins for a Container Runtime?
response: For specific information about how a Container Runtime manages CNI plugins, see the documentation for that Container Runtime, such as containerd or CRI-O.
title: Network Plugins — Installation

instruction: What should I do if I encounter issues with CNI plugins after the removal of dockershim?
response: If you are facing issues following the removal of dockershim, see Troubleshooting CNI plugin-related errors for assistance.
title: Network Plugins — Installation

instruction: What is the purpose of a liveness probe in a Kubernetes pod?
response: Liveness probes determine when to restart a container, for example, to catch a deadlock where an application is running but unable to make progress.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Liveness probe

instruction: What happens when a container fails its liveness probe more times than the configured tolerance?
response: The kubelet restarts that container.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Liveness probe

instruction: Do liveness probes wait for readiness probes to succeed?
response: No, liveness probes do not wait for readiness probes to succeed. You can define initialDelaySeconds or use a startup probe to wait before executing a liveness probe.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Liveness probe

instruction: What happens when a PersistentVolumeClaim is created without a specific storage class requested?
response: The DefaultStorageClass admission controller automatically adds a default storage class to the PersistentVolumeClaim.
title: Admission Control in Kubernetes — DefaultStorageClass

instruction: What is the behavior of the DefaultStorageClass admission controller when multiple storage classes are marked as default?
response: When more than one storage class is marked as default, Kubernetes uses the most recently created default StorageClass for PersistentVolumeClaims with no storageClassName set.
title: Admission Control in Kubernetes — DefaultStorageClass

instruction: Does the DefaultStorageClass admission controller act on updates to PersistentVolumeClaims?
response: No, the DefaultStorageClass admission controller ignores any PersistentVolumeClaim updates; it acts only on creation.
title: Admission Control in Kubernetes — DefaultStorageClass

instruction: What happens to Pods managed by a ReplicaSet if they are deleted or terminated?
response: A ReplicaSet replaces Pods that are deleted or terminated for any reason.
title: ReplicaSet — Bare Pods

instruction: Why is it recommended to use a ReplicaSet even for applications that require only a single Pod?
response: It is recommended to use a ReplicaSet even for applications that require only a single Pod because it replaces Pods that are deleted or terminated, similar to a process supervisor.
title: ReplicaSet — Bare Pods

instruction: Who is responsible for local container restarts in a ReplicaSet?
response: A ReplicaSet delegates local container restarts to some agent on the node such as Kubelet.
title: ReplicaSet — Bare Pods

instruction: How do you verify that feature gates are active after configuring them?
response: To verify that feature gates are active after configuring them, you can use methods that apply to kubeadm clusters where control plane components run as static pods. The exact method is not specified, but it implies that there are cluster-specific methods to verify the status of feature gates.
title: Enable Or Disable Feature Gates — Configure multiple feature gates

instruction: What is a Resource backend in the context of a Kubernetes Ingress object?
response: A Resource backend is an ObjectRef to another Kubernetes resource within the same namespace as the Ingress object.
title: Ingress — Resource backends

instruction: Can both Service and Resource backends be specified in the same Ingress object?
response: No, a Resource backend is a mutually exclusive setting with Service, and will fail validation if both are specified.
title: Ingress — Resource backends

instruction: How can you verify the configuration of an Ingress object with a Resource backend?
response: You can view it with the command: kubectl describe ingress <ingress-object-name>. For example, kubectl describe ingress ingress-resource-backend.
title: Ingress — Resource backends

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'apiGroup', 'kind', and 'name' fields in a Resource backend configuration?
response: These fields are used to reference another Kubernetes resource. 'apiGroup' specifies the API group of the resource, 'kind' specifies the kind of the resource, and 'name' specifies the name of the resource.
title: Ingress — Resource backends

instruction: What is the purpose of the `pretty` parameter in the StorageClass delete collection request?
response: The `pretty` parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If set to `true`, the output will be pretty-printed. Defaults to `false` unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: StorageClass — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: How does the `continue` parameter work in the StorageClass delete collection request?
response: The `continue` parameter should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is a server-defined value that clients may only use with identical query parameters (except for the value of continue). If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: StorageClass — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting `dryRun` to `All` in the StorageClass delete collection request?
response: When `dryRun` is set to `All`, all dry run stages will be processed. This indicates that modifications should not be persisted.
title: StorageClass — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the `propagationPolicy` parameter in the StorageClass delete collection request?
response: The `propagationPolicy` parameter determines whether and how garbage collection will be performed. Acceptable values are: 'Orphan', 'Background', and 'Foreground'. This field or `OrphanDependents` may be set, but not both.
title: StorageClass — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: How do I create a namespace to isolate resources for a Quality of Service (QoS) example?
response: You can create a namespace using the command `kubectl create namespace qos-example`.
title: Configure Quality of Service for Pods — Create a namespace

instruction: What are the requirements for a Pod to be given a QoS class of Guaranteed?
response: For a Pod to be given a QoS class of Guaranteed, every Container in the Pod must have a memory limit and a memory request, and the memory limit must equal the memory request. Additionally, every Container in the Pod must have a CPU limit and a CPU request, and the CPU limit must equal the CPU request.
title: Configure Quality of Service for Pods — Create a namespace

instruction: How can I verify that a Pod has been assigned a QoS class of Guaranteed?
response: You can verify that a Pod has been assigned a QoS class of Guaranteed by viewing its detailed information using the command `kubectl get pod <pod-name> --namespace <namespace> --output=yaml`, and checking the `status.qosClass` field in the output.
title: Configure Quality of Service for Pods — Create a namespace

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in a GET request to watch a PersistentVolumeClaim?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers may ignore this flag if they do not implement bookmarks, and clients should not assume bookmarks are returned at any specific interval.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — getWatch

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in a list call, and what is its relationship with the `limit` field?
response: The `continue` field is used to retrieve more results from the server. When a list call returns a `continue` field, clients can use it to retrieve the next set of results. The `limit` field sets a maximum number of responses to return for a list call. If more items exist, the server will set the `continue` field on the list metadata. The `continue` field is not supported when watch is true.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — getWatch

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` in a watch request, and what are the requirements for using this field?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. The `resourceVersionMatch` option must also be set. The `resourceVersionMatch` option determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — getWatch

instruction: Where should the credential provider plugin binary be stored on each node in the cluster?
response: The credential provider plugin binary should be stored in a known directory on every node in the cluster.
title: Configure a kubelet image credential provider — Installing Plugins on Nodes

instruction: What two flags are required to be set on the kubelet to use a credential provider plugin?
response: The two flags required to be set on the kubelet are --image-credential-provider-config and --image-credential-provider-bin-dir.
title: Configure a kubelet image credential provider — Installing Plugins on Nodes

instruction: What is the purpose of the --image-credential-provider-config flag?
response: The --image-credential-provider-config flag specifies the path to the credential provider plugin config file.
title: Configure a kubelet image credential provider — Installing Plugins on Nodes

instruction: What is the purpose of the --image-credential-provider-bin-dir flag?
response: The --image-credential-provider-bin-dir flag specifies the path to the directory where credential provider plugin binaries are located.
title: Configure a kubelet image credential provider — Installing Plugins on Nodes

instruction: What is the command to convert a Docker Compose file to Kubernetes resources using kompose?
response: The command to convert a Docker Compose file to Kubernetes resources using kompose is `kompose --file <docker-compose-file> convert`.
title: Translate a Docker Compose File to Kubernetes Resources — kompose convert

instruction: What happens when multiple Docker Compose files are provided to kompose convert?
response: When multiple Docker Compose files are provided, the configuration is merged. Any common configuration will be overridden by subsequent files.
title: Translate a Docker Compose File to Kubernetes Resources — kompose convert

instruction: How can you specify multiple Docker Compose files for kompose convert?
response: You can specify multiple Docker Compose files using the `-f` flag, like this: `kompose -f <file1> -f <file2> convert`.
title: Translate a Docker Compose File to Kubernetes Resources — kompose convert

instruction: What is the effect of an unsupported key in a Docker Compose file when using kompose convert?
response: If an unsupported key is encountered in a Docker Compose file, kompose will print a warning message indicating that the key is being ignored, and then continue with the conversion process.
title: Translate a Docker Compose File to Kubernetes Resources — kompose convert

instruction: What is the purpose of the sidecar container in the provided Deployment?
response: The sidecar container, based on Alpine Linux, acts as a helper container. It writes a file in HTML that has its content based on a ConfigMap. The content includes the current date and the preferred color from the ConfigMap.
title: Updating Configuration via a ConfigMap — Update configuration via a ConfigMap in a Pod possessing a sidecar container

instruction: What happens to the output of the service URL after editing the ConfigMap 'color' to change the value of key 'color' from 'blue' to 'green'?
response: After editing the ConfigMap 'color' to change the value of key 'color' from 'blue' to 'green', the output of the service URL will change to reflect the new color value. The output will initially still show 'blue' for a short period, then switch to showing 'green'.
title: Updating Configuration via a ConfigMap — Update configuration via a ConfigMap in a Pod possessing a sidecar container

instruction: What is the purpose of the `pretty` parameter in the `getList` API for DeviceTaintRule?
response: The `pretty` parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If set to `true`, the output will be formatted for readability. Defaults to `false` unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool.
title: DeviceTaintRule — getList

instruction: How does the `continue` parameter work in the `getList` API for DeviceTaintRule?
response: The `continue` parameter is used to retrieve more results from the server. It should be set to the value provided by the server in the `continue` field of the list metadata. The server may reject a continue value it does not recognize. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: DeviceTaintRule — getList

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents` to `true` in the `getList` API for DeviceTaintRule?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. A synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent once all such events have been sent, reporting the ResourceVersion corresponding to the set of objects.
title: DeviceTaintRule — getList

instruction: What is the default CPU management policy used by the kubelet?
response: The kubelet uses CFS quota to enforce pod CPU limits by default.
title: Resource managers — CPU manager

instruction: What are the two available CPU management policies provided by the CPU Manager?
response: The two available policies are 'none' and 'static'. The 'none' policy provides no affinity beyond what the OS scheduler does automatically, while the 'static' policy allows containers in Guaranteed pods with integer CPU requests to access exclusive CPUs on the node.
title: Resource managers — CPU manager

instruction: How does the 'static' CPU management policy manage CPU allocation for Guaranteed pods with integer CPU requests?
response: The 'static' policy manages a shared pool of CPUs that initially contains all CPUs in the node. As Guaranteed pods whose containers fit the requirements for being statically assigned are scheduled to the node, CPUs are removed from the shared pool and placed in the cpuset for the container. This increases CPU affinity and decreases context switches due to throttling for the CPU-bound workload.
title: Resource managers — CPU manager

instruction: What is the effect of enabling the 'full-pcpus-only' policy option for the static CPU management policy?
response: When the 'full-pcpus-only' policy option is specified, the static policy will always allocate full physical cores, rather than allocating CPUs using a topology-aware best-fit allocation.
title: Resource managers — CPU manager

instruction: What happens to sidecar containers in a Pod during shutdown?
response: The kubelet delays sending the TERM signal to sidecar containers until the last main container has fully terminated. Sidecar containers are terminated in the reverse order they are defined in the Pod spec.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Pod shutdown and sidecar containers

instruction: What can cause a Pod to enter forced termination during shutdown?
response: If the grace period expires before the termination process is complete, or if the Pod has a preStop hook that exceeds the termination grace period, the Pod may enter forced termination.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Pod shutdown and sidecar containers

instruction: How are containers terminated during forced termination?
response: During forced termination, all remaining containers in the Pod are terminated simultaneously with a short grace period.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Pod shutdown and sidecar containers

instruction: Can preStop hooks be removed if sidecar containers are used?
response: Yes, if you have used preStop hooks to control the termination order without sidecar containers, you can now remove them and allow the kubelet to manage sidecar termination automatically.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Pod shutdown and sidecar containers

instruction: What needs to happen to device plugins before upgrading a node to a new Kubernetes release with a newer device plugin API version?
response: Device plugins must be upgraded to support both the current and new API versions before the node is upgraded.
title: Upgrade A Cluster — Device Plugins

instruction: Why must device plugins support both API versions during a node upgrade?
response: To guarantee that device allocations continue to complete successfully during the upgrade.
title: Upgrade A Cluster — Device Plugins

instruction: Where can I find more information about API compatibility and Kubelet Device Manager API versions?
response: Refer to 'API compatibility' and 'Kubelet Device Manager API Versions' for more details.
title: Upgrade A Cluster — Device Plugins

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /apis/admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1/watch/mutatingadmissionpolicies request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: MutatingAdmissionPolicy — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /apis/admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1/watch/mutatingadmissionpolicies request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. The server may reject a continue value it does not recognize. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token.
title: MutatingAdmissionPolicy — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /apis/admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1/watch/mutatingadmissionpolicies request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent.
title: MutatingAdmissionPolicy — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `resourceVersionMatch` field interact with `resourceVersion` in the GET /apis/admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1/watch/mutatingadmissionpolicies request?
response: When `resourceVersionMatch` is set, it determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: MutatingAdmissionPolicy — getWatch List

instruction: How do I enable traffic shaping support for pods in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: To enable traffic shaping support, you must add the bandwidth plugin to your CNI configuration file (default /etc/cni/net.d) and ensure that the binary is included in your CNI bin dir (default /opt/cni/bin).
title: Network Plugins — Support traffic shaping

instruction: What annotations can I use to set ingress and egress bandwidth for a pod?
response: You can use the kubernetes.io/ingress-bandwidth and kubernetes.io/egress-bandwidth annotations to set the ingress and egress bandwidth for a pod. For example, you can add these annotations to your pod with values like '1M' for 1 megabit per second.
title: Network Plugins — Support traffic shaping

instruction: Can I use a custom plugin for bandwidth control instead of the official bandwidth plugin?
response: Yes, you can use your own plugin with bandwidth control functionality instead of the official bandwidth plugin offered by the CNI plugin team.
title: Network Plugins — Support traffic shaping

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'directory' field in kubeadm Patches configuration?
response: The 'directory' field specifies a path to a directory containing patch files to be applied to components deployed by kubeadm. These patch files are named in the format 'target[suffix][+patchtype].extension', where 'target' can be one of 'kube-apiserver', 'kube-controller-manager', 'kube-scheduler', 'etcd', 'kubeletconfiguration', or 'corednsdeployment'.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta4) — Patches

instruction: What are the supported patch formats and how are they specified in kubeadm Patches configuration?
response: The supported patch formats in kubeadm Patches configuration are 'strategic', 'merge', and 'json', which match the patch formats supported by kubectl. The patch format can be specified in the patch file name as '[+patchtype]', with 'strategic' being the default if not specified.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta4) — Patches

instruction: How are patches applied when there are multiple patches with different suffixes in the same directory?
response: Patches are applied in alpha-numeric order based on their suffix. If a patch file name includes a suffix, it will be applied before patches with a later suffix.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta4) — Patches

instruction: How do you enable enhanced Pod readiness in a Kubernetes Pod?
response: To enable enhanced Pod readiness, you set 'readinessGates' in the Pod's 'spec' to specify a list of additional conditions that the kubelet evaluates for Pod readiness.
title: Pod Conditions — Enhanced Pod readiness

instruction: What happens if a custom condition specified in 'readinessGates' is not found in the Pod's 'status.conditions' field?
response: If Kubernetes cannot find a condition in the 'status.conditions' field of a Pod, the status of the condition is defaulted to 'False'.
title: Pod Conditions — Enhanced Pod readiness

instruction: What format must the names of custom Pod conditions follow?
response: The Pod conditions you add must have names that meet the Kubernetes label key format.
title: Pod Conditions — Enhanced Pod readiness

instruction: How is the Impersonate-User header value used to determine the impersonation mode?
response: The Impersonate-User header value is used to determine the impersonation mode based on its prefix. If it does not start with 'system:serviceaccount:' or 'system:node:', it is considered generic user impersonation. If it starts with 'system:serviceaccount:', it is considered ServiceAccount impersonation. If it starts with 'system:node:', it is considered node impersonation.
title: User Impersonation — Impersonation modes

instruction: What verbs are required for a user to impersonate another generic user?
response: To impersonate a generic user, the verbs 'impersonate:user-info' and 'impersonate-on:user-info:<verb>' are required, where <verb> is the specific action to be performed while impersonating.
title: User Impersonation — Impersonation modes

instruction: What is the difference between 'impersonate:arbitrary-node' and 'impersonate:associated-node'?
response: 'impersonate:arbitrary-node' allows impersonating any specified node, while 'impersonate:associated-node' only allows impersonating the node to which the impersonator is bound.
title: User Impersonation — Impersonation modes

instruction: What happens to an image volume if the Pod is deleted and recreated?
response: The volume gets re-resolved, which means that new remote content will become available on Pod recreation.
title: Volumes — image

instruction: How is the image volume mounted in a Pod?
response: The OCI object gets mounted in a single directory (spec.containers[*].volumeMounts[*].mountPath) and will be mounted read-only.
title: Volumes — image

instruction: What is the effect of the spec.securityContext.fsGroupChangePolicy field on an image volume?
response: The field spec.securityContext.fsGroupChangePolicy has no effect on this volume type.
title: Volumes — image

instruction: When are subPath or subPathExpr mounts for containers supported for image volumes?
response: subPath or subPathExpr mounts for containers (spec.containers[*].volumeMounts[*].subPath, spec.containers[*].volumeMounts[*].subPathExpr) are only supported from Kubernetes v1.33.
title: Volumes — image

instruction: What configuration parameters are used by the kubelet to communicate with the container runtime?
response: The kubelet uses 'containerRuntimeEndpoint' for runtime operations and 'imageServiceEndpoint' for image management operations.
title: Local Files And Paths Used By The Kubelet — Container runtime

instruction: Where does the kubelet expose a socket for device plugins to register?
response: The kubelet exposes a socket at the path /var/lib/kubelet/device-plugins/kubelet.sock.
title: Local Files And Paths Used By The Kubelet — Container runtime

instruction: What directory must device plugin sockets be located in to register with the kubelet?
response: Device plugin sockets must be located in the directory /var/lib/kubelet/device-plugins/.
title: Local Files And Paths Used By The Kubelet — Container runtime

instruction: How is discovery information published in a Kubernetes cluster without discovery aggregation?
response: Without discovery aggregation, discovery is published in levels, with the root endpoints publishing discovery information for downstream documents.
title: The Kubernetes API — Unaggregated discovery

instruction: What endpoint provides a list of all group versions supported by a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The list of all group versions supported by a cluster is published at the /api and /apis endpoints.
title: The Kubernetes API — Unaggregated discovery

instruction: How does kubectl fetch the list of resources supported by a cluster?
response: kubectl uses the /apis/<group>/<version> endpoints to fetch the list of resources supported by a cluster, which advertise the list of resources served under a particular group version.
title: The Kubernetes API — Unaggregated discovery

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/networkpolicies endpoint?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: NetworkPolicy — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/networkpolicies endpoint?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is server-defined and clients may only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: NetworkPolicy — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/networkpolicies endpoint?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent.
title: NetworkPolicy — getWatch List

instruction: What is the purpose of enabling the TopologyManager feature gate on a node?
response: Enabling the TopologyManager feature gate allows the kubelet to use topology hints when making resource assignment decisions.
title: Nodes — Node topology

instruction: Where can I find more information about Control Topology Management Policies on a Node?
response: You can find more information about Control Topology Management Policies on a Node in the corresponding documentation section.
title: Nodes — Node topology

instruction: What is the purpose of EndpointSlices in Kubernetes?
response: EndpointSlices contain references to a set of network endpoints and are used to group network endpoints together by unique combinations of IP family, protocol, port number, and Service name.
title: EndpointSlices — EndpointSlice API

instruction: How are EndpointSlices created and managed in Kubernetes?
response: The control plane automatically creates EndpointSlices for any Kubernetes Service that has a selector specified. The control plane also manages EndpointSlices to have no more than 100 endpoints each, which can be configured with the --max-endpoints-per-slice kube-controller-manager flag.
title: EndpointSlices — EndpointSlice API

instruction: What is the role of EndpointSlices in routing internal traffic in Kubernetes?
response: EndpointSlices act as the source of truth for kube-proxy when it comes to how to route internal traffic.
title: EndpointSlices — EndpointSlice API

instruction: What are the requirements for the name of an EndpointSlice object in Kubernetes?
response: The name of an EndpointSlice object must be a valid DNS subdomain name.
title: EndpointSlices — EndpointSlice API

instruction: How can I troubleshoot why a pod could not be deployed or became rejected at a node in the context of topology management?
response: You can use the pod status, system logs, kubelet state file, and device plugin resource API to troubleshoot the issue.
title: Troubleshooting Topology Management — Sources of troubleshooting information

instruction: What information can I find in the pod status related to topology management?
response: The pod status indicates topology affinity errors.
title: Troubleshooting Topology Management — Sources of troubleshooting information

instruction: What can I find in the kubelet state file related to topology management?
response: The kubelet state file includes a dump of the internal state of the Memory Manager, including the node map and memory maps.
title: Troubleshooting Topology Management — Sources of troubleshooting information

instruction: How can I retrieve information about the memory reserved for containers?
response: You can use the device plugin resource API to retrieve information about the memory reserved for containers.
title: Troubleshooting Topology Management — Sources of troubleshooting information

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the Workload getList All Namespaces API?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: Workload — getList All Namespaces

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the Workload getList All Namespaces API?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is server-defined and clients may only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: Workload — getList All Namespaces

instruction: What is the effect of setting `limit` in the Workload getList All Namespaces API?
response: Setting a limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items. If more items exist, the server will set the `continue` field on the list metadata to a value that can be used with the same initial query to retrieve the next set of results.
title: Workload — getList All Namespaces

instruction: How does `resourceVersion` and `resourceVersionMatch` interact in the Workload getList All Namespaces API?
response: ResourceVersion sets a constraint on what resource versions a request may be served from. ResourceVersionMatch determines how resourceVersion is applied to list calls and it is highly recommended to set resourceVersionMatch for list calls where resourceVersion is set.
title: Workload — getList All Namespaces

instruction: What is the primary benefit of using the Kubelet Pod Info gRPC API?
response: The primary benefit is to increase reliability by removing the dependency on the Kubernetes API server for node-local information and to reduce load on the control plane.
title: Kubelet Pod Info gRPC API

instruction: Who has access to the Kubelet Pod Info gRPC API?
response: Access to this API is restricted to local admin users, typically root, through file permissions on the UNIX socket.
title: Kubelet Pod Info gRPC API

instruction: What type of components can query information using the Kubelet Pod Info gRPC API?
response: Node-local components can query information about pods running on the node directly from the kubelet using this API.
title: Kubelet Pod Info gRPC API

instruction: What is the purpose of specifying resource requests for containers in a Pod?
response: The kube-scheduler uses this information to decide which node to place the Pod on.
title: Pods — Resource requests and limits

instruction: How are CPU limits enforced for containers in a Pod?
response: CPU limits are enforced by CPU throttling. When a container approaches its CPU limit, the kernel restricts its access to CPU.
title: Pods — Resource requests and limits

instruction: What happens when a container exceeds its memory limit?
response: Memory limits are enforced by the kernel with out-of-memory (OOM) kills when a container exceeds its limit.
title: Pods — Resource requests and limits

instruction: How do you specify a desired file path for a specific ConfigMap item in a Pod?
response: You use the 'path' field to specify the desired file path for specific ConfigMap items.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a ConfigMap — Add ConfigMap data to a specific path in the Volume

instruction: Where will the SPECIAL_LEVEL item from the ConfigMap be mounted in the Pod?
response: The SPECIAL_LEVEL item will be mounted in the 'config-volume' volume at '/etc/config/keys'.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a ConfigMap — Add ConfigMap data to a specific path in the Volume

instruction: What is the command run in the test-container of the Pod and what is its expected output?
response: The command '/bin/sh -c "cat /etc/config/keys"' is run in the test-container. When the Pod runs, this command produces the output 'very'.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a ConfigMap — Add ConfigMap data to a specific path in the Volume

instruction: What is the primary benefit of keeping a Pod's memory request low?
response: By keeping a Pod's memory request low, you give the Pod a good chance of being scheduled.
title: Assign Memory Resources to Containers and Pods — Motivation for memory requests and limits

instruction: What are the two benefits of having a memory limit greater than the memory request for a Pod?
response: The Pod can have bursts of activity where it makes use of memory that happens to be available. The amount of memory a Pod can use during a burst is limited to some reasonable amount.
title: Assign Memory Resources to Containers and Pods — Motivation for memory requests and limits

instruction: Why should memory requests and limits be configured for Containers in a cluster?
response: By configuring memory requests and limits for the Containers that run in your cluster, you can make efficient use of the memory resources available on your cluster's Nodes.
title: Assign Memory Resources to Containers and Pods — Motivation for memory requests and limits

instruction: What is the IP address assigned to the Nginx Pod created with `kubectl run nginx --image nginx`?
response: The IP address assigned to the Nginx Pod is 10.200.0.4.
title: Adding entries to Pod /etc/hosts with HostAliases — Default hosts file content

instruction: What is the default content of the hosts file in a Kubernetes Pod?
response: The default hosts file content includes IPv4 and IPv6 boilerplates like localhost and its own hostname. Specifically, it includes entries for 127.0.0.1, ::1, and the Pod's own IP address and hostname.
title: Adding entries to Pod /etc/hosts with HostAliases — Default hosts file content

instruction: How can you verify the IP address of a Pod and its corresponding hosts file entry?
response: You can use `kubectl get pods --output wide` to examine the Pod IP, and then use `kubectl exec <pod_name> -- cat /etc/hosts` to verify the hosts file entry. For example, for the Nginx Pod, the IP address is 10.200.0.4, and the hosts file entry is `10.200.0.4 nginx`.
title: Adding entries to Pod /etc/hosts with HostAliases — Default hosts file content

instruction: What happens when maxParallelImagePulls is set to n?
response: With maxParallelImagePulls set to n, only n images can be pulled at the same time, and any image pull beyond n will have to wait until at least one ongoing image pull is complete.
title: Images — Maximum parallel image pulls

instruction: What is the purpose of limiting the number of parallel image pulls?
response: Limiting the number of parallel image pulls prevents image pulling from consuming too much network bandwidth or disk I/O, when parallel image pulling is enabled.
title: Images — Maximum parallel image pulls

instruction: What are the requirements for setting maxParallelImagePulls to a value greater than or equal to 2?
response: If you set maxParallelImagePulls to be greater than or equal to 2, you must set serializeImagePulls to false.
title: Images — Maximum parallel image pulls

instruction: What happens if maxParallelImagePulls is set to an invalid value?
response: The kubelet will fail to start with an invalid maxParallelImagePulls setting.
title: Images — Maximum parallel image pulls

instruction: What is the purpose of the fieldPath in a ValidationRule?
response: The fieldPath represents the field path returned when the validation fails. It must be a relative JSON path scoped to the location of this x-kubernetes-validations extension in the schema and refer to an existing field.
title: CustomResourceDefinition — ValidationRule

instruction: How do you access a field with special characters in its name in a CEL expression?
response: For field names which contain special characters, use `['specialName']` to refer to the field name. For example, for attribute `foo.34$` appears in a list `testList`, the fieldPath could be set to `.testList['foo.34$']`.
title: CustomResourceDefinition — ValidationRule

instruction: What are the possible enum values for the reason field in a ValidationRule?
response: The currently supported reasons are: "FieldValueInvalid", "FieldValueForbidden", "FieldValueRequired", "FieldValueDuplicate".
title: CustomResourceDefinition — ValidationRule

instruction: How is the `self` variable in a CEL expression bound?
response: The `self` variable in the CEL expression is bound to the scoped value. The Rule is scoped to the location of the x-kubernetes-validations extension in the schema.
title: CustomResourceDefinition — ValidationRule

instruction: How is the swap limit determined for a container with LimitedSwap enabled?
response: The swap limit is calculated as (containerMemoryRequest / nodeTotalMemory) × totalPodsSwapAvailable, where containerMemoryRequest is the container's memory request, nodeTotalMemory is the total physical memory on the node, and totalPodsSwapAvailable is the total swap memory available for use by Pods.
title: Swap memory management — How is the swap limit being determined with LimitedSwap?

instruction: Which types of QoS Pods are prohibited from utilizing swap memory with LimitedSwap?
response: Pods that do not fall under the Burstable QoS classification, specifically BestEffort and Guaranteed QoS Pods, are prohibited from utilizing swap memory with LimitedSwap.
title: Swap memory management — How is the swap limit being determined with LimitedSwap?

instruction: How can a container within a Burstable QoS Pod opt-out of swap usage?
response: A container within a Burstable QoS Pod can opt-out of swap usage by specifying memory requests that are equal to memory limits.
title: Swap memory management — How is the swap limit being determined with LimitedSwap?

instruction: What is the default scope for IngressClass parameters?
response: The default scope for IngressClass parameters is cluster-wide.
title: Ingress — IngressClass scope

instruction: How do you configure an IngressClass to use namespaced-scoped parameters?
response: To configure an IngressClass to use namespaced-scoped parameters, set the .spec.parameters.scope field to Namespace and specify the namespace and name of the parameters resource.
title: Ingress — IngressClass scope

instruction: What are the benefits of using namespace-scoped parameters for IngressClass configuration?
response: Namespace-scoped parameters help the cluster operator delegate control over the configuration to the application team, reducing the need for cluster operator approval for configuration changes and avoiding the need for specific access controls such as RBAC roles and bindings.
title: Ingress — IngressClass scope

instruction: Is the IngressClass API itself cluster-scoped or namespaced?
response: The IngressClass API itself is always cluster-scoped.
title: Ingress — IngressClass scope

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubernetes.io/os label in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The kubernetes.io/os label is used to indicate the operating system of a Node or Pod. For nodes, it is automatically populated by the kubelet with the runtime.GOOS value as defined by Go. For pods, it can be set manually but should match the Go runtime.GOOS string for the operating system the pod works with.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — kubernetes.io/os

instruction: What happens if the kubernetes.io/os label value for a Pod does not match the label value on a Node?
response: If the kubernetes.io/os label value for a Pod does not match the label value on a Node, the kubelet on the node will not admit the Pod. However, this mismatch is not considered by the kube-scheduler.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — kubernetes.io/os

instruction: Can I manually set the kubernetes.io/os label on a Pod, and what should I consider when doing so?
response: Yes, you can manually set the kubernetes.io/os label on a Pod. When doing so, you should set it to the Go runtime.GOOS string for the operating system that the Pod actually works with.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — kubernetes.io/os

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'enabled' field in KubeletAuthorizationMode?
response: The 'enabled' field allows bearer token authentication backed by the tokenreviews.authentication.k8s.io API.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — KubeletAuthorizationMode

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'cacheTTL' field in KubeletAuthorizationMode?
response: The 'cacheTTL' field enables caching of authentication results.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — KubeletAuthorizationMode

instruction: What are the prerequisites for running this DRA tutorial?
response: You need a Kubernetes cluster, kubectl command-line tool configured to communicate with your cluster, and a cluster with at least two nodes that are not acting as control plane hosts.
title: Install Drivers and Allocate Devices with DRA — Objectives

instruction: Can this tutorial be run on a cluster with a different authorization mechanism?
response: Yes, but you will have to adapt the steps around defining roles and permissions.
title: Install Drivers and Allocate Devices with DRA — Objectives

instruction: How can I verify the Kubernetes version of my cluster?
response: You can verify the Kubernetes version of your cluster by running the command 'kubectl version'.
title: Install Drivers and Allocate Devices with DRA — Objectives

instruction: What should I do if my cluster is not running Kubernetes 1.36?
response: If your cluster is not currently running Kubernetes 1.36, you should check the documentation for the version of Kubernetes that you plan to use.
title: Install Drivers and Allocate Devices with DRA — Objectives

instruction: How should the kubelet be configured to prevent scheduling pods that will trigger eviction due to memory pressure?
response: The kubelet should be configured with an eviction policy and the scheduler should not schedule pods if they will trigger eviction. The kubelet should also reserve memory for system daemons using the --system-reserved flag.
title: Node-pressure Eviction — Good practices

instruction: What is the purpose of the --system-reserved flag in the kubelet configuration?
response: The --system-reserved flag reserves memory for system daemons, such as the kernel and kubelet. In the example configuration, --system-reserved=memory=1.5Gi reserves 1.5GiB of memory for the system.
title: Node-pressure Eviction — Good practices

instruction: How is the eviction threshold calculated in the example kubelet configuration?
response: The eviction threshold is calculated as 95% of the total memory capacity minus the system reserved memory. In the example, the node has 10GiB of memory, 10% (1GiB) is reserved for system daemons, and the eviction threshold is set to 95% utilization, which is --eviction-hard=memory.available<500Mi.
title: Node-pressure Eviction — Good practices

instruction: What is the purpose of a persistentVolumeClaim volume in a Pod?
response: A persistentVolumeClaim volume is used to mount a PersistentVolume into a Pod.
title: Volumes — persistentVolumeClaim

instruction: Why would a user use a PersistentVolumeClaim instead of directly using a PersistentVolume?
response: A user would use a PersistentVolumeClaim to 'claim' durable storage without knowing the details of the particular cloud environment.
title: Volumes — persistentVolumeClaim

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' option in the DELETE collection API for ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBindings?
response: The continue option should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It allows clients to retrieve the next set of results from a previous query with identical query parameters.
title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What are the valid values for the 'propagationPolicy' field in the DELETE collection API for ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBindings?
response: The valid values for the 'propagationPolicy' field are: 'Orphan' - orphan the dependents; 'Background' - allow the garbage collector to delete the dependents in the background; 'Foreground' - a cascading policy that deletes all dependents in the foreground.
title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential' to true in the DELETE collection API for ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBindings?
response: If set to true, it will trigger an unsafe deletion of the resource in case the normal deletion flow fails with a corrupt object error. This may potentially break the cluster if the workload associated with the resource being unsafe-deleted relies on normal deletion flow.
title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What are the valid authorization modes for the kubelet server?
response: The valid authorization modes for the kubelet server are 'AlwaysAllow' and 'Webhook'.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — KubeletAuthorization

instruction: How does Webhook mode determine authorization for requests to the kubelet server?
response: Webhook mode uses the SubjectAccessReview API to determine authorization.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — KubeletAuthorization

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'webhook' field in KubeletAuthorization?
response: The 'webhook' field contains settings related to Webhook authorization.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — KubeletAuthorization

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /api/v1/resourcequotas request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers may ignore this flag if they do not implement bookmarks, and clients should not assume bookmarks are returned at any specific interval.
title: ResourceQuota — getList All Namespaces

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /api/v1/resourcequotas request?
response: The `continue` field is used to retrieve more results from the server.
