The 'pulling' directory stores records about images the Kubelet is attempting to pull, while the 'pulled' directory stores records about images that were successfully pulled by the Kubelet, along with metadata about the credentials used for the pulls.
title: Local Files And Paths Used By The Kubelet — Graceful node shutdown

instruction: What happens to a running Pod when a ConfigMap that is mapped into it using a configMap volume or a projected volume is updated?
response: When a ConfigMap that is mapped into a running Pod using either a configMap volume or a projected volume is updated, the running Pod sees the update almost immediately. However, the application only sees the change if it is written to either poll for changes or watch for file updates.
title: Updating Configuration via a ConfigMap — Objectives

instruction: How can you verify that a Pod's configuration has been updated after changing a ConfigMap that it uses?
response: To verify that a Pod's configuration has been updated after changing a ConfigMap that it uses, you can tail (follow) the logs of the Pod and observe if the log output changes to reflect the updated configuration.
title: Updating Configuration via a ConfigMap — Objectives

instruction: What command can be used to view the logs of a Deployment and verify that the configuration update is reflected?
response: You can use the command 'kubectl logs deployments/configmap-volume --follow' to view the logs of a Deployment and verify that the configuration update is reflected.
title: Updating Configuration via a ConfigMap — Objectives

instruction: What is the format of the --allow-metric-labels flag?
response: The format of the --allow-metric-labels flag is a map from metric-label to value allow-list of this label. The key's format is <MetricName>,<LabelName>. The value's format is <allowed_value>,<allowed_value>...
title: kube-scheduler — Options

instruction: How does the --allow-metric-labels flag interact with the --allow-metric-labels-manifest flag?
response: The --allow-metric-labels flag will override the manifest file specified by the --allow-metric-labels-manifest flag.
title: kube-scheduler — Options

instruction: What is the purpose of the --authentication-kubeconfig flag?
response: The --authentication-kubeconfig flag specifies a kubeconfig file pointing at the 'core' kubernetes server with enough rights to create tokenreviews.authentication.k8s.io. This is optional. If empty, all token requests are considered to be anonymous and no client CA is looked up in the cluster.
title: kube-scheduler — Options

instruction: What is the default duration to cache responses from the webhook token authenticator?
response: The default duration to cache responses from the webhook token authenticator is 10 seconds, as specified by the --authentication-token-webhook-cache-ttl flag.
title: kube-scheduler — Options

instruction: What is required to use device health monitoring for dynamic resource allocation in Kubernetes?
response: The ResourceHealthStatus feature gate must be enabled, and the DRA driver must implement the DRAResourceHealth gRPC service.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — Device Health Monitoring

instruction: How is the health status of a device assigned to a container exposed in Kubernetes?
response: The health status of a device is exposed in the Pod's status, specifically in the allocatedResourcesStatus field in the status of each container.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — Device Health Monitoring

instruction: What happens if the kubelet does not receive a health update from a DRA driver within a timeout period?
response: The device's health status is marked as 'Unknown'. The timeout period can be configured by the DRA driver on a per-device basis using the health_check_timeout_seconds field in the DeviceHealth gRPC message, with a default timeout of 30 seconds.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — Device Health Monitoring

instruction: Why is device health monitoring important for stateful applications running on specialized hardware?
response: It is critical to know when a device has failed or become unhealthy, and also helpful to find out if the device recovers, providing crucial visibility for users and controllers to react to hardware failures.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — Device Health Monitoring

instruction: What is the minimum Kubernetes version required to enable and configure certificate rotation for the kubelet?
response: Kubernetes version 1.8.0 or later is required.
title: Configure Certificate Rotation for the Kubelet

instruction: How does the kubelet handle certificate expiration and renewal?
response: The kubelet uses certificates for authenticating to the Kubernetes API. By default, these certificates are issued with one year expiration. Kubernetes contains kubelet certificate rotation, that will automatically generate a new key and request a new certificate from the Kubernetes API as the current certificate approaches expiration. Once the new certificate is available, it will be used for authenticating connections to the Kubernetes API.
title: Configure Certificate Rotation for the Kubelet

instruction: What triggers the kubelet to request a new certificate from the Kubernetes API?
response: The kubelet certificate rotation feature automatically generates a new key and requests a new certificate from the Kubernetes API as the current certificate approaches expiration.
title: Configure Certificate Rotation for the Kubelet

instruction: What is the purpose of the minReadySeconds field in a DaemonSetSpec?
response: The minReadySeconds field specifies the minimum number of seconds for which a newly created DaemonSet pod should be ready without any of its container crashing, for it to be considered available. If not specified, it defaults to 0, meaning the pod will be considered available as soon as it is ready.
title: DaemonSet — DaemonSetSpec

instruction: What is the significance of the revisionHistoryLimit field in a DaemonSetSpec?
response: The revisionHistoryLimit field determines the number of old history to retain, allowing for rollback. It defaults to 10 if not specified. This field is a pointer to distinguish between an explicit zero and an unspecified value.
title: DaemonSet — DaemonSetSpec

instruction: What are the requirements for the selector field in a DaemonSetSpec?
response: The selector field is a label query over pods that are managed by the daemon set. It must match the pod template's labels in order for the pod to be controlled by the DaemonSet.
title: DaemonSet — DaemonSetSpec

instruction: What is the allowed value for template.spec.restartPolicy in a DaemonSetSpec?
response: The only allowed value for template.spec.restartPolicy in a DaemonSetSpec is 'Always'.
title: DaemonSet — DaemonSetSpec

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in a GET /apis/scheduling.k8s.io/v1/watch/priorityclasses/{name} request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: PriorityClass — getWatch

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in a GET /apis/scheduling.k8s.io/v1/watch/priorityclasses/{name} request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is server-defined and clients may only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: PriorityClass — getWatch

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` in a GET /apis/scheduling.k8s.io/v1/watch/priorityclasses/{name} request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. A synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent once all such events have been sent, reporting the ResourceVersion corresponding to the set of objects.
title: PriorityClass — getWatch

instruction: What is the purpose of the `pretty` parameter in the GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/resourceclaims endpoint?
response: The `pretty` parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If set to `true`, the output will be pretty-printed. Defaults to `false` unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: ResourceClaim — getList

instruction: How does the `continue` parameter work in the GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/resourceclaims endpoint?
response: The `continue` parameter should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is a server-defined value that clients may only use with identical query parameters (except for the value of continue). If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error. Clients may then restart their list without the continue field or send another list request with the token received with the 410 error.
title: ResourceClaim — getList

instruction: What is the effect of setting `resourceVersionMatch` to NotOlderThan in a watch request?
response: When `resourceVersionMatch` is set to NotOlderThan, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection that are at least as new as the provided `resourceVersion`. A synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent when the state is synced to a `resourceVersion` at least as fresh as the one provided by the ListOptions.
title: ResourceClaim — getList

instruction: What is the initial delay for container start retries when the ReduceDefaultCrashLoopBackOffDecay feature gate is enabled?
response: The initial delay for container start retries is 1 second.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Reduced container restart delay

instruction: How does the maximum delay for container start retries change when the ReduceDefaultCrashLoopBackOffDecay feature gate is enabled?
response: The maximum delay for container start retries changes from 300 seconds (5 minutes) to 60 seconds.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Reduced container restart delay

instruction: What is the rate at which the delay increases with each restart when the ReduceDefaultCrashLoopBackOffDecay feature gate is enabled?
response: The delay increases exponentially by 2x with each restart.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Reduced container restart delay

instruction: How does the RBAC API prevent privilege escalation when creating or updating roles or role bindings?
response: The RBAC API prevents users from escalating privileges by editing roles or role bindings, and this is enforced at the API level.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Privilege escalation prevention and bootstrapping

instruction: What are the conditions under which a user can create or update a role in Kubernetes RBAC?
response: A user can create or update a role if they already have all the permissions contained in the role, at the same scope as the object being modified, or if they are granted explicit permission to perform the 'escalate' verb on the 'roles' or 'clusterroles' resource in the 'rbac.authorization.k8s.io' API group.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Privilege escalation prevention and bootstrapping

instruction: How can a user be allowed to create or update roles that include specific permissions?
response: A user can be allowed to create or update roles that include specific permissions by being granted those permissions implicitly (by giving them those permissions) or explicitly (by giving them permission to perform the 'escalate' verb on 'roles' or 'clusterroles' resources in the 'rbac.authorization.k8s.io' API group).
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Privilege escalation prevention and bootstrapping

instruction: How do you modify the default service account for a namespace to use an image pull secret named 'myregistrykey'?
response: You can use the command 'kubectl patch serviceaccount default -p '{"imagePullSecrets": [{"name": "myregistrykey"}]}'' or edit the service account object manually with 'kubectl edit serviceaccount/default'.
title: Configure Service Accounts for Pods — Add image pull secret to service account

instruction: What changes should be made to the ServiceAccount configuration file when adding an imagePullSecret?
response: Delete the line with key 'resourceVersion', add lines for 'imagePullSecrets:', and save it. Make sure to leave the 'uid' value unchanged.
title: Configure Service Accounts for Pods — Add image pull secret to service account

instruction: What is the expected outcome of adding an imagePullSecret to a ServiceAccount?
response: The ServiceAccount will have the imagePullSecret configured, allowing it to pull images from a private registry. The imagePullSecrets field will be updated with the name of the secret, for example: 'imagePullSecrets: - name: myregistrykey'.
title: Configure Service Accounts for Pods — Add image pull secret to service account

instruction: How can I verify that the Pods I ran are being selected by the Service?
response: You can verify that the Pods are being selected by the Service by checking the EndpointSlices. Use the command `kubectl get endpointslices -l kubernetes.io/service-name=<service-name>` to confirm that the EndpointSlice controller has found the correct Pods for your Service.
title: Debug Services — Does the Service have any EndpointSlices?

instruction: What should I check if the ENDPOINTS column is empty when running `kubectl get endpointslices`?
response: If the ENDPOINTS column is empty, you should check that the `spec.selector` field of your Service actually selects for `metadata.labels` values on your Pods. A common mistake is to have a typo or other error, such as the Service selecting for `app=hostnames`, but the Deployment specifying `run=hostnames`.
title: Debug Services — Does the Service have any EndpointSlices?

instruction: What does a high restart count in the Pods indicate?
response: A high restart count in the Pods can indicate intermittent connectivity issues. If the restart count is high, you should read more about how to debug pods.
title: Debug Services — Does the Service have any EndpointSlices?

instruction: How can I retrieve a list of existing node join tokens on a control plane node?
response: You can retrieve a list of existing node join tokens by running the command `sudo kubeadm token list` on a control plane node.
title: Adding Linux worker nodes — Additional information for kubeadm join

instruction: What is the default expiration time for node join tokens created by kubeadm?
response: By default, node join tokens expire after 24 hours.
title: Adding Linux worker nodes — Additional information for kubeadm join

instruction: How can I create a new node join token on a control plane node?
response: You can create a new node join token by running the command `sudo kubeadm token create` on a control plane node.
title: Adding Linux worker nodes — Additional information for kubeadm join

instruction: How can I obtain the value of --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash on a control plane node?
response: You can obtain the value of --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash by running the following commands on a control plane node: `sudo cat /etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt | openssl x509 -pubkey | openssl rsa -pubin -outform der 2>/dev/null | openssl dgst -sha256 -hex | sed 's/^.* //'`.
title: Adding Linux worker nodes — Additional information for kubeadm join

instruction: What triggers node-pressure eviction in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Node-pressure eviction is triggered when the kubelet monitors that resources like memory, disk space, and filesystem inodes on a node reach specific consumption levels.
title: Node-pressure Eviction

instruction: How does the kubelet handle pod termination during node-pressure eviction?
response: During node-pressure eviction, the kubelet sets the phase for the selected pods to Failed and terminates the pod. The kubelet does not respect the configured PodDisruptionBudget or the pod's terminationGracePeriodSeconds.
title: Node-pressure Eviction

instruction: What is the difference between node-pressure eviction and API-initiated eviction?
response: Node-pressure eviction is the process by which the kubelet proactively terminates pods to reclaim resource on nodes, whereas API-initiated eviction is a different process.
title: Node-pressure Eviction

instruction: How does the kubelet handle pod termination grace period during node-pressure eviction with hard eviction thresholds?
response: If hard eviction thresholds are used, the kubelet uses a 0s grace period for termination, resulting in immediate shutdown.
title: Node-pressure Eviction

instruction: What is the purpose of the Baseline policy in Pod Security Standards?
response: The Baseline policy is aimed at ease of adoption for common containerized workloads while preventing known privilege escalations. It is targeted at application operators and developers of non-critical applications.
title: Pod Security Standards — Baseline

instruction: What type of access is disallowed for HostProcess containers in the Baseline policy?
response: Privileged access to the host is disallowed in the Baseline policy for HostProcess containers.
title: Pod Security Standards — Baseline

instruction: What are the allowed values for the 'spec.securityContext.windowsOptions.hostProcess' field in the Baseline policy?
response: The allowed values for this field are Undefined/nil or false.
title: Pod Security Standards — Baseline

instruction: What is the recommended approach for HostPorts in the Baseline policy?
response: HostPorts should be disallowed entirely (recommended) or restricted to a known list.
title: Pod Security Standards — Baseline

instruction: What is the default AppArmor profile applied by the Baseline policy on supported hosts?
response: The RuntimeDefault AppArmor profile is applied by default.
title: Pod Security Standards — Baseline

instruction: What tool can be used to scan an image before deploying containers in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: An image scanning tool.
title: Application Security Checklist — Image security

instruction: How can the container image signature be validated before deploying to a Kubernetes cluster?
response: By using container signing.
title: Application Security Checklist — Image security

instruction: What should be configured to control ingress and egress traffic from pods in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: NetworkPolicies should be configured to only allow expected ingress and egress traffic from the pods.
title: Application Security Checklist — Image security

instruction: Is NetworkPolicy assumed to be available and enforced across different clusters?
response: No, when writing an application that users will deploy to different clusters, you should consider whether you can assume that NetworkPolicy is available and enforced.
title: Application Security Checklist — Image security

instruction: What is the format of the CVE feed available from the Kubernetes project?
response: The Kubernetes project publishes a programmatically accessible feed of published security issues in JSON and RSS feed formats.
title: Official CVE Feed

instruction: What are the two most common resources to specify for a container in a Pod?
response: The most common resources to specify are CPU and memory (RAM).
title: Resource Management for Pods and Containers

instruction: What is the role of the kube-scheduler when resource requests are specified for containers in a Pod?
response: The kube-scheduler uses the resource request information to decide which node to place the Pod on.
title: Resource Management for Pods and Containers

instruction: What is the role of the kubelet when resource limits are specified for a container?
response: The kubelet enforces the resource limits so that the running container is not allowed to use more of that resource than the limit set. The kubelet also reserves at least the request amount of that system resource specifically for that container to use.
title: Resource Management for Pods and Containers

instruction: What are the steps to manually rotate CA certificates in a Kubernetes cluster with a HA configuration and multiple API servers?
response: The steps include distributing new CA certificates and private keys to all control plane nodes, updating the --root-ca-file flag for the kube-controller-manager, restarting the kube-controller-manager, updating ServiceAccount Secrets, restarting pods using in-cluster configurations, and updating kube-apiserver, kube-scheduler, and kubelet configurations.
title: Manual Rotation of CA Certificates — Rotate the CA certificates manually

instruction: How do you update the kube-controller-manager to trust both old and new CA certificates?
response: Update the --root-ca-file flag for the kube-controller-manager to include both old and new CA certificates, then restart the kube-controller-manager.
title: Manual Rotation of CA Certificates — Rotate the CA certificates manually

instruction: What is the purpose of annotating DaemonSets and Deployments during CA certificate rotation?
response: Annotating DaemonSets and Deployments triggers pod replacement in a safer rolling fashion, allowing for a smooth transition to the new CA certificates.
title: Manual Rotation of CA Certificates — Rotate the CA certificates manually

instruction: How do you verify that the cluster functionality is working correctly after rotating CA certificates?
response: Verify the cluster functionality by checking the logs from control plane components, kubelet, and kube-proxy, ensuring that no TLS errors are reported.
title: Manual Rotation of CA Certificates — Rotate the CA certificates manually

instruction: What is the purpose of the bootstrap-kubelet.conf file in TLS bootstrapping?
response: The bootstrap-kubelet.conf file allows kubelet to do TLS bootstrapping by temporarily authenticating with the Kubernetes API server to submit a certificate signing request (CSR) for a locally created key pair.
title: Implementation details — TLS Bootstrap

instruction: How is the temporary authentication during TLS bootstrapping validated?
response: The temporary authentication is validated against the token saved during the kubeadm init process (or with additional tokens created with kubeadm token command).
title: Implementation details — TLS Bootstrap

instruction: Who manages the automatic approval of certificate signing requests (CSRs) during TLS bootstrapping?
response: The automatic CSR approval is managed by the csrapprover controller, according to the configuration present in the kubeadm init process.
title: Implementation details — TLS Bootstrap

instruction: What files are saved to be used by the kubelet for joining the cluster after successful TLS bootstrapping?
response: The ca.crt file and kubelet.conf file are saved to be used by the kubelet for joining the cluster, while bootstrap-kubelet.conf is deleted.
title: Implementation details — TLS Bootstrap

instruction: How can I configure a static Pod with a file system hosted configuration file?
response: You can configure a static Pod with a file system hosted configuration file by placing a standard Pod definition in JSON or YAML format in a specific directory, and then configuring the kubelet to scan that directory by setting the staticPodPath field in the kubelet configuration file.
title: Create static Pods — Create a static pod

instruction: What is the purpose of the staticPodPath field in the kubelet configuration file?
response: The staticPodPath field in the kubelet configuration file specifies the directory that the kubelet will periodically scan for YAML/JSON files, and create/delete static Pods as they appear/disappear.
title: Create static Pods — Create a static pod

instruction: How do I specify a directory for static Pod manifests using a command line argument?
response: You can specify a directory for static Pod manifests using the deprecated --pod-manifest-path command line argument when starting the kubelet. For example, --pod-manifest-path=/etc/kubernetes/manifests/.
title: Create static Pods — Create a static pod

instruction: What files will the kubelet ignore when scanning the specified directory for static Pod manifests?
response: The kubelet will ignore files starting with dots when scanning the specified directory for static Pod manifests.
title: Create static Pods — Create a static pod

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'pretty' parameter in the PUT /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ingressclasses/{name} request?
response: The 'pretty' parameter is used to pretty-print the output. It defaults to 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: IngressClass — putReplace

instruction: What are the valid values for the 'fieldValidation' parameter in the PUT /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ingressclasses/{name} request?
response: The valid values for 'fieldValidation' are: 'Ignore', 'Warn', and 'Strict'. 'Ignore' ignores unknown fields and duplicates, 'Warn' sends a warning for unknown fields and duplicates, and 'Strict' fails the request with a BadRequest error if there are unknown fields or duplicates.
title: IngressClass — putReplace

instruction: What is the significance of the 'dryRun' parameter in the PUT /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ingressclasses/{name} request?
response: The 'dryRun' parameter indicates that modifications should not be persisted. If present, the request will not make any changes. The only valid value is 'All', which processes all dry run stages.
title: IngressClass — putReplace

instruction: What is the default container registry used by kubeadm to pull control plane images?
response: The default container registry used by kubeadm to pull control plane images is registry.k8s.io.
title: kubeadm config — Synopsis

instruction: How can you specify a non-default Kubernetes version for the control plane when using kubeadm config images pull?
response: You can specify a non-default Kubernetes version for the control plane by using the --kubernetes-version flag followed by the desired version.
title: kubeadm config — Synopsis

instruction: What is the purpose of the --cri-socket flag in kubeadm config images pull?
response: The --cri-socket flag specifies the path to the CRI socket to connect. If empty, kubeadm will try to auto-detect this value; use this option only if you have more than one CRI installed or if you have a non-standard CRI socket.
title: kubeadm config — Synopsis

instruction: What feature gates are available for use with kubeadm config images pull?
response: The available feature gates are NodeLocalCRISocket, PublicKeysECDSA (DEPRECATED), and RootlessControlPlane (ALPHA).
title: kubeadm config — Synopsis

instruction: What is the possible value for the 'action' field in a ContainerRestartRule?
response: The only possible value for the 'action' field is 'Restart', which indicates that the container should be restarted.
title: Pod — ContainerRestartRule

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'exitCodes' field in a ContainerRestartRule?
response: The 'exitCodes' field represents the exit codes to check on container exits, allowing for specific exit codes to trigger a restart.
title: Pod — ContainerRestartRule

instruction: What action is taken on a container exit if the requirements are satisfied in a ContainerRestartRule?
response: If the requirements are satisfied, the action taken on a container exit is to restart the container, as specified by the 'action' field with the value 'Restart'.
title: Pod — ContainerRestartRule

instruction: What actions can the kubelet invoke to perform a diagnostic on a container?
response: The kubelet can invoke ExecAction, TCPSocketAction, and HTTPGetAction to perform a diagnostic on a container.
title: Pods — Container probes

instruction: How does the kubelet perform ExecAction, TCPSocketAction, and HTTPGetAction?
response: ExecAction is performed with the help of the container runtime, while TCPSocketAction and HTTPGetAction are checked directly by the kubelet.
title: Pods — Container probes

instruction: Where can I find more information about probes?
response: You can read more about probes in the Pod Lifecycle documentation.
title: Pods — Container probes

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'pretty' parameter in the PATCH /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/serviceaccounts/{name} request?
response: The 'pretty' parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If 'true', the output will be pretty printed. It defaults to 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: ServiceAccount — patchPatch

instruction: What are the valid values for the 'fieldValidation' parameter in the PATCH /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/serviceaccounts/{name} request?
response: The valid values for 'fieldValidation' are: 'Ignore', 'Warn', and 'Strict'. 'Ignore' ignores unknown fields and duplicate fields. 'Warn' sends a warning for unknown fields and duplicate fields. 'Strict' fails the request with a BadRequest error if there are unknown fields or duplicate fields.
title: ServiceAccount — patchPatch

instruction: How do you restrict access to Secrets for a ServiceAccount in Kubernetes?
response: You can add the annotation 'kubernetes.io/enforce-mountable-secrets' to the ServiceAccount and set it to 'true'. This ensures that the ServiceAccount's Secrets can only be mounted on specified types of resources.
title: Service Accounts — Restricting access to Secrets (deprecated)

instruction: What are the mounting restrictions for Secrets when the 'kubernetes.io/enforce-mountable-secrets' annotation is set to 'true'?
response: The name of each Secret that is mounted as a volume, referenced using 'envFrom', or referenced using 'imagePullSecrets' in a Pod must appear in the 'secrets' field of the Pod's ServiceAccount.
title: Service Accounts — Restricting access to Secrets (deprecated)

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'kubernetes.io/enforce-mountable-secrets' annotation in Kubernetes ServiceAccounts?
response: The annotation enhances the security posture of a cluster by restricting the mounting of Secrets to only specified types of resources, ensuring that Secrets are accessed only by the appropriate resources.
title: Service Accounts — Restricting access to Secrets (deprecated)

instruction: How can a user be authorized to use the adminAccess field in a ResourceClaim or ResourceClaimTemplate?
response: A user can be authorized to use the adminAccess field if they are allowed to create ResourceClaim or ResourceClaimTemplate objects in namespaces labeled with resource.kubernetes.io/admin-access: "true".
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — Admin access

instruction: What are the requirements for enabling the admin access feature?
response: The admin access feature is a beta feature and is enabled by default with the DRAAdminAccess feature gate in the kube-apiserver, kube-scheduler, and kubelet.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — Admin access

instruction: What are the implications of granting admin access in a multi-tenant cluster?
response: Admin access is a privileged mode and should not be granted to regular users in multi-tenant clusters, as it grants access to in-use devices and may enable additional permissions when making the device available in a container, which could be misused by non-admin users.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — Admin access

instruction: How do I check whether an action is allowed using kubectl?
response: You can use the kubectl auth can-i command to check whether an action is allowed.
title: kubectl reference

instruction: What is the purpose of kubectl apply view-last-applied?
response: The kubectl apply view-last-applied command is used to view the latest last-applied-configuration annotations of a resource/object.
title: kubectl reference

instruction: How do I create a cluster role using kubectl?
response: You can create a cluster role using the kubectl create clusterrole command.
title: kubectl reference

instruction: What is the purpose of kubectl rollout history?
response: The kubectl rollout history command is used to view the rollout history of a resource.
title: kubectl reference

instruction: How do I update environment variables on a pod template using kubectl?
response: You can update environment variables on a pod template using the kubectl set env command.
title: kubectl reference

instruction: How do I display the current-context using kubectl?
response: You can display the current-context using the kubectl config current-context command.
title: kubectl reference

instruction: What is the purpose of kubectl auth reconcile?
response: The kubectl auth reconcile command is used to reconcile rules for RBAC role, role binding, cluster role, and cluster role binding objects.
title: kubectl reference

instruction: What is the HTTP method and endpoint to retrieve a ServiceAccount in Kubernetes?
response: The HTTP method is GET and the endpoint is /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/serviceaccounts/{name}.
title: ServiceAccount — getRead

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'pretty' query parameter when retrieving a ServiceAccount?
response: The 'pretty' query parameter is used to pretty-print the output if set to 'true'. It defaults to 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool like curl or wget.
title: ServiceAccount — getRead

instruction: What is the expected HTTP status code when successfully retrieving a ServiceAccount?
response: The expected HTTP status code is 200, which indicates an OK response and returns the ServiceAccount object.
title: ServiceAccount — getRead

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /apis/policy/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/poddisruptionbudgets request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: PodDisruptionBudget — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /apis/policy/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/poddisruptionbudgets request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is a server-defined value that clients may only use with identical query parameters. If the continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: PodDisruptionBudget — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /apis/policy/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/poddisruptionbudgets request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event.
title: PodDisruptionBudget — getWatch List

instruction: What is the primary requirement for cloning a CSI Volume in Kubernetes?
response: The primary requirement for cloning a CSI Volume is that the source PVC must be bound and available (not in use) and exist in the same namespace as the destination PVC.
title: CSI Volume Cloning

instruction: Can a CSI Volume be cloned across different namespaces?
response: No, you can only clone a PVC when it exists in the same namespace as the destination PVC.
title: CSI Volume Cloning

instruction: Is it possible to clone a volume with a different VolumeMode setting?
response: No, cloning can only be performed between two volumes that use the same VolumeMode setting.
title: CSI Volume Cloning

instruction: Can the destination volume have a different Storage Class than the source volume?
response: Yes, the destination volume can be the same or a different storage class as the source. You can also use the default storage class by omitting storageClassName in the spec.
title: CSI Volume Cloning

instruction: How can I adjust the CPU or memory resources of a Pod after it's been created?
response: Kubernetes provides two approaches: in-place Pod vertical scaling, where you update the container's resource specifications using the Pod's /resize subresource, or updating the Pod template in the workload object and letting the controller replace Pods with new ones.
title: Resource Management for Pods and Containers — Resizing container resources

instruction: What is required to perform an in-place resize of a Pod's resources?
response: To perform an in-place resize, you need to update the container's resource specifications using the Pod's /resize subresource. You can also control whether a container restart is required by setting the resizePolicy field in the container specification.
title: Resource Management for Pods and Containers — Resizing container resources

instruction: How can I automatically manage Pod resource recommendations?
response: You can use the Vertical Pod Autoscaler to automatically manage Pod resource recommendations.
title: Resource Management for Pods and Containers — Resizing container resources

instruction: What is the purpose of the beta.kubernetes.io/instance-type label in Kubernetes?
response: The Kubelet populates this label with the instance type as defined by the cloud provider, allowing you to target certain workloads to certain instance types.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — beta.kubernetes.io/instance-type (deprecated)

instruction: When is the beta.kubernetes.io/instance-type label typically set?
response: This label is set only if you are using a cloud provider.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — beta.kubernetes.io/instance-type (deprecated)

instruction: What is the recommended approach to scheduling workloads in Kubernetes, and how does it relate to the beta.kubernetes.io/instance-type label?
response: You should aim to schedule based on properties rather than on instance types. This means relying on the Kubernetes scheduler to perform resource-based scheduling, rather than targeting specific instance types like g2.2xlarge.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — beta.kubernetes.io/instance-type (deprecated)

instruction: What happens to Pods during node downtime or zonal outages in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Kubernetes marks Pods as NotReady to allow load balancers to reroute traffic to available zones and nodes.
title: Admission Webhook Good Practices — Use a high-availability deployment model

instruction: Why might a mutating webhook server timeout or cause delays during node downtime or zonal outages?
response: The mutating webhook server might timeout or cause delays due to the number of affected Pods being updated, which can trigger the webhook.
title: Admission Webhook Good Practices — Use a high-availability deployment model

instruction: How should webhook operations be designed to handle unavoidable incidents in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Webhook operations should exclude updates that are a result of Kubernetes responding to unavoidable incidents, such as node downtime or zonal outages.
title: Admission Webhook Good Practices — Use a high-availability deployment model

instruction: What is the HTTP method and path to retrieve a specific Ingress resource by name and namespace?
response: The HTTP method is GET and the path is /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/ingresses/{name}
title: Ingress — getRead

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'pretty' query parameter in the Ingress retrieval request?
response: The 'pretty' query parameter is used to pretty-print the output if set to 'true'. It defaults to 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool like curl or wget.
title: Ingress — getRead

instruction: What is the expected HTTP status code and response body when successfully retrieving an Ingress resource?
response: The expected HTTP status code is 200 OK and the response body is the Ingress resource.
title: Ingress — getRead

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1beta2/watch/devicetaintrules request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: DeviceTaintRule — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1beta2/watch/devicetaintrules request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is server-defined and clients may only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: DeviceTaintRule — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1beta2/watch/devicetaintrules request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent.
title: DeviceTaintRule — getWatch List

instruction: How do I enable the EventedPLEG feature gate on the kubelet?
response: You can manage the kubelet feature gates by editing the kubelet config file and restarting the kubelet service. You need to do this on each node where you are using this feature. Make sure the node is drained before proceeding.
title: Switching from Polling to CRI Event-based Updates to Container Status — Switching to Evented PLEG

instruction: What version of CRI-O is required to use event-based container status updates?
response: CRI-O version 1.26+ is required to use event-based container status updates.
title: Switching from Polling to CRI Event-based Updates to Container Status — Switching to Evented PLEG

instruction: How do I verify that the kubelet is using event-based container stage change monitoring?
response: To verify that the kubelet is using event-based container stage change monitoring, look for the term 'EventedPLEG' in the kubelet logs. The output should indicate that the kubelet is using event-based container state monitoring.
title: Switching from Polling to CRI Event-based Updates to Container Status — Switching to Evented PLEG

instruction: How do I enable CRI-O to emit CRI events?
response: You can enable CRI-O to emit CRI events by starting the CRI-O daemon with the flag '--enable-pod-events=true' or by using a dropin config with the lines '[crio.runtime]' and 'enable_pod_events: true'.
title: Switching from Polling to CRI Event-based Updates to Container Status — Switching to Evented PLEG

instruction: What is the purpose of client-go credential plugins in Kubernetes?
response: The purpose of client-go credential plugins is to enable client side integrations with authentication protocols not natively supported by k8s.io/client-go, such as LDAP, Kerberos, OAuth2, and SAML.
title: Authenticating — client-go credential plugins

instruction: How do credential plugins interact with the Kubernetes authentication system?
response: Credential plugins execute an external command to receive user credentials, implement protocol-specific logic, and return opaque credentials to use. These credentials are then interpreted by a server-side component with support for the webhook token authenticator.
title: Authenticating — client-go credential plugins

instruction: What is typically required for credential plugin use cases?
response: Almost all credential plugin use cases require a server-side component with support for the webhook token authenticator to interpret the credential format produced by the client plugin.
title: Authenticating — client-go credential plugins

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'allowWatchBookmarks' field in the GET /api/v1/watch/persistentvolumes/{name} request?
response: The 'allowWatchBookmarks' field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: PersistentVolume — getWatch

instruction: What happens when the 'continue' field is set in a list call and the specified continue value is no longer valid?
response: The server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token. If the client needs a consistent list, it must restart their list without the continue field.
title: PersistentVolume — getWatch

instruction: What is the semantic of the watch request when 'sendInitialEvents' option is set?
response: When 'sendInitialEvents' option is set, we require 'resourceVersionMatch' option to also be set. The semantic of the watch request is as following: - 'resourceVersionMatch' = NotOlderThan is interpreted as 'data at least as new as the provided 'resourceVersion'' and the bookmark event is send when the state is synced to a 'resourceVersion' at least as fresh as the one provided by the ListOptions.
title: PersistentVolume — getWatch

instruction: What is the default value of maxContainerRestartPeriod if it is not set in the KubeletConfiguration?
response: The default value of maxContainerRestartPeriod is the internal crashloopbackoff maximum, which is 300 seconds.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — CrashLoopBackOffConfig

instruction: What is the minimum allowed value for maxContainerRestartPeriod?
response: The minimum allowed value for maxContainerRestartPeriod is 1 second.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — CrashLoopBackOffConfig

instruction: What is the maximum allowed value for maxContainerRestartPeriod?
response: The maximum allowed value for maxContainerRestartPeriod is 300 seconds.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — CrashLoopBackOffConfig

instruction: What is in-place Pod vertical scaling, and what benefits does it provide?
response: In-place Pod vertical scaling, also known as resizing a Pod's container-level CPU and memory resources, allows you to adjust resource allocation for running containers without disrupting the application. This can potentially avoid application disruption.
title: Pod Lifecycle — In-place Pod resize

instruction: How do you update a Pod's desired state to perform an in-place resize?
response: You update the Pod's desired state using the /resize subresource. The kubelet then attempts to apply the new resource values to the running containers.
title: Pod Lifecycle — In-place Pod resize

instruction: What considerations should you keep in mind when performing an in-place resize?
response: Only CPU and memory resources can be resized in-place. The Pod's Quality of Service (QoS) class is determined at creation and cannot be changed by resizing. You can configure whether a container restart is required for the resize using resizePolicy in the container specification.
title: Pod Lifecycle — In-place Pod resize

instruction: What is the default filesystem type for a GCE Persistent Disk in Kubernetes if it is not specified?
response: The default filesystem type is inferred to be 'ext4' if it is not specified.
title: PersistentVolume — GCEPersistentDiskVolumeSource

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'readOnly' field in the GCEPersistentDiskVolumeSource?
response: The 'readOnly' field forces the ReadOnly setting in VolumeMounts. It defaults to false.
title: PersistentVolume — GCEPersistentDiskVolumeSource

instruction: What is the significance of the 'pdName' field in the GCEPersistentDiskVolumeSource?
response: The 'pdName' field is a unique name of the PD resource in GCE, used to identify the disk in GCE.
title: PersistentVolume — GCEPersistentDiskVolumeSource

instruction: What is the purpose of the --subresource flag in kubectl commands?
response: The flag --subresource=[subresource-name] is used with kubectl commands like get, patch, edit, apply and replace to fetch and update status, scale and resize subresource of the resources you specify.
title: Update API Objects in Place Using kubectl patch — Update an object's replica count usingkubectl patchwith--subresource

instruction: How can you use kubectl patch with --subresource to update a Deployment's replica count?
response: You can use kubectl patch with --subresource to update a Deployment's replica count by specifying the subresource as 'scale' and providing a JSON patch to update the replicas field in the Deployment's spec section.
title: Update API Objects in Place Using kubectl patch — Update an object's replica count usingkubectl patchwith--subresource

instruction: What is the output of kubectl get deployment nginx-deployment -o yaml after using kubectl patch with --subresource to update the replica count?
response: The output will show the updated Deployment with the new replica count, including the updated spec section with replicas set to 3, and the updated status section with availableReplicas, readyReplicas and replicas all set to 3.
title: Update API Objects in Place Using kubectl patch — Update an object's replica count usingkubectl patchwith--subresource

instruction: What is the purpose of a Pod in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Pods are designed to support multiple cooperating processes (as containers) that form a cohesive unit of service. They can share resources and dependencies, communicate with one another, and coordinate when and how they are terminated.
title: Pods — Pods with multiple containers

instruction: What is the difference between a Pod with multiple containers and a Pod with a single container?
response: A Pod with multiple containers is used when multiple co-located containers need to work together and share resources, whereas a Pod with a single container is the most common Kubernetes use case where a Pod can be thought of as a wrapper around a single container.
title: Pods — Pods with multiple containers

instruction: What is the purpose of init containers in a Pod?
response: Init containers run and complete before the app containers are started. They can be used to provide auxiliary services to the main application Pod, such as setting up shared volumes or configuring network policies.
title: Pods — Pods with multiple containers

instruction: What is the effect of setting the restart policy to Always for init containers?
response: Setting the Always restart policy ensures that the containers where you set it are treated as sidecars that are kept running during the entire lifetime of the Pod.
title: Pods — Pods with multiple containers

instruction: What is the default value for the 'pretty' parameter in the DELETE /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/servicecidrs request?
response: The default value for the 'pretty' parameter is 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: ServiceCIDR — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential' to 'true' in the DELETE request?
response: Setting 'ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential' to 'true' will trigger an unsafe deletion of the resource in case the normal deletion flow fails with a corrupt object error, ignoring finalizer constraints, skipping precondition checks, and removing the object from the storage.
title: ServiceCIDR — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' field in the response of the DELETE request?
response: The 'continue' field is used to retrieve more results from the server, and clients should only use the presence of this field to determine whether more results are available.
title: ServiceCIDR — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'limit' in the DELETE request?
response: Setting 'limit' may return fewer than the requested amount of items (up to zero items) in the event all requested objects are filtered out, and clients should only use the presence of the 'continue' field to determine whether more results are available.
title: ServiceCIDR — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'resourceVersion' parameter in the DELETE request?
response: The 'resourceVersion' parameter sets a constraint on what resource versions a request may be served from.
title: ServiceCIDR — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'orphanDependents' to 'true' in the DELETE request?
response: Setting 'orphanDependents' to 'true' will add the 'orphan' finalizer to the object's finalizers list.
title: ServiceCIDR — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'propagationPolicy' parameter in the DELETE request?
response: The 'propagationPolicy' parameter determines whether and how garbage collection will be performed.
title: ServiceCIDR — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the primary purpose of using client TLS certificates on worker nodes in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The primary purpose of using client TLS certificates on worker nodes is to ensure private and secure communication between components, specifically between kubelet and kube-proxy on worker nodes and the Kubernetes control plane components, particularly kube-apiserver.
title: TLS bootstrapping

instruction: What was introduced in Kubernetes version 1.4 to simplify the process of bootstrapping worker nodes with TLS client certificates?
response: Kubernetes introduced a certificate request and signing API in version 1.4 to simplify the process of bootstrapping worker nodes with TLS client certificates.
title: TLS bootstrapping

instruction: What is the main challenge in bootstrapping worker nodes with TLS client certificates?
response: The main challenge in bootstrapping worker nodes with TLS client certificates is that the process often lies outside the scope of Kubernetes and requires significant additional work, making it challenging to initialize or scale a cluster.
title: TLS bootstrapping

instruction: What happens when a Pod's memory request exceeds the capacity of any Node in the cluster?
response: The Pod remains in the PENDING state indefinitely, and it will not be scheduled to run on any Node.
title: Assign Memory Resources to Containers and Pods — Specify a memory request that is too big for your Nodes

instruction: How does the Pod's memory request relate to the memory requests of its Containers?
response: The Pod's memory request is the sum of the memory requests for all the Containers in the Pod.
title: Assign Memory Resources to Containers and Pods — Specify a memory request that is too big for your Nodes

instruction: What is the reason for the Pod's status being PENDING, as shown by the command 'kubectl get pod memory-demo-3 --namespace=mem-example'?
response: The reason is that there are no available Nodes that match all of the following predicates: Insufficient memory.
title: Assign Memory Resources to Containers and Pods — Specify a memory request that is too big for your Nodes

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'allow-all-egress' NetworkPolicy?
response: The purpose of the 'allow-all-egress' NetworkPolicy is to allow all outgoing connections from pods in a namespace, ensuring that no additional policy can deny any outgoing connection from those pods.
title: Network Policies — Allow all egress traffic

instruction: How does the 'allow-all-egress' NetworkPolicy affect ingress traffic to pods?
response: The 'allow-all-egress' NetworkPolicy has no effect on isolation for ingress to any pod.
title: Network Policies — Allow all egress traffic

instruction: What is the effect of the 'podSelector' set to an empty object in the 'allow-all-egress' policy?
response: The 'podSelector' set to an empty object in the 'allow-all-egress' policy allows all pods in the namespace to be selected, effectively allowing all outgoing connections from all pods.
title: Network Policies — Allow all egress traffic

instruction: What is the purpose of the lastPhaseTransitionTime field in PersistentVolumeStatus?
response: The lastPhaseTransitionTime field represents the time when the phase transitioned from one to another, and it automatically resets to the current time every time a volume phase transitions.
title: PersistentVolume — PersistentVolumeStatus

instruction: What does the phase field in PersistentVolumeStatus indicate, and what are its possible enum values?
response: The phase field indicates if a volume is available, bound to a claim, or released by a claim. The possible enum values are: Available, Bound, Failed, Pending, and Released.
title: PersistentVolume — PersistentVolumeStatus

instruction: What is the purpose of the message field in PersistentVolumeStatus?
response: The message field is a human-readable message indicating details about why the volume is in its current state.
title: PersistentVolume — PersistentVolumeStatus

instruction: What is the purpose of the reason field in PersistentVolumeStatus?
response: The reason field is a brief CamelCase string that describes any failure and is meant for machine parsing and tidy display in the CLI.
title: PersistentVolume — PersistentVolumeStatus

instruction: What happens to a failed container inside a Pod in Kubernetes?
response: Kubernetes restarts it based on the restartPolicy.
title: Kubernetes Self-Healing

instruction: How does Kubernetes maintain the desired number of replicas for a Pod in a Deployment or StatefulSet?
response: Kubernetes creates a replacement Pod to maintain the specified number of replicas.
title: Kubernetes Self-Healing

instruction: What happens to a PersistentVolume (PV) attached to a Pod on a node that fails?
response: Kubernetes can reattach the volume to a new Pod on a different node.
title: Kubernetes Self-Healing

instruction: What is the role of the PersistentVolume controller in Kubernetes self-healing?
response: It manages volume attachment and detachment for stateful workloads.
title: Kubernetes Self-Healing

instruction: What is the purpose of CredentialProviderConfig in Kubelet?
response: CredentialProviderConfig is the configuration containing information about each exec credential provider, which Kubelet reads from disk and enables as specified by the CredentialProvider type.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1) — Resource Types

instruction: How does Kubelet handle multiple credential providers for a single image?
response: If multiple providers match against a single image, Kubelet returns credentials from all providers. The results are combined, and if providers return overlapping auth keys, the value from the provider earlier in the list is attempted first.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1) — Resource Types

instruction: What is the required field in CredentialProviderConfig?
response: The required field in CredentialProviderConfig is 'providers', which is a list of credential provider plugins that will be enabled by the kubelet.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1) — Resource Types

instruction: When performing a minor version upgrade for kubelet, what is the first step?
response: First, you must drain the node (or nodes) that you are upgrading.
title: Upgrading kubeadm clusters — Additional information

instruction: What is the recommended version match between kubelet and kubeadm?
response: The Kubernetes project recommends that you match your kubelet and kubeadm versions.
title: Upgrading kubeadm clusters — Additional information

instruction: How can you verify that the kubelet service has successfully restarted after the upgrade?
response: You can execute systemctl status kubelet or view the service logs with journalctl -xeu kubelet.
title: Upgrading kubeadm clusters — Additional information

instruction: Can kubeadm upgrade be used to reconfigure an existing cluster?
response: No, kubeadm upgrade does not support reconfiguration of an existing cluster. Instead, follow the steps in Reconfiguring a kubeadm cluster.
title: Upgrading kubeadm clusters — Additional information

instruction: How can you persist modifications to a Node object after a kubeadm upgrade?
response: You can prepare a kubectl patch and apply it to the Node object using kubectl patch no <node-name> --patch-file <patch-file>.
title: Reconfiguring a kubeadm cluster — Persisting the reconfiguration

instruction: What is the main source of control plane configuration in a kubeadm cluster?
response: The main source of control plane configuration is the ClusterConfiguration object stored in the cluster.
title: Reconfiguring a kubeadm cluster — Persisting the reconfiguration

instruction: How can you persist kubelet node-specific configuration after a kubeadm upgrade?
response: Either update the file /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml manually post-upgrade or include flags in the file /var/lib/kubelet/kubeadm-flags.env, noting that some flags are deprecated.
title: Reconfiguring a kubeadm cluster — Persisting the reconfiguration

instruction: How do you scale down a Deployment in Kubernetes?
response: You can scale down a Deployment by setting the --replicas flag to a lower value using the kubectl scale command.
title: Horizontal Manual Scaling for a Deployment — Scaling down a Deployment

instruction: What happens to the excess Pods when scaling down a Deployment?
response: Kubernetes gracefully terminates the excess Pods, respecting each Pod's terminationGracePeriodSeconds setting.
title: Horizontal Manual Scaling for a Deployment — Scaling down a Deployment

instruction: How can you verify that a scaled-down Deployment has the correct number of Pods?
response: You can verify the number of Pods by running the kubectl get pods command with the label app=nginx.
title: Horizontal Manual Scaling for a Deployment — Scaling down a Deployment

instruction: What is the purpose of a Kubernetes object?
response: A Kubernetes object is a 'record of intent' that represents the desired state of your cluster's workload, and the Kubernetes system works to ensure that the object exists.
title: Objects In Kubernetes

instruction: What can Kubernetes objects describe?
response: Kubernetes objects can describe what containerized applications are running, the resources available to those applications, and the policies around how those applications behave.
title: Objects In Kubernetes

instruction: How do you interact with Kubernetes objects?
response: You can interact with Kubernetes objects by using the Kubernetes API directly or through the kubectl command-line interface, which makes the necessary API calls for you.
title: Objects In Kubernetes

instruction: What is the default value for the 'pretty' parameter in the DELETE /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/podtemplates request?
response: The default value for the 'pretty' parameter is 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: PodTemplate — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What happens when the 'continue' field is set and the specified continue value is no longer valid?
response: The server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token. The client may send another list request with the token received with the 410 error, and the server will respond with a list starting from the next key, but from the latest snapshot.
title: PodTemplate — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential' to true?
response: It will trigger an unsafe deletion of the resource in case the normal deletion flow fails with a corrupt object error. This may potentially break the cluster if the workload associated with the resource being unsafe-deleted relies on normal deletion flow.
title: PodTemplate — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the default policy for garbage collection when neither 'OrphanDependents' nor 'PropagationPolicy' is set?
response: The default policy is decided by the existing finalizer set in the metadata.finalizers and the resource-specific default policy.
title: PodTemplate — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What are the recommended minimum authentication methods to enable in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: You should usually use at least two methods: service account tokens for ServiceAccounts and at least one other method for (normal) user authentication.
title: Authenticating — Authentication methods

instruction: What happens when multiple authenticator modules are enabled in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: When multiple authenticator modules are enabled, the first module to successfully authenticate the request short-circuits evaluation.
title: Authenticating — Authentication methods

instruction: Is the order of authenticator modules guaranteed when multiple are enabled?
response: No, the API server does not guarantee the order authenticators run in.
title: Authenticating — Authentication methods

instruction: What is the purpose of the `continue` field in the GET /api/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/pods API call?
response: The `continue` field is used to retrieve more results from the server. It should be set when retrieving more results from the server and should be used in conjunction with the same initial query to retrieve the next set of results.
title: Pod — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting `limit` to a specific value in the GET /api/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/pods API call?
response: Setting a limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items (up to zero items) in the event all requested objects are filtered out. The server guarantees that the objects returned when using continue will be identical to issuing a single list call without a limit.
title: Pod — getWatch List

instruction: What is the purpose of the `sendInitialEvents` option in the GET /api/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/pods API call?
response: The `sendInitialEvents` option may be set together with `watch=true` to begin the watch stream with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection.
title: Pod — getWatch List

instruction: What are the possible formats for log messages in Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) and what is the default format?
response: The possible formats for log messages are specified by the 'format' field, which can be a string. The default format is 'text'.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — LoggingConfiguration

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'flushFrequency' field in Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) and how is it parsed?
response: The 'flushFrequency' field specifies the maximum time between log flushes. It can be a string, parsed as a duration (e.g. '1s'), or an integer, representing the maximum number of nanoseconds (e.g. 1s = 1000000000).
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — LoggingConfiguration

instruction: What is the default verbosity threshold for log messages in Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) and how does it affect logging?
response: The default verbosity threshold is zero, which logs only the most important messages. Higher values enable additional messages, and error messages are always logged.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — LoggingConfiguration

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'vmodule' field in Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) and what are its limitations?
response: The 'vmodule' field allows overriding the verbosity threshold for individual files, but it is only supported for the 'text' log format.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — LoggingConfiguration

instruction: What is the typical way a container runtime exposes access to interact with containers on a Kubernetes node?
response: The container runtime exposes a Unix socket that the kubelet can access.
title: Kubernetes API Server Bypass Risks — Container runtime socket

instruction: What can an attacker with access to the container runtime socket do?
response: An attacker with access to this socket can launch new containers or interact with running containers.
title: Kubernetes API Server Bypass Risks — Container runtime socket

instruction: How can an attacker escalate privileges using the compromised container runtime socket?
response: An attacker could use the containers that run on the compromised node to access Secrets or other confidential data that could be used to escalate privileges to other worker nodes or to control plane components.
title: Kubernetes API Server Bypass Risks — Container runtime socket

instruction: What is the recommended method for authenticating other Kubernetes components to kube-apiserver?
response: Running these components as a DaemonSet in the kube-system namespace with a proper service account and appropriate permissions is the recommended method.
title: TLS bootstrapping — Other authenticating components

instruction: What are the two options for generating credentials for other Kubernetes components?
response: The old way of creating and distributing certificates, and running these components as a DaemonSet in the kube-system namespace with a proper service account and appropriate permissions.
title: TLS bootstrapping — Other authenticating components

instruction: Why is running components as a DaemonSet in the kube-system namespace considered the simplest way to configure such services?
response: Since the kubelet itself is loaded on each node and is sufficient to start base services, running components as a DaemonSet leverages this existing infrastructure.
title: TLS bootstrapping — Other authenticating components

instruction: What is the purpose of the Dockershim Removal FAQ articles?
response: The Dockershim Removal FAQ articles provide information and answers to frequently asked questions about the deprecation and removal of dockershim in Kubernetes.
title: Articles on dockershim Removal and on Using CRI-compatible Runtimes

instruction: Where can you provide feedback on the dockershim removal process?
response: You can provide feedback via the GitHub issue 'Dockershim removal feedback & issues' (k/kubernetes/#106917).
title: Articles on dockershim Removal and on Using CRI-compatible Runtimes

instruction: What is the status of the dockershim removal process?
response: The articles and proposals listed provide information on the removal process, including the Kubernetes blog posts 'Kubernetes is Moving on From Dockershim: Commitments and Next Steps' (published 2022/01/07) and 'Dockershim removal is coming. Are you ready?' (published 2021/11/12).
title: Articles on dockershim Removal and on Using CRI-compatible Runtimes

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the `GET /apis/admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1/watch/mutatingwebhookconfigurations` API call?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type `BOOKMARK` from the server, but servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: MutatingWebhookConfiguration — getWatch List

instruction: What happens when the `continue` field is set in the `GET /apis/admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1/watch/mutatingwebhookconfigurations` API call?
response: The server will return a list starting from the next key, but from the latest snapshot, which is inconsistent from the previous list results - objects that are created, modified, or deleted after the first list request will be included in the response, as long as their keys are after the `next key`.
title: MutatingWebhookConfiguration — getWatch List

instruction: What is the semantic of the watch request when `sendInitialEvents` option is set?
response: When `sendInitialEvents` option is set, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic `Bookmark` event will be sent. The bookmark will report the ResourceVersion (RV) corresponding to the set of objects, and be marked with `"k8s.io/initial-events-end": "true"` annotation.
title: MutatingWebhookConfiguration — getWatch List

instruction: How do you configure logging for ZooKeeper in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can configure logging for ZooKeeper by modifying the log4j.properties file, which is generated by the zkGenConfig.sh script. By default, ZooKeeper uses a time and size based rolling file appender for its logging configuration.
title: Running ZooKeeper, A Distributed System Coordinator — Configuring logging

instruction: What is the simplest way to safely log inside a container in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The simplest way to safely log inside a container is to write logs to standard out, as Kubernetes will handle log rotation for you and implement a sane retention policy to prevent log files from exhausting local storage media.
title: Running ZooKeeper, A Distributed System Coordinator — Configuring logging

instruction: How do you retrieve the last 20 log lines from a ZooKeeper Pod in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can use the kubectl logs command with the --tail option to retrieve the last 20 log lines from a ZooKeeper Pod. For example, kubectl logs zk-0 --tail 20.
title: Running ZooKeeper, A Distributed System Coordinator — Configuring logging

instruction: How do you delete a long-lived ServiceAccount token if you know the name of the Secret that contains it?
response: You can delete the Secret using the command `kubectl delete secret name-of-secret`.
title: Managing Service Accounts — Delete/invalidate a long-lived/legacy ServiceAccount token

instruction: If you don't know the name of the Secret that contains the ServiceAccount token, how do you find it?
response: You can use the command `kubectl -n examplens get serviceaccount/example-automated-thing -o yaml` to retrieve the ServiceAccount details, which will include the name of the Secret.
title: Managing Service Accounts — Delete/invalidate a long-lived/legacy ServiceAccount token

instruction: What is the next step after finding the Secret that contains the ServiceAccount token?
response: You should delete the Secret using the command `kubectl -n examplens delete secret/example-automated-thing-token-zyxwv`.
title: Managing Service Accounts — Delete/invalidate a long-lived/legacy ServiceAccount token

instruction: What is the purpose of the reservedMemory setting in Kubernetes?
response: The reservedMemory setting allows you to split the total reserved memory and assign it across many NUMA nodes, reserving memory for the kubelet or operating system processes to help assure node stability.
title: Control Memory Management Policies on a Node — Reserved memory configuration

instruction: How do you specify reservations for multiple NUMA nodes using the reservedMemory setting?
response: You can specify reservations that span multiple NUMA nodes by using a semicolon as a separator, or you can specify a comma-separated list of memory reservations, of different memory types, per NUMA node.
title: Control Memory Management Policies on a Node — Reserved memory configuration

instruction: What happens if you omit the reservedMemory parameter, and how does it affect node allocatable memory?
response: If you omit the reservedMemory parameter, you should be aware that the quantity of reserved memory from all NUMA nodes should be equal to the quantity of node allocatable memory. If at least one node allocatable parameter is non-zero, you will need to specify reservedMemory for at least one NUMA node.
title: Control Memory Management Policies on a Node — Reserved memory configuration

instruction: What determines whether a quota measures usage for a resource?
response: A quota will only measure usage for a resource if it matches the intersection of enumerated scopes.
title: Resource Quotas — Quota scopes

instruction: What happens if a resource is specified on the quota outside of the allowed set?
response: Resources specified on the quota outside of the allowed set results in a validation error.
title: Resource Quotas — Quota scopes

instruction: What is the purpose of the scopeSelector field in a ResourceQuota with a scope set?
response: The scopeSelector field allows you to define one or more match expressions that specify an operator and, if relevant, a set of values to match.
title: Resource Quotas — Quota scopes

instruction: What is the format for a valid IPv4 address in an IPAddress object?
response: A valid IPv4 address in an IPAddress object is represented in the format of four decimal digits separated by dots, suppressing leading zeros.
title: Networking

instruction: What is the purpose of the ingressclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class annotation?
response: The ingressclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class annotation is used to indicate that an IngressClass should be considered the default class, which will be assigned to new Ingress resources without a specified class.
title: Networking

instruction: What is the format for a valid IPv6 address in an IPAddress object?
response: A valid IPv6 address in an IPAddress object is represented in the format defined by RFC 5952.
title: Networking

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'dryRun' parameter in the ServiceAccount POST request?
response: The 'dryRun' parameter indicates that modifications should not be persisted. An invalid or unrecognized 'dryRun' directive will result in an error response and no further processing of the request.
title: ServiceAccount — postCreate

instruction: What are the valid values for the 'fieldValidation' parameter in the ServiceAccount POST request?
response: The valid values for 'fieldValidation' are: Ignore, Warn, and Strict. The default behavior is Ignore prior to v1.23, and Warn in v1.23+.
title: ServiceAccount — postCreate

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'fieldManager' parameter in the ServiceAccount POST request?
response: The 'fieldManager' parameter is a name associated with the actor or entity that is making these changes. The value must be less than or 128 characters long, and only contain printable characters.
title: ServiceAccount — postCreate

instruction: What is the purpose of the Service API in Kubernetes?
response: The Service API is an abstraction to help you expose groups of Pods over a network, decoupling frontends from backends and enabling them to access a logical set of endpoints without needing to track the set of backends themselves.
title: Service — Services in Kubernetes

instruction: How is the set of Pods targeted by a Service typically determined?
response: The set of Pods targeted by a Service is usually determined by a selector that you define.
title: Service — Services in Kubernetes

instruction: What is the difference between an Ingress and a Service in Kubernetes?
response: Ingress is not a Service type, but it acts as the entry point for your cluster, consolidating routing rules into a single resource, whereas a Service is an abstraction to expose groups of Pods over a network.
title: Service — Services in Kubernetes

instruction: How do you restart a deployment using kubectl?
response: You can restart a deployment using the command kubectl rollout restart deployment/nginx.
title: GETTING STARTED — restart

instruction: What is the general syntax for restarting a daemon set with kubectl?
response: The general syntax for restarting a daemon set is kubectl rollout restart daemonset/abc.
title: GETTING STARTED — restart

instruction: How do you restart all deployments with the app=nginx label using kubectl?
response: You can restart all deployments with the app=nginx label using the command kubectl rollout restart deployment --selector=app=nginx.
title: GETTING STARTED — restart
