instruction: What is the syntax for restarting a resource using kubectl?
response: The syntax for restarting a resource is kubectl rollout restart RESOURCE.
title: GETTING STARTED — restart

instruction: What is the purpose of an Init Container in a Pod?
response: The init container runs to completion before the application container starts, allowing it to perform initialization tasks such as downloading and installing dependencies.
title: Configure Pod Initialization — Create a Pod that has an Init Container

instruction: How do the init container and the application container share a Volume?
response: The init container and the application container share a Volume by mounting it at different paths. The init container mounts the shared Volume at /work-dir, and the application container mounts the shared Volume at /usr/share/nginx/html.
title: Configure Pod Initialization — Create a Pod that has an Init Container

instruction: What command does the init container run to initialize the Volume?
response: The init container runs the command wget -O /work-dir/index.html http://info.cern.ch to download the index.html file from the specified URL and write it to the root directory of the nginx server.
title: Configure Pod Initialization — Create a Pod that has an Init Container

instruction: What is the purpose of the Hardening Guide - Authentication Mechanisms page?
response: The purpose of this page is to provide guidance on hardening authentication mechanisms in Kubernetes.
title: Hardening Guide - Authentication Mechanisms — What's next

instruction: Where can I report a problem or suggest an improvement for the Kubernetes documentation?
response: You can report a problem or suggest an improvement by opening an issue in the GitHub Repository.
title: Hardening Guide - Authentication Mechanisms — What's next

instruction: What is the recommended platform for asking Kubernetes usage questions?
response: The recommended platform for asking Kubernetes usage questions is Stack Overflow.
title: Hardening Guide - Authentication Mechanisms — What's next

instruction: What are some topics covered under Cluster Administration in Kubernetes?
response: The topics covered include managing nodes, node autoscaling, resource quota for shared clusters, generating certificates, Kubernetes container environment, controlling access to the Kubernetes API, authenticating, authorization, using admission controllers, admission webhook good practices, using sysctls in a Kubernetes cluster, and auditing.
title: Cluster Administration — Managing a cluster

instruction: How does Kubernetes implement access control for its own API?
response: Kubernetes implements access control for its own API as described in the topic 'Controlling Access to the Kubernetes API'.
title: Cluster Administration — Managing a cluster

instruction: What is the difference between authentication and authorization in Kubernetes?
response: Authentication and authorization are separate concepts in Kubernetes. Authentication explains the various authentication options, while authorization controls how HTTP calls are handled.
title: Cluster Administration — Managing a cluster

instruction: What is the purpose of admission controllers in Kubernetes?
response: Admission controllers are plug-ins that intercept requests to the Kubernetes API server after authentication and authorization, as explained in the topic 'Using Admission Controllers'.
title: Cluster Administration — Managing a cluster

instruction: What is the benefit of configuring a native sidecar container to start ahead of init containers?
response: You can configure a native sidecar container to start ahead of init containers.
title: Adopting Sidecar Containers — Benefits of a built-in sidecar container

instruction: How are sidecar containers terminated in a Kubernetes Pod?
response: Sidecar containers are terminated with a SIGTERM signal once all the regular containers are completed and terminated. If the sidecar container isn’t gracefully shut down, a SIGKILL signal will be used to terminate it.
title: Adopting Sidecar Containers — Benefits of a built-in sidecar container

instruction: How do native sidecar containers behave with Jobs when restartPolicy is set to OnFailure or Never?
response: Native sidecar containers do not block Pod completion with Jobs, when restartPolicy is set to OnFailure or Never.
title: Adopting Sidecar Containers — Benefits of a built-in sidecar container

instruction: What are the allowed kubelet versions when using kubeadm?
response: The allowed kubelet versions are the same version as kubeadm or three versions older. For example, if kubeadm is at version 1.36, the kubelet version on the host must be at 1.36, 1.35, 1.34, or 1.33.
title: Creating a cluster with kubeadm — kubeadm's skew against the kubelet

instruction: What are the version requirements for joining a node to a cluster created with kubeadm?
response: The kubeadm binary used for kubeadm join must match the last version of kubeadm used to create the cluster with kubeadm init or to upgrade the same node with kubeadm upgrade.
title: Creating a cluster with kubeadm — kubeadm's skew against the kubelet

instruction: What version of kubeadm can be used to upgrade a node created or upgraded with a different version of kubeadm?
response: The version of kubeadm used for upgrading the node must be at the same minor version or one minor version newer than the version of kubeadm used for managing the node.
title: Creating a cluster with kubeadm — kubeadm's skew against the kubelet

instruction: Does the scheduler consider QoS when choosing preemption targets?
response: No, the scheduler's preemption logic does not consider QoS when choosing preemption targets.
title: Pod Priority and Preemption — Interactions between Pod priority and quality of service

instruction: How does the kubelet determine pod order for node-pressure eviction?
response: The kubelet uses Priority to determine pod order for node-pressure eviction.
title: Pod Priority and Preemption — Interactions between Pod priority and quality of service

instruction: Can a Pod with lower priority be evicted if its usage does not exceed its requests?
response: Yes, a Pod with lower priority that is not exceeding its requests may be evicted if another Pod with higher priority exceeds its requests.
title: Pod Priority and Preemption — Interactions between Pod priority and quality of service

instruction: What is the purpose of the Pod failure policy in Kubernetes?
response: The Pod failure policy is used to avoid unnecessary Pod restarts based on custom Pod Conditions.
title: Handling retriable and non-retriable pod failures with Pod failure policy — Using Pod failure policy to avoid unnecessary Pod retries based on custom Pod Conditions

instruction: How do you add a custom condition to a Pod in Kubernetes?
response: You can add a custom condition to a Pod by creating a patch file, selecting a Pod, and applying the patch using the `kubectl patch` command.
title: Handling retriable and non-retriable pod failures with Pod failure policy — Using Pod failure policy to avoid unnecessary Pod retries based on custom Pod Conditions

instruction: What is the expected outcome when a Job is configured with a Pod failure policy and a Pod fails due to a custom condition?
response: The Job will be marked as failed with a condition reason of `PodFailurePolicy` and a message containing more detailed information about the Job termination.
title: Handling retriable and non-retriable pod failures with Pod failure policy — Using Pod failure policy to avoid unnecessary Pod retries based on custom Pod Conditions

instruction: What happens if a ConfigMap referenced in a Pod specification does not exist?
response: If the ConfigMap doesn't exist, the configuration for which it provides data in the Pod (for example: environment variable, mounted volume) will be empty.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a ConfigMap — Optional ConfigMaps

instruction: What happens if a ConfigMap exists but the referenced key is non-existent?
response: If the referenced key is non-existent, the data is also empty.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a ConfigMap — Optional ConfigMaps

instruction: Can you mark volumes and files provided by a ConfigMap as optional?
response: Yes, you can mark volumes and files provided by a ConfigMap as optional. Kubernetes always creates the mount paths for the volume, even if the referenced ConfigMap or key doesn't exist.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a ConfigMap — Optional ConfigMaps

instruction: What happens if the number of completions is set to less than the number of items in the queue?
response: Not all items will be processed.
title: Coarse Parallel Processing Using a Work Queue — Caveats

instruction: What happens if the number of completions is set to more than the number of items in the queue?
response: The Job will not appear to be completed, even though all items in the queue have been processed. It will start additional pods which will block waiting for a message.
title: Coarse Parallel Processing Using a Work Queue — Caveats

instruction: How can you ensure that the number of completions matches the number of items in the queue?
response: You would need to make your own mechanism to spot when there is work to do and measure the size of the queue, setting the number of completions to match.
title: Coarse Parallel Processing Using a Work Queue — Caveats

instruction: What is the unlikely race condition that can occur with this pattern?
response: If the container is killed in between the time that the message is acknowledged by the amqp-consume command and the time that the container exits with success, or if the node crashes before the kubelet is able to post the success of the pod back to the API server, then the Job will not appear to be complete, even though all items in the queue have been processed.
title: Coarse Parallel Processing Using a Work Queue — Caveats

instruction: What is the purpose of a LimitRange in a Kubernetes namespace?
response: A LimitRange is a policy to constrain the resource allocations (limits and requests) that you can specify for each applicable object kind in a namespace.
title: Limit Ranges

instruction: What types of constraints can a LimitRange enforce in a namespace?
response: A LimitRange can enforce minimum and maximum compute resources usage per Pod or Container, enforce minimum and maximum storage request per PersistentVolumeClaim, enforce a ratio between request and limit for a resource, set default request/limit for compute resources, and automatically inject them to Containers at runtime.
title: Limit Ranges

instruction: What happens when there is at least one LimitRange object in a namespace?
response: Kubernetes constrains resource allocations to Pods in a particular namespace.
title: Limit Ranges

instruction: What is the purpose of the podFailurePolicy field in a Job specification?
response: The podFailurePolicy field enables your cluster to handle Pod failures based on the container exit codes and the Pod conditions, providing a more fine-grained control over Pod failures than the Pod backoff failure policy.
title: Jobs — Pod failure policy

instruction: What happens when a Pod failure policy is matched with the FailJob action?
response: When a Pod failure policy is matched with the FailJob action, the Pod's job is marked as Failed, and all running Pods are terminated.
title: Jobs — Pod failure policy

instruction: How do you restrict a rule in the podFailurePolicy to a specific container?
response: You can restrict a rule to a specific container by specifying its name in spec.podFailurePolicy.rules[*].onExitCodes.containerName. When not specified, the rule applies to all containers.
title: Jobs — Pod failure policy

instruction: What is the default handling when no rule matches a Pod failure in the podFailurePolicy?
response: When no rule matches the Pod failure, the default handling applies.
title: Jobs — Pod failure policy

instruction: What is the purpose of the NodeSpec configSource field?
response: The configSource field is used to specify the source of the node's configuration for the DynamicKubeletConfig feature. However, this feature is removed and the field is deprecated.
title: Node — NodeSpec

instruction: What is the relationship between the podCIDR and podCIDRs fields in NodeSpec?
response: The podCIDR field represents the pod IP range assigned to the node, and the podCIDRs field represents the IP ranges assigned to the node for usage by Pods on that node. If the podCIDRs field is specified, the 0th entry must match the podCIDR field.
title: Node — NodeSpec

instruction: What is the effect of setting the unschedulable field to true in NodeSpec?
response: Setting the unschedulable field to true controls node schedulability of new pods, making the node unschedulable by default.
title: Node — NodeSpec

instruction: How do I list the currently supported addons in Minikube?
response: You can list the currently supported addons by running the command `minikube addons list`.
title: Hello Minikube — Enable addons

instruction: How do I enable an addon in Minikube?
response: You can enable an addon by running the command `minikube addons enable <addon_name>`, for example, `minikube addons enable metrics-server`.
title: Hello Minikube — Enable addons

instruction: How do I check the output from the metrics-server addon?
response: You can check the output from the metrics-server addon by running the command `kubectl top pods`.
title: Hello Minikube — Enable addons

instruction: What is the default image registry used by kubeadm?
response: By default, kubeadm pulls images from registry.k8s.io.
title: kubeadm init — Using custom images

instruction: How can you override the default image registry used by kubeadm?
response: You can override this behavior by using kubeadm with a configuration file.
title: kubeadm init — Using custom images

instruction: What steps are required to ensure that custom images are pushed to a custom repository in paths that kubeadm can consume?
response: You must pull images from the default paths at registry.k8s.io using kubeadm config images {list|pull}, and then push images to the paths from kubeadm config images list --config=config.yaml, where config.yaml contains the custom imageRepository, and/or imageTag for etcd and CoreDNS.
title: kubeadm init — Using custom images

instruction: What is the purpose of the CredentialProvider in Kubelet Configuration?
response: The CredentialProvider represents an exec plugin to be invoked by the kubelet to provide credentials for image pull.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1) — CredentialProvider

instruction: How does the kubelet determine which CredentialProvider to invoke for a given image?
response: The kubelet uses the matchImages field in the CredentialProviderConfig to match against the requested image. A match exists when all of the following are true: Both contain the same number of domain parts and each part matches, the URL path of an imageMatch must be a prefix of the target image URL path, and if the imageMatch contains a port, then the port must match in the image as well.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1) — CredentialProvider

instruction: What is the purpose of the defaultCacheDuration field in the CredentialProviderConfig?
response: The defaultCacheDuration field specifies the default duration the plugin will cache credentials in-memory if a cache duration is not provided in the plugin response.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1) — CredentialProvider

instruction: What is the required input version of the exec CredentialProviderRequest?
response: The required input version of the exec CredentialProviderRequest is credentialprovider.kubelet.k8s.io/v1.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1) — CredentialProvider

instruction: What is the benefit of using a DRA driver that supports seamless upgrades?
response: Using a DRA driver that supports seamless upgrades can minimize scheduling delays when your driver updates.
title: Good practices for Dynamic Resource Allocation as a Cluster Admin — Use drivers with seamless upgrade if available

instruction: What are the potential issues with driver downtime for upgrades if seamless upgrades are not available?
response: During driver downtime for upgrades, you may observe that pods cannot start unless the claims they depend on were already prepared for use, and cleanup after the last pod which used a claim gets delayed until the driver is available again.
title: Good practices for Dynamic Resource Allocation as a Cluster Admin — Use drivers with seamless upgrade if available

instruction: What version of kubelet is required to use seamless upgrades with DRA drivers?
response: Kubelet versions 1.33 and above are required to use seamless upgrades with DRA drivers.
title: Good practices for Dynamic Resource Allocation as a Cluster Admin — Use drivers with seamless upgrade if available

instruction: How do you advertise a new node-level extended resource to the cluster?
response: The cluster operator can submit a PATCH HTTP request to the API server to specify the available quantity in the status.capacity for a node in the cluster.
title: Resource Management for Pods and Containers — Managing extended resources

instruction: What is the delay between patching the node capacity with a new resource and the time when the first Pod that requests the resource can be scheduled on that node?
response: There may be a short delay between patching the node capacity with a new resource and the time when the first Pod that requests the resource can be scheduled on that node.
title: Resource Management for Pods and Containers — Managing extended resources

instruction: How do you specify the extended resources that are handled by scheduler extenders in scheduler configuration?
response: You can specify the extended resources that are handled by scheduler extenders in scheduler configuration by including the managedResources field in the extender configuration.
title: Resource Management for Pods and Containers — Managing extended resources

instruction: How do you configure a scheduler policy to indicate that a cluster-level extended resource is handled by the scheduler extender?
response: You can configure a scheduler policy to indicate that a cluster-level extended resource is handled by the scheduler extender by including the ignoredByScheduler field set to true in the managedResources configuration.
title: Resource Management for Pods and Containers — Managing extended resources

instruction: What are the minimum requirements for running this tutorial?
response: You need to have a Kubernetes cluster and the kubectl command-line tool configured to communicate with your cluster. It is recommended to run this tutorial on a cluster with at least two nodes that are not acting as control plane hosts.
title: Connect a Frontend to a Backend Using Services — Before you begin

instruction: How can you create a Kubernetes cluster if you don't already have one?
response: You can create one by using minikube or by using one of these Kubernetes playgrounds: iximiuz Labs, Killercoda, or KodeKloud.
title: Connect a Frontend to a Backend Using Services — Before you begin

instruction: What is the alternative to Services with external load balancers if your environment does not support it?
response: You can use a Service of type NodePort instead.
title: Connect a Frontend to a Backend Using Services — Before you begin

instruction: What is the purpose of the `continue` field in the CSIStorageCapacity API?
response: The `continue` field is used to retrieve more results from the server. It should be set when retrieving more results and should be used in conjunction with identical query parameters from a previous query result.
title: CSIStorageCapacity — getList All Namespaces

instruction: What happens if the client uses a continue value that is no longer valid due to expiration or a configuration change on the server?
response: The server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token. The client must restart their list without the continue field to receive a consistent list.
title: CSIStorageCapacity — getList All Namespaces

instruction: What is the semantic of the watch request when `sendInitialEvents` option is set?
response: When `sendInitialEvents` option is set, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. A synthetic "Bookmark" event will be sent once all such events have been sent, reporting the ResourceVersion (RV) corresponding to the set of objects.
title: CSIStorageCapacity — getList All Namespaces

instruction: What factors directly affect memory usage by node-local-dns Pods?
response: Memory usage by node-local-dns Pods is influenced by the DNS query pattern, but not directly by the cluster size or the number of Services/EndpointSlices.
title: Using NodeLocal DNSCache in Kubernetes Clusters — Setting memory limits

instruction: How can you reduce memory usage by node-local-dns Pods?
response: You can reduce memory usage by specifying smaller cache sizes or by setting an upper limit using the max_concurrent option in the forward plugin.
title: Using NodeLocal DNSCache in Kubernetes Clusters — Setting memory limits

instruction: What happens if a node-local-dns Pod exceeds its configured resource limit?
response: If a node-local-dns Pod attempts to use more memory than is available, the operating system may shut down that pod's container, leading to a brief DNS downtime each time the container fails.
title: Using NodeLocal DNSCache in Kubernetes Clusters — Setting memory limits

instruction: How can you determine a suitable memory limit for node-local-dns Pods?
response: You can determine a suitable memory limit by running node-local-dns pods without a limit and measuring the peak usage, or by setting up and using a VerticalPodAutoscaler in recommender mode.
title: Using NodeLocal DNSCache in Kubernetes Clusters — Setting memory limits

instruction: What is the purpose of the `progressDeadlineSeconds` parameter in a Deployment spec?
response: The `progressDeadlineSeconds` parameter denotes the number of seconds the Deployment controller waits before indicating that the Deployment progress has stalled.
title: Deployments — Failed Deployment

instruction: How can you detect if a Deployment has exceeded its progress deadline?
response: You can use the `kubectl rollout status` command, which returns a non-zero exit code if the Deployment has exceeded the progression deadline.
title: Deployments — Failed Deployment

instruction: What is the meaning of a `Progressing` condition with `status: 'False'` and `reason: ProgressDeadlineExceeded` in a Deployment's status?
response: This indicates that the Deployment progress deadline has been exceeded, and the Deployment rollout has stalled.
title: Deployments — Failed Deployment

instruction: What is the difference between `status: 'True'` and `status: 'False'` in a `Progressing` condition?
response: A `status: 'True'` means that the Deployment is either in the middle of a rollout and it is progressing or that it has successfully completed its progress and the minimum required new replicas are available. A `status: 'False'` means that the Deployment progress has stalled or failed.
title: Deployments — Failed Deployment

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'allowWatchBookmarks' parameter in the GET /apis/node.k8s.io/v1/watch/runtimeclasses request?
response: The 'allowWatchBookmarks' parameter requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: RuntimeClass — getWatch List

instruction: What happens when the 'continue' field is empty and the 'limit' parameter is specified in the GET /apis/node.k8s.io/v1/watch/runtimeclasses request?
response: Clients may assume that no more results are available.
title: RuntimeClass — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'sendInitialEvents=true' together with 'watch=true' in the GET /apis/node.k8s.io/v1/watch/runtimeclasses request?
response: The watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event with the ResourceVersion (RV) corresponding to the set of objects, and then proceed as usual, sending watch events corresponding to changes to objects watched.
title: RuntimeClass — getWatch List

instruction: What are the possible statuses of a ControllerModifyVolume operation?
response: The possible statuses are: Pending, InProgress, and Infeasible.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — ModifyVolumeStatus

instruction: What does the 'Infeasible' status indicate in a ControllerModifyVolume operation?
response: The 'Infeasible' status indicates that the request has been rejected as invalid by the CSI driver. To resolve the error, a valid VolumeAttributesClass needs to be specified.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — ModifyVolumeStatus

instruction: How should consumers handle unknown statuses in a ControllerModifyVolume operation?
response: Consumers should check for unknown statuses and fail appropriately.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — ModifyVolumeStatus

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' parameter in the DELETE /apis/storage.k8s.io/v1/csinodes API call?
response: The 'continue' parameter is used to retrieve more results from the server when the initial query returns a limited number of items. It should be set to the value received from a previous query result with identical query parameters, and the server may reject a continue value it does not recognize.
title: CSINode — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'limit' to a non-zero value in the DELETE /apis/storage.k8s.io/v1/csinodes API call?
response: Setting a limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items, and clients should only use the presence of the 'continue' field to determine whether more results are available. The server guarantees that the objects returned when using continue will be identical to issuing a single list call without a limit.
title: CSINode — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential' parameter in the DELETE /apis/storage.k8s.io/v1/csinodes API call?
response: This parameter triggers an unsafe deletion of the resource in case the normal deletion flow fails with a corrupt object error. It ignores finalizer constraints, skips precondition checks, and removes the object from the storage, which may potentially break the cluster if the workload associated with the resource being unsafe-deleted relies on normal deletion flow.
title: CSINode — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'resourceVersion' and 'resourceVersionMatch' in the DELETE /apis/storage.k8s.io/v1/csinodes API call?
response: Setting 'resourceVersion' and 'resourceVersionMatch' determines the constraint on what resource versions a request may be served from, and how resourceVersion is applied to list calls, respectively. It is highly recommended that resourceVersionMatch be set for list calls where resourceVersion is set.
title: CSINode — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What happens to pods in a StatefulSet when a node is shut down but not detected by kubelet's Node Shutdown Manager?
response: The pods will be stuck in terminating status on the shutdown node and cannot move to a new running node, and if there are volumes used by the pods, the VolumeAttachments will not be deleted from the original shutdown node.
title: Node Shutdowns — Non-graceful node shutdown handling

instruction: How can a user mitigate the situation where pods in a StatefulSet are stuck in terminating status on a shutdown node?
response: A user can manually add the taint node.kubernetes.io/out-of-service with either NoExecute or NoSchedule effect to a Node marking it out-of-service.
title: Node Shutdowns — Non-graceful node shutdown handling

instruction: What happens to pods on a node marked out-of-service with the taint node.kubernetes.io/out-of-service?
response: The pods on the node will be forcefully deleted if there are no matching tolerations on it and volume detach operations for the pods terminating on the node will happen immediately.
title: Node Shutdowns — Non-graceful node shutdown handling

instruction: When should the out-of-service taint be removed after manually adding it to a node?
response: The out-of-service taint should be removed after the pods are moved to a new node and the user has checked that the shutdown node has been recovered.
title: Node Shutdowns — Non-graceful node shutdown handling

instruction: What are VolumeSnapshotContents in the context of Kubernetes?
response: VolumeSnapshotContents are resources in the cluster that carry the details of the real volume snapshot on the storage system, available for use by cluster users.
title: Volume Snapshots — Lifecycle of a volume snapshot and volume snapshot content

instruction: How are VolumeSnapshotContents created?
response: A cluster administrator creates a number of VolumeSnapshotContents, which exist in the Kubernetes API and are available for consumption.
title: Volume Snapshots — Lifecycle of a volume snapshot and volume snapshot content

instruction: What is the purpose of a VolumeSnapshotClass in dynamically provisioning a snapshot?
response: The VolumeSnapshotClass specifies storage provider-specific parameters to use when taking a snapshot from a PersistentVolumeClaim.
title: Volume Snapshots — Lifecycle of a volume snapshot and volume snapshot content

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /api/v1/pods request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: Pod — getList All Namespaces

instruction: What happens when a client uses the `continue` field to retrieve more results from the server, but the specified continue value is no longer valid?
response: The server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token. The client may send another list request with the token received with the 410 error, and the server will respond with a list starting from the next key, but from the latest snapshot.
title: Pod — getList All Namespaces

instruction: What is the effect of setting `limit` in the GET /api/v1/pods request?
response: Setting a limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items (up to zero items) in the event all requested objects are filtered out. Clients may assume that no more results are available if limit is specified and the continue field is empty.
title: Pod — getList All Namespaces

instruction: What is required to use custom volume populators in Kubernetes?
response: You must enable the AnyVolumeDataSource feature gate for the kube-apiserver and kube-controller-manager.
title: Volume Populators and Data Sources — Volume populators and data sources

instruction: What is the difference between the dataSource and dataSourceRef fields in a PVC spec?
response: The dataSource field can only contain a reference to another PersistentVolumeClaim or to a VolumeSnapshot, whereas the dataSourceRef field can contain a reference to any object in the same namespace, except for core objects other than PVCs.
title: Volume Populators and Data Sources — Volume populators and data sources

instruction: What is the recommended approach for clusters with the AnyVolumeDataSource feature gate enabled?
response: Use of the dataSourceRef field is preferred over the dataSource field.
title: Volume Populators and Data Sources — Volume populators and data sources

instruction: What is the primary purpose of kubectl auth reconcile?
response: The primary purpose of kubectl auth reconcile is to reconcile rules for RBAC role, role binding, cluster role, and cluster role binding objects.
title: kubectl auth reconcile — Synopsis

instruction: What happens to missing objects when using kubectl auth reconcile?
response: Missing objects are created, and the containing namespace is created for namespaced objects, if required.
title: kubectl auth reconcile — Synopsis

instruction: How does kubectl auth reconcile handle existing roles and bindings?
response: Existing roles are updated to include the permissions in the input objects, and remove extra permissions if --remove-extra-permissions is specified. Existing bindings are updated to include the subjects in the input objects, and remove extra subjects if --remove-extra-subjects is specified.
title: kubectl auth reconcile — Synopsis

instruction: What is the default value for the --certificate-renewal flag?
response: The default value for the --certificate-renewal flag is true.
title: kubeadm upgrade — Options

instruction: How can you specify a custom kubeconfig file for kubeadm to use?
response: You can specify a custom kubeconfig file for kubeadm to use by setting the --kubeconfig flag to the path of the file.
title: kubeadm upgrade — Options

instruction: What is the purpose of the --patches flag?
response: The --patches flag is used to specify a directory containing patch files that can be applied during the upgrade process.
title: kubeadm upgrade — Options

instruction: What is the default patch type used when applying patches via the --patches flag?
response: The default patch type used when applying patches via the --patches flag is 'strategic'.
title: kubeadm upgrade — Options

instruction: How can you skip certain phases during the upgrade process?
response: You can skip certain phases during the upgrade process by listing them in the --skip-phases flag.
title: kubeadm upgrade — Options

instruction: What is the recommended way to clean up the control plane in a kubeadm cluster?
response: You can use kubeadm reset on the control plane host to trigger a best-effort clean up.
title: Creating a cluster with kubeadm — Clean up the control plane

instruction: What should be matched with the kubeadm version in a kubeadm cluster?
response: The versions of the control plane components, kube-proxy, and kubelet.
title: Creating a cluster with kubeadm — Clean up the control plane

instruction: Where can I find more information about the kubeadm reset subcommand and its options?
response: See the kubeadm reset reference documentation.
title: Creating a cluster with kubeadm — Clean up the control plane

instruction: How can you return snapshot logs from a pod with multiple containers?
response: You can use the command `kubectl logs nginx --all-containers=true` or `kubectl logs nginx -all-containers=true` to return snapshot logs from a pod with multiple containers.
title: GETTING STARTED — logs

instruction: How can you display only the most recent 20 lines of output in a pod?
response: You can use the command `kubectl logs --tail=20 nginx` to display only the most recent 20 lines of output in a pod.
title: GETTING STARTED — logs

instruction: How can you show logs from a pod written in the last hour?
response: You can use the command `kubectl logs --since=1h nginx` to show logs from a pod written in the last hour.
title: GETTING STARTED — logs

instruction: What is the default authorization mechanism provided by Kubernetes?
response: Kubernetes ships an integrated Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) component that matches an incoming user or group to a set of permissions bundled into roles.
title: Securing a Cluster — API Authorization

instruction: Why is it recommended to use the Node and RBAC authorizers together with the NodeRestriction admission plugin?
response: It is recommended that you use the Node and RBAC authorizers together, in combination with the NodeRestriction admission plugin.
title: Securing a Cluster — API Authorization

instruction: What should be considered when creating limited roles to prevent accidental escalation?
response: More limited roles should be carefully reviewed to prevent accidental escalation.
title: Securing a Cluster — API Authorization

instruction: What should be reviewed when a cluster uses Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA)?
response: If your cluster uses Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA), review DRA synthetic subresource authorization (resourceclaims/binding and resourceclaims/driver) and grant only the minimum required verbs to each component.
title: Securing a Cluster — API Authorization

instruction: Why does the kubelet manage the hosts file for each container in a Pod?
response: The kubelet manages the hosts file to prevent the container runtime from modifying it after the containers have already been started.
title: Adding entries to Pod /etc/hosts with HostAliases — Why does the kubelet manage the hosts file?

instruction: What happens if you make manual changes to the hosts file inside a container?
response: Those changes are lost when the container exits.
title: Adding entries to Pod /etc/hosts with HostAliases — Why does the kubelet manage the hosts file?

instruction: Why is it necessary for the kubelet to manage the hosts file even when using container runtimes other than Docker Engine?
response: To ensure the outcome is as intended regardless of which container runtime is used.
title: Adding entries to Pod /etc/hosts with HostAliases — Why does the kubelet manage the hosts file?

instruction: What is the purpose of Finalizer Protection for Service LoadBalancers?
response: Finalizer Protection for Service LoadBalancers was introduced to prevent cloud resources from being orphaned after the associated Service is deleted.
title: Create an External Load Balancer — Garbage collecting load balancers

instruction: What is the name of the finalizer attached to a Service with type LoadBalancer?
response: The finalizer attached to a Service with type LoadBalancer is named service.kubernetes.io/load-balancer-cleanup.
title: Create an External Load Balancer — Garbage collecting load balancers

instruction: What prevents the service controller from crashing and leaving dangling load balancer resources?
response: The finalizer will only be removed after the load balancer resource is cleaned up, preventing the service controller from crashing and leaving dangling load balancer resources.
title: Create an External Load Balancer — Garbage collecting load balancers

instruction: How does a Pod access a Persistent Volume (PV) that it has claimed?
response: A Pod accesses a claimed PV by including a persistentVolumeClaim section in its volumes block.
title: Persistent Volumes — Using

instruction: What happens to a bound Persistent Volume (PV) once a user has claimed it?
response: The bound PV belongs to the user for as long as they need it.
title: Persistent Volumes — Using

instruction: What is required to schedule a Pod and access its claimed Persistent Volume (PV)?
response: A user must include a persistentVolumeClaim section in a Pod's volumes block to schedule the Pod and access its claimed PV.
title: Persistent Volumes — Using

instruction: What is the minimum requirement for a Pod network add-on in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: You must deploy a Container Network Interface (CNI) based Pod network add-on so that your Pods can communicate with each other.
title: Creating a cluster with kubeadm — Installing a Pod network add-on

instruction: How can you resolve a collision between the Pod network and host networks?
response: You should think of a suitable CIDR block to use instead, then use that during kubeadm init with --pod-network-cidr and as a replacement in your network plugin's YAML.
title: Creating a cluster with kubeadm — Installing a Pod network add-on

instruction: How can you confirm that a Pod network is working in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can confirm that it is working by checking that the CoreDNS Pod is Running in the output of kubectl get pods --all-namespaces.
title: Creating a cluster with kubeadm — Installing a Pod network add-on

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` parameter in a GET /apis/batch/v1/watch/jobs request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` parameter requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: Job — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What happens if a client uses a continue value from a previous query result with different query parameters?
response: The server may reject a continue value it does not recognize, and respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token.
title: Job — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the effect of setting `resourceVersionMatch` to `NotOlderThan` when `sendInitialEvents` is true?
response: The bookmark event is sent when the state is synced to a `resourceVersion` at least as fresh as the one provided by the ListOptions, and is interpreted as 'data at least as new as the provided `resourceVersion`'.
title: Job — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What happens to a Job's Pods when the Job is deleted using kubectl?
response: When you delete the job using kubectl, all the pods it created are deleted too.
title: Jobs — Job termination and cleanup

instruction: How does a Job's activeDeadlineSeconds field affect its termination?
response: The activeDeadlineSeconds field applies to the duration of the job, no matter how many Pods are created. Once a Job reaches activeDeadlineSeconds, all of its running Pods are terminated and the Job status will become type: Failed with reason: DeadlineExceeded.
title: Jobs — Job termination and cleanup

instruction: What is the relationship between a Job's activeDeadlineSeconds and backoffLimit fields?
response: A Job's activeDeadlineSeconds takes precedence over its backoffLimit. Therefore, a Job that is retrying one or more failed Pods will not deploy additional Pods once it reaches the time limit specified by activeDeadlineSeconds, even if the backoffLimit is not yet reached.
title: Jobs — Job termination and cleanup

instruction: How do you generate a GMSA credential spec in JSON format?
response: You can generate a GMSA credential spec in JSON format using the PowerShell script and the New-CredentialSpec command. For example, to create a GMSA credential spec named WebApp1, invoke New-CredentialSpec -Name WebApp1 -AccountName WebApp1 -Domain $(Get-ADDomain -Current LocalComputer).
title: Configure GMSA for Windows Pods and containers — Create GMSA credential spec resources

instruction: What are the necessary header fields to convert a credspec file from JSON to YAML format and make it a GMSACredentialSpec custom resource?
response: The necessary header fields are apiVersion, kind, metadata, and credspec. These fields should be applied to the credspec file in YAML format to make it a GMSACredentialSpec custom resource that can be configured in Kubernetes.
title: Configure GMSA for Windows Pods and containers — Create GMSA credential spec resources

instruction: How do you apply a GMSACredentialSpec custom resource to a Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can apply a GMSACredentialSpec custom resource to a Kubernetes cluster using the kubectl apply command. For example, to apply the credential spec resource saved as gmsa-Webapp1-credspec.yaml, use the command kubectl apply -f gmsa-Webapp1-credspec.yml.
title: Configure GMSA for Windows Pods and containers — Create GMSA credential spec resources

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the `GET /apis/admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1/watch/mutatingwebhookconfigurations/{name}` API endpoint?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field allows the client to request watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag, and clients should not assume bookmarks are returned at any specific interval.
title: MutatingWebhookConfiguration — getWatch

instruction: What is the effect of setting `limit` in the `GET /apis/admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1/watch/mutatingwebhookconfigurations/{name}` API endpoint?
response: Setting `limit` returns a maximum number of responses, and the server sets the `continue` field on the list metadata to a value that can be used with the same initial query to retrieve the next set of results.
title: MutatingWebhookConfiguration — getWatch

instruction: What is the purpose of the `sendInitialEvents` field in the `GET /apis/admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1/watch/mutatingwebhookconfigurations/{name}` API endpoint?
response: The `sendInitialEvents` field allows the client to request synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection when `watch=true`. This is useful for clients to get the current state of objects before watching for changes.
title: MutatingWebhookConfiguration — getWatch

instruction: What is the default range of UIDs/GIDs assigned by the kubelet to pods?
response: The default range of UIDs/GIDs assigned by the kubelet to pods is above 0-65535, based on the assumption that the host's files and processes use UIDs/GIDs within this range.
title: User Namespaces — Set up a node to support user namespaces

instruction: What are the constraints for the subordinate ID range that you assign to the kubelet user?
response: The subordinate user ID, that starts the UID range for Pods, must be a multiple of 65536 and must also be greater than or equal to 65536. The subordinate ID count must be a multiple of 65536 and must be at least 65536 x <maxPods> where <maxPods> is the maximum number of pods that can run on the node.
title: User Namespaces — Set up a node to support user namespaces

instruction: How do you configure a custom range for user IDs and group IDs for pods?
response: To configure a custom range, the node needs to have a user kubelet in the system, the binary getsubids installed and in the PATH for the kubelet binary, and a configuration of subordinate UIDs/GIDs for the kubelet user.
title: User Namespaces — Set up a node to support user namespaces

instruction: What type of Kubernetes object is 'kubernetes.io/config.hash' an annotation on?
response: The 'kubernetes.io/config.hash' annotation is used on a Pod.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — kubernetes.io/config.hash

instruction: What is the value of the 'kubernetes.io/config.hash' annotation when a static Pod is created by the kubelet?
response: The value of the annotation is the UID of the Pod.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — kubernetes.io/config.hash

instruction: What is set by the kubelet when creating a static Pod based on a given manifest?
response: The kubelet sets the .spec.nodeName to the current node name as if the Pod was scheduled to the node.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — kubernetes.io/config.hash

instruction: What type of Kubernetes object is the annotation 'kube-scheduler-simulator.sigs.k8s.io/selected-node' used on?
response: The annotation 'kube-scheduler-simulator.sigs.k8s.io/selected-node' is used on a Pod.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — kube-scheduler-simulator.sigs.k8s.io/selected-node

instruction: What is the purpose of the annotation 'kube-scheduler-simulator.sigs.k8s.io/selected-node'?
response: This annotation records the node that is selected by the scheduling cycle, used by https://sigs.k8s.io/kube-scheduler-simulator.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — kube-scheduler-simulator.sigs.k8s.io/selected-node

instruction: What type of Kubernetes object is the label 'kubernetes.io/arch' used on?
response: The label 'kubernetes.io/arch' is used on a Node.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — kube-scheduler-simulator.sigs.k8s.io/selected-node

instruction: How is the value of the label 'kubernetes.io/arch' populated?
response: The Kubelet populates this with runtime.GOARCH as defined by Go.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — kube-scheduler-simulator.sigs.k8s.io/selected-node

instruction: What are the feature gates used to toggle beta and alpha CPU management policy options?
response: The feature gates CPUManagerPolicyBetaOptions and CPUManagerPolicyAlphaOptions are used to toggle beta and alpha CPU management policy options, respectively.
title: Control CPU Management Policies on the Node — Static policy options

instruction: How can the full-pcpus-only option be enabled in the CPUManager policy options?
response: The full-pcpus-only option can be enabled by adding full-pcpus-only=true to the CPUManager policy options.
title: Control CPU Management Policies on the Node — Static policy options

instruction: What happens when both full-pcpus-only and distribute-cpus-across-numa options are set?
response: When both full-pcpus-only and distribute-cpus-across-numa options are set, CPUs will be distributed across NUMA nodes in chunks of full-pcpus rather than individual cores.
title: Control CPU Management Policies on the Node — Static policy options

instruction: How can the strict-cpu-reservation option be enabled and what steps need to be taken afterwards?
response: The strict-cpu-reservation option can be enabled by adding strict-cpu-reservation=true to the CPUManager policy options followed by removing the /var/lib/kubelet/cpu_manager_state file and restarting kubelet.
title: Control CPU Management Policies on the Node — Static policy options

instruction: What is the purpose of the `continue` field in the response of a GET /apis/coordination.k8s.io/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/leases request?
response: The `continue` field is used to retrieve more results from the server. It should be set when retrieving more results and should be set to the value from a previous query result with identical query parameters (except for the value of continue).
title: Lease — getWatch List

instruction: What is the purpose of the `sendInitialEvents` option when `watch=true`?
response: The `sendInitialEvents` option may be set together with `watch=true` to start the watch stream with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic "Bookmark" event will be sent.
title: Lease — getWatch List

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' field in the response of the GET /apis/discovery.k8s.io/v1/endpointslices endpoint?
response: The 'continue' field is used to retrieve more results from the server when the list of objects is too large to be returned in a single response. It should be set when retrieving more results from the server and is used to resume a list query from where it left off.
title: EndpointSlice — getList All Namespaces

instruction: What happens when the client uses the 'limit' field and the 'continue' field is empty?
response: When the client uses the 'limit' field and the 'continue' field is empty, clients may assume that no more results are available.
title: EndpointSlice — getList All Namespaces

instruction: What is the semantic of the watch request when 'sendInitialEvents' option is set?
response: When 'sendInitialEvents' option is set, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic "Bookmark" event will be sent, reporting the ResourceVersion (RV) corresponding to the set of objects, and be marked with "k8s.io/initial-events-end": "true" annotation.
title: EndpointSlice — getList All Namespaces

instruction: How do you rollback to the previous deployment in Kubernetes?
response: You can use the command `kubectl rollout undo deployment/abc` to rollback to the previous deployment.
title: GETTING STARTED — rollout

instruction: How do you check the rollout status of a daemonset in Kubernetes?
response: You can use the command `kubectl rollout status daemonset/foo` to check the rollout status of a daemonset.
title: GETTING STARTED — rollout

instruction: How do you restart a deployment in Kubernetes?
response: You can use the command `kubectl rollout restart deployment/abc` to restart a deployment.
title: GETTING STARTED — rollout

instruction: How do you restart deployments with a specific label in Kubernetes?
response: You can use the command `kubectl rollout restart deployment --selector=app=nginx` to restart deployments with the label `app=nginx`.
title: GETTING STARTED — rollout

instruction: What are the valid resource types for rollout management in Kubernetes?
response: Valid resource types include deployments, daemonsets, and statefulsets.
title: GETTING STARTED — rollout

instruction: What is the role of the container runtime in swap memory management?
response: The container runtime is responsible for writing swap configuration settings to the container-level cgroup.
title: Swap memory management — Container runtime integration

instruction: How does the kubelet interact with the container runtime to configure swap?
response: The kubelet uses the container runtime API to direct the container runtime to apply swap configuration settings.
title: Swap memory management — Container runtime integration

instruction: What specific configuration is applied by the container runtime for cgroup v2?
response: The container runtime applies the memory.swap.max configuration setting for cgroup v2.
title: Swap memory management — Container runtime integration

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' field in the GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/resourceclaims/{name} API call?
response: The 'continue' field is used to retrieve more results from the server when the initial query result is not exhaustive. It should be set when retrieving more results and can be used to restart the list without the 'continue' field to get a consistent list.
title: ResourceClaim — getWatch

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'limit' in the GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/resourceclaims/{name} API call?
response: Setting 'limit' may return fewer than the requested amount of items in the event all requested objects are filtered out. The server guarantees that the objects returned when using 'continue' will be identical to issuing a single list call without a limit.
title: ResourceClaim — getWatch

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'sendInitialEvents' option in the GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/resourceclaims/{name} API call?
response: The 'sendInitialEvents' option may be set together with 'watch=true' to begin the watch stream with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. This option requires 'resourceVersionMatch' to also be set.
title: ResourceClaim — getWatch

instruction: What are the different types of cluster networking in Kubernetes?
response: Kubernetes clusters can be categorized into IPv4 only, IPv6 only, IPv4/IPv6 or IPv6/IPv4 dual-stack, and dual-stack types, depending on the IP families configured for the network plugin, kube-apiserver, and kubelet/cloud-controller-manager.
title: Cluster Networking — Cluster networking types

instruction: How do Kubernetes clusters determine the IP families to use?
response: Kubernetes clusters consider the IP families present on the Pods, Services, and Nodes objects, as indicated in node.status.addresses or pod.status.ips, and not the existing IPs of the represented objects.
title: Cluster Networking — Cluster networking types

instruction: What is the requirement for all components in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: All components in a Kubernetes cluster must agree on the configured primary IP family.
title: Cluster Networking — Cluster networking types

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the `GET /apis/apps/v1/controllerrevisions` API call?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK' from the server. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag, and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: ControllerRevision — getList All Namespaces

instruction: What happens when the `continue` field is set in a list call, and the server does not recognize the continue value?
response: The server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token. The client must restart their list without the continue field to get a consistent list.
title: ControllerRevision — getList All Namespaces

instruction: What is the purpose of the `sendInitialEvents` field in the `GET /apis/apps/v1/controllerrevisions` API call?
response: The `sendInitialEvents` field sends synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection when `watch=true`. It requires the `resourceVersionMatch` option to also be set.
title: ControllerRevision — getList All Namespaces

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the `getWatch` API call?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field allows the client to request watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag, and clients should not assume bookmarks are returned at any specific interval.
title: CertificateSigningRequest — getWatch

instruction: What happens when the `continue` field is set in a list call, and the server returns a 410 ResourceExpired error?
response: The client must restart their list without the continue field. Otherwise, the client may send another list request with the token received with the 410 error, and the server will respond with a list starting from the next key, but from the latest snapshot, which is inconsistent from the previous list results.
title: CertificateSigningRequest — getWatch

instruction: What is the semantic of the watch request when `sendInitialEvents` option is set, and `resourceVersionMatch` is set to NotOlderThan?
response: The semantic is 'data at least as new as the provided `resourceVersion' and the bookmark event is sent when the state is synced to a `resourceVersion` at least as fresh as the one provided by the ListOptions.
title: CertificateSigningRequest — getWatch

instruction: What is the default value for the Kubelet's insecure port?
response: The insecure port is controlled by the readOnlyPort option, and its default value is 0 (disabled).
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — KubeletConfiguration

instruction: How does the Kubelet determine the key exchange mechanisms for the server?
response: The Kubelet uses an internal preference order to choose key exchange mechanisms from the list specified in tlsCurvePreferences, or the default Go curves if the list is empty.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — KubeletConfiguration

instruction: What is the effect of setting rotateCertificates to true?
response: Setting rotateCertificates to true enables client certificate rotation, which requires an approver to approve the certificate signing requests (CSRs).
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — KubeletConfiguration

instruction: What is the default authentication mode for the Kubelet's server?
response: The default authentication mode is anonymous, with enabled set to false.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — KubeletConfiguration

instruction: What is the default authorization mode for the Kubelet's server?
response: The default authorization mode is Webhook, with cacheAuthorizedTTL set to 5m and cacheUnauthorizedTTL set to 30s.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — KubeletConfiguration

instruction: What is the purpose of the CRIListStreaming feature gate in Kubernetes?
response: The CRIListStreaming feature gate enables the kubelet to use server-side streaming RPCs to retrieve large numbers of container results, bypassing the per-message size limit imposed by gRPC.
title: Container Runtime Interface (CRI) — List streaming

instruction: What types of environments benefit from the CRIListStreaming feature gate?
response: High container churn environments, such as CI/CD systems, and large-scale batch processing workloads, can benefit from the CRIListStreaming feature gate.
title: Container Runtime Interface (CRI) — List streaming

instruction: What happens if the container runtime does not support streaming RPCs?
response: If the container runtime does not support streaming RPCs, the kubelet automatically falls back to the standard unary RPCs for backward compatibility.
title: Container Runtime Interface (CRI) — List streaming

instruction: What permissions are required to create a TokenRequest in Kubernetes?
response: Users with create rights on serviceaccounts/token can create TokenRequests.
title: Role Based Access Control Good Practices — Token request

instruction: What permissions are required to control webhooks that can read or mutate cluster objects?
response: Users with control over validatingwebhookconfigurations or mutatingwebhookconfigurations can control such webhooks.
title: Role Based Access Control Good Practices — Token request

instruction: How do container runtimes handle container logs in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Container runtimes handle container logs by redirecting any output generated to a containerized application's stdout and stderr streams, and implementing this in different ways, but with standardized integration with the kubelet through the CRI logging format.
title: Logging Architecture — How nodes handle container logs

instruction: What happens to a container's logs when it restarts?
response: If a container restarts, the kubelet keeps one terminated container with its logs.
title: Logging Architecture — How nodes handle container logs

instruction: How do clients access logs in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Clients can access logs by running kubectl logs, which makes logs available to clients via a special feature of the Kubernetes API.
title: Logging Architecture — How nodes handle container logs

instruction: What is the default value of the 'pretty' parameter in the GET /apis/apiregistration.k8s.io/v1/apiservices API call?
response: The default value of the 'pretty' parameter is 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: APIService — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' parameter in the GET /apis/apiregistration.k8s.io/v1/apiservices API call?
response: The 'continue' parameter is used to retrieve more results from the server. It should be set when retrieving more results from the server, and clients may only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters (except for the value of continue) and the server may reject a continue value it does not recognize.
title: APIService — getList

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'limit' to a specific value in the GET /apis/apiregistration.k8s.io/v1/apiservices API call?
response: Setting a limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items (up to zero items) in the event all requested objects are filtered out, and clients should only use the presence of the continue field to determine whether more results are available.
title: APIService — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'sendInitialEvents' parameter in the GET /apis/apiregistration.k8s.io/v1/apiservices API call?
response: The 'sendInitialEvents' parameter may be set together with 'watch=true' to begin the watch stream with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection.
title: APIService — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubectl rollout pause command?
response: The kubectl rollout pause command marks the provided resource as paused.
title: kubectl rollout — See Also

instruction: How can you undo a previous rollout using kubectl?
response: You can undo a previous rollout using the kubectl rollout undo command.
title: kubectl rollout — See Also

instruction: What does the kubectl rollout status command display?
response: The kubectl rollout status command shows the status of the rollout.
title: kubectl rollout — See Also

instruction: What is the purpose of preflight checks in kubeadm?
response: The purpose of preflight checks in kubeadm is to verify preconditions and avoid common cluster startup problems.
title: Implementation details — Preflight checks

instruction: How can the user skip specific preflight checks or all of them in kubeadm?
response: The user can skip specific preflight checks or all of them with the --ignore-preflight-errors option.
title: Implementation details — Preflight checks

instruction: What happens if the Kubernetes version to use is at least one minor version higher than the kubeadm CLI version?
response: [Warning] is displayed.
title: Implementation details — Preflight checks

instruction: What is the purpose of approval for CertificateSigningRequests (CSRs) in Kubernetes?
response: Before a signer issues a certificate based on a CSR, the signer typically checks that the issuance for that CSR has been approved.
title: Certificates and Certificate Signing Requests — Approval or rejection

instruction: How does the kube-controller-manager handle certificate approval for node credentials?
response: The kube-controller-manager ships with a built-in approver for certificates with a signerName of kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client-kubelet that delegates various permissions on CSRs for node credentials to authorization.
title: Certificates and Certificate Signing Requests — Approval or rejection

instruction: What options must be set to configure IPv4/IPv6 dual-stack in the kube-apiserver?
response: You must set --service-cluster-ip-range with both an IPv4 CIDR and an IPv6 CIDR.
title: IPv4/IPv6 dual-stack — Configure IPv4/IPv6 dual-stack

instruction: What is required for bare metal dual-stack nodes?
response: You must set the --node-ip option with both an IPv4 IP and an IPv6 IP, as the cloud provider does not support dual-stack node IPs.
title: IPv4/IPv6 dual-stack — Configure IPv4/IPv6 dual-stack

instruction: What is the default node-cidr-mask-size for IPv4 and IPv6?
response: The default node-cidr-mask-size is /24 for IPv4 and /64 for IPv6.
title: IPv4/IPv6 dual-stack — Configure IPv4/IPv6 dual-stack

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubelet in managing workload placement policies?
response: The kubelet provides methods to enable more complex workload placement policies while keeping the abstraction free from explicit placement directives.
title: Control CPU Management Policies on the Node

instruction: Where can you find detailed information on resource management for pods and containers?
response: You can find detailed information on resource management for pods and containers in the Resource Management for Pods and Containers documentation.
title: Control CPU Management Policies on the Node

instruction: Where can you find detailed information on how the kubelet implements resource management?
response: You can find detailed information on how the kubelet implements resource management in the Node ResourceManagers documentation.
title: Control CPU Management Policies on the Node

instruction: How do I verify that the PodSecurityPolicy admission controller is disabled?
response: You can verify that the PodSecurityPolicy admission controller is disabled by manually running a test by impersonating a user without access to any PodSecurityPolicies, or by verifying in the API server logs. At startup, the API server outputs log lines listing the loaded admission controller plugins. You should see PodSecurity (in the validating admission controllers), and neither list should contain PodSecurityPolicy.
title: Migrate from PodSecurityPolicy to the Built-In PodSecurity Admission Controller — 5. Disable PodSecurityPolicy

instruction: What should I do after disabling the PSP admission controller?
response: Once you are certain the PSP admission controller is disabled (and after sufficient soak time to be confident you won't need to roll back), you are free to delete your PodSecurityPolicies and any associated Roles, ClusterRoles, RoleBindings and ClusterRoleBindings (just make sure they don't grant any other unrelated permissions).
title: Migrate from PodSecurityPolicy to the Built-In PodSecurity Admission Controller — 5. Disable PodSecurityPolicy

instruction: How can you replicate a permissive ABAC policy using RBAC role bindings?
response: You can replicate a permissive ABAC policy using RBAC role bindings by creating a clusterrolebinding that allows all service accounts to act as cluster administrators.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Permissive RBAC permissions

instruction: What is the effect of automatically assigning service account credentials to applications running in containers?
response: Applications running in containers with automatically assigned service account credentials can perform any action against the API, including viewing secrets and modifying permissions.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Permissive RBAC permissions

instruction: What is the recommended course of action after transitioning to use RBAC?
response: After transitioning to use RBAC, you should adjust the access controls for your cluster to ensure that they meet your information security needs.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Permissive RBAC permissions

instruction: How can you parallelize token distribution for easier automation during kubeadm init?
response: You can parallelize token distribution by using the generated token on both the control plane node and worker nodes concurrently.
title: kubeadm init — Automating kubeadm

instruction: What is the required format of the token generated by kubeadm?
response: The token must have the form <6 character string>.<16 character string> and match the regex [a-z0-9]{6}.[a-z0-9]{16}.
title: kubeadm init — Automating kubeadm

instruction: How can you generate a token for kubeadm init and kubeadm join?
response: You can generate a token using the command kubeadm token generate.
title: kubeadm init — Automating kubeadm

instruction: What annotation should be set to true on an IngressClass resource to make it the default IngressClass for a cluster?
response: ingressclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class should be set to true.
title: Ingress — Default IngressClass

instruction: What happens to new Ingresses without an ingressClassName field specified when a default IngressClass is set?
response: New Ingresses without an ingressClassName field specified will be assigned the default IngressClass.
title: Ingress — Default IngressClass

instruction: What is the recommended way to specify the default IngressClass?
response: It is recommended to specify the default IngressClass explicitly.
title: Ingress — Default IngressClass

instruction: What are the minimum requirements for the Kubernetes cluster to run this tutorial?
response: You need to have a Kubernetes cluster, and the kubectl command-line tool must be configured to communicate with your cluster. It is recommended to run this tutorial on a cluster with at least two nodes that are not acting as control plane hosts.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler Walkthrough — Before you begin

instruction: How can you check the version of your Kubernetes server?
response: You can check the version by entering kubectl version. If you're running an older release of Kubernetes, refer to the version of the documentation for that release.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler Walkthrough — Before you begin

instruction: What additional component is required for this walkthrough, and how can you deploy it?
response: You need to use a cluster that has a Metrics Server deployed and configured. To deploy the Metrics Server, see the metrics-server documentation. If you are running Minikube, run the command minikube addons enable metrics-server.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler Walkthrough — Before you begin

instruction: What is the purpose of the /flagz endpoint in Kubernetes?
response: The /flagz endpoint shows you the command line arguments that were used to start a component, and is for debugging purposes only.
title: Kubernetes z-pages — flagz

instruction: How can you request a structured response format from the /flagz endpoint?
response: You can request the structured response by using the Accept header with the value application/json;v=v1beta1;g=config.k8s.io;as=Flagz.
title: Kubernetes z-pages — flagz

instruction: What is the default response format of the /flagz endpoint if no Accept header is specified?
response: The default response format is plain text.
title: Kubernetes z-pages — flagz

instruction: What are some ways to ensure the security of the supply chain for container images in Kubernetes?
response: You can scan container images and other artifacts for known vulnerabilities, ensure software distribution uses encryption in transit, adopt and follow processes to update dependencies, use validation mechanisms such as digital certificates, subscribe to feeds to alert you to security risks, and restrict access to artifacts.
title: Cloud Native Security and Kubernetes — Distributelifecycle phase

instruction: How can you ensure the security of the supply chain for the cluster and other components that execute your application?
response: You can adopt and follow processes to update dependencies when updates are available, especially in response to security announcements, and use validation mechanisms such as digital certificates for supply chain assurance.
title: Cloud Native Security and Kubernetes — Distributelifecycle phase

instruction: What is the purpose of using a private registry to store container images in Kubernetes?
response: The purpose of using a private registry is to restrict access to artifacts, allowing only authorized clients to pull images.
title: Cloud Native Security and Kubernetes — Distributelifecycle phase

instruction: Where can I find documentation for configuration files and command arguments for each Kubernetes binary?
response: The Reference section of the online documentation has a page for each binary, including kube-apiserver, kube-controller-manager, kube-scheduler, kubelet, and kube-proxy.
title: Extending Kubernetes — Configuration

instruction: Can I change configuration files and command arguments in a hosted Kubernetes service or a distribution with managed installation?
response: No, they may not always be changeable, and when they are, they are usually only changeable by the cluster operator, and may require restarting processes.
title: Extending Kubernetes — Configuration

instruction: What are the benefits of using built-in policy APIs like ResourceQuota, NetworkPolicy, and Role-based Access Control (RBAC) compared to configuration files and command arguments?
response: Built-in policy APIs are typically usable even with hosted Kubernetes services and managed Kubernetes installations, and provide a defined support policy like other Kubernetes APIs, making them recommended over configuration files and command arguments where suitable.
title: Extending Kubernetes — Configuration

instruction: What is the recommended approach for enforcing systemReserved resources?
response: It is recommended to enforce systemReserved resources only if a user has profiled their nodes exhaustively to come up with precise estimates and is confident in their ability to recover if any process in that group is oom-killed.
title: Reserve Compute Resources for System Daemons — General Guidelines

instruction: How can you minimize disruption when enforcing kubeReserved and systemReserved resources?
response: Enforcing only compressible resources for kubeReserved and systemReserved is less likely to cause disruption while ensuring that the resource is allocated appropriately when there is contention.
title: Reserve Compute Resources for System Daemons — General Guidelines

instruction: What is the initial step for enforcing resource reservations?
response: To begin with, enforce 'Allocatable' on pods.
title: Reserve Compute Resources for System Daemons — General Guidelines

instruction: How will the resource requirements of kube system daemons change over time?
response: The resource requirements of kube system daemons may grow over time as more and more features are added. However, the Kubernetes project will attempt to bring down utilization of node system daemons in future releases.
title: Reserve Compute Resources for System Daemons — General Guidelines

instruction: What types of server certificates are required by a Kubernetes cluster?
response: A Kubernetes cluster requires server certificates for the API server endpoint, etcd server, each kubelet, and optionally for the front-proxy.
title: PKI certificates and requirements — How certificates are used by your cluster

instruction: What types of client certificates are required by a Kubernetes cluster?
response: A Kubernetes cluster requires client certificates for each kubelet, API server, controller manager, scheduler, and optionally for administrators and the front-proxy.
title: PKI certificates and requirements — How certificates are used by your cluster

instruction: What is the purpose of client certificates for kube-proxy in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Client certificates for kube-proxy are used to authenticate to the API server, one for each node.
title: PKI certificates and requirements — How certificates are used by your cluster

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the CSINode watch list API?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type `BOOKMARK`. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: CSINode — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting the `limit` field in the CSINode watch list API?
response: Setting a limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items (up to zero items) in the event all requested objects are filtered out. The server guarantees that the objects returned when using continue will be identical to issuing a single list call without a limit.
title: CSINode — getWatch List

instruction: What is the purpose of the `sendInitialEvents` field in the CSINode watch list API?
response: The `sendInitialEvents` field may be set together with `watch=true` to begin the watch stream with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection.
title: CSINode — getWatch List

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersionMatch` field in the CSINode watch list API?
response: The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls and is highly recommended to be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: CSINode — getWatch List

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' field in the DELETE request?
response: The 'continue' field is used to retrieve more results from the server when the response is paginated. It should be set when retrieving more results and should be used with the same initial query parameters to retrieve the next set of results.
title: Workload — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential' to true?
response: When set to true, it triggers an unsafe deletion of the resource in case the normal deletion flow fails with a corrupt object error. This may potentially break the cluster if the workload associated with the resource being unsafe-deleted relies on normal deletion flow.
title: Workload — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the default policy for garbage collection when 'propagationPolicy' is not specified?
