response: The default policy is decided by the existing finalizer set in the metadata.finalizers and the resource-specific default policy.
title: Workload — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'resourceVersionMatch' to a specific value?
response: It determines how resourceVersion is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that resourceVersionMatch be set for list calls where resourceVersion is set.
title: Workload — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the primary function of the kubelet agent on a node?
response: The kubelet agent ensures that containers described in PodSpecs are running and healthy.
title: Cluster Architecture — Node components

instruction: Does the kubelet manage containers not created by Kubernetes?
response: No, the kubelet only manages containers created by Kubernetes.
title: Cluster Architecture — Node components

instruction: What is the default cgroup driver used by CRI-O, and how can you switch to the cgroupfs driver?
response: CRI-O uses the systemd cgroup driver by default, but you can switch to the cgroupfs driver by editing /etc/crio/crio.conf or placing a drop-in configuration in /etc/crio/crio.conf.d/02-cgroup-manager.conf. To do this, set the cgroup_manager value to "cgroupfs" and the conmon_cgroup value to "pod".
title: Container Runtimes — CRI-O

instruction: How can you enable automatic detection of the cgroup driver for CRI-O in Kubernetes v1.28?
response: You can enable automatic detection of the cgroup driver as an alpha feature in Kubernetes v1.28. For more details, see the systemd cgroup driver documentation.
title: Container Runtimes — CRI-O

instruction: What is the CRI socket path used by default for CRI-O, and how can you configure the pause image?
response: The CRI socket is /var/run/crio/crio.sock by default. You can configure the pause image by setting the pause_image value in your CRI-O config to a specific image, such as registry.k8s.io/pause:3.10. This config option supports live configuration reload.
title: Container Runtimes — CRI-O

instruction: How do you specify a memory request for a Pod at pod-level?
response: To specify a memory request for a Pod at pod-level, include the resources.requests.memory field in the Pod spec manifest.
title: Assign Pod-level CPU and memory resources — Create a namespace

instruction: What is the difference between a memory request and a memory limit for a Pod?
response: A memory request is the amount of memory that a Pod requests from the Kubernetes system, while a memory limit is the maximum amount of memory that a Pod is allowed to use.
title: Assign Pod-level CPU and memory resources — Create a namespace

instruction: How do you verify that a Pod is running and has the specified memory request and limit?
response: You can verify that a Pod is running and has the specified memory request and limit by using the command kubectl get pod <pod-name> --namespace <namespace-name> --output yaml.
title: Assign Pod-level CPU and memory resources — Create a namespace

instruction: What is the default value for the `ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential` field?
response: The default value is false.
title: ControllerRevision — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting `orphanDependents` to true/false?
response: The 'orphan' finalizer will be added to/removed from the object's finalizers list.
title: ControllerRevision — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What are the acceptable values for the `propagationPolicy` field?
response: The acceptable values are: 'Orphan', 'Background', and 'Foreground'.
title: ControllerRevision — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersion` field?
response: It sets a constraint on what resource versions a request may be served from.
title: ControllerRevision — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting `limit` to a specific value?
response: It returns a maximum number of responses to return for a list call, and the server may set the `continue` field on the list metadata to retrieve the next set of results.
title: ControllerRevision — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: Will Node authorization mode restrict requests from kubelets outside the system:nodes group?
response: No, the Node authorization mode would not restrict requests from these kubelets.
title: Using Node Authorization — Kubelets outside thesystem:nodesgroup

instruction: What would happen to kubelets that have credentials placing them in the system:nodes group but without a username in the system:node:... format?
response: These kubelets would not be authorized by the Node authorization mode and would need to continue to be authorized via whatever mechanism currently authorizes them.
title: Using Node Authorization — Kubelets outside thesystem:nodesgroup

instruction: How would the NodeRestriction admission plugin handle requests from kubelets that do not have a username in the system:node:... format?
response: The NodeRestriction admission plugin would ignore requests from these kubelets, since the default node identifier implementation would not consider that a node identity.
title: Using Node Authorization — Kubelets outside thesystem:nodesgroup

instruction: What are the objectives of creating a cluster with kubeadm?
response: The objectives are to install a single control-plane Kubernetes cluster and install a Pod network on the cluster so that your Pods can talk to each other.
title: Creating a cluster with kubeadm — Objectives

instruction: How does kubeadm determine the IP address for advertising and listening?
response: kubeadm tries to find a usable IP on the network interfaces associated with a default gateway on a host. You can use the command 'ip route show' to find this IP address on a Linux host.
title: Creating a cluster with kubeadm — Objectives

instruction: How can you configure the API server advertise address for control plane nodes created with both init and join?
response: You can use the flag '--apiserver-advertise-address' and set it in the kubeadm API as InitConfiguration.localAPIEndpoint and JoinConfiguration.controlPlane.localAPIEndpoint.
title: Creating a cluster with kubeadm — Objectives

instruction: What happens if you change the IP addresses assigned to control plane components?
response: Changing these IP addresses would require signing new certificates and restarting the affected components, so that the change in certificate files is reflected.
title: Creating a cluster with kubeadm — Objectives

instruction: What are the possible values for the swapBehavior field in KubeletConfiguration?
response: The possible values for the swapBehavior field are "", "NoSwap", and "LimitedSwap".
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — MemorySwapConfiguration

instruction: What is the effect of setting swapBehavior to "LimitedSwap" on container workloads?
response: When swapBehavior is set to "LimitedSwap", workload swap usage is limited to a proportionate amount based on the container's memory request.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — MemorySwapConfiguration

instruction: How do you create a ResourceQuota object with specific compute resources in Kubernetes?
response: You can create a ResourceQuota object with specific compute resources by creating a YAML file containing the quota specification and then applying it to a namespace using `kubectl create -f` command. For example, you can create a file named `compute-resources.yaml` with the quota specification and then run `kubectl create -f ./compute-resources.yaml --namespace myspace` to apply it to the `myspace` namespace.
title: Resource Quotas — Viewing and Setting Quotas

instruction: How do you view the current quotas in a namespace?
response: You can view the current quotas in a namespace by running `kubectl get quota --namespace <namespace>` command. For example, to view the quotas in the `myspace` namespace, you can run `kubectl get quota --namespace myspace`.
title: Resource Quotas — Viewing and Setting Quotas

instruction: How do you create an object count quota for a specific resource in Kubernetes?
response: You can create an object count quota for a specific resource in Kubernetes by using the `count/<resource>.<group>` syntax when creating a quota. For example, to create a quota that limits the number of deployments in the `apps` group to 2, you can run `kubectl create quota test --hard count/deployments.apps=2 --namespace myspace`.
title: Resource Quotas — Viewing and Setting Quotas

instruction: How do you view the details of a quota in Kubernetes?
response: You can view the details of a quota in Kubernetes by running `kubectl describe quota <quota-name> --namespace <namespace>` command. For example, to view the details of the `compute-resources` quota in the `myspace` namespace, you can run `kubectl describe quota compute-resources --namespace myspace`.
title: Resource Quotas — Viewing and Setting Quotas

instruction: What are the supported eviction types for out-of-resource management in Kubernetes?
response: Evictions are supported for memory and ephemeral-storage only.
title: Reserve Compute Resources for System Daemons — Eviction Thresholds

instruction: What is the purpose of the evictionHard setting in KubeletConfiguration?
response: The evictionHard setting allows you to reserve some memory and attempt to evict pods whenever memory availability on the node drops below the reserved value, thus reducing the probability of system OOMs.
title: Reserve Compute Resources for System Daemons — Eviction Thresholds

instruction: How does the evictionHard setting impact available resources for pods?
response: Resources reserved for evictions are not available for pods. Pods cannot use more than capacity - eviction-hard.
title: Reserve Compute Resources for System Daemons — Eviction Thresholds

instruction: What is the purpose of a Service Account in Kubernetes?
response: A Service Account provides an identity for processes that run in a Pod, and maps to a ServiceAccount object.
title: Configure Service Accounts for Pods

instruction: How does a Service Account authenticate to the Kubernetes API server?
response: When you authenticate to the API server, you identify yourself as a particular user.
title: Configure Service Accounts for Pods

instruction: Why does Kubernetes not have a User API?
response: Kubernetes recognises the concept of a user, however, Kubernetes itself does not have a User API.
title: Configure Service Accounts for Pods

instruction: What is the default value for the minReadySeconds field in a ReplicationControllerSpec?
response: The default value for the minReadySeconds field is 0, which means a newly created pod will be considered available as soon as it is ready.
title: ReplicationController — ReplicationControllerSpec

instruction: What is the purpose of the replicas field in a ReplicationControllerSpec?
response: The replicas field specifies the number of desired replicas, which is a pointer to distinguish between explicit zero and unspecified. The default value for replicas is 1.
title: ReplicationController — ReplicationControllerSpec

instruction: What happens if the selector field in a ReplicationControllerSpec is empty?
response: If the selector field is empty, it is defaulted to the labels present on the Pod template.
title: ReplicationController — ReplicationControllerSpec

instruction: What is the primary function of the Job controller in Kubernetes?
response: The Job controller ensures that the desired state for a Job is achieved by creating or removing Pods to carry out the task and then stop.
title: Controllers — Control via API server

instruction: How does the Job controller interact with the cluster API server?
response: The Job controller tells the API server to create or remove Pods to achieve the desired state for a Job.
title: Controllers — Control via API server

instruction: What happens to a Job object after the work is done?
response: The Job controller updates the Job object to mark it as Finished.
title: Controllers — Control via API server

instruction: Are all sysctls enabled by default in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Yes, all safe sysctls are enabled by default.
title: Using sysctls in a Kubernetes Cluster — Enabling Unsafe Sysctls

instruction: What happens to pods with disabled unsafe sysctls?
response: Pods with disabled unsafe sysctls will be scheduled, but will fail to launch.
title: Using sysctls in a Kubernetes Cluster — Enabling Unsafe Sysctls

instruction: How are unsafe sysctls enabled on a node-by-node basis?
response: Unsafe sysctls are enabled on a node-by-node basis with a flag of the kubelet; for example: kubelet --allowed-unsafe-sysctls 'kernel.msg*,net.core.somaxconn'...
title: Using sysctls in a Kubernetes Cluster — Enabling Unsafe Sysctls

instruction: How can you determine if a container is being terminated due to resource limits?
response: You can call kubectl describe pod on the Pod of interest to check the resource limits and the reason for termination.
title: Resource Management for Pods and Containers — My container is terminated

instruction: What does the 'OOMKilled' reason in the container's status indicate?
response: The 'OOMKilled' reason indicates that the container tried to use more memory than its limit, resulting in termination.
title: Resource Management for Pods and Containers — My container is terminated

instruction: What should you do if you find that the application is behaving as expected after checking for memory leaks?
response: You should consider setting a higher memory limit (and possibly request) for that container.
title: Resource Management for Pods and Containers — My container is terminated

instruction: What is the purpose of a data source in Kubernetes?
response: A data source lets you request that a new volume be pre-populated with existing data when creating a PersistentVolumeClaim.
title: Volume Populators and Data Sources

instruction: How do volume populators carry out the population of a new volume?
response: Volume populators are the controllers that carry out the population based on the data source referenced by the PersistentVolumeClaim.
title: Volume Populators and Data Sources

instruction: What are some built-in data sources supported by Kubernetes?
response: Kubernetes has built-in support for data sources that clone an existing volume or that restore a volume snapshot.
title: Volume Populators and Data Sources

instruction: How do you create a namespace in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can create a namespace using the command `kubectl create namespace <namespace-name>`, as shown in the example with `kubectl create namespace mem-example`.
title: Assign Memory Resources to Containers and Pods — Create a namespace

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resources.requests.memory` and `resources.limits.memory` fields in a container's resource manifest?
response: The `resources.requests.memory` field specifies a memory request for a container, while the `resources.limits.memory` field specifies a memory limit for a container.
title: Assign Memory Resources to Containers and Pods — Create a namespace

instruction: How do you specify a memory request and limit for a container in a Pod configuration file?
response: You can specify a memory request and limit for a container by including the `resources.requests.memory` and `resources.limits.memory` fields in the container's resource manifest, as shown in the example with `requests: { memory: "100Mi" }` and `limits: { memory: "200Mi" }`.
title: Assign Memory Resources to Containers and Pods — Create a namespace

instruction: How do you verify that a Pod's container is running and has the specified memory request and limit?
response: You can verify that a Pod's container is running and has the specified memory request and limit by running the command `kubectl get pod <pod-name> --namespace <namespace-name>`, and then running `kubectl get pod <pod-name> --output yaml --namespace <namespace-name>` to view detailed information about the Pod.
title: Assign Memory Resources to Containers and Pods — Create a namespace

instruction: How do you view the metrics for a Pod, including its memory usage?
response: You can view the metrics for a Pod, including its memory usage, by running the command `kubectl top pod <pod-name> --namespace <namespace-name>`, as shown in the example with `kubectl top pod memory-demo --namespace mem-example`.
title: Assign Memory Resources to Containers and Pods — Create a namespace

instruction: What is the Service API object in Kubernetes?
response: Service is a top-level resource in the Kubernetes REST API.
title: Service — API Object

instruction: Where can you find more information about the Service API object?
response: You can find more details about the Service API object.
title: Service — API Object

instruction: What is recommended for learning more about Services in Kubernetes?
response: Follow the Connecting Applications with Services tutorial.
title: Service — API Object

instruction: What could be causing kubeadm init to hang after printing out the control plane waiting message?
response: The most common causes are network connection problems, the cgroup driver mismatch between the container runtime and the kubelet, or control plane containers crashlooping or hanging.
title: Troubleshooting kubeadm — kubeadm blocks waiting for control plane during installation

instruction: How can I check for network connectivity issues?
response: Check that your machine has full network connectivity before continuing.
title: Troubleshooting kubeadm — kubeadm blocks waiting for control plane during installation

instruction: How can I diagnose cgroup driver issues?
response: See Configuring a cgroup driver for more information on how to configure it properly.
title: Troubleshooting kubeadm — kubeadm blocks waiting for control plane during installation

instruction: How can I investigate control plane container issues?
response: Run docker ps to check the status of control plane containers, and then run docker logs to investigate each container.
title: Troubleshooting kubeadm — kubeadm blocks waiting for control plane during installation

instruction: What is the purpose of the subPathExpr field in a Kubernetes Pod?
response: The subPathExpr field is used to construct subPath directory names from downward API environment variables.
title: Volumes — Using subPath with expanded environment variables

instruction: What is the relationship between the subPath and subPathExpr properties in a Kubernetes Pod?
response: The subPath and subPathExpr properties are mutually exclusive, meaning that a Pod can use either subPath or subPathExpr, but not both at the same time.
title: Volumes — Using subPath with expanded environment variables

instruction: How does the hostPath volume in the example use the Pod name from the downwardAPI?
response: The hostPath volume takes the Pod name from the downwardAPI, which is used to construct the directory path within the host directory.
title: Volumes — Using subPath with expanded environment variables

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' field in the DELETE /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/roles API endpoint?
response: The 'continue' field is used to retrieve more results from the server when the limit of responses is reached. It should be set when retrieving more results from the server and should be used with the same initial query to retrieve the next set of results.
title: Role — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the difference between 'limit' and 'continue' fields in the DELETE /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/roles API endpoint?
response: The 'limit' field sets a maximum number of responses to return for a list call, while the 'continue' field is used to retrieve more results from the server when the limit of responses is reached.
title: Role — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the default policy for garbage collection when 'propagationPolicy' is not set?
response: The default policy is decided by the existing finalizer set in the metadata.finalizers and the resource-specific default policy.
title: Role — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the ClusterRoleBinding object in Kubernetes?
response: The ClusterRoleBinding object references a ClusterRole and adds who information via Subject, allowing you to define which users or groups have access to a particular ClusterRole.
title: ClusterRoleBinding

instruction: What is the immutable field in the ClusterRoleBinding object?
response: The immutable field in the ClusterRoleBinding object is roleRef, which can only reference a ClusterRole in the global namespace.
title: ClusterRoleBinding

instruction: What happens if the RoleRef in the ClusterRoleBinding object cannot be resolved?
response: If the RoleRef cannot be resolved, the Authorizer must return an error.
title: ClusterRoleBinding

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /apis/admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1/watch/mutatingadmissionpolicybindings request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK' from the server, but servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag.
title: MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding — getWatch List

instruction: What happens when a client uses the `limit` field in a list call, and the continue field is empty?
response: The client may assume that no more results are available.
title: MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding — getWatch List

instruction: What is the semantic of the watch request when `sendInitialEvents` option is set, and `resourceVersionMatch` is set to NotOlderThan?
response: The bookmark event is sent when the state is synced to a `resourceVersion` at least as fresh as the one provided by the ListOptions.
title: MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding — getWatch List

instruction: What is the default filesystem type for an AWS EBS disk if it is not specified?
response: The default filesystem type is 'ext4'.
title: PersistentVolume — AWSElasticBlockStoreVolumeSource

instruction: What happens if you try to mount an AWS EBS disk as read/write multiple times?
response: An AWS EBS disk can only be mounted as read/write once.
title: PersistentVolume — AWSElasticBlockStoreVolumeSource

instruction: How do you specify the partition to mount for an AWS EBS disk?
response: You can specify the partition by setting the 'partition' field to the desired partition number. For example, for volume /dev/sda1, you specify the partition as '1'.
title: PersistentVolume — AWSElasticBlockStoreVolumeSource

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'volumeID' field in the AWS EBS disk configuration?
response: The 'volumeID' field is the unique ID of the persistent disk resource in AWS (Amazon EBS volume).
title: PersistentVolume — AWSElasticBlockStoreVolumeSource

instruction: What is the default value for the 'pretty' parameter in the GET /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ingressclasses request?
response: The default value for the 'pretty' parameter is 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: IngressClass — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' parameter in the GET /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ingressclasses request?
response: The 'continue' parameter is used to retrieve more results from the server. It should be set when retrieving more results from the server, and clients should only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters (except for the value of continue) and the server may reject a continue value it does not recognize.
title: IngressClass — getList

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'limit' to a non-zero value in the GET /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ingressclasses request?
response: Setting a limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items (up to zero items) in the event all requested objects are filtered out, and clients should only use the presence of the continue field to determine whether more results are available.
title: IngressClass — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'sendInitialEvents' parameter in the GET /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ingressclasses request?
response: The 'sendInitialEvents' parameter may be set together with 'watch=true' to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent.
title: IngressClass — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /api/v1/watch/services request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type "BOOKMARK". Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag, and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: Service — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What happens when a client uses the `continue` field to retrieve more results from the server, but the continue value is no longer valid?
response: The server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token. The client must restart their list without the continue field to get a consistent list.
title: Service — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents` to true together with `watch` to true?
response: The watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic "Bookmark" event with the ResourceVersion (RV) corresponding to the set of objects. Afterwards, the watch stream will proceed as usual, sending watch events corresponding to changes to objects watched.
title: Service — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the recommended storage for secrets required by pods?
response: Secrets should be stored within Kubernetes Secrets, as opposed to alternatives such as ConfigMap.
title: Security Checklist — Secrets

instruction: How can secrets from third-party storages be injected into pods?
response: A mechanism such as the Secrets Store CSI Driver can be used to inject secrets from third-party storages as a volume.
title: Security Checklist — Secrets

instruction: What is the preferred method for mounting secrets to pods?
response: Secrets should be automatically mounted through volumes, preferably stored in memory like with the emptyDir.medium option.
title: Security Checklist — Secrets

instruction: How can service account tokens be prevented from being mounted into pods that do not require them?
response: Service account tokens should not be mounted by setting automountServiceAccountToken to false either within the service account or specifically for a pod.
title: Security Checklist — Secrets

instruction: What cloud provider resources should be considered when requesting a quota increase for a large cluster?
response: Computer instances, CPUs, Storage volumes, In-use IP addresses, Packet filtering rule sets, Number of load balancers, Network subnets, and Log streams.
title: Considerations for large clusters — Cloud provider resource quotas

instruction: Why is it necessary to gate cluster scaling actions when creating a large cluster?
response: Some cloud providers rate limit the creation of new instances, so scaling actions should be gated to bring up new nodes in batches with a pause between batches.
title: Considerations for large clusters — Cloud provider resource quotas

instruction: How do container runtimes capture logs from a containerized application?
response: Container runtimes capture a containerized application's output from standard output (stdout) and standard error (stderr) streams, and the integration with the kubelet is standardized through the CRI logging format.
title: Observability — Logs

instruction: Where do system components that run inside containers write logs by default?
response: System components that run inside containers always write to .log files in /var/log, bypassing the default container logging mechanism.
title: Observability — Logs

instruction: What is the purpose of a node-level logging agent in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The node-level logging agent tails log files and forwards entries to a central log store, and is often used in conjunction with a log aggregation pipeline.
title: Observability — Logs

instruction: What is the recommended location for storing system component and container logs?
response: System component and container logs stored under /var/log require log rotation to prevent uncontrolled growth.
title: Observability — Logs

instruction: Can a cloned PVC be consumed independently of its original dataSource PVC?
response: Yes, a cloned PVC can be consumed independently of its original dataSource PVC. It can be used, cloned, snapshotted, or deleted without consideration for its original dataSource PVC.
title: CSI Volume Cloning — Usage

instruction: Does the original dataSource PVC affect the cloned PVC after it's created?
response: No, the original dataSource PVC is not linked to the newly created clone in any way. This means the original dataSource PVC can be modified or deleted without affecting the cloned PVC.
title: CSI Volume Cloning — Usage

instruction: Can a cloned PVC be deleted independently of its original dataSource PVC?
response: Yes, a cloned PVC can be deleted independently of its original dataSource PVC. This is because the cloned PVC is an independent object and can be managed independently of its original dataSource PVC.
title: CSI Volume Cloning — Usage

instruction: What is the impact of the --logging-format=json flag on Kubernetes logs?
response: The --logging-format=json flag changes the format of logs from klog native format to JSON format.
title: System Logs — JSON log format

instruction: Are all Kubernetes logs guaranteed to be in JSON format?
response: No, not all logs are guaranteed to be written in JSON format, for example, during process start.
title: System Logs — JSON log format

instruction: What should be considered when parsing Kubernetes logs?
response: When parsing logs, make sure you can handle log lines that are not JSON as well, due to the possibility of non-JSON log lines.
title: System Logs — JSON log format

instruction: What are the keys with special meaning in the JSON log format?
response: The keys with special meaning are ts (timestamp as Unix time), v (verbosity), err (error string), and msg (message).
title: System Logs — JSON log format

instruction: How does Kubernetes detect a kubelet restart?
response: Kubernetes detects a kubelet restart if the Node's Ready condition remains unchanged, meaning it has not transitioned from true to false.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Detection of kubelet restarts

instruction: What happens to container statuses after a kubelet restart by default?
response: By default, the kubelet does not change container statuses after a restart. Containers that were in a ready: true state remain ready.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Detection of kubelet restarts

instruction: What is the legacy behavior of the kubelet regarding container statuses after a restart, and when can it be enabled?
response: The legacy behavior is to always modify the containers' ready value to be false after a kubelet restart. This behavior can be enabled in Kubernetes 1.36 by using the ChangeContainerStatusOnKubeletRestart feature gate, but it is recommended to file a bug report if issues are encountered.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Detection of kubelet restarts

instruction: How does kubeadm store its KubeProxyConfiguration?
response: kubeadm stores its KubeProxyConfiguration in a ConfigMap in the kube-system namespace called kube-proxy.
title: Reconfiguring a kubeadm cluster — Applying kube-proxy configuration changes

instruction: What command can be used to edit the KubeProxyConfiguration in a kubeadm cluster?
response: The command to edit the KubeProxyConfiguration is kubectl edit cm -n kube-system kube-proxy.
title: Reconfiguring a kubeadm cluster — Applying kube-proxy configuration changes

instruction: What key contains the KubeProxyConfiguration in the kube-proxy ConfigMap?
response: The KubeProxyConfiguration is located under the data.config.conf key in the kube-proxy ConfigMap.
title: Reconfiguring a kubeadm cluster — Applying kube-proxy configuration changes

instruction: How can you restart all kube-proxy Pods after updating the kube-proxy ConfigMap?
response: You can restart all kube-proxy Pods by deleting the existing Pods with kubectl delete po -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-proxy, and new Pods that use the updated ConfigMap will be created.
title: Reconfiguring a kubeadm cluster — Applying kube-proxy configuration changes

instruction: What is the purpose of the clusterTrustBundle projected volume source in Kubernetes?
response: The clusterTrustBundle projected volume source injects the contents of one or more ClusterTrustBundle objects as an automatically-updating file in the container filesystem.
title: Projected Volumes — clusterTrustBundle projected volumes

instruction: How can you select a ClusterTrustBundle object for the clusterTrustBundle projected volume source?
response: You can select a ClusterTrustBundle object by name using the name field or by signer name using the signerName field, optionally with a labelSelector to designate a set of ClusterTrustBundle objects.
title: Projected Volumes — clusterTrustBundle projected volumes

instruction: What is the default behavior of the kubelet when a ClusterTrustBundle object is not found or does not match the specified signerName and labelSelector?
response: By default, the kubelet will prevent the pod from starting if the named ClusterTrustBundle is not found, or if signerName / labelSelector do not match any ClusterTrustBundles.
title: Projected Volumes — clusterTrustBundle projected volumes

instruction: How can you configure the kubelet to start the pod even if the ClusterTrustBundle object is not found or does not match the specified signerName and labelSelector?
response: You can set the optional field to true, and the pod will start up with an empty file at the specified path.
title: Projected Volumes — clusterTrustBundle projected volumes

instruction: What does the 'pretty' parameter do in the GET /apis/certificates.k8s.io/v1beta1/clustertrustbundles request?
response: If 'true', then the output is pretty printed. Defaults to 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: ClusterTrustBundle — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' parameter in the GET /apis/certificates.k8s.io/v1beta1/clustertrustbundles request?
response: The continue option should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is used to continue a list query from where it left off, but the client must restart their list without the continue field if they need a consistent list.
title: ClusterTrustBundle — getList

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'sendInitialEvents=true' together with 'watch=true' in the GET /apis/certificates.k8s.io/v1beta1/clustertrustbundles request?
response: The watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, and a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent once all such events have been sent, reporting the ResourceVersion (RV) corresponding to the set of objects.
title: ClusterTrustBundle — getList

instruction: How can a Windows HostProcess Pod access volume mounts?
response: Applications running inside the container can access volume mounts directly via the volumeMount's specified mountPath, just like Linux and non-HostProcess Windows containers.
title: Create a Windows HostProcess Pod — Containerd v1.7 (and greater)

instruction: Are there any backwards compatibility considerations for accessing volumes in a Windows HostProcess Pod?
response: Yes, for backwards compatibility, volumes can also be accessed via using the same relative paths configured by containerd v1.6.
title: Create a Windows HostProcess Pod — Containerd v1.7 (and greater)

instruction: What is an example path to access service account tokens within a Windows HostProcess Pod container?
response: An example path to access service account tokens within the container is c:\var\run\secrets\kubernetes.io\serviceaccount, or one of the other paths specified in the documentation.
title: Create a Windows HostProcess Pod — Containerd v1.7 (and greater)

instruction: What is the purpose of using ephemeral containers for debugging?
response: Ephemeral containers are useful for interactive troubleshooting when kubectl exec is insufficient because a container has crashed or a container image doesn't include debugging utilities.
title: Debug Running Pods — Debugging with an ephemeral debug container

instruction: How can you add an ephemeral container to a running Pod using kubectl?
response: You can use the kubectl debug command to add an ephemeral container to a running Pod. The command is in the format kubectl debug -it <pod-name> --image <image-name> --target <target-container-name>.
title: Debug Running Pods — Debugging with an ephemeral debug container

instruction: Why is the --target parameter necessary when adding an ephemeral container to a Pod?
response: The --target parameter is necessary because kubectl run does not enable process namespace sharing in the pod it creates, and it's required to target the process namespace of another container.
title: Debug Running Pods — Debugging with an ephemeral debug container

instruction: What is the default value for IDsPerPod in KubeletConfiguration?
response: The default value for IDsPerPod is 65536.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — UserNamespaces

instruction: On non-Linux systems like Windows, what values are allowed for IDsPerPod?
response: On non-Linux systems like Windows, only null or absent values are allowed for IDsPerPod.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — UserNamespaces

instruction: What are the constraints for the value of IDsPerPod?
response: The value of IDsPerPod must be a multiple of 65536 and less than 1<<32.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — UserNamespaces

instruction: What is the purpose of exposing a Service onto an external IP address?
response: You may want to expose a Service onto an external IP address for parts of your application, such as frontends, that are accessible from outside of your cluster.
title: Service — Service type

instruction: How do Kubernetes Service types allow you to specify what kind of Service you want?
response: Kubernetes Service types allow you to specify what kind of Service you want by providing available type values and their behaviors.
title: Service — Service type

instruction: What is the exception to the nested design of the type field in the Service API?
response: You can define a LoadBalancer Service by disabling the load balancer NodePort allocation.
title: Service — Service type

instruction: What are the two primary ways to share a Kubernetes cluster for multi-tenancy?
response: There are two primary ways to share a Kubernetes cluster for multi-tenancy: using Namespaces (that is, a Namespace per tenant) or by virtualizing the control plane (that is, virtual control plane per tenant).
title: Multi-tenancy — Implementations

instruction: What are the benefits of using Namespaces for multi-tenancy?
response: Namespace isolation is well-supported by Kubernetes, has a negligible resource cost, and provides mechanisms to allow tenants to interact appropriately, such as by allowing service-to-service communication.
title: Multi-tenancy — Implementations

instruction: What are the limitations of using Namespaces for multi-tenancy?
response: However, it can be difficult to configure, and doesn't apply to Kubernetes resources that can't be namespaced, such as Custom Resource Definitions, Storage Classes, and Webhooks.
title: Multi-tenancy — Implementations

instruction: What are the benefits of using control plane virtualization for multi-tenancy?
response: Control plane virtualization allows for isolation of non-namespaced resources at the cost of somewhat higher resource usage and more difficult cross-tenant sharing.
title: Multi-tenancy — Implementations

instruction: What is the recommended approach for storage provisioning in a multi-tenant Kubernetes cluster?
response: Dynamic volume provisioning is recommended for security and data-isolation, and volume types that use node resources should be avoided.
title: Multi-tenancy — Storage isolation

instruction: How can you isolate storage for tenants in a shared Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can use a separate StorageClass for each tenant or configure a PersistentVolumeClaim as a namespaced resource to dedicate storage to tenants.
title: Multi-tenancy — Storage isolation

instruction: What reclaim policy should be set for a shared StorageClass to ensure storage isolation?
response: The reclaim policy should be set to Delete to prevent a PersistentVolume from being reused across different namespaces.
title: Multi-tenancy — Storage isolation

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' field in the GET /api/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/replicationcontrollers API call?
response: The 'continue' field is used to retrieve more results from the server. It should be set when retrieving more results and is server-defined, so clients can only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters.
title: ReplicationController — getWatch List

instruction: What happens when a client uses the 'limit' field in the GET /api/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/replicationcontrollers API call?
response: The server will set the 'continue' field on the list metadata to a value that can be used with the same initial query to retrieve the next set of results. If limit is specified and the continue field is empty, clients may assume that no more results are available.
title: ReplicationController — getWatch List

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'sendInitialEvents' field in the GET /api/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/replicationcontrollers API call?
response: The 'sendInitialEvents' field may be set together with 'watch=true' to begin the watch stream with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent.
title: ReplicationController — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting imagePullPolicy to Always, even when using a registry with reliable accessibility?
response: The container runtime can notice that the image layers already exist on the node, so they don't need to be downloaded again.
title: Images — Image pull policy

instruction: What is the recommended approach to specifying a container image version in production, and why?
response: You should specify a meaningful tag such as v1.42.0 and/or a digest, as using the :latest tag is harder to track which version of the image is running and more difficult to roll back properly.
title: Images — Image pull policy

instruction: What is the difference between using an image tag and an image digest, and how does it affect code versioning?
response: An image digest uniquely identifies a specific version of the image, so Kubernetes runs the same code every time it starts a container with that image name and digest specified. Specifying an image by digest pins the code that you run so that a change at the registry cannot lead to a mix of versions.
title: Images — Image pull policy

instruction: Under what conditions is the imagePullPolicy field automatically set by the cluster when omitted?
response: The imagePullPolicy field is automatically set to IfNotPresent if you specify the digest for the container image, to Always if you specify the :latest tag, and to Always if you don't specify the tag for the container image. If you specify a tag that isn't :latest, the imagePullPolicy is automatically set to IfNotPresent.
title: Images — Image pull policy

instruction: What is the purpose of the HTTPRoute kind in Kubernetes?
response: The HTTPRoute kind specifies routing behavior of HTTP requests from a Gateway listener to backend network endpoints.
title: Gateway API — HTTPRoute

instruction: How does an HTTPRoute represent a backend network endpoint for a Service?
response: An implementation may represent the backend network endpoint as a Service IP or the backing EndpointSlices of the Service.
title: Gateway API — HTTPRoute

instruction: What happens when a new HTTPRoute is defined?
response: Defining a new HTTPRoute may result in configuring additional traffic routes in a cloud load balancer or in-cluster proxy server.
title: Gateway API — HTTPRoute

instruction: What is the security risk of allowing arbitrary PersistentVolume creation?
response: Granting unrestricted access to create PersistentVolumes, including hostPath volumes, poses a security risk as it allows a Pod to access the underlying host filesystem(s) on the associated node, which can lead to privilege escalation.
title: Role Based Access Control Good Practices — Persistent volume creation

instruction: Who should be granted access to create PersistentVolume objects?
response: Only trusted users, such as cluster operators who need this access for their work, and the Kubernetes control plane components that create PersistentVolumes based on PersistentVolumeClaims for automatic provisioning should be granted access to create PersistentVolume objects.
title: Role Based Access Control Good Practices — Persistent volume creation

instruction: What is the recommended approach for accessing persistent storage when it is required?
response: Trusted administrators should create PersistentVolumes, and constrained users should use PersistentVolumeClaims to access that storage.
title: Role Based Access Control Good Practices — Persistent volume creation

instruction: What is the purpose of the `continue` field in the PodCertificateRequest getWatch List API?
response: The `continue` field is used to retrieve more results from the server when the limit is reached. It should be set when retrieving more results from the server and should be used with identical query parameters (except for the value of continue) to ensure consistent results.
title: PodCertificateRequest — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting `limit` to a specific value when using the PodCertificateRequest getWatch List API?
response: Setting a limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items (up to zero items) in the event all requested objects are filtered out. Clients should only use the presence of the continue field to determine whether more results are available.
title: PodCertificateRequest — getWatch List

instruction: What is the purpose of periodically reviewing Kubernetes RBAC settings?
response: To identify and remove redundant entries and prevent possible privilege escalations.
title: Role Based Access Control Good Practices — Periodic review

instruction: What happens if an attacker creates a user account with the same name as a deleted user?
response: The attacker can automatically inherit all the rights of the deleted user, including rights assigned to that user.
title: Role Based Access Control Good Practices — Periodic review

instruction: What is the purpose of the $ITEM placeholder in the job-tmpl.yaml template?
response: The $ITEM placeholder is used as a placeholder that needs to be filled in before the template can be used as a valid Kubernetes manifest.
title: Parallel Processing using Expansions — Create Jobs based on a template

instruction: What is the initial state of the downloaded job-tmpl.yaml file?
response: The file is not yet a valid Kubernetes manifest; it is a YAML representation of a Job object with placeholders that need to be filled in.
title: Parallel Processing using Expansions — Create Jobs based on a template

instruction: What are some common causes of Kubernetes cluster downtime?
response: VM shutdown, network partition, crashes in Kubernetes software, data loss or unavailability of persistent storage, and operator error are common causes of Kubernetes cluster downtime.
title: Troubleshooting Clusters — Contributing causes

instruction: What happens if the API server VM shuts down or the apiserver crashes?
response: If the API server VM shuts down or the apiserver crashes, users will be unable to stop, update, or start new pods, services, or replication controllers. Existing pods and services will continue to work normally unless they depend on the Kubernetes API. Manual recovery or recreation of apiserver state is necessary before the apiserver can be restarted.
title: Troubleshooting Clusters — Contributing causes

instruction: What are the consequences of a network partition within the cluster or between the cluster and users?
response: A network partition within the cluster or between the cluster and users can cause partition A to think the nodes in partition B are down, while partition B thinks the apiserver is down.
title: Troubleshooting Clusters — Contributing causes

instruction: What happens if a node (VM or physical machine) shuts down?
response: If a node (VM or physical machine) shuts down, pods on that node will stop running.
title: Troubleshooting Clusters — Contributing causes

instruction: What are the consequences of a kubelet software fault?
response: A kubelet software fault can cause the kubelet to crash, preventing it from starting new pods on the node. The node may be marked unhealthy, and replication controllers may start new pods elsewhere.
title: Troubleshooting Clusters — Contributing causes

instruction: What are the consequences of cluster operator error?
response: Cluster operator error can result in loss of pods, services, etc., loss of apiserver backing store, and users being unable to read the API.
title: Troubleshooting Clusters — Contributing causes

instruction: How does a Kubernetes Namespace provide isolation for different tenants?
response: A Namespace provides a mechanism for isolating groups of API resources within a single cluster, allowing tenants to name their resources without having to consider what other tenants are doing.
title: Multi-tenancy — Control plane isolation

instruction: What are some examples of Kubernetes security policies that are scoped to namespaces?
response: RBAC Roles and Network Policies are namespace-scoped resources, and using RBAC, Users and Service Accounts can be restricted to a namespace.
title: Multi-tenancy — Control plane isolation

instruction: Why is it a good practice to isolate every workload in its own Namespace, even if multiple workloads are operated by the same tenant?
response: This ensures that each workload has its own identity and can be configured with an appropriate security policy.
title: Multi-tenancy — Control plane isolation

instruction: What is the potential issue with relying solely on mutating webhooks for configuration changes?
response: Mutating webhooks run to completion before any validating webhooks run, and there is no stable order in which mutations are applied to objects, which means your mutations could get overwritten by a mutating webhook that runs at a later time.
title: Admission Webhook Good Practices — Validate mutations before admission

instruction: How can you ensure that configuration changes made by a mutating webhook are not overwritten?
response: You can add a validating admission controller like a ValidatingAdmissionWebhook or a ValidatingAdmissionPolicy to your cluster to ensure that your mutations are still present.
title: Admission Webhook Good Practices — Validate mutations before admission

instruction: What is an example of a validating webhook that can be used to ensure configuration changes are retained?
response: A validating webhook that ensures init containers retain the restartPolicy: Always configuration after all mutations were completed is an example.
title: Admission Webhook Good Practices — Validate mutations before admission

instruction: What are the possible values for the `accessModes` field in a PersistentVolumeClaimSpec?
response: The possible values for the `accessModes` field are not explicitly listed in the provided text, but it is mentioned that more information can be found at https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/persistent-volumes#access-modes-1.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — PersistentVolumeClaimSpec

instruction: What is the purpose of the `volumeAttributesClassName` field in a PersistentVolumeClaimSpec?
response: The `volumeAttributesClassName` field is used to set the VolumeAttributesClass used by the claim, which can be changed after the claim is created. It allows the CSI driver to create or update the volume with the attributes defined in the corresponding VolumeAttributesClass.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — PersistentVolumeClaimSpec

instruction: What is the difference between the `dataSource` and `dataSourceRef` fields in a PersistentVolumeClaimSpec?
response: The `dataSource` field allows only two specific types of objects, while the `dataSourceRef` field allows any non-core object, as well as PersistentVolumeClaim objects. The `dataSourceRef` field also preserves all values and generates an error if a disallowed value is specified, whereas the `dataSource` field ignores disallowed values.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — PersistentVolumeClaimSpec

instruction: What is the purpose of the `volumeMode` field in a PersistentVolumeClaimSpec?
response: The `volumeMode` field defines what type of volume is required by the claim. If not included in the claim spec, the value of `Filesystem` is implied.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — PersistentVolumeClaimSpec

instruction: What is the default restart policy for a Pod in Kubernetes?
response: The default value for the restartPolicy field in the spec of a Pod is Always.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Container restarts

instruction: How do sidecar containers behave in terms of restarts compared to regular app containers?
response: Sidecar containers ignore the Pod-level restartPolicy field and use their own container-level restartPolicy field, which is always set to Always, and can restart independently of the main application container.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Container restarts

instruction: What is the purpose of the ContainerRestartRules feature gate in Kubernetes?
response: The ContainerRestartRules feature gate allows you to specify restartPolicy and restartPolicyRules on individual containers to override the Pod restart policy.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Container restarts

instruction: What is the exponential backoff delay used by the kubelet when restarting containers?
response: The exponential backoff delay used by the kubelet is 10s, 20s, 40s, ..., capped at 300 seconds (5 minutes).
title: Pod Lifecycle — Container restarts

instruction: What are the supported container restart policies in Kubernetes?
response: The supported container restart policies are Always, OnFailure, and Never.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Container restarts

instruction: What is the purpose of the ImagePullIntent record in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The ImagePullIntent record is a record of the kubelet attempting to pull an image.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — ImagePullIntent

instruction: What is the format of the image field in the ImagePullIntent record?
response: The image field is a string representing the image spec from a Container's image field, and its filename is a SHA-256 hash of this value to avoid filename-unsafe characters.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — ImagePullIntent

instruction: What is the value of the apiVersion field in the ImagePullIntent record?
response: The value of the apiVersion field is kubelet.config.k8s.io/v1beta1.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — ImagePullIntent

instruction: How do you list available contexts in Kubernetes?
response: You can list available contexts using the command kubectl config get-contexts.
title: Troubleshooting kubectl — Verify contexts

instruction: What command do you use to switch to a specific context in Kubernetes?
response: You use the command kubectl config use-context <context-name> to switch to a specific context.
title: Troubleshooting kubectl — Verify contexts

instruction: What is the central component of a Kubernetes cluster that you should check if you're having trouble interacting with the cluster?
response: The central component of a Kubernetes cluster that you should check is the kube-apiserver server.
title: Troubleshooting kubectl — Verify contexts

instruction: What are some steps you can take to troubleshoot issues with the API server in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can check if the API server's host is reachable using the ping command, check the cluster's network connectivity and firewall, and if using a cloud provider, check the cloud provider's health check status for the cluster's API server.
title: Troubleshooting kubectl — Verify contexts

instruction: What is the purpose of the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-additional-resource-tags annotation?
response: The annotation is used to configure tags for a load balancer based on the comma-separated key/value pairs in its value.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-additional-resource-tags (beta)

instruction: What type of Kubernetes object can the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-additional-resource-tags annotation be used on?
response: The annotation can be used on a Service object.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-additional-resource-tags (beta)

instruction: What is the format of the key/value pairs in the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-additional-resource-tags annotation?
response: The key/value pairs are comma-separated.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-additional-resource-tags (beta)

instruction: What environment variables does the kubelet add for each active Service in a Pod?
response: The kubelet adds {SVCNAME}_SERVICE_HOST and {SVCNAME}_SERVICE_PORT variables, where the Service name is upper-cased and dashes are converted to underscores.
title: Service — Discovering services

instruction: What is the ordering requirement when using environment variables to discover a Service?
response: The Service must be created before the client Pods come into existence, otherwise the client Pods won't have their environment variables populated.
title: Service — Discovering services

instruction: Is there an ordering requirement when using DNS to discover a Service?
response: No, there is no ordering requirement when using DNS to discover the cluster IP for a Service.
title: Service — Discovering services

instruction: What is the default behavior of Kubernetes nodes regarding resource allocation?
response: Kubernetes nodes can consume all available capacity on a node by default, leading to resource competition between pods and system daemons.
title: Reserve Compute Resources for System Daemons

instruction: Why is it a problem when pods and system daemons compete for resources on a node?
response: This leads to resource starvation issues on the node.
title: Reserve Compute Resources for System Daemons

instruction: What feature does the kubelet expose to reserve compute resources for system daemons?
response: The feature named 'Node Allocatable' helps to reserve compute resources for system daemons.
title: Reserve Compute Resources for System Daemons

instruction: What volume controllers are not implemented in cloud controller manager?
response: Cloud controller manager does not implement any of the volume controllers found in kube-controller-manager.
title: Cloud Controller Manager Administration — Limitations

instruction: Why are volume controllers not implemented in cloud controller manager?
response: The volume integrations also require coordination with kubelets.
title: Cloud Controller Manager Administration — Limitations

instruction: What is being planned to address the limitations of cloud controller manager?
response: The necessary support will be added to cloud controller manager so that clouds can fully integrate with volumes, as CSI evolves and stronger support for flex volume plugins is added.
title: Cloud Controller Manager Administration — Limitations

instruction: What is the purpose of the volume.beta.kubernetes.io/mount-options annotation on a PersistentVolume?
response: A Kubernetes administrator can specify additional mount options for when a PersistentVolume is mounted on a node.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — volume.beta.kubernetes.io/mount-options (deprecated)

instruction: What type of Kubernetes object can have the volume.beta.kubernetes.io/mount-options annotation when dynamically provisioning a volume?
response: A PersistentVolumeClaim.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — volume.beta.kubernetes.io/mount-options (deprecated)

instruction: What is the value of the volume.beta.kubernetes.io/mount-options annotation on a PersistentVolumeClaim?
response: The name of a volume plugin that is supposed to provision a volume for this PVC.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — volume.beta.kubernetes.io/mount-options (deprecated)

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` parameter in the `GET /apis/apps/v1/watch/replicasets` API call?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` parameter requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK' from the server, which allows the client to resume the watch from a specific point in the event stream.
title: ReplicaSet — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: How does the `continue` parameter work in the `GET /apis/apps/v1/watch/replicasets` API call?
response: The `continue` parameter is used to retrieve more results from the server. It should be set when retrieving more results, and the client should only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: ReplicaSet — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the effect of setting `limit` in the `GET /apis/apps/v1/watch/replicasets` API call?
response: Setting `limit` returns a maximum number of responses to return for a list call. If more items exist, the server will set the `continue` field on the list metadata to a value that can be used with the same initial query to retrieve the next set of results. The server guarantees that the objects returned when using continue will be identical to issuing a single list call without a limit.
title: ReplicaSet — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: Where are the device metadata files stored inside a container when using a directly referenced ResourceClaim to allocate devices?
response: The device metadata files are stored at /var/run/kubernetes.io/dra-device-attributes/resourceclaims/<claimName>/<requestName>/<driverName>-metadata.json.

instruction: What is the output of running kubectl logs gpu-metadata-reader after the Pod is running?
response: The output is similar to === DRA device metadata === followed by the path to the metadata file, which is /var/run/kubernetes.io/dra-device-attributes/resourceclaims/gpu-claim/gpu/gpu.example.com-metadata.json.
title: Access DRA Device Metadata — Access device metadata with a ResourceClaim

instruction: What is the structure of the JSON object containing device attributes like the model, driver version, and device UUID?
response: The JSON structure is described in the metadata schema, but the provided text does not include the schema details.
title: Access DRA Device Metadata — Access device metadata with a ResourceClaim

instruction: What is the default value of the 'pretty' parameter in the GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/deviceclasses request?

title: DeviceClass — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' parameter in the GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/deviceclasses request?
response: The 'continue' parameter is used to retrieve more results from the server. It should be set when retrieving more results from the server and should be used with identical query parameters (except for the value of continue) from a previous query result.
title: DeviceClass — getList

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'limit' to a non-zero value in the GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/deviceclasses request?
response: Setting a non-zero limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items in the event all requested objects are filtered out. The server guarantees that the objects returned when using continue will be identical to issuing a single list call without a limit.
title: DeviceClass — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'sendInitialEvents' parameter in the GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/deviceclasses request?

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the `GET /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/watch/roles` API call?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK' from the server, which can be ignored by servers that do not implement bookmarks.
title: Role — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What happens when the `continue` field is set and the server returns a 410 ResourceExpired error?
response: The server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token, and the client must restart their list without the continue field to receive a consistent list of results.
title: Role — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` together with `watch=true` in the `GET /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/watch/roles` API call?
response: The watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event with the ResourceVersion (RV) corresponding to the set of objects, and then proceed as usual, sending watch events corresponding to changes to objects watched.
title: Role — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the semantic of the watch request when `resourceVersionMatch` is set to NotOlderThan?
response: The semantic is 'data at least as new as the provided `resourceVersion`', and the bookmark event is sent when the state is synced to a `resourceVersion` at least as fresh as the one provided by the ListOptions.
title: Role — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the purpose of the base security hardening recommendations in this checklist?
response: The base security hardening recommendations in this checklist provide security hardening recommendations that would apply to most applications deploying to Kubernetes.
title: Application Security Checklist — Base security hardening

instruction: Why is it recommended to set a memory limit equal to or greater than the request for workloads?
response: Setting a memory limit equal to or greater than the request for workloads helps prevent the workload from consuming more memory than requested, which can help prevent memory-related security issues.
title: Application Security Checklist — Base security hardening

instruction: When might it be necessary to set a CPU limit on a workload?
response: It might be necessary to set a CPU limit on sensitive workloads to prevent them from consuming excessive CPU resources, which can help prevent security issues.
title: Application Security Checklist — Base security hardening

response: The 'allowWatchBookmarks' field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK' from the server. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag, and clients should not assume bookmarks are returned at any specific interval.

instruction: What happens when the 'limit' field is specified in a list call, and the 'continue' field is empty?

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'resourceVersionMatch' parameter in the GET /apis/policy/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/poddisruptionbudgets API call?

instruction: What are the possible values for the 'effect' field in a Toleration?
response: The possible values for the 'effect' field are 'NoExecute', 'NoSchedule', and 'PreferNoSchedule'.
title: Toleration

instruction: What is the default value for the 'operator' field in a Toleration?
response: The default value for the 'operator' field is 'Equal'.
title: Toleration

instruction: What is the meaning of the 'tolerationSeconds' field when it is not set?
response: When 'tolerationSeconds' is not set, it means that the toleration tolerates the taint forever and does not evict the pod.
title: Toleration

instruction: What is the cause of the issue with flex-volume support on Linux distributions such as Fedora CoreOS or Flatcar Container Linux?
response: The directory /usr is mounted as a read-only filesystem, which prevents the flex-volume directory from being writeable.
title: Troubleshooting kubeadm — /usris mounted read-only on nodes

instruction: How can you configure the flex-volume directory using the kubeadm configuration file to work around this issue?
response: You can pass a file using the --config flag on the primary control-plane Node created using kubeadm init, with the specified configuration to override the default path of /usr/libexec/kubernetes/kubelet-plugins/volume/exec/.
title: Troubleshooting kubeadm — /usris mounted read-only on nodes

instruction: What is an alternative solution to modifying the kubeadm configuration file to make the /usr mount writeable?
response: You can modify /etc/fstab to make the /usr mount writeable, but be advised that this is modifying a design principle of the Linux distribution.
title: Troubleshooting kubeadm — /usris mounted read-only on nodes

instruction: What is the default value for the 'pretty' parameter in the GET /apis/storage.k8s.io/v1/storageclasses request?
response: The default value for the 'pretty' parameter is 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).

response: The 'continue' parameter is used to retrieve more results from the server. It should be set when retrieving more results from the server, and clients should only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters (except for the value of continue).
title: StorageClass — getList

instruction: What is the effect of setting the 'limit' parameter to a non-zero value in the GET /apis/storage.k8s.io/v1/storageclasses request?
response: Setting a non-zero 'limit' may return fewer than the requested amount of items (up to zero items) in the event all requested objects are filtered out. The server guarantees that the objects returned when using continue will be identical to issuing a single list call without a limit.

response: The 'resourceVersionMatch' parameter determines how resourceVersion is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that resourceVersionMatch be set for list calls where resourceVersion is set.

instruction: What is the default behavior for ignoring missing template keys in kubectl run?
response: The default is true, which means that any errors in templates when a field or map key is missing in the template will be ignored, but only for golang and jsonpath output formats.
title: kubectl run — Options

instruction: What is the purpose of the --cascade flag in kubectl run?
response: It selects the deletion cascading strategy for the dependents, such as Pods created by a ReplicationController, and can be set to 'background', 'orphan', or 'foreground'.
title: kubectl run — Options

instruction: What is the default restart policy for a Pod created with kubectl run?
response: The default restart policy is 'Always', which means that the Pod will be restarted if it exits.
title: kubectl run — Options

instruction: What is the effect of setting --attach to true in kubectl run?
response: If set to true, kubectl run will wait for the Pod to start running and then attach to the Pod as if 'kubectl attach ...' were called, unless '--restart=Never' is set, in which case the exit code of the container process is returned.
title: kubectl run — Options

instruction: What is the purpose of the --override-type flag in kubectl run?
response: It specifies the method used to override the generated object, which can be 'json', 'merge', or 'strategic'.
title: kubectl run — Options

instruction: What is the effect of setting --rm to true in kubectl run?
response: If set to true, the Pod will be deleted after it exits, only when attaching to the container, such as with '--attach' or with '-i/--stdin'.
title: kubectl run — Options

instruction: What is the purpose of the --save-config flag in kubectl run?
response: It saves the configuration of the current object in its annotation, which is useful for performing kubectl apply on this object in the future.
title: kubectl run — Options

instruction: What is the effect of setting --show-managed-fields to true in kubectl run?
response: It keeps the managedFields when printing objects in JSON or YAML format.
title: kubectl run — Options

instruction: What is the purpose of the --template flag in kubectl run?
response: It specifies a template string or path to a template file to use when outputting in a specific format, such as 'go-template' or 'go-template-file'.
title: kubectl run — Options

instruction: What is the effect of setting --wait to true in kubectl run?
response: It waits for resources to be gone before returning, which includes waiting for finalizers.
title: kubectl run — Options

instruction: What is the purpose of the `kubectl expose` command in this example?
response: The `kubectl expose` command creates a Service object that exposes the deployment to the outside world, allowing it to be accessed from outside the cluster.
title: Exposing an External IP Address to Access an Application in a Cluster — Objectives

instruction: What is the difference between the `Port` and `NodePort` values in the Service description?
response: The `Port` value (8080 in this example) is the port number that the Service listens on, while the `NodePort` value (32377 in this example) is the port number that is exposed to the outside world on each node in the cluster.
title: Exposing an External IP Address to Access an Application in a Cluster — Objectives

instruction: How can you access the Hello World application from outside the cluster?
response: You can access the Hello World application by using the external IP address (LoadBalancer Ingress) of the Service and the port number specified in the Service description, for example, `curl http://<external-ip>:<port>`.
title: Exposing an External IP Address to Access an Application in a Cluster — Objectives

instruction: What is the purpose of specifying a host in an Ingress rule?
response: The host specified in an Ingress rule determines the host that the rule applies to, and the rule will only apply to incoming HTTP traffic for that host.
title: Ingress — Ingress rules

instruction: What happens to incoming requests that do not match a path in the Ingress spec?
response: Incoming requests that do not match a path in the Ingress spec are typically sent to a defaultBackend configured in the Ingress controller.
title: Ingress — Ingress rules

instruction: What is required for a path in an Ingress rule to match an incoming request?
response: Both the host and path of an incoming request must match the content of the Ingress rule before the load balancer directs traffic to the referenced Service.
title: Ingress — Ingress rules

instruction: What type of Kubernetes object uses the 'kubernetes.io/config.source' annotation?
response: A Pod uses the 'kubernetes.io/config.source' annotation.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — kubernetes.io/config.source

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'kubernetes.io/config.source' annotation?
response: This annotation is added by the kubelet to indicate where the Pod comes from.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — kubernetes.io/config.source

instruction: What are the possible values of the 'kubernetes.io/config.source' annotation for static Pods?
response: For static Pods, the annotation value could be 'file' or 'http', depending on where the Pod manifest is located.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — kubernetes.io/config.source

instruction: What are the core design principles for a cluster set up by kubeadm init and kubeadm join?
response: The cluster should be secure, user-friendly, and extendable. It should enforce RBAC, use the Node Authorizer, and enable secure communication between control plane components and the API server and kubelets.
title: Implementation details — Core design principles

instruction: What is the recommended workflow for initializing a cluster with kubeadm?
response: The user should run kubeadm init, export the KUBECONFIG environment variable, and then apply the network plugin configuration using kubectl apply -f <network-plugin-of-choice.yaml>.
title: Implementation details — Core design principles

instruction: How does kubeadm support extensibility in cluster configuration?
response: kubeadm does not favor any particular network provider and allows users to configure the cluster network out-of-scope. It also provides the possibility to use a config file for customizing various parameters.
title: Implementation details — Core design principles

instruction: What is the purpose of CertificateSigningRequest objects in Kubernetes?
response: CertificateSigningRequest objects provide a mechanism to obtain x509 certificates by submitting a certificate signing request, and having it asynchronously approved and issued.
title: Certificates

instruction: What are the two types of certificates that Kubelets use the CertificateSigningRequest API to obtain?
response: Kubelets use this API to obtain client certificates to authenticate to kube-apiserver and serving certificates for TLS endpoints kube-apiserver can connect to securely.
title: Certificates

instruction: What is the purpose of ClusterTrustBundle objects in Kubernetes?
response: ClusterTrustBundle objects are a cluster-scoped container for X.509 trust anchors (root certificates) that can be mounted by pods using the clusterTrustBundle projection.
title: Certificates

instruction: How can users with namespace-level access to a cluster read ClusterTrustBundles?
response: Users who only have namespace-level access to a cluster can read ClusterTrustBundles by impersonating a service account that they have access to.
title: Certificates

instruction: What is the purpose of PodCertificateRequest objects in Kubernetes?
response: PodCertificateRequest objects encode a pod requesting a certificate from a given signer, which is used to implement podCertificate projected volumes.
title: Certificates

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'dryRun' parameter in the Ingress DELETE API?
response: The 'dryRun' parameter indicates that modifications should not be persisted. It allows the user to test the deletion process without actually deleting the Ingress resource.
title: Ingress — deleteDelete

instruction: What is the relationship between 'orphanDependents' and 'PropagationPolicy'?
response: The 'orphanDependents' field is deprecated and should be replaced with 'PropagationPolicy'. Either 'orphanDependents' or 'PropagationPolicy' may be set, but not both.
title: Ingress — deleteDelete

instruction: What happens to in-flight requests to the kube-apiserver during an etcd upgrade with kubeadm?
