title: LimitRange — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What happens when the `limit` parameter is specified and the `continue` field is empty in a GET /api/v1/watch/limitranges request?
response: Clients may assume that no more results are available.
title: LimitRange — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` together with `watch=true` in a GET /api/v1/watch/limitranges request?
response: The watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event with the ResourceVersion (RV) corresponding to the set of objects, and then proceed as usual, sending watch events corresponding to changes to objects watched.
title: LimitRange — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'devicePath' field in the AttachedVolume API?
response: The 'devicePath' field in the AttachedVolume API represents the device path where the volume should be available.
title: Node — AttachedVolume

instruction: What is the difference between the 'name' field in ConfigMapNodeConfigSource and AttachedVolume?
response: The 'name' field in ConfigMapNodeConfigSource is the metadata.name of the referenced ConfigMap, whereas in AttachedVolume, it is the name of the attached volume.
title: Node — AttachedVolume

instruction: Why is the 'resourceVersion' field forbidden in Node.Spec but required in Node.Status?
response: The 'resourceVersion' field is forbidden in Node.Spec because it is not required for the Node's configuration, but it is required in Node.Status to reflect the current state of the referenced ConfigMap.
title: Node — AttachedVolume

instruction: What is the status of the ConfigMapNodeConfigSource API?
response: The ConfigMapNodeConfigSource API is deprecated since 1.22.
title: Node — AttachedVolume

instruction: What happens to fields not specified in a Kubernetes object configuration file when the object is created?
response: The API server sets certain fields to default values in the live configuration.
title: Declarative Management of Kubernetes Objects Using Configuration Files — Default field values

instruction: Can defaulted fields be re-defaulted in a patch request?
response: No, defaulted fields are not re-defaulted unless they are explicitly cleared as part of a patch request.
title: Declarative Management of Kubernetes Objects Using Configuration Files — Default field values

instruction: Why is it recommended to explicitly define certain fields defaulted by the server in the configuration file?
response: It makes it easier to recognize conflicting values that will not be re-defaulted by the server.
title: Declarative Management of Kubernetes Objects Using Configuration Files — Default field values

instruction: What fields should be explicitly defined in the object configuration file according to the recommendation?
response: Selectors and PodTemplate labels on workloads, such as Deployment, StatefulSet, Job, DaemonSet, ReplicaSet, and ReplicationController, and Deployment rollout strategy.
title: Declarative Management of Kubernetes Objects Using Configuration Files — Default field values

instruction: What is the purpose of the AllocatedDeviceStatus in Kubernetes?
response: AllocatedDeviceStatus contains the status of an allocated device, if the driver chooses to report it. This may include driver-specific information.
title: ResourceClaim — AllocatedDeviceStatus

instruction: What is the maximum number of entries allowed in the conditions field of AllocatedDeviceStatus?
response: Must not contain more than 8 entries.
title: ResourceClaim — AllocatedDeviceStatus

instruction: What is the maximum allowed length of the raw data in the data field of AllocatedDeviceStatus?
response: The length of the raw data must be smaller or equal to 10 Ki.
title: ResourceClaim — AllocatedDeviceStatus

instruction: What is the format requirement for the driver name in AllocatedDeviceStatus?
response: Must be a DNS subdomain and should end with a DNS domain owned by the vendor of the driver. It should use only lower case characters.
title: ResourceClaim — AllocatedDeviceStatus

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' field in the Job getList API?
response: The 'continue' field is used to retrieve more results from the server. It should be set when retrieving more results, and clients may only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters (except for the value of continue).
title: Job — getList

instruction: What happens when the client uses the 'limit' field in the Job getList API?
response: When the 'limit' field is specified, the server may return fewer than the requested amount of items (up to zero items) in the event all requested objects are filtered out. Clients should only use the presence of the continue field to determine whether more results are available.
title: Job — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'resourceVersionMatch' field in the Job getList API?
response: The 'resourceVersionMatch' field determines how the 'resourceVersion' is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that 'resourceVersionMatch' be set for list calls where 'resourceVersion' is set.
title: Job — getList

instruction: What happens when the 'sendInitialEvents' option is set to true in the Job getList API?
response: When 'sendInitialEvents' is set to true, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent, and the watch stream will proceed as usual, sending watch events corresponding to changes (subsequent to the RV) to objects watched.
title: Job — getList

instruction: How do you configure the endpoint for the OpenTelemetry Collector receiver in the kubelet?
response: You can configure the endpoint by setting the 'endpoint' value in the tracing configuration. The default value is 'localhost:4317'.
title: Traces For Kubernetes System Components — kubelet traces

instruction: What happens when the 'samplingRatePerMillion' is set to one million (1000000) in the kubelet configuration?
response: When the 'samplingRatePerMillion' is set to one million (1000000), every span will be sent to the exporter.
title: Traces For Kubernetes System Components — kubelet traces

instruction: What are the potential performance overheads of exporting spans in the kubelet?
response: Exporting spans comes with a small performance overhead on the networking and CPU side, depending on the overall configuration of the system.
title: Traces For Kubernetes System Components — kubelet traces

instruction: How can you mitigate performance issues caused by tracing enabled in a cluster?
response: You can mitigate the problem by either reducing the 'samplingRatePerMillion' or disabling tracing completely by removing the configuration.
title: Traces For Kubernetes System Components — kubelet traces

instruction: How do you download the latest stable release of the kubelet?
response: You can download the latest stable release of the kubelet using the command `curl -LO https://dl.k8s.io/release/$(curl -L -s https://dl.k8s.io/release/stable.txt)/bin/linux/amd64/kubelet` for x86-64 or `curl -LO https://dl.k8s.io/release/$(curl -L -s https://dl.k8s.io/release/stable.txt)/bin/linux/arm64/kubelet` for ARM64.
title: Running Kubelet in Standalone Mode — Download and set up the kubelet

instruction: What configuration options are set in the kubelet.yaml file for this tutorial?
response: The kubelet.yaml file sets the following configuration options: authentication webhook is disabled, authorization mode is set to AlwaysAllow, enableServer is set to false, logging format is set to text, address is set to 127.0.0.1, readOnlyPort is set to 10255, staticPodPath is set to /etc/kubernetes/manifests, and containerRuntimeEndpoint is set to unix:///var/run/crio/crio.sock.
title: Running Kubelet in Standalone Mode — Download and set up the kubelet

instruction: How do you enable and start the kubelet service?
response: You can enable and start the kubelet service by running the commands `sudo systemctl daemon-reload` and `sudo systemctl enable --now kubelet.service`.
title: Running Kubelet in Standalone Mode — Download and set up the kubelet

instruction: How do you check the status of the kubelet service?
response: You can check the status of the kubelet service by running the command `sudo systemctl is-active kubelet.service` or by checking the journal logs with `sudo journalctl -u kubelet.service`.
title: Running Kubelet in Standalone Mode — Download and set up the kubelet

instruction: How do you query the kubelet's API /healthz endpoint?
response: You can query the kubelet's API /healthz endpoint by running the command `curl http://localhost:10255/healthz?verbose`.
title: Running Kubelet in Standalone Mode — Download and set up the kubelet

instruction: How do you query the kubelet's API /pods endpoint?
response: You can query the kubelet's API /pods endpoint by running the command `curl http://localhost:10255/pods | jq '.'`.
title: Running Kubelet in Standalone Mode — Download and set up the kubelet

instruction: What is the default behavior of Kubernetes API server authorization?
response: Access is denied by default.
title: Authorization — Authorization verdicts

instruction: What happens when multiple authorization modules are configured in the Kubernetes API server?
response: Each module is checked in sequence, and if any authorizer approves or denies a request, that decision is immediately returned and no other authorizer is consulted.
title: Authorization — Authorization verdicts

instruction: What is the overall deny verdict in Kubernetes API server authorization?
response: The API server rejects the request and responds with an HTTP 403 (Forbidden) status.
title: Authorization — Authorization verdicts

instruction: How do you copy the administrator kubeconfig file from the control-plane node to a workstation?
response: You can use the scp command to copy the file, assuming SSH access is enabled for root, like this: scp root@<control-plane-host>:/etc/kubernetes/admin.conf .
title: Creating a cluster with kubeadm — (Optional) Controlling your cluster from machines other than the control-plane node

instruction: What are the implications of using the admin.conf file?
response: The admin.conf file gives the user superuser privileges over the cluster, and it's recommended to use it sparingly. For normal users, it's recommended to generate a unique credential and grant privileges to that instead.
title: Creating a cluster with kubeadm — (Optional) Controlling your cluster from machines other than the control-plane node

instruction: How do you generate a unique credential for a user?
response: You can use the kubeadm kubeconfig user --client-name <CN> command to generate a unique credential, which will print out a KubeConfig file to STDOUT that you should save to a file and distribute to your user.
title: Creating a cluster with kubeadm — (Optional) Controlling your cluster from machines other than the control-plane node

instruction: What is the default value of the 'pretty' parameter in the DELETE /apis/admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1/mutatingwebhookconfigurations request?
response: The default value of the 'pretty' parameter is 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: MutatingWebhookConfiguration — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What happens when a client uses the 'continue' parameter with an invalid or expired value?
response: The server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token. The client may send another list request with the token received with the 410 error, and the server will respond with a list starting from the next key, but from the latest snapshot.
title: MutatingWebhookConfiguration — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting the 'ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential' parameter to true?
response: It will trigger an unsafe deletion of the resource in case the normal deletion flow fails with a corrupt object error. A resource is considered corrupt if it can not be retrieved from the underlying storage successfully because of a) its data can not be transformed e.g. decryption failure, or b) it fails to decode into an object.
title: MutatingWebhookConfiguration — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the default policy for garbage collection when the 'propagationPolicy' parameter is not set?
response: The default policy is decided by the existing finalizer set in the metadata.finalizers and the resource-specific default policy.
title: MutatingWebhookConfiguration — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the default value for the cluster name in kubeadm?
response: The default value is 'kubernetes'.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta4) — ClusterConfiguration

instruction: How do you specify the type of asymmetric encryption algorithm used for keys and certificates in kubeadm?
response: You can specify the type of asymmetric encryption algorithm used for keys and certificates by setting the encryptionAlgorithm field to one of 'RSA-2048', 'RSA-3072', 'RSA-4096', 'ECDSA-P256', or 'ECDSA-P384'.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta4) — ClusterConfiguration

instruction: What is the default validity period for a non-CA certificate generated by kubeadm?
response: The default validity period for a non-CA certificate generated by kubeadm is 8760h (365 days * 24 hours = 1 year).
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta4) — ClusterConfiguration

instruction: What is the default validity period for a CA certificate generated by kubeadm?
response: The default validity period for a CA certificate generated by kubeadm is 87600h (365 days * 24 hours * 10 = 10 years).
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta4) — ClusterConfiguration

instruction: What is the default value for the 'pretty' parameter in the DELETE /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3/resourcepoolstatusrequests request?
response: The default value for the 'pretty' parameter is 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: ResourcePoolStatusRequest — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What happens when the 'continue' field is set and the client needs a consistent list?
response: The client must restart their list without the continue field. Otherwise, the client may send another list request with the token received with the 410 error, and the server will respond with a list starting from the next key, but from the latest snapshot, which is inconsistent from the previous list results.
title: ResourcePoolStatusRequest — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential' to true?
response: It will trigger an unsafe deletion of the resource in case the normal deletion flow fails with a corrupt object error. A resource is considered corrupt if it can not be retrieved from the underlying storage successfully because of a) its data can not be transformed e.g. decryption failure, or b) it fails to decode into an object.
title: ResourcePoolStatusRequest — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the LeaderMigrationConfiguration configuration file in the Leader Migration process?
response: The LeaderMigrationConfiguration configuration file represents the state of controller-to-manager assignment and is used to configure the Leader Migration process.
title: Migrate Replicated Control Plane To Use Cloud Controller Manager — Initial Leader Migration configuration

instruction: What is the effect of setting the component to * for both sides in the LeaderMigrationConfiguration configuration file?
response: Setting the component to * for both sides makes the configuration file consistent between both parties of the migration, allowing the controllers to run under either controller managers.
title: Migrate Replicated Control Plane To Use Cloud Controller Manager — Initial Leader Migration configuration

instruction: What are the steps to enable Leader Migration on each control plane node?
response: To enable Leader Migration on each control plane node, save the LeaderMigrationConfiguration configuration file to /etc/leadermigration.conf, update the manifest of kube-controller-manager to mount the file inside the container, add the --enable-leader-migration and --leader-migration-config=/etc/leadermigration.conf arguments, and restart kube-controller-manager.
title: Migrate Replicated Control Plane To Use Cloud Controller Manager — Initial Leader Migration configuration

instruction: What is the default value for the 'pretty' parameter in the DELETE /apis/storagemigration.k8s.io/v1beta1/storageversionmigrations request?
response: The default value for the 'pretty' parameter is 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: StorageVersionMigration — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What happens if the client uses a continue value that is no longer valid due to expiration or a configuration change on the server?
response: The server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token. The client may send another list request with the token received with the 410 error, and the server will respond with a list starting from the next key, but from the latest snapshot.
title: StorageVersionMigration — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'dryRun' parameter in the PATCH /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/clusterroles/{name} request?
response: The 'dryRun' parameter indicates that modifications should not be persisted. An invalid or unrecognized 'dryRun' directive will result in an error response and no further processing of the request.
title: ClusterRole — patchPatch

instruction: What are the valid values for the 'fieldValidation' parameter in the PATCH /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/clusterroles/{name} request?
response: The valid values for 'fieldValidation' are 'Ignore', 'Warn', and 'Strict'. 'Ignore' will silently drop unknown fields, 'Warn' will send a warning for unknown fields and duplicate fields, and 'Strict' will fail the request with a BadRequest error for unknown or duplicate fields.
title: ClusterRole — patchPatch

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'fieldManager' parameter in the PATCH /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/clusterroles/{name} request?
response: The 'fieldManager' parameter is a name associated with the actor or entity making changes. It is required for apply requests but optional for non-apply patch types. The value must be less than or 128 characters long and only contain printable characters.
title: ClusterRole — patchPatch

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubeadm certs generate-csr command?
response: The kubeadm certs generate-csr command generates CSRs for all known certificates managed by kubeadm.
title: Certificate Management with kubeadm — Generate CSRs

instruction: What happens if you delete the ca.key file from control plane nodes in External CA mode?
response: The active kube-controller-manager in this cluster will not be able to sign kubelet client certificates, and automatic kubelet client certificate rotation will be disabled.
title: Certificate Management with kubeadm — Generate CSRs

instruction: When can you skip processing the kubelet.conf.csr file on secondary control plane and worker nodes?
response: You can skip processing the kubelet.conf.csr file if the active kube-controller-manager will be responsible for signing new kubelet client certificates.
title: Certificate Management with kubeadm — Generate CSRs

instruction: What command should you execute on primary and secondary control plane nodes to generate all CSR files?
response: You should execute the command sudo kubeadm certs generate-csr on primary and secondary control plane nodes.
title: Certificate Management with kubeadm — Generate CSRs

instruction: What is the default mechanism for collecting pod and container metrics in the kubelet?
response: The kubelet collects pod and container metrics via cAdvisor by default.
title: CRI Pod & Container Metrics

instruction: What feature gate must be enabled to use the CRI-based collection mechanism?
response: The PodAndContainerStatsFromCRI feature gate must be enabled.
title: CRI Pod & Container Metrics

instruction: What are the minimum compatible CRI implementation versions required for CRI-based collection?
response: containerd version 1.6.0 or CRI-O version 1.23.0 or later are required.
title: CRI Pod & Container Metrics

instruction: What is the primary difference between DaemonSet and static Pods?
response: Static Pods cannot be managed with kubectl or other Kubernetes API clients, unlike DaemonSet.
title: DaemonSet — Static Pods

instruction: What is a use case for static Pods?
response: Static Pods are useful in cluster bootstrapping cases due to their independence from the apiserver.
title: DaemonSet — Static Pods

instruction: Are static Pods a recommended practice?
response: No, static Pods may be deprecated in the future.
title: DaemonSet — Static Pods

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' field in the PodCertificateRequest watch API?
response: The 'continue' field is used to retrieve more results from the server. It should be set when retrieving more results and is server-defined, so clients can only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters (except for the value of continue).
title: PodCertificateRequest — getWatch

instruction: What happens when a client uses the 'limit' field in the PodCertificateRequest watch API?
response: When the 'limit' field is specified, the server may return fewer than the requested amount of items (up to zero items) in the event all requested objects are filtered out. Clients should only use the presence of the continue field to determine whether more results are available.
title: PodCertificateRequest — getWatch

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'sendInitialEvents' to true in the PodCertificateRequest watch API?
response: When 'sendInitialEvents' is set to true, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent, reporting the ResourceVersion (RV) corresponding to the set of objects.
title: PodCertificateRequest — getWatch

instruction: What is the default scope for an IngressClassParametersReference?
response: The default scope for an IngressClassParametersReference is 'Cluster'.
title: IngressClass — IngressClassParametersReference

instruction: Is the 'namespace' field required when specifying a 'scope' of 'Namespace'?
response: Yes, the 'namespace' field is required when specifying a 'scope' of 'Namespace'.
title: IngressClass — IngressClassParametersReference

instruction: What happens if the 'apiGroup' field is not specified for a third-party type?
response: If the 'apiGroup' field is not specified for a third-party type, the specified 'Kind' must be in the core API group.
title: IngressClass — IngressClassParametersReference

instruction: What happens when an Ingress has no rules specified?
response: All traffic is sent to a single default backend.
title: Ingress — DefaultBackend

instruction: Where is the default backend specified in an Ingress resource?
response: The default backend is specified in the .spec.defaultBackend field, but it is not specified in the Ingress resource itself, rather it is a configuration option of the Ingress controller.
title: Ingress — DefaultBackend

instruction: What happens if the defaultBackend is not set in an Ingress resource?
response: The handling of requests that do not match any of the rules will be up to the ingress controller, and you should consult the documentation for your ingress controller to find out how it handles this case.
title: Ingress — DefaultBackend

instruction: What flag is used to provide the Kubernetes CA key and certificate to kube-controller-manager?
response: The --cluster-signing-cert-file and --cluster-signing-key-file flags are used to provide the Kubernetes CA key and certificate to kube-controller-manager.
title: TLS bootstrapping — Access to key and certificate

instruction: How is the Kubernetes CA key and certificate provided to kube-apiserver?
response: The Kubernetes CA key and certificate are provided to kube-apiserver with the --client-ca-file flag, as described in the kube-apiserver configuration section.
title: TLS bootstrapping — Access to key and certificate

instruction: What flag is used to configure the validity duration of signed certificates?
response: The --cluster-signing-duration flag is used to configure the validity duration of signed certificates.
title: TLS bootstrapping — Access to key and certificate

instruction: What is the purpose of the service.beta.kubernetes.io/load-balancer-source-ranges annotation?
response: The service.beta.kubernetes.io/load-balancer-source-ranges annotation is used by the AWS load balancer controller to specify the source IP ranges that are allowed to access the load balancer.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/load-balancer-source-ranges (deprecated)

instruction: What is the recommended replacement for the service.beta.kubernetes.io/load-balancer-source-ranges annotation?
response: You should set .spec.loadBalancerSourceRanges for the Service instead of using the service.beta.kubernetes.io/load-balancer-source-ranges annotation.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/load-balancer-source-ranges (deprecated)

instruction: What is the purpose of the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-ssl-cert annotation?
response: The service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-ssl-cert annotation is used to configure TLS for a Service of type: LoadBalancer based on an AWS Resource Name (ARN) of the X.509 certificate that the load balancer listener should use.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/load-balancer-source-ranges (deprecated)

instruction: What are some examples of extension points in the Kubernetes API server?
response: The API server has several types of extension points that allow authenticating requests, blocking them based on their content, editing content, and handling deletion.
title: Extending Kubernetes — Extension points

instruction: How can you extend the behavior of the Kubernetes scheduler?
response: You can extend the scheduling behavior by using the extension points described in the Scheduling extensions section.
title: Extending Kubernetes — Extension points

instruction: What is the role of the kubelet in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The kubelet runs on servers (nodes), and helps pods appear like virtual servers with their own IPs on the cluster network. It also mounts and unmounts volumes for pods and their containers.
title: Extending Kubernetes — Extension points

instruction: What are some examples of plugins that can be used to customize the behavior of clients in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Some examples of plugins include Network Plugins, Device Plugins, and Storage Plugins.
title: Extending Kubernetes — Extension points

instruction: What is the purpose of a VolumeSnapshotClass in Kubernetes?
response: A VolumeSnapshotClass provides a way to describe the 'classes' of storage when provisioning a volume snapshot.
title: Volume Snapshot Classes

instruction: What prerequisites are suggested for understanding VolumeSnapshotClass?
response: Familiarity with volume snapshots and storage classes is suggested.
title: Volume Snapshot Classes

instruction: How can you determine if a Node supports SupplementalGroups control?
response: You can see if the feature is supported in the Node status by checking the 'status.features.supplementalGroupsPolicy' field.
title: Configure a Security Context for a Pod or Container — Implementations

instruction: What happens when a pod with SupplementalGroupsPolicy=Strict is scheduled to a node that does not support this feature?
response: At the alpha release (v1.31 to v1.32), the pod's supplemental groups policy falls back to the Merge policy silently. However, since the beta release (v1.33), the pod creation will be rejected by kubelet.
title: Configure a Security Context for a Pod or Container — Implementations

instruction: What warning event will be generated when a pod is rejected due to SupplementalGroupsPolicyNotSupported?
response: A warning event with reason=SupplementalGroupsPolicyNotSupported will be generated, with a message like 'SupplementalGroupsPolicy=Strict is not supported in this node'.
title: Configure a Security Context for a Pod or Container — Implementations

instruction: What happens to a Pod when a container in it fails, and the restartPolicy is set to 'OnFailure'?
response: The Pod stays on the node, but the container is re-run.
title: Jobs — Handling Pod and container failures

instruction: How does the Job controller handle a Pod failure when the restartPolicy is set to 'Never'?
response: The Job controller starts a new Pod.
title: Jobs — Handling Pod and container failures

instruction: What is the default behavior when a Pod fails, and how can it be customized?
response: By default, each pod failure is counted towards the backoffLimit limit, but it can be customized by setting the Job's pod failure policy.
title: Jobs — Handling Pod and container failures

instruction: What happens if a Pod is terminated, but its status is still 'Running'?
response: The Job controller does not consider it a failure until the Pod is terminal (its status.phase is Failed or Succeeded).
title: Jobs — Handling Pod and container failures

instruction: What is the name of the Pod created using the provided configuration file?
response: The name of the Pod is secret-test-pod.
title: Distribute Credentials Securely Using Secrets — Create a Pod that has access to the secret data through a Volume

instruction: Where is the secret data exposed to the Container through a Volume?
response: The secret data is exposed to the Container through a Volume mounted under /etc/secret-volume.
title: Distribute Credentials Securely Using Secrets — Create a Pod that has access to the secret data through a Volume

instruction: How do you list the files in the /etc/secret-volume directory inside the Container?
response: You can list the files in the /etc/secret-volume directory by running the command ls /etc/secret-volume inside the Container.
title: Distribute Credentials Securely Using Secrets — Create a Pod that has access to the secret data through a Volume

instruction: How do you display the contents of the username and password files inside the Container?
response: You can display the contents of the username and password files by running the commands echo '$(cat /etc/secret-volume/username)' and echo '$(cat /etc/secret-volume/password)' inside the Container.
title: Distribute Credentials Securely Using Secrets — Create a Pod that has access to the secret data through a Volume

instruction: What types of APIs are documented in this section?
response: This section hosts the documentation for 'unpublished' APIs which are used to configure Kubernetes components or tools.
title: Reference — Config APIs

instruction: Why are most of the APIs in this section not exposed by the API server in a RESTful way?
response: Most of these APIs are not exposed by the API server in a RESTful way though they are essential for a user or an operator to use or manage a cluster.
title: Reference — Config APIs

instruction: What is an example of a kubelet configuration API?
response: An example of a kubelet configuration API is kubelet configuration (v1alpha1), kubelet configuration (v1beta1), and kubelet configuration (v1).
title: Reference — Config APIs

instruction: What is the basic syntax for creating an ingress with kubectl create ingress?
response: The basic syntax is kubectl create ingress NAME --rule=host/path=service:port[,tls[=secret]].
title: kubectl create ingress — Synopsis

instruction: How can you specify multiple rules for an ingress using kubectl create ingress?
response: You can specify multiple rules using the --rule option multiple times, or by separating each rule with a comma.
title: kubectl create ingress — Synopsis

instruction: What is the purpose of the --class option when creating an ingress with kubectl create ingress?
response: The --class option specifies the Ingress Class for the ingress.
title: kubectl create ingress — Synopsis

instruction: How can you specify annotations for an ingress using kubectl create ingress?
response: You can specify annotations using the --annotation option followed by the annotation key and value.
title: kubectl create ingress — Synopsis

instruction: What is the purpose of the --default-backend option when creating an ingress with kubectl create ingress?
response: The --default-backend option specifies the default backend service for the ingress.
title: kubectl create ingress — Synopsis

instruction: What is the default scheme used by the kubelet when performing an HTTP probe?
response: The default scheme used by the kubelet when performing an HTTP probe is HTTP.
title: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes — HTTP probes

instruction: What happens when the kubelet follows a redirect in an HTTP probe?
response: If the redirect is to the same host, the kubelet follows it. If the redirect is to a different hostname, the kubelet treats the probe as successful and records a ProbeWarning event.
title: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes — HTTP probes

instruction: What is the maximum size of the response body that the kubelet reads when processing an httpGet probe?
response: The kubelet stops reading the response body after 10KiB.
title: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes — HTTP probes

instruction: What is the recommended approach for reliable httpGet probes?
response: It is strongly recommended to use dedicated health check endpoints that return a minimal response body.
title: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes — HTTP probes

instruction: What are the valid values for issuer.audienceMatchPolicy in the AudienceMatchPolicyType?
response: AudienceMatchPolicyType is a set of valid values for issuer.audienceMatchPolicy, but the specific values are not listed in this section.
title: kube-apiserver Configuration (v1alpha1) — AudienceMatchPolicyType

instruction: What is the purpose of the name field in the AuthorizationConfiguration?
response: The name field is used to describe the webhook and is explicitly used in monitoring machinery for metrics, and must be a DNS1123 label like myauthorizername or subdomains like myauthorizer.example.domain.
title: kube-apiserver Configuration (v1alpha1) — AudienceMatchPolicyType

instruction: What is the relationship between the Type field and the webhook field in the AuthorizationConfiguration?
response: The webhook field must be defined when Type=Webhook and must not be defined when Type!=Webhook.
title: kube-apiserver Configuration (v1alpha1) — AudienceMatchPolicyType

instruction: What is the purpose of a GatewayClass in the Gateway API?
response: A GatewayClass defines a set of gateways with common configuration and is managed by a controller that implements the class.
title: Gateway API — Resource model

instruction: How are Gateway and HTTPRoute related in the Gateway API?
response: A Gateway can have one or more HTTPRoute objects associated with it, which define HTTP-specific rules for mapping traffic from a Gateway listener to backend network endpoints.
title: Gateway API — Resource model

instruction: What is the relationship between a Gateway and its associated GatewayClass?
response: A Gateway object is associated with exactly one GatewayClass, which describes the gateway controller responsible for managing Gateways of this class.
title: Gateway API — Resource model

instruction: What is the purpose of a GRPCRoute in the Gateway API?
response: A GRPCRoute defines gRPC-specific rules for mapping traffic from a Gateway listener to a representation of backend network endpoints, which are often represented as a Service.
title: Gateway API — Resource model

instruction: Where does the kubelet make a TCP probe connection for a Pod?
response: The kubelet makes the probe connection at the node, not in the Pod.
title: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes — TCP probes

instruction: Can a service name be used in the host parameter for a TCP probe?
response: No, because the kubelet is unable to resolve it.
title: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes — TCP probes

instruction: How can you configure Kubernetes to use gRPC for application startup, liveness, or readiness checks?
response: You can configure Kubernetes to use gRPC by implementing the gRPC Health Checking Protocol in your application and specifying the port and service name in the probe configuration.
title: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes — TCP probes

instruction: What is the recommended way to specify a custom service name and probe type in Kubernetes?
response: The Kubernetes project recommends using a name that concatenates the service name and probe type, separated by a hyphen, for example, myservice-liveness.
title: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes — TCP probes

instruction: What happens to running Pods when the kubelet is restarted?
response: Pods (and their containers) continue to run even during the kubelet restart.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Pod behavior during kubelet restarts

instruction: How does the kubelet behave when stopping or restarting while there are running Pods on a node?
response: The kubelet does not stop all local Pods before it stops itself.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Pod behavior during kubelet restarts

instruction: What is an alternative to stopping Pods on a node when the kubelet is restarted?
response: You can use kubectl drain to stop the Pods on a node.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Pod behavior during kubelet restarts

instruction: What does the '*' wildcard represent in the 'apiGroups' field of a PolicyRule?
response: The '*' wildcard in the 'apiGroups' field represents all API groups.
title: PolicyRule

instruction: Can a PolicyRule apply to both API resources and non-resource URL paths?
response: No, a PolicyRule can either apply to API resources or non-resource URL paths, but not both.
title: PolicyRule

instruction: What does the 'ResourceNames' field in a PolicyRule do?
response: The 'ResourceNames' field is an optional white list of names that the rule applies to. An empty set means that everything is allowed.
title: PolicyRule

instruction: What is the purpose of a ResourceQuota in a Kubernetes namespace?
response: A ResourceQuota restricts the memory request total for all Pods running in a namespace.
title: Configure Memory and CPU Quotas for a Namespace — Discussion

instruction: What alternative to ResourceQuota can be used to restrict individual Pods or containers?
response: A LimitRange can be used to restrict individual Pods or containers.
title: Configure Memory and CPU Quotas for a Namespace — Discussion

instruction: What types of resource quotas can be restricted using a ResourceQuota?
response: Resource quotas for memory limit, cpu request, and cpu limit can be restricted using a ResourceQuota.
title: Configure Memory and CPU Quotas for a Namespace — Discussion

instruction: What are the supported operators for field selectors in Kubernetes?
response: The supported operators for field selectors are =, ==, and !=. = and == mean the same thing.
title: Field Selectors — Supported operators

instruction: How can you use multiple field selectors together in a Kubernetes query?
response: Field selectors can be chained together as a comma-separated list.
title: Field Selectors — Supported operators

instruction: What is the purpose of the --config flag in kubeadm commands?
response: The --config flag allows passing a configuration file from disk to most kubeadm commands.
title: Implementation details — The kubeadm configuration file format

instruction: How do different kubeadm subcommands require different kinds of configuration files?
response: Different kubeadm subcommands, such as kubeadm init, kubeadm join, kubeadm upgrade, and kubeadm reset, require different kinds of configuration files, such as InitConfiguration, JoinConfiguration, UpgradeConfiguration, and ResetConfiguration, respectively.
title: Implementation details — The kubeadm configuration file format

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubeadm config migrate command?
response: The kubeadm config migrate command can be used to migrate an older format configuration file to a newer (current) configuration format, but it only supports migrating from deprecated configuration formats to the current format.
title: Implementation details — The kubeadm configuration file format

instruction: How do feature gates control the availability of Alpha or Beta features in Kubernetes?
response: Feature gates control the availability of Alpha or Beta features in Kubernetes by allowing administrators to enable or disable specific features on a cluster-wide basis. Each feature gate has a default value, which determines whether the feature is enabled or disabled by default.
title: Feature Gates — Feature gates for Alpha or Beta features

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'Since' and 'Until' columns in the feature gate table?
response: The 'Since' and 'Until' columns in the feature gate table indicate the Kubernetes version range during which a feature is in a specific stage (Alpha or Beta). The 'Since' column specifies the version from which the feature is available, and the 'Until' column specifies the version up to which the feature is available.
title: Feature Gates — Feature gates for Alpha or Beta features

instruction: How do feature gates handle feature deprecation?
response: Feature gates handle feature deprecation by automatically disabling features when they are deprecated. This ensures that deprecated features are not accidentally enabled on a cluster, which could lead to unexpected behavior or security issues.
title: Feature Gates — Feature gates for Alpha or Beta features

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubernetes.io/tls Secret type in Kubernetes?
response: The kubernetes.io/tls Secret type is for storing a certificate and its associated key that are typically used for TLS.
title: Secrets — TLS Secrets

instruction: How must the tls.key and tls.crt keys be provided in the data field of a Secret configuration?
response: The tls.key and tls.crt keys must be provided in the data field of the Secret configuration, and they can be either base64 encoded or provided as string data.
title: Secrets — TLS Secrets

instruction: What is the advantage of using the kubernetes.io/tls Secret type over an Opaque type for TLS authentication?
response: Using the kubernetes.io/tls Secret type helps ensure the consistency of Secret format in your project, as the API server verifies if the required keys are set for a Secret of this type.
title: Secrets — TLS Secrets

instruction: How can you create a TLS Secret using kubectl?
response: You can create a TLS Secret using kubectl with the tls subcommand, providing the path to a .PEM encoded public key certificate and a private key file.
title: Secrets — TLS Secrets

instruction: What is the primary function of the descheduler component in Node autoscaling?
response: The descheduler provides Node consolidation functionality based on custom policies.
title: Node Autoscaling — Related components

instruction: What other features does the descheduler offer in addition to Node consolidation?
response: The descheduler also optimizes Nodes and Pods, for example, by deleting frequently restarting Pods.
title: Node Autoscaling — Related components

instruction: What is the recommended cluster configuration for running this tutorial?
response: It is recommended to run this tutorial on a cluster with at least two nodes that are not acting as control plane hosts.
title: Harden Dynamic Resource Allocation in Your Cluster

instruction: How can I check the version of the kubectl command-line tool?
response: To check the version, enter kubectl version.
title: Harden Dynamic Resource Allocation in Your Cluster

instruction: What are some alternatives to creating a cluster from scratch?
response: You can use minikube or one of these Kubernetes playgrounds: iximiuz Labs, Killercoda, KodeKloud.
title: Harden Dynamic Resource Allocation in Your Cluster

instruction: What is the purpose of the PodLogsQuerySplitStreams feature gate in Kubernetes?
response: The PodLogsQuerySplitStreams feature gate enables the kubelet to split out logs from the two standard streams produced by a container: standard output and standard error.
title: Logging Architecture — Container log streams

instruction: How can you access the log streams directly via the Pod API with the feature gate enabled?
response: You can fetch a specific stream by specifying the stream name (either Stdout or Stderr), using the stream query string, and must have access to read the log subresource of that Pod.
title: Logging Architecture — Container log streams

instruction: What is the command to fetch only the stderr log stream from a Pod?
response: You can run kubectl get --raw "/api/v1/namespaces/default/pods/counter-err/log?stream=Stderr" to fetch only the stderr log stream from a Pod.
title: Logging Architecture — Container log streams

instruction: What is the purpose of the Kubernetes glossary?
response: The Kubernetes glossary is intended to be a comprehensive, standardized list of Kubernetes terminology, including technical terms specific to Kubernetes and more general terms that provide useful context.
title: Glossary

instruction: What is an add-on in Kubernetes, and how are they used?
response: Add-ons are resources that extend the functionality of Kubernetes. They can be installed and used to enhance the capabilities of a Kubernetes cluster. The 'Installing addons' section explains more about using add-ons with your cluster and lists some popular add-ons.
title: Glossary

instruction: What is an admission controller in Kubernetes, and what are its functions?
response: An admission controller is a piece of code that intercepts requests to the Kubernetes API server prior to persistence of the object. Admission controllers are configurable for the Kubernetes API server and may be 'validating', 'mutating', or both. They can reject requests, modify objects, or validate them.
title: Glossary

instruction: What is affinity in Kubernetes, and how is it used?
response: Affinity in Kubernetes is a set of rules that give hints to the scheduler about where to place pods. There are two kinds of affinity: node affinity and pod-to-pod affinity. The rules are defined using Kubernetes labels and selectors specified in pods and can be either required or preferred.
title: Glossary

instruction: What is the aggregation layer in Kubernetes, and what does it do?
response: The aggregation layer lets you install additional Kubernetes-style APIs in your cluster. When configured, you can add APIService objects to 'claim' a URL path in the Kubernetes API.
title: Glossary

instruction: What is an annotation in Kubernetes, and how is it used?
response: An annotation is a key-value pair used to attach arbitrary non-identifying metadata to objects. The metadata can be small or large, structured or unstructured, and can include characters not permitted by labels. Clients can retrieve this metadata.
title: Glossary

instruction: What is an API group in Kubernetes, and how is it used?
response: An API group is a set of related paths in the Kubernetes API. You can enable or disable each API group by changing the configuration of your API server. An API group makes it easier to extend the Kubernetes API.
title: Glossary

instruction: What is an API resource in Kubernetes, and how is it used?
response: An API resource, also known as a resource, is an entity in the Kubernetes type system, corresponding to an endpoint on the Kubernetes API. A resource typically represents an object, and some resources represent an operation on other objects.
title: Glossary

instruction: What is an API server in Kubernetes, and how is it used?
response: The API server is a component of the Kubernetes control plane that exposes the Kubernetes API. It is the front end for the Kubernetes control plane and can be scaled horizontally by deploying more instances.
title: Glossary

instruction: What is API-initiated eviction in Kubernetes, and how is it used?
response: API-initiated eviction is the process by which you use the Eviction API to create an Eviction object that triggers graceful pod termination. It respects your configured PodDisruptionBudgets and terminationGracePeriodSeconds.
title: Glossary

instruction: What is an app container in Kubernetes, and how is it used?
response: An app container is the container in a pod that is started after any init containers have completed. An init container lets you separate initialization details that are important for the overall workload and don't need to keep running once the application container has started.
title: Glossary

instruction: What is the difference between a ClusterRole and a Role in Kubernetes?
response: A ClusterRole is a cluster-level, logical grouping of PolicyRules, whereas a Role is a namespaced, logical grouping of PolicyRules.
title: Rbac

instruction: Can a RoleBinding reference a Role in the same namespace or a ClusterRole in the global namespace?
response: Yes, a RoleBinding can reference a Role in the same namespace or a ClusterRole in the global namespace.
title: Rbac

instruction: What is the purpose of a ClusterRoleBinding in Kubernetes?
response: A ClusterRoleBinding references a ClusterRole and adds who information via Subject, allowing a user or service account to be granted permissions at the cluster level.
title: Rbac

instruction: Do RoleBindings in a given namespace only have effect in that namespace?
response: Yes, RoleBindings in a given namespace only have effect in that namespace.
title: Rbac

instruction: What are the possible values for the --validate flag?
response: The possible values for the --validate flag are strict (or true), warn, ignore (or false).
title: kubectl create rolebinding — Options

instruction: What is the purpose of the --save-config flag?
response: The --save-config flag saves the configuration of the current object in its annotation, making it useful for future kubectl apply operations.
title: kubectl create rolebinding — Options

instruction: What is the purpose of the NodeSwapStatus field in the NodeSystemInfo?
response: NodeSwapStatus represents swap memory information.
title: Node — NodeSwapStatus

instruction: What does the 'machineID' field in NodeSystemInfo represent?
response: MachineID reported by the node. For unique machine identification in the cluster this field is preferred.
title: Node — NodeSwapStatus

instruction: What is the difference between 'machineID' and 'systemUUID' in NodeSystemInfo?
response: MachineID is preferred for unique machine identification in the cluster, while SystemUUID is specific to Red Hat hosts.
title: Node — NodeSwapStatus

instruction: How do I create a Pod with an init container?
response: To create a Pod with an init container, you need to learn more about the process, which is not covered in this section. However, you can start by checking the Kubernetes documentation on creating a Pod.
title: Init Containers — What's next

instruction: What types of probes are available in Kubernetes?
response: In Kubernetes, there are three types of probes: liveness, readiness, and startup probes. These probes are used to check the health and status of containers within a Pod.
title: Init Containers — What's next

instruction: How can you get a list of the Pods of the DRA driver DaemonSet across all worker nodes?
response: You can use the command `kubectl get pod -l app.kubernetes.io/name=dra-example-driver -n dra-tutorial` to get a list of the Pods of the DRA driver DaemonSet across all worker nodes.
title: Install Drivers and Allocate Devices with DRA — Verify the DRA driver installation

instruction: What is the initial responsibility of each node's local DRA driver?
response: The initial responsibility of each node's local DRA driver is to update the cluster with what devices are available to Pods on that node, by publishing its metadata to the ResourceSlices API.
title: Install Drivers and Allocate Devices with DRA — Verify the DRA driver installation

instruction: How can you check for available ResourceSlices?
response: You can use the command `kubectl get resourceslices` to check for available ResourceSlices.
title: Install Drivers and Allocate Devices with DRA — Verify the DRA driver installation

instruction: What is the purpose of the KubeletCrashLoopBackOffMax feature gate?
response: With the KubeletCrashLoopBackOffMax feature gate enabled, you can reconfigure the maximum delay between container start retries from the default of 300s (5 minutes).
title: Pod Lifecycle — Configurable container restart delay

instruction: How do you configure the maximum container restart period per node using kubelet configuration?
response: Under the crashLoopBackOff section in your kubelet configuration, set the maxContainerRestartPeriod field between "1s" and "300s".
title: Pod Lifecycle — Configurable container restart delay

instruction: What happens if the maxContainerRestartPeriod you configure is less than the default initial value of 10s?
response: If the maxContainerRestartPeriod you configure is less than the default initial value of 10s, the initial delay will instead be set to the configured maximum.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Configurable container restart delay

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the `GET /apis/batch/v1/watch/cronjobs` API call?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK' from the server, but servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: CronJob — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the effect of setting `limit` in the `GET /apis/batch/v1/watch/cronjobs` API call?
response: Setting a limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items (up to zero items) in the event all requested objects are filtered out, and the server guarantees that the objects returned when using continue will be identical to issuing a single list call without a limit.
title: CronJob — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the purpose of the `sendInitialEvents` field in the `GET /apis/batch/v1/watch/cronjobs` API call?
response: The `sendInitialEvents` field may be set together with `watch=true` to produce the current state of objects in the collection, and a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent with the ResourceVersion (RV) corresponding to the set of objects.
title: CronJob — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What are the three API endpoints provided by the Kubernetes API server to indicate its current status?
response: The Kubernetes API server provides 3 API endpoints (healthz, livez, and readyz) to indicate the current status of the API server.
title: Kubernetes API health endpoints

instruction: What is the recommended replacement for the deprecated healthz endpoint?
response: The livez and readyz endpoints are recommended replacements for the deprecated healthz endpoint.
title: Kubernetes API health endpoints

instruction: How can you specify the startup duration for the livez endpoint?
response: You can specify the startup duration for the livez endpoint using the --livez-grace-period flag.
title: Kubernetes API health endpoints

instruction: How can you determine if the API server is ready to accept traffic?
response: You can determine if the API server is ready to accept traffic by using the /readyz endpoint and checking for a failure status code. If /readyz returns a failure status code, it indicates the server is still initializing or temporarily unable to serve requests.
title: Kubernetes API health endpoints

instruction: How can you exclude specific checks when using the health API endpoints?
response: You can exclude specific checks by using the exclude query parameter. For example, you can use the command curl -k 'https://localhost:6443/readyz?verbose&exclude=etcd'.
title: Kubernetes API health endpoints

instruction: What is the purpose of the `--clusterrole` flag in the `kubectl create clusterrolebinding` command?
response: The `--clusterrole` flag specifies the cluster role to be bound to the users or groups.
title: GETTING STARTED — clusterrolebinding

instruction: How can you specify multiple users in a single `kubectl create clusterrolebinding` command?
response: You can use the `--user` flag multiple times to specify multiple users.
title: GETTING STARTED — clusterrolebinding

instruction: What is the effect of the `--dry-run` flag in the `kubectl create clusterrolebinding` command?
response: The `--dry-run` flag allows you to test the command without actually creating the cluster role binding.
title: GETTING STARTED — clusterrolebinding

instruction: What is the purpose of the --aggregation-rule flag when creating a ClusterRole?
response: The --aggregation-rule flag is used to specify an aggregation label selector for combining ClusterRoles.
title: kubectl create clusterrole — Options

instruction: What is the default behavior when a field or map key is missing in a template, and how can this be changed?
response: The default behavior is to ignore any errors in templates when a field or map key is missing. This can be changed by setting the --allow-missing-template-keys flag to false.
title: kubectl create clusterrole — Options

instruction: What are the possible values for the --validate flag, and what do they do?
response: The possible values for the --validate flag are strict, warn, and ignore. 'strict' or 'true' will use a schema to validate the input and fail the request if invalid, 'warn' will warn about unknown or duplicate fields without blocking the request, and 'false' or 'ignore' will not perform any schema validation.
title: kubectl create clusterrole — Options

instruction: What types of Kubernetes API objects can be constrained by quotas?
response: API resources that can be constrained by quotas include pods, replicationcontrollers, resourcequotas, secrets, configmaps, persistentvolumeclaims, services of type NodePort, and services of type LoadBalancer.
title: Configure Quotas for API Objects — Notes

instruction: What is the string used to identify a Pod in quota constraints?
response: The string used to identify a Pod in quota constraints is "pods".
title: Configure Quotas for API Objects — Notes

instruction: What is the string used to identify a Service of type LoadBalancer in quota constraints?
response: The string used to identify a Service of type LoadBalancer in quota constraints is "services.loadbalancers".
title: Configure Quotas for API Objects — Notes

instruction: What is the recommended approach for including persistent storage in configuration templates that need to run on a wide range of clusters?
response: Include PersistentVolumeClaim objects in your bundle of config, but do not include PersistentVolume objects, as the user instantiating the config may not have permission to create PersistentVolumes.
title: Persistent Volumes — Writing Portable Configuration

instruction: How should the storage class name be handled when instantiating a template that requires persistent storage?
response: Give the user the option to provide a storage class name, and if provided, put that value into the persistentVolumeClaim.storageClassName field. If not provided, leave the field as nil, which will cause a PV to be automatically provisioned with the default StorageClass in the cluster.
title: Persistent Volumes — Writing Portable Configuration

instruction: What should be done if a PVC is not getting bound after some time?
response: Surface this to the user, as it may indicate that the cluster has no dynamic storage support or the cluster has no storage system. In this case, the user should create a matching PV or cannot deploy config requiring PVCs, respectively.
title: Persistent Volumes — Writing Portable Configuration

instruction: What is the purpose of the externalCloudVolumePlugin field in KubeCloudSharedConfiguration?
response: The externalCloudVolumePlugin field specifies the plugin to use when cloudProvider is "external", and it is currently used by the in-repo cloud providers to handle node and volume control in the KCM.
title: kube-controller-manager Configuration (v1alpha1) — KubeCloudSharedConfiguration

instruction: What is the effect of setting a longer nodeSyncPeriod in KubeCloudSharedConfiguration?
response: Setting a longer nodeSyncPeriod will result in fewer calls to the cloud provider, but may delay the addition of new nodes to the cluster.
title: kube-controller-manager Configuration (v1alpha1) — KubeCloudSharedConfiguration

instruction: What is the purpose of the AllocateNodeCIDRs field in KubeCloudSharedConfiguration?
response: The AllocateNodeCIDRs field enables CIDRs for Pods to be allocated and, if ConfigureCloudRoutes is true, to be set on the cloud provider.
title: kube-controller-manager Configuration (v1alpha1) — KubeCloudSharedConfiguration

instruction: What is the purpose of the CIDRAllocatorType field in KubeCloudSharedConfiguration?
response: The CIDRAllocatorType field determines what kind of pod CIDR allocator will be used.
title: kube-controller-manager Configuration (v1alpha1) — KubeCloudSharedConfiguration

instruction: What is the default value for the 'allowExperimentalUpgrades' field in the UpgradeApplyConfiguration?
response: The default value for the 'allowExperimentalUpgrades' field is false.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta4) — UpgradeApplyConfiguration

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'dryRun' to true in the UpgradeApplyConfiguration?
response: When 'dryRun' is set to true, kubeadm will not apply any changes and will only output what would be done.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta4) — UpgradeApplyConfiguration

instruction: What is the default value for the 'imagePullPolicy' field in the UpgradeApplyConfiguration?
response: If the 'imagePullPolicy' field is unset, kubeadm will default it to 'IfNotPresent', or pull the required images if not present on the host.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta4) — UpgradeApplyConfiguration

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'forceUpgrade' to true in the UpgradeApplyConfiguration?
response: When 'forceUpgrade' is set to true, kubeadm will upgrade the cluster without prompting for confirmation.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta4) — UpgradeApplyConfiguration

instruction: What is the recommended replacement for the Ceph RBD provisioner?
response: The CephFS RBD CSI driver is recommended as a replacement for the Ceph RBD provisioner.
title: Storage Classes — Ceph RBD (deprecated)

instruction: What is the default value for the 'imageFormat' parameter in the Ceph RBD provisioner?
response: The default value for the 'imageFormat' parameter is '2'.
title: Storage Classes — Ceph RBD (deprecated)

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'imageFeatures' parameter in the Ceph RBD provisioner?
response: The 'imageFeatures' parameter is optional and should only be used if 'imageFormat' is set to '2'. Currently supported features are 'layering' only.
title: Storage Classes — Ceph RBD (deprecated)

instruction: What happens to the associated volumes when deleting the Pods in a StatefulSet?
response: The associated volumes are not deleted, allowing you to copy data off the volume before deletion.
title: Delete a StatefulSet — Persistent Volumes

instruction: What might happen if you delete the PVC after the pods have terminated?
response: Deleting the PVC after the pods have terminated might trigger deletion of the backing Persistent Volumes depending on the storage class and reclaim policy.
title: Delete a StatefulSet — Persistent Volumes

instruction: Can you assume you can access a volume after deleting the PVC?
response: No, you should never assume ability to access a volume after claim deletion.
title: Delete a StatefulSet — Persistent Volumes

instruction: What are the possible values for the Ready condition in a node?
response: The Ready condition can have three possible values: True, False, and Unknown. True indicates that the node is healthy and ready to accept pods, False indicates that the node is not healthy and is not accepting pods, and Unknown indicates that the node controller has not heard from the node in the last node-monitor-grace-period (default is 50 seconds).
title: Node Status — Conditions

instruction: How does the Kubernetes control plane automatically create taints when problems occur on nodes?
response: The Kubernetes control plane automatically creates taints that match the conditions affecting the node when the status of the Ready condition remains Unknown or False for longer than the kube-controller-manager's NodeMonitorGracePeriod, which defaults to 50 seconds. This will cause either an node.kubernetes.io/unreachable taint, for an Unknown status, or a node.kubernetes.io/not-ready taint, for a False status, to be added to the Node.
title: Node Status — Conditions

instruction: What happens to existing pods scheduled to a node when a NoExecute taint is applied?
response: Existing pods scheduled to the node may be evicted due to the application of NoExecute taints.
title: Node Status — Conditions

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'default-deny-all' NetworkPolicy?
response: The 'default-deny-all' NetworkPolicy prevents all ingress and egress traffic, ensuring that even pods not selected by any other NetworkPolicy will not be allowed to send or receive traffic.
title: Network Policies — Default deny all ingress and all egress traffic

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'podSelector' to an empty object in the NetworkPolicy?
response: Setting 'podSelector' to an empty object means that the policy applies to all pods in the namespace, as no pods are excluded from the policy.
title: Network Policies — Default deny all ingress and all egress traffic

instruction: What are the 'policyTypes' specified in the 'default-deny-all' NetworkPolicy?
response: The 'policyTypes' specified are 'Ingress' and 'Egress', meaning the policy denies both incoming and outgoing traffic.
title: Network Policies — Default deny all ingress and all egress traffic

instruction: What is the default behavior for allocating CIDRs for Pods in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The default behavior for allocating CIDRs for Pods in a Kubernetes cluster is determined by the --allocate-node-cidrs flag, which requires the --cluster-cidr flag to be set.
