instruction: How can you verify the details of a service account token Secret after creation?
response: Run `kubectl -n examplens describe secret mysecretname`. This will display metadata like the ServiceAccount name, UID, and the contents of the Secret's data fields including the token, certificate, and namespace.
title: Managing Service Accounts — Create additional API tokens

instruction: What is the purpose of the PUT /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/serviceaccounts/{name} endpoint in Kubernetes?
response: This endpoint is used to replace an existing ServiceAccount in a specified namespace. It allows full replacement of the ServiceAccount resource, requiring the name, namespace, and a valid ServiceAccount body in the request.
title: ServiceAccount — putReplace

instruction: What does the 'dryRun' parameter do when included in this PUT request?
response: The 'dryRun' parameter, when present, instructs the server to process the request without persisting any modifications. Valid values include 'All' to simulate all dry run stages. If an invalid value is provided, the request fails with an error and no further processing occurs.
title: ServiceAccount — putReplace

instruction: How is the kubernetes.io/hostname label populated on a node?
response: The Kubelet automatically populates the kubernetes.io/hostname label with the hostname of the node. This value can be overridden by passing the --hostname-override flag to the kubelet when starting the node.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — kubernetes.io/hostname

instruction: What is the purpose of the --hostname-override flag in Kubernetes?
response: The --hostname-override flag allows administrators to set a custom hostname value for the kubernetes.io/hostname label on a node, overriding the node's actual hostname.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — kubernetes.io/hostname

instruction: How does the kubernetes.io/hostname label relate to Kubernetes topology?
response: The kubernetes.io/hostname label is used as part of the topology hierarchy in Kubernetes. It serves as a foundational identifier for node-level topology, which connects to higher-level topology labels like topology.kubernetes.io/zone.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — kubernetes.io/hostname

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'dryRun' parameter when creating a ClusterRole via the Kubernetes API?
response: The 'dryRun' parameter indicates that modifications should not be persisted. Valid values include 'All', which processes all dry run stages. An invalid directive results in an error and no further processing.
title: ClusterRole — postCreate

instruction: How does the 'fieldValidation' parameter affect requests to create a ClusterRole?
response: The 'fieldValidation' parameter controls how the server handles unknown or duplicate fields. Valid options are 'Ignore' (drops fields silently), 'Warn' (sends warnings for dropped fields), and 'Strict' (fails the request if unknown or duplicate fields exist).
title: ClusterRole — postCreate

instruction: How can you determine which NUMA nodes a pod is pinned to using the memory manager state file?
response: Look for the 'numaAffinity' field in the 'entries' section of the memory manager state file. For example, the pod with ID 'fa9bdd38-6df9-4cf9-aa67-8c4814da37a8' is pinned to NUMA nodes 0 and 1 as indicated by 'numaAffinity': [0, 1].
title: Troubleshooting Topology Management — Examine the memory manager state on a node

instruction: What is the total amount of free 'conventional' memory available in the NUMA group containing nodes 0 and 1?
response: Sum the 'free' values from the 'memory' section of each NUMA node in the group. Node 0 has 'free': 0 and node 1 has 'free': 103739236352. The total is 0 + 103739236352 = 103739236352 bytes.
title: Troubleshooting Topology Management — Examine the memory manager state on a node

instruction: How is system-reserved memory configured for NUMA node 0 in this example?
response: The 'systemReserved' value for node 0 is 3221225472 bytes (3Gi), configured by the administrator using the --reserved-memory flag. This is specified in the 'memory' section of node 0's state under 'systemReserved': 3221225472.
title: Troubleshooting Topology Management — Examine the memory manager state on a node

instruction: How do I print the supported API versions in Kubernetes?
response: Use the command `kubectl api-versions` to print the supported API versions in the format 'group/version'.
title: GETTING STARTED — api-versions

instruction: What command lists supported API resources with additional details?
response: Run `kubectl api-resources -o wide` to display supported API resources with more detailed information.
title: GETTING STARTED — api-versions

instruction: How can I filter API resources by namespaced status?
response: Use `kubectl api-resources --namespaced=true` for namespaced resources or `--namespaced=false` for non-namespaced resources.
title: GETTING STARTED — api-versions

instruction: How do I list API resources for a specific API group?
response: Specify the `--api-group` flag, e.g., `kubectl api-resources --api-group=rbac.authorization.k8s.io` to filter by the given API group.
title: GETTING STARTED — api-versions

instruction: When should the 'arbitrary-node:*' permission be used in Kubernetes dynamic resource allocation?
response: The 'arbitrary-node:*' permission should only be used for components that must update resource claims from any node in the cluster, such as multi-node controllers that require cross-node status updates.
title: Harden Dynamic Resource Allocation in Your Cluster — Grant multi-node controller permissions only when needed

instruction: What resources and verbs are defined in the 'dra-multinode-status-updater' ClusterRole for resource claims?
response: The ClusterRole grants 'get', 'patch', and 'update' permissions on 'resourceclaims/status' within the 'resource.k8s.io' API group, enabling status management of resource claims.
title: Harden Dynamic Resource Allocation in Your Cluster — Grant multi-node controller permissions only when needed

instruction: How is the driver-specific resource configured in the ClusterRole for dynamic resource allocation?
response: The ClusterRole specifies 'arbitrary-node:patch' and 'arbitrary-node:update' verbs for the 'resourceclaims/driver' resource, restricted to the 'dra.example.com' resourceName in the 'resource.k8s.io' API group.
title: Harden Dynamic Resource Allocation in Your Cluster — Grant multi-node controller permissions only when needed

instruction: How can you configure the kubelet to fetch credentials for private container images in Kubernetes?
response: You can configure the kubelet to invoke a plugin binary to dynamically fetch registry credentials for a container image. This requires kubelet-level configuration to enable, as described in the 'Configure a kubelet image credential provider' documentation.
title: Images — Kubelet credential provider for authenticated image pulls

instruction: Why can't static Pods use ServiceAccount or Secret to provide private registry credentials?
response: Static Pods cannot use ServiceAccount or Secret to provide private registry credentials because their specification cannot reference other API resources. This limitation makes the kubelet credential provider the most suitable solution for such scenarios.
title: Images — Kubelet credential provider for authenticated image pulls

instruction: What is the primary advantage of using the kubelet credential provider over other methods for private image pulls?
response: The kubelet credential provider is the most robust and versatile method for fetching credentials for private registries. It allows dynamic credential retrieval without requiring the Pod specification to reference other API resources, which is particularly useful for static Pods.
title: Images — Kubelet credential provider for authenticated image pulls

instruction: What is the purpose of the ProxyTerminatingEndpoints feature gate in kube-proxy when using the Local traffic policy?
response: When enabled, kube-proxy uses an algorithm to prioritize non-terminating endpoints. If all local endpoints are terminating, it forwards traffic to them to allow graceful draining of connections for NodePort and LoadBalancer Services during rolling updates.
title: Virtual IPs and Service Proxies — Traffic to terminating endpoints

instruction: How does kube-proxy handle traffic routing when all local endpoints for a Service are marked as terminating?
response: If the ProxyTerminatingEndpoints feature is enabled and all local endpoints are terminating, kube-proxy will forward traffic to those terminating endpoints to ensure they can drain connections before termination.
title: Virtual IPs and Service Proxies — Traffic to terminating endpoints

instruction: Why is routing traffic to terminating endpoints important for LoadBalancer Services with externalTrafficPolicy: Local?
response: This ensures nodes scaling down Pods can continue receiving traffic until health checks fail, allowing external load balancers to remove the node from the backend pool after termination completes.
title: Virtual IPs and Service Proxies — Traffic to terminating endpoints

instruction: What problem does the ProxyTerminatingEndpoints feature solve during a rolling update of a deployment?
response: It prevents traffic loss by allowing nodes with 0 replicas to temporarily accept traffic to terminating Pods while health checks detect the node's unavailability and remove it from the load balancer backend.
title: Virtual IPs and Service Proxies — Traffic to terminating endpoints

instruction: How does the 'continue' parameter work when deleting Kubernetes leases in a namespace?
response: The 'continue' parameter allows clients to retrieve more results from a server by using a token from a previous query. If the token expires (typically 5-15 minutes) or the server configuration changes, the server returns a 410 ResourceExpired error with a new token. Clients must restart the list without 'continue' for consistency or use the new token for potentially inconsistent results. This parameter is unsupported when 'watch' is true.
title: Lease — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: How does the 'continue' parameter work when deleting a collection of IPAddresses in Kubernetes?
response: The 'continue' parameter allows clients to retrieve more results from a server by using a token from a previous query. If the token expires or becomes invalid (e.g., due to server configuration changes), the server returns a 410 ResourceExpired error with a new token. Clients must restart the list without 'continue' for consistency or use the new token for potentially inconsistent results. This parameter is not supported when 'watch' is true.
title: IPAddress — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'dryRun' parameter in the DELETE /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ipaddresses endpoint?
response: The 'dryRun' parameter indicates that modifications should not be persisted. Valid values include 'All' to process all dry run stages. If an invalid value is provided, the request fails with an error. This is useful for testing deletions without affecting the cluster state.
title: IPAddress — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What are the risks of using 'ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential' when deleting IPAddresses?
response: Setting 'ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential' to true triggers an unsafe deletion, bypassing finalizers and precondition checks. This can corrupt the cluster if the resource's workload depends on normal deletion flow. It should only be used when explicitly needed to delete corrupt resources, as it may break cluster stability.
title: IPAddress — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: If a container specifies a memory limit but no request, what value is assigned to the memory request?
response: The container's memory request is automatically set to match its specified memory limit. In the example provided, the request is set to 1Gi, the same as the limit, instead of using a default request value like 256Mi.
title: Configure Default Memory Requests and Limits for a Namespace — What if you specify a container's limit, but not its request?

instruction: Why wasn't the default memory request value of 256Mi applied in the example?
response: Because the container explicitly defined a memory limit. When a limit is specified and no request is provided, Kubernetes sets the request equal to the limit rather than using any namespace-level default request value.
title: Configure Default Memory Requests and Limits for a Namespace — What if you specify a container's limit, but not its request?

instruction: How can you pass feature gates to kubeadm init?
response: You can pass feature gates to kubeadm init either by using the --feature-gates flag directly during the command execution or by specifying them in the featureGates field of a configuration file passed via the --config option.
title: kubeadm init — Using kubeadm init with feature gates

instruction: What is the default value and GA version for the NodeLocalCRISocket feature gate?
response: The NodeLocalCRISocket feature gate has a default value of true, was introduced in alpha version 1.32, beta in 1.34, and graduated to GA in 1.36.
title: kubeadm init — Using kubeadm init with feature gates

instruction: What is the purpose of the subPath property in Kubernetes volumeMounts?
response: The subPath property allows mounting a specific subdirectory within a referenced volume instead of the volume's root, enabling multiple components within a Pod to share the same volume for different purposes.
title: Volumes — Using subPath

instruction: How is the MySQL container's volume configured in the example LAMP stack Pod?
response: The MySQL container mounts the /var/lib/mysql path using the subPath 'mysql' from the 'site-data' volume, isolating its data within a subdirectory of the shared volume.
title: Volumes — Using subPath

instruction: What are the two kubectl commands to update a Deployment's container image from nginx:1.14.2 to nginx:1.16.1?
response: The two commands are: `kubectl set image deployment.v1.apps/nginx-deployment nginx=nginx:1.16.1` or `kubectl set image deployment/nginx-deployment nginx=nginx:1.16.1`. Both specify the Deployment name (`nginx-deployment`), container name (`nginx`), and new image (`nginx:1.16.1`).
title: Deployments — Updating a Deployment

instruction: How can you check the status of a Deployment rollout?
response: Use `kubectl rollout status deployment/nginx-deployment`. The output indicates progress (e.g., '2 out of 3 new replicas have been updated...') or success ('deployment "nginx-deployment" successfully rolled out').
title: Deployments — Updating a Deployment

instruction: What are the default rolling update strategy parameters for a Deployment in terms of max unavailable and max surge?
response: By default, a Deployment ensures at least 75% of desired Pods are available (25% max unavailable) and allows up to 125% of desired Pods (25% max surge). For example, with 3 replicas, it maintains 3 available and allows up to 4 Pods during updates.
title: Deployments — Updating a Deployment

instruction: Why does a Pod in the 'test' namespace fail to resolve a Service named 'data' in the 'prod' namespace when querying just 'data'?
response: A Pod in the 'test' namespace queries for 'data' without specifying a namespace, so Kubernetes limits the search to the Pod's own namespace. Since the 'data' Service exists in the 'prod' namespace, the query returns no results. To resolve this, the query must explicitly include the namespace, such as 'data.prod'.
title: DNS for Services and Pods — Namespaces of Services

instruction: How does Kubernetes expand DNS queries using the /etc/resolv.conf file in a Pod?
response: Kubernetes configures each Pod's /etc/resolv.conf with search domains that append to DNS queries. For example, a query for 'data' may be expanded to 'data.test.svc.cluster.local' by appending the Pod's namespace and default Kubernetes domains. The 'search' option in resolv.conf defines these domains, enabling shorter queries to resolve fully qualified domain names.
title: DNS for Services and Pods — Namespaces of Services

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'ndots:5' option in the /etc/resolv.conf file?
response: The 'ndots:5' option determines how many dots in a DNS query are required before it is treated as an absolute domain name. Queries with fewer dots than this threshold are expanded using the search domains listed in resolv.conf. This allows shorter queries to resolve correctly without fully qualifying the domain name.
title: DNS for Services and Pods — Namespaces of Services

instruction: What is the purpose of the `apiVersion` and `kind` fields in the KubeControllerManagerConfiguration?
response: The `apiVersion` specifies the API version (`kubecontrollermanager.config.k8s.io/v1alpha1`) and `kind` defines the type of the configuration object (`KubeControllerManagerConfiguration`), which together identify the configuration structure for the kube-controller-manager.
title: kube-controller-manager Configuration (v1alpha1) — KubeControllerManagerConfiguration

instruction: What does the `GenericControllerManagerConfiguration` field configure?
response: The `GenericControllerManagerConfiguration` holds configuration parameters for a generic controller-manager, providing shared settings applicable to multiple controllers managed by the kube-controller-manager.
title: kube-controller-manager Configuration (v1alpha1) — KubeControllerManagerConfiguration

instruction: What is the role of the `KubeCloudSharedConfiguration` field?
response: The `KubeCloudSharedConfiguration` contains settings for features shared between the cloud controller manager and the kube-controller manager, enabling coordinated behavior for cloud-integrated operations.
title: kube-controller-manager Configuration (v1alpha1) — KubeControllerManagerConfiguration

instruction: What does the `AttachDetachControllerConfiguration` manage?
response: The `AttachDetachControllerConfiguration` holds configuration for the AttachDetachController, which is responsible for managing the attachment and detachment of volumes to/from nodes in the cluster.
title: kube-controller-manager Configuration (v1alpha1) — KubeControllerManagerConfiguration

instruction: What happens when a client uses an expired 'continue' token in a GET request for EndpointSlices?
response: If the specified 'continue' value is no longer valid due to expiration or server configuration changes, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error and a continue token. The client must either restart the list without the 'continue' field for a consistent list or use the new token to retrieve the next set of results, which may include inconsistent data due to potential changes after the first request.
title: EndpointSlice — getList

instruction: How does the 'limit' parameter affect the results of an EndpointSlice list request?
response: The 'limit' parameter specifies the maximum number of responses to return. If more items exist, the server sets the 'continue' field for pagination. However, the server may return fewer items if all are filtered out. Clients should use the presence of the 'continue' field to determine if more results are available. This parameter is not supported during watch operations.
title: EndpointSlice — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of setting 'sendInitialEvents=true' with 'watch=true' in an EndpointSlice request?
response: When 'sendInitialEvents=true' is set with 'watch=true', the watch stream starts with synthetic events to provide the current state of objects, followed by a bookmark event indicating the ResourceVersion. This ensures clients receive a consistent snapshot of the current state before subsequent change events. The 'resourceVersionMatch' must also be set to define how resource versions are applied.
title: EndpointSlice — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/persistentvolumeclaims/{name}/status endpoint in Kubernetes?
response: This endpoint retrieves the status of a specific PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) in a given namespace, providing information about the PVC's current state and conditions.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — getRead Status

instruction: Which parameters are required to use the GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/persistentvolumeclaims/{name}/status endpoint?
response: The required parameters are `name` (the name of the PVC) and `namespace` (the namespace where the PVC resides). The optional `pretty` parameter controls whether the output is formatted for readability.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — getRead Status

instruction: What does a 200 OK response from this endpoint indicate?
response: A 200 OK response indicates that the request was successful and the current status of the specified PersistentVolumeClaim was returned in the response body.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — getRead Status

instruction: How should a client handle a 410 ResourceExpired error when using the 'continue' parameter in a watch request?
response: When a 410 ResourceExpired error occurs, the client must restart their list without the 'continue' field to ensure a consistent list. Alternatively, the client may send another list request with the token received in the error, but the server will return results from the latest snapshot, which may include objects created, modified, or deleted after the initial request. This approach is inconsistent with previous results and should be used only if consistency is not required.
title: ServiceAccount — getWatch

instruction: How are Pods in a StatefulSet uniquely identified, and what naming convention do they follow?
response: Pods in a StatefulSet are uniquely identified by an ordinal index assigned by the StatefulSet controller. Their names follow the format <statefulset name>-<ordinal index>, such as web-0 and web-1 for a StatefulSet named 'web' with two replicas.
title: StatefulSet Basics — Pods in a StatefulSet

instruction: What command can be used to list Pods managed by a StatefulSet, and what does the output indicate about their structure?
response: The command `kubectl get pods -l app=nginx` lists Pods with the label `app=nginx`. The output shows Pods like web-0 and web-1, demonstrating their stable, ordinal-based naming and the StatefulSet's replica count (e.g., two replicas in the example).
title: StatefulSet Basics — Pods in a StatefulSet

instruction: Why does the StatefulSet controller assign ordinal indices to Pods, and how does this affect their network identity?
response: The StatefulSet controller assigns ordinal indices to ensure each Pod has a stable, unique identity. This guarantees predictable network identities (e.g., DNS names like web-0.nginx) and maintains consistent Pod ordering and lifecycle management.
title: StatefulSet Basics — Pods in a StatefulSet

instruction: What is the syntax to restart a specific deployment using kubectl rollout restart?
response: The syntax to restart a specific deployment is `kubectl rollout restart deployment/NAME`, where NAME is the name of the deployment. For example, `kubectl rollout restart deployment/nginx` restarts the deployment named nginx.
title: kubectl rollout restart — Synopsis

instruction: How can you restart all deployments in a specific namespace?
response: To restart all deployments in a specific namespace, use the `-n` flag followed by the namespace name. For example, `kubectl rollout restart deployment -n test-namespace` restarts all deployments in the test-namespace namespace.
title: kubectl rollout restart — Synopsis

instruction: Which resource types are supported by kubectl rollout restart according to the examples?
response: The supported resource types include deployments and daemon sets. Examples provided are `kubectl rollout restart deployment/NAME` and `kubectl rollout restart daemonset/NAME`.
title: kubectl rollout restart — Synopsis

instruction: How can you use a label selector to restart deployments with a specific label?
response: Use the `--selector` flag with the label key-value pair. For example, `kubectl rollout restart deployment --selector=app=nginx` restarts all deployments labeled with app=nginx.
title: kubectl rollout restart — Synopsis

instruction: What should a client do if it receives a 410 ResourceExpired error when using the 'continue' parameter in a ResourceClaimTemplate list request?
response: If a client receives a 410 ResourceExpired error, it must restart the list request without the 'continue' field to obtain a consistent snapshot. Alternatively, the client may use the provided continue token from the error response to retrieve the next set of results, but this will return data from the latest server snapshot, which may include objects modified after the initial request.
title: ResourceClaimTemplate — getList

instruction: How does the 'limit' parameter interact with the 'continue' parameter in ResourceClaimTemplate list requests?
response: The 'limit' parameter specifies the maximum number of items to return in a single list response. If the server returns fewer items than the requested limit, the 'continue' field in the response will contain a token to retrieve the next set of results. Clients must use this token in subsequent requests to paginate through the full list.
title: ResourceClaimTemplate — getList

instruction: What Kubernetes version is required to use event-based container status updates?
response: Kubernetes v1.27 includes beta support for event-based container status updates, but your Kubernetes server must be at or later than version 1.26 to use this feature.
title: Switching from Polling to CRI Event-based Updates to Container Status — Before you begin

instruction: How do you verify that your Kubernetes server meets the version requirement for this feature?
response: To check the version, enter `kubectl version`. Your Kubernetes server must be at or later than version 1.26.
title: Switching from Polling to CRI Event-based Updates to Container Status — Before you begin

instruction: What happens if the container runtime does not support container lifecycle events?
response: The kubelet automatically switches back to the legacy generic PLEG mechanism if the container runtime does not announce support for container lifecycle events, even if the feature gate is enabled.
title: Switching from Polling to CRI Event-based Updates to Container Status — Before you begin

instruction: How do you enable a specific feature gate for the API server using kubeadm?
response: To enable a feature gate for the API server, create a ClusterConfiguration file with the `apiServer.extraArgs.feature-gates` field set to `"FeatureName=true"`. This configuration is then passed to `kubeadm init` using the `--config` flag.
title: Enable Or Disable Feature Gates — During cluster initialization

instruction: What is the correct syntax for configuring feature gates in the Kubelet configuration file?
response: In the KubeletConfiguration file, feature gates are configured using the `featureGates` field as a map, e.g., `featureGates: FeatureName: true`. This differs from other components that use the `feature-gates` string format.
title: Enable Or Disable Feature Gates — During cluster initialization

instruction: Which Kubernetes components require explicit feature gate configuration when using kubeadm?
response: When using kubeadm, feature gates must be explicitly configured for the API server, controller manager, scheduler, and kubelet. Each component has its own section in the configuration file where `feature-gates` or `featureGates` parameters are set.
title: Enable Or Disable Feature Gates — During cluster initialization

instruction: What command initializes a Kubernetes cluster using a custom configuration file with feature gates enabled?
response: The command `kubeadm init --config kubeadm-config.yaml` initializes the cluster using the specified configuration file, which includes feature gate settings for multiple components.
title: Enable Or Disable Feature Gates — During cluster initialization

instruction: What Kubernetes version is required for HostProcess containers to be enabled by default?
response: Kubernetes v1.36 is required for the HostProcess container feature to be enabled by default.
title: Create a Windows HostProcess Pod — Before you begin

instruction: How does the kubelet communicate with containerd to run HostProcess containers?
response: The kubelet communicates with containerd directly by passing the hostprocess flag via CRI (Container Runtime Interface).
title: Create a Windows HostProcess Pod — Before you begin

instruction: What containerd version is required to run HostProcess containers in Kubernetes 1.36?
response: The latest version of containerd (v1.6+) is required to run HostProcess containers in Kubernetes 1.36.
title: Create a Windows HostProcess Pod — Before you begin

instruction: Where can users find instructions to install containerd for HostProcess container support?
response: Users can follow the provided 'How to install containerd' link in the documentation to install the necessary containerd version.
title: Create a Windows HostProcess Pod — Before you begin

instruction: What is the common pattern for managing ZooKeeper processes in distributed systems?
response: The common pattern is to use a watchdog (supervisory process) to restart failed processes. This ensures reliability in distributed systems by automatically recovering from process failures.
title: Running ZooKeeper, A Distributed System Coordinator — Managing the ZooKeeper process

instruction: How should you manage ZooKeeper processes when deploying in Kubernetes?
response: Instead of using an external supervisory utility, you should leverage Kubernetes itself as the watchdog. Kubernetes will automatically restart failed ZooKeeper server processes (JVM) as part of its orchestration capabilities.
title: Running ZooKeeper, A Distributed System Coordinator — Managing the ZooKeeper process

instruction: What is the difference between the 'FailJob' and 'FailIndex' actions in PodFailurePolicyRule?
response: The 'FailJob' action marks the entire job as Failed and terminates all running pods. The 'FailIndex' action marks a specific index of the job as Failed, preventing restarts within that index, but requires the 'backoffLimitPerIndex' to be set. 'FailJob' affects the entire job, while 'FailIndex' targets individual indices.
title: Job — PodFailurePolicyRule

instruction: How does the 'Ignore' action affect the backoffLimit counter in a Job?
response: The 'Ignore' action prevents the counter toward the .backoffLimit from incrementing when a pod fails. This means the job's .status.failed field is not updated, and a replacement pod is created without affecting the backoffLimit.
title: Job — PodFailurePolicyRule

instruction: Why can't both 'onExitCodes' and 'onPodConditions' be used in the same PodFailurePolicyRule?
response: The documentation explicitly states that only one of 'onExitCodes' or 'onPodConditions' can be used in each rule, but not both. This ensures that failure handling is based on either container exit codes or pod conditions, not a combination of both.
title: Job — PodFailurePolicyRule

instruction: What is the maximum number of pod condition patterns allowed in 'onPodConditions'?
response: The 'onPodConditions' field allows a maximum of 20 elements in the list of pod condition patterns. This limit ensures the policy remains manageable and avoids excessive complexity in matching conditions.
title: Job — PodFailurePolicyRule

instruction: What is the purpose of the `experimental.windows.kubernetes.io/isolation-type` annotation and how is it used?
response: This annotation is used to specify Hyper-V isolation for Windows containers in Kubernetes. It is applied to Pod resources and requires the value `hyperv` to enable Hyper-V isolation. Note that this annotation is deprecated, and users should check for updated alternatives.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — experimental.windows.kubernetes.io/isolation-type (deprecated)

instruction: What is the role of the `gateway.networking.k8s.io/generator` annotation in Gateway API resources?
response: This annotation is added by tools that automatically generate Gateway API resources (e.g., `ingress2gateway`). It identifies the tool that created the resource but has no functional impact on Gateway API behavior. It is informational only and can be applied to Gateway, HTTPRoute, and other Gateway API resources.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — experimental.windows.kubernetes.io/isolation-type (deprecated)

instruction: How does Kubernetes determine the amount of swap available to a Pod under the LimitedSwap behavior?
response: Under LimitedSwap, the amount of swap available to a Pod is determined based on the proportion of the memory requested by the Pod relative to the node's total memory. This ensures swap allocation is tied to the Pod's memory request, reserving some swap space for system processes.
title: Swap memory management — Unutilized swap space

instruction: Why might Guaranteed QoS class Pods leave swap memory unused in Kubernetes 1.36 and later?
response: In Kubernetes 1.36 and later, Guaranteed QoS class Pods are explicitly prohibited from using swap memory. This means any swap space proportional to their memory requests remains unused by Kubernetes workloads, even if system processes could utilize it.
title: Swap memory management — Unutilized swap space

instruction: What risk arises when many Pods are ineligible for swapping under LimitedSwap?
response: When many Pods are ineligible for swapping (e.g., Guaranteed QoS class Pods), a significant portion of the node's swap space may remain unutilized by Kubernetes workloads. This could lead to inefficient swap usage, though it ensures some swap is reserved for critical system processes.
title: Swap memory management — Unutilized swap space

instruction: What is the purpose of reserving swap memory for non-Kubernetes processes under LimitedSwap?
response: Reserving swap memory for non-Kubernetes processes ensures that system daemons, the kubelet, and other critical node-level operations have access to swap space, even if Kubernetes workloads are restricted or unable to use it.
title: Swap memory management — Unutilized swap space

instruction: What causes CoreDNS pods to enter CrashLoopBackOff due to loop detection, and how can this be addressed?
response: CoreDNS pods may enter CrashLoopBackOff if they detect a loop during operation. Workarounds are available to prevent Kubernetes from restarting the pods repeatedly, though specific implementation details are not provided in the text.
title: Troubleshooting kubeadm — corednspods haveCrashLoopBackOfforErrorstate

instruction: What is an example of a Kubernetes feature gate that primarily affects the kube-apiserver?
response: StructuredAuthenticationConfiguration is an example of a feature gate that primarily affects the kube-apiserver.
title: Enable Or Disable Feature Gates — Understanding component-specific requirements

instruction: Which component is primarily affected by the GracefulNodeShutdown feature gate?
response: The GracefulNodeShutdown feature gate primarily affects the kubelet component.
title: Enable Or Disable Feature Gates — Understanding component-specific requirements

instruction: What should you do before enabling feature gates in production environments?
response: Always test feature gates in non-production environments first to ensure stability and compatibility.
title: Enable Or Disable Feature Gates — Understanding component-specific requirements

instruction: What is the risk associated with using alpha-level feature gates in Kubernetes?
response: Alpha features may be removed without notice, so they should be used with caution and only in non-production environments.
title: Enable Or Disable Feature Gates — Understanding component-specific requirements

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'expressionWarnings' field in the TypeChecking structure of a ValidatingAdmissionPolicy?
response: The 'expressionWarnings' field contains an array of type-checking warnings for each CEL expression in the ValidatingAdmissionPolicy. These warnings indicate potential issues with the expressions during validation but do not prevent the policy from being applied.
title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicy — TypeChecking

instruction: Which variables are accessible in CEL expressions used for ValidatingAdmissionPolicy validation rules?
response: CEL expressions in ValidatingAdmissionPolicy have access to the following variables: 'object' (incoming request object), 'oldObject' (existing object), 'request' (API request attributes), 'params' (policy parameters), 'namespaceObject' (namespace object), 'variables' (composited variables), and 'authorizer' (for authorization checks). Additionally, 'authorizer.requestResource' is available for resource-specific checks.
title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicy — TypeChecking

instruction: Under what conditions is the 'message' field required in a ValidatingAdmissionPolicy validation rule?
response: The 'message' field is required if the validation expression contains line breaks. If unset, the default message is 'failed rule: {Rule}' or 'failed Expression: {Expression}' if no rule is specified. The message must not contain line breaks itself.
title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicy — TypeChecking

instruction: What authentication/authorization requirements must be addressed when deploying the Cloud Controller Manager on a cloud provider?
response: The cloud provider may require a token or IAM rules to authenticate and authorize access to their APIs. Additionally, the cloud-controller-manager must be configured with appropriate Kubernetes RBAC rules to communicate with the Kubernetes API server.
title: Cloud Controller Manager Administration — Requirements

instruction: How does the Cloud Controller Manager achieve high availability, and how does this compare to the kube-controller-manager?
response: The Cloud Controller Manager can be set up for high availability using leader election, which is enabled by default. This approach is similar to the high availability setup required for the kube-controller-manager.
title: Cloud Controller Manager Administration — Requirements

instruction: What Kubernetes-specific authorization mechanism is required for the Cloud Controller Manager to function properly?
response: The Cloud Controller Manager requires Kubernetes RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) rules to be configured, granting it the necessary permissions to interact with the Kubernetes API server.
title: Cloud Controller Manager Administration — Requirements

instruction: What IP address management (IPAM) options are supported on Windows in Kubernetes?
response: The supported IPAM options on Windows are host-local, azure-vnet-ipam (specifically for azure-cni), and Windows Server IPAM as a fallback option when no other IPAM is configured.
title: Networking on Windows — IP address management (IPAM)

instruction: Why can't the kubelet --node-labels flag be used to apply restricted labels like node-role.kubernetes.io/* during node registration?
response: The NodeRestriction admission controller, enabled by default in kubeadm, blocks self-application of restricted labels by kubelets. Attempting to use --node-labels for these labels will cause the node to fail registration with the API server.
title: Creating a cluster with kubeadm — Managed node labels

instruction: How should restricted labels (e.g., node-role.kubernetes.io/*) be applied to a node after it has joined the cluster?
response: Use the kubectl label command after the node has joined the cluster. Ensure you are using a privileged kubeconfig, such as /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf, to apply these labels manually.
title: Creating a cluster with kubeadm — Managed node labels

instruction: Why is it critical for each ZooKeeper server in a Kubernetes ensemble to have a unique identifier in the myid file?
response: Unique identifiers are required for consensus protocols like Zab to ensure processes can agree on committed data. If two Pods share the same ordinal, their ZooKeeper servers would conflict by claiming the same identity, breaking consensus and causing data inconsistency.
title: Running ZooKeeper, A Distributed System Coordinator — Achieving consensus

instruction: How does Kubernetes ensure ZooKeeper servers resolve to the correct endpoints in the ensemble?
response: Kubernetes creates A records for each Pod when it becomes Ready. The FQDNs (e.g., zk-0.zk-hs.default.svc.cluster.local) resolve to the specific Pod's endpoint, ensuring each server's identity in the ensemble is uniquely and correctly mapped to its myid configuration.
title: Running ZooKeeper, A Distributed System Coordinator — Achieving consensus

instruction: What role do Pod status transitions play in the ZooKeeper ensemble's ability to achieve consensus?
response: Pod status transitions (e.g., Pending → Running → Ready) indicate when a ZooKeeper server is fully operational. Only Ready Pods contribute to consensus via the Zab protocol, as their A records are active and their server entries in zoo.cfg are properly resolved.
title: Running ZooKeeper, A Distributed System Coordinator — Achieving consensus

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'allowWatchBookmarks' parameter in the StatefulSet list API, and how should clients handle its behavior?
response: The 'allowWatchBookmarks' parameter requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag, and clients should not assume bookmarks are returned at any specific interval or that the server will send BOOKMARK events during a session. If not a watch, this field is ignored.
title: StatefulSet — getList All Namespaces

instruction: How does the 'continue' parameter work for paginating through StatefulSet results, and what happens if it becomes invalid?
response: The 'continue' parameter retrieves more results from a previous query. If the value is expired (typically 5-15 minutes) or the server configuration changes, the server responds with a 410 ResourceExpired error and a new continue token. Clients must restart the list without 'continue' for consistency or use the new token for potentially inconsistent results.
title: StatefulSet — getList All Namespaces

instruction: What is the effect of setting the 'limit' parameter when listing StatefulSets, and how does the server handle remaining results?
response: The 'limit' parameter specifies the maximum number of responses to return. If more items exist, the server sets the 'continue' field for subsequent requests. If no 'continue' is returned, clients may assume no more results. Limit is unsupported with watch and ensures a consistent snapshot of the list state at the time of the first request.
title: StatefulSet — getList All Namespaces

instruction: What are the common causes of the TopologyAffinityError in Kubernetes?
response: The TopologyAffinityError typically occurs when a node lacks sufficient resources to satisfy a pod's request or when the pod's request is rejected due to Topology Manager policy constraints.
title: Troubleshooting Topology Management — TroubleshootTopologyAffinityError

instruction: How can you identify a pod that is experiencing a TopologyAffinityError?
response: Use the command `kubectl get pods` to check the pod's status. A pod with the status `TopologyAffinityError` indicates this issue, as shown in the example output with the pod named 'guaranteed'.
title: Troubleshooting Topology Management — TroubleshootTopologyAffinityError

instruction: What commands can be used to retrieve detailed information about a TopologyAffinityError?
response: Run `kubectl describe pod <id>` to inspect the pod's details or use `kubectl events` to view event logs, which will include a warning message like 'Resources cannot be allocated with Topology locality'.
title: Troubleshooting Topology Management — TroubleshootTopologyAffinityError

instruction: How can you view detailed execution information about Init Containers in a Kubernetes Pod?
response: Use the command 'kubectl describe pod <pod-name>'. This displays detailed status, including Container IDs, states, exit codes, and restart counts for each Init Container.
title: Debug Init Containers — Getting details about Init Containers

instruction: What does the 'CrashLoopBackOff' status in an Init Container indicate, and how can you identify it?
response: The 'CrashLoopBackOff' status indicates the Init Container is repeatedly crashing. It appears under the 'State: Waiting' section with 'Reason: CrashLoopBackOff', and the 'Last State' shows 'Terminated' with 'Reason: Error' and a non-zero exit code (e.g., Exit Code: 1).
title: Debug Init Containers — Getting details about Init Containers

instruction: How can you programmatically retrieve Init Container statuses using a Go template?
response: Use the command 'kubectl get pod <pod-name> --template '{{.status.initContainerStatuses}}''. This outputs the same detailed status information as 'kubectl describe pod', formatted via a Go template for automation or scripting.
title: Debug Init Containers — Getting details about Init Containers

instruction: What is the purpose of setting 'defaultAction': 'SCMP_ACT_ERRNO' in the seccomp profile compared to 'SCMP_ACT_LOG'?
response: Setting 'SCMP_ACT_ERRNO' causes the kernel to return an error for any syscalls not explicitly allowed, preventing them from executing. This contrasts with 'SCMP_ACT_LOG', which only logs unauthorized syscalls but allows them to proceed. The ERRNO action enforces stricter security by blocking unintended syscalls.
title: Restrict a Container's Syscalls with seccomp — Create a Pod with a seccomp profile that only allows necessary syscalls

instruction: How does the 'fine-grained.json' seccomp profile ensure no syslog entries are generated when the container runs?
response: The profile explicitly allows all necessary syscalls via 'action': 'SCMP_ACT_ALLOW' and sets 'defaultAction': 'SCMP_ACT_ERRNO' for unauthorized syscalls. This configuration blocks unauthorized syscalls from executing and avoids logging them, resulting in no syslog output if the container only uses allowed syscalls.
title: Restrict a Container's Syscalls with seccomp — Create a Pod with a seccomp profile that only allows necessary syscalls

instruction: What steps verify that the seccomp profile is working as intended in the example?
response: After creating the Pod, you monitor syslog for 'http-echo' entries using 'tail -f /var/log/syslog | grep http-echo'. Then, you expose the Pod via a NodePort Service, access it with 'curl', and confirm no syslog output appears. This absence indicates the allowed syscalls suffice and unauthorized ones are blocked.
title: Restrict a Container's Syscalls with seccomp — Create a Pod with a seccomp profile that only allows necessary syscalls

instruction: What securityContext configuration is applied to the container in the Pod manifest?
response: The container's securityContext sets 'allowPrivilegeEscalation': 'false' to prevent privilege escalation. Additionally, the Pod's securityContext references a seccomp profile via 'localhostProfile': 'profiles/fine-grained.json' to restrict syscalls.
title: Restrict a Container's Syscalls with seccomp — Create a Pod with a seccomp profile that only allows necessary syscalls

instruction: How can I verify that Cilium Pods are running in my Kubernetes cluster?
response: Run the command `kubectl get pods --namespace=kube-system -l k8s-app=cilium` to list all Cilium Pods in the kube-system namespace. This will show Pods like `cilium-kkdhz` with their status, readiness, and other details.
title: Use Cilium for NetworkPolicy — Deploying Cilium for Production Use

instruction: What is the role of a Cilium Pod in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: A Cilium Pod runs on each node in the cluster and enforces network policy on traffic to and from Pods on that node using Linux BPF (Berkeley Packet Filter).
title: Use Cilium for NetworkPolicy — Deploying Cilium for Production Use

instruction: Where can I find detailed instructions for deploying Cilium in a production environment?
response: Refer to the 'Cilium Kubernetes Installation Guide' for detailed requirements, instructions, and example production DaemonSet files for deploying Cilium in production.
title: Use Cilium for NetworkPolicy — Deploying Cilium for Production Use

instruction: How can you identify pods running under a specific PodSecurityPolicy (PSP) in a Kubernetes cluster?
response:
Use the kubectl command with a jsonpath filter to check the kubernetes.io/psp annotation. For example: `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o jsonpath='{range .items[?(@.metadata.annotations.kubernetes.io/psp=="$PSP_NAME")]}{.metadata.namespace} {.metadata.name}{"
"}{end}'` where $PSP_NAME is the name of the PSP being checked.
title: Migrate from PodSecurityPolicy to the Built-In PodSecurity Admission Controller — 2.c. Rollout the updated PSPs

instruction: What is the syntax to update a deployment's service account using kubectl?
response: Use `kubectl set serviceaccount deployment <deployment-name> <service-account-name>`. For example, `kubectl set serviceaccount deployment nginx-deployment serviceaccount1` updates the deployment's service account to 'serviceaccount1'.
title: kubectl set serviceaccount — Synopsis

instruction: How can you preview the YAML output of a service account update without applying it to the cluster?
response: Use `kubectl set sa -f <filename> <service-account-name> --local --dry-run=client -o yaml`. For example, `kubectl set sa -f nginx-deployment.yaml serviceaccount1 --local --dry-run=client -o yaml` generates the YAML output locally without contacting the API server.
title: kubectl set serviceaccount — Synopsis

instruction: Which Kubernetes resources are supported by the `kubectl set serviceaccount` command?
response: Supported resources include replicationcontroller (rc), deployment (deploy), daemonset (ds), job, replicaset (rs), and statefulset.
title: kubectl set serviceaccount — Synopsis

instruction: What is the shorthand alias for `kubectl set serviceaccount`?
response: The shorthand alias is `kubectl set sa`.
title: kubectl set serviceaccount — Synopsis

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` parameter in the GET /api/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/endpoints/{name} endpoint?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` parameter requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag. Clients should not assume bookmarks are returned at any specific interval or that the server will send BOOKMARK events during a session.
title: Endpoints — getWatch

instruction: How does the `continue` parameter handle expired or invalid tokens in the watch endpoint?
response: If a `continue` token is expired or invalid (due to server configuration changes or expiration, typically 5-15 minutes), the server responds with a 410 ResourceExpired error. Clients must either restart the list without the `continue` field for a consistent view or use the new token from the error to resume from the latest snapshot.
title: Endpoints — getWatch

instruction: What is the behavior of the `sendInitialEvents` parameter when used with `watch=true`?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set with `watch=true`, the watch stream starts with synthetic events representing the current state of objects, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event. This ensures the client receives the latest state before subsequent change events. The `resourceVersionMatch` parameter must also be set for this to work.
title: Endpoints — getWatch

instruction: What checks does `kubeadm upgrade apply` perform before starting an upgrade?
response: `kubeadm upgrade apply` checks that the API server is reachable, all nodes are in the Ready state, the control plane is healthy, and enforces version skew policies to ensure compatibility.
title: Upgrading kubeadm clusters — How it works

instruction: What certificate-related actions does `kubeadm upgrade apply` perform during an upgrade?
response: `kubeadm upgrade apply` creates new certificate and key files for the API server if the existing ones are about to expire in 180 days and backs up the old files.
title: Upgrading kubeadm clusters — How it works

instruction: What are the two primary kubelet configuration settings for controlling container log rotation?
response: The two primary kubelet configuration settings are containerLogMaxSize (default 10Mi) and containerLogMaxFiles (default 5). These control the maximum size of each log file and the maximum number of files allowed per container, respectively.
title: Logging Architecture — Log rotation

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' parameter in a watch request for CSIDriver resources, and how should clients handle expired continue tokens?
response: The 'continue' parameter allows clients to retrieve additional results from a watch request by using a token provided in a previous response. If the token expires (typically after 5-15 minutes) or the server configuration changes, the server will return a 410 ResourceExpired error with a new continue token. Clients must either restart the list without the 'continue' field for a consistent view or use the new token to resume from the next key, though this may result in inconsistent results if objects have changed.
title: CSIDriver — getWatch

instruction: What command is used to roll back a Kubernetes deployment to its previous working version?
response: The command to roll back a deployment is `kubectl rollout undo deployments/<deployment-name>`. In the example, `kubectl rollout undo deployments/kubernetes-bootcamp` reverts the deployment to the previous image version (v2).
title: Performing a Rolling Update — Roll back an update

instruction: Why do Pods enter the `ImagePullBackOff` state when using the image tag `v10`?
response: Pods enter the `ImagePullBackOff` state because the image tagged `v10` does not exist in the repository. This is confirmed by running `kubectl describe pods` and observing the `Events` section indicating the image version is missing.
title: Performing a Rolling Update — Roll back an update

instruction: How can you verify that a rollback successfully reverted Pods to the previous image version?
response: After rolling back, use `kubectl describe pods` to inspect the running Pods. The output will confirm the Pods are now using the previous stable image version (v2) instead of the invalid v10 tag.
title: Performing a Rolling Update — Roll back an update

instruction: What is the purpose of specifying containerPort: 80 in the nginx Deployment?
response: Specifying containerPort: 80 makes the nginx container accessible within the cluster via its assigned pod IP address. This allows other pods or nodes in the cluster to communicate with the container using the pod's IP address on port 80 without requiring NAT rules or host port forwarding.
title: Connecting Applications with Services — Exposing pods to the cluster

instruction: How can you verify the IP addresses assigned to the nginx pods in the cluster?
response: You can verify the IP addresses by running 'kubectl get pods -l run=my-nginx -o custom-columns=POD_IP:.status.podIPs'. This command lists the pod IPs for all pods labeled 'run=my-nginx', showing their internal cluster IP addresses (e.g., 10.244.3.4 and 10.244.2.5 in the example).
title: Connecting Applications with Services — Exposing pods to the cluster

instruction: Why is it possible to access the nginx pods directly via their pod IPs without configuring host port forwarding?
response: The Kubernetes networking model ensures that pods are reachable by their IP addresses from any node in the cluster. This eliminates the need for NAT rules or host port forwarding, allowing direct communication between pods using their assigned IPs (e.g., using 'curl' on the pod IPs from any node).
title: Connecting Applications with Services — Exposing pods to the cluster

instruction: What does the example demonstrate about the Kubernetes networking model?
response: The example demonstrates that Kubernetes allows pods to be accessed directly via their assigned IP addresses without requiring special NAT rules or host port mapping. This enables multiple pods to use the same container port (e.g., 80) on the same node while remaining accessible from any node or pod in the cluster.
title: Connecting Applications with Services — Exposing pods to the cluster

instruction: What is the primary purpose of local ephemeral storage in Kubernetes nodes?
response: Local ephemeral storage in Kubernetes nodes is used for scratch space, caching, container logs, writable layers of running containers, and container images. It provides temporary storage without long-term durability guarantees.
title: Local ephemeral storage

instruction: How does the kubelet utilize local ephemeral storage for Pods?
response: The kubelet provides scratch space to Pods by mounting emptyDir volumes into containers. It also uses this storage to hold node-level container logs, container images, and the writable layers of running containers.
title: Local ephemeral storage

instruction: What are the two requirements for enforcing resource quotas on ephemeral storage?
response: To enforce resource quotas on ephemeral storage, an admin must set the resource quota in a namespace, and a user must specify ephemeral-storage limits in the Pod spec. If the user omits the limit, the quota is not enforced.
title: Local ephemeral storage

instruction: What happens if a Pod does not specify an ephemeral-storage limit in its specification?
response: If a Pod does not specify an ephemeral-storage limit in its specification, Kubernetes will not enforce the resource quota for ephemeral storage, even if a namespace-level quota exists.
title: Local ephemeral storage

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'dryRun' parameter when deleting a Kubernetes Deployment, and what are its valid values?
response: The 'dryRun' parameter indicates that modifications should not be persisted, allowing users to test the deletion without actually removing the resource. Valid values include 'All', which processes all dry run stages. An invalid directive results in an error and no further processing.
title: Deployment — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: How does the 'gracePeriodSeconds' parameter affect the deletion of a Deployment, and what does a value of zero signify?
response: The 'gracePeriodSeconds' specifies the time in seconds before the Deployment is deleted. A value of zero indicates immediate deletion, bypassing the default grace period. If not specified, the server uses the per-object default grace period.
title: Deployment — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the difference between 'propagationPolicy' and 'orphanDependents' when deleting a Deployment, and why is 'orphanDependents' deprecated?
response: 'propagationPolicy' defines garbage collection behavior (e.g., 'Orphan', 'Background', 'Foreground'), while 'orphanDependents' is a deprecated boolean that adds/removes the 'orphan' finalizer. 'propagationPolicy' is preferred as it provides more explicit control over cascading deletion and is not deprecated.
title: Deployment — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the minimum node requirement for the Kubernetes cluster when following this tutorial?
response: The tutorial recommends running it on a cluster with at least two nodes that are not acting as control plane hosts.
title: Create an External Load Balancer — Before you begin

instruction: What environments are required to support external load balancers for this task?
response: Your cluster must be running in a cloud or other environment that already has support for configuring external load balancers.
title: Create an External Load Balancer — Before you begin

instruction: What tools or services are mentioned for creating a Kubernetes cluster if one does not already exist?
response: You can create a cluster using minikube or use Kubernetes playgrounds like iximiuz Labs, Killercoda, or KodeKloud.
title: Create an External Load Balancer — Before you begin

instruction: What command-line tool must be configured to communicate with the Kubernetes cluster?
response: The kubectl command-line tool must be configured to communicate with your cluster.
title: Create an External Load Balancer — Before you begin

instruction: What conditions must be met for Kubernetes to use the mount option for SELinux relabeling instead of recursive relabeling?
response: To use the mount option for SELinux relabeling, the following conditions must be met: (1) The Pod must use a PersistentVolumeClaim with accessModes: ["ReadWriteOncePod"] or have the SELinuxMount feature gate enabled and the Pod's spec.securityContext.seLinuxChangePolicy set to nil (default) or MountOption. (2) The Pod (or its containers) must have seLinuxOptions set. (3) The PersistentVolume must be a legacy in-tree iscsi, rbd, or fc volume type, or a CSI driver that explicitly supports mounting with -o context by setting spec.seLinuxMount: true in its CSIDriver instance.
title: Configure a Security Context for a Pod or Container — Efficient SELinux volume relabeling

instruction: What is the role of the SELinuxWarningController in Kubernetes 1.36?
response: The SELinuxWarningController, introduced in Kubernetes 1.36, detects when multiple Pods using the same volume have conflicting SELinux labels. It emits events to the affected Pods and raises a metric (selinux_warning_controller_selinux_volume_conflict) with pod names and namespaces as labels. This helps cluster admins identify Pods affected by SELinux label conflicts and proactively opt them out of the optimization by setting spec.securityContext.seLinuxChangePolicy: Recursive.
title: Configure a Security Context for a Pod or Container — Efficient SELinux volume relabeling

instruction: What is the primary goal of the Kubernetes RBAC privilege escalation risk section?
response: The primary goal is to provide visibility into areas where cluster operators must exercise caution to avoid inadvertently granting excessive access to Kubernetes clusters, which could lead to privilege escalation or unintended system impacts.
title: Role Based Access Control Good Practices — Kubernetes RBAC - privilege escalation risks

instruction: What types of privileges in Kubernetes RBAC are highlighted as potential risks?
response: Privileges that allow users or service accounts to escalate their own privileges within the cluster or affect systems outside the cluster are highlighted as risks. These require careful management to prevent unintended access escalation.
title: Role Based Access Control Good Practices — Kubernetes RBAC - privilege escalation risks

instruction: What responsibility do cluster operators have regarding RBAC privilege management?
response: Cluster operators must ensure they do not inadvertently grant more access than intended by carefully reviewing and restricting privileges that could lead to privilege escalation or external system impacts.
title: Role Based Access Control Good Practices — Kubernetes RBAC - privilege escalation risks

instruction: What is the purpose of using the dry-run command when testing a Pod Security level?
response: The dry-run command evaluates existing pods in the namespace against the proposed Pod Security level without enforcing the policy. It returns warnings for any pods that would be invalid under the new level, allowing for testing before enforcement.
title: Migrate from PodSecurityPolicy to the Built-In PodSecurity Admission Controller — 3.b. Verify the Pod Security level

instruction: How does audit mode differ from enforce mode in Pod Security settings?
response: Audit mode records violations of the Pod Security level in audit logs without blocking non-compliant pods, while enforce mode actively prevents such pods from being created. Audit mode allows for monitoring and review over time, whereas enforce mode enforces compliance immediately.
title: Migrate from PodSecurityPolicy to the Built-In PodSecurity Admission Controller — 3.b. Verify the Pod Security level

instruction: What should be done if unexpected violations are found during dry-run or audit testing?
response: If unexpected violations are found, you should either update the violating workloads to meet the policy requirements or adjust the namespace's Pod Security level to a less restrictive setting.
title: Migrate from PodSecurityPolicy to the Built-In PodSecurity Admission Controller — 3.b. Verify the Pod Security level

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' parameter in the GET /apis/apiextensions.k8s.io/v1/watch/customresourcedefinitions endpoint, and how should clients handle expired continue tokens?
response: The 'continue' parameter allows clients to retrieve subsequent results from a server after an initial query. If the server returns a 410 ResourceExpired error with a new continue token, clients must either restart the list without 'continue' for consistency or use the new token to get results from the latest snapshot. Expired tokens are typically invalid after 5-15 minutes or due to server configuration changes.
title: CustomResourceDefinition — getWatch List

instruction: How does the 'sendInitialEvents' parameter affect a watch stream when set to true, and what is the role of the 'resourceVersionMatch' parameter in this context?
response: When 'sendInitialEvents=true' is set with 'watch=true', the server sends synthetic events to reflect the current state of objects before normal watch events. The 'resourceVersionMatch' parameter determines how the provided 'resourceVersion' is applied. For example, if 'resourceVersionMatch=NotOlderThan', the bookmark event is sent when the state is synced to a resource version at least as fresh as the provided value. 'resourceVersionMatch' is required when 'sendInitialEvents' is set.
title: CustomResourceDefinition — getWatch List

instruction: How is the kube-proxy add-on deployed in a Kubernetes cluster using kubeadm?
response: The kube-proxy add-on is deployed as a DaemonSet. It uses a ServiceAccount in the kube-system namespace, which is bound to the system:node-proxier ClusterRole for permissions. The API server's location is retrieved from a ConfigMap, and credentials (ca.crt and token) are obtained from the ServiceAccount.
title: Implementation details — Install addons

instruction: Why is the CoreDNS service named 'kube-dns' in Kubernetes clusters managed by kubeadm?
response: The CoreDNS service is named 'kube-dns' for compatibility with the legacy kube-dns add-on. This naming ensures existing configurations and references to the DNS service continue to function without modification.
title: Implementation details — Install addons

instruction: What permissions are granted to the CoreDNS ServiceAccount in the kube-system namespace?
response: The CoreDNS ServiceAccount in the kube-system namespace is bound to the system:coredns ClusterRole, which provides the necessary privileges for the CoreDNS component to operate within the cluster.
title: Implementation details — Install addons

instruction: What change occurred in Kubernetes 1.21 regarding the 'kube-dns' add-on when using kubeadm?
response: In Kubernetes 1.21, support for using the legacy 'kube-dns' add-on with kubeadm was removed. However, CoreDNS can still be used with kubeadm even if the related Service is named 'kube-dns' for backward compatibility.
title: Implementation details — Install addons

instruction: How does the Kubernetes scheduler use a DeviceClass's extendedResourceName to allocate resources for a pod?
response: The scheduler selects devices matching the DeviceClass's extendedResourceName when a pod requests that extended resource. For example, if a DeviceClass specifies 'example.com/gpu' as its extendedResourceName, a pod requesting 'example.com/gpu: 2' will be scheduled on a node with at least two devices matching the DeviceClass's criteria.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — DRA alpha features

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'deviceclass.resource.kubernetes.io/' extended resource prefix in DRA?
response: This prefix allows users to allocate devices of a specific DeviceClass without explicitly creating a ResourceClaim. Using this prefix with a DeviceClass name (e.g., 'deviceclass.resource.kubernetes.io/gpu.example.com') automatically generates a ResourceClaim requesting an ExactCount of devices from that DeviceClass, even if the DeviceClass does not define its own extendedResourceName.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — DRA alpha features

instruction: Why do MySQL and WordPress require PersistentVolumes in this example?
response: MySQL and WordPress require PersistentVolumes to store their data persistently. The example specifies that each application needs a PersistentVolume to ensure data is retained beyond the lifecycle of individual pods or deployments.
title: Example: Deploying WordPress and MySQL with Persistent Volumes — Create PersistentVolumeClaims and PersistentVolumes

instruction: How is the StorageClass determined for a PersistentVolumeClaim in this scenario?
response: If a PersistentVolumeClaim does not explicitly specify a StorageClass, the cluster's default StorageClass is automatically used. This is the behavior described in the text for environments where a default StorageClass is installed.
title: Example: Deploying WordPress and MySQL with Persistent Volumes — Create PersistentVolumeClaims and PersistentVolumes

instruction: What happens when a PersistentVolumeClaim is created in this example?
response: When a PersistentVolumeClaim is created, a PersistentVolume is dynamically provisioned based on the StorageClass configuration. This process occurs automatically as part of the PVC creation step in the described deployment workflow.
title: Example: Deploying WordPress and MySQL with Persistent Volumes — Create PersistentVolumeClaims and PersistentVolumes

instruction: What command is used to restart the kubelet service after changing the container runtime?
response: The command `systemctl start kubelet` is used to restart the kubelet service after changing the container runtime on a node.
title: Changing the Container Runtime on a Node from Docker Engine to containerd — Restart the kubelet

instruction: How can you verify that containerd is the active container runtime on a node after migration?
response: Run `kubectl get nodes -o wide` and check that containerd is listed as the runtime for the node that was updated.
title: Changing the Container Runtime on a Node from Docker Engine to containerd — Restart the kubelet

instruction: What are the commands to remove Docker Engine from CentOS and Ubuntu systems?
response: On CentOS, use `sudo yum remove docker-ce docker-ce-cli`. On Ubuntu, use `sudo apt-get purge docker-ce docker-ce-cli`.
title: Changing the Container Runtime on a Node from Docker Engine to containerd — Restart the kubelet

instruction: What happens to Docker images, containers, and configuration files when using the provided Docker removal commands?
response: The provided commands remove Docker Engine but do not delete images, containers, volumes, or customized configuration files. These must be manually removed following Docker's official uninstallation instructions.
title: Changing the Container Runtime on a Node from Docker Engine to containerd — Restart the kubelet

instruction: Which file should be edited to configure the kubelet to use containerd as the container runtime with kubeadm?
response: Edit the file /var/lib/kubelet/kubeadm-flags.env and add the flag --container-runtime-endpoint=unix:///run/containerd/containerd.sock to specify the containerd runtime endpoint.
title: Changing the Container Runtime on a Node from Docker Engine to containerd — Configure the kubelet to use containerd as its container runtime

instruction: What is the primary purpose of PersistentVolumes (PVs) in Kubernetes?
response: PersistentVolumes (PVs) provide an abstraction for storage resources in a Kubernetes cluster, decoupling storage management from compute instance management. They represent a piece of storage that can be provisioned by administrators or dynamically via StorageClasses, and they exist independently of individual Pods that use them.
title: Persistent Volumes

instruction: How do PersistentVolumeClaims (PVCs) differ from Pods in terms of resource consumption?
response: PersistentVolumeClaims (PVCs) are requests for storage by users, similar to how Pods request compute resources like CPU and memory. While Pods consume node resources, PVCs consume PV resources by specifying storage size and access modes (e.g., ReadWriteOnce, ReadOnlyMany).
title: Persistent Volumes

instruction: What role does StorageClass play in managing PersistentVolumes?
response: StorageClass enables cluster administrators to define different classes of storage (e.g., varying performance tiers) without exposing implementation details to users. It allows administrators to offer PersistentVolumes with properties beyond size and access modes, such as performance characteristics, while abstracting the underlying storage system (e.g., NFS, iSCSI).
title: Persistent Volumes

instruction: What are the key lifecycle characteristics of a PersistentVolume compared to a regular Volume?
response: Unlike regular Volumes, which are tied to the lifecycle of a Pod, PersistentVolumes have an independent lifecycle. They are cluster resources that persist beyond the Pod's existence and can be dynamically provisioned, retained, or deleted based on policies defined by administrators.
title: Persistent Volumes

instruction: What happens if the .spec.selector of a StatefulSet does not match its .spec.template.metadata.labels?
response: Failing to specify a matching Pod Selector will result in a validation error during StatefulSet creation.
title: StatefulSets — Pod Selector

instruction: Under what conditions does .spec.volumeClaimTemplates provide stable storage for a StatefulSet?
response: Stable storage is provided if either: (1) the specified StorageClass is set up for dynamic provisioning, or (2) the cluster already contains a PersistentVolume with the correct StorageClass and sufficient available storage space.
title: StatefulSets — Pod Selector

instruction: What is the purpose of the ImagePullServiceAccount in Kubernetes?
response: The ImagePullServiceAccount is a representation of a Kubernetes service account object used by the kubelet to send a service account token to the credential provider plugin for image pull credentials.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — ImagePullServiceAccount

instruction: Which fields are required for the ImagePullServiceAccount object?
response: The required fields for the ImagePullServiceAccount object are uid, namespace, and name, all of which are strings.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1beta1) — ImagePullServiceAccount

instruction: What is required in the .spec.template of a ReplicaSet pod template and why is it important?
response: The .spec.template of a ReplicaSet pod template is required to have labels in place. This is important to avoid overlapping with the selectors of other controllers, which might otherwise attempt to adopt the Pod unintentionally, as demonstrated by the example label 'tier: frontend' in the frontend.yaml configuration.
title: ReplicaSet — Pod Template

instruction: What is the only allowed value for the restart policy in a ReplicaSet pod template, and what is its default?
response: The only allowed value for the restart policy in a ReplicaSet pod template is 'Always', and this is the default value. This ensures that Pods managed by the ReplicaSet are always restarted if they terminate, maintaining the desired state of the ReplicaSet.
title: ReplicaSet — Pod Template

instruction: How do I create a PersistentVolume in Kubernetes?
response: Refer to the linked documentation on 'Creating a PersistentVolume' for step-by-step instructions on defining and provisioning a PersistentVolume resource.
title: Persistent Volumes — What's next

instruction: What is the purpose of the Persistent Storage design document?
response: The Persistent Storage design document provides detailed architectural and implementation insights into Kubernetes' persistent storage features, including design decisions and technical specifications.
title: Persistent Volumes — What's next

instruction: Which APIs are associated with persistent storage in Kubernetes?
response: The APIs related to persistent storage are `PersistentVolume` and `PersistentVolumeClaim`, which manage storage resources and their claims by workloads, respectively.
title: Persistent Volumes — What's next

instruction: How do I create a PersistentVolumeClaim?
response: Follow the 'Creating a PersistentVolumeClaim' guide in the linked documentation to define a claim that binds to an available PersistentVolume.
title: Persistent Volumes — What's next

instruction: What is the default CPU management policy used by the kubelet to enforce pod CPU limits?
response: The kubelet uses CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler) quota by default to enforce pod CPU limits.
title: Control CPU Management Policies on the Node — Configuring CPU management policies

instruction: Why might CPU-bound workloads experience performance issues when using the default CPU management policy?
response: CPU-bound workloads may experience performance issues due to CPU cache affinity and scheduling latency when the workload migrates between different CPU cores as a result of throttling or core availability changes.
title: Control CPU Management Policies on the Node — Configuring CPU management policies

instruction: What alternative CPU management policies does the kubelet support for workloads sensitive to CPU cache affinity?
response: The kubelet allows alternative CPU management policies to determine placement preferences on the node for workloads where CPU cache affinity and scheduling latency significantly affect performance.
title: Control CPU Management Policies on the Node — Configuring CPU management policies

instruction: What does the 'loadBalancer' field in IngressStatus represent?
response: The 'loadBalancer' field in IngressStatus contains the current status of the load-balancer associated with the Ingress resource.
title: Ingress — IngressStatus

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'items' field in IngressList?
response: The 'items' field in IngressList is an array that contains the list of Ingress resources in the collection.
title: Ingress — IngressStatus

instruction: What is the role of the 'apiVersion' field in IngressList?
response: The 'apiVersion' field in IngressList defines the versioned schema of the object representation. Servers should convert recognized schemas to the latest internal value and may reject unrecognized values.
title: Ingress — IngressStatus

instruction: What is the significance of the 'metadata' field in IngressList?
response: The 'metadata' field in IngressList provides standard object metadata for the list, such as resource version and other common metadata attributes.
title: Ingress — IngressStatus

instruction: What is the correct procedure to upgrade kubeadm on Ubuntu/Debian systems for the first control plane node?
