response: The pv.kubernetes.io/bind-completed annotation indicates that the lifecycle of the PVC has passed through initial binding setup, which changes how the control plane interprets the state of PVC objects.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — failure-domain.beta.kubernetes.io/region (deprecated)

instruction: What is the significance of the value of the pv.kubernetes.io/bind-completed annotation?
response: The value of the pv.kubernetes.io/bind-completed annotation does not matter to Kubernetes.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — failure-domain.beta.kubernetes.io/region (deprecated)

instruction: On which Kubernetes object is the pv.kubernetes.io/bind-completed annotation used?
response: The pv.kubernetes.io/bind-completed annotation is used on a PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC).
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — failure-domain.beta.kubernetes.io/region (deprecated)

instruction: What is the purpose of the spec.selector field in a Deployment?
response: The spec.selector field defines how the created ReplicaSet finds which Pods to manage. It is a map of {key,value} pairs, where a single {key,value} in the matchLabels map is equivalent to an element of matchExpressions, whose key field is 'key', the operator is 'In', and the values array contains only 'value'. All of the requirements, from both matchLabels and matchExpressions, must be satisfied in order to match.
title: Deployments — Creating a Deployment

instruction: How do you create a Deployment using the provided example YAML file?
response: To create the Deployment, run the command kubectl apply -f https://k8s.io/examples/controllers/nginx-deployment.yaml. You can then check if the Deployment was created by running kubectl get deployments.
title: Deployments — Creating a Deployment

instruction: What information is displayed when you run kubectl get deployments?
response: When you run kubectl get deployments, the following fields are displayed: NAME, READY, UP-TO-DATE, AVAILABLE, and AGE. The READY field displays how many replicas of the application are available to your users, the UP-TO-DATE field displays the number of replicas that have been updated to achieve the desired state, the AVAILABLE field displays how many replicas of the application are available to your users, and the AGE field displays the amount of time that the application has been running.
title: Deployments — Creating a Deployment

instruction: How do you check the rollout status of a Deployment?
response: To check the rollout status of a Deployment, run the command kubectl rollout status deployment/<deployment-name>. This will display the current rollout status, including the number of replicas that have been updated and whether the rollout is complete.
title: Deployments — Creating a Deployment

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' option in the GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/configmaps request?
response: The continue option is used to retrieve more results from the server when the initial response is incomplete. The value of continue should be set to the value returned in the previous query result with identical query parameters, and the server may reject a continue value it does not recognize.
title: ConfigMap — getList

instruction: How does the 'limit' parameter affect the response of the GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/configmaps request?
response: The limit parameter sets a maximum number of responses to return for a list call. If more items exist, the server will set the 'continue' field on the list metadata to a value that can be used with the same initial query to retrieve the next set of results. Setting a limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items in the event all requested objects are filtered out.
title: ConfigMap — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'sendInitialEvents' option in the GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/configmaps request?
response: The sendInitialEvents option is used to produce the current state of objects in the collection when watch=true. The watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event, and then proceed as usual, sending watch events corresponding to changes to objects watched.
title: ConfigMap — getList

instruction: How does the 'resourceVersionMatch' parameter interact with the 'resourceVersion' parameter in the GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/configmaps request?
response: The resourceVersionMatch parameter determines how the resourceVersion is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that resourceVersionMatch be set for list calls where resourceVersion is set. The semantic of the watch request depends on the value of resourceVersionMatch, which can be set to 'NotOlderThan' to interpret the data as 'at least as new as the provided resourceVersion'.
title: ConfigMap — getList

instruction: What is the order in which Pods are updated in a StatefulSet using the RollingUpdate strategy?
response: The Pods in the StatefulSet are updated in reverse ordinal order. The StatefulSet controller terminates each Pod, and waits for it to transition to Running and Ready prior to updating the next Pod.
title: StatefulSet Basics — RollingUpdate

instruction: How can updates to a StatefulSet that uses the RollingUpdate strategy be split into partitions?
response: Updates to a StatefulSet that uses the RollingUpdate strategy can be split into partitions by specifying .spec.updateStrategy.rollingUpdate.partition. The value of 'partition' determines which ordinals a change applies to.
title: StatefulSet Basics — RollingUpdate

instruction: What happens to a Pod that fails during an update in a StatefulSet using the RollingUpdate strategy?
response: The controller attempts to continue to keep the application healthy and the update consistent in the presence of intermittent failures. Pods that have already received the update will be restored to the updated version, and Pods that have not yet received the update will be restored to the previous version.
title: StatefulSet Basics — RollingUpdate

instruction: How can a staged update be triggered in a StatefulSet using the RollingUpdate strategy with partitions?
response: A staged update can be triggered by decrementing the partition specified in the updateStrategy. This allows for a canary rollout of the modified template to test it before applying it to all Pods in the StatefulSet.
title: StatefulSet Basics — RollingUpdate

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the FlowSchema watch list API?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: FlowSchema — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the FlowSchema watch list API?
response: The `continue` field is used to retrieve more results from the server. If the specified `continue` value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token. The client may then send another list request with the token received with the 410 error to get the next set of results.
title: FlowSchema — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents` to `true` in the FlowSchema watch list API?
response: When `sendInitialEvents` is set to `true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent, and then the watch stream will proceed as usual, sending watch events corresponding to changes to objects watched.
title: FlowSchema — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `resourceVersionMatch` field interact with the `sendInitialEvents` field in the FlowSchema watch list API?
response: When `sendInitialEvents` is set, `resourceVersionMatch` must also be set. The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls, and its value affects the semantic of the watch request, including when the bookmark event is sent.
title: FlowSchema — getWatch List

instruction: What is the primary resource for learning about Kubernetes API access control?
response: The primary resource for learning about Kubernetes API access control is the documentation titled 'Controlling Access to the Kubernetes API'.
title: API Access Control

instruction: What are some methods of authentication in Kubernetes?
response: Kubernetes supports several methods of authentication, including Authenticating with Bootstrap Tokens, and Certificate Signing Requests.
title: API Access Control

instruction: What are some types of authorization in Kubernetes?
response: Kubernetes supports several types of authorization, including Role Based Access Control, Attribute Based Access Control, Node Authorization, and Webhook Authorization.
title: API Access Control

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubelet TLS bootstrapping process?
response: The kubelet TLS bootstrapping process is used for Kubelet Authentication & Authorization.
title: API Access Control

instruction: Where does kubeadm store its KubeletConfiguration during cluster creation and upgrade?
response: Kubeadm stores its KubeletConfiguration in a ConfigMap called kubelet-config in the kube-system namespace.
title: Reconfiguring a kubeadm cluster — Applying kubelet configuration changes

instruction: How do you apply kubelet configuration changes to a kubeadm node after editing the kubelet-config ConfigMap?
response: To apply the changes, log in to the kubeadm node, run kubeadm upgrade node phase kubelet-config, edit the /var/lib/kubelet/kubeadm-flags.env file to apply additional configuration with flags, and then restart the kubelet service with systemctl restart kubelet.
title: Reconfiguring a kubeadm cluster — Applying kubelet configuration changes

instruction: What command is used to edit the kubelet-config ConfigMap in the kube-system namespace?
response: The command kubectl edit cm -n kube-system kubelet-config is used to edit the kubelet-config ConfigMap.
title: Reconfiguring a kubeadm cluster — Applying kubelet configuration changes

instruction: Where is the updated kubelet configuration stored after running kubeadm upgrade node phase kubelet-config?
response: The updated kubelet configuration is stored in the local file /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml.
title: Reconfiguring a kubeadm cluster — Applying kubelet configuration changes

instruction: What is the purpose of the apiVersion field in a PersistentVolumeClaim?
response: The apiVersion field defines the versioned schema of this representation of an object. Servers should convert recognized schemas to the latest internal value, and may reject unrecognized values.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim

instruction: What information does the spec field in a PersistentVolumeClaim provide?
response: The spec field defines the desired characteristics of a volume requested by a pod author.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim

instruction: Can the kind field in a PersistentVolumeClaim be updated?
response: No, the kind field cannot be updated. It is a string value representing the REST resource this object represents, and servers may infer this from the endpoint the client submits requests to.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim

instruction: What is the purpose of the status field in a PersistentVolumeClaim?
response: The status field represents the current information/status of a persistent volume claim, and is read-only.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim

instruction: What is the recommended replacement for the deprecated azureDisk in-tree storage driver in Kubernetes?
response: The Kubernetes project suggests that you use the Azure Disk third party storage driver instead.
title: Storage Classes — Azure Disk

instruction: What happens if a storage account is not provided in the StorageClass configuration for Azure Disk?
response: If a storage account is not provided, all storage accounts associated with the resource group are searched to find one that matches skuName and location.
title: Storage Classes — Azure Disk

instruction: Why is it recommended to set the value for secretNamespace explicitly in a multi-tenancy context?
response: It is strongly recommended to set the value for secretNamespace explicitly to prevent the storage account credentials from being read by other users.
title: Storage Classes — Azure Disk

instruction: What permission needs to be added for clusterrole system:controller:persistent-volume-binder when RBAC and Controller Roles are enabled?
response: The create permission of resource secret needs to be added for clusterrole system:controller:persistent-volume-binder.
title: Storage Classes — Azure Disk

instruction: What is the required format for the name of a secret that backs a bootstrap token in Kubernetes?
response: The name of the secret must be of the form 'bootstrap-token-<token id>'.
title: Authenticating with Bootstrap Tokens — Bootstrap Token Secret Format

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'usage-bootstrap-authentication' field in a bootstrap token secret, and how must it be set to enable this functionality?
response: The 'usage-bootstrap-authentication' field indicates that the token can be used to authenticate to the API server as a bearer token. To enable this functionality, the field must be set to 'true'.
title: Authenticating with Bootstrap Tokens — Bootstrap Token Secret Format

instruction: How is the expiration time of a bootstrap token specified, and what happens to tokens that have expired?
response: The expiration time of a bootstrap token is specified in the 'expiration' field using an absolute UTC time in RFC3339 format. Expired tokens are rejected when used for authentication and ignored during ConfigMap signing.
title: Authenticating with Bootstrap Tokens — Bootstrap Token Secret Format

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'tokencleaner' controller in relation to bootstrap tokens, and why is it useful?
response: The 'tokencleaner' controller is used to automatically delete expired tokens. Enabling this controller is useful for maintaining token hygiene and preventing the accumulation of expired tokens.
title: Authenticating with Bootstrap Tokens — Bootstrap Token Secret Format

instruction: How can I query the availability of devices in resource pools using the ResourcePoolStatusRequest API?
response: To check resource pool status, create a ResourcePoolStatusRequest specifying the driver name and optionally a limit on the number of pools returned or a specific pool name. Then, wait for the controller to process the request using 'kubectl wait --for=condition=Complete resourcepoolstatusrequest/<request-name> --timeout=30s', and read the status to see pool availability using 'kubectl get resourcepoolstatusrequest/<request-name> -o yaml'.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — Resource pool status

instruction: What information is included in the status of a ResourcePoolStatusRequest?
response: The status includes poolCount (total number of pools matching the filter), pools (a list of pool details), and conditions (Complete or Failed condition types). Each pool detail contains driver, poolName, generation, resourceSliceCount, totalDevices, allocatedDevices, availableDevices, unavailableDevices, nodeName, and validationError.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — Resource pool status

instruction: How do I get updated availability data for resource pools?
response: To get updated availability data, delete the existing ResourcePoolStatusRequest and recreate it. Completed requests are automatically cleaned up after 1 hour.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — Resource pool status

instruction: What are the requirements for using the ResourcePoolStatusRequest feature?
response: This feature requires explicit RBAC permissions on the ResourcePoolStatusRequest resource and the DRAResourcePoolStatus feature gate to be enabled in the kube-apiserver and kube-controller-manager. Additionally, it is an alpha feature.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — Resource pool status

instruction: What information is contained in the tokenAttributes field of the CredentialProviderConfig file for a credential provider plugin?
response: The tokenAttributes field contains information about the service account token that will be passed to the plugin, including the intended audience for the token and whether the plugin requires the pod to have a service account.
title: Configure a kubelet image credential provider — Service Account Token for Image Pulls

instruction: What are the benefits of using service account token credentials for image pulls in Kubernetes?
response: Using service account token credentials can enable the following use-cases: Avoid needing a kubelet/node-based identity to pull images from a registry, and allow workloads to pull images based on their own runtime identity without long-lived/persisted secrets.
title: Configure a kubelet image credential provider — Service Account Token for Image Pulls

instruction: What is the purpose of the ComponentStatus API in Kubernetes and what is its current status?
response: The ComponentStatus API holds the cluster validation information. However, this API is deprecated in v1.19+.
title: Core

instruction: What information does the Endpoints API provide in Kubernetes and what is its current status?
response: The Endpoints API provides a collection of endpoints that implement the actual service, including addresses and ports. However, it is a legacy API and does not contain information about all Service features. It is deprecated in v1.33+, and users are recommended to use discoveryv1.EndpointSlice for complete information about Service endpoints.
title: Core

instruction: What is the purpose of the Event resource in Kubernetes and how should it be treated by event consumers?
response: The Event resource is a report of an event somewhere in the cluster. Events have a limited retention time, and triggers and messages may evolve with time. Event consumers should treat events as informative, best-effort, supplemental data and not rely on the timing of an event with a given Reason reflecting a consistent underlying trigger.
title: Core

instruction: What is the difference between a PersistentVolume (PV) and a PersistentVolumeClaim in Kubernetes?
response: A PersistentVolume (PV) is a storage resource provisioned by an administrator, analogous to a node. A PersistentVolumeClaim, on the other hand, is a user's request for and claim to a persistent volume.
title: Core

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' field in the NetworkPolicy getList API?
response: The 'continue' field is used to retrieve more results from the server. It should be set when retrieving more results, and its value is server-defined. If the specified 'continue' value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error along with a continue token.
title: NetworkPolicy — getList

instruction: How does the 'limit' field affect the response of the NetworkPolicy getList API?
response: The 'limit' field sets a maximum number of responses to return for a list call. If more items exist, the server will set the 'continue' field on the list metadata to a value that can be used with the same initial query to retrieve the next set of results. Setting a limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items in the event all requested objects are filtered out.
title: NetworkPolicy — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'sendInitialEvents' field in the NetworkPolicy getList API?
response: The 'sendInitialEvents' field is used to produce the current state of objects in the collection when 'watch=true'. It sends synthetic events to produce the current state, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event, and then proceeds with the watch stream as usual.
title: NetworkPolicy — getList

instruction: How does the 'resourceVersionMatch' field interact with the 'sendInitialEvents' field in the NetworkPolicy getList API?
response: When 'sendInitialEvents' is set, 'resourceVersionMatch' must also be set. The 'resourceVersionMatch' field determines how 'resourceVersion' is applied to list calls, and it is highly recommended to set it for list calls where 'resourceVersion' is set.
title: NetworkPolicy — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the `continue` field in the `GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/watch/deviceclasses/{name}` request?
response: The `continue` field is used to retrieve more results from the server. It should be set when retrieving more results from the server, and its value is server-defined. If the specified `continue` value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token.
title: DeviceClass — getWatch

instruction: How does the `limit` field affect the response of the `GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/watch/deviceclasses/{name}` request?
response: The `limit` field sets a maximum number of responses to return for a list call. If more items exist, the server will set the `continue` field on the list metadata to a value that can be used with the same initial query to retrieve the next set of results. Setting a limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items in the event all requested objects are filtered out.
title: DeviceClass — getWatch

instruction: What is the purpose of the `sendInitialEvents` field in the `GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/watch/deviceclasses/{name}` request?
response: The `sendInitialEvents` field is used to send synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection when `watch=true`. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent, and the watch stream will proceed as usual, sending watch events corresponding to changes to objects watched.
title: DeviceClass — getWatch

instruction: How does the `resourceVersionMatch` field interact with the `sendInitialEvents` field in the `GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/watch/deviceclasses/{name}` request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents` option is set, the `resourceVersionMatch` option must also be set. The semantic of the watch request is as follows: if `resourceVersionMatch` = NotOlderThan, it is interpreted as 'data at least as new as the provided `resourceVersion`', and the bookmark event is sent when the state is synced to a `resourceVersion` at least as fresh as the one provided by the ListOptions.
title: DeviceClass — getWatch

instruction: What annotation can be used to indicate that an IngressClass should be considered default?
response: The ingressclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class annotation can be used to indicate that an IngressClass should be considered default.
title: IngressClass

instruction: What happens to new Ingress resources without a class specified when a single IngressClass resource has the ingressclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class annotation set to true?
response: New Ingress resources without a class specified will be assigned this default class.
title: IngressClass

instruction: What is the purpose of the apiVersion field in an IngressClass resource?
response: The apiVersion field defines the versioned schema of this representation of an object. Servers should convert recognized schemas to the latest internal value, and may reject unrecognized values.
title: IngressClass

instruction: What is the purpose of the spec field in an IngressClass resource?
response: The spec field represents the desired state of the IngressClass, which is of type IngressClassSpec.
title: IngressClass

instruction: What is the purpose of adding labels to nodes in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Adding labels to nodes allows you to target Pods for scheduling on specific nodes or groups of nodes, ensuring that specific Pods only run on nodes with certain isolation, security, or regulatory properties.
title: Assigning Pods to Nodes — Node labels

instruction: Why is it important to choose label keys that the kubelet cannot modify when using labels for node isolation?
response: Choosing label keys that the kubelet cannot modify prevents a compromised node from setting those labels on itself, which could lead to the scheduler scheduling workloads onto the compromised node.
title: Assigning Pods to Nodes — Node labels

instruction: How can you prevent the kubelet from setting or modifying certain labels on a node?
response: The NodeRestriction admission plugin prevents the kubelet from setting or modifying labels with a node-restriction.kubernetes.io/ prefix. To use this feature, you must be using the Node authorizer and have enabled the NodeRestriction admission plugin.
title: Assigning Pods to Nodes — Node labels

instruction: What is the correct way to add labels for node isolation using the node-restriction.kubernetes.io/ prefix?
response: To add labels for node isolation, ensure you are using the Node authorizer and have enabled the NodeRestriction admission plugin. Then, add labels with the node-restriction.kubernetes.io/ prefix to your nodes, and use those labels in your node selectors. For example, example.com.node-restriction.kubernetes.io/fips=true or example.com.node-restriction.kubernetes.io/pci-dss=true.
title: Assigning Pods to Nodes — Node labels

instruction: Where can I find detailed information on certificate management with kubeadm?
response: For detailed information on certificate management with kubeadm, see the Certificate Management with kubeadm document, which includes information about using external CA, custom certificates, and certificate renewal.
title: kubeadm init — Certificate management with kubeadm

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubeadm configuration file for running the kubelet by systemd?
response: The kubeadm package ships with a configuration file for running the kubelet by systemd, which is part of the kubeadm DEB/RPM package and is not touched by the kubeadm CLI.
title: kubeadm init — Certificate management with kubeadm

instruction: How can I find further information about managing the kubeadm drop-in file for systemd?
response: For further information on managing the kubeadm drop-in file for systemd, see the document titled Managing the kubeadm drop-in file for systemd.
title: kubeadm init — Certificate management with kubeadm

instruction: What was used to specify Ingress classes before Kubernetes 1.18?
response: Before Kubernetes 1.18, Ingress classes were specified with a kubernetes.io/ingress.class annotation on the Ingress.
title: Ingress — Deprecated annotation

instruction: What is the replacement for the kubernetes.io/ingress.class annotation in Kubernetes 1.18 and later?
response: The ingressClassName field on Ingresses is a replacement for the kubernetes.io/ingress.class annotation, but it is not a direct equivalent. Instead of referencing the name of the Ingress controller, it references an IngressClass resource that contains additional Ingress configuration.
title: Ingress — Deprecated annotation

instruction: What is the main difference between the kubernetes.io/ingress.class annotation and the ingressClassName field?
response: The main difference is that the annotation was generally used to reference the name of the Ingress controller that should implement the Ingress, while the ingressClassName field references an IngressClass resource that contains additional Ingress configuration, including the name of the Ingress controller.
title: Ingress — Deprecated annotation

instruction: What are the two primary modes of finding a Service in Kubernetes?
response: The two primary modes of finding a Service in Kubernetes are environment variables and DNS. The environment variables mode works out of the box, while the DNS mode requires the CoreDNS cluster addon.
title: Connecting Applications with Services — Accessing the Service

instruction: Why might the environment variables for a Service not be available to a Pod when it starts?
response: The environment variables for a Service might not be available to a Pod when it starts if the Pod is created before the Service. This can be resolved by killing the Pod and waiting for the Deployment to recreate it, ensuring the Service exists before the Pod.
title: Connecting Applications with Services — Accessing the Service

instruction: How can you ensure that the scheduler spreads the Pods of a Service across multiple machines?
response: You can ensure that the scheduler spreads the Pods of a Service across multiple machines by creating the Service before creating the Pods. This allows the scheduler to distribute the Pods across different nodes, provided all nodes have equal capacity.
title: Connecting Applications with Services — Accessing the Service

instruction: What command can be used to verify the environment variables available to a Pod?
response: The command 'kubectl exec <pod_name> -- printenv | grep SERVICE' can be used to verify the environment variables available to a Pod, where <pod_name> is the name of the Pod.
title: Connecting Applications with Services — Accessing the Service

instruction: What are the primary purposes of the connections from the API server to the kubelet in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The connections from the API server to the kubelet are used for fetching logs for pods, attaching to running pods, and providing the kubelet's port-forwarding functionality.
title: Communication between Nodes and the Control Plane — API server to kubelet

instruction: How does the API server verify the kubelet's serving certificate by default, and what are the security implications of this default behavior?
response: By default, the API server does not verify the kubelet's serving certificate, making the connection subject to man-in-the-middle attacks and unsafe to run over untrusted and/or public networks.
title: Communication between Nodes and the Control Plane — API server to kubelet

instruction: What measures can be taken to secure the connection between the API server and the kubelet if verifying the kubelet's serving certificate is not possible?
response: If verifying the kubelet's serving certificate is not possible, SSH tunneling can be used between the API server and kubelet to avoid connecting over an untrusted or public network. Additionally, enabling Kubelet authentication and/or authorization can help secure the kubelet API.
title: Communication between Nodes and the Control Plane — API server to kubelet

instruction: How can the API server be configured to verify the kubelet's serving certificate, enhancing the security of their connection?
response: The API server can be configured to verify the kubelet's serving certificate by using the --kubelet-certificate-authority flag to provide a root certificate bundle for verification purposes.
title: Communication between Nodes and the Control Plane — API server to kubelet

instruction: How are individual fields in maps overridden in Kubelet configuration directory merging?
response: Individual fields in maps can be selectively overridden, regardless of their value types. However, for map[string][]string, the entire list associated with a specific field gets overridden.
title: Kubelet Configuration Directory Merging — Maps, including Nested Structures

instruction: What happens when a field like featureGates or staticPodURLHeader is overridden in a file in the --config-dir directory?
response: The entire list associated with a specific field gets overridden for map[string][]string fields like staticPodURLHeader, but for other fields like featureGates, individual values can be selectively overridden.
title: Kubelet Configuration Directory Merging — Maps, including Nested Structures

instruction: How are nested structures in Kubelet configuration files merged?
response: Nested structures in Kubelet configuration files are merged by overriding individual fields. For example, if a nested field like featureGates.MemoryQoS is overridden in a file in the --config-dir directory, the resulting configuration will contain the overridden value.
title: Kubelet Configuration Directory Merging — Maps, including Nested Structures

instruction: What is the resulting configuration when a main Kubelet configuration file and a file in the --config-dir directory are merged?
response: The resulting configuration will contain the merged values from both files. Fields that are not present in the main configuration file but are present in the file in the --config-dir directory will be added to the resulting configuration, and fields that are present in both files will be overridden by the values in the file in the --config-dir directory.
title: Kubelet Configuration Directory Merging — Maps, including Nested Structures

instruction: How can you create an nginx Deployment in Kubernetes?
response: You can create an nginx Deployment in Kubernetes by running the command 'kubectl create deployment nginx --image=nginx'. This command creates a Deployment with an nginx Pod.
title: Declare Network Policy — Create annginxdeployment and expose it via a service

instruction: What command is used to expose a Deployment through a Service in Kubernetes?
response: The command 'kubectl expose deployment <deployment_name> --port=<port_number>' is used to expose a Deployment through a Service in Kubernetes. For example, 'kubectl expose deployment nginx --port=80' exposes the nginx Deployment through a Service named nginx on port 80.
title: Declare Network Policy — Create annginxdeployment and expose it via a service

instruction: Where can you find the namespace of a Deployment and its corresponding Pod in Kubernetes?
response: The namespace of a Deployment and its corresponding Pod can be found by running the command 'kubectl get svc,pod'. This command displays the namespace, name, cluster IP, external IP, port, and age of the Service and Pod.
title: Declare Network Policy — Create annginxdeployment and expose it via a service

instruction: How can you limit the total sum of storage for volumes that can be requested in a given namespace?
response: You can limit the total sum of storage for volumes by setting a quota for the 'requests.storage' resource in the namespace.
title: Resource Quotas — Quota for storage

instruction: Can you limit consumption of storage resources based on associated StorageClass, and if so, how?
response: Yes, you can limit consumption of storage resources based on associated StorageClass by setting a quota for the '<storage-class-name>.storageclass.storage.k8s.io/requests.storage' resource, where <storage-class-name> is the name of the StorageClass.
title: Resource Quotas — Quota for storage

instruction: What happens to container logs when using a CRI container runtime in relation to ephemeral storage quotas?
response: When using a CRI container runtime, container logs will count against the ephemeral storage quota, which can result in the unexpected eviction of pods that have exhausted their storage quotas.
title: Resource Quotas — Quota for storage

instruction: How do you define a quota for separate StorageClasses, such as 'gold' and 'bronze'?
response: You can define a quota for separate StorageClasses by setting a quota for the '<storage-class-name>.storageclass.storage.k8s.io/requests.storage' resource, for example: 'gold.storageclass.storage.k8s.io/requests.storage: 500Gi' and 'bronze.storageclass.storage.k8s.io/requests.storage: 100Gi'.
title: Resource Quotas — Quota for storage

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' option in the DELETE /apis/apiextensions.k8s.io/v1/customresourcedefinitions request?
response: The continue option should be set when retrieving more results from the server. Since this value is server defined, clients may only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters (except for the value of continue) and the server may reject a continue value it does not recognize.
title: CustomResourceDefinition — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential' to true in the DELETE /apis/apiextensions.k8s.io/v1/customresourcedefinitions request?
response: If set to true, it will trigger an unsafe deletion of the resource in case the normal deletion flow fails with a corrupt object error. A resource is considered corrupt if it can not be retrieved from the underlying storage successfully because of a) its data can not be transformed e.g. decryption failure, or b) it fails to decode into an object. NOTE: unsafe deletion ignores finalizer constraints, skips precondition checks, and removes the object from the storage.
title: CustomResourceDefinition — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'propagationPolicy' field in the DELETE /apis/apiextensions.k8s.io/v1/customresourcedefinitions request?
response: The propagationPolicy field determines whether and how garbage collection will be performed. Acceptable values are: 'Orphan' - orphan the dependents; 'Background' - allow the garbage collector to delete the dependents in the background; 'Foreground' - a cascading policy that deletes all dependents in the foreground.
title: CustomResourceDefinition — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'gracePeriodSeconds' to zero in the DELETE /apis/apiextensions.k8s.io/v1/customresourcedefinitions request?
response: If 'gracePeriodSeconds' is set to zero, it means delete immediately. The value zero indicates delete immediately.
title: CustomResourceDefinition — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: How can I authenticate to a private container image repository for a Kubernetes Pod?
response: You can configure image pull Secrets to authenticate to a private repository. These Secrets are configured at the Pod level using the imagePullSecrets field, which is a list of references to Secrets in the same namespace.
title: Secrets — Container image pull Secrets

instruction: What is the purpose of the imagePullSecrets field in a Kubernetes Pod?
response: The imagePullSecrets field is used to pass a Secret that contains a Docker (or other) image registry password to the kubelet, allowing it to pull a private image on behalf of the Pod.
title: Secrets — Container image pull Secrets

instruction: Can I manually create imagePullSecrets and reference them from a ServiceAccount?
response: Yes, you can manually create imagePullSecrets and reference these from a ServiceAccount. Any Pods created with that ServiceAccount will have their imagePullSecrets field set to that of the service account.
title: Secrets — Container image pull Secrets

instruction: Where can I find more information about specifying imagePullSecrets and the imagePullSecrets field?
response: You can find more information about specifying imagePullSecrets from the container images documentation and the PodSpec API.
title: Secrets — Container image pull Secrets

instruction: What is a Node in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: A Node is a worker machine in Kubernetes and may be either a virtual or a physical machine, depending on the cluster.
title: Viewing Pods and Nodes — Nodes

instruction: What components run on every Kubernetes Node?
response: Every Kubernetes Node runs at least: Kubelet, a process responsible for communication between the Kubernetes control plane and the Node, and a container runtime (like Docker) responsible for pulling the container image from a registry, unpacking the container, and running the application.
title: Viewing Pods and Nodes — Nodes

instruction: How does the Kubernetes control plane handle scheduling of pods across Nodes in the cluster?
response: The Kubernetes control plane automatically handles scheduling the pods across the Nodes in the cluster, taking into account the available resources on each Node.
title: Viewing Pods and Nodes — Nodes

instruction: How can an application provide additional feedback or signals into PodStatus in Kubernetes?
response: An application can inject extra feedback or signals into PodStatus by setting readinessGates in the Pod's spec to specify a list of additional conditions that the kubelet evaluates for Pod readiness.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Pod readiness

instruction: What happens if Kubernetes cannot find a condition in the status.conditions field of a Pod?
response: If Kubernetes cannot find such a condition in the status.conditions field of a Pod, the status of the condition is defaulted to 'False'.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Pod readiness

instruction: What is the requirement for the names of the Pod conditions that are added using readinessGates?
response: The Pod conditions you add must have names that meet the Kubernetes label key format.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Pod readiness

instruction: What command is used to create a new namespace in Kubernetes for isolating resources?
response: The command kubectl create namespace <namespace_name> is used to create a new namespace, for example: kubectl create namespace quota-mem-cpu-example.
title: Configure Memory and CPU Quotas for a Namespace — Create a namespace

instruction: How do you create a ResourceQuota in a specific namespace using a YAML manifest file?
response: You can create a ResourceQuota by applying a YAML manifest file using the command kubectl apply -f <yaml_file_url> --namespace=<namespace_name>, for example: kubectl apply -f https://k8s.io/examples/admin/resource/quota-mem-cpu.yaml --namespace=quota-mem-cpu-example.
title: Configure Memory and CPU Quotas for a Namespace — Create a namespace

instruction: What are the requirements imposed by the example ResourceQuota on the namespace?
response: The ResourceQuota requires each container in the namespace to have a memory request, memory limit, CPU request, and CPU limit. Additionally, it enforces the following totals for all Pods in the namespace: memory requests must not exceed 1 GiB, memory limits must not exceed 2 GiB, CPU requests must not exceed 1 CPU, and CPU limits must not exceed 2 CPU.
title: Configure Memory and CPU Quotas for a Namespace — Create a namespace

instruction: How can you view detailed information about a ResourceQuota in a specific namespace?
response: You can view detailed information about a ResourceQuota using the command kubectl get resourcequota <resourcequota_name> --namespace=<namespace_name> --output=yaml, for example: kubectl get resourcequota mem-cpu-demo --namespace=quota-mem-cpu-example --output=yaml.
title: Configure Memory and CPU Quotas for a Namespace — Create a namespace

instruction: What API groups and versions are available in Kubernetes?
response: The available API groups and their versions include admissionregistration.k8s.io (v1, v1beta1, v1alpha1), apiextensions.k8s.io (v1), apiregistration.k8s.io (v1), apps (v1), authentication.k8s.io (v1), authorization.k8s.io (v1), autoscaling (v2, v1), batch (v1), certificates.k8s.io (v1, v1beta1, v1alpha1), coordination.k8s.io (v1, v1beta1, v1alpha2), core (v1), discovery.k8s.io (v1), events.k8s.io (v1), flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io (v1), internal.apiserver.k8s.io (v1alpha1), networking.k8s.io (v1, v1beta1), node.k8s.io (v1), policy (v1), rbac.authorization.k8s.io (v1), resource.k8s.io (v1, v1beta2, v1beta1, v1alpha3), scheduling.k8s.io (v1, v1alpha2), storage.k8s.io (v1, v1beta1), and storagemigration.k8s.io (v1beta1).
title: API Groups

instruction: Which API group has the most versions available?
response: The coordination.k8s.io API group has the most versions available, with v1, v1beta1, and v1alpha2. However, the resource.k8s.io API group also has multiple versions available, including v1, v1beta2, v1beta1, and v1alpha3, making it the API group with the most versions.
title: API Groups

instruction: What version of the autoscaling API group is recommended for use?
response: The available versions for the autoscaling API group are v2 and v1, but the recommended version is not specified in the provided text. However, based on the versioning, v2 is likely the more recent and recommended version.
title: API Groups

instruction: What happens to the IP addresses of Pods in a StatefulSet when they are deleted and recreated?
response: The IP addresses associated with the Pods may have changed. This is why it is important not to configure other applications to connect to Pods in a StatefulSet by the IP address of a particular Pod, but instead to connect to Pods by resolving their hostname.
title: StatefulSet Basics — Using stable network identities

instruction: How can an application find and connect to the active members of a StatefulSet?
response: The application should query the CNAME of the headless Service. The SRV records associated with the CNAME will contain only the Pods in the StatefulSet that are Running and Ready. Alternatively, if the application wants to find any healthy Pod in a StatefulSet, it can connect to the IP address of a type: ClusterIP Service backed by the Pods in that StatefulSet.
title: StatefulSet Basics — Using stable network identities

instruction: What type of Service can be used to connect to any healthy Pod in a StatefulSet without needing to track each specific Pod?
response: A type: ClusterIP Service backed by the Pods in the StatefulSet can be used. This can be the same Service that tracks the StatefulSet (specified in the serviceName of the StatefulSet) or a separate Service that selects the right set of Pods.
title: StatefulSet Basics — Using stable network identities

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' option in the DELETE /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/ingresses request?
response: The continue option should be set when retrieving more results from the server. Since this value is server defined, clients may only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters (except for the value of continue) and the server may reject a continue value it does not recognize.
title: Ingress — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential' to true in the DELETE request?
response: If set to true, it will trigger an unsafe deletion of the resource in case the normal deletion flow fails with a corrupt object error. A resource is considered corrupt if it can not be retrieved from the underlying storage successfully. NOTE: unsafe deletion ignores finalizer constraints, skips precondition checks, and removes the object from the storage.
title: Ingress — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'gracePeriodSeconds' parameter in the DELETE request?
response: The duration in seconds before the object should be deleted. Value must be non-negative integer. The value zero indicates delete immediately. If this value is nil, the default grace period for the specified type will be used.
title: Ingress — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the difference between 'orphanDependents' and 'propagationPolicy' in the DELETE request?
response: The 'orphanDependents' field is deprecated and should not be used. Instead, the 'propagationPolicy' field should be used to specify whether and how garbage collection will be performed. Acceptable values are: 'Orphan' - orphan the dependents; 'Background' - allow the garbage collector to delete the dependents in the background; 'Foreground' - a cascading policy that deletes all dependents in the foreground.
title: Ingress — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: How can I use a Service to access an application within a Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can learn more about using a Service to access an application in a cluster by following the provided documentation link.
title: Connecting Applications with Services — What's next

instruction: What is the recommended approach to connect a front end to a back end in a Kubernetes environment?
response: The recommended approach is to use a Service to connect the front end to the back end, and you can find more information about this in the provided documentation.
title: Connecting Applications with Services — What's next

instruction: How can I create an external load balancer in a Kubernetes environment?
response: You can learn more about creating an external load balancer by following the provided documentation link.
title: Connecting Applications with Services — What's next

instruction: How can you add additional entries to a Pod's /etc/hosts file in Kubernetes?
response: You can add additional entries to a Pod's /etc/hosts file by configuring HostAliases for a Pod under .spec.hostAliases in the Pod's configuration.
title: Adding entries to Pod /etc/hosts with HostAliases — Adding additional entries with hostAliases

instruction: What is the format for specifying hostAliases in a Pod's configuration?
response: The format for specifying hostAliases is a list of objects with 'ip' and 'hostnames' fields, where 'ip' is the IP address and 'hostnames' is a list of hostnames to resolve to that IP address.
title: Adding entries to Pod /etc/hosts with HostAliases — Adding additional entries with hostAliases

instruction: How can you verify that the hostAliases have been applied to a Pod's /etc/hosts file?
response: You can verify that the hostAliases have been applied by running 'kubectl logs <pod-name>' to view the contents of the Pod's /etc/hosts file, which will include the additional entries specified by the hostAliases at the bottom.
title: Adding entries to Pod /etc/hosts with HostAliases — Adding additional entries with hostAliases

instruction: What command is used to convert a Docker Compose file to Kubernetes resources?
response: The command 'kompose convert' is used to convert a Docker Compose file to Kubernetes resources.
title: Translate a Docker Compose File to Kubernetes Resources — Use Kompose

instruction: How do you apply the converted Kubernetes resources to a cluster?
response: You can apply the converted Kubernetes resources to a cluster by running the command 'kubectl apply -f <output file>'.
title: Translate a Docker Compose File to Kubernetes Resources — Use Kompose

instruction: How do you access your application after deploying it to a Kubernetes cluster using Kompose?
response: You can access your application by using the 'minikube service <service-name>' command if you're using Minikube, or by looking up the IP address of your service using 'kubectl describe svc <service-name>' and then accessing it using 'curl http://<IP-address>'.
title: Translate a Docker Compose File to Kubernetes Resources — Use Kompose

instruction: How do you clean up the resources used by the example application deployment?
response: You can clean up the resources used by the example application deployment by running the command 'kubectl delete -f <output files>'.
title: Translate a Docker Compose File to Kubernetes Resources — Use Kompose

instruction: Where does kubeadm write static Pod manifest files for control plane components?
response: Kubeadm writes static Pod manifest files for control plane components to /etc/kubernetes/manifests. The kubelet watches this directory for Pods to be created on startup.
title: Implementation details — Generate static Pod manifests for control plane components

instruction: What are the common properties shared by all static Pods?
response: All static Pods are deployed on the kube-system namespace, get tier:control-plane and component:{component-name} labels, use the system-node-critical priority class, and have hostNetwork: true set to allow control plane startup before a network is configured.
title: Implementation details — Generate static Pod manifests for control plane components

instruction: How does the controller-manager and scheduler refer to the API server?
response: The controller-manager and scheduler use the address 127.0.0.1 to refer to the API server, as hostNetwork: true is set on all static Pods to allow control plane startup before a network is configured.
title: Implementation details — Generate static Pod manifests for control plane components

instruction: What parameters affect the static Pod manifest for the API server?
response: The static Pod manifest for the API server is affected by parameters such as apiserver-advertise-address, apiserver-bind-port, service-cluster-ip-range, etcd-servers address, and related TLS settings, as well as cloud provider parameters if specified.
title: Implementation details — Generate static Pod manifests for control plane components

instruction: Where does kubeadm install the configuration file for how systemd should run the kubelet?
response: The configuration file is written to /usr/lib/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf and is used by systemd to augment the basic kubelet.service.
title: Configuring each kubelet in your cluster using kubeadm — The kubelet drop-in file for systemd

instruction: How can I override the default kubelet configuration settings provided by kubeadm?
response: You can override the default settings by creating a directory /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/ and placing your customizations in a file there, such as local-overrides.conf. This allows you to add new settings or override the unit settings configured by kubeadm.
title: Configuring each kubelet in your cluster using kubeadm — The kubelet drop-in file for systemd

instruction: What is the purpose of the EnvironmentFile=-/var/lib/kubelet/kubeadm-flags.env line in the 10-kubeadm.conf file?
response: The EnvironmentFile=-/var/lib/kubelet/kubeadm-flags.env line sources a file that kubeadm init and kubeadm join generate at runtime, populating the KUBELET_KUBEADM_ARGS variable dynamically. This file contains dynamic environment settings for the kubelet.
title: Configuring each kubelet in your cluster using kubeadm — The kubelet drop-in file for systemd

instruction: What is the order of precedence for kubelet configuration settings, and which setting takes the highest priority?
response: The order of precedence for kubelet configuration settings is determined by the flag chain, with KUBELET_EXTRA_ARGS having the highest priority in the event of conflicting settings. This means that user-specified flag overrides with KUBELET_EXTRA_ARGS, sourced from /etc/default/kubelet (for DEBs) or /etc/sysconfig/kubelet (for RPMs), will take precedence over other settings.
title: Configuring each kubelet in your cluster using kubeadm — The kubelet drop-in file for systemd

instruction: What is the definition of 'Allocatable' on a Kubernetes node?
response: Allocatable on a Kubernetes node is defined as the amount of compute resources that are available for pods.
title: Reserve Compute Resources for System Daemons — Node Allocatable

instruction: Which compute resources are supported for Node Allocatable as of now?
response: CPU, memory, and ephemeral-storage are supported as of now.
title: Reserve Compute Resources for System Daemons — Node Allocatable

instruction: How is Node Allocatable exposed in the Kubernetes API and CLI?
response: Node Allocatable is exposed as part of the v1.Node object in the API and as part of kubectl describe node in the CLI.
title: Reserve Compute Resources for System Daemons — Node Allocatable

instruction: Which component is responsible for reserving resources for system daemons?
response: The kubelet is responsible for reserving resources for two categories of system daemons.
title: Reserve Compute Resources for System Daemons — Node Allocatable

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubectl set env command?
response: The kubectl set env command is used to update environment variables on a pod template.
title: kubectl set — See Also

instruction: How can you update the image of a pod template using kubectl?
response: You can update the image of a pod template using the kubectl set image command.
title: kubectl set — See Also

instruction: What is the function of the kubectl set resources command?
response: The kubectl set resources command is used to update resource requests/limits on objects with pod templates.
title: kubectl set — See Also

instruction: What does the kubectl set serviceaccount command do?
response: The kubectl set serviceaccount command updates the service account of a resource.
title: kubectl set — See Also

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' option in the CSIStorageCapacity getList API?
response: The continue option should be set when retrieving more results from the server. Since this value is server defined, clients may only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters (except for the value of continue) and the server may reject a continue value it does not recognize.
title: CSIStorageCapacity — getList

instruction: How does the 'limit' parameter affect the response of the CSIStorageCapacity getList API?
response: The limit parameter is a maximum number of responses to return for a list call. If more items exist, the server will set the `continue` field on the list metadata to a value that can be used with the same initial query to retrieve the next set of results. Setting a limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items (up to zero items) in the event all requested objects are filtered out.
title: CSIStorageCapacity — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'sendInitialEvents' option in the CSIStorageCapacity getList API?
response: The `sendInitialEvents=true` option may be set together with `watch=true`. In that case, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent. The bookmark will report the ResourceVersion (RV) corresponding to the set of objects, and be marked with `'k8s.io/initial-events-end': 'true'` annotation.
title: CSIStorageCapacity — getList

instruction: How does the 'resourceVersionMatch' option interact with the 'sendInitialEvents' option in the CSIStorageCapacity getList API?
response: When `sendInitialEvents` option is set, we require `resourceVersionMatch` option to also be set. The semantic of the watch request is as following: `resourceVersionMatch` = NotOlderThan is interpreted as 'data at least as new as the provided `resourceVersion`' and the bookmark event is send when the state is synced to a `resourceVersion` at least as fresh as the one provided by the ListOptions.
title: CSIStorageCapacity — getList

instruction: What types of certificates can kubelets obtain using the CertificateSigningRequest API?
response: Kubelets can obtain client certificates to authenticate to kube-apiserver (with the 'kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client-kubelet' signerName) and serving certificates for TLS endpoints kube-apiserver can connect to securely (with the 'kubernetes.io/kubelet-serving' signerName).
title: CertificateSigningRequest

instruction: What are the possible signerNames that can be used to request client certificates using the CertificateSigningRequest API?
response: The possible signerNames that can be used to request client certificates are 'kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client-kubelet' for kubelets, 'kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client' for other clients, and custom non-Kubernetes signers.
title: CertificateSigningRequest

instruction: What is the purpose of the `continue` field in the GET /api/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/pods/{name} request?
response: The `continue` field is used to retrieve more results from the server. It should be set when retrieving more results from the server, and its value is server-defined. If the specified `continue` value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token.
title: Pod — getWatch

instruction: How does the `limit` field affect the response of the GET /api/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/pods/{name} request?
response: The `limit` field sets a maximum number of responses to return for a list call. If more items exist, the server will set the `continue` field on the list metadata to a value that can be used with the same initial query to retrieve the next set of results. Setting a limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items in the event all requested objects are filtered out.
title: Pod — getWatch

instruction: What is the purpose of the `sendInitialEvents` field in the GET /api/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/pods/{name} request?
response: The `sendInitialEvents` field is used to send synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection when `watch=true`. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent, and the watch stream will proceed as usual, sending watch events corresponding to changes to objects watched.
title: Pod — getWatch

instruction: How does the `resourceVersionMatch` field affect the response of the GET /api/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/pods/{name} request?
response: The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set. The semantic of the watch request depends on the value of `resourceVersionMatch`, which can be set to 'NotOlderThan' or other values.
title: Pod — getWatch

instruction: How can I check if the current user has permission to create deployments in the 'dev' namespace using kubectl?
response: You can use the 'kubectl auth can-i' subcommand, for example: 'kubectl auth can-i create deployments --namespace dev'. This command uses the SelfSubjectAccessReview API to determine if the current user can perform the given action.
title: Authorization — Checking API access

instruction: Can I use kubectl to check the permissions of another user or a ServiceAccount?
response: Yes, you can use the '--as' flag with 'kubectl auth can-i' to impersonate another user or a ServiceAccount. For example, to check if a ServiceAccount named 'dev-sa' in the 'dev' namespace can list Pods in the 'target' namespace, you can use: 'kubectl auth can-i list pods --namespace target --as system:serviceaccount:dev:dev-sa'.
title: Authorization — Checking API access

instruction: What is the SelfSubjectAccessReview API and how can it be used to check API authorization?
response: The SelfSubjectAccessReview API is part of the authorization.k8s.io API group, which exposes the API server authorization to external services. You can create a SelfSubjectAccessReview resource to query the API server authorization. For example, you can create a YAML file with the SelfSubjectAccessReview specification and use 'kubectl create' to send the query to the API server. The response status field of the returned object will contain the result of the query.
title: Authorization — Checking API access

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` flag in the ReplicationController watch API?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` flag requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: ReplicationController — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: How does the `continue` option work when retrieving more results from the server in the ReplicationController list API?
response: The `continue` option should be set when retrieving more results from the server. The value is server-defined, and clients may only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token.
title: ReplicationController — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the purpose of the `sendInitialEvents` option in the ReplicationController watch API?
response: The `sendInitialEvents` option, when set to `true`, will begin the watch stream with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent, and the watch stream will proceed as usual, sending watch events corresponding to changes to objects watched.
title: ReplicationController — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: How does the `resourceVersionMatch` option affect the behavior of the ReplicationController list API?
response: The `resourceVersionMatch` option determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set. The semantic of the watch request depends on the value of `resourceVersionMatch`, which can be set to 'NotOlderThan' or other values.
title: ReplicationController — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the first step in rotating a decryption key for Kubernetes without incurring downtime?
response: Generate a new key and add it as the second key entry for the current provider on all control plane nodes.
title: Encrypting Confidential Data at Rest — Rotate a decryption key

instruction: What happens if all copies of the new encryption key are lost?
response: If you lose all copies of this key you would need to delete all the resources that were encrypted under the lost key, and workloads may not operate as expected during the time that at-rest encryption is broken.
title: Encrypting Confidential Data at Rest — Rotate a decryption key

instruction: How do you update existing Secrets to use the new encryption key?
response: As a privileged user, run 'kubectl get secrets --all-namespaces -o json | kubectl replace -f -' to encrypt all existing Secrets with the new key.
title: Encrypting Confidential Data at Rest — Rotate a decryption key

instruction: What is the final step after updating all existing Secrets to use the new key and making a secure backup of the new key?
response: Remove the old decryption key from the configuration.
title: Encrypting Confidential Data at Rest — Rotate a decryption key

instruction: What is the typical port used by the API server for secure HTTPS connections in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The API server is configured to listen for remote connections on a secure HTTPS port, typically 443.
title: Communication between Nodes and the Control Plane

instruction: How can pods securely connect to the API server in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Pods can securely connect to the API server by leveraging a service account, which allows Kubernetes to automatically inject the public root certificate and a valid bearer token into the pod when it is instantiated.
title: Communication between Nodes and the Control Plane

instruction: What is the recommended approach for provisioning client credentials to the kubelet for secure connection to the API server?
response: A good approach is that the client credentials provided to the kubelet are in the form of a client certificate, which can be automated through kubelet TLS bootstrapping.
title: Communication between Nodes and the Control Plane

instruction: How do control plane components communicate with the API server in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The control plane components communicate with the API server over the secure port, which is the same port used by nodes and pods, ensuring that all communication with the API server is secured by default.
title: Communication between Nodes and the Control Plane

instruction: How are health check requests from local kubelets to kube-apiservers treated by default in terms of API priority and fairness?
response: By default, health check requests from local kubelets to kube-apiservers are assigned to the global-default FlowSchema and the corresponding global-default priority level, where they can be crowded out by other traffic.
title: API Priority and Fairness — Health check concurrency exemption

instruction: What configuration change can be made to exempt health check requests from rate limiting?
response: Adding a FlowSchema with a specific configuration, such as the one named 'health-for-strangers', can exempt health check requests from rate limiting. This FlowSchema should have a matchingPrecedence, a priorityLevelConfiguration set to 'exempt', and specific rules for nonResourceURLs like '/healthz', '/livez', and '/readyz' with verbs set to '*' and subjects set to 'system:unauthenticated'.
title: API Priority and Fairness — Health check concurrency exemption

instruction: Which specific URLs are exempt from rate limiting in the 'health-for-strangers' FlowSchema?
response: The '/healthz', '/livez', and '/readyz' URLs are exempt from rate limiting in the 'health-for-strangers' FlowSchema, for all verbs and for the 'system:unauthenticated' group.
title: API Priority and Fairness — Health check concurrency exemption

instruction: What are the different authorization modes available in Kubernetes and how are they specified?
response: Kubernetes provides several authorization modes, including ABAC (Attribute-based access control), RBAC (Role-based access control), Node, Webhook, AlwaysAllow, and AlwaysDeny. These modes can be specified using the --authorization-mode command line argument, and multiple modes can be combined, separated by commas (e.g., --authorization-mode=Node,RBAC,Webhook).
title: Authorization — Command line authorization mode configuration

instruction: How does Kubernetes prioritize authorization modules when multiple modes are specified?
response: When multiple authorization modes are specified, Kubernetes checks them in the order they are listed on the API server's command line. An earlier module has higher priority to allow or deny a request.
title: Authorization — Command line authorization mode configuration

instruction: Can the --authorization-mode command line argument be used in conjunction with the --authorization-config argument?
response: No, the --authorization-mode command line argument cannot be combined with the --authorization-config command line argument, which is used for configuring authorization using a local file.
title: Authorization — Command line authorization mode configuration

instruction: How does Kubernetes determine the username from a client certificate?
response: Kubernetes determines the username from the commonName field in the subject of the certificate. For example, commonName=bob represents a user with username 'bob'.
title: Authenticating — X.509 client certificates

instruction: What are the requirements for a client certificate to be valid for authentication in Kubernetes?
response: The certificate must be valid based on the X.509 notBefore and notAfter attributes, have an extended key usage that includes client authentication (ClientAuth), and contain a commonName (OID 2.5.4.3) attribute.
title: Authenticating — X.509 client certificates

instruction: How can a user's group memberships be indicated in a client certificate?
response: Client certificates can indicate a user's group memberships using the certificate's organization fields. To include multiple group memberships for a user, include multiple organization fields in the certificate. For example, a distinguished name for a certificate might be CN=Ada Lovelace,O=Users,O=Staff,O=Programmers, which would place the user into the groups 'Programmers', 'Staff', 'system:authenticated', and 'Users'.
title: Authenticating — X.509 client certificates

instruction: What is the purpose of the AllowParsingUserUIDFromCertAuth feature gate in Kubernetes?
response: The AllowParsingUserUIDFromCertAuth feature gate allows Kubernetes to parse an optional user UID from a certificate. The feature gate is enabled by default and requires the certificate to have the attribute 1.3.6.1.4.1.57683.2 included.
title: Authenticating — X.509 client certificates

instruction: What is the purpose of the PodReadyToStartContainers condition in a Pod's lifecycle?
response: The PodReadyToStartContainers condition indicates whether a Pod has a runtime sandbox with networking configured, allowing the kubelet to start pulling container images and create containers.
title: Pod Conditions — PodReadyToStartContainers

instruction: When is the PodReadyToStartContainers condition set to False by the kubelet?
response: The PodReadyToStartContainers condition is set to False when the kubelet detects that a Pod does not have a runtime sandbox with networking configured, which can occur early in the Pod's lifecycle or after the node or Pod sandbox virtual machine reboots without the Pod getting evicted.
title: Pod Conditions — PodReadyToStartContainers

instruction: How does the PodReadyToStartContainers condition affect the initialization of a Pod with init containers?
response: For a Pod with init containers, the kubelet sets the Initialized condition to True after the init containers have successfully completed, which happens after successful sandbox creation and network configuration by the runtime plugin.
title: Pod Conditions — PodReadyToStartContainers

instruction: What is the relationship between the PodReadyToStartContainers condition and the initialization of a Pod without init containers?
response: For a Pod without init containers, the kubelet sets the Initialized condition to True before sandbox creation and network configuration starts, which is before the PodReadyToStartContainers condition is set to True.
title: Pod Conditions — PodReadyToStartContainers

instruction: What are the possible results of a probe execution in Kubernetes?
response: Each probe has one of three results. If a container does not provide a particular probe, the kubelet always considers the result as Success. For readiness probes specifically, the result is considered Failure before the initial delay.
title: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes — Check mechanisms

instruction: What happens if a container does not provide a particular probe?
response: If a container does not provide a particular probe, the kubelet always considers the result as Success. However, for readiness probes specifically, the result is considered Failure before the initial delay.
title: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes — Check mechanisms

instruction: How does the kubelet handle the result of each probe execution?
response: The kubelet evaluates the result of each probe execution and takes action accordingly.
title: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes — Check mechanisms

instruction: What is the purpose of the `continue` option in the ClusterTrustBundle watch request?
response: The `continue` option is used to retrieve more results from the server. It should be set to the value returned by the server in the previous query result, and it allows the client to retrieve the next set of results. If the specified `continue` value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: ClusterTrustBundle — getWatch

instruction: How does the `sendInitialEvents` option affect the watch stream in the ClusterTrustBundle watch request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents` is set to `true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent, and then the watch stream will proceed as usual, sending watch events corresponding to changes to objects watched.
title: ClusterTrustBundle — getWatch

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersionMatch` option in the ClusterTrustBundle watch request?
response: The `resourceVersionMatch` option determines how the `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set. The `resourceVersionMatch` option is used in conjunction with the `sendInitialEvents` option to determine the semantic of the watch request.
title: ClusterTrustBundle — getWatch

instruction: How does the `limit` option affect the results of the ClusterTrustBundle watch request?
response: The `limit` option sets a maximum number of responses to return for a list call. If more items exist, the server will set the `continue` field on the list metadata to a value that can be used with the same initial query to retrieve the next set of results. Setting a `limit` may return fewer than the requested amount of items if all requested objects are filtered out.
title: ClusterTrustBundle — getWatch

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' option in the CSINode getList API?
response: The continue option is used to retrieve more results from the server. It should be set when retrieving more results from the server, and its value is server-defined. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token.
title: CSINode — getList

instruction: How does the 'limit' parameter affect the CSINode getList API response?
response: The limit parameter sets a maximum number of responses to return for a list call. If more items exist, the server will set the 'continue' field on the list metadata to a value that can be used with the same initial query to retrieve the next set of results. Setting a limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items in the event all requested objects are filtered out.
title: CSINode — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'sendInitialEvents' option in the CSINode getList API?
response: The sendInitialEvents option is used to produce the current state of objects in the collection when watch=true. It sends synthetic events to produce the current state of objects, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event, and then proceeds with watch events corresponding to changes to objects watched.
title: CSINode — getList

instruction: How does the 'resourceVersionMatch' parameter interact with the 'sendInitialEvents' option in the CSINode getList API?
response: When sendInitialEvents is set, the resourceVersionMatch option must also be set. The semantic of the watch request depends on the value of resourceVersionMatch, which determines how resourceVersion is applied to list calls. If resourceVersionMatch is set to NotOlderThan, it is interpreted as 'data at least as new as the provided resourceVersion', and the bookmark event is sent when the state is synced to a resourceVersion at least as fresh as the one provided.
title: CSINode — getList

instruction: What happens if the PostStart lifecycle handler fails?
response: If the PostStart lifecycle handler fails, the container is terminated and restarted according to its restart policy.
title: Pod — Lifecycle

instruction: Under what circumstances is the PreStop lifecycle handler not called?
response: The PreStop handler is not called if the container crashes or exits.
title: Pod — Lifecycle

instruction: What determines the default signal sent to a container when it is being stopped if StopSignal is not specified?
response: The default signal sent to a container when it is being stopped, if StopSignal is not specified, is defined by the container runtime in use.
title: Pod — Lifecycle

instruction: Can StopSignal be set for Pods with an empty .spec.os.name?
response: No, StopSignal can only be set for Pods with a non-empty .spec.os.name.
title: Pod — Lifecycle

instruction: How can you aggregate several ClusterRoles into one combined ClusterRole in Kubernetes?
response: You can aggregate several ClusterRoles into one combined ClusterRole by using an aggregationRule that defines a label selector. The controller watches for ClusterRole objects with an aggregationRule set and combines the rules of the matched ClusterRoles into the rules field of the aggregated ClusterRole.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Aggregated ClusterRoles
