instruction: What happens when you create a new ClusterRole that matches the label selector of an existing aggregated ClusterRole?
response: When you create a new ClusterRole that matches the label selector of an existing aggregated ClusterRole, the new rules are automatically added to the aggregated ClusterRole by the control plane.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Aggregated ClusterRoles

instruction: How can you extend the default user-facing roles in Kubernetes to include rules for custom resources?
response: You can extend the default user-facing roles in Kubernetes to include rules for custom resources by using ClusterRole aggregation. This allows you to include rules for custom resources, such as those served by CustomResourceDefinitions or aggregated API servers, to extend the default roles.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Aggregated ClusterRoles

instruction: What is the purpose of the aggregationRule in a ClusterRole, and how does it work?
response: The aggregationRule in a ClusterRole defines a label selector that the controller uses to match other ClusterRole objects that should be combined into the rules field of the aggregated ClusterRole. The controller watches for ClusterRole objects with an aggregationRule set and automatically fills in the rules field of the aggregated ClusterRole with the rules of the matched ClusterRoles.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Aggregated ClusterRoles

instruction: What is the primary function of the kubectl proxy in a Kubernetes environment?
response: The kubectl proxy runs on a user's desktop or in a pod, proxies from a localhost address to the Kubernetes apiserver, locates the apiserver, and adds authentication headers. It uses HTTP to communicate with the client and HTTPS to communicate with the apiserver.
title: Proxies in Kubernetes

instruction: How does the apiserver proxy facilitate communication in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The apiserver proxy is a bastion built into the apiserver that connects a user outside of the cluster to cluster IPs which might not be reachable otherwise. It runs in the apiserver processes, uses HTTPS (or HTTP if configured) to communicate with the client, and can use HTTP or HTTPS to communicate with the target, which can be a Node, Pod, or Service. It also provides load balancing when used to reach a Service.
title: Proxies in Kubernetes

instruction: What is the role of the kube proxy in a Kubernetes cluster, and what protocols does it support?
response: The kube proxy runs on each node in the cluster, proxies UDP, TCP, and SCTP, and provides load balancing. It is only used to reach services and does not understand HTTP.
title: Proxies in Kubernetes

instruction: Under what conditions are Cloud Load Balancers created in a Kubernetes environment, and what protocols do they typically support?
response: Cloud Load Balancers are created automatically when the Kubernetes service has type LoadBalancer. They are provided by some cloud providers and typically support UDP/TCP only, with SCTP support varying by cloud provider implementation.
title: Proxies in Kubernetes

instruction: What should I do if the API server fails to start due to an invalid manifest file at startup?
response: Check API server logs for validation errors, fix the manifest file, and restart the API server.
title: Manifest-Based Admission Control — Troubleshooting

instruction: How can I troubleshoot policies not being enforced after updating a file?
response: Check the automatic_reloads_total{status='failure'} metric and API server logs, fix the manifest, and wait for the next reload cycle.
title: Manifest-Based Admission Control — Troubleshooting

instruction: Why are webhook requests failing in my manifest-based admission control setup?
response: Verify that the URL specified in clientConfig.url is accessible from the kube-apiserver, as the webhook URL may not be reachable.
title: Manifest-Based Admission Control — Troubleshooting

instruction: I'm trying to create an API object with a .static.k8s.io suffix, but it's not working. What's the issue?
response: The .static.k8s.io suffix is reserved for manifest-based configurations when the feature gate is enabled. Use a different name for API-based objects.
title: Manifest-Based Admission Control — Troubleshooting

instruction: How can you determine the mode in which kube-proxy is running on a node?
response: You can query the kube-proxy mode by fetching http://localhost:10249/proxyMode on the node where kube-proxy is running. The output will indicate whether kube-proxy is running in iptables mode or not.
title: Using Source IP — Objectives

instruction: What happens to packets sent to a ClusterIP from within the cluster when kube-proxy is running in iptables mode?
response: Packets sent to ClusterIP from within the cluster are never source NAT'd if you're running kube-proxy in iptables mode. This means that the original source IP address of the packet is preserved.
title: Using Source IP — Objectives

instruction: How can you test source IP preservation by creating a Service over an application?
response: You can test source IP preservation by creating a Service over the source IP app using the command 'kubectl expose deployment source-ip-app --name=clusterip --port=80 --target-port=8080'. Then, you can hit the ClusterIP from a pod in the same cluster and verify that the client_address is the client pod's IP address.
title: Using Source IP — Objectives

instruction: What is the expected output when using wget to query a local webserver through a ClusterIP Service?
response: The expected output will include the client_address, which is the IP address of the client pod. The client_address will be the same as the IP address of the pod from which the wget command is run, whether the client pod and server pod are in the same node or in different nodes.
title: Using Source IP — Objectives

instruction: What issue arises when trying to join a v1.18 Node to a v1.17 cluster using kubeadm?
response: The issue arises due to missing RBAC for the bootstrap-token user to be able to GET a Node object, which was added in v1.18 kubeadm to prevent joining a Node with the same name that already exists.
title: Troubleshooting kubeadm — Not possible to join a v1.18 Node to a v1.17 cluster due to missing RBAC

instruction: How can I workaround the issue of not being able to join a v1.18 Node to a v1.17 cluster created by kubeadm?
response: There are two options to workaround the issue: execute 'kubeadm init phase bootstrap-token' on a control-plane node using kubeadm v1.18, or apply the necessary RBAC manually using 'kubectl apply -f' with the provided ClusterRole and ClusterRoleBinding YAML configuration.
title: Troubleshooting kubeadm — Not possible to join a v1.18 Node to a v1.17 cluster due to missing RBAC

instruction: What is the effect of running 'kubeadm init phase bootstrap-token' on a control-plane node using kubeadm v1.18?
response: Running 'kubeadm init phase bootstrap-token' enables the rest of the bootstrap-token permissions, in addition to allowing a v1.18 Node to join a v1.17 cluster.
title: Troubleshooting kubeadm — Not possible to join a v1.18 Node to a v1.17 cluster due to missing RBAC

instruction: What are the different ways to configure local ephemeral storage on a Kubernetes node?
response: Kubernetes supports three ways to configure local ephemeral storage on a node: Single filesystem, Runtime filesystem, and Split image filesystem.
title: Local ephemeral storage — Configurations for local ephemeral storage

instruction: Where does the kubelet write node-level container logs by default?
response: The kubelet writes logs to files inside its configured log directory, which is /var/log by default, and has a base directory for other locally stored data at /var/lib/kubelet by default.
title: Local ephemeral storage — Configurations for local ephemeral storage

instruction: What is required for the Split image filesystem configuration?
response: The Split image filesystem configuration requires support for the containerfs eviction signals, and the container runtime must support this layout. For details, see node-pressure eviction.
title: Local ephemeral storage — Configurations for local ephemeral storage

instruction: Can the kubelet measure local storage use with any configuration?
response: No, the kubelet can only measure local storage use when the node is set up using one of the supported configurations for local ephemeral storage. If a different configuration is used, the kubelet does not apply resource limits for ephemeral local storage.
title: Local ephemeral storage — Configurations for local ephemeral storage

instruction: What are some scenarios where the Eviction API may return 429 or 500 responses and get stuck?
response: The Eviction API may return 429 or 500 responses and get stuck if, for example, a ReplicaSet creates pods for your application but new pods do not enter a Ready state, or if the last evicted Pod had a long termination grace period.
title: API-initiated Eviction — Troubleshooting stuck evictions

instruction: What steps can be taken to troubleshoot and resolve stuck evictions in Kubernetes?
response: To troubleshoot and resolve stuck evictions, you can try aborting or pausing the automated operation causing the issue and investigate the stuck application before restarting the operation, or wait a while and then directly delete the Pod from your cluster control plane instead of using the Eviction API.
title: API-initiated Eviction — Troubleshooting stuck evictions

instruction: Why might a Pod's termination grace period cause issues with the Eviction API?
response: A Pod's long termination grace period can cause issues with the Eviction API, leading to stuck evictions, because the Eviction API may wait for the Pod to terminate before proceeding, resulting in 429 or 500 responses.
title: API-initiated Eviction — Troubleshooting stuck evictions

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' option in the GET /apis/flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1/prioritylevelconfigurations request?
response: The continue option is used to retrieve more results from the server when the initial response is incomplete. The value of continue is server-defined and can be used to fetch the next set of results with identical query parameters.
title: PriorityLevelConfiguration — getList

instruction: How does the 'limit' parameter affect the response of the GET /apis/flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1/prioritylevelconfigurations request?
response: The limit parameter sets a maximum number of responses to return for a list call. If more items exist, the server will set the 'continue' field on the list metadata to a value that can be used with the same initial query to retrieve the next set of results.
title: PriorityLevelConfiguration — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'sendInitialEvents' option when used with 'watch=true' in the GET /apis/flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1/prioritylevelconfigurations request?
response: The sendInitialEvents option is used to produce the current state of objects in the collection as synthetic events when the watch stream begins. This is followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event, after which the watch stream proceeds as usual, sending watch events corresponding to changes to objects watched.
title: PriorityLevelConfiguration — getList

instruction: How does the 'resourceVersionMatch' parameter affect the response of the GET /apis/flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1/prioritylevelconfigurations request when 'sendInitialEvents' is set to true?
response: When sendInitialEvents is set to true, the resourceVersionMatch parameter determines how the resourceVersion is applied to list calls. If resourceVersionMatch is set to NotOlderThan, the bookmark event is sent when the state is synced to a resourceVersion at least as fresh as the one provided by the ListOptions.
title: PriorityLevelConfiguration — getList

instruction: What is required to migrate to cgroup v2 in a Kubernetes environment?
response: To migrate to cgroup v2, ensure that you meet the requirements, then upgrade to a kernel version that enables cgroup v2 by default. The kubelet automatically detects that the OS is running on cgroup v2 and performs accordingly with no additional configuration required.
title: About cgroup v2 — Migrating to cgroup v2

instruction: How do applications that directly access the cgroup file system need to be updated for cgroup v2?
response: Applications that directly access the cgroup file system need to be updated to newer versions that support cgroup v2, as cgroup v2 uses a different API than cgroup v1. This includes updating third-party monitoring and security agents, as well as specific applications like cAdvisor, Java applications, and Node.js applications.
title: About cgroup v2 — Migrating to cgroup v2

instruction: What versions of Java applications are recommended for use with cgroup v2?
response: For Java applications, prefer to use versions which fully support cgroup v2, such as OpenJDK / HotSpot: jdk8u372, 11.0.16, 15 and later, IBM Semeru Runtimes: 8.0.382.0, 11.0.20.0, 17.0.8.0, and later, and IBM Java: 8.0.8.6 and later.
title: About cgroup v2 — Migrating to cgroup v2

instruction: What is the recommended approach for Node.js applications that do not detect cgroup v2 memory limits?
response: For Node.js applications that do not detect cgroup v2 memory limits, such as versions before v20.3.0, set the heap size explicitly, for example with the --max-old-space-size flag, to avoid incorrectly sized heap and out-of-memory (OOM) terminations.
title: About cgroup v2 — Migrating to cgroup v2

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' option in the ClusterRole getList API?
response: The continue option is used to retrieve more results from the server when the initial response is incomplete. It should be set to the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters, and the server may reject a continue value it does not recognize.
title: ClusterRole — getList

instruction: How does the 'limit' parameter affect the response of the ClusterRole getList API?
response: The limit parameter sets a maximum number of responses to return for a list call. If more items exist, the server will set the 'continue' field on the list metadata to a value that can be used with the same initial query to retrieve the next set of results. Setting a limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items in the event all requested objects are filtered out.
title: ClusterRole — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'sendInitialEvents' option in the ClusterRole getList API?
response: The sendInitialEvents option is used to produce the current state of objects in the collection when watch=true. It sends synthetic events to produce the current state of objects, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event, and then proceeds with the watch stream as usual, sending watch events corresponding to changes to objects watched.
title: ClusterRole — getList

instruction: How does the 'resourceVersionMatch' parameter interact with the 'sendInitialEvents' option in the ClusterRole getList API?
response: When sendInitialEvents is set, the resourceVersionMatch option must also be set. The semantic of the watch request depends on the value of resourceVersionMatch, which determines how the resourceVersion is applied to the list call. If resourceVersionMatch is not set or set to an invalid value, an error is returned.
title: ClusterRole — getList

instruction: What command should I run to join a Windows worker node to a Kubernetes cluster if I have the token and the control plane host and port?
response: You should run the command that was output by kubeadm init, for example: kubeadm join --token <token> <control-plane-host>:<control-plane-port> --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash sha256:<hash>.
title: Adding Windows worker nodes — Runkubeadm join

instruction: How can I obtain a node join token if the current one has expired?
response: You can create a new token by running the command 'sudo kubeadm token create' on the control plane node.
title: Adding Windows worker nodes — Runkubeadm join

instruction: What should I do if I don't have the value of --discovery-token-ca-cert-hash?
response: You can get it by running the following commands on the control plane node: sudo cat /etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt | openssl x509 -pubkey | openssl rsa -pubin -outform der 2>/dev/null | openssl dgst -sha256 -hex | sed 's/^.* //'.
title: Adding Windows worker nodes — Runkubeadm join

instruction: How can I verify that a node has successfully joined the cluster?
response: You can verify that a node has successfully joined the cluster by running 'kubectl get nodes' on the control plane node. The newly joined node should appear in the output a few seconds after the join command completes.
title: Adding Windows worker nodes — Runkubeadm join

instruction: Can ConfigMaps or Secrets be used with static Pods in Kubernetes?
response: No, you cannot use ConfigMaps or Secrets with static Pods.
title: Secrets — Using Secrets with static Pods

instruction: What is the benefit of marking Secrets as immutable in Kubernetes?
response: Marking Secrets as immutable protects against accidental or unwanted updates that could cause application outages and improves cluster performance by reducing load on kube-apiserver.
title: Secrets — Using Secrets with static Pods

instruction: How does marking Secrets as immutable affect the kubelet's behavior in Kubernetes?
response: When Secrets are marked as immutable, the kubelet does not need to maintain a watch on them, which reduces the load on kube-apiserver.
title: Secrets — Using Secrets with static Pods

instruction: What is the purpose of the `continue` field in the GET /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/roles request?
response: The `continue` field is used to retrieve more results from the server. It should be set when retrieving more results, and its value is server-defined. If the specified `continue` value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token.
title: Role — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `limit` field affect the response of the GET /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/roles request?
response: The `limit` field is a maximum number of responses to return for a list call. If more items exist, the server will set the `continue` field on the list metadata to a value that can be used with the same initial query to retrieve the next set of results. Setting a limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items, and clients should only use the presence of the `continue` field to determine whether more results are available.
title: Role — getWatch List

instruction: What is the purpose of the `sendInitialEvents` field in the GET /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/roles request?
response: The `sendInitialEvents` field is used to begin the watch stream with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent, and the watch stream will proceed as usual, sending watch events corresponding to changes to objects watched.
title: Role — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `resourceVersionMatch` field interact with the `sendInitialEvents` field in the GET /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/roles request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents` is set, the `resourceVersionMatch` option must also be set. The semantic of the watch request depends on the value of `resourceVersionMatch`, which determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. If `resourceVersionMatch` is set to NotOlderThan, the bookmark event is sent when the state is synced to a `resourceVersion` at least as fresh as the one provided by the ListOptions.
title: Role — getWatch List

instruction: What steps are required to switch from a local encryption provider to the KMS provider and re-encrypt all secrets in Kubernetes?
response: To switch from a local encryption provider to the KMS provider and re-encrypt all secrets, add the KMS provider as the first entry in the configuration file, restart all kube-apiserver processes, and then run the command 'kubectl get secrets --all-namespaces -o json | kubectl replace -f -' to force all secrets to be re-encrypted using the KMS provider.
title: Using a KMS provider for data encryption — Switching from a local encryption provider to the KMS provider

instruction: How do you configure the KMS provider in the encryption configuration file?
response: The KMS provider is configured in the encryption configuration file by adding it as the first entry, specifying the apiVersion as 'v2', the name of the KMS plugin, and the endpoint, for example: 'kms: { apiVersion: v2, name: myKmsPlugin, endpoint: unix:///tmp/socketfile.sock }'.
title: Using a KMS provider for data encryption — Switching from a local encryption provider to the KMS provider

instruction: What command is used to force all secrets to be re-encrypted using the KMS provider after switching from a local encryption provider?
response: The command 'kubectl get secrets --all-namespaces -o json | kubectl replace -f -' is used to force all secrets to be re-encrypted using the KMS provider after switching from a local encryption provider.
title: Using a KMS provider for data encryption — Switching from a local encryption provider to the KMS provider

instruction: How can you list all ServiceAccount resources in your current namespace?
response: You can list all ServiceAccount resources in your current namespace with the command: kubectl get serviceaccounts.
title: Configure Service Accounts for Pods — Use more than one ServiceAccount

instruction: What is required for the name of a ServiceAccount object?
response: The name of a ServiceAccount object must be a valid DNS subdomain name.
title: Configure Service Accounts for Pods — Use more than one ServiceAccount

instruction: How can you use a non-default service account with a Pod?
response: To use a non-default service account, set the spec.serviceAccountName field of a Pod to the name of the ServiceAccount you wish to use. This can only be done when creating a Pod or in a template for a new Pod.
title: Configure Service Accounts for Pods — Use more than one ServiceAccount

instruction: Can the serviceAccountName field of an existing Pod be updated?
response: No, the spec.serviceAccountName field of a Pod that already exists cannot be updated.
title: Configure Service Accounts for Pods — Use more than one ServiceAccount

instruction: What is the main difference between local volumes and hostPath volumes in Kubernetes?
response: Local volumes are used in a durable and portable manner without manually scheduling Pods to nodes, whereas hostPath volumes require manual scheduling. The system is aware of the volume's node constraints by looking at the node affinity on the PersistentVolume.
title: Volumes — local

instruction: Can local volumes be used with dynamic provisioning in Kubernetes?
response: No, local volumes can only be used as a statically created PersistentVolume. Dynamic provisioning is not supported.
title: Volumes — local

instruction: What happens to a Pod using a local volume if the underlying node becomes unhealthy?
response: If a node becomes unhealthy, then the local volume becomes inaccessible to the Pod. The Pod using this volume is unable to run. Applications using local volumes must be able to tolerate this reduced availability, as well as potential data loss, depending on the durability characteristics of the underlying disk.
title: Volumes — local

instruction: Why is it recommended to set volumeBindingMode to WaitForFirstConsumer when using local volumes?
response: Delaying volume binding ensures that the PersistentVolumeClaim binding decision will also be evaluated with any other node constraints the Pod may have, such as node resource requirements, node selectors, Pod affinity, and Pod anti-affinity.
title: Volumes — local

instruction: What is the purpose of the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-access-log-emit-interval annotation in Kubernetes?
response: The service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-access-log-emit-interval annotation is used to configure the load balancer for a Service based on the cloud controller manager integration with AWS elastic load balancing. It determines how often the load balancer writes log entries.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-access-log-emit-interval (beta)

instruction: How do you specify the interval at which the load balancer writes log entries using the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-access-log-emit-interval annotation?
response: You can specify the interval by setting the value of the annotation. For example, setting the value to '5' means the log writes occur 5 seconds apart.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-access-log-emit-interval (beta)

instruction: On which Kubernetes object is the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-access-log-emit-interval annotation used?
response: The service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-access-log-emit-interval annotation is used on a Service object.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-access-log-emit-interval (beta)

instruction: What fields can be set by users when creating a CertificateSigningRequest object?
response: Only the request, signerName, expirationSeconds, and usages fields can be set on creation. Other fields are derived by Kubernetes and cannot be modified by users.
title: CertificateSigningRequest

instruction: What information is contained in the status field of a CertificateSigningRequest object?
response: The status field contains information about whether the request is approved or denied, and the certificate issued by the signer, or the failure condition indicating signer failure.
title: CertificateSigningRequest

instruction: What are the benefits of creating a custom resource within the Kubernetes platform, as opposed to implementing it outside of the platform?
response: Creating a custom resource within the Kubernetes platform provides many features for your API, including CRUD operations, Watch operations, Discovery, json-patch, merge-patch, HTTPS, Built-in Authentication, Built-in Authorization, Finalizers, Admission Webhooks, UI/CLI Display, and Client Libraries Generation.
title: Custom Resources — Common Features

instruction: How do clients like kubectl and dashboard interact with custom resources in Kubernetes?
response: Clients like kubectl and dashboard automatically offer list, display, and field edit operations on custom resources, thanks to the Discovery feature. Additionally, they can display custom resources and distinguish between unset fields and zero-valued fields.
title: Custom Resources — Common Features

instruction: What security features are available for custom resources in Kubernetes?
response: Custom resources in Kubernetes have access to Built-in Authentication, which uses the core API server for authentication, and Built-in Authorization, which can reuse the authorization used by the core API server, such as RBAC.
title: Custom Resources — Common Features

instruction: Can custom resources in Kubernetes be extended with additional metadata, and if so, how?
response: Yes, custom resources in Kubernetes can be extended with additional metadata, including labels and annotations, which are common metadata that tools know how to edit for both core and custom resources.
title: Custom Resources — Common Features

instruction: How can administrators limit storage consumption in a Kubernetes namespace?
response: Administrators can limit the number of Persistent Volume Claims (PVCs) in a namespace as well as the cumulative capacity of those PVCs using a ResourceQuota object.
title: Limit Storage Consumption — ResourceQuota to limit PVC count and cumulative storage capacity

instruction: What happens when a new PVC exceeds the maximum allowed count or cumulative capacity in a namespace?
response: New PVCs that exceed either the maximum count or cumulative capacity will be rejected.
title: Limit Storage Consumption — ResourceQuota to limit PVC count and cumulative storage capacity

instruction: How can you define a ResourceQuota to limit the number of PVCs and their cumulative storage capacity in a namespace?
response: You can define a ResourceQuota using an API object of kind ResourceQuota, specifying the hard limits for persistentvolumeclaims and requests.storage, such as in the example: apiVersion: v1, kind: ResourceQuota, spec: { hard: { persistentvolumeclaims: '5', requests.storage: '5Gi' } }.
title: Limit Storage Consumption — ResourceQuota to limit PVC count and cumulative storage capacity

instruction: What are the steps to install containerd on a Linux system?
response: To install containerd on a Linux system, follow these steps: Install the containerd.io package from the official Docker repositories, configure containerd by running 'sudo mkdir -p /etc/containerd' and 'containerd config default | sudo tee /etc/containerd/config.toml', and then restart containerd using 'sudo systemctl restart containerd'.
title: Changing the Container Runtime on a Node from Docker Engine to containerd — Install Containerd

instruction: How do I configure containerd on a Windows system using PowerShell?
response: To configure containerd on a Windows system using PowerShell, start a PowerShell session, download containerd using 'curl.exe', extract and configure it, review the configuration, and then start containerd using './containerd.exe -register-service' and 'Start-Service containerd'.
title: Changing the Container Runtime on a Node from Docker Engine to containerd — Install Containerd

instruction: What configuration options may need to be adjusted after installing containerd on a Windows system?
response: After installing containerd on a Windows system, you may want to adjust the sandbox_image (Kubernetes pause image) and the cni bin_dir and conf_dir locations in the configuration file.
title: Changing the Container Runtime on a Node from Docker Engine to containerd — Install Containerd

instruction: Is it recommended to exclude containerd from Windows Defender scans, and if so, how do I do it?
response: Yes, it is highly recommended to exclude containerd from Windows Defender scans. You can do this by running the command 'Add-MpPreference -ExclusionProcess "$Env:ProgramFilescontainerdcontainerd.exe"'.
title: Changing the Container Runtime on a Node from Docker Engine to containerd — Install Containerd

instruction: How do you increase the pod-level CPU request and limit to 300m for a pod named 'pod-level-resize-demo'?
response: You can use kubectl patch with the --subresource resize command line argument: kubectl patch pod pod-level-resize-demo --subresource resize --patch '{"spec":{"resources":{"requests":{"cpu":"300m"}, "limits":{"cpu":"300m"}}}}'
title: Resize CPU and Memory Resources assigned to Pods — Example: Resizing Pod-Level Resources

instruction: What happens to the restart count of Container 1 when the pod-level CPU limit is patched from 200m to 300m?
response: The restart count of Container 1 increases to 1, indicating that the container was restarted to apply the CPU change. This occurs because Container 1 did not specify an explicit CPU limit and its underlying resource configuration implicitly adopted the Pod's overall CPU limit as its effective maximum consumption boundary.
title: Resize CPU and Memory Resources assigned to Pods — Example: Resizing Pod-Level Resources

instruction: Why was Container 1 restarted when the pod-level CPU limit was patched?
response: Container 1 was restarted because its resizePolicy was set to RestartContainer for CPU, and the kubelet was obligated to restart the container to correctly apply the change in the underlying resource enforcement mechanism.
title: Resize CPU and Memory Resources assigned to Pods — Example: Resizing Pod-Level Resources

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'endpoints' field in a GlusterfsPersistentVolumeSource?
response: The 'endpoints' field specifies the endpoint name that details Glusterfs topology.
title: PersistentVolume — GlusterfsPersistentVolumeSource

instruction: What happens if the 'endpointsNamespace' field is empty in a GlusterfsPersistentVolumeSource?
response: If the 'endpointsNamespace' field is empty, the EndpointNamespace defaults to the same namespace as the bound PVC.
title: PersistentVolume — GlusterfsPersistentVolumeSource

instruction: Can a Glusterfs volume be mounted with read-only permissions, and if so, how?
response: Yes, a Glusterfs volume can be mounted with read-only permissions by setting the 'readOnly' field to true. By default, it is set to false.
title: PersistentVolume — GlusterfsPersistentVolumeSource

instruction: What is the purpose of the --rootfs flag in kubeadm init?
response: The --rootfs flag specifies the path to the 'real' host root filesystem, causing kubeadm to chroot into the provided path.
title: kubeadm init — Options inherited from parent commands

instruction: Where are the kubeconfig files written by kubeadm init?
response: kubeadm init writes kubeconfig files in /etc/kubernetes/ for the kubelet, the controller-manager, and the scheduler to connect to the API server, each with its own identity. Additional kubeconfig files are written for kubeadm as an administrative entity (admin.conf) and for a super admin user that can bypass RBAC (super-admin.conf).
title: kubeadm init — Options inherited from parent commands

instruction: What is the default DNS server installed by kubeadm init in Kubernetes version 1.11 and later?
response: In Kubernetes version 1.11 and later, CoreDNS is the default DNS server installed by kubeadm init.
title: kubeadm init — Options inherited from parent commands

instruction: Where are the static Pod manifests written by kubeadm init?
response: Static Pod manifests are written to /etc/kubernetes/manifests; the kubelet watches this directory for Pods to create on startup.
title: kubeadm init — Options inherited from parent commands

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /api/v1/watch/persistentvolumeclaims request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /api/v1/watch/persistentvolumeclaims request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. The server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error if the continue value is no longer valid. Clients may use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters to retrieve the next set of results.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /api/v1/watch/persistentvolumeclaims request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersionMatch` field in the GET /api/v1/watch/persistentvolumeclaims request?
response: The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the purpose of EndpointSubset in Kubernetes?
response: EndpointSubset is a group of addresses with a common set of ports. The expanded set of endpoints is the Cartesian product of Addresses x Ports.
title: Endpoints — EndpointSubset

instruction: How are the resulting endpoints generated from EndpointSubset?
response: Given a set of addresses and ports, the resulting set of endpoints is the Cartesian product of Addresses x Ports. For example, given two addresses and two ports, the resulting endpoints will be a combination of each address with each port.
title: Endpoints — EndpointSubset

instruction: What is the difference between addresses and notReadyAddresses in EndpointSubset?
response: addresses contain IP addresses which offer the related ports that are marked as ready, while notReadyAddresses contain IP addresses which offer the related ports but are not currently marked as ready.
title: Endpoints — EndpointSubset

instruction: What is the purpose of the `pretty` parameter in the GET /apis/autoscaling/v2/namespaces/{namespace}/horizontalpodautoscalers request?
response: The `pretty` parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If set to 'true', the output will be pretty printed. Defaults to 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersion` and `resourceVersionMatch` parameters in the GET /apis/autoscaling/v2/namespaces/{namespace}/horizontalpodautoscalers request?
response: The `resourceVersion` parameter sets a constraint on what resource versions a request may be served from. The `resourceVersionMatch` parameter determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler — getList

instruction: How does the `sendInitialEvents` option work when used with `watch=true`?
response: `sendInitialEvents=true` may be set together with `watch=true`. In that case, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler — getList

instruction: How can I verify that a new Pod has its imagePullSecrets field set automatically when using the default ServiceAccount?
response: You can use the command 'kubectl get pod <pod_name> -o jsonpath='{.spec.imagePullSecrets[0].name}{"\n"}'' to verify that the imagePullSecrets field is set automatically.
title: Configure Service Accounts for Pods — Verify that imagePullSecrets are set for new Pods

instruction: What is the expected output of the command 'kubectl get pod nginx -o jsonpath='{.spec.imagePullSecrets[0].name}{"\n"}'' if the imagePullSecrets field is set automatically?
response: The expected output is the name of the imagePullSecret, for example 'myregistrykey'.
title: Configure Service Accounts for Pods — Verify that imagePullSecrets are set for new Pods

instruction: Does a new Pod created in the current namespace with the default ServiceAccount have its spec.imagePullSecrets field set automatically?
response: Yes, a new Pod created in the current namespace with the default ServiceAccount has its spec.imagePullSecrets field set automatically.
title: Configure Service Accounts for Pods — Verify that imagePullSecrets are set for new Pods

instruction: What finalizers are added by StorageObjectInUseProtection to PVCs and PVs?
response: The kubernetes.io/pvc-protection finalizer is added to PVCs and the kubernetes.io/pv-protection finalizer is added to PVs.
title: Admission Control in Kubernetes — StorageObjectInUseProtection

instruction: What prevents a PVC or PV from being removed immediately after a user deletes it?
response: The PVC or PV is not removed until the finalizer is removed from the PVC or PV by the PVC or PV Protection Controller.
title: Admission Control in Kubernetes — StorageObjectInUseProtection

instruction: How do you delete a StatefulSet without deleting its Pods?
response: Use `kubectl delete` with the `--cascade=orphan` parameter. For example: `kubectl delete statefulset web --cascade=orphan`.
title: StatefulSet Basics — Non-cascading delete

instruction: What happens to a Pod's data when its StatefulSet is deleted and then recreated?
response: The Pod's data persists because the StatefulSet never deletes the PersistentVolumes associated with a Pod. When the StatefulSet is recreated and relaunches the Pod, its original PersistentVolume is remounted.
title: StatefulSet Basics — Non-cascading delete

instruction: Why was `web-2` terminated after recreating the StatefulSet?
response: After recreating the StatefulSet with `replicas` equal to 2, `web-2` was terminated because there were already two Running and Ready Pods: `web-0` and `web-1`.
title: StatefulSet Basics — Non-cascading delete

instruction: What happens if the node specified in the nodeName field does not exist?
response: The Pod will not run, and in some cases may be automatically deleted.
title: Assigning Pods to Nodes — nodeName

instruction: How does nodeName affect scheduling and node selection compared to nodeSelector and affinity rules?
response: Using nodeName overrules using nodeSelector or affinity and anti-affinity rules, and the scheduler ignores the Pod, allowing the kubelet on the named node to try to place the Pod.
title: Assigning Pods to Nodes — nodeName

instruction: What are some limitations of using nodeName for node selection?
response: Some limitations include: if the named node does not exist, the Pod will not run; if the node does not have the resources, the Pod will fail; and node names in cloud environments are not always predictable or stable.
title: Assigning Pods to Nodes — nodeName

instruction: What will be the reason for Pod failure if it cannot be accommodated on the node specified by nodeName due to resource constraints?
response: The reason for Pod failure will indicate why, for example OutOfmemory or OutOfcpu.
title: Assigning Pods to Nodes — nodeName

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'hosts' field in IngressTLS?
response: The 'hosts' field in IngressTLS is a list of hosts included in the TLS certificate. The values in this list must match the name/s used in the tlsSecret.
title: Ingress — IngressTLS

instruction: What happens if the 'hosts' field is left unspecified in IngressTLS?
response: If the 'hosts' field is left unspecified in IngressTLS, it defaults to the wildcard host setting for the loadbalancer controller fulfilling this Ingress.
title: Ingress — IngressTLS

instruction: How is TLS termination handled when the 'secretName' field is specified in IngressTLS?
response: When the 'secretName' field is specified in IngressTLS, TLS traffic is terminated on port 443 using the secret with the specified name.
title: Ingress — IngressTLS

instruction: What takes precedence when there is a conflict between the SNI host in a listener and the 'Host' header field used by an IngressRule?
response: In the event of a conflict between the SNI host in a listener and the 'Host' header field used by an IngressRule, the SNI host is used for termination and the value of the 'Host' header is used for routing.
title: Ingress — IngressTLS

instruction: What is a ServiceAccount in Kubernetes and what is its purpose?
response: A ServiceAccount is a type of non-human account in Kubernetes that provides a distinct identity in a Kubernetes cluster. It allows application Pods, system components, and entities to identify as that ServiceAccount, which is useful for authenticating to the API server or implementing identity-based security policies.
title: Service Accounts

instruction: What are the key properties of a ServiceAccount in Kubernetes?
response: Service accounts have the following properties: they are namespaced, meaning each service account is bound to a Kubernetes namespace; they are lightweight, existing in the cluster and defined in the Kubernetes API; and they are portable, making configurations that include service account definitions easily portable.
title: Service Accounts

instruction: How does a ServiceAccount differ from a user account in Kubernetes?
response: Service accounts are different from user accounts in that they are used for workloads and automation, while user accounts are for people. Service accounts exist as objects in the Kubernetes API, while user accounts are treated as opaque data by the API server. Service accounts are controlled using Kubernetes RBAC or other authorization mechanisms, while user accounts use Kubernetes RBAC or other identity and access management mechanisms.
title: Service Accounts

instruction: What happens to a namespace in Kubernetes when it is created?
response: Every namespace gets a default ServiceAccount upon creation.
title: Service Accounts

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' option in the DELETE collection API for CronJobs?
response: The continue option should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It allows clients to retrieve the next set of results from a previous query with identical query parameters.
title: CronJob — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What are the valid values for the 'propagationPolicy' field in the DELETE collection API for CronJobs?
response: The valid values for the 'propagationPolicy' field are: 'Orphan' - orphan the dependents; 'Background' - allow the garbage collector to delete the dependents in the background; 'Foreground' - a cascading policy that deletes all dependents in the foreground.
title: CronJob — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'orphanDependents' to true in the DELETE collection API for CronJobs?
response: If 'orphanDependents' is set to true, the 'orphan' finalizer will be added to the object's finalizers list, causing the dependent objects to be orphaned.
title: CronJob — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'gracePeriodSeconds' field in the DELETE collection API for CronJobs?
response: The 'gracePeriodSeconds' field specifies the duration in seconds before the object should be deleted. A value of zero indicates delete immediately.
title: CronJob — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What happens to the files created or modified by a container when it crashes or is stopped?
response: When a container crashes or is stopped, the container state is not saved, and all of the files that were created or modified during the lifetime of the container are lost.
title: Volumes — Why volumes are important

instruction: Why is it challenging for multiple containers in a Pod to share files?
response: It can be challenging to set up and access a shared filesystem across all of the containers in a Pod.
title: Volumes — Why volumes are important

instruction: How can the Kubernetes volume abstraction help with data persistence and shared storage issues in containers?
response: The Kubernetes volume abstraction can help solve both data persistence and shared storage problems by providing a way to persist data and share files between containers in a Pod.
title: Volumes — Why volumes are important

instruction: What is the purpose of the --allow-missing-template-keys option in kubectl create ingress?
response: The --allow-missing-template-keys option allows ignoring any errors in templates when a field or map key is missing in the template. This option only applies to golang and jsonpath output formats.
title: kubectl create ingress — Options

instruction: How do you specify a default backend service for an ingress object using kubectl create ingress?
response: You can specify a default backend service for an ingress object using the --default-backend option, followed by the service name and port in the format of svcname:port.
title: kubectl create ingress — Options

instruction: What are the possible values for the --dry-run option in kubectl create ingress?
response: The --dry-run option can take one of three values: 'none', 'server', or 'client'. 'none' means no dry run, 'server' means submit a server-side request without persisting the resource, and 'client' means only print the object that would be sent without sending it.
title: kubectl create ingress — Options

instruction: How do you specify a rule for an ingress object using kubectl create ingress?
response: You can specify a rule for an ingress object using the --rule option, followed by the rule in the format host/path=service:port[,tls=secretname].
title: kubectl create ingress — Options

instruction: How can I observe the performance of the DRA kubeletplugin gRPC NodePrepareResources operation?
response: You can observe the performance of the DRA kubeletplugin gRPC NodePrepareResources operation by using the histogram_quantile metric, specifically by calculating the 99th percentile of the sum of rates of dra_grpc_operations_duration_seconds_bucket with method_name matching '.*NodePrepareResources' over a 5-minute window.
title: Good practices for Dynamic Resource Allocation as a Cluster Admin — DRA kubeletplugin operations

instruction: What metrics can I use to monitor the DRA kubeletplugin gRPC NodeUnprepareResources operation?
response: You can monitor the DRA kubeletplugin gRPC NodeUnprepareResources operation by observing the histogram_quantile metric, specifically by calculating the 99th percentile of the sum of rates of dra_grpc_operations_duration_seconds_bucket with method_name matching '.*NodeUnprepareResources' over a 5-minute window.
title: Good practices for Dynamic Resource Allocation as a Cluster Admin — DRA kubeletplugin operations

instruction: What is the time window for calculating the rate of DRA gRPC operations?
response: The time window for calculating the rate of DRA gRPC operations is 5 minutes.
title: Good practices for Dynamic Resource Allocation as a Cluster Admin — DRA kubeletplugin operations

instruction: What are the defined audit levels in a Kubernetes audit policy?
response: The defined audit levels are: None, Metadata, Request, and RequestResponse. None doesn't log events that match the rule, Metadata logs events with metadata but not request or response body, Request logs events with request metadata and body but not response body, and RequestResponse logs events with request metadata, request body, and response body.
title: Auditing — Audit policy

instruction: How do you specify an audit policy for the kube-apiserver?
response: You can pass a file with the policy to kube-apiserver using the --audit-policy-file flag. If the flag is omitted, no events are logged.
title: Auditing — Audit policy

instruction: What is the effect of a policy with no rules in a Kubernetes audit policy file?
response: A policy with no (0) rules is treated as illegal.
title: Auditing — Audit policy

instruction: What are some possible causes for a DaemonSet rolling update to be stuck?
response: Some possible causes for a DaemonSet rolling update to be stuck include: new DaemonSet pods can't be scheduled on at least one node (e.g., due to resource constraints), or the recent DaemonSet template update is broken (e.g., container crash looping, non-existent container image), or clock skew between master and nodes if .spec.minReadySeconds is specified in the DaemonSet.
title: Perform a Rolling Update on a DaemonSet — Watching the rolling update status

instruction: How do you troubleshoot a stuck DaemonSet rolling update when new pods can't be scheduled on a node?
response: To troubleshoot a stuck DaemonSet rolling update when new pods can't be scheduled on a node, compare the output of 'kubectl get nodes' and 'kubectl get pods -l name=fluentd-elasticsearch -o wide -n kube-system' to find nodes without the DaemonSet pods scheduled. Then, delete some non-DaemonSet pods from those nodes to make room for new DaemonSet pods.
title: Perform a Rolling Update on a DaemonSet — Watching the rolling update status

instruction: What should you do if a DaemonSet rollout is blocked by a previous unhealthy rollout?
response: If a DaemonSet rollout is blocked by a previous unhealthy rollout, update the DaemonSet template again. A new rollout will not be blocked by previous unhealthy rollouts.
title: Perform a Rolling Update on a DaemonSet — Watching the rolling update status

instruction: What types of volume sources can be projected into a single directory in Kubernetes?
response: The types of volume sources that can be projected are: secret, downwardAPI, configMap, serviceAccountToken, clusterTrustBundle, and podCertificate.
title: Projected Volumes

instruction: What is a key requirement for all sources in a projected volume?
response: All sources are required to be in the same namespace as the Pod.
title: Projected Volumes

instruction: Where can I find logs related to container runtime on a worker node?
response: You can find logs from the kubelet, responsible for running containers on the node, in /var/log/kubelet.log.
title: Troubleshooting Clusters — Worker Nodes

instruction: How do I troubleshoot traffic issues to Service endpoints on a worker node?
response: You can find logs from kube-proxy, which directs traffic to Service endpoints, in /var/log/kube-proxy.log.
title: Troubleshooting Clusters — Worker Nodes

instruction: What label must a Pod have to be allowed to query the nginx service?
response: The Pod must have the label 'access: true'.
title: Declare Network Policy — Limit access to thenginxservice

instruction: What is the name of the NetworkPolicy object that limits access to the nginx service?
response: The name of the NetworkPolicy object is 'access-nginx'.
title: Declare Network Policy — Limit access to thenginxservice

instruction: What is the apiVersion of the NetworkPolicy object that limits access to the nginx service?
response: The apiVersion of the NetworkPolicy object is 'networking.k8s.io/v1'.
title: Declare Network Policy — Limit access to thenginxservice

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /api/v1/watch/secrets request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: Secret — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /api/v1/watch/secrets request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. The server may reject a continue value it does not recognize. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token.
title: Secret — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /api/v1/watch/secrets request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent.
title: Secret — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What are the possible values for `resourceVersionMatch` and how do they affect the request?
response: The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set. The only valid value for `resourceVersionMatch` is 'NotOlderThan', which is interpreted as 'data at least as new as the provided `resourceVersion`'.
title: Secret — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: How can I prevent kubeadm from automatically approving kubelet client certificates when a Bootstrap Token is used for authentication?
response: You can turn off the CSR auto-approver by executing the command 'kubectl delete clusterrolebinding kubeadm:node-autoapprove-bootstrap'. This will block kubeadm join until the admin has manually approved the CSR in flight.
title: kubeadm join — Securing your installation even more

instruction: What is the process for manually approving a Certificate Signing Request (CSR) in a kubeadm installation?
response: You can use 'kubectl get csr' to see the CSRs in a Pending state, then use 'kubectl certificate approve <csr-name>' to approve the CSR. This will change the CSR resource to an Active state.
title: kubeadm join — Securing your installation even more

instruction: How can I disable the public access to the cluster-info ConfigMap in a kubeadm installation?
response: You can turn off public access to the cluster-info ConfigMap by executing the command 'kubectl -n kube-public delete rolebinding kubeadm:bootstrap-signer-clusterinfo'. This should be done after kubeadm init but before kubeadm join.
title: kubeadm join — Securing your installation even more

instruction: What is an alternative way to provide the discovery information to kubeadm join, instead of using the --discovery-token flag?
response: You can use the --discovery-file flag with a cluster-info.yaml file, which can be obtained by fetching the cluster-info file from the API Server using 'kubectl -n kube-public get cm cluster-info -o jsonpath='{.data.kubeconfig}' | tee cluster-info.yaml'.
title: kubeadm join — Securing your installation even more

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /api/v1/podtemplates request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: PodTemplate — getList All Namespaces

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /api/v1/podtemplates request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is server-defined and clients may only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: PodTemplate — getList All Namespaces

instruction: What is the effect of setting `limit` in the GET /api/v1/podtemplates request?
response: Setting a limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items. If more items exist, the server will set the `continue` field on the list metadata to a value that can be used to retrieve the next set of results. The server guarantees that the objects returned when using continue will be identical to issuing a single list call without a limit.
title: PodTemplate — getList All Namespaces

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersion` and `resourceVersionMatch` fields in the GET /api/v1/podtemplates request?
response: The `resourceVersion` field sets a constraint on what resource versions a request may be served from. The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: PodTemplate — getList All Namespaces

instruction: What is the required format for the name of a PersistentVolumeClaim object in Kubernetes?
response: The name of a PersistentVolumeClaim object must be a valid DNS subdomain name.
title: Persistent Volumes — PersistentVolumeClaims

instruction: What access mode is specified in the given PersistentVolumeClaim example?
response: The access mode specified is ReadWriteOnce.
title: Persistent Volumes — PersistentVolumeClaims

instruction: What is the purpose of the storageClassName field in a PersistentVolumeClaim specification?
response: The storageClassName field specifies the StorageClass to be used for the PersistentVolumeClaim. In this example, it is set to 'slow'.
title: Persistent Volumes — PersistentVolumeClaims

instruction: How is the selector field used in a PersistentVolumeClaim specification?
response: The selector field is used to filter the available PersistentVolumes based on labels. It consists of matchLabels and matchExpressions. In this example, it matches labels with 'release: stable' and an environment that is 'dev'.
title: Persistent Volumes — PersistentVolumeClaims

instruction: What happens to a container's memory limit if only a memory request is specified in its manifest and a default memory limit is defined for the namespace?
response: The container's memory limit is set to the default memory limit defined for the namespace. In this case, the default memory limit is 512MiB.
title: Configure Default Memory Requests and Limits for a Namespace — What if you specify a container's request, but not its limit?

instruction: How can you verify the memory request and limit of a Pod's container after it has been created?
response: You can use the command `kubectl get pod <pod-name> --output=yaml --namespace=<namespace>` to view the Pod's specification, which includes the container's memory request and limit.
title: Configure Default Memory Requests and Limits for a Namespace — What if you specify a container's request, but not its limit?

instruction: When is a liveness probe not necessary in a Kubernetes container?
response: If the process in your container is able to crash on its own whenever it encounters an issue or becomes unhealthy, you do not necessarily need a liveness probe.
title: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes — When should you use a liveness probe?

instruction: How can you configure a liveness probe to restart a container if it fails?
response: Specify a liveness probe and a restartPolicy of Always or OnFailure.
title: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes — When should you use a liveness probe?

instruction: What is a common pattern for implementing liveness probes in relation to readiness probes?
response: Use the same low-cost HTTP endpoint as for readiness probes, but with a higher failureThreshold.
title: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes — When should you use a liveness probe?

instruction: What is the purpose of the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-manage-backend-security-group-rules annotation?
response: The AWS load balancer controller uses this annotation to manage backend security group rules.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-manage-backend-security-group-rules (beta)

instruction: How is the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-name annotation used in conjunction with other annotations?
response: If you set this annotation on a Service, and also annotate that Service with service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type: "external", and use the AWS load balancer controller in your cluster, then the AWS load balancer controller sets the name of that load balancer to the value you set for this annotation.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-manage-backend-security-group-rules (beta)

instruction: What is the purpose of the podCertificate projected volume in Kubernetes?
response: The podCertificate projected volume securely provisions a private key and X.509 certificate chain for a pod to use as client or server credentials.
title: Projected Volumes — podCertificate projected volumes

instruction: How does Kubelet handle refreshing the private key and certificate chain?
response: Kubelet will handle refreshing the private key and certificate chain when they get close to expiration. The application just has to make sure that it reloads the file promptly when it changes, with a mechanism like inotify or polling.
title: Projected Volumes — podCertificate projected volumes

instruction: What are the valid values for the keyType field in a podCertificate projection?
response: The valid values for the keyType field are ED25519, ECDSAP256, ECDSAP384, ECDSAP521, RSA3072, and RSA4096.
title: Projected Volumes — podCertificate projected volumes

instruction: What is the maximum lifetime for a certificate issued by a Kubernetes built-in signer?
response: Kubernetes built-in signers are restricted to a max lifetime of 86400 (1 day).
title: Projected Volumes — podCertificate projected volumes

instruction: Which identities typically update ResourceClaim status in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The identities that typically update ResourceClaim status in a Kubernetes cluster are kube-scheduler or a custom allocation controller, node-local DRA drivers, and multi-node DRA status controllers.
title: Harden Dynamic Resource Allocation in Your Cluster — Identify DRA components that write status

instruction: What additional permission requirement was introduced in Kubernetes v1.36 for DRA status updates?
response: Starting in Kubernetes v1.36, DRA status updates require synthetic subresource permissions in addition to resourceclaims/status.
title: Harden Dynamic Resource Allocation in Your Cluster — Identify DRA components that write status

instruction: What is the purpose of the `continue` option in the DELETE collection API?
response: The continue option should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It allows clients to retrieve the next set of results from a previous query with identical query parameters.
title: Event — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting `dryRun` to `All` in the DELETE collection API?
response: When `dryRun` is set to `All`, all dry run stages will be processed. This indicates that modifications should not be persisted.
title: Event — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the difference between `orphanDependents` and `propagationPolicy` in the DELETE collection API?
response: Both fields control the deletion of dependent objects. `orphanDependents` is deprecated and replaced by `propagationPolicy`. `propagationPolicy` allows for more control over garbage collection, with acceptable values being 'Orphan', 'Background', and 'Foreground'.
title: Event — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersion` and `resourceVersionMatch` fields in the DELETE collection API?
response: The `resourceVersion` field sets a constraint on what resource versions a request may be served from. The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls, and it is highly recommended to set it for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: Event — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What happens when a workload uses a specific ResourceClaim instead of a ResourceClaimTemplate?
response: If the workload uses a specific ResourceClaim, Kubernetes checks whether that ResourceClaim exists in the cluster. If the ResourceClaim doesn't exist, the Pods won't deploy.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — Workflow for Kubernetes

instruction: How does the Kubernetes scheduler select a ResourceSlice for a Pod's ResourceClaim?
response: The scheduler uses a first-fit strategy and evaluates pools and ResourceSlices in lexicographical order by their names. Drivers can prioritize specific slices or pools by naming them appropriately.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — Workflow for Kubernetes

instruction: What are the criteria for a ResourceSlice to be considered eligible for a Pod's ResourceClaim?
response: The nodes that can access the resources are eligible to run the Pod, and the ResourceSlice has unallocated resources that match the requirements of the Pod's ResourceClaim.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — Workflow for Kubernetes

instruction: What happens after resource allocation is complete for a Pod?
response: When resource allocation is complete, the scheduler places the Pod on a node that can access the allocated resource. The device driver and the kubelet on that node configure the device and the Pod's access to the device.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — Workflow for Kubernetes

instruction: How do I delete a debugging Pod after finishing its use?
response: To delete a debugging Pod, first verify its name with `kubectl get pods`, then use `kubectl delete pod <pod-name> --now`. Replace `<pod-name>` with the actual name of your debugging Pod.
title: Debugging Kubernetes Nodes With Kubectl — Cleaning up

instruction: What is the purpose of the `--now` flag when deleting a Pod?
response: The text does not explicitly explain the purpose of the `--now` flag.
title: Debugging Kubernetes Nodes With Kubectl — Cleaning up

instruction: How can I verify the name of a debugging Pod?
response: You can verify the name of a debugging Pod by running `kubectl get pods`. This command will list all current pods, including the debugging Pod, along with their statuses.
title: Debugging Kubernetes Nodes With Kubectl — Cleaning up

instruction: How do I mark a StorageClass as non-default?
response: To mark a StorageClass as non-default, you need to change the value of the annotation storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class to false. For example, to mark the StorageClass 'standard' as non-default, you can use the command: kubectl patch storageclass standard -p '{"metadata": {"annotations":{"storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class":"false"}}}')
title: Change the default StorageClass — Changing the default StorageClass

instruction: What happens when multiple StorageClasses are marked as default?
response: If more than one StorageClass is marked as default, a PersistentVolumeClaim without an explicitly defined storageClassName will be created using the most recently created default StorageClass.
title: Change the default StorageClass — Changing the default StorageClass

instruction: How do I verify that a StorageClass is the default?
response: You can verify that a StorageClass is the default by listing the StorageClasses in your cluster using kubectl get storageclass. The default StorageClass will be marked by (default).
title: Change the default StorageClass — Changing the default StorageClass

instruction: How do you set dependent environment variables for containers in a Pod?
response: You can set dependent environment variables for containers in a Pod by using $(VAR_NAME) in the value of env in the configuration file.
title: Define Dependent Environment Variables — Define an environment dependent variable for a container

instruction: What happens when an environment variable is referenced before it's defined in the env list?
response: If an environment variable is referenced before it's defined in the env list, it will not be resolved correctly and will be treated as a normal string. For example, in the provided manifest, UNCHANGED_REFERENCE fails to resolve $(PROTOCOL) because PROTOCOL is defined further down the list.
title: Define Dependent Environment Variables — Define an environment dependent variable for a container

instruction: How can you escape the $(VAR_NAME) syntax to prevent environment variable expansion?
response: You can escape the $(VAR_NAME) syntax by using a double $: $$(VAR_NAME). Escaped references are never expanded, regardless of whether the referenced variable is defined or not. This is demonstrated by the ESCAPED_REFERENCE case in the example.
title: Define Dependent Environment Variables — Define an environment dependent variable for a container

instruction: What is the recommended KMS API version for plugin compatibility?
response: The Kubernetes project recommends making your plugin compatible with the stable v2 KMS API.
title: Using a KMS provider for data encryption — Developing a KMS plugin gRPC server

instruction: How often does the API server poll the Status procedure call when the plugin is healthy?
response: The API server polls the Status procedure call approximately every minute when everything is healthy.
title: Using a KMS provider for data encryption — Developing a KMS plugin gRPC server

instruction: What are the requirements for the EncryptRequest procedure call response?
response: The response must include the ciphertext, the key_id for the KEK used, and, optionally, any metadata that the KMS plugin needs to aid in future DecryptRequest calls (via the annotations field). The plugin must guarantee that any distinct plaintext results in a distinct response (ciphertext, key_id, annotations).
title: Using a KMS provider for data encryption — Developing a KMS plugin gRPC server

instruction: What is the recommended latency for DecryptRequest procedure calls?
response: Plugin implementations should aim to keep each DecryptRequest procedure call's latency at under 10 milliseconds.
title: Using a KMS provider for data encryption — Developing a KMS plugin gRPC server

instruction: How does the kubelet register a node with the apiserver?
response: The kubelet can register the node with the apiserver using one of: the hostname; a flag to override the hostname; or specific logic for a cloud provider.
title: kubelet — Synopsis

instruction: What are the mechanisms through which the kubelet receives PodSpecs?
response: The kubelet receives PodSpecs primarily through the apiserver, and also through a file or an HTTP endpoint passed as a parameter on the command line.
title: kubelet — Synopsis

instruction: How does the kubelet obtain container manifests?
response: The kubelet obtains container manifests from a PodSpec from the apiserver, a file, or an HTTP endpoint.
title: kubelet — Synopsis

instruction: What is the default monitoring period for updates to container manifests provided through a file or HTTP endpoint?
response: The default monitoring period for updates to container manifests provided through a file or HTTP endpoint is 20 seconds.
title: kubelet — Synopsis

instruction: What is the default namespace displayed in the Kubernetes Dashboard?
response: The default namespace displayed in the Kubernetes Dashboard is the 'default' namespace.
title: Deploy and Access the Kubernetes Dashboard — Using Dashboard

instruction: What information is shown in the Node list view in the Kubernetes Dashboard?
response: The Node list view in the Kubernetes Dashboard contains CPU and memory usage metrics aggregated across all Nodes.
title: Deploy and Access the Kubernetes Dashboard — Using Dashboard

instruction: Can I view logs from containers belonging to a single Pod in the Kubernetes Dashboard?
response: Yes, Pod lists and detail pages in the Kubernetes Dashboard link to a logs viewer that is built into Dashboard, allowing for drilling down logs from containers belonging to a single Pod.
title: Deploy and Access the Kubernetes Dashboard — Using Dashboard

instruction: What type of information is displayed in the detail views for workloads in the Kubernetes Dashboard?
response: Detail views for workloads in the Kubernetes Dashboard show status and specification information and surface relationships between objects, such as Pods that a ReplicaSet is controlling or new ReplicaSets and HorizontalPodAutoscalers for Deployments.
title: Deploy and Access the Kubernetes Dashboard — Using Dashboard

instruction: What are the three kinds of probes that the kubelet can perform on running containers?
response: The kubelet can optionally perform and react to three kinds of probes on running containers: liveness, readiness, and startup probes.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Container probes

instruction: How does the kubelet perform a diagnostic on a container?
response: The kubelet performs a diagnostic on a container by either executing code within the container or making a network request.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Container probes

instruction: What actions can Kubernetes take based on the results of a probe?
response: Based on the probe results, Kubernetes can restart unhealthy containers or stop sending traffic to containers that are not ready.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Container probes

instruction: What is the purpose of the `pretty` parameter in the GET /apis/storagemigration.k8s.io/v1beta1/storageversionmigrations request?
response: The `pretty` parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If set to `true`, the output will be pretty printed. Defaults to `false` unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: StorageVersionMigration — getList

instruction: How does the `continue` parameter work in the GET /apis/storagemigration.k8s.io/v1beta1/storageversionmigrations request?
response: The `continue` parameter should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is a server-defined value that clients may only use with identical query parameters (except for the value of continue). If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error. Clients may then send another list request with the token received with the 410 error to retrieve the next set of results.
title: StorageVersionMigration — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersion` and `resourceVersionMatch` parameters in the GET /apis/storagemigration.k8s.io/v1beta1/storageversionmigrations request?
response: The `resourceVersion` parameter sets a constraint on what resource versions a request may be served from. The `resourceVersionMatch` parameter determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set. The `resourceVersionMatch` value can be set to NotOlderThan, which is interpreted as 'data at least as new as the provided `resourceVersion`'.
title: StorageVersionMigration — getList

instruction: How does the `sendInitialEvents` parameter work with `watch=true` in the GET /apis/storagemigration.k8s.io/v1beta1/storageversionmigrations request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent, reporting the ResourceVersion (RV) corresponding to the set of objects, and be marked with 'k8s.io/initial-events-end': 'true' annotation.
title: StorageVersionMigration — getList

instruction: Why can't a StatefulSet be scaled down when one of its Pods is unhealthy?
response: Scaling down a StatefulSet only occurs when all of its stateful Pods are running and ready. If a Pod is unhealthy, Kubernetes cannot determine if the issue is due to a permanent or transient fault, and scaling down could lead to the StatefulSet becoming unavailable.
title: Scale a StatefulSet — Scaling down does not work right

instruction: What are the risks of scaling a StatefulSet when one of its Pods is unhealthy due to a permanent fault?
response: If a Pod is unhealthy due to a permanent fault and the StatefulSet is scaled without correcting the fault, the StatefulSet membership may drop below the minimum number of replicas needed to function correctly, causing it to become unavailable.
title: Scale a StatefulSet — Scaling down does not work right

instruction: Why might it be better to delay scaling a StatefulSet when a Pod is unhealthy due to a transient fault?
response: If a Pod is unhealthy due to a transient fault, scaling operations may interfere with the application's behavior, particularly in distributed databases where nodes joining and leaving simultaneously can cause issues. It's better to perform scaling when the stateful application cluster is completely healthy.
title: Scale a StatefulSet — Scaling down does not work right

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'dryRun' parameter in the ClusterRole delete request?
