response: You can verify that the Pod is running on the chosen node by running the command 'kubectl get pods --output=wide'.
title: Assign Pods to Nodes using Node Affinity — Schedule a Pod using preferred node affinity

instruction: What is the effect of the 'preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution' node affinity on Pod scheduling?
response: The Pod will prefer a node that has a 'disktype=ssd' label, but it will not be strictly required.
title: Assign Pods to Nodes using Node Affinity — Schedule a Pod using preferred node affinity

instruction: What command can be used to create a Pod from the provided manifest?
response: The command 'kubectl apply -f https://k8s.io/examples/pods/pod-nginx-preferred-affinity.yaml' can be used to create a Pod from the provided manifest.
title: Assign Pods to Nodes using Node Affinity — Schedule a Pod using preferred node affinity

instruction: What are the supported JSON types in Kubernetes CustomResourceDefinition?
response: The supported JSON types are: bool, int64, float64, string, []interface{}, map[string]interface{}, and nil.
title: CustomResourceDefinition — JSON

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'default' field in JSONSchemaProps?
response: The 'default' field provides a default value for undefined object fields. This feature is in beta and requires spec.preserveUnknownFields to be false.
title: CustomResourceDefinition — JSON

instruction: How does the 'format' field in JSONSchemaProps validate string values?
response: The 'format' field validates string values against a set of predefined formats, including bsonobjectid, uri, email, hostname, ipv4, ipv6, cidr, mac, uuid, uuid3, uuid4, uuid5, isbn, isbn10, isbn13, creditcard, ssn, hexcolor, rgbcolor, byte, password, date, duration, and datetime.
title: CustomResourceDefinition — JSON

instruction: What does the 'x-kubernetes-embedded-resource' field indicate in JSONSchemaProps?
response: The 'x-kubernetes-embedded-resource' field indicates that the value is an embedded Kubernetes runtime.Object, with TypeMeta and ObjectMeta. The type must be object.
title: CustomResourceDefinition — JSON

instruction: Which type of volume plugins support volume snapshots in Kubernetes?
response: Volume snapshots only support the out-of-tree CSI volume plugins.
title: Persistent Volumes — Volume Snapshot and Restore Volume from Snapshot Support

instruction: What is the purpose of the dataSource field in a PersistentVolumeClaim specification?
response: The dataSource field is used to specify the source of the data for the PersistentVolumeClaim, such as a VolumeSnapshot. In this example, it is used to restore a PVC from a snapshot named 'new-snapshot-test'.
title: Persistent Volumes — Volume Snapshot and Restore Volume from Snapshot Support

instruction: How do you specify that a PersistentVolumeClaim should be restored from a VolumeSnapshot?
response: To restore a PVC from a VolumeSnapshot, you specify the VolumeSnapshot name and kind in the dataSource field of the PVC specification, along with the apiGroup of the snapshot.
title: Persistent Volumes — Volume Snapshot and Restore Volume from Snapshot Support

instruction: How can I manage the threat of privilege escalation via Pod creation?
response: You can manage the threat of privilege escalation via Pod creation by checking your access control and ensuring you're properly managing that risk.
title: Security Checklist — What's next

instruction: What additional resources are available for securing a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Additional resources for securing a Kubernetes cluster include the 'Securing a Cluster' guide for protecting against accidental or malicious access, the 'Cluster Multi-tenancy guide' for configuration recommendations, and the blog post 'A Closer Look at NSA/CISA Kubernetes Hardening Guidance' for complementary hardening guidance.
title: Security Checklist — What's next

instruction: What should I read for further information on authorization if I use Kubernetes RBAC?
response: If you use Kubernetes RBAC, you should read 'RBAC Good Practices' for further information on authorization.
title: Security Checklist — What's next

instruction: What is the purpose of the sysadmin profile when debugging a Pod?
response: The sysadmin profile includes a set of properties that grant System Administrator (root) privileges, allowing the ephemeral container to run with full capabilities as a privileged container.
title: Debug Running Pods — Applying a Static Profile

instruction: How can you verify that an ephemeral container was created as a privileged container?
response: You can use the command kubectl get pod myapp -o jsonpath='{.spec.ephemeralContainers[0].securityContext}' to check the security context of the ephemeral container. This will return a JSON object indicating whether the container is privileged.
title: Debug Running Pods — Applying a Static Profile

instruction: What is the significance of the CapPrm and CapEff values in the output of grep Cap /proc/$$/status?
response: The CapPrm and CapEff values indicate the capabilities of the ephemeral container process. In this case, the output shows that the container process is granted full capabilities as a privileged container, as indicated by the value 000001ffffffffff.
title: Debug Running Pods — Applying a Static Profile

instruction: How is the load balancer created for a Service of type LoadBalancer?
response: The actual creation of the load balancer happens asynchronously. Kubernetes starts by making changes equivalent to requesting a Service of type NodePort, and the cloud-controller-manager component then configures the external load balancer to forward traffic to that assigned node port.
title: Service — type: LoadBalancer

instruction: Can you specify the loadBalancerIP for a Service of type LoadBalancer, and what are the implications?
response: You can specify the loadBalancerIP for a Service of type LoadBalancer, but this field is deprecated since Kubernetes v1.24 and may be removed in a future API version. If the cloud provider does not support this feature, the specified loadBalancerIP is ignored. It's recommended to use provider-specific annotations instead.
title: Service — type: LoadBalancer

instruction: How are load balancer health checks implemented for Kubernetes managed load balancers?
response: The Kubernetes APIs do not define how health checks have to be implemented for Kubernetes managed load balancers. Instead, it's the cloud providers who decide on the behavior, and load balancer health checks are extensively used within the context of supporting the externalTrafficPolicy field for Services.
title: Service — type: LoadBalancer

instruction: What is the purpose of the spec.loadBalancerClass field for a Service of type LoadBalancer?
response: The spec.loadBalancerClass field enables you to use a load balancer implementation other than the cloud provider default. If specified, it's assumed that a load balancer implementation that matches the specified class is watching for Services, and any default load balancer implementation will ignore Services that have this field set.
title: Service — type: LoadBalancer

instruction: What is the HTTP method and path to retrieve a specific PersistentVolumeClaim by name and namespace?
response: The HTTP method is GET, and the path is /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/persistentvolumeclaims/{name}.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — getRead

instruction: What query parameter can be used to pretty-print the output of a PersistentVolumeClaim retrieval request?
response: The query parameter is 'pretty'. If set to 'true', the output will be pretty-printed. It defaults to 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool like curl or wget.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — getRead

instruction: What is the expected HTTP status code for a successful retrieval of a PersistentVolumeClaim?
response: The expected HTTP status code is 200, which indicates an OK response. The response body will contain the PersistentVolumeClaim object.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — getRead

instruction: What is the purpose of a PersistentVolume in Kubernetes?
response: A PersistentVolume (PV) is a resource that represents a piece of networked storage in a cluster, which can be used by a Pod to store data persistently.
title: PersistentVolume

instruction: What are the types of Persistent Volumes available in Kubernetes?
response: The text does not explicitly list all types of Persistent Volumes, but mentions that there are multiple types.
title: PersistentVolume

instruction: What is a PersistentVolumeClaim in Kubernetes?
response: A PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) is a request for storage by a user or a Pod, which can be bound to a PersistentVolume to provide persistent storage.
title: PersistentVolume

instruction: Can a PersistentVolumeClaim be used directly as a volume in a Pod?
response: Yes, a PersistentVolumeClaim can be used directly as a volume in a Pod, which is referred to as 'Claims As Volumes'.
title: PersistentVolume

instruction: What is the purpose of the `continue` option in the DELETE /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/services endpoint?
response: The continue option should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It allows clients to retrieve the next set of results from a previous query with identical query parameters.
title: Service — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting `dryRun` to `All` in the DELETE /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/services endpoint?
response: When `dryRun` is set to `All`, all dry run stages will be processed, and modifications will not be persisted.
title: Service — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the `propagationPolicy` field in the DELETE /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/services endpoint?
response: The `propagationPolicy` field determines whether and how garbage collection will be performed on dependent objects. Acceptable values are 'Orphan', 'Background', and 'Foreground'.
title: Service — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting `ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential` to `true` in the DELETE /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/services endpoint?
response: If set to `true`, it will trigger an unsafe deletion of the resource in case the normal deletion flow fails with a corrupt object error. This may potentially break the cluster if the workload associated with the resource being unsafe-deleted relies on normal deletion flow.
title: Service — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the primary benefit of node isolation in multi-tenancy?
response: The primary benefit of node isolation is that it reduces the noisy tenant issue, as all pods running on a node will belong to a single tenant, and the risk of information disclosure is slightly lower because an attacker will only have access to the containers and volumes mounted to that node.
title: Multi-tenancy — Node Isolation

instruction: What are some potential risks associated with node isolation in a multi-tenant Kubernetes cluster?
response: Although node isolation isolates tenant workloads from each other at the node level, a skilled attacker could still use the permissions assigned to the kubelet or other pods running on the node to move laterally within the cluster and gain access to tenant workloads running on other nodes.
title: Multi-tenancy — Node Isolation

instruction: How can node isolation be implemented in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Node isolation can be implemented using pod node selectors. For example, nodes for each tenant can be configured with taints so that only pods with the corresponding toleration can run on them. A mutating webhook could then be used to automatically add tolerations and node affinities to pods deployed into tenant namespaces.
title: Multi-tenancy — Node Isolation

instruction: What are some advantages of node isolation over sandboxing containers in terms of billing, compatibility, and performance?
response: Node isolation is a little easier to reason about from a billing standpoint than sandboxing containers since you can charge back per node rather than per pod. It also has fewer compatibility and performance issues and may be easier to implement than sandboxing containers.
title: Multi-tenancy — Node Isolation

instruction: What is the purpose of hashing the image spec in ImagePullIntent?
response: The filename is a SHA-256 hash of the image spec value to avoid filename-unsafe characters like ':' and '/'.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1alpha1) — ImagePullIntent

instruction: What is the apiVersion for the ImagePullIntent resource?
response: The apiVersion for the ImagePullIntent resource is kubelet.config.k8s.io/v1alpha1.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1alpha1) — ImagePullIntent

instruction: What field in the ImagePullIntent record contains the image spec from a Container's image field?
response: The field in the ImagePullIntent record that contains the image spec from a Container's image field is called 'image'.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1alpha1) — ImagePullIntent

instruction: What resources are used to demonstrate limiting storage consumption in a namespace?
response: The resources used are ResourceQuota, LimitRange, and PersistentVolumeClaim.
title: Limit Storage Consumption

instruction: What are the prerequisites for running this tutorial?
response: You need to have a Kubernetes cluster and the kubectl command-line tool configured to communicate with your cluster. It is recommended to run this tutorial on a cluster with at least two nodes that are not acting as control plane hosts.
title: Limit Storage Consumption

instruction: How can you create a Kubernetes cluster for testing if you don't already have one?
response: You can create a cluster using minikube or use one of these Kubernetes playgrounds: iximiuz Labs, Killercoda, KodeKloud.
title: Limit Storage Consumption

instruction: How do you check the version of your Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can check the version by entering 'kubectl version'.
title: Limit Storage Consumption

instruction: How can I check the state of a Pod's containers?
response: You can use kubectl describe pod <name-of-pod> to check the state of a Pod's containers.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Container states

instruction: What are the three possible states of a container in Kubernetes?
response: The three possible states of a container in Kubernetes are Waiting, Running, and Terminated.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Container states

instruction: What happens after the scheduler assigns a Pod to a Node?
response: Once the scheduler assigns a Pod to a Node, the kubelet starts creating containers for that Pod using a container runtime.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Container states

instruction: How do you mark a Pod as not-ready for scheduling in Kubernetes?
response: You can mark a Pod as not-ready for scheduling by creating it with one or more scheduling gates in its spec.
title: Pod Scheduling Readiness — Usage example

instruction: What is the expected output of `kubectl get pod test-pod` for a Pod in SchedulingGated state?
response: The output reveals the Pod's status as SchedulingGated, with READY showing 0/1, and the age of the Pod.
title: Pod Scheduling Readiness — Usage example

instruction: How do you inform the scheduler that a Pod is ready for scheduling after it was previously gated?
response: You can inform the scheduler that a Pod is ready for scheduling by removing its schedulingGates entirely, which can be done by reapplying a modified manifest without the schedulingGates field.
title: Pod Scheduling Readiness — Usage example

instruction: What is the expected status of a Pod after removing its scheduling gates and if it doesn't request any CPU/memory resources?
response: The Pod's state is expected to transition from SchedulingGated to Running, and it will be assigned an IP address and a node.
title: Pod Scheduling Readiness — Usage example

instruction: What happens to running containers when the kubelet restarts?
response: The containers that are already running continue to run. The kubelet re-establishes its view of the Node, and reconciles the running containers against the desired state.
title: What Happens After A Node Restart — Impact of a kubelet restart

instruction: Why does the node become temporarily reported as NotReady after a kubelet restart?
response: The node is temporarily reported as NotReady until the kubelet finishes initializing. While the node is NotReady, the scheduler does not place new Pods on it.
title: What Happens After A Node Restart — Impact of a kubelet restart

instruction: What is the effect of a kubelet restart on Pod readiness and EndpointSlices?
response: The kubelet preserves the readiness of running containers across a restart. Each Pod's readiness drives EndpointSlices, Endpoints, and downstream configuration.
title: What Happens After A Node Restart — Impact of a kubelet restart

instruction: What happens to ongoing image pulls during a kubelet restart?
response: Ongoing image pulls are cancelled. Depending on the container runtime, a cancelled pull may have to start over from the beginning when it is retried.
title: What Happens After A Node Restart — Impact of a kubelet restart

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'pretty' parameter in the PUT request to update a PersistentVolumeClaim?
response: The 'pretty' parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If 'true', the output will be pretty printed. It defaults to 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool like curl or wget.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — putReplace

instruction: What are the valid values for the 'fieldValidation' parameter when updating a PersistentVolumeClaim?
response: The valid values for 'fieldValidation' are: 'Ignore', 'Warn', and 'Strict'. 'Ignore' ignores unknown and duplicate fields. 'Warn' sends a warning for unknown and duplicate fields. 'Strict' fails the request if there are unknown or duplicate fields.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — putReplace

instruction: What is the significance of the 'fieldManager' field when updating a PersistentVolumeClaim?
response: The 'fieldManager' field is a name associated with the actor or entity making changes to the PersistentVolumeClaim. It must be less than 128 characters long and contain only printable characters.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — putReplace

instruction: What is the purpose of the resizer in a VolumeAttributesClass?
response: The resizer determines what volume plugin is used for modifying PersistentVolumes (PVs).
title: Volume Attributes Classes — Resizer

instruction: What is required for the resizer field in a VolumeAttributesClass?
response: The field 'driverName' must be specified.
title: Volume Attributes Classes — Resizer

instruction: How can an end user update a PersistentVolumeClaim to use a new VolumeAttributesClass?
response: The end user can update the PVC with the new VolumeAttributesClass by changing the 'volumeAttributesClassName' field in the PVC specification and applying the changes.
title: Volume Attributes Classes — Resizer

instruction: Where can I find more information on how the resizer works with VolumeAttributesClass?
response: You can refer to the CSI external-resizer documentation for more information on how the resizer works with VolumeAttributesClass.
title: Volume Attributes Classes — Resizer

instruction: In Kubernetes clusters created using v1.22 or later, do the aggregated 'edit' and 'admin' roles include write access to EndpointSlices?
response: No, as a mitigation for CVE-2021-25740, write access to EndpointSlices is not part of the aggregated 'edit' and 'admin' roles in clusters created using Kubernetes v1.22 or later.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Write access for EndpointSlices

instruction: What can be done to retain write access to EndpointSlices in the aggregated roles for new clusters created with Kubernetes v1.22 or later?
response: You can create a custom ClusterRole with the necessary permissions and aggregate it to the 'edit' role by setting the 'rbac.authorization.k8s.io/aggregate-to-edit' label to 'true'.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Write access for EndpointSlices

instruction: Why was write access to EndpointSlices removed from the aggregated 'edit' and 'admin' roles in Kubernetes v1.22?
response: Write access to EndpointSlices was removed as a mitigation for CVE-2021-25740, because it could allow writers to direct LoadBalancer or Ingress implementations to expose backend IPs that would not otherwise be accessible, and circumvent network policies or security controls.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Write access for EndpointSlices

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in a GET request to watch volume attachments?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: VolumeAttachment — getWatch

instruction: How does the `continue` field work when retrieving more results from the server?
response: The `continue` option should be set when retrieving more results from the server. The server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error if the continue value is no longer valid. Clients may start a watch from the last resourceVersion value returned by the server and not miss any modifications.
title: VolumeAttachment — getWatch

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` with `watch=true` in a GET request to watch volume attachments?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event.
title: VolumeAttachment — getWatch

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersionMatch` field in a GET request to watch volume attachments?
response: The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: VolumeAttachment — getWatch

instruction: How does the kubelet measure storage use for local ephemeral storage?
response: The kubelet measures storage use in emptyDir volumes (except tmpfs), directories holding node-level logs, and writeable container layers. It supports two methods: periodic scanning and filesystem project quota.
title: Local ephemeral storage — Ephemeral storage consumption management

instruction: What happens if a Pod exceeds its local ephemeral storage limit and the kubelet is managing local ephemeral storage as a resource?
response: If a Pod exceeds its local ephemeral storage limit and the kubelet is managing local ephemeral storage as a resource, the kubelet sets an eviction signal that triggers Pod eviction. For container-level isolation, the kubelet marks the Pod for eviction if a container's writable layer and log usage exceeds its storage limit. For pod-level isolation, the kubelet marks the Pod for eviction if the sum of local ephemeral storage usage from all containers and emptyDir volumes exceeds the overall Pod storage limit.
title: Local ephemeral storage — Ephemeral storage consumption management

instruction: How can you enable project quotas for monitoring storage use in Kubernetes?
response: To enable project quotas, you need to enable the LocalStorageCapacityIsolationFSQuotaMonitoring=true feature gate using the featureGates field in the kubelet configuration. Additionally, ensure that the root filesystem (or optional runtime filesystem) has project quotas enabled and is mounted with project quotas enabled. For ext4 filesystems, you need to enable the project quota tracking feature while the filesystem is not mounted using the command 'sudo tune2fs -O project -Q prjquota /dev/block-device'.
title: Local ephemeral storage — Ephemeral storage consumption management

instruction: What is the difference between periodic scanning and filesystem project quota for measuring Pod storage use?
response: Periodic scanning involves regular, scheduled checks that scan each emptyDir volume, container log directory, and writeable container layer to measure storage use. Filesystem project quota, on the other hand, uses operating-system level features to track storage use. Quotas are faster and more accurate than directory scanning, as they can track storage use even if a file is deleted but still has an open file descriptor.
title: Local ephemeral storage — Ephemeral storage consumption management

instruction: What gRPC service does the kubelet provide for resource discovery?
response: The kubelet provides a PodResourceLister gRPC service.
title: Troubleshooting Topology Management — Check the device plugin resource API

instruction: What type of information can be retrieved from the List gRPC endpoint?
response: Information about reserved memory for each container can be retrieved, which is contained in protobuf ContainerMemory message.
title: Troubleshooting Topology Management — Check the device plugin resource API

instruction: For which QoS class can this information be retrieved?
response: This information can be retrieved solely for pods in Guaranteed QoS class.
title: Troubleshooting Topology Management — Check the device plugin resource API

instruction: What does the ImagePullBackOff status mean for a container in a Kubernetes Pod?
response: The ImagePullBackOff status means that a container could not start because Kubernetes could not pull a container image, possibly due to an invalid image name or pulling from a private registry without an imagePullSecret.
title: Images — ImagePullBackOff

instruction: Will Kubernetes continue to try pulling the image if it encounters an ImagePullBackOff error?
response: Yes, Kubernetes will keep trying to pull the image with an increasing back-off delay until it reaches a compiled-in limit of 300 seconds (5 minutes).
title: Images — ImagePullBackOff

instruction: What are the core principles that shaped the design and architecture of Gateway API?
response: The core principles that shaped the design and architecture of Gateway API are: role-oriented, portable, expressive, and extensible.
title: Gateway API

instruction: What are the three main organizational roles that Gateway API kinds are modeled after?
response: The three main organizational roles that Gateway API kinds are modeled after are: Infrastructure Provider, Cluster Operator, and Application Developer.
title: Gateway API

instruction: What is the role of an Infrastructure Provider in the context of Gateway API?
response: An Infrastructure Provider manages infrastructure that allows multiple isolated clusters to serve multiple tenants, such as a cloud provider.
title: Gateway API

instruction: How does Gateway API support traffic routing use cases?
response: Gateway API kinds support functionality for common traffic routing use cases such as header-based matching, traffic weighting, and others that were only possible in Ingress by using custom annotations.
title: Gateway API

instruction: What is the purpose of the `pretty` parameter in the GET /apis/admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1/mutatingwebhookconfigurations request?
response: The `pretty` parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If set to 'true', the output will be pretty-printed. Defaults to 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: MutatingWebhookConfiguration — getList

instruction: How does the `continue` parameter work in the GET /apis/admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1/mutatingwebhookconfigurations request?
response: The `continue` parameter should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is a server-defined value that clients may only use with identical query parameters (except for the value of continue). If the continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error. Clients may then restart their list without the continue field or send another list request with the token received with the 410 error.
title: MutatingWebhookConfiguration — getList

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /apis/admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1/mutatingwebhookconfigurations request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` are set, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent, reporting the ResourceVersion (RV) corresponding to the set of objects, and marked with the 'k8s.io/initial-events-end': 'true' annotation.
title: MutatingWebhookConfiguration — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the --rootfs string option in kubeadm join?
response: The --rootfs string option specifies the path to the 'real' host root filesystem, causing kubeadm to chroot into the provided path.
title: kubeadm join — Options inherited from parent commands

instruction: How does kubeadm verify the authenticity of cluster information downloaded from the API server?
response: By default, kubeadm uses the bootstrap token and the CA key hash to verify the authenticity of cluster information. The root CA can also be discovered directly via a file or URL.
title: kubeadm join — Options inherited from parent commands

instruction: What are the steps involved in TLS bootstrapping for a worker node in kubeadm join?
response: The TLS bootstrap uses the shared token to temporarily authenticate with the Kubernetes API server to submit a certificate signing request (CSR); by default the control plane signs this CSR request automatically.
title: kubeadm join — Options inherited from parent commands

instruction: What additional steps are performed when joining a control-plane node using kubeadm join?
response: For control-plane nodes, additional steps include downloading certificates shared among control-plane nodes, generating control-plane component manifests, certificates and kubeconfig, and adding a new local etcd member.
title: kubeadm join — Options inherited from parent commands

instruction: What is the purpose of the `imageRepository` field in `ImageMeta`?
response: The `imageRepository` field sets the container registry to pull images from. If not set, the `imageRepository` defined in `ClusterConfiguration` will be used instead.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta4) — ImageMeta

instruction: What happens to the version of components during upgrades if `imageTag` is set in `ImageMeta`?
response: If `imageTag` is set, kubeadm does not change automatically the version of the components during upgrades.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta4) — ImageMeta

instruction: What happens to existing roles when using kubectl auth reconcile with a manifest file?
response: Existing roles are updated to include the permissions in the input objects, and remove extra permissions if --remove-extra-permissions is specified.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — kubectl auth reconcile

instruction: How can you test applying a manifest file of RBAC objects without making any changes?
response: You can use the --dry-run option with kubectl auth reconcile, for example: kubectl auth reconcile -f my-rbac-rules.yaml --dry-run.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — kubectl auth reconcile

instruction: What is the purpose of the --remove-extra-permissions and --remove-extra-subjects options when using kubectl auth reconcile?
response: These options are used to remove extra permissions from roles and extra subjects from bindings, respectively, when applying a manifest file of RBAC objects.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — kubectl auth reconcile

instruction: What is the default method used by the kubelet to collect memory usage statistics?
response: The kubelet polls cAdvisor to collect memory usage stats at a regular interval.
title: Node-pressure Eviction — Known issues

instruction: What is a potential issue with the default method of collecting memory usage statistics?
response: If memory usage increases rapidly within the polling interval, the kubelet may not observe MemoryPressure fast enough, and the OOM killer will still be invoked.
title: Node-pressure Eviction — Known issues

instruction: How can the kubelet be configured to get immediate notification when a memory threshold is crossed?
response: The --kernel-memcg-notification flag can be used to enable the memcg notification API on the kubelet.
title: Node-pressure Eviction — Known issues

instruction: What is a viable workaround for the issue of rapid memory usage increases when not trying to achieve extreme utilization?
response: Using the --kube-reserved and --system-reserved flags to allocate memory for the system can help achieve a sensible measure of overcommit.
title: Node-pressure Eviction — Known issues

instruction: What is the purpose of Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) lets a cluster manage availability and allocation of hardware resources to satisfy Pod-based claims for hardware requirements and preferences.
title: Install Drivers and Allocate Devices with DRA

instruction: What components are responsible for managing hardware resources through the DRA APIs?
response: A mixture of Kubernetes built-in components (like the Kubernetes scheduler, kubelet, and kube-controller-manager) and third-party drivers from device owners (called DRA drivers) share the responsibility to advertise, allocate, prepare, mount, healthcheck, unprepare, and cleanup resources throughout the Pod lifecycle.
title: Install Drivers and Allocate Devices with DRA

instruction: What API group is used for DRA specific APIs?
response: The resource.k8s.io API group is used for DRA specific APIs, including DeviceClasses, ResourceSlices, ResourceClaims, as well as new fields in the Pod spec itself.
title: Install Drivers and Allocate Devices with DRA

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in a GET /apis/apps/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/deployments/{name} request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: Deployment — getWatch

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in a GET /apis/apps/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/deployments/{name} request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is server-defined and clients may only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: Deployment — getWatch

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` in a GET /apis/apps/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/deployments/{name} request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event.
title: Deployment — getWatch

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersionMatch` field in a GET /apis/apps/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/deployments/{name} request?
response: The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: Deployment — getWatch

instruction: What is the default behavior of the --allow-missing-template-keys option?
response: The default behavior of the --allow-missing-template-keys option is to ignore any errors in templates when a field or map key is missing in the template, and it only applies to golang and jsonpath output formats.
title: kubectl create cronjob — Options

instruction: What are the possible values for the --dry-run option?
response: The possible values for the --dry-run option are 'none', 'server', or 'client'. If 'client' strategy is used, only print the object that would be sent, without sending it. If 'server' strategy is used, submit server-side request without persisting the resource.
title: kubectl create cronjob — Options

instruction: What is the purpose of the --save-config option?
response: The --save-config option is used to save the configuration of the current object in its annotation. This flag is useful when you want to perform kubectl apply on this object in the future.
title: kubectl create cronjob — Options

instruction: What are the supported values for the --restart option?
response: The supported values for the --restart option are 'OnFailure' and 'Never'.
title: kubectl create cronjob — Options

instruction: What is the exception to the RBAC system's prevention of users creating clusterroles with more rights than they possess?
response: The exception is the 'escalate' verb, which allows users to effectively escalate their privileges.
title: Role Based Access Control Good Practices — Escalate verb

instruction: What is the effect of granting users the 'escalate' verb in Kubernetes RBAC?
response: Granting users the 'escalate' verb allows them to bypass Kubernetes' built-in protections against privilege escalation, enabling them to create bindings to roles with rights they do not already have.
title: Role Based Access Control Good Practices — Escalate verb

instruction: How can you add custom entries to a Pod's /etc/hosts file?
response: You can add custom entries to a Pod's /etc/hosts file using the HostAliases field in PodSpec.
title: Adding entries to Pod /etc/hosts with HostAliases

instruction: Why should you avoid modifying a Pod's /etc/hosts file directly using an init container or other means?
response: Modifying a Pod's /etc/hosts file directly using an init container or other means may be overwritten by the kubelet during Pod creation or restart. The recommended approach is to use the hostAliases field in PodSpec.
title: Adding entries to Pod /etc/hosts with HostAliases

instruction: How can I set the default subnet used by services when running kubeadm init?
response: You can pass the --service-cidr parameter to kubeadm init. For example, to use the subnet 10.96.0.0/12, run kubeadm init --service-cidr 10.96.0.0/12.
title: Configuring each kubelet in your cluster using kubeadm — Kubelet configuration patterns

instruction: What is the purpose of the KubeletConfiguration object in kubeadm?
response: The KubeletConfiguration object provides a versioned, structured API object that can configure most parameters in the kubelet and push out this configuration to each running kubelet in the cluster.
title: Configuring each kubelet in your cluster using kubeadm — Kubelet configuration patterns

instruction: How do I specify the DNS address used by the kubelet in a KubeletConfiguration object?
response: You can specify the DNS address used by the kubelet by setting the clusterDNS field in the KubeletConfiguration object. For example, you can set it to a list of IP addresses, such as [10.96.0.10].
title: Configuring each kubelet in your cluster using kubeadm — Kubelet configuration patterns

instruction: What is the purpose of the `pretty` parameter in the GET /apis/apiextensions.k8s.io/v1/customresourcedefinitions request?
response: The `pretty` parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If set to 'true', the output will be pretty-printed. Defaults to 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: CustomResourceDefinition — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersion` and `resourceVersionMatch` parameters in the GET /apis/apiextensions.k8s.io/v1/customresourcedefinitions request?
response: The `resourceVersion` parameter sets a constraint on what resource versions a request may be served from. The `resourceVersionMatch` parameter determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: CustomResourceDefinition — getList

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /apis/apiextensions.k8s.io/v1/customresourcedefinitions request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` are set, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. A synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent once all such events have been sent, reporting the ResourceVersion (RV) corresponding to the set of objects.
title: CustomResourceDefinition — getList

instruction: What is the recommended endpoint for OpenTelemetry tracing clients?
response: The recommended endpoint is unset, and it defaults to localhost:4317, which is the otlp grpc default.
title: kube-apiserver Configuration (v1)

instruction: How can I disable anonymous unauthenticated access to API information in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: To disable anonymous unauthenticated access, add the --anonymous-auth=false flag to the API server configuration.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — API discovery roles

instruction: What is the purpose of the system:discovery ClusterRole in Kubernetes?
response: The system:discovery ClusterRole allows read-only access to API discovery endpoints needed to discover and negotiate an API level.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — API discovery roles

instruction: How can I view the configuration of the API discovery roles in Kubernetes using kubectl?
response: To view the configuration of these roles, run kubectl get clusterroles system:discovery -o yaml.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — API discovery roles

instruction: Which ClusterRoles are bound to the system:unauthenticated group by default in Kubernetes versions prior to v1.14?
response: Prior to v1.14, the system:basic-user and system:discovery ClusterRoles were bound to the system:unauthenticated group by default.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — API discovery roles

instruction: What is the purpose of the `pretty` parameter in the GET /apis/admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1/validatingadmissionpolicybindings endpoint?
response: The `pretty` parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If set to `true`, the output will be formatted for readability. It defaults to `false` unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding — getList

instruction: How does the `continue` parameter work when retrieving more results from the server?
response: The `continue` parameter should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is a server-defined value that clients may use to retrieve the next set of results. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error. Clients may then restart their list without the continue field or send another list request with the token received with the 410 error.
title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding — getList

instruction: What is the effect of setting `resourceVersionMatch` to NotOlderThan when `sendInitialEvents` is set to true?
response: When `resourceVersionMatch` is set to NotOlderThan and `sendInitialEvents` is set to true, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. The bookmark event will be sent when the state is synced to a `resourceVersion` at least as fresh as the one provided by the ListOptions.
title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding — getList

instruction: How does the `limit` parameter affect the number of responses returned for a list call?
response: The `limit` parameter sets a maximum number of responses to return for a list call. If more items exist, the server will set the `continue` field on the list metadata to a value that can be used to retrieve the next set of results. Setting a limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items if all requested objects are filtered out.
title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in a GET request to `/apis/batch/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/cronjobs`?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers may ignore this flag if they do not implement bookmarks, and clients should not assume bookmarks are returned at any specific interval.
title: CronJob — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` with `watch=true` in a GET request to `/apis/batch/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/cronjobs`?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event reporting the ResourceVersion.
title: CronJob — getWatch List

instruction: What command can be used to retrieve the value of a key with dots in a config map?
response: kubectl get configmap myconfig -o jsonpath='{.data.ca\.crt}'
title: kubectl Quick Reference — Viewing and finding resources

instruction: How do you get all worker nodes in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: kubectl get node --selector='!node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane'
title: kubectl Quick Reference — Viewing and finding resources

instruction: How do you specify a ConfigMap to be used as a volume in a Pod?
response: You add the ConfigMap name under the 'volumes' section of the Pod specification.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a ConfigMap — Populate a Volume with data stored in a ConfigMap

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'volumeMounts.mountPath' field in a Pod specification?
response: The 'volumeMounts.mountPath' field specifies the directory where the ConfigMap data will be added. In this case, it is set to '/etc/config'.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a ConfigMap — Populate a Volume with data stored in a ConfigMap

instruction: What is the effect of the command 'ls /etc/config/' in the Pod specification?
response: The command 'ls /etc/config/' lists the directory files with names that match the keys in the ConfigMap. The output will be the keys of the ConfigMap, such as 'SPECIAL_LEVEL' and 'SPECIAL_TYPE'.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a ConfigMap — Populate a Volume with data stored in a ConfigMap

instruction: What character encoding is used to expose text data as files from a ConfigMap?
response: Text data is exposed as files using the UTF-8 character encoding. To use some other character encoding, you can use 'binaryData' in the ConfigMap object.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a ConfigMap — Populate a Volume with data stored in a ConfigMap

instruction: What is the DNS name of the backend Service that the frontend uses to send requests?
response: The DNS name of the backend Service is 'hello', which is the value of the 'name' field in the backend Service configuration file.
title: Connect a Frontend to a Backend Using Services — Creating the frontend

instruction: What type of Service is used for the frontend, and what does it imply?
response: The frontend Service is of type 'LoadBalancer', which means it uses a load balancer provisioned by the cloud provider and is accessible from outside the cluster.
title: Connect a Frontend to a Backend Using Services — Creating the frontend

instruction: How do you create the frontend Deployment and Service?
response: You create the frontend Deployment and Service by running the following commands: 'kubectl apply -f https://k8s.io/examples/service/access/frontend-deployment.yaml' and 'kubectl apply -f https://k8s.io/examples/service/access/frontend-service.yaml'.
title: Connect a Frontend to a Backend Using Services — Creating the frontend

instruction: What signal does kubelet send to the main process in each container to initiate graceful termination?
response: kubelet sends a TERM (aka. SIGTERM) signal to the main process in each container to initiate graceful termination.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Termination of Pods

instruction: What happens to a Pod if the kubelet or container runtime's management service is restarted while waiting for processes to terminate?
response: If the kubelet or the container runtime's management service is restarted while waiting for processes to terminate, the cluster retries from the start including the full original grace period.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Termination of Pods

instruction: What signal is sent to any remaining processes in a Pod after the grace period has expired?
response: Once the grace period has expired, the KILL signal is sent to any remaining processes.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Termination of Pods

instruction: What gRPC methods does the Kubelet Pod Info API provide?
response: The API provides two gRPC methods: one returns a list of all pods currently managed by the kubelet on the node, and the other returns a stream of pod updates.
title: Kubelet Pod Info gRPC API — Endpoint

instruction: How does the kubelet handle pod updates through the gRPC API?
response: The kubelet sends updated pod information through a stream whenever a pod's state changes locally.
title: Kubelet Pod Info gRPC API — Endpoint

instruction: How does the kubelet systemd watchdog integration work on Linux nodes?
response: The kubelet systemd watchdog integration works by requiring the kubelet to periodically send a keep-alive signal to the systemd process. If the signal is not received within a specified timeout period, the kubelet is considered unresponsive and is terminated.
title: Kubelet Systemd Watchdog

instruction: What happens to the kubelet if it does not respond to the systemd watchdog within the timeout period?
response: If the kubelet does not respond to the systemd watchdog within the timeout period, the watchdog will kill the kubelet.
title: Kubelet Systemd Watchdog

instruction: Is the kubelet systemd watchdog integration enabled by default in Kubernetes 1.36?
response: No, the kubelet systemd watchdog integration is not enabled by default in Kubernetes 1.36.
title: Kubelet Systemd Watchdog

instruction: What is an alternative to using the systemd watchdog for kubelet health checks?
response: An alternative to using the systemd watchdog for kubelet health checks is periodically requesting the kubelet's /healthz endpoint.
title: Kubelet Systemd Watchdog

instruction: What is the default accessibility of cloud metadata APIs to pods running on an instance?
response: By default, these APIs are accessible by pods running on an instance.
title: Securing a Cluster — Restricting cloud metadata API access

instruction: What kind of data can be contained in cloud metadata APIs that can be accessed by pods?
response: These APIs can contain cloud credentials for that node, or provisioning data such as kubelet credentials.
title: Securing a Cluster — Restricting cloud metadata API access

instruction: How can access to cloud metadata APIs be restricted in a Kubernetes cluster running on a cloud platform?
response: Limit permissions given to instance credentials, use network policies to restrict pod access to the metadata API, and avoid using provisioning data to deliver secrets.
title: Securing a Cluster — Restricting cloud metadata API access

instruction: How does the kubelet collect metrics on Linux nodes versus Windows nodes?
response: On Linux nodes, the kubelet collects metrics by reading cgroups and various system files. On Windows nodes, it collects metrics via a different mechanism that does not rely on paths.
title: Local Files And Paths Used By The Kubelet

instruction: How many Pods does the StatefulSet controller create for the ZooKeeper ensemble?
response: The StatefulSet controller creates three Pods.
title: Running ZooKeeper, A Distributed System Coordinator — Creating a ZooKeeper ensemble

instruction: What is the purpose of the advertiseAddress field in InitConfiguration?
response: The advertiseAddress field in InitConfiguration specifies the IP address that the API Server will advertise it is listening on. This value equals the --apiserver-advertise-address flag of kubeadm init.
title: Dual-stack support with kubeadm — Create a dual-stack cluster

instruction: How do you specify the node IP addresses for a dual-stack cluster using kubeadm?
response: You can specify the node IP addresses for a dual-stack cluster using the nodeRegistration.kubeletExtraArgs field in InitConfiguration. For example: nodeRegistration: kubeletExtraArgs: - name: "node-ip" value: "10.100.0.2,fd00:1:2:3::2"
title: Dual-stack support with kubeadm — Create a dual-stack cluster

instruction: What is the purpose of a RoleBinding in Kubernetes?
response: A RoleBinding references a role, but does not contain it. It adds who information via Subjects and namespace information by which namespace it exists in.
title: RoleBinding

instruction: What are the possible references for the roleRef field in a RoleBinding?
response: The roleRef field in a RoleBinding can reference a Role in the same namespace or a ClusterRole in the global namespace.
title: RoleBinding

instruction: What happens if the RoleRef in a RoleBinding cannot be resolved?
response: If the RoleRef cannot be resolved, the Authorizer must return an error.
title: RoleBinding

instruction: What is the effect of RoleBindings in a given namespace?
response: RoleBindings in a given namespace only have effect in that namespace.
title: RoleBinding

instruction: How can I check the expiration of certificates managed by kubeadm?
response: You can use the `check-expiration` subcommand: `kubeadm certs check-expiration`.
title: Certificate Management with kubeadm — Certificate expiry and management

instruction: Why is the kubelet.conf configuration file not included in the list of certificates checked by kubeadm?
response: Kubeadm configures kubelet for automatic certificate renewal with rotatable certificates under `/var/lib/kubelet/pki`.
title: Certificate Management with kubeadm — Certificate expiry and management

instruction: What should I do if I have a node created with kubeadm init from a version prior to 1.17 and the kubelet client certificate has expired?
response: You need to manually update kubelet.conf to point to the rotated kubelet client certificates by replacing `client-certificate-data` and `client-key-data` with `client-certificate: /var/lib/kubelet/pki/kubelet-client-current.pem` and `client-key: /var/lib/kubelet/pki/kubelet-client-current.pem`.
title: Certificate Management with kubeadm — Certificate expiry and management

instruction: What is the HTTP method and endpoint to retrieve a ClusterRole by name?
response: The HTTP method is GET and the endpoint is /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/clusterroles/{name}.
title: ClusterRole — getRead

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'pretty' query parameter when retrieving a ClusterRole?
response: The 'pretty' query parameter is used to pretty-print the output if set to 'true'. It defaults to 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool like curl or wget.
title: ClusterRole — getRead

instruction: What is the expected HTTP status code when successfully retrieving a ClusterRole?
response: The expected HTTP status code is 200, which indicates an OK response.
title: ClusterRole — getRead

instruction: What happens to the Pods running on a Node when the Node dies?
response: When a worker node dies, the Pods running on the Node are also lost.
title: Using a Service to Expose Your App — Overview of Kubernetes Services

instruction: What is the purpose of a Service in Kubernetes?
response: A Service in Kubernetes is an abstraction which defines a logical set of Pods and a policy by which to access them. Services enable a loose coupling between dependent Pods.
title: Using a Service to Expose Your App — Overview of Kubernetes Services

instruction: How can a Service be exposed outside the cluster?
response: A Service can be exposed outside the cluster by specifying a type in the spec of the Service, such as NodePort or LoadBalancer. NodePort exposes the Service on the same port of each selected Node in the cluster using NAT, while LoadBalancer creates an external load balancer in the current cloud and assigns a fixed, external IP to the Service.
title: Using a Service to Expose Your App — Overview of Kubernetes Services

instruction: What is the purpose of a Service without a selector in the spec?
response: A Service created without a selector will also not create the corresponding Endpoints object. This allows users to manually map a Service to specific endpoints.
title: Using a Service to Expose Your App — Overview of Kubernetes Services

instruction: What type of interface does Headlamp provide for cluster management and troubleshooting?
response: Headlamp provides a modern, user-friendly graphical interface.
title: Other Tools — Headlamp

instruction: How can Headlamp be deployed?
response: Headlamp supports both in-cluster deployment and desktop application modes.
title: Other Tools — Headlamp

instruction: What kind of controls does Headlamp use to manage user permissions?
response: Headlamp uses RBAC-based controls that adapt to user permissions.
title: Other Tools — Headlamp

instruction: Is Headlamp a required component of a Kubernetes cluster?
response: No, Headlamp is an optional Kubernetes cluster component.
title: Other Tools — Headlamp

instruction: How can I query the kubelet's API endpoint to obtain a list of Pods?
response: You can query the kubelet's API endpoint at http://localhost:10255/pods using a tool like curl and pipe the output to jq '.'
title: Running Kubelet in Standalone Mode — Find out information about the kubelet and the Pod

instruction: How do I obtain the IP address of a specific Pod, such as the static-web Pod?
response: You can obtain the IP address of the static-web Pod by querying the kubelet's API endpoint at http://localhost:10255/pods and piping the output to jq '.items[].status.podIP'
title: Running Kubelet in Standalone Mode — Find out information about the kubelet and the Pod

instruction: How can I connect to an nginx server Pod using its IP address?
response: You can connect to the nginx server Pod by visiting http://<IP>:<Port>, where <IP> is the IP address of the Pod (e.g. http://10.85.0.4) and <Port> is the port number (default is 80)
title: Running Kubelet in Standalone Mode — Find out information about the kubelet and the Pod

instruction: What is the recommended way to distribute traffic to control plane nodes in a cloud environment?
response: In a cloud environment, you should place your control plane nodes behind a TCP forwarding load balancer.
title: Creating Highly Available Clusters with kubeadm — Command line interface

instruction: What is the default port for the kube-apiserver health check?
response: The default port for the kube-apiserver health check is 6443.
title: Creating Highly Available Clusters with kubeadm — Command line interface

instruction: How do you test the connection to the load balancer before the API server is running?
response: You can test the connection using the command: nc -zv -w 2 <LOAD_BALANCER_IP> <PORT>. A connection refused error is expected.
title: Creating Highly Available Clusters with kubeadm — Command line interface

instruction: What should you do if a timeout occurs when testing the connection to the load balancer?
response: If a timeout occurs, reconfigure the load balancer to communicate with the control plane node.
title: Creating Highly Available Clusters with kubeadm — Command line interface

instruction: What happens when a Pod is created with spec.nodeName already set and required ResourceClaims do not exist or are not allocated?
response: The kubelet will fail to run the Pod and re-check periodically because those requirements might still get fulfilled later.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — Pre-scheduled Pods

instruction: How can a Pod be made to run on a specific node while still going through the normal scheduling flow?
response: Create the Pod with a node selector that exactly matches the desired node, or mutate the incoming Pod at admission time to unset the spec.nodeName field and use a node selector instead.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — Pre-scheduled Pods

instruction: Why is it better to avoid bypassing the scheduler when creating a Pod?
response: A Pod that is assigned to a node blocks normal resources (RAM, CPU) that then cannot be used for other Pods while the Pod is stuck.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — Pre-scheduled Pods

instruction: What happens to a Pod's hostname and FQDN when hostnameOverride is set in the Pod spec?
response: When hostnameOverride is set in the Pod spec, the kubelet unconditionally sets both the Pod's hostname and fully qualified domain name (FQDN) to the hostnameOverride value.
title: Pod Hostname — Hostname with pod's hostnameOverride

instruction: What are the limitations and standards for the hostnameOverride field in a Pod spec?
response: The hostnameOverride field has a length limitation of 64 characters and must adhere to the DNS subdomain names standard defined in RFC 1123.
title: Pod Hostname — Hostname with pod's hostnameOverride

instruction: How does setting hostnameOverride affect the Pod's A and/or AAAA records in the cluster DNS server when hostname and subdomain fields are also set?
response: When hostnameOverride is set alongside hostname and subdomain fields, the hostname inside the Pod is overridden to the hostnameOverride value, but the Pod's A and/or AAAA records in the cluster DNS server are still generated based on the hostname and subdomain fields.
title: Pod Hostname — Hostname with pod's hostnameOverride

instruction: What happens when hostnameOverride is set along with hostNetwork and setHostnameAsFQDN fields?
response: If hostnameOverride is set, you cannot simultaneously set the hostNetwork and setHostnameAsFQDN fields. The API server will explicitly reject any create request attempting this combination.
title: Pod Hostname — Hostname with pod's hostnameOverride

instruction: What are the basic commands for creating Kubernetes objects?
response: The basic commands for creating Kubernetes objects are: create, clusterrole, clusterrolebinding, configmap, cronjob, deployment, ingress, job, namespace, poddisruptionbudget, priorityclass, quota, role, rolebinding, secret, secret docker-registry, secret generic, secret tls, service, service clusterip, service externalname, service loadbalancer, service nodeport, serviceaccount, token.
title: GETTING STARTED

instruction: What are the commands for managing applications in Kubernetes?
response: The commands for managing applications in Kubernetes are categorized under APP MANAGEMENT and include: apply, edit-last-applied, set-last-applied, view-last-applied, edit-last-applied, set-last-applied, view-last-applied, annotate, autoscale, debug, diff, edit, kustomize, label, patch, replace, rollout, history, pause, restart, resume, status, undo, history, pause, restart, resume, status, undo, scale, set, env, image, resources, selector, serviceaccount, subject, env, image, resources, selector, serviceaccount, subject, wait.
title: GETTING STARTED

instruction: What is the recommended way to access the kubectl reference documentation?
response: The recommended way to access the kubectl reference documentation is to use the new kubectl reference. This page is deprecated and will be removed in a future release. For a single-page view, see the printable version.
title: GETTING STARTED

instruction: What are the possible values for the 'operator' field in ContainerRestartRuleOnExitCodes?
response: The possible values for the 'operator' field are 'In' and 'NotIn'. 'In' satisfies the requirement if the container exit code is in the set of specified values, while 'NotIn' satisfies the requirement if the container exit code is not in the set of specified values.
title: Pod — ContainerRestartRuleOnExitCodes

instruction: What is the maximum number of elements allowed in the 'values' field of ContainerRestartRuleOnExitCodes?
response: At most 255 elements are allowed in the 'values' field.
title: Pod — ContainerRestartRuleOnExitCodes

instruction: How does the 'operator' field affect the interpretation of the 'values' field in ContainerRestartRuleOnExitCodes?
response: If the 'operator' is 'In', the container exit code must be in the set of specified 'values' to satisfy the requirement. If the 'operator' is 'NotIn', the container exit code must not be in the set of specified 'values' to satisfy the requirement.
title: Pod — ContainerRestartRuleOnExitCodes

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3/watch/resourcepoolstatusrequests request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: ResourcePoolStatusRequest — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3/watch/resourcepoolstatusrequests request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. The server may reject a continue value it does not recognize. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token.
title: ResourcePoolStatusRequest — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3/watch/resourcepoolstatusrequests request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent.
title: ResourcePoolStatusRequest — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `resourceVersionMatch` field interact with `resourceVersion` in the GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3/watch/resourcepoolstatusrequests request?
response: When `resourceVersionMatch` is set, it determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: ResourcePoolStatusRequest — getWatch List

instruction: What argument does the kubelet process accept to control automatic certificate rotation?
response: The kubelet process accepts the --rotate-certificates argument to control if it will automatically request a new certificate as the expiration of the current certificate approaches.
title: Configure Certificate Rotation for the Kubelet — Enabling client certificate rotation

instruction: How can the duration for which certificates are issued be controlled in the cluster?
response: The kube-controller-manager process accepts the --cluster-signing-duration argument (or --experimental-cluster-signing-duration prior to version 1.19) to control how long certificates will be issued for.
title: Configure Certificate Rotation for the Kubelet — Enabling client certificate rotation

instruction: What types of workloads can benefit from the optimizations provided by the Topology Manager in Kubernetes?
response: Workloads in fields such as telecommunications, scientific computing, machine learning, financial services, and data analytics can benefit from the optimizations provided by the Topology Manager.
title: Control Topology Management Policies on a node

instruction: What is the main goal of the Topology Manager in Kubernetes?
response: The main goal of the Topology Manager is to coordinate the set of components responsible for optimizations related to CPU isolation, memory, and device locality.
title: Control Topology Management Policies on a node

instruction: Why are optimizations related to CPU isolation, memory, and device locality required in a high-performance environment?
response: Optimizations related to CPU isolation, memory, and device locality are required to extract the best performance in a high-performance environment comprising a combination of CPUs and hardware accelerators.
title: Control Topology Management Policies on a node

instruction: What is a Kubernetes Pod and what resources does it provide to its containers?
response: A Pod is a Kubernetes abstraction that represents a group of one or more application containers and some shared resources for those containers. The shared resources include storage as Volumes, networking as a unique cluster IP address, and information about how to run each container.
title: Viewing Pods and Nodes — Kubernetes Pods

instruction: What happens to a Pod if the Node it is scheduled on fails?
response: If a Node fails, identical Pods are scheduled on other available Nodes in the cluster.
title: Viewing Pods and Nodes — Kubernetes Pods

instruction: Can containers in a Pod communicate with each other and how?
response: Yes, containers in a Pod can communicate with each other. They share an IP address and port space, and are always co-located and co-scheduled, running in a shared context on the same Node.
title: Viewing Pods and Nodes — Kubernetes Pods

instruction: What are the main differences in OS concepts between Linux and Windows that affect Kubernetes API compatibility?
response: The main differences are in identity, file permissions, file paths, and signals. Windows uses a binary security identifier (SID) for identity, an access control list based on SIDs for file permissions, and a different convention for file paths. Signals are handled differently on Windows, with UI threads, console apps, and services implementing various control handlers.
title: Windows containers in Kubernetes — API compatibility

instruction: How do file permissions and paths differ between Windows and Linux in Kubernetes?
response: Windows uses an access control list based on security identifiers (SIDs), whereas Linux uses a bitmask based on object permissions and UID+GID. Windows file paths use backslashes (\), while Linux uses forward slashes (/). The Go IO libraries can handle both, but setting paths or command lines inside a container may require using \.
title: Windows containers in Kubernetes — API compatibility

instruction: What are the limitations of using securityContext in Windows containers compared to Linux?
response: Several securityContext settings are not possible or have limited functionality on Windows, including allowPrivilegeEscalation, capabilities, privileged, procMount, readOnlyRootFilesystem, runAsGroup, runAsNonRoot, runAsUser (use runAsUserName instead), and seLinuxOptions. Some settings like runAsNonRoot have specific behavior on Windows, preventing containers from running as ContainerAdministrator.
title: Windows containers in Kubernetes — API compatibility

instruction: How do Pod specifications differ between Windows and Linux in terms of host namespace sharing and networking?
response: Host namespace sharing (hostIPC, hostpid) and host networking (hostNetwork) are not possible on Windows. Pods always run with a container network, and setting dnsPolicy to ClusterFirstWithHostNet is not supported on Windows.
title: Windows containers in Kubernetes — API compatibility

instruction: What is the purpose of a readiness probe in Kubernetes?
response: A readiness probe is used to detect situations where an application is temporarily unable to serve traffic, such as during startup or when depending on external services. It allows the pod to not receive traffic through Kubernetes Services until it reports that it is ready.
title: Configure Liveness, Readiness and Startup Probes — Define readiness probes

instruction: How do you configure a readiness probe in Kubernetes?
response: Readiness probes are configured similarly to liveness probes, but using the 'readinessProbe' field instead of 'livenessProbe'. The configuration can include exec, HTTP, or TCP probes, and parameters such as 'initialDelaySeconds' and 'periodSeconds'.
title: Configure Liveness, Readiness and Startup Probes — Define readiness probes

instruction: Can readiness and liveness probes be used together for the same container?
response: Yes, readiness and liveness probes can be used in parallel for the same container. This ensures that traffic does not reach a container that is not ready for it, and that containers are restarted when they fail.
title: Configure Liveness, Readiness and Startup Probes — Define readiness probes

instruction: How do I determine which Node a specific Pod, such as mysql-2, is currently running on?
response: You can use the command `kubectl get pod mysql-2 -o wide` to find out which Node the Pod is on. The Node name will be shown in the last column of the output.
title: Run a Replicated Stateful Application — Drain a Node

instruction: What command can I use to drain a Node in Kubernetes, and what options should I consider?
response: To drain a Node, you can use the command `kubectl drain <node-name> --force --delete-emptydir-data --ignore-daemonsets`. Be cautious with the impact on other workloads.
title: Run a Replicated Stateful Application — Drain a Node

instruction: How can I monitor the rescheduling of a Pod, such as mysql-2, after draining its Node?
response: You can watch the Pod's status and Node assignment change using the command `kubectl get pod mysql-2 -o wide --watch`.
title: Run a Replicated Stateful Application — Drain a Node

instruction: What command should I run to return a Node to a normal state after it has been drained and its Pods have been rescheduled?
response: To return a Node to a normal state, use the command `kubectl uncordon <node-name>`, replacing `<node-name>` with the actual name of the Node.
title: Run a Replicated Stateful Application — Drain a Node

instruction: What is the purpose of the ingress field in LoadBalancerStatus?
response: The ingress field in LoadBalancerStatus is a list containing ingress points for the load-balancer. Traffic intended for the service should be sent to these ingress points.
title: Service — LoadBalancerStatus

instruction: What information does PortStatus in LoadBalancerStatus record?
response: PortStatus in LoadBalancerStatus records the error condition of a service port, including the error message, port number, and protocol of the service port.
title: Service — LoadBalancerStatus

instruction: What are the supported protocol values for a service port in LoadBalancerStatus?
response: The supported protocol values for a service port in LoadBalancerStatus are: "TCP", "UDP", and "SCTP".
title: Service — LoadBalancerStatus

instruction: How should cloud provider specific error values be formatted in the error field of PortStatus?
response: Cloud provider specific error values must have names that comply with the format foo.example.com/CamelCase.
title: Service — LoadBalancerStatus

instruction: What is the purpose of the allocateLoadBalancerNodePorts field in a ServiceSpec?
response: The allocateLoadBalancerNodePorts field defines if NodePorts will be automatically allocated for services with type LoadBalancer. It defaults to true.
title: Service — ServiceSpec

instruction: How does the clusterIP field affect the creation of a Service?
response: If an address is specified manually for clusterIP, it must be in-range and not in use. Otherwise, creation of the service will fail. Setting this to 'None' makes a 'headless service'.
title: Service — ServiceSpec

instruction: What are the implications of setting externalTrafficPolicy to 'Local' in a ServiceSpec?
response: When externalTrafficPolicy is set to 'Local', the proxy will configure the service so that each node delivers traffic only to the node-local endpoints of the service, without masquerading the client source IP.
title: Service — ServiceSpec

instruction: What is the purpose of the healthCheckNodePort field in a ServiceSpec?
response: The healthCheckNodePort field specifies the healthcheck nodePort for the service, which is used by external systems to determine if a given node holds endpoints for this service.
title: Service — ServiceSpec

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubectl create clusterrolebinding command?
response: The kubectl create clusterrolebinding command is used to create a cluster role binding for a particular cluster role.
title: kubectl create clusterrolebinding — Synopsis

instruction: What are the required and optional flags for the kubectl create clusterrolebinding command?
response: The required flags are --clusterrole. The optional flags are --user, --group, --serviceaccount, and --dry-run.
title: kubectl create clusterrolebinding — Synopsis

instruction: What happens to containers on a node during a reboot?
response: During a node reboot, every container on the node stops. After the node boots, the kubelet and container runtime start again with no containers actually running, and the kubelet recreates them.
title: What Happens After A Node Restart — Impact of a node reboot

instruction: How can the impact of a planned reboot be reduced?
response: Before a planned reboot, the impact can be reduced by cordoning the node, so the scheduler stops placing new Pods on it, and then draining it to evict the existing Pods gracefully.
title: What Happens After A Node Restart — Impact of a node reboot

instruction: What happens to Pods managed by a controller if a node stays down longer than the configured toleration period?
response: If a node stays down longer than the configured toleration period, only Pods managed by a controller (such as a Deployment, StatefulSet, or DaemonSet) get a replacement Pod, which might schedule onto a different node.
title: What Happens After A Node Restart — Impact of a node reboot

instruction: What happens to local storage tied to the lifetime of a container or Pod during a node reboot?
response: Local storage tied to the lifetime of a container or Pod can be lost during a node reboot. A container's writable layer is discarded when the container is recreated, and data written there does not survive the reboot.
title: What Happens After A Node Restart — Impact of a node reboot

instruction: What is the purpose of the `pretty` parameter in the GET /apis/batch/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/cronjobs endpoint?
response: The `pretty` parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If set to `true`, the output will be pretty-printed. Defaults to `false` unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: CronJob — getList

instruction: What is the effect of setting `limit` and `continue` fields when making a list call?
response: Setting a limit may return fewer than the requested amount of items. If the continue field is empty, clients may assume that no more results are available. The server guarantees that the objects returned when using continue will be identical to issuing a single list call without a limit.
title: CronJob — getList

instruction: How does the `resourceVersion` and `resourceVersionMatch` parameters interact in list calls?
response: resourceVersion sets a constraint on what resource versions a request may be served from. resourceVersionMatch determines how resourceVersion is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that resourceVersionMatch be set for list calls where resourceVersion is set.
title: CronJob — getList

instruction: What is the main difference in handling resource requests and limits for init containers compared to app containers?
