response: The resource requests and limits for an init container are handled differently, as documented in Resource sharing within containers.
title: Init Containers — Differences from regular containers

instruction: Do regular init containers support lifecycle, livenessProbe, readinessProbe, or startupProbe fields?
response: No, regular init containers (excluding sidecar containers) do not support these fields.
title: Init Containers — Differences from regular containers

instruction: How does kubelet run multiple init containers specified for a Pod?
response: kubelet runs each init container sequentially. Each init container must succeed before the next can run.
title: Init Containers — Differences from regular containers

instruction: How can I increase the number of seats available across all priority levels for a particular kube-apiserver?
response: You can increase the values for the max-requests-inflight and max-mutating-requests-inflight flags, or horizontally scale the number of kube-apiserver instances.
title: API Priority and Fairness — Priority and fairness settings

instruction: How can I isolate non-essential or expensive requests that would starve a concurrency level if shared with other flows?
response: You can create a new FlowSchema whose subject matches the user making these requests or create a FlowSchema that matches what the request is, and map it to a PriorityLevelConfiguration with a low share of seats.
title: API Priority and Fairness — Priority and fairness settings

instruction: What is the effect of editing a default FlowSchema or PriorityLevelConfiguration?
response: Editing a default FlowSchema or PriorityLevelConfiguration will require setting the apf.kubernetes.io/autoupdate-spec annotation to false, and creating a new PriorityLevelConfiguration will reduce the number of seats designated for existing levels.
title: API Priority and Fairness — Priority and fairness settings

instruction: How can I increase the NominalConcurrencyShares for a PriorityLevelConfiguration serving high-priority requests in Kubernetes version 1.26 and later?
response: You can increase the NominalConcurrencyShares for the PriorityLevelConfiguration serving your high-priority requests, or increase the LendablePercent for competing priority levels so that the given priority level has a higher pool of seats it can borrow.
title: API Priority and Fairness — Priority and fairness settings

instruction: What is the default value of the 'name' field in NodeRegistrationOptions if not provided?
response: The default value of the 'name' field is the hostname of the node.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta3) — NodeRegistrationOptions

instruction: How can you prevent a control-plane node from being tainted during registration?
response: To prevent a control-plane node from being tainted, set the 'taints' field to an empty list, i.e., 'taints: []' in the YAML file.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta3) — NodeRegistrationOptions

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'kubeletExtraArgs' field in NodeRegistrationOptions?
response: The 'kubeletExtraArgs' field passes through extra arguments to the kubelet. These arguments are passed to the kubelet command line via the environment file kubeadm writes at runtime for the kubelet to source, overriding the generic base-level configuration in the 'kubelet-config' ConfigMap.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta3) — NodeRegistrationOptions

instruction: What are the allowed values for the 'imagePullPolicy' field in NodeRegistrationOptions?
response: The allowed values for the 'imagePullPolicy' field are 'Always', 'IfNotPresent', or 'Never'. If not set, kubeadm defaults it to 'IfNotPresent'.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta3) — NodeRegistrationOptions

instruction: What are the default RBAC policies for service accounts outside the kube-system namespace?
response: Default RBAC policies grant no permissions to service accounts outside the kube-system namespace, beyond the permissions given by API discovery roles.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — ServiceAccount permissions

instruction: What is the best practice for granting permissions to a ServiceAccount?
response: The best practice is to grant a role to an application-specific service account. This requires the application to specify a serviceAccountName in its pod spec, and for the service account to be created.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — ServiceAccount permissions

instruction: What are the implications of granting cluster-admin permissions to the default ServiceAccount in the kube-system namespace?
response: Granting cluster-admin permissions to the default ServiceAccount in the kube-system namespace allows add-ons to run with super-user access, but also means the kube-system namespace contains Secrets that grant super-user access to your cluster's API.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — ServiceAccount permissions

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /apis/autoscaling/v2/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/horizontalpodautoscalers request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /apis/autoscaling/v2/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/horizontalpodautoscalers request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is a server-defined value that clients may only use with identical query parameters. If the continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /apis/autoscaling/v2/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/horizontalpodautoscalers request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` are set, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler — getWatch List

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersionMatch` field in the GET /apis/autoscaling/v2/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/horizontalpodautoscalers request?
response: The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended to set `resourceVersionMatch` for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler — getWatch List

instruction: What is the purpose of the `pretty` parameter in the DELETE /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/clusterroles endpoint?
response: The `pretty` parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If set to `true`, the output will be pretty printed. Defaults to `false` unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: ClusterRole — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting `orphanDependents` to `true` or `false` in the DELETE /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/clusterroles endpoint?
response: If `orphanDependents` is set to `true` or `false`, the "orphan" finalizer will be added to/removed from the object's finalizers list. This field is deprecated in favor of `propagationPolicy`.
title: ClusterRole — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What are the acceptable values for the `propagationPolicy` field in the DELETE /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/clusterroles endpoint?
response: The acceptable values for `propagationPolicy` are: 'Orphan' - orphan the dependents; 'Background' - allow the garbage collector to delete the dependents in the background; 'Foreground' - a cascading policy that deletes all dependents in the foreground.
title: ClusterRole — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: How is the process ID limit for a Pod specified in Kubernetes?
response: The process ID limit for a Pod is specified at the node level, not as a resource limit for a particular Pod.
title: Process ID Limits And Reservations — Pod PID limits

instruction: How can you configure the process ID limit for a Node in Kubernetes?
response: You can configure the process ID limit for a Node by specifying the command line parameter --pod-max-pids to the kubelet, or by setting PodPidsLimit in the kubelet configuration file.
title: Process ID Limits And Reservations — Pod PID limits

instruction: Can different Nodes have different PID limits?
response: Yes, each Node can have a different PID limit.
title: Process ID Limits And Reservations — Pod PID limits

instruction: How can a container in a Pod create an HTTPS URL for the Kubernetes API server?
response: By fetching the KUBERNETES_SERVICE_HOST and KUBERNETES_SERVICE_PORT_HTTPS environment variables.
title: Accessing the Kubernetes API from a Pod — Directly accessing the REST API

instruction: What is the recommended way to authenticate to the API server from a Pod?
response: The recommended way is with a service account credential.
title: Accessing the Kubernetes API from a Pod — Directly accessing the REST API

instruction: Where can a Pod find the service account token, certificate bundle, and default namespace for API operations?
response: The service account token is placed at /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token, the certificate bundle at /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ca.crt, and the default namespace at /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/namespace.
title: Accessing the Kubernetes API from a Pod — Directly accessing the REST API

instruction: How do containers in a Pod share data with each other?
response: Containers in a Pod can share data by accessing a set of shared storage volumes specified in the Pod.
title: Pods — Resource sharing and communication

instruction: What happens to persistent data in a Pod if one of its containers needs to be restarted?
response: Persistent data in a Pod survives even if one of the containers within needs to be restarted, thanks to the use of volumes.
title: Pods — Resource sharing and communication

instruction: Where can I find more information on how Kubernetes implements shared storage for Pods?
response: You can find more information on how Kubernetes implements shared storage and makes it available to Pods in the Storage section of the documentation.
title: Pods — Resource sharing and communication

instruction: What is the purpose of NetworkPolicies in Kubernetes?
response: NetworkPolicies are used to restrict ingress and egress traffic for a workload.
title: Policies

instruction: What do LimitRanges manage in Kubernetes?
response: LimitRanges manage resource allocation constraints across different object kinds.
title: Policies

instruction: What is the function of ResourceQuotas in Kubernetes?
response: ResourceQuotas limit resource consumption for a namespace.
title: Policies

instruction: What are Kubernetes policies?
response: Kubernetes policies are configurations that manage other configurations or runtime behaviors.
title: Policies

instruction: How does the API server communicate with nodes in a Kubernetes cluster when using SSH tunnels?
response: The API server initiates an SSH tunnel to each node in the cluster, connecting to the SSH server listening on port 22, and passes all traffic destined for a kubelet, node, pod, or service through the tunnel.
title: Communication between Nodes and the Control Plane — SSH tunnels

instruction: What is the primary security benefit of using SSH tunnels for communication between the control plane and nodes in Kubernetes?
response: The tunnel ensures that the traffic is not exposed outside of the network in which the nodes are running.
title: Communication between Nodes and the Control Plane — SSH tunnels

instruction: What happens to the kubelet after a node restart?
response: The kubelet's sync loop is relevant to understanding what happens after a node restart, and can be learned about in the provided documentation.
title: What Happens After A Node Restart — What's next

instruction: How can I safely remove pods from a node before restarting it?
response: You can learn how to safely drain a node, which involves safely removing pods from the node before restarting it.
title: What Happens After A Node Restart — What's next

instruction: What is node-pressure eviction in Kubernetes?
response: Node-pressure eviction is a concept that can be learned about in the provided documentation, which may be relevant to understanding node restarts.
title: What Happens After A Node Restart — What's next

instruction: What are the stages of a pod's life cycle?
response: The pod life cycle is discussed in the provided documentation, which can help understand what happens to pods during a node restart.
title: What Happens After A Node Restart — What's next

instruction: What happens to workloads when the NoSwap behavior is chosen and the kubelet is configured to tolerate swap space?
response: Your workloads don't use any swap. However, processes outside of Kubernetes-managed containers can utilize swap.
title: Swap memory management — Swap behaviors

instruction: Can processes outside of Kubernetes-managed containers utilize swap when the NoSwap behavior is chosen?
response: Yes, processes outside of Kubernetes-managed containers, such as systemd services and even the kubelet itself, can utilize swap.
title: Swap memory management — Swap behaviors

instruction: Can different nodes in a Kubernetes cluster use different swap behaviors?
response: Yes, different nodes in your cluster can use different swap behaviors.
title: Swap memory management — Swap behaviors

instruction: What types of API objects can be referenced in the spec of a static Pod?
response: None, a static Pod's spec cannot refer to other API objects.
title: Static Pods — Limitations

instruction: What type of containers are not supported by static Pods?
response: Ephemeral containers are not supported by static Pods.
title: Static Pods — Limitations

instruction: When running a Pod on every node in a clustered Kubernetes setup, what is the recommended approach instead of using static Pods?
response: You should probably be using a DaemonSet instead of static Pods.
title: Static Pods — Limitations

instruction: Why are static Pods suitable for bootstrapping control plane components?
response: Static Pods are started by the kubelet before the API server is available.
title: Static Pods — Limitations

instruction: What happens to ephemeral volumes when a Pod ceases to exist?
response: When a Pod ceases to exist, Kubernetes destroys ephemeral volumes.
title: Volumes — How volumes work

instruction: How do you specify volumes for a Pod and mount them into containers?
response: To use a volume, specify the volumes to provide for the Pod in .spec.volumes and declare where to mount those volumes into containers in .spec.containers[*].volumeMounts.
title: Volumes — How volumes work

instruction: Can volumes mount within other volumes?
response: No, volumes cannot mount within other volumes.
title: Volumes — How volumes work

instruction: What is preserved across container restarts for any kind of volume in a given Pod?
response: Data is preserved across container restarts for any kind of volume in a given Pod.
title: Volumes — How volumes work

instruction: What should I do if I want to report a problem or suggest an improvement for Kubernetes?
response: If you want to report a problem or suggest an improvement, you should open an issue in the GitHub Repository.
title: Ingress Controllers — What's next

instruction: Who is responsible for third-party products or projects referenced on Kubernetes pages?
response: The Kubernetes project authors aren't responsible for third-party products or projects that provide functionality required by Kubernetes.
title: Ingress Controllers — What's next

instruction: What is the purpose of defining a cluster role for a GMSA credential spec resource in Kubernetes?
response: To authorize the use verb on a specific GMSA resource by a subject, typically a service account.
title: Configure GMSA for Windows Pods and containers — Configure cluster role to enable RBAC on specific GMSA credential specs

instruction: What is the name of the cluster role and the GMSA credential spec resource defined in the example YAML file?
response: The cluster role name is 'webapp1-role' and the GMSA credential spec resource name is 'gmsa-WebApp1'.
title: Configure GMSA for Windows Pods and containers — Configure cluster role to enable RBAC on specific GMSA credential specs

instruction: How do you apply the cluster role configuration to your Kubernetes cluster?
response: Save the configuration to a file (e.g., 'gmsa-webapp1-role.yaml') and apply it using the command 'kubectl apply -f gmsa-webapp1-role.yaml'.
title: Configure GMSA for Windows Pods and containers — Configure cluster role to enable RBAC on specific GMSA credential specs

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in a GET /apis/apps/v1/watch/statefulsets request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers may ignore this flag if they do not implement bookmarks, and clients should not assume bookmarks are returned at any specific interval.
title: StatefulSet — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in a GET /apis/apps/v1/watch/statefulsets request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is a server-defined value that clients may only use with identical query parameters, and the server may reject an unrecognized continue value. If the continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: StatefulSet — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` in a GET /apis/apps/v1/watch/statefulsets request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event reporting the ResourceVersion.
title: StatefulSet — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: How does the `resourceVersionMatch` field interact with `resourceVersion` in a GET /apis/apps/v1/watch/statefulsets request?
response: When `resourceVersionMatch` is set, it determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended to set `resourceVersionMatch` for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set. The `resourceVersionMatch` value 'NotOlderThan' is interpreted as 'data at least as new as the provided `resourceVersion`'.
title: StatefulSet — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the effect of setting the kubernetes.io/enforce-mountable-secrets annotation to true on a ServiceAccount?
response: When set to true, Kubernetes enforces rules that Secrets mounted as volumes, referenced in envFrom, or used in imagePullSecrets must be listed in the ServiceAccount's secrets field. Pods failing to comply will fail to start with an error message.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — kubernetes.io/enforce-mountable-secrets (deprecated)

instruction: What happens to a running Pod if the kubernetes.io/enforce-mountable-secrets annotation is changed to true or a referenced Secret is removed from the ServiceAccount?
response: The Pod continues to run. The new rules only apply when a Pod is created or updated.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — kubernetes.io/enforce-mountable-secrets (deprecated)

instruction: Can a Pod reference a Secret not listed in the ServiceAccount's secrets field if the reference is marked as optional?
response: No, if a Pod references a Secret not listed in the ServiceAccount's secrets field, even if marked as optional, the Pod will fail to start and an error will be generated.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — kubernetes.io/enforce-mountable-secrets (deprecated)

instruction: How can you set up external reverse proxying with a Service of type LoadBalancer?
response: You can use a Service set to type: LoadBalancer as a way to set up external reverse proxying, where the connection from client to load balancer is TLS encrypted and the load balancer is the TLS server peer.
title: Protocols for Services — TLS

instruction: What protocol is typically set for a Service when using a load balancer as a TLS server?
response: Typically, you set the protocol to TCP and set an annotation (usually specific to your cloud provider) that configures the load balancer to act as a TLS server.
title: Protocols for Services — TLS

instruction: How is the TLS identity configured for a load balancer acting as a TLS server?
response: You would configure the TLS identity (as server, and possibly also as a client that connects to your workload) using mechanisms that are specific to your cloud provider.
title: Protocols for Services — TLS

instruction: What is the default validity period of a bootstrap token created by kubeadm init?
response: The default token created by kubeadm init has a limited validity of 24 hours.
title: Implementation details — Configure TLS-Bootstrapping for node joining

instruction: How does kubeadm ensure that users in the system:bootstrappers:kubeadm:default-node-token group can access the certificate signing API?
response: Kubeadm ensures that users in system:bootstrappers:kubeadm:default-node-token group are able to access the certificate signing API by creating a ClusterRoleBinding named kubeadm:kubelet-bootstrap between the group above and the default RBAC role system:node-bootstrapper.
title: Implementation details — Configure TLS-Bootstrapping for node joining

instruction: What role is required to be created to grant POST permission to /apis/certificates.k8s.io/certificatesigningrequests/nodeclient?
response: The role system:certificates.k8s.io:certificatesigningrequests:nodeclient should be created, granting POST permission to /apis/certificates.k8s.io/certificatesigningrequests/nodeclient.
title: Implementation details — Configure TLS-Bootstrapping for node joining

instruction: How does kubeadm handle certificate rotation for nodes?
response: Kubeadm ensures that certificate rotation is enabled for nodes, and that a new certificate request for nodes will get its CSR request automatically approved by the csrapprover controller through a ClusterRoleBinding named kubeadm:node-autoapprove-certificate-rotation.
title: Implementation details — Configure TLS-Bootstrapping for node joining

instruction: What is the purpose of the PodCertificateRequest API in Kubernetes?
response: The PodCertificateRequest API is used by kubelets to implement podCertificate projected volumes, allowing a pod to request a certificate from a given signer.
title: PodCertificateRequest

instruction: What are the main fields in a PodCertificateRequest object?
response: The main fields in a PodCertificateRequest object are: apiVersion, kind, metadata, spec, and status. The spec field contains details about the certificate being requested, and the status field contains the issued certificate and a set of conditions.
title: PodCertificateRequest

instruction: What is the significance of the apiVersion field in a PodCertificateRequest object?
response: The apiVersion field defines the versioned schema of the PodCertificateRequest object. Servers will convert recognized schemas to the latest internal value and may reject unrecognized values.
title: PodCertificateRequest

instruction: What are the key differences between ephemeral containers and regular containers in Kubernetes?
response: Ephemeral containers lack guarantees for resources or execution, and they will never be automatically restarted. They are not suitable for building applications.
title: Ephemeral Containers — What is an ephemeral container?

instruction: Which fields are disallowed for ephemeral containers?
response: Fields such as ports, livenessProbe, readinessProbe, and resources are disallowed for ephemeral containers. For a complete list of allowed fields, see the EphemeralContainer reference documentation.
title: Ephemeral Containers — What is an ephemeral container?

instruction: How are ephemeral containers created in Kubernetes?
response: Ephemeral containers are created using a special ephemeralcontainers handler in the API, rather than by adding them directly to pod.spec. Therefore, it's not possible to add an ephemeral container using kubectl edit.
title: Ephemeral Containers — What is an ephemeral container?

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /api/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/limitranges request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: LimitRange — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /api/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/limitranges request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is server-defined and clients may only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: LimitRange — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /api/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/limitranges request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent.
title: LimitRange — getWatch List

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'driver' field in OpaqueDeviceConfiguration?
response: The 'driver' field is used to determine which kubelet plugin needs to be passed these configuration parameters.
title: DeviceClass — OpaqueDeviceConfiguration

instruction: What are the requirements for the 'driver' field in OpaqueDeviceConfiguration?
response: The 'driver' field must be a DNS subdomain, should end with a DNS domain owned by the vendor of the driver, and should use only lower case characters.
title: DeviceClass — OpaqueDeviceConfiguration

instruction: What are the constraints on the 'parameters' field in OpaqueDeviceConfiguration?
response: The 'parameters' field can contain arbitrary data, but its length must be smaller or equal to 10 Ki, and it is the responsibility of the driver developer to handle validation and versioning.
title: DeviceClass — OpaqueDeviceConfiguration

instruction: What are the required certificates and their corresponding key usages for a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The required certificates include kube-etcd, kube-etcd-peer, kube-etcd-healthcheck-client, kube-apiserver-etcd-client, kube-apiserver, kube-apiserver-kubelet-client, and front-proxy-client. Their key usages are as follows: server - digital signature, key encipherment, server auth; client - digital signature, key encipherment, client auth.
title: PKI certificates and requirements — All certificates

instruction: What is the difference in certificate generation for kubeadm users with external etcd versus a standard setup?
response: For kubeadm users with external etcd, the certificates kube-etcd, kube-etcd-peer, and kube-etcd-healthcheck-client are not generated.
title: PKI certificates and requirements — All certificates

instruction: What is the scenario referred to as 'external CA' in the kubeadm documentation?
response: The scenario where you are copying CA certificates to your cluster without private keys is referred to as 'external CA' in the kubeadm documentation.
title: PKI certificates and requirements — All certificates

instruction: What is the purpose of the subPath field in a volume mount?
response: The subPath field allows specific files or directories from the mounted PersistentVolume to be exposed at different locations within the container.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a PersistentVolume for Storage — Clean up

instruction: How many volume mounts will be made on the nginx container in the given example?
response: Two volume mounts will be made on the nginx container: one for the static website at /usr/share/nginx/html and another for the default config at /etc/nginx/nginx.conf.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a PersistentVolume for Storage — Clean up

instruction: How can a cloud-native component authenticate to another application within the same Kubernetes cluster without using a Secret?
response: You can use a ServiceAccount and its tokens to identify your client.
title: Secrets — Alternatives to Secrets

instruction: What are some alternatives to using Secrets for managing sensitive data in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Alternatives include using ServiceAccounts, third-party tools that manage sensitive data, custom signers for X.509 certificates, device plugins to expose node-local encryption hardware, or combining multiple options.
title: Secrets — Alternatives to Secrets

instruction: Can you use a device plugin to expose node-local encryption hardware to a specific Pod?
response: Yes, you can use a device plugin to expose node-local encryption hardware to a specific Pod, for example, scheduling trusted Pods onto nodes that provide a Trusted Platform Module, configured out-of-band.
title: Secrets — Alternatives to Secrets

instruction: How can an operator be used to manage short-lived session tokens for Pods in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: An operator can fetch short-lived session tokens from an external service, create Secrets based on those tokens, and ensure they are valid for Pods running in the cluster.
title: Secrets — Alternatives to Secrets

instruction: How can I provision a new volume pre-populated with data from a snapshot in Kubernetes?
response: You can provision a new volume pre-populated with data from a snapshot by using the dataSource field in the PersistentVolumeClaim object.
title: Volume Snapshots — Provisioning Volumes from Snapshots

instruction: Where can I find more details about volume snapshot and restoring a volume from a snapshot?
response: For more details, see Volume Snapshot and Restore Volume from Snapshot.
title: Volume Snapshots — Provisioning Volumes from Snapshots

instruction: What happens to a Pod if the node it is scheduled to run on crashes before the Pod starts?
response: If Kubernetes isn't able to start the Pod on the selected node (for example, if the node crashes before the Pod starts), then that particular Pod never starts.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Pod lifetime

instruction: Can a Pod be scheduled to run on multiple nodes during its lifetime?
response: No, Pods are only scheduled once in their lifetime; assigning a Pod to a specific node is called binding.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Pod lifetime

instruction: How does Kubernetes handle faults within a Pod?
response: While a Pod is running, the kubelet is able to restart containers to handle some kind of faults. Within a Pod, Kubernetes tracks different container states and determines what action to take to make the Pod healthy again.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Pod lifetime

instruction: What is the purpose of Pod Scheduling Readiness?
response: You can use Pod Scheduling Readiness to delay scheduling for a Pod until all its scheduling gates are removed. For example, you might want to define a set of Pods but only trigger scheduling once all the Pods have been created.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Pod lifetime

instruction: What is the last Kubernetes release in which you can still use the Accelerators feature gate?
response: The last Kubernetes release in which you can still use the Accelerators feature gate is 1.10.
title: Feature Gates (removed) — Feature gates that are removed

instruction: When was the AdmissionWebhookMatchConditions feature gate promoted to GA?
response: The AdmissionWebhookMatchConditions feature gate was promoted to GA in Kubernetes release 1.30, and it was removed in release 1.32.
title: Feature Gates (removed) — Feature gates that are removed

instruction: What is the Kubernetes release when the AppArmor feature gate was promoted to GA?
response: The AppArmor feature gate was promoted to GA in Kubernetes release 1.31, and it is still available.
title: Feature Gates (removed) — Feature gates that are removed

instruction: In which Kubernetes release was the CSIMigrationPortworx feature gate promoted to GA?
response: The CSIMigrationPortworx feature gate was promoted to GA in Kubernetes release 1.33.
title: Feature Gates (removed) — Feature gates that are removed

instruction: How do you project Pod-level fields into a running container as environment variables?
response: You can project Pod-level fields into a running container as environment variables by using the `env` field in the container specification, where each environment variable definition has a `valueFrom` section with a `fieldRef` that specifies the Pod field to use as the value.
title: Expose Pod Information to Containers Through Environment Variables — Use Pod fields as values for environment variables

instruction: What is the purpose of the `fieldPath` in a `fieldRef`?
response: The `fieldPath` in a `fieldRef` specifies the path to the Pod field that should be used as the value for the environment variable. For example, `spec.nodeName` refers to the node name of the Pod, while `metadata.name` refers to the name of the Pod.
title: Expose Pod Information to Containers Through Environment Variables — Use Pod fields as values for environment variables

instruction: How can you verify that the environment variables have been successfully set in the container?
response: You can verify that the environment variables have been successfully set in the container by executing a shell inside the container using `kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -- sh` and then running the `printenv` command to view the environment variables.
title: Expose Pod Information to Containers Through Environment Variables — Use Pod fields as values for environment variables

instruction: What is the effect of the command and args fields in the container configuration file?
response: The command and args fields in the container configuration file specify that when the container starts, it writes the values of five environment variables to stdout every ten seconds.
title: Expose Pod Information to Containers Through Environment Variables — Use Pod fields as values for environment variables

instruction: What is the primary difference between sidecar containers and application containers in a Kubernetes pod?
response: Sidecar containers do not execute the primary application logic; instead, they provide supporting functionality to the main application.
title: Sidecar Containers — Differences from application containers

instruction: Can sidecar containers be updated, scaled, or maintained independently of application containers?
response: Yes, sidecar containers have their own independent lifecycles and can be started, stopped, and restarted independently of app containers.
title: Sidecar Containers — Differences from application containers

instruction: What happens to sidecar containers during pod termination in Kubernetes?
response: Sidecar containers receive the SIGTERM signal, followed by the SIGKILL signal, before they have time to terminate gracefully, and exit codes different from 0 are normal and should be generally ignored by external tooling.
title: Sidecar Containers — Differences from application containers

instruction: How long must a deprecated behavior in Kubernetes function after its announced deprecation?
response: A deprecated behavior in Kubernetes must function for no less than 1 year after its announced deprecation.
title: Kubernetes Deprecation Policy — Deprecating a feature or behavior

instruction: Can a generally available feature be deprecated in favor of a Beta replacement?
response: No, a generally available feature cannot be deprecated in favor of a Beta replacement. The replacement must not be less stable than the feature being deprecated.
title: Kubernetes Deprecation Policy — Deprecating a feature or behavior

instruction: What happens to a feature gate as a feature moves to the GA stage?
response: As a feature moves to the GA stage, the associated feature gate is deprecated and becomes non-operational. After the deprecation window is complete, the feature gate is removed and calls to it are no longer accepted.
title: Kubernetes Deprecation Policy — Deprecating a feature or behavior

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/watch/networkpolicies request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: NetworkPolicy — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/watch/networkpolicies request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is server-defined and clients may only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: NetworkPolicy — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/watch/networkpolicies request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent.
title: NetworkPolicy — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersionMatch` field in the GET /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/watch/networkpolicies request?
response: The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: NetworkPolicy — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What happens to a request that is not rejected by other authentication methods when anonymous access is enabled?
response: The request is treated as an anonymous request, given a username of system:anonymous and a group of system:unauthenticated.
title: Authenticating — Anonymous requests

instruction: How can anonymous access be disabled on the API server?
response: Anonymous access can be disabled by passing the --anonymous-auth=false command line option to the API server.
title: Authenticating — Anonymous requests

instruction: Is anonymous access enabled by default, and under what conditions?
response: Anonymous access is enabled by default if an authorization mode other than AlwaysAllow is used.
title: Authenticating — Anonymous requests

instruction: What is the purpose of the devicePath field in a volumeDevice?
response: The devicePath field specifies the path inside of the container that the device will be mapped to.
title: Pod — VolumeDevice

instruction: How is the mount propagation of a volume mount determined?
response: The mount propagation of a volume mount is determined by the mountPropagation field, which can take on values of "Bidirectional", "HostToContainer", or "None". If not set, MountPropagationNone is used.
title: Pod — VolumeDevice

instruction: What is the effect of setting recursiveReadOnly to IfPossible or Enabled in a volume mount?
response: If recursiveReadOnly is set to IfPossible or Enabled, the mount is made recursively read-only if it is supported by the container runtime. If set to IfPossible or Enabled, MountPropagation must be set to None or unspecified.
title: Pod — VolumeDevice

instruction: What is the relationship between subPath and subPathExpr in a volume mount?
response: subPath and subPathExpr are mutually exclusive. subPathExpr allows environment variable references to be expanded using the container's environment, while subPath does not.
title: Pod — VolumeDevice

instruction: How do you increase the number of Pods in a Kubernetes Deployment?
response: You can increase the number of Pods in your Deployment by applying a new YAML file that sets the 'replicas' field to the desired number of Pods.
title: Run a Stateless Application Using a Deployment — Scaling the application by increasing the replica count

instruction: What command can be used to verify that a Deployment has the expected number of Pods?
response: You can use the command 'kubectl get pods -l app=nginx' to verify that the Deployment has the expected number of Pods.
title: Run a Stateless Application Using a Deployment — Scaling the application by increasing the replica count

instruction: What is the command to apply a new YAML file to scale a Deployment?
response: The command to apply a new YAML file to scale a Deployment is 'kubectl apply -f <yaml_file>'.
title: Run a Stateless Application Using a Deployment — Scaling the application by increasing the replica count

instruction: How do the kube-scheduler and internal ResourceClaim controller impact API server calls, memory, and CPU utilization compared to non-DRA scheduled Pods?
response: The kube-scheduler and internal ResourceClaim controller increase the number of API server calls, memory, and CPU utilization for Pods using DRA claims compared to non-DRA scheduled Pods.
title: Good practices for Dynamic Resource Allocation as a Cluster Admin — Monitor and tune components for higher load, especially in high scale environments

instruction: What are the critical QPS and Burst parameters to consider during Pod scheduling with DRA in the kube-controller-manager?
response: The QPS and Burst parameters in the client-go configuration within kube-controller-manager are critical during Pod scheduling with DRA.
title: Good practices for Dynamic Resource Allocation as a Cluster Admin — Monitor and tune components for higher load, especially in high scale environments

instruction: How can cluster administrators determine the optimal QPS and Burst values for kube-controller-manager in a DRA-enabled cluster?
response: The optimal QPS and Burst values depend on factors like number of nodes/pods, rate of pod creation, and churn. Monitoring metrics and referring to the SIG Scalability README on Kubernetes scalability thresholds can help. For example, in a scale test with 100 nodes, 720 long-lived pods, and 80 churn pods, kube-controller-manager QPS could be set to 75 and Burst to 150.
title: Good practices for Dynamic Resource Allocation as a Cluster Admin — Monitor and tune components for higher load, especially in high scale environments

instruction: What metrics should be monitored to better understand the performance impact of DRA on a deployment?
response: Monitoring metrics related to kube-scheduler, kube-controller-manager, and kubelet can help understand the performance impact of DRA. Refer to the Kubernetes Metrics Reference for more information on stable metrics in Kubernetes.
title: Good practices for Dynamic Resource Allocation as a Cluster Admin — Monitor and tune components for higher load, especially in high scale environments

instruction: What types of API objects can bound service account tokens be tied to?
response: Bound service account tokens can be tied to Pod, Secret, and Node API objects.
title: Managing Service Accounts — Bound service account tokens

instruction: What happens to authentication with a bound token if the referenced object is pending deletion for 60 seconds or more?
response: If the referenced object or the ServiceAccount is pending deletion for 60 seconds or more, authentication with that token will fail.
title: Managing Service Accounts — Bound service account tokens

instruction: What information is stored as extra 'private claims' in the issued JWT when a token is bound to an object?
response: The object's metadata.name and metadata.uid are stored as extra 'private claims' in the issued JWT.
title: Managing Service Accounts — Bound service account tokens

instruction: Under what condition will a request with a bound token not be authenticated?
response: A request with a bound token will not be authenticated if the referenced object no longer exists or its metadata.uid does not match.
title: Managing Service Accounts — Bound service account tokens

instruction: What verbs are required to create and retrieve a CertificateSigningRequest?
response: The verbs required to create and retrieve a CertificateSigningRequest are create, get, list, and watch, for the resource certificatesigningrequests in the group certificates.k8s.io.
title: Certificates and Certificate Signing Requests — Certificate signing authorization

instruction: What permissions are needed to approve a CertificateSigningRequest?
response: To approve a CertificateSigningRequest, you need to have the following permissions: get, list, watch on certificatesigningrequests; update on certificatesigningrequests/approval; and approve on signers with a specific resourceName.
title: Certificates and Certificate Signing Requests — Certificate signing authorization

instruction: How can you authorize a user to sign a CertificateSigningRequest for a specific signer?
response: You can authorize a user to sign a CertificateSigningRequest for a specific signer by granting them the sign verb on the signers resource with a specific resourceName, such as example.com/my-signer-name, or using a wildcard like example.com/* to authorize for all signers in the 'example.com' domain.
title: Certificates and Certificate Signing Requests — Certificate signing authorization

instruction: What ClusterRole is used to authorize a bootstrapping node to create and retrieve a certificate signing request (CSR)?
response: The ClusterRole used for this purpose is system:node-bootstrapper.
title: TLS bootstrapping — Authorize kubelet to create CSR

instruction: How do you authorize the system:bootstrappers group to create CSRs in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: You create a ClusterRoleBinding that binds the system:bootstrappers group to the system:node-bootstrapper ClusterRole.
title: TLS bootstrapping — Authorize kubelet to create CSR

instruction: What is the name of the ClusterRoleBinding that enables bootstrapping nodes to create CSRs?
response: The name of the ClusterRoleBinding is create-csrs-for-bootstrapping.
title: TLS bootstrapping — Authorize kubelet to create CSR

instruction: Which PodSecurityPolicy fields are not covered by the Pod Security Standards and can be removed?
response: The fields that can be removed are: .spec.allowedHostPaths, .spec.allowedFlexVolumes, .spec.allowedCSIDrivers, .spec.forbiddenSysctls, .spec.runtimeClass, .spec.runAsGroup, .spec.supplementalGroups, and .spec.fsGroup.
title: Migrate from PodSecurityPolicy to the Built-In PodSecurity Admission Controller — 2.b. Eliminate options not covered by the Pod Security Standards

instruction: What mutating fields in PodSecurityPolicy are required to support the Pod Security Standards?
response: The mutating fields required to support the Pod Security Standards are: .spec.requiredDropCapabilities, .spec.seLinux, .spec.runAsUser, and .spec.allowPrivilegeEscalation. These fields will need to be handled on a case-by-case basis.
title: Migrate from PodSecurityPolicy to the Built-In PodSecurity Admission Controller — 2.b. Eliminate options not covered by the Pod Security Standards

instruction: Why might an admission webhook be needed in addition to Pod Security Admission?
response: An admission webhook might be needed if you must enforce options not covered by the Pod Security Standards.
title: Migrate from PodSecurityPolicy to the Built-In PodSecurity Admission Controller — 2.b. Eliminate options not covered by the Pod Security Standards

instruction: What is the default value that Kubelet uses for enforcement of allocatable resources across pods?
response: The default value is 'pods'. This means Kubelet enforces 'Allocatable' across pods by default.
title: Reserve Compute Resources for System Daemons — Enforcing Node Allocatable

instruction: How does Kubelet enforce 'Allocatable' across pods when the overall usage exceeds 'Allocatable'?
response: Kubelet enforces 'Allocatable' by evicting pods whenever the overall usage across all pods exceeds 'Allocatable'. More details on eviction policy can be found on the node pressure eviction page.
title: Reserve Compute Resources for System Daemons — Enforcing Node Allocatable

instruction: What additional values can be specified in the KubeletConfiguration setting 'enforceNodeAllocatable' to enforce kubeReserved and systemReserved?
response: The values 'kube-reserved' and 'system-reserved' can be specified in the same setting to enforce kubeReserved and systemReserved respectively.
title: Reserve Compute Resources for System Daemons — Enforcing Node Allocatable

instruction: What is required to enforce kubeReserved or systemReserved?
response: To enforce kubeReserved or systemReserved, kubeReservedCgroup or systemReservedCgroup needs to be specified respectively.
title: Reserve Compute Resources for System Daemons — Enforcing Node Allocatable

instruction: What happens when a VolumeSnapshot is created without specifying a VolumeSnapshotClass?
response: Kubernetes automatically selects a default VolumeSnapshotClass that has a CSI driver matching the CSI driver of the PVC's StorageClass.
title: Volume Snapshot Classes — VolumeSnapshotClass dependencies

instruction: What is a requirement for multiple default VolumeSnapshotClass objects to coexist in a cluster?
response: Each default VolumeSnapshotClass object must be associated with a unique CSI driver.
title: Volume Snapshot Classes — VolumeSnapshotClass dependencies

instruction: What happens if multiple default VolumeSnapshotClass objects are created with the same CSI driver?
response: A VolumeSnapshot creation will fail because Kubernetes cannot determine which one to use.
title: Volume Snapshot Classes — VolumeSnapshotClass dependencies

instruction: What is the default value of .spec.backoffLimit for a Job if not set?
response: The default value of .spec.backoffLimit for a Job is 6.
title: Jobs — Pod backoff failure policy

instruction: How is the number of retries calculated for a Job to determine if it should be considered failed?
response: The number of retries is calculated in two ways: 1) The number of Pods with .status.phase = "Failed". 2) When using restartPolicy = "OnFailure", the number of retries in all the containers of Pods with .status.phase equal to Pending or Running.
title: Jobs — Pod backoff failure policy

instruction: What happens to Failed Pods associated with a Job when the Job controller recreates them?
response: Failed Pods associated with the Job are recreated by the Job controller with an exponential back-off delay (10s, 20s, 40s ...) capped at six minutes.
title: Jobs — Pod backoff failure policy

instruction: What is the default value of .spec.backoffLimit when .spec.backoffLimitPerIndex is specified for an Indexed Job?
response: When .spec.backoffLimitPerIndex is specified, .spec.backoffLimit defaults to 2147483647 (MaxInt32).
title: Jobs — Pod backoff failure policy

instruction: How do you run an nginx Deployment and expose it using kubectl?
response: To run an nginx Deployment and expose it, you can use the commands 'kubectl create deployment --image=nginx nginx-app' to start the pod running nginx, and then 'kubectl expose deployment nginx-app --port=80 --name=nginx-http' to expose a port through a service.
title: kubectl for Docker Users — docker run

instruction: What is the equivalent of 'docker run -d --restart=always -e DOMAIN=cluster --name nginx-app -p 80:80 nginx' using kubectl?
response: The equivalent is achieved by two commands: 'kubectl create deployment --image=nginx nginx-app' to start the pod running nginx with a Deployment, and 'kubectl set env deployment/nginx-app DOMAIN=cluster' to set the environment variable DOMAIN=cluster. Exposing the port is done with 'kubectl expose deployment nginx-app --port=80 --name=nginx-http'.
title: kubectl for Docker Users — docker run

instruction: How do you run a pod in the foreground and attach to it using kubectl?
response: To run a pod in the foreground and attach to it, you can use 'kubectl run [i] [--tty] --attach <name> --image=<image>'. This allows you to attach stdin, stdout, and stderr to the container.
title: kubectl for Docker Users — docker run

instruction: How do you set a quota for the total number of Pods in a namespace?
response: You can set a quota for the total number of Pods in a namespace using the syntax 'count/pods'.
title: Resource Quotas — Quota on object count

instruction: What is the purpose of setting a quota on the number of Secrets in a namespace?
response: Setting a quota on the number of Secrets in a namespace helps protect against exhaustion of control plane storage, as Secrets can be large in size and too many can prevent servers and controllers from starting.
title: Resource Quotas — Quota on object count

instruction: What is the difference between 'count/pods' and 'pods' quota syntax?
response: 'count/pods' is a general syntax for setting quota on the total number of Pods, while 'pods' quota syntax specifically counts and enforces a maximum on the number of Pods created in a single namespace that are not terminal.
title: Resource Quotas — Quota on object count

instruction: Does the core Kubernetes control plane enforce quota for APIs added through API aggregation?
response: No, the core Kubernetes control plane does not enforce quota for APIs added through API aggregation; the extension API server is expected to provide quota enforcement if needed.
title: Resource Quotas — Quota on object count

instruction: What is the purpose of the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-nlb-target-type annotation in a Kubernetes Service?
response: The AWS load balancer controller uses this annotation.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-nlb-target-type (beta)

instruction: On which Kubernetes object is the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-nlb-target-type annotation used?
response: This annotation is used on a Service.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-nlb-target-type (beta)

instruction: Where can I find more information about the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-nlb-target-type annotation?
response: See annotations in the AWS load balancer controller documentation.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-nlb-target-type (beta)

instruction: What is the delivery guarantee for Kubernetes lifecycle hooks?
response: Hook delivery is intended to be at least once, meaning a hook may be called multiple times for any given event.
title: Container Lifecycle Hooks — Hook delivery guarantees

instruction: Can a Kubernetes lifecycle hook be called multiple times for the same event?
response: Yes, a hook may be called multiple times for any given event, such as PostStart or PreStop.
title: Container Lifecycle Hooks — Hook delivery guarantees

instruction: What happens if a kubelet restarts while sending a hook?
response: If a kubelet restarts in the middle of sending a hook, the hook might be resent after the kubelet comes back up.
title: Container Lifecycle Hooks — Hook delivery guarantees

instruction: What are the key characteristics of an HA cluster with external etcd topology?
response: In an HA cluster with external etcd topology, each control plane node runs an instance of kube-apiserver, kube-scheduler, and kube-controller-manager. Etcd members run on separate hosts, and each etcd host communicates with the kube-apiserver of each control plane node.
title: Options for Highly Available Topology — External etcd topology

instruction: How does the external etcd topology affect cluster redundancy compared to the stacked HA topology?
response: The external etcd topology decouples the control plane and etcd member, providing an HA setup where losing a control plane instance or an etcd member has less impact and does not affect the cluster redundancy as much as the stacked HA topology.
title: Options for Highly Available Topology — External etcd topology

instruction: What are the minimum host requirements for an HA cluster with external etcd topology?
response: A minimum of three hosts for control plane nodes and three hosts for etcd nodes are required for an HA cluster with this topology, making a total of six hosts.
title: Options for Highly Available Topology — External etcd topology

instruction: Which container runtimes support running Kubernetes inside Rootless Docker or Rootless Podman?
response: kind and minikube support running Kubernetes inside Rootless Docker or Rootless Podman.
title: Running Kubernetes Node Components as a Non-root User — kind

instruction: What is Sysbox and how does it support running Kubernetes?
response: Sysbox is an open-source container runtime that supports running system-level workloads such as Docker and Kubernetes inside unprivileged containers isolated with the Linux user namespace. It supports running Kubernetes inside unprivileged containers without requiring Cgroup v2 and without the KubeletInUserNamespace feature gate.
title: Running Kubernetes Node Components as a Non-root User — kind

instruction: How does Sysbox enable running Kubernetes inside unprivileged containers?
response: Sysbox exposes specially crafted /proc and /sys filesystems inside the container plus several other advanced OS virtualization techniques.
title: Running Kubernetes Node Components as a Non-root User — kind

instruction: What happens to the kubelet when upgrading the Kubernetes version on a node?
response: The kubelet restarts.
title: Container Runtime Interface (CRI) — Upgrading

instruction: Why does the kubelet fail to register if the container runtime does not support the v1 CRI API?
response: The kubelet fails to register and reports an error if the container runtime does not support the v1 CRI API.
title: Container Runtime Interface (CRI) — Upgrading

instruction: What is required for a gRPC re-dial to succeed after the container runtime has been upgraded?
response: The container runtime must support the v1 CRI API for the connection to succeed.
title: Container Runtime Interface (CRI) — Upgrading

instruction: What might be necessary after the container runtime is correctly configured to support the v1 CRI API?
response: A restart of the kubelet might be required.
title: Container Runtime Interface (CRI) — Upgrading

instruction: What is the purpose of the `pretty` parameter in the DELETE /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ingressclasses endpoint?
response: The `pretty` parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If set to `true`, the output will be pretty-printed. Defaults to `false` unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: IngressClass — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting `dryRun` to `All` in the DELETE /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ingressclasses endpoint?
response: When `dryRun` is set to `All`, all dry run stages will be processed. This indicates that modifications should not be persisted.
title: IngressClass — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the `propagationPolicy` field in the DELETE /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ingressclasses endpoint?
response: The `propagationPolicy` field determines whether and how garbage collection will be performed. Acceptable values are: 'Orphan', 'Background', and 'Foreground'. This field or `OrphanDependents` may be set, but not both.
title: IngressClass — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting `ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential` to `true` in the DELETE /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ingressclasses endpoint?
response: If set to `true`, it will trigger an unsafe deletion of the resource in case the normal deletion flow fails with a corrupt object error. This may potentially break the cluster if the workload associated with the resource being unsafe-deleted relies on normal deletion flow.
title: IngressClass — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /api/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/resourcequotas/{name} request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: ResourceQuota — getWatch

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /api/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/resourcequotas/{name} request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. The server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error if the continue value is no longer valid. Clients may use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters to retrieve the next set of results.
title: ResourceQuota — getWatch

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /api/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/resourcequotas/{name} request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event.
title: ResourceQuota — getWatch

instruction: What is the recommended alternative to using Secrets for authenticating as a ServiceAccount?
response: ServiceAccount token volume projection.
title: Configure Service Accounts for Pods — What's next

instruction: Where can I read about background on OIDC discovery?
response: The ServiceAccount signing key retrieval Kubernetes Enhancement Proposal or the OIDC Discovery Spec.
title: Configure Service Accounts for Pods — What's next

instruction: What is the effect of CertificateSubjectRestriction on CertificateSigningRequest resources?
response: It rejects any request that specifies a 'group' (or 'organization attribute') of system:masters for CertificateSigningRequest resources with a spec.signerName of kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client.
title: Admission Control in Kubernetes — CertificateSubjectRestriction

instruction: What does the default ingress class admission controller do to Ingress objects?
response: It observes creation of Ingress objects that do not request any specific ingress class and automatically adds a default ingress class to them.
title: Admission Control in Kubernetes — CertificateSubjectRestriction

instruction: What happens when more than one ingress class is marked as default?
response: The admission controller rejects any creation of Ingress with an error and an administrator must revisit their IngressClass objects and mark only one as default (with the annotation "ingressclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class").
title: Admission Control in Kubernetes — CertificateSubjectRestriction

instruction: How does Pod failure policy help in avoiding unnecessary Pod retries in a Job?
response: Pod failure policy helps in avoiding unnecessary Pod retries by defining rules that determine when a Pod failure is considered non-retriable. For example, if a Pod fails with a specific exit code, the policy can be set to fail the Job immediately instead of retrying the Pod.
title: Handling retriable and non-retriable pod failures with Pod failure policy — Usage scenarios

instruction: What happens to a Job if Pod failure policy is disabled and a Pod fails with an exit code that would have been considered non-retriable with the policy enabled?
response: If Pod failure policy is disabled, the Job will retry the Pod until it reaches the backoffLimit. In the example given, this would result in a delay of at least 9 minutes before the Job fails, due to exponential backoff and parallelism.
title: Handling retriable and non-retriable pod failures with Pod failure policy — Usage scenarios

instruction: How can you verify that a Job has been terminated due to Pod failure policy?
response: You can verify that a Job has been terminated due to Pod failure policy by inspecting the Job status. The Job status will display conditions such as 'FailureTarget' and 'Failed' with a reason field set to 'PodFailurePolicy' and a message field with more information about the termination.
title: Handling retriable and non-retriable pod failures with Pod failure policy — Usage scenarios

instruction: What is the default value of the 'name' field in NodeRegistrationOptions?
response: The default value of the 'name' field is the hostname of the node if not provided.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta4) — NodeRegistrationOptions

instruction: How can I prevent a control-plane node from being tainted during registration?
response: To prevent a control-plane node from being tainted, set the 'taints' field to an empty list, i.e., 'taints: []' in the YAML file.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta4) — NodeRegistrationOptions

instruction: What are the allowed values for the 'imagePullPolicy' field in NodeRegistrationOptions?
response: The allowed values for the 'imagePullPolicy' field are 'Always', 'IfNotPresent', or 'Never'. If this field is unset, kubeadm defaults it to 'IfNotPresent'.
title: kubeadm Configuration (v1beta4) — NodeRegistrationOptions

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /apis/apps/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/controllerrevisions/{name} request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: ControllerRevision — getWatch

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /apis/apps/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/controllerrevisions/{name} request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. The server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error if the continue value is no longer valid. Clients may use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters to retrieve the next set of results.
title: ControllerRevision — getWatch

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /apis/apps/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/controllerrevisions/{name} request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event.
title: ControllerRevision — getWatch

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersionMatch` field in the GET /apis/apps/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/controllerrevisions/{name} request?
response: The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: ControllerRevision — getWatch

instruction: What is the purpose of a startup probe in Kubernetes?
response: A startup probe verifies whether the application within a container is started, allowing the application time to finish its initialization before liveness or readiness probes are executed.
title: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes — Types of probe

instruction: How does the kubelet react if a startup probe fails?
response: If the startup probe fails, the kubelet kills the container, and the container is subjected to its restart policy.
title: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes — Types of probe

instruction: When are liveness and readiness probes executed if a startup probe is configured?
response: Kubernetes does not execute liveness or readiness probes until the startup probe succeeds.
title: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes — Types of probe

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'pretty' parameter in the RoleBinding putReplace request?
response: The 'pretty' parameter is used to pretty-print the output. It defaults to 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: RoleBinding — putReplace

instruction: What are the valid values for the 'fieldValidation' parameter in the RoleBinding putReplace request?
response: The valid values for 'fieldValidation' are: 'Ignore', 'Warn', and 'Strict'. 'Ignore' ignores unknown and duplicate fields. 'Warn' sends a warning for unknown and duplicate fields. 'Strict' fails the request with a BadRequest error if there are unknown or duplicate fields.
title: RoleBinding — putReplace

instruction: What is the significance of the 'fieldManager' field in the RoleBinding putReplace request?
response: The 'fieldManager' field is a name associated with the actor or entity making changes. It must be less than 128 characters long and contain only printable characters.
title: RoleBinding — putReplace

instruction: What are the possible status responses for a successful RoleBinding putReplace request?
response: The possible status responses for a successful RoleBinding putReplace request are 200 (OK) and 201 (Created), both returning the RoleBinding object.
title: RoleBinding — putReplace

instruction: How can an application set custom Pod conditions for Pod readiness?
response: Applications and operators should use the PATCH action on the Pod's status subresource. This can be achieved using kubectl patch with --subresource=status, or a Kubernetes client library.
title: Pod Conditions — Status for Pod readiness

instruction: What are the conditions for a Pod to be considered ready when using custom conditions?
response: A Pod is evaluated to be ready only when two conditions are met: all containers in the Pod are ready, and all conditions specified in readinessGates are True.
title: Pod Conditions — Status for Pod readiness

instruction: What happens to a Pod's Ready condition when its containers are Ready but at least one custom condition is missing or False?
response: The kubelet sets the Pod's Ready condition to status: "False" with reason: ReadinessGatesNotReady.
title: Pod Conditions — Status for Pod readiness

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'pretty' parameter in the PATCH /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/roles/{name} request?
response: The 'pretty' parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If 'true', the output will be pretty printed. It defaults to 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: Role — patchPatch

instruction: What are the valid values for the 'fieldValidation' parameter in the PATCH /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/roles/{name} request?
response: The valid values for 'fieldValidation' are: 'Ignore', 'Warn', and 'Strict'. 'Ignore' ignores unknown and duplicate fields. 'Warn' sends a warning for unknown and duplicate fields. 'Strict' fails the request with a BadRequest error if there are unknown or duplicate fields.
title: Role — patchPatch

instruction: Where do the kubelet and container runtime write logs on Linux nodes that use systemd?
response: On Linux nodes that use systemd, the kubelet and container runtime write to journald by default.
title: Logging Architecture — Log locations

instruction: How can I change the directory where kubelet writes pod logs on a node?
response: You can change the pod logs directory from the default /var/log/pods to a custom path by configuring the podLogsDir parameter in the kubelet's configuration file.
title: Logging Architecture — Log locations

instruction: Does Kubernetes manage log rotation for cluster components that write logs to a shared volume?
response: No, Kubernetes does not manage log rotation for cluster components that write logs to a shared volume. You need to consider and ensure that those logs are rotated.
title: Logging Architecture — Log locations

instruction: Where does the kubelet write logs on Windows nodes by default?
response: By default, the kubelet writes logs to files within the directory C:\var\logs.
title: Logging Architecture — Log locations

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' option in the DELETE collection API?
response: The continue option should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It allows clients to retrieve the next set of results from a previous query with identical query parameters.
title: MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'propagationPolicy' field in the DELETE collection API?
response: The 'propagationPolicy' field determines whether and how garbage collection will be performed. It can be set to 'Orphan', 'Background', or 'Foreground' to control the deletion of dependents.
title: MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential' to true in the DELETE collection API?
response: If set to true, it will trigger an unsafe deletion of the resource in case the normal deletion flow fails with a corrupt object error. This may potentially break the cluster if the workload associated with the resource being unsafe-deleted relies on normal deletion flow.
title: MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What conditions must a Job meet to have the Job controller create a Workload with a gang scheduling policy?
response: The Job must meet the following conditions: .spec.parallelism is greater than 1, .spec.completionMode is Indexed, .spec.parallelism equals .spec.completions, and .spec.template.spec.schedulingGroup is not set.
title: Jobs — Integrate with Workload APIs

instruction: How does the Job controller schedule Pods for a Job that meets the conditions for gang scheduling when the WorkloadWithJob feature gate is enabled?
response: The Job controller creates a Workload with a gang scheduling policy and a PodGroup object before creating any Pods, ensuring that all Pods in the Job are scheduled together or none are scheduled.
title: Jobs — Integrate with Workload APIs

instruction: What happens to the Workload and PodGroup objects created by the Job controller when the Job is deleted?
response: The Workload and PodGroup objects are automatically garbage collected when the Job is deleted, as they are owned by the Job via ownerReferences.
title: Jobs — Integrate with Workload APIs

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'pretty' parameter in the PATCH /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/persistentvolumeclaims/{name}/status request?
response: The 'pretty' parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If 'true', the output will be pretty printed. It defaults to 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).

instruction: What are the valid values for the 'fieldValidation' parameter in the PATCH /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/persistentvolumeclaims/{name}/status request?
response: The valid values for 'fieldValidation' are: 'Ignore', 'Warn', and 'Strict'. 'Ignore' ignores unknown fields and duplicate fields. 'Warn' sends a warning for unknown fields and duplicate fields. 'Strict' fails the request with a BadRequest error if there are unknown fields or duplicate fields.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — patchPatch Status

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'force' to 'true' in the PATCH /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/persistentvolumeclaims/{name}/status request?
response: Setting 'force' to 'true' allows the user to re-acquire conflicting fields owned by other people. However, the 'force' flag must be unset for non-apply patch requests.
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — patchPatch Status

instruction: What are the possible HTTP status code responses for a successful PATCH /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/persistentvolumeclaims/{name}/status request?
response: The possible HTTP status code responses for a successful request are 200 (OK) and 201 (Created).
title: PersistentVolumeClaim — patchPatch Status

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' option in the DELETE /api/v1/persistentvolumes endpoint?
response: The continue option should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It allows clients to retrieve the next set of results from a previous query.

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'dryRun' to 'All' in the DELETE /api/v1/persistentvolumes endpoint?
response: When 'dryRun' is set to 'All', all dry run stages will be processed, and modifications will not be persisted.
title: PersistentVolume — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'propagationPolicy' field in the DELETE /api/v1/persistentvolumes endpoint?
response: The 'propagationPolicy' field determines whether and how garbage collection will be performed. It can be set to 'Orphan', 'Background', or 'Foreground'.
title: PersistentVolume — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential' to true in the DELETE /api/v1/persistentvolumes endpoint?
response: If set to true, it will trigger an unsafe deletion of the resource in case the normal deletion flow fails with a corrupt object error. This may potentially break the cluster.

instruction: What authorization tools should be used to control access to Dynamic Resource Allocation APIs?
response: Authorization tools like RBAC, or another solution, should be used to control access to the right APIs depending on the persona of the user.
title: Good practices for Dynamic Resource Allocation as a Cluster Admin

instruction: Who should have access to DeviceClasses and ResourceSlices in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: DeviceClasses and ResourceSlices should be restricted to admins and the DRA drivers.
title: Good practices for Dynamic Resource Allocation as a Cluster Admin

instruction: What APIs do cluster operators need access to in order to deploy Pods with claims?
response: Cluster operators that will be deploying Pods with claims will need access to ResourceClaim and ResourceClaimTemplate APIs, both of which are namespace scoped.
title: Good practices for Dynamic Resource Allocation as a Cluster Admin

instruction: How does a Node get added to the API server?
response: There are two main ways to have Nodes added to the API server: 1) the kubelet on a node self-registers to the control plane, or 2) a human user manually adds a Node object.
title: Nodes — Management

instruction: What checks does the control plane perform on a new Node object?
response: The control plane checks whether the new Node object is valid by verifying that a kubelet has registered to the API server that matches the metadata.name field of the Node, and that the node is healthy (i.e., all necessary services are running).
title: Nodes — Management

instruction: What happens to a Node object that is deemed invalid?
