response: Kubernetes keeps the object for the invalid Node and continues checking to see whether it becomes healthy. The node is ignored for any cluster activity until it becomes healthy. You, or a controller, must explicitly delete the Node object to stop that health checking.
title: Nodes — Management

instruction: What is a requirement for the name of a Node object?
response: The name of a Node object must be a valid DNS subdomain name.
title: Nodes — Management

instruction: What is the purpose of the `pretty` parameter in the GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/pods request?
response: The `pretty` parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If set to 'true', the output will be pretty-printed. Defaults to 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: Pod — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersion` and `resourceVersionMatch` parameters in the GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/pods request?
response: The `resourceVersion` parameter sets a constraint on what resource versions a request may be served from. The `resourceVersionMatch` parameter determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: Pod — getList

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/pods request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent.
title: Pod — getList

instruction: What API version of CRI does the kubelet require for Kubernetes v1.26 and later?
response: For Kubernetes v1.26 and later, the kubelet requires that the container runtime supports the v1 CRI API.
title: Container Runtime Interface (CRI) — The API

instruction: What happens if a container runtime does not support the v1 CRI API?
response: If a container runtime does not support the v1 API, the kubelet will not register the node.
title: Container Runtime Interface (CRI) — The API

instruction: How can the container runtime endpoint be configured within the kubelet?
response: The container runtime endpoint can be configured separately within the kubelet by using the --container-runtime-endpoint command line flag.
title: Container Runtime Interface (CRI) — The API

instruction: What are the implications of granting access to the nodes/proxy sub-resource in Kubernetes?
response: Granting access to the nodes/proxy sub-resource allows users to execute commands on every pod on the node(s) to which they have rights, bypassing audit logging and admission control.
title: Role Based Access Control Good Practices — Access toproxysubresource of Nodes

instruction: Is get permission on nodes/proxy a read-only permission?
response: No, get permission on nodes/proxy is not a read-only permission. It provides access to privileged kubelet APIs that can retrieve container logs or execute and attach to pod processes.
title: Role Based Access Control Good Practices — Access toproxysubresource of Nodes

instruction: How can the nodes/proxy sub-resource APIs be exercised?
response: The nodes/proxy sub-resource APIs can be exercised via websocket HTTP GET requests, which only requires authorization of the get verb.
title: Role Based Access Control Good Practices — Access toproxysubresource of Nodes

instruction: How does ZooKeeper ensure durability of its data?
response: ZooKeeper commits all entries to a durable Write-Ahead Log (WAL) and periodically writes snapshots of in-memory state to storage media.
title: Running ZooKeeper, A Distributed System Coordinator — Providing durable storage

instruction: What happens to the PersistentVolumes when the StatefulSet's Pods are recreated?
response: When the StatefulSet recreates its Pods, it remounts the Pods' PersistentVolumes. The volumeMounts section of the StatefulSet's container template mounts the PersistentVolumes in the ZooKeeper servers' data directories, ensuring that all writes made to the ZooKeeper servers' WALs and snapshots remain durable.
title: Running ZooKeeper, A Distributed System Coordinator — Providing durable storage

instruction: How can you verify that the ZooKeeper ensemble still serves the original value after terminating and recreating all of the Pods in the zk StatefulSet?
response: You can use the command 'kubectl exec zk-2 zkCli.sh get /hello' to get the value that was entered during the sanity test, which will show that the ensemble still serves the original value.
title: Running ZooKeeper, A Distributed System Coordinator — Providing durable storage

instruction: What is the purpose of the volumeClaimTemplates field in the zk StatefulSet's spec?
response: The volumeClaimTemplates field specifies a PersistentVolume provisioned for each Pod, which is used to provide durable storage for the ZooKeeper servers' data directories.
title: Running ZooKeeper, A Distributed System Coordinator — Providing durable storage

instruction: What is the purpose of creating a SOCKS5 tunnel in this task?
response: The purpose of creating a SOCKS5 tunnel is to allow HTTPS traffic between the client and the Kubernetes API to flow over the tunnel, which is itself tunneled over SSH.
title: Use a SOCKS5 Proxy to Access the Kubernetes API — Task context

instruction: What components are involved in the SOCKS5 tutorial setup?
response: The components involved are a local client machine with a local SSH SOCKS5 proxy, a remote server with an SSH server, and the Kubernetes API.
title: Use a SOCKS5 Proxy to Access the Kubernetes API — Task context

instruction: How does the client communicate with the Kubernetes API in this setup?
response: The client communicates with the Kubernetes API by sending proxied HTTPS traffic through the local SSH SOCKS5 proxy, which establishes an SSH tunnel with the remote SSH server, and then the traffic is forwarded to the Kubernetes API.
title: Use a SOCKS5 Proxy to Access the Kubernetes API — Task context

instruction: Why should cluster administrators configure authorization for Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) components?
response: Cluster administrators should configure authorization for DRA components because they update ResourceClaim status, and this requires explicit, least-privilege RBAC.
title: Hardening Guide - Dynamic Resource Allocation

instruction: What changes were made to DRA status updates in Kubernetes v1.36?
response: In Kubernetes v1.36, DRA status updates began using synthetic subresources and, in some cases, specialized node-aware verbs.
title: Hardening Guide - Dynamic Resource Allocation

instruction: What type of RBAC should be configured for DRA components updating ResourceClaim status?
response: Explicit, least-privilege RBAC should be configured for DRA components updating ResourceClaim status.
title: Hardening Guide - Dynamic Resource Allocation

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /apis/policy/v1/watch/poddisruptionbudgets request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: PodDisruptionBudget — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /apis/policy/v1/watch/poddisruptionbudgets request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. The server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error if the continue value is no longer valid. Clients may use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters to retrieve the next set of results.
title: PodDisruptionBudget — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /apis/policy/v1/watch/poddisruptionbudgets request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` are set, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event.
title: PodDisruptionBudget — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersionMatch` field in the GET /apis/policy/v1/watch/poddisruptionbudgets request?
response: The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: PodDisruptionBudget — getWatch List All Namespaces

instruction: What type of PersistentVolume is suitable for a production cluster?
response: In a production cluster, you would not use hostPath. Instead, a cluster administrator would provision a network resource like a Google Compute Engine persistent disk, an NFS share, or an Amazon Elastic Block Store volume.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a PersistentVolume for Storage — Create a PersistentVolume

instruction: What is the access mode of the hostPath PersistentVolume created in the example?
response: The access mode of the hostPath PersistentVolume is ReadWriteOnce, which means the volume can be mounted as read-write by a single Node.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a PersistentVolume for Storage — Create a PersistentVolume

instruction: What is the initial status of a newly created PersistentVolume?
response: The initial status of a newly created PersistentVolume is Available, indicating that it has not yet been bound to a PersistentVolumeClaim.
title: Configure a Pod to Use a PersistentVolume for Storage — Create a PersistentVolume

instruction: How can I simulate an increased load on the php-apache service to test the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler?
response: You can simulate an increased load by running a client Pod with a container that sends queries to the php-apache service in an infinite loop. This can be achieved by executing the command: kubectl run -i --tty load-generator --rm --image=busybox:1.28 --restart=Never -- /bin/sh -c "while sleep 0.01; do wget -q -O- http://php-apache; done"
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler Walkthrough — Increase the load

instruction: What is the expected outcome on the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler when the CPU load increases beyond the target value?
response: When the CPU load increases beyond the target value, the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler will scale up the Deployment. For example, if the target CPU utilization is 50% and the actual CPU consumption increases to 305%, the autoscaler will resize the Deployment to a higher number of replicas, such as from 1 to 7 replicas.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler Walkthrough — Increase the load

instruction: How can I verify that the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler has successfully scaled up the Deployment?
response: You can verify that the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler has successfully scaled up the Deployment by running kubectl get hpa php-apache --watch and observing the increase in replicas. Additionally, you can run kubectl get deployment php-apache to confirm that the replica count matches the figure from the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler Walkthrough — Increase the load

instruction: What is the default filesystem type used when mounting a Cinder volume if fsType is not specified?
response: The default filesystem type used is 'ext4'.
title: PersistentVolume — CinderPersistentVolumeSource

instruction: What are the requirements for a Cinder volume to be mounted to a container in Kubernetes?
response: A Cinder volume must exist before mounting to a container and must be in the same region as the kubelet.
title: PersistentVolume — CinderPersistentVolumeSource

instruction: What is the purpose of the secretRef field in the CinderPersistentVolumeSource?
response: The secretRef field points to a secret object containing parameters used to connect to OpenStack.
title: PersistentVolume — CinderPersistentVolumeSource

instruction: What happens to the ReadOnly setting in VolumeMounts if readOnly is set to true in the CinderPersistentVolumeSource?
response: If readOnly is set to true, it will force the ReadOnly setting in VolumeMounts.
title: PersistentVolume — CinderPersistentVolumeSource

instruction: How do you grant the default service account in the kube-system namespace full privilege to the API using ABAC?
response: To grant full privilege, add a policy line to your policy file with the following content: {"apiVersion": "abac.authorization.kubernetes.io/v1beta1", "kind": "Policy", "spec": {"user": "system:serviceaccount:kube-system:default", "namespace": "*", "resource": "*", "apiGroup": "*"}}
title: Using ABAC Authorization — A quick note on service accounts

instruction: What action is required for the apiserver to pick up new policy lines?
response: The apiserver will need to be restarted to pick up the new policy lines.
title: Using ABAC Authorization — A quick note on service accounts

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in a GET request to `/apis/apps/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/statefulsets`?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers may ignore this flag if they do not implement bookmarks, and clients should not assume bookmarks are returned at any specific interval.
title: StatefulSet — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in a list call to retrieve more results from the server?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is a server-defined value that clients may only use with identical query parameters, except for the value of `continue`. If the `continue` value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: StatefulSet — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in a GET request to `/apis/apps/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/statefulsets`?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event. The bookmark will report the ResourceVersion corresponding to the set of objects.
title: StatefulSet — getWatch List

instruction: What are the four supported allocation policies for the Topology Manager in Kubernetes?
response: The four supported policies are: none, best-effort, restricted, and single-numa-node.
title: Control Topology Management Policies on a node — Topology manager policies

instruction: What is the default policy for the Topology Manager, and what does it do?
response: The default policy is 'none'. This policy does not perform any topology alignment.
title: Control Topology Management Policies on a node — Topology manager policies

instruction: How does the 'best-effort' policy handle container placement when the preferred NUMA Node affinity is not available?
response: With the 'best-effort' policy, if the preferred NUMA Node affinity for a container is not available, the Topology Manager stores this information but still admits the pod to the node. The Hint Providers can then use this information when making resource allocation decisions.
title: Control Topology Management Policies on a node — Topology manager policies

instruction: How do you specify the ingress class for an Ingress object?
response: You specify the ingress class for an Ingress object by setting the ingressClassName field on the Ingress object to the .metadata.name of your ingress class resource.
title: Ingress Controllers — Using multiple Ingress controllers

instruction: What happens if you don't specify an IngressClass for an Ingress and your cluster has exactly one IngressClass marked as default?
response: If you don't specify an IngressClass for an Ingress and your cluster has exactly one IngressClass marked as default, then Kubernetes applies the cluster's default IngressClass to the Ingress.
title: Ingress Controllers — Using multiple Ingress controllers

instruction: How do you mark an IngressClass as default?
response: You mark an IngressClass as default by setting the ingressclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class annotation on that IngressClass to the string value "true".
title: Ingress Controllers — Using multiple Ingress controllers

instruction: What is the HTTP method and endpoint to retrieve an IngressClass by name?
response: The HTTP method is GET and the endpoint is /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ingressclasses/{name}.
title: IngressClass — getRead

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'pretty' query parameter in the GET /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ingressclasses/{name} request?
response: The 'pretty' query parameter is used to pretty-print the output if set to 'true'. It defaults to 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: IngressClass — getRead

instruction: What is the expected HTTP status code and response body when successfully retrieving an IngressClass?
response: The expected HTTP status code is 200 OK and the response body is the IngressClass object.
title: IngressClass — getRead

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'resource' field in IngressBackend?
response: The 'resource' field is an ObjectRef to another Kubernetes resource in the namespace of the Ingress object. If 'resource' is specified, 'service.Name' and 'service.Port' must not be specified.
title: Ingress — IngressBackend

instruction: Can 'resource' and 'service' fields be specified simultaneously in IngressBackend?
response: No, 'resource' and 'service' fields are mutually exclusive settings. If 'resource' is specified, 'service' must not be specified, and vice versa.
title: Ingress — IngressBackend

instruction: What are the recommended steps to update an immutable ConfigMap in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: To update an immutable ConfigMap, you should either change the name of the ConfigMap and switch to running Pods that reference the new name, replace all the nodes in your cluster that have previously run a Pod that used the old value, or restart the kubelet on any node where the kubelet previously loaded the old ConfigMap.
title: Updating Configuration via a ConfigMap — Update configuration via an immutable ConfigMap that is mounted as a volume

instruction: How do you update a Deployment to reference a new immutable ConfigMap?
response: To update a Deployment to reference a new immutable ConfigMap, edit the Deployment using 'kubectl edit deployment', update the existing volume definition to use the new ConfigMap, and save the changes. This will trigger a rollout, and new Pods will be created referencing the new ConfigMap.
title: Updating Configuration via a ConfigMap — Update configuration via an immutable ConfigMap that is mounted as a volume

instruction: What happens to old Pods after updating a Deployment to reference a new immutable ConfigMap?
response: After updating a Deployment to reference a new immutable ConfigMap, the old Pods will terminate, and new Pods will be created referencing the new ConfigMap. You can monitor the status of the Pods using 'kubectl get pods' to ensure that all old Pods have terminated and new Pods are in a ready state.
title: Updating Configuration via a ConfigMap — Update configuration via an immutable ConfigMap that is mounted as a volume

instruction: What should be done with the old immutable ConfigMap after all Deployments have been updated to use the new one?
response: Once all Deployments have migrated to use the new immutable ConfigMap, it is advised to delete the old one using 'kubectl delete configmap'.
title: Updating Configuration via a ConfigMap — Update configuration via an immutable ConfigMap that is mounted as a volume

instruction: What is the purpose of the GetAllocatableResources gRPC endpoint in Kubernetes device plugins?
response: The GetAllocatableResources endpoint provides information on resources initially available on the worker node, offering more details than what kubelet exports to APIServer. It should be used to evaluate allocatable resources on a node.
title: Device Plugins — GetAllocatableResourcesgRPC endpoint

instruction: How should the GetAllocatableResources endpoint be used in conjunction with other endpoints to evaluate free/unallocated resources on a node?
response: GetAllocatableResources should be used in conjunction with the List() endpoint to evaluate free/unallocated resources on a node.
title: Device Plugins — GetAllocatableResourcesgRPC endpoint

instruction: What happens to the result obtained by GetAllocatableResources when the underlying resources exposed to kubelet change?
response: The result obtained by GetAllocatableResources remains the same unless the underlying resources exposed to kubelet change. When changes occur, such as hotplug/hotunplug or device health changes, the client is expected to call the GetAllocatableResources endpoint again.
title: Device Plugins — GetAllocatableResourcesgRPC endpoint

instruction: How should a device monitoring agent be deployed and configured to access the pod-resources directory and kubelet.sock?
response: A device monitoring agent can be deployed as a daemon or as a DaemonSet. It must run in a privileged security context, and the pod-resources directory must be mounted as a Volume in the device monitoring agent's PodSpec. When accessing pod-resources/kubelet.sock from a DaemonSet or other app deployed as a container, it is recommended to mount the pod-resources directory instead of the socket file itself.
title: Device Plugins — GetAllocatableResourcesgRPC endpoint

instruction: What is the default format for log messages in LoggingConfiguration?
response: The default format for log messages is 'text'.
title: kube-proxy Configuration (v1alpha1) — LoggingConfiguration

instruction: How is the flush frequency for log messages specified in LoggingConfiguration?
response: The flush frequency can be specified as a string parsed as a duration (e.g. '1s') or as an integer representing the maximum number of nanoseconds (e.g. 1s = 1000000000).
title: kube-proxy Configuration (v1alpha1) — LoggingConfiguration

instruction: What is the purpose of the verbosity field in LoggingConfiguration?
response: The verbosity field determines which log messages are logged, with a default value of zero that logs only the most important messages. Higher values enable additional messages, and error messages are always logged.
title: kube-proxy Configuration (v1alpha1) — LoggingConfiguration

instruction: Are VModule configuration and format options available for all log formats?
response: No, VModule configuration is only supported for the 'text' log format, and format options are specific to the selected format and only available when the LoggingAlphaOptions feature gate is enabled.
title: kube-proxy Configuration (v1alpha1) — LoggingConfiguration

instruction: What is the purpose of the `pretty` parameter in the DELETE /apis/certificates.k8s.io/v1/certificatesigningrequests endpoint?
response: The `pretty` parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If set to `true`, the output will be pretty-printed, but this is disabled by default unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool like curl or wget.
title: CertificateSigningRequest — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: How does the `continue` parameter work in the DELETE /apis/certificates.k8s.io/v1/certificatesigningrequests endpoint?
response: The `continue` parameter is used to retrieve more results from the server. It should be set to the value provided by the server in the `continue` field of the list metadata. The server may reject a `continue` value it does not recognize, and clients may need to restart their list without the `continue` field if the value is no longer valid.
title: CertificateSigningRequest — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting `dryRun` to `All` in the DELETE /apis/certificates.k8s.io/v1/certificatesigningrequests endpoint?
response: Setting `dryRun` to `All` indicates that all dry run stages should be processed. This allows the request to be validated without actually persisting any modifications.
title: CertificateSigningRequest — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the `propagationPolicy` parameter in the DELETE /apis/certificates.k8s.io/v1/certificatesigningrequests endpoint?
response: The `propagationPolicy` parameter determines how garbage collection will be performed. It can be set to 'Orphan', 'Background', or 'Foreground', and determines how dependent objects will be handled during deletion.
title: CertificateSigningRequest — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What are the two key components of a VolumeAttributesClass in Kubernetes?
response: A VolumeAttributesClass contains two key components: driverName and parameters. These are used when a PersistentVolume (PV) belonging to the class needs to be dynamically provisioned or modified.
title: Volume Attributes Classes — The VolumeAttributesClass API

instruction: Can the parameters of an existing VolumeAttributesClass object be changed after it's created?
response: No, the parameters in an existing VolumeAttributesClass object are immutable. However, the name of a VolumeAttributesClass object in a PersistentVolumeClaim is mutable.
title: Volume Attributes Classes — The VolumeAttributesClass API

instruction: How do users request a particular VolumeAttributesClass for their PersistentVolumeClaim?
response: Users can request a particular VolumeAttributesClass by specifying the name of the VolumeAttributesClass object in their PersistentVolumeClaim. The name of a VolumeAttributesClass object is significant and is used to identify the class.
title: Volume Attributes Classes — The VolumeAttributesClass API

instruction: What is the default mode in kubeadm for discovering the cluster CA to trust?
response: The default mode in kubeadm is to download the cluster configuration, including the root CA, and validate it using the token, as well as validate that the root CA public key matches the provided hash and that the API server certificate is valid under the root CA.
title: kubeadm join — Discovering what cluster CA to trust

instruction: How is the CA key hash formatted and generated?
response: The CA key hash has the format 'sha256:<hex_encoded_hash>'. It can be generated using OpenSSL CLI: openssl x509 -pubkey -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt | openssl rsa -pubin -outform der 2>/dev/null | openssl dgst -sha256 -hex | sed 's/^.* //'
title: kubeadm join — Discovering what cluster CA to trust

instruction: What are the advantages and disadvantages of using the --discovery-token-unsafe-skip-ca-verification flag?
response: The advantages of using --discovery-token-unsafe-skip-ca-verification flag are that it still protects against many network-level attacks and the token can be generated ahead of time and shared with the control-plane node and worker nodes. However, the disadvantages are that if an attacker is able to steal a bootstrap token via some vulnerability, they can use that token to impersonate the control-plane node to other bootstrapping nodes.
title: kubeadm join — Discovering what cluster CA to trust

instruction: How can kubeadm join be used with a discovery file?
response: kubeadm join can be used with a discovery file by specifying the file path or a remote HTTPS URL using the --discovery-file flag. The discovery file is a regular Kubernetes kubeconfig file.
title: kubeadm join — Discovering what cluster CA to trust

instruction: What is the purpose of the ClusterRole named 'dra-node-driver-status-updater'?
response: The ClusterRole 'dra-node-driver-status-updater' is used to grant permissions for a Node-local DRA driver to update the status of ResourceClaims and to patch and update the driver information associated with a specific node.
title: Hardening Guide - Dynamic Resource Allocation — Node-local DRA driver permissions

instruction: What resources and verbs are allowed by the 'dra-node-driver-status-updater' ClusterRole for 'resource.k8s.io' apiGroup?
response: For the 'resource.k8s.io' apiGroup, the ClusterRole allows 'get', 'patch', and 'update' verbs on 'resourceclaims/status' resources, and 'associated-node:patch' and 'associated-node:update' verbs on 'resourceclaims/driver' resources with the specific resourceName 'dra.example.com'.
title: Hardening Guide - Dynamic Resource Allocation — Node-local DRA driver permissions

instruction: What is the HTTP method and endpoint to retrieve the status of a specific Ingress resource in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: The HTTP method is GET and the endpoint is /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/ingresses/{name}/status.
title: Ingress — getRead Status

instruction: What query parameter can be used to pretty-print the output of the Ingress status response?
response: The query parameter is 'pretty'. If set to 'true', the output will be pretty-printed. It defaults to 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool like curl or wget.
title: Ingress — getRead Status

instruction: What is the expected HTTP status code and response type when successfully retrieving the status of an Ingress resource?
response: The expected HTTP status code is 200 and the response type is OK with the Ingress resource.
title: Ingress — getRead Status

instruction: What is the limitation of enabling serverTLSBootstrap for kubelet serving certificates?
response: One known limitation of enabling serverTLSBootstrap for kubelet serving certificates is that the CSRs (Certificate Signing Requests) for these certificates cannot be automatically approved by the default signer in the kube-controller-manager - kubernetes.io/kubelet-serving. This requires action from the user or a third-party controller.
title: Certificate Management with kubeadm — Enabling signed kubelet serving certificates

instruction: How can I approve a pending CSR for a kubelet serving certificate?
response: To approve a pending CSR, use the command: kubectl certificate approve <CSR-name>. You can view the pending CSRs using: kubectl get csr.
title: Certificate Management with kubeadm — Enabling signed kubelet serving certificates

instruction: What is the default expiration period for kubelet serving certificates and what happens when they expire?
response: By default, kubelet serving certificates expire after one year. When they are close to expiration, a new set of CSRs for the serving certificates will be created and must be approved to complete the rotation, due to the KubeletConfiguration field rotateCertificates being set to true.
title: Certificate Management with kubeadm — Enabling signed kubelet serving certificates

instruction: How do you create a ServiceAccount object in Kubernetes?
response: You can create a ServiceAccount object using a Kubernetes client like kubectl or a manifest that defines the object.
title: Service Accounts — How to use service accounts

instruction: How do you grant permissions to a ServiceAccount object?
response: You can grant permissions to a ServiceAccount object using an authorization mechanism such as RBAC.
title: Service Accounts — How to use service accounts

instruction: What are the steps to use a Kubernetes service account?
response: To use a Kubernetes service account, you need to create a ServiceAccount object, grant permissions to the ServiceAccount object, assign the ServiceAccount object to Pods during Pod creation, and optionally retrieve the ServiceAccount token if using an external service.
title: Service Accounts — How to use service accounts

instruction: Where can I find instructions for configuring Service Accounts for Pods?
response: You can find instructions for configuring Service Accounts for Pods in the 'Configure Service Accounts for Pods' documentation.
title: Service Accounts — How to use service accounts

instruction: What is the minimum required Kubernetes server version to run node components as a non-root user?
response: The Kubernetes server must be at or later than version 1.22.
title: Running Kubernetes Node Components as a Non-root User — Before you begin

instruction: How do you check the version of your Kubernetes server?
response: To check the version, enter `kubectl version`.
title: Running Kubernetes Node Components as a Non-root User — Before you begin

instruction: What are the requirements for the unprivileged user to run Kubernetes node components?
response: The unprivileged user must be listed in `/etc/subuid` and `/etc/subgid`.
title: Running Kubernetes Node Components as a Non-root User — Before you begin

instruction: What feature gate needs to be enabled to run the kubelet in a user namespace?
response: The `KubeletInUserNamespace` feature gate needs to be enabled.
title: Running Kubernetes Node Components as a Non-root User — Before you begin

instruction: What is the purpose of the bindTimeoutSeconds field in VolumeBindingArgs?
response: The bindTimeoutSeconds field specifies the timeout in seconds for the volume binding operation. It is a non-negative integer, with a default value of 600 if not specified. A value of zero indicates no waiting.
title: kube-scheduler Configuration (v1) — VolumeBindingArgs

instruction: How is the utilization calculated for scoring nodes in VolumeBindingArgs?
response: The utilization is calculated by dividing the total requested storage of the pod by the total capacity of feasible PVs on each node.
title: kube-scheduler Configuration (v1) — VolumeBindingArgs

instruction: What are the default shape points for scoring nodes based on utilization in VolumeBindingArgs?
response: The default shape points are 10 for 0 utilization and 0 for 100 utilization. These points are used to define the score function shape for nodes based on the utilization of provisioned PVs.
title: kube-scheduler Configuration (v1) — VolumeBindingArgs

instruction: What are the constraints for specifying shape points in VolumeBindingArgs?
response: Each point must contain utilization (ranging from 0 to 100) and its associated score (ranging from 0 to 10). All points must be sorted in increasing order by utilization.
title: kube-scheduler Configuration (v1) — VolumeBindingArgs

instruction: What is the purpose of the `continue` option in the GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/secrets request?
response: The continue option should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It allows clients to retrieve the next set of results from a previous query with identical query parameters.
title: Secret — getList

instruction: How does the `resourceVersion` field affect the list of returned objects?
response: The resourceVersion field sets a constraint on what resource versions a request may be served from. It allows clients to request objects at a specific version or newer. The resourceVersionMatch field determines how this constraint is applied.
title: Secret — getList

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` with `watch=true` in the GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/secrets request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic Bookmark event.
title: Secret — getList

instruction: What is the purpose of the `limit` field in the GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/secrets request?
response: The limit field specifies a maximum number of responses to return for a list call. If more items exist, the server will set the continue field on the list metadata to a value that can be used to retrieve the next set of results.
title: Secret — getList

instruction: How do you specify pod overhead for a RuntimeClass in Kubernetes?
response: You specify pod overhead for a RuntimeClass by defining the 'overhead' field in the RuntimeClass definition, which includes 'podFixed' values for memory and CPU, such as 'memory: 120Mi' and 'cpu: 250m'.
title: Pod Overhead — Configuring Pod overhead

instruction: What happens to a Pod's resource quota calculations and node scheduling when a RuntimeClass with overhead is specified?
response: When a RuntimeClass with overhead is specified for a Pod, the overhead is taken into account for resource quota calculations, node scheduling, as well as Pod cgroup sizing. The admission controller updates the workload's PodSpec to include the overhead as described in the RuntimeClass.
title: Pod Overhead — Configuring Pod overhead

instruction: How can you verify the updated pod overhead value for a Pod in Kubernetes?
response: You can verify the updated pod overhead value for a Pod by running the command 'kubectl get pod <pod-name> -o jsonpath='{.spec.overhead}'', which will output the overhead values, such as 'map[cpu:250m memory:120Mi]'.
title: Pod Overhead — Configuring Pod overhead

instruction: How does the kubelet use pod overhead when creating a new cgroup for a Pod?
response: The kubelet sets an upper limit for the pod cgroup associated with a resource based on the sum of the container limits plus the overhead defined in the PodSpec. For CPU, the kubelet sets 'cpu.shares' based on the sum of container requests plus the overhead defined in the PodSpec.
title: Pod Overhead — Configuring Pod overhead

instruction: What is the default Kubernetes container image registry that kubeadm uses?
response: The default Kubernetes container image registry that kubeadm uses is registry.k8s.io.
title: Creating Highly Available Clusters with kubeadm — Container images

instruction: How can you deploy a highly-available cluster with kubeadm when hosts do not have access to pull images from the registry?
response: To deploy a highly-available cluster with kubeadm when hosts do not have access to pull images, you must ensure by some other means that the correct container images are already available on the relevant hosts.
title: Creating Highly Available Clusters with kubeadm — Container images

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubernetes.io/service-account.name annotation in a Secret?
response: This annotation records the name of the ServiceAccount that the token (stored in the Secret of type kubernetes.io/service-account-token) represents.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — kubernetes.io/service-account.name

instruction: What information does the kubernetes.io/service-account.uid annotation provide about a ServiceAccount?
response: This annotation records the unique ID of the ServiceAccount that the token (stored in the Secret of type kubernetes.io/service-account-token) represents.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — kubernetes.io/service-account.name

instruction: What are the requirements for an application name in a Kubernetes deployment?
response: The application name must be unique within the selected Kubernetes namespace. It must start with a lowercase character, end with a lowercase character or a number, and contain only lowercase letters, numbers, and dashes (-). It is limited to 24 characters. Leading and trailing spaces are ignored.
title: Deploy and Access the Kubernetes Dashboard — Specifying application details

instruction: What is the purpose of specifying a container image in a Kubernetes deployment?
response: The container image specification is the URL of a public Docker container image on any registry, or a private image. The container image specification must end with a colon.
title: Deploy and Access the Kubernetes Dashboard — Specifying application details

instruction: How do you expose a Service to an external IP address outside of the cluster in Kubernetes?
response: You can expose a Service onto an external, maybe public IP address outside of your cluster by creating an external Service. For external Services, you may need to open up one or more ports to do so.
title: Deploy and Access the Kubernetes Dashboard — Specifying application details

instruction: What are the requirements for creating a namespace in Kubernetes?
response: The namespace name may contain a maximum of 63 alphanumeric characters and dashes (-) but cannot contain capital letters. Namespace names should not consist of only numbers.
title: Deploy and Access the Kubernetes Dashboard — Specifying application details

instruction: How do you load an AppArmor profile onto all nodes in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can load an AppArmor profile onto all nodes in a Kubernetes cluster by using SSH to install the profiles on each node. First, get the list of node names using kubectl get node, then iterate over the list and use SSH to load the profile on each node.
title: Restrict a Container's Access to Resources with AppArmor — Example

instruction: How can you verify that a container is running with a specific AppArmor profile?
response: You can verify that a container is running with a specific AppArmor profile by checking the /proc/1/attr/current file inside the container using kubectl exec. The output should display the name of the profile and its enforcement mode.
title: Restrict a Container's Access to Resources with AppArmor — Example

instruction: What happens if you try to run a pod with an AppArmor profile that hasn't been loaded onto the node?
response: If you try to run a pod with an AppArmor profile that hasn't been loaded onto the node, the pod will be created successfully but will be stuck in the Pending state. The kubelet will generate an error message indicating that the AppArmor profile was not found.
title: Restrict a Container's Access to Resources with AppArmor — Example

instruction: How does the Kubelet allocate resources when the Topology Manager scope is set to 'pod'?
response: The Kubelet performs a single NUMA alignment for the entire pod based on the resource budget defined in .spec.resources. The resulting NUMA-aligned resource pool is then partitioned into Exclusive Slices and a Pod Shared Pool.
title: Resource managers — How pod-level resource managers work

instruction: What happens to the resources of init containers that run to completion in a pod?
response: Their resources are added to a per-pod reusable set, rather than being returned to the node's resource pool. This allows these resources to be made reusable for subsequent app containers.
title: Resource managers — How pod-level resource managers work

instruction: Why is a pod rejected at admission if the sum of resource requests from all Guaranteed containers exactly equals the total resource budget, leaving no resources for other containers?
response: Because this configuration would produce an empty pod shared pool, which is not allowed if there are containers that require a shared pool.
title: Resource managers — How pod-level resource managers work

instruction: What happens to resources that are overallocated when using the 'pod' scope, but not fully utilized by shared pool containers?
response: These resources will be assigned and reserved for the pod, effectively being wasted during the whole execution of the pod.
title: Resource managers — How pod-level resource managers work

instruction: How should webhooks be designed to handle potential changes to Kubernetes APIs?
response: Webhooks should be designed under the assumption that Kubernetes APIs might change in a later version. This means not taking the stability of an API for granted.
title: Admission Webhook Good Practices — Plan for future updates to fields

instruction: What can happen if a mutating webhook responds to a broad range of API requests?
response: A mutating webhook that responds to a broad range of API requests might unintentionally trigger itself. For example, if the webhook creates Event objects for every mutation, it may respond to its own Event object creation requests.
title: Admission Webhook Good Practices — Plan for future updates to fields

instruction: How can a webhook avoid triggering itself when creating new resources?
response: To avoid triggering itself, a webhook can set a unique label in any resources it creates and exclude this label from its webhook match conditions.
title: Admission Webhook Good Practices — Plan for future updates to fields

instruction: What is the structure of the 'credentialMapping' field in an ImagePulledRecord?
response: The 'credentialMapping' field is a map of image to the set of credentials that it was previously pulled with. The image in this case is the content of a pod's container image field that's got its tag/digest removed. For example, it could map 'hello-world' to { 'nodePodsAccessible': true }.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1alpha1) — ImagePulledRecord

instruction: Where do device metadata files live inside containers?
response: Device metadata files live inside containers under /var/run/kubernetes.io/dra-device-attributes.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — DRA device metadata in containers

instruction: How do drivers update metadata files to signal changes to consumers?
response: When a driver updates a metadata file, the embedded metadata.generation field must increase so consumers can detect changes.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — DRA device metadata in containers

instruction: How are device metadata files exposed to containers?
response: Files are typically exposed via CDI bind-mounts, but other mechanisms are permitted as long as the file appears at the correct path and is read-only inside the container.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — DRA device metadata in containers

instruction: What API version and kind are used for device metadata objects?
response: Device metadata objects use the DeviceMetadata API (metadata.resource.k8s.io/v1alpha1) with kind 'DeviceMetadata' and apiVersion 'metadata.resource.k8s.io/v1alpha1'.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — DRA device metadata in containers

instruction: How do I clean up resources created in the DRA tutorial?
response: To clean up the resources created in this tutorial, run the following commands: kubectl delete namespace dra-tutorial, kubectl delete deviceclass gpu.example.com, kubectl delete clusterrole dra-example-driver-role, kubectl delete clusterrolebinding dra-example-driver-role-binding, and kubectl delete priorityclass dra-driver-high-priority.
title: Install Drivers and Allocate Devices with DRA — Cleaning up

instruction: What specific resources need to be deleted to clean up after the DRA tutorial?
response: The resources that need to be deleted are: namespace dra-tutorial, deviceclass gpu.example.com, clusterrole dra-example-driver-role, clusterrolebinding dra-example-driver-role-binding, and priorityclass dra-driver-high-priority.
title: Install Drivers and Allocate Devices with DRA — Cleaning up

instruction: What determines the storage medium of an emptyDir volume?
response: The storage medium of an emptyDir volume is determined by the medium of the filesystem holding the kubelet root dir (typically /var/lib/kubelet).
title: Volumes — Resources

instruction: Is there a limit on how much space an emptyDir or hostPath volume can consume?
response: No, there is no limit on how much space an emptyDir or hostPath volume can consume.
title: Volumes — Resources

instruction: How can you request space for a volume using a resource specification?
response: You can learn about requesting space using a resource specification by reading how to manage resources.
title: Volumes — Resources

instruction: What gRPC service does the kubelet export for device plugin registration?
response: The kubelet exports a Registration gRPC service with a single RPC method Register that takes a RegisterRequest and returns an Empty response.
title: Device Plugins — Device plugin registration

instruction: What information does a device plugin need to send to the kubelet during registration?
response: During registration, the device plugin needs to send the name of its Unix socket, the Device Plugin API version against which it was built, and the ResourceName it wants to advertise.
title: Device Plugins — Device plugin registration

instruction: How are extended resources advertised to the API server after a device plugin registers with the kubelet?
response: Following a successful registration, the device plugin sends the kubelet the list of devices it manages, and the kubelet is then in charge of advertising those resources to the API server as part of the kubelet node status update.
title: Device Plugins — Device plugin registration

instruction: What are the requirements for requesting extended resources in a Pod specification?
response: Extended resources are only supported as integer resources and cannot be overcommitted. Devices cannot be shared between containers. Requesting extended resources is similar to how you manage requests and limits for other resources.
title: Device Plugins — Device plugin registration

instruction: What is the first step to change the container runtime on a node from Docker Engine to containerd?
response: Install containerd. For more information, see containerd's installation documentation and follow the containerd guide for specific prerequisites.
title: Changing the Container Runtime on a Node from Docker Engine to containerd — Before you begin

instruction: How do you safely drain a node before changing its container runtime?
response: Run the command 'kubectl drain <node-to-drain> --ignore-daemonsets', replacing <node-to-drain> with the name of the node you are draining.
title: Changing the Container Runtime on a Node from Docker Engine to containerd — Before you begin

instruction: What commands are used to stop and disable Docker service on a node?
response: The commands to stop and disable Docker service are 'systemctl stop kubelet' and 'systemctl disable docker.service --now', respectively.
title: Changing the Container Runtime on a Node from Docker Engine to containerd — Before you begin

instruction: What is the default StatefulSetUpdateStrategy type?
response: The default type is RollingUpdate.
title: StatefulSet — StatefulSetUpdateStrategy

instruction: What happens when a Pod is manually deleted in a StatefulSet with the OnDelete update strategy?
response: When a Pod is manually deleted in a StatefulSet with the OnDelete update strategy, it is recreated from the StatefulSetSpec.
title: StatefulSet — StatefulSetUpdateStrategy

instruction: How do new Pods are created during a scale operation with the RollingUpdate strategy?
response: When a scale operation is performed with the RollingUpdate strategy, new Pods will be created from the specification version indicated by the StatefulSet's updateRevision.
title: StatefulSet — StatefulSetUpdateStrategy

instruction: What is the current status of the Kubernetes Dashboard project?
response: The Kubernetes Dashboard project has been archived and is no longer actively maintained.
title: Deploy and Access the Kubernetes Dashboard

instruction: What can be used as an alternative to Kubernetes Dashboard for new installations?
response: Headlamp can be used as an alternative to Kubernetes Dashboard for new installations.
title: Deploy and Access the Kubernetes Dashboard

instruction: What are some of the features and uses of the Kubernetes Dashboard?
response: Dashboard is a web-based Kubernetes user interface that can be used to deploy containerized applications to a Kubernetes cluster, troubleshoot applications, manage cluster resources, get an overview of applications running on the cluster, and create or modify individual Kubernetes resources.
title: Deploy and Access the Kubernetes Dashboard

instruction: What is a PodTemplate in Kubernetes?
response: A PodTemplate is a specification for creating Pods, and is included in workload resources such as Deployments, Jobs, and DaemonSets.
title: Pods — Pod templates

instruction: How do changes to a Pod template affect existing Pods?
response: Modifying the pod template or switching to a new pod template has no direct effect on the Pods that already exist. The workload resource needs to create replacement Pods that use the updated template.
title: Pods — Pod templates

instruction: What happens when a StatefulSet's Pod template is updated?
response: The StatefulSet starts to create new Pods based on the updated template, and eventually replaces all of the old Pods with new Pods.
title: Pods — Pod templates

instruction: Does the kubelet directly observe or manage Pod template updates on Nodes?
response: No, the kubelet does not directly observe or manage any of the details around pod templates and updates; those details are abstracted away.
title: Pods — Pod templates

instruction: What are the prerequisites for running Kubelet in standalone mode?
response: The prerequisites include admin (root) access to a Linux system that uses systemd and iptables (or nftables with iptables emulation), access to the Internet to download required components, a container runtime that implements the Kubernetes CRI, network plugins (CNI), and required CLI tools such as curl, tar, and jq.
title: Running Kubelet in Standalone Mode — Objectives

instruction: What components need to be installed on a Linux system to run Kubelet in standalone mode?
response: The components that need to be installed are cri-o and kubelet, and they should be run as systemd services.
title: Running Kubelet in Standalone Mode — Objectives

instruction: What is the goal of launching a Pod running nginx in this tutorial?
response: The goal is to launch a Pod running nginx that listens to requests on TCP port 80 on the Pod's IP address, and to learn how the different components of the solution interact among themselves.
title: Running Kubelet in Standalone Mode — Objectives

instruction: What is Pod Overhead in Kubernetes and what does it account for?
response: Pod Overhead is a way to account for the resources consumed by the Pod infrastructure on top of the container requests & limits. It represents the additional system resources taken by the Pod itself when running on a Node.
title: Pod Overhead

instruction: When is the Pod's overhead set in Kubernetes?
response: The Pod's overhead is set at admission time according to the overhead associated with the Pod's RuntimeClass.
title: Pod Overhead

instruction: How is a Pod's overhead considered during scheduling and eviction in Kubernetes?
response: A pod's overhead is considered in addition to the sum of container resource requests when scheduling a Pod. Similarly, the kubelet will include the Pod overhead when sizing the Pod cgroup, and when carrying out Pod eviction ranking.
title: Pod Overhead

instruction: How do I enable leader election for a multiple-scheduler configuration in Kubernetes?
response: To enable leader election, update the KubeSchedulerConfiguration in the ConfigMap (e.g., my-scheduler-config) with the following fields: leaderElection.leaderElect to true, leaderElection.resourceNamespace to <lock-object-namespace>, and leaderElection.resourceName to <lock-object-name>.
title: Configure Multiple Schedulers — Enable leader election

instruction: What RBAC updates are required when enabling leader election for multiple schedulers?
response: If RBAC is enabled, update the system:kube-scheduler cluster role by adding your scheduler name to the resourceNames of the rule applied for endpoints and leases resources.
title: Configure Multiple Schedulers — Enable leader election

instruction: How do I update the system:kube-scheduler cluster role to support multiple schedulers with leader election?
response: Edit the cluster role using kubectl edit clusterrole system:kube-scheduler, and add your scheduler name to the resourceNames of the rule applied for endpoints and leases resources, as shown in the example: ... resourceNames: - kube-scheduler - my-scheduler ...
title: Configure Multiple Schedulers — Enable leader election

instruction: What is the schedule specified in the CronJob manifest example provided?
response: The schedule specified in the CronJob manifest example is "* * * * *", which means the job will run every minute.
title: Running Automated Tasks with a CronJob — Creating a CronJob

instruction: How can you verify that a CronJob has successfully scheduled a job?
response: You can verify that a CronJob has successfully scheduled a job by checking the LAST SCHEDULE time in the CronJob status output using the command "kubectl get cronjob hello".
title: Running Automated Tasks with a CronJob — Creating a CronJob

instruction: What command can be used to view the standard output of a pod created by a job scheduled by a CronJob?
response: To view the standard output of a pod created by a job scheduled by a CronJob, first find the pod name using "kubectl get pods --selector job-name=<job-name> --output=jsonpath={.items[*].metadata.name}", then use "kubectl logs <pod-name>".
title: Running Automated Tasks with a CronJob — Creating a CronJob

instruction: What happens to existing RBAC rules using the impersonate verb when the feature gate for constrained impersonation is enabled?
response: Existing RBAC rules using the impersonate verb continue to function when the feature gate is enabled.
title: User Impersonation — Working withimpersonateverb

instruction: What is the order of checks performed by the API server when an impersonation request is made?
response: When an impersonation request is made, the API server first checks for constrained impersonation permissions. If those checks fail, it falls back to checking the impersonate permission.
title: User Impersonation — Working withimpersonateverb

instruction: What label is applied to all default ClusterRoles and ClusterRoleBindings created by the API server?
response: The label applied to all default ClusterRoles and ClusterRoleBindings is kubernetes.io/bootstrapping=rbac-defaults.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Default roles and role bindings

instruction: What prefix indicates that a ClusterRole or ClusterRoleBinding is directly managed by the cluster control plane?
response: The system: prefix indicates that a ClusterRole or ClusterRoleBinding is directly managed by the cluster control plane.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Default roles and role bindings

instruction: What happens when a PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) with an unspecified volumeMode requests a PersistentVolume (PV) with a volumeMode of Block?
response: There will be no bind.
title: Persistent Volumes — Binding Block Volumes

instruction: Can a PersistentVolumeClaim with a volumeMode of Block be bound to a PersistentVolume with a volumeMode of Filesystem?
response: No, there will be no bind.
title: Persistent Volumes — Binding Block Volumes

instruction: What is the result when both the PersistentVolume and PersistentVolumeClaim have a volumeMode of Block?
response: The volume will be bound.
title: Persistent Volumes — Binding Block Volumes

instruction: What happens when a PersistentVolumeClaim with a volumeMode of Filesystem requests a PersistentVolume with a volumeMode of Block?
response: There will be no bind.
title: Persistent Volumes — Binding Block Volumes

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/watch/ipaddresses request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: IPAddress — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/watch/ipaddresses request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. The server may reject a continue value it does not recognize. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token.
title: IPAddress — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/watch/ipaddresses request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent.
title: IPAddress — getWatch List

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersionMatch` field in the GET /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/watch/ipaddresses request?
response: The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: IPAddress — getWatch List

instruction: What is the required format for the 'name' field in a CredentialProvider configuration?
response: The 'name' field is required to be a string that matches the name of the provider executable as seen by the kubelet, and it must be unique across all providers.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1alpha1) — CredentialProvider

instruction: How does the kubelet determine if a CredentialProvider plugin should be invoked for an image?
response: The kubelet checks if the requested image matches any of the strings in the 'matchImages' list. A match exists when both the image and the matchImage have the same number of domain parts and each part matches, the URL path of the matchImage is a prefix of the target image URL path, and if the matchImage contains a port, it must match in the image as well.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1alpha1) — CredentialProvider

instruction: What are the supported formats for globs in the 'matchImages' field?
response: Globs are supported as subdomains like *.k8s.io or k8s.*.io, and top-level-domains such as k8s.*. Matching partial subdomains like app*.k8s.io is also supported. Each glob can only match a single subdomain segment.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1alpha1) — CredentialProvider

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'defaultCacheDuration' field in a CredentialProvider configuration?
response: The 'defaultCacheDuration' field specifies the default duration the plugin will cache credentials in-memory if a cache duration is not provided in the plugin response.
title: Kubelet Configuration (v1alpha1) — CredentialProvider

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubernetes.io/limit-ranger annotation in a Pod?
response: The kubernetes.io/limit-ranger annotation records that resource defaults were specified for the Pod and they were applied successfully.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — kubernetes.io/limit-ranger

instruction: What happens to Pods deployed after a LimitRange is defined in a namespace?
response: Pods deployed after a LimitRange is defined in a namespace will have the limits applied to them.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — kubernetes.io/limit-ranger

instruction: What is the default behavior of Kubernetes regarding resource limits for containers?
response: Kubernetes by default doesn't provide any resource limit, which means unless you explicitly define limits, your container can consume unlimited CPU and memory.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — kubernetes.io/limit-ranger

instruction: How do I create a 4GiB encrypted swap file on a Kubernetes node?
response: To create a 4GiB encrypted swap file, first allocate storage and restrict access with `fallocate --length 4GiB /swapfile` and `chmod 600 /swapfile`. Then, create an encrypted device with `cryptsetup --type plain --cipher aes-xts-plain64 --key-size 256 -d /dev/urandom open /swapfile cryptswap`. Format the swap space with `mkswap /dev/mapper/cryptswap` and activate it with `swapon /dev/mapper/cryptswap`.
title: Configuring swap memory on Kubernetes nodes — Create a swap file and turn swap on

instruction: How can I verify that swap is enabled on a Kubernetes node?
response: You can verify that swap is enabled using either the `swapon -s` command or the `free` command. The `swapon -s` command will display details about the swap file, including its filename, type, size, used space, and priority. The `free` command will display memory and swap usage statistics.
title: Configuring swap memory on Kubernetes nodes — Create a swap file and turn swap on

instruction: How do I configure a Kubernetes node to activate swap at boot time?
response: To activate swap at boot time, you can either set up a systemd unit to activate the swap or add a line to `/etc/fstab`. For encrypted swap, a systemd unit is typically used, which allows the system to delay kubelet start until swap is available. For unencrypted swap, you can add a line similar to `/swapfile swap swap defaults 0 0` to `/etc/fstab`.
title: Configuring swap memory on Kubernetes nodes — Create a swap file and turn swap on

instruction: What is the default RBAC configuration for Kubernetes Dashboard?
response: The default RBAC configuration for Kubernetes Dashboard is minimal.
title: Deploy and Access the Kubernetes Dashboard — Accessing the Dashboard UI

instruction: How can I access the Kubernetes Dashboard UI?
response: You can access the Kubernetes Dashboard UI by running the command 'kubectl -n kubernetes-dashboard port-forward svc/kubernetes-dashboard-kong-proxy 8443:443' and then navigating to 'https://localhost:8443' on the machine where the command is executed.
title: Deploy and Access the Kubernetes Dashboard — Accessing the Dashboard UI

instruction: What is required to log in to the Kubernetes Dashboard?
response: To log in to the Kubernetes Dashboard, you need a Bearer Token. You can create a token by following the guide on creating a sample user.
title: Deploy and Access the Kubernetes Dashboard — Accessing the Dashboard UI

instruction: Can the Kubernetes Dashboard UI be accessed remotely?
response: No, the Kubernetes Dashboard UI can only be accessed from the machine where the 'kubectl port-forward' command is executed.
title: Deploy and Access the Kubernetes Dashboard — Accessing the Dashboard UI

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in a GET /api/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/secrets request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: Secret — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in a GET /api/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/secrets request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is server-defined and clients may only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: Secret — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in a GET /api/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/secrets request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event.
title: Secret — getWatch List

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersionMatch` field in a GET /api/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/secrets request?
response: The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: Secret — getWatch List

instruction: What are the minimum requirements for the control-plane nodes in a kubeadm cluster?
response: Three or more machines that meet kubeadm's minimum requirements, including a container runtime already set up and working.
title: Creating Highly Available Clusters with kubeadm — Before you begin

instruction: What is the recommended number of control plane nodes for leader selection in case of machine or zone failure?
response: An odd number of control plane nodes can help with leader selection in the case of machine or zone failure.
title: Creating Highly Available Clusters with kubeadm — Before you begin

instruction: What are the requirements for etcd cluster members in a stacked etcd topology?
response: Three or more additional machines that will become etcd cluster members, with kubeadm and kubelet installed, and a container runtime already set up and working. Having an odd number of members in the etcd cluster is a requirement for achieving optimal voting quorum.
title: Creating Highly Available Clusters with kubeadm — Before you begin

instruction: How do you upgrade the kubelet and kubectl on Ubuntu, Debian, or HypriotOS?
response: To upgrade the kubelet and kubectl on Ubuntu, Debian, or HypriotOS, first run `sudo apt-mark unhold kubelet kubectl`, then `sudo apt-get update`, followed by `sudo apt-get install -y kubelet=<version> kubectl=<version>`, and finally `sudo apt-mark hold kubelet kubectl`. Replace `<version>` with the latest patch version, such as `1.36.x-*`.
title: Upgrading Linux nodes — Upgrade kubelet and kubectl

instruction: What command is used to restart the kubelet after upgrading?
response: To restart the kubelet after upgrading, run `sudo systemctl daemon-reload` followed by `sudo systemctl restart kubelet`.
title: Upgrading Linux nodes — Upgrade kubelet and kubectl

instruction: How do you upgrade the kubelet and kubectl on systems with DNF or DNF5?
response: For systems with DNF or DNF5, upgrade the kubelet and kubectl by running `sudo yum install -y kubelet-<version> kubectl-<version> --disableexcludes=kubernetes` or `sudo yum install -y kubelet-<version> kubectl-<version> --setopt=disable_excludes=kubernetes`, respectively. Replace `<version>` with the latest patch version, such as `1.36.x-*`.
title: Upgrading Linux nodes — Upgrade kubelet and kubectl

instruction: What are the valid effects of a taint on pods that do not tolerate the taint?
response: The valid effects are NoSchedule, PreferNoSchedule, and NoExecute.
title: Node — Taint

instruction: What is the effect of the 'NoExecute' taint effect on existing pods?
response: Evict any already-running pods that do not tolerate the taint. Currently enforced by NodeController.
title: Node — Taint

instruction: How does the 'NoSchedule' taint effect impact new pods scheduling onto a node?
response: Do not allow new pods to schedule onto the node unless they tolerate the taint, but allow all pods submitted to Kubelet without going through the scheduler to start, and allow all already-running pods to continue running. Enforced by the scheduler.
title: Node — Taint

instruction: What is the difference between 'NoSchedule' and 'PreferNoSchedule' taint effects?
response: Like NoSchedule, but the scheduler tries not to schedule new pods onto the node, rather than prohibiting new pods from scheduling onto the node entirely. Enforced by the scheduler.
title: Node — Taint

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'pretty' parameter in the RoleBinding postCreate request?
response: The 'pretty' parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If 'true', the output will be pretty printed. It defaults to 'false' unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: RoleBinding — postCreate

instruction: What are the valid values for the 'fieldValidation' parameter in the RoleBinding postCreate request?
response: The valid values for 'fieldValidation' are: 'Ignore', 'Warn', and 'Strict'. 'Ignore' ignores unknown and duplicate fields. 'Warn' sends a warning for unknown and duplicate fields. 'Strict' fails the request with a BadRequest error if there are unknown or duplicate fields.
title: RoleBinding — postCreate

instruction: What are the possible status responses for a successful RoleBinding postCreate request?
response: The possible status responses for a successful RoleBinding postCreate request are: 200 OK, 201 Created, and 202 Accepted. Each of these responses returns the created RoleBinding.
title: RoleBinding — postCreate

instruction: How do I list the PersistentVolumes in my Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can list the PersistentVolumes in your cluster by running the command `kubectl get pv`.
title: Change the Reclaim Policy of a PersistentVolume — Changing the reclaim policy of a PersistentVolume

instruction: How do I change the reclaim policy of a PersistentVolume?
response: You can change the reclaim policy of a PersistentVolume by running the command `kubectl patch pv <your-pv-name> -p '{"spec":{"persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy":"Retain"}}'`, where `<your-pv-name>` is the name of your chosen PersistentVolume.
title: Change the Reclaim Policy of a PersistentVolume — Changing the reclaim policy of a PersistentVolume

instruction: What happens to a PersistentVolume with a reclaim policy of Retain when its claim is deleted?
response: When a PersistentVolume has a reclaim policy of Retain and its claim is deleted, it will not be automatically deleted.
title: Change the Reclaim Policy of a PersistentVolume — Changing the reclaim policy of a PersistentVolume

instruction: How do I verify that a PersistentVolume has the correct reclaim policy?
response: You can verify that a PersistentVolume has the correct reclaim policy by running the command `kubectl get pv` and checking the RECLAIMPOLICY column in the output.
title: Change the Reclaim Policy of a PersistentVolume — Changing the reclaim policy of a PersistentVolume

instruction: What is the purpose of topology management in Kubernetes?
response: The purpose of topology management in Kubernetes is to enable more complex workload placement policies while keeping the abstraction free from explicit placement directives, allowing the kubelet to configure the host operating system for optimal pod and container placement.
title: Troubleshooting Topology Management

instruction: What is NUMA and how is it related to topology management in Kubernetes?
response: NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access) refers to a hardware concept where CPUs are topologically closer to specific regions of memory due to the physical layout of hardware components. In Kubernetes, NUMA domains are an example of inner boundaries that topology management helps the kubelet to configure for optimal pod and container placement.
title: Troubleshooting Topology Management

instruction: What is the purpose of the `pretty` parameter in the DELETE /apis/apiregistration.k8s.io/v1/apiservices endpoint?
response: The `pretty` parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If set to `true`, the output will be pretty-printed, but this is only done by default if the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool like curl or wget.
title: APIService — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: How does the `continue` parameter work when listing APIServices?
response: The `continue` parameter is used to retrieve more results from the server. It should be set to the value provided by the server in the `continue` field of the list metadata. The server may reject a `continue` value it does not recognize. If the specified `continue` value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: APIService — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting `dryRun` to `All` in the DELETE /apis/apiregistration.k8s.io/v1/apiservices endpoint?
response: Setting `dryRun` to `All` indicates that all dry run stages should be processed. This means that the request will be validated and tested, but no actual changes will be made.
title: APIService — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the `propagationPolicy` parameter when deleting APIServices?
response: The `propagationPolicy` parameter determines how garbage collection will be performed when deleting an APIService. It can be set to 'Orphan', 'Background', or 'Foreground', and determines how dependent objects will be handled.
title: APIService — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the `pretty` parameter in the DELETE /apis/storage.k8s.io/v1/volumeattributesclasses endpoint?
response: The `pretty` parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If set to `true`, the output will be pretty-printed, but this is only done by default if the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool like curl or wget.
title: VolumeAttributesClass — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: How does the `continue` parameter work when retrieving more results from the server?
response: The `continue` parameter should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is a server-defined value that clients may use to retrieve the next set of results. If the continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error. Clients may then restart their list without the continue field or send another list request with the token received with the 410 error.
title: VolumeAttributesClass — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting `dryRun` to `All` in the DELETE /apis/storage.k8s.io/v1/volumeattributesclasses endpoint?
response: When `dryRun` is set to `All`, all dry run stages will be processed. This means that modifications will not be persisted, and an invalid or unrecognized dryRun directive will result in an error response and no further processing of the request.
title: VolumeAttributesClass — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the describedObject field in ObjectMetricSource?
response: The describedObject field specifies the description of a Kubernetes object, including its kind, name, and API version.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler — ObjectMetricSource

instruction: How is the target metric identified in ObjectMetricSource?
response: The target metric is identified by name and selector using the metric field.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler — ObjectMetricSource

instruction: What does the target field specify in ObjectMetricSource?
response: The target field specifies the target value for the given metric.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler — ObjectMetricSource

instruction: What is the simplest way to use images from a public registry in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: No configuration is required. Some cloud providers automatically cache or mirror public images, which improves availability and reduces the time to pull images.
title: Images — Use cases

instruction: How can you ensure that proprietary images are only accessible to authorized users in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can use a hosted private registry, run an internal private registry behind your firewall with open read access, or use a hosted container image registry service that controls image access.
title: Images — Use cases

instruction: What is the recommended approach for a multi-tenant cluster where each tenant needs its own private registry?
response: Ensure the AlwaysPullImages admission controller is active, run a private registry with authorization required, generate registry credentials for each tenant, store into a Secret, and propagate the Secret to every tenant namespace.
title: Images — Use cases

instruction: How can you manage access to multiple private registries in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can create one Secret per registry. The tenant then adds that Secret to imagePullSecrets of each namespace.
title: Images — Use cases

instruction: What are the two methods for defining resources that a workload needs in Kubernetes?
