response: You can use either a ResourceClaim or a ResourceClaimTemplate. A ResourceClaim is used when you want multiple Pods to share access to specific devices, while a ResourceClaimTemplate is used when you want Pods to have independent access to separate, similarly-configured devices.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — ResourceClaims and ResourceClaimTemplates

instruction: How does the lifetime of a ResourceClaim generated from a ResourceClaimTemplate relate to the lifetime of the corresponding Pod?
response: The lifetime of each generated ResourceClaim is bound to the lifetime of the corresponding Pod. Similarly, when using a PodGroup ResourceClaimTemplate, the lifetime of each generated ResourceClaim is bound to the lifetime of the corresponding PodGroup.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — ResourceClaims and ResourceClaimTemplates

instruction: What is the purpose of providing a prioritized list of subrequests for requests in a ResourceClaim or ResourceClaimTemplate?
response: The prioritized list feature allows users to specify alternative devices that can be used by the workload if the primary choice is not available. The scheduler will select the first subrequest that can be allocated.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — ResourceClaims and ResourceClaimTemplates

instruction: What is the benefit of generating ResourceClaims for a PodGroup instead of individual Pods?
response: Generating ResourceClaims for a PodGroup allows many more than 256 Pods to share a ResourceClaim, and enables Pods within a PodGroup to share access to devices allocated to the generated ResourceClaim without needing to know the exact name of the ResourceClaim.
title: Dynamic Resource Allocation — ResourceClaims and ResourceClaimTemplates

instruction: How should I upgrade a cluster deployed using kubeadm?
response: Refer to 'Upgrading kubeadm clusters' for detailed information on how to upgrade the cluster.
title: Upgrade A Cluster — kubeadm

instruction: What sequence should I follow to manually update the control plane during a cluster upgrade?
response: The sequence is: etcd (all instances), kube-apiserver (all control plane hosts), kube-controller-manager, kube-scheduler, and cloud controller manager (if used).
title: Upgrade A Cluster — kubeadm

instruction: What should I do with each node in my cluster during an upgrade?
response: For each node, drain the node and then either replace it with a new node that uses the 1.36 kubelet, or upgrade the kubelet on that node and bring the node back into service.
title: Upgrade A Cluster — kubeadm

instruction: When should I install the latest version of kubectl during a cluster upgrade?
response: You should install the latest version of kubectl after upgrading the cluster and after updating the control plane.
title: Upgrade A Cluster — kubeadm

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in a GET /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/watch/clusterroles/{name} request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: ClusterRole — getWatch

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in a GET /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/watch/clusterroles/{name} request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is server-defined and clients may only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: ClusterRole — getWatch

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in a GET /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/watch/clusterroles/{name} request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event.
title: ClusterRole — getWatch

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersionMatch` field in a GET /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/watch/clusterroles/{name} request?
response: The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: ClusterRole — getWatch

instruction: What protocols are supported by Kubernetes Services?
response: Kubernetes supports SCTP, TCP (the default), and UDP protocols with Services.
title: Protocols for Services

instruction: Can I specify the application protocol used by a Service?
response: Yes, when you define a Service, you can specify the application protocol that it uses.
title: Protocols for Services

instruction: What are some special cases for application protocols typically using TCP as a transport protocol in Kubernetes Services?
response: Some special cases include HTTP, HTTPS, PROXY protocol, and TLS termination at the load balancer.
title: Protocols for Services

instruction: What is the purpose of the system:auth-delegator ClusterRole?
response: The system:auth-delegator ClusterRole allows delegated authentication and authorization checks. This is commonly used by add-on API servers for unified authentication and authorization.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Other component roles

instruction: Which ClusterRole is used by the kube-dns component?
response: The kube-dns ClusterRole is used by the kube-dns component, bound to the kube-dns service account in the kube-system namespace.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Other component roles

instruction: What access does the system:kubelet-api-admin ClusterRole provide?
response: The system:kubelet-api-admin ClusterRole allows full access to the kubelet API.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Other component roles

instruction: What resources can be accessed with the system:persistent-volume-provisioner ClusterRole?
response: The system:persistent-volume-provisioner ClusterRole allows access to the resources required by most dynamic volume provisioners.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Other component roles

instruction: What is the primary function of Ingress in a Kubernetes cluster?
response: Ingress exposes HTTP and HTTPS routes from outside the cluster to services within the cluster, and traffic routing is controlled by rules defined on the Ingress resource.
title: Ingress — What is Ingress?

instruction: What are some capabilities of an Ingress configuration?
response: An Ingress may be configured to give Services externally-reachable URLs, load balance traffic, terminate SSL / TLS, and offer name-based virtual hosting.
title: Ingress — What is Ingress?

instruction: How does an Ingress controller handle traffic to fulfill an Ingress?
response: An Ingress controller is responsible for fulfilling the Ingress, usually with a load balancer, though it may also configure your edge router or additional frontends to help handle the traffic.
title: Ingress — What is Ingress?

instruction: What type of Services are used to expose services other than HTTP and HTTPS to the internet?
response: Exposing services other than HTTP and HTTPS to the internet typically uses a service of type Service.Type=NodePort or Service.Type=LoadBalancer.
title: Ingress — What is Ingress?

instruction: What is the purpose of setting clusterIP to None in the Service definition?
response: To create a headless service.
title: Job with Pod-to-Pod Communication — Example

instruction: How do pods communicate with each other using hostnames in this example?
response: Using the format <pod-hostname>.<headless-service-name>, for example: example-job-${i}.headless-svc.
title: Job with Pod-to-Pod Communication — Example

instruction: What is the command executed in the container to ping other pods?
response: A bash script that pings other pods using hostnames, retrying if the ping fails, with a command like: ping -c 1 example-job-${i}.headless-svc.
title: Job with Pod-to-Pod Communication — Example

instruction: What is the purpose of the `pretty` parameter in the GET /apis/certificates.k8s.io/v1beta1/namespaces/{namespace}/podcertificaterequests endpoint?
response: The `pretty` parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If set to `true`, the output will be pretty-printed. Defaults to `false` unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: PodCertificateRequest — getList

instruction: How does the `continue` parameter work in the GET /apis/certificates.k8s.io/v1beta1/namespaces/{namespace}/podcertificaterequests endpoint?
response: The `continue` parameter should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is a server-defined value that clients may only use with identical query parameters (except for the value of continue). If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: PodCertificateRequest — getList

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /apis/certificates.k8s.io/v1beta1/namespaces/{namespace}/podcertificaterequests endpoint?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent.
title: PodCertificateRequest — getList

instruction: What happens if the reservedMemory values are not compatible with kubeReserved, systemReserved, and memory.available settings?
response: The Memory Manager will show an error on startup.
title: Control Memory Management Policies on a Node — Constraints on NUMA memory reservation

instruction: How should the quantity of memory reserved by setting reservedMemory be adjusted in relation to the hard eviction threshold?
response: The quantity of memory reserved by setting reservedMemory should be increased by the hard eviction threshold; otherwise, the kubelet will not start Memory Manager and display an error.
title: Control Memory Management Policies on a Node — Constraints on NUMA memory reservation

instruction: What is the default hard eviction threshold for memory?
response: The default hard eviction threshold is 100MiB.
title: Control Memory Management Policies on a Node — Constraints on NUMA memory reservation

instruction: Can you provide an example of a correct configuration that uses reservedMemory?
response: A correct configuration example is: memoryManagerPolicy: Static, kubeReserved: {cpu: "4", memory: "4Gi"}, systemReserved: {cpu: "1", memory: "1Gi"}, reservedMemory: [{numaNode: 0, limits: {memory: "3Gi"}}, {numaNode: 1, limits: {memory: "2148Mi"}}].
title: Control Memory Management Policies on a Node — Constraints on NUMA memory reservation

instruction: What protocol is used to wrap connections forwarded by a load balancer configured with a Service of type LoadBalancer?
response: The PROXY protocol is used to wrap connections.
title: Protocols for Services — PROXY protocol

instruction: How does the load balancer behave when either side closes the connection when using the PROXY protocol?
response: When either side closes the connection, the load balancer also triggers a connection close and sends any remaining data where feasible.
title: Protocols for Services — PROXY protocol

instruction: What type of Service and protocol are typically used when configuring a load balancer to use the PROXY protocol?
response: Typically, a Service with type LoadBalancer and protocol TCP are used.
title: Protocols for Services — PROXY protocol

instruction: What is the purpose of a Role in Kubernetes?
response: A Role is a namespaced, logical grouping of PolicyRules that can be referenced as a unit by a RoleBinding.
title: Role

instruction: What are the required fields for a Role object in Kubernetes?
response: The required fields for a Role object are apiVersion, kind, metadata, and rules.
title: Role

instruction: What is the significance of the 'rules' field in a Role object?
response: The 'rules' field in a Role object holds all the PolicyRules for that Role.
title: Role

instruction: What is the effect of setting `dryRun` to `All` in the DELETE collection API?
response: When `dryRun` is set to `All`, all dry run stages will be processed, and modifications will not be persisted.
title: LimitRange — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the difference between `orphanDependents` and `propagationPolicy` in the DELETE collection API?
response: Both fields control the deletion of dependent objects. `orphanDependents` is deprecated and replaced by `propagationPolicy`. `propagationPolicy` allows for more control over garbage collection, with options for 'Orphan', 'Background', and 'Foreground'.
title: LimitRange — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersion` field in the DELETE collection API?
response: The `resourceVersion` field sets a constraint on what resource versions a request may be served from. It helps ensure that clients receive a consistent view of the resources.
title: LimitRange — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: How can a Pod access a private registry without requiring node configuration by a cluster administrator?
response: By specifying imagePullSecrets when defining the Pod. Only Pods which provide their own keys can access the private registry.
title: Images — Using a private registry

instruction: What are the requirements for using pre-pulled images on a node?
response: All Pods can use any images cached on a node, but it requires root access to all nodes to set up.
title: Images — Using a private registry

instruction: Who is responsible for node configuration when using the method to configure nodes to authenticate to a private registry?
response: The cluster administrator is responsible for node configuration.
title: Images — Using a private registry

instruction: What is an alternative to specifying imagePullSecrets or configuring nodes, to fetch credentials for private registries?
response: Using a kubelet credential provider plugin to dynamically fetch credentials for private registries.
title: Images — Using a private registry

instruction: How do you update a Deployment to add a new container to its Pods using kubectl?
response: Create a strategic merge patch file (e.g., patch-file.yaml) that specifies the new container, then apply it to the Deployment using kubectl patch deployment <deployment-name> --patch-file <patch-file.yaml>.
title: Update API Objects in Place Using kubectl patch — Use a strategic merge patch to update a Deployment

instruction: What happens to the Pods of a Deployment when you update its spec using kubectl patch?
response: The old Pods are terminated and replaced by new ones that comply with the updated Deployment spec. The new Pods have different names from the old Pods.
title: Update API Objects in Place Using kubectl patch — Use a strategic merge patch to update a Deployment

instruction: How can you verify that a Pod has been updated with a new container after patching its Deployment?
response: Use kubectl get pod <pod-name> --output yaml to view the Pod's configuration. The output will show the list of containers, including the new one added by the patch.
title: Update API Objects in Place Using kubectl patch — Use a strategic merge patch to update a Deployment

instruction: Where can I find more information about the metrics-server?
response: You can find more information about the metrics-server in the metrics-server repository, metrics-server design, metrics-server FAQ, metrics-server known issues, and metrics-server releases.
title: Resource metrics pipeline — What's next

instruction: How can I learn about how the kubelet serves node metrics and access them via the Kubernetes API?
response: You can learn about how the kubelet serves node metrics and how to access them via the Kubernetes API by reading the Node Metrics Data documentation.
title: Resource metrics pipeline — What's next

instruction: What performance issue was observed with system-critical daemons and services during testing and based on user feedback?
response: The performance of system-critical daemons and services might degrade, implying that system daemons, including the kubelet, could operate slower than usual.
title: Swap memory management — Disable swap for system-critical daemons

instruction: How can the performance issue of system-critical daemons and services be addressed?
response: It is advisable to configure the cgroup of the system slice to prevent swapping by setting memory.swap.max=0.
title: Swap memory management — Disable swap for system-critical daemons

instruction: What are the prerequisites for joining a node to a dual-stack cluster with kubeadm?
response: The node must have an IPv6 routable network interface and allow IPv6 forwarding.
title: Dual-stack support with kubeadm — Join a node to dual-stack cluster

instruction: How do you specify the IP addresses for a node joining a dual-stack cluster using kubeadm configuration file?
response: In the kubeadm configuration file, you can specify the IP addresses for a node by setting the `node-ip` value in `kubeletExtraArgs` to a comma-separated list of IPv4 and IPv6 addresses, for example: "10.100.0.2,fd00:1:2:3::3"
title: Dual-stack support with kubeadm — Join a node to dual-stack cluster

instruction: What is the purpose of the `advertiseAddress` field in the kubeadm configuration file for joining a control plane node?
response: The `advertiseAddress` field specifies the IP address that the API Server will advertise it is listening on. This value equals the `--apiserver-advertise-address` flag of `kubeadm join`.
title: Dual-stack support with kubeadm — Join a node to dual-stack cluster

instruction: How can you verify that a Pod is being terminated with a graceful termination period configuration?
response: You can verify that a Pod is being terminated with a graceful termination period configuration by checking its status after deletion. For example, after running `kubectl delete pod nginx-deployment-7768647bf9-b4b9s`, you can run `kubectl get pods` to see that the Pod status changes to 'Terminating'. The Pod remains in this state for the duration of the terminationGracePeriodSeconds, which is 120 seconds in this example.
title: Explore Termination Behavior for Pods And Their Endpoints — Example flow with endpoint termination

instruction: What happens to the endpoint of a Pod when it is being terminated?
response: When a Pod is being terminated, its endpoint remains in the terminating state with its 'ready' status set to 'false'. This allows applications to communicate their state during termination and clients, such as load balancers, to implement connection draining functionality. The endpoint will be deleted when the Pod is fully deleted.
title: Explore Termination Behavior for Pods And Their Endpoints — Example flow with endpoint termination

instruction: How do clients, such as load balancers, detect terminating endpoints in Kubernetes?
response: In Kubernetes, endpoints that are terminating always have their 'ready' status set to 'false'. Clients, such as load balancers, can detect terminating endpoints by checking this status. For traffic draining on a terminating Pod, the actual readiness can be checked as a condition 'serving'.
title: Explore Termination Behavior for Pods And Their Endpoints — Example flow with endpoint termination

instruction: How can I ensure that connections from a particular client are passed to the same Pod each time?
response: You can configure session affinity based on the client's IP address.
title: Service — Session stickiness

instruction: What are the recommended alternatives to using externalIPs for exposing a Service?
response: Consider using an external load balancer controller or a Gateway API implementation instead.
title: Service — Session stickiness

instruction: How can I expose a Service on an external IP address?
response: You can specify externalIPs for any service type when defining a Service. For example, you can add an external IP address to the .spec.externalIPs field in the Service definition.
title: Service — Session stickiness

instruction: What happens to network traffic that arrives at the cluster with the external IP address as the destination IP and the port matching the Service?
response: The traffic is routed to one of the endpoints for that Service using rules and routes configured by Kubernetes.
title: Service — Session stickiness

instruction: How can you separate several log streams from different parts of your application?
response: By having your sidecar containers write to their own stdout and stderr streams, or by running a logging agent in the sidecar container that reads logs from a file, socket, or journald.
title: Logging Architecture — Using a sidecar container with the logging agent

instruction: What is the recommended approach for an application that writes to a single file?
response: It's recommended to set /dev/stdout as the destination rather than implement the streaming sidecar container approach.
title: Logging Architecture — Using a sidecar container with the logging agent

instruction: How can you access each log stream separately for a pod with multiple sidecar containers?
response: You can access each log stream separately by running kubectl logs <pod-name> <sidecar-container-name>.
title: Logging Architecture — Using a sidecar container with the logging agent

instruction: What is an alternative to using a sidecar container with a logging agent?
response: You can use the kubelet and the logging agent that already run on each node by having your sidecar containers write to their own stdout and stderr streams.
title: Logging Architecture — Using a sidecar container with the logging agent

instruction: What is the correct way to define environment variable values in a file to ensure they are taken literally by the kubelet?
response: The value must be enclosed in single quotes, with no form of interpolation, expansion, or concatenation, and no unquoted or double-quoted values are allowed.
title: Define Environment Variable Values Using An Init Container — Unsupported forms

instruction: Are multiple adjacent quoted strings supported when defining environment variable values?
response: No, multiple adjacent quoted strings are not supported. For example, VAR='val1''val2' is not supported.
title: Define Environment Variable Values Using An Init Container — Unsupported forms

instruction: Is interpolation or expansion supported when defining environment variable values?
response: No, any form of interpolation, expansion, or concatenation is not supported. For example, VAR='a'$OTHER or VAR=${OTHER} are not supported.
title: Define Environment Variable Values Using An Init Container — Unsupported forms

instruction: What is the purpose of the `pretty` parameter in the DELETE /apis/storage.k8s.io/v1/volumeattachments endpoint?
response: The `pretty` parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If set to `true`, the output will be pretty-printed. Defaults to `false` unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool (curl and wget).
title: VolumeAttachment — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting `dryRun` to `All` in the DELETE /apis/storage.k8s.io/v1/volumeattachments endpoint?
response: When `dryRun` is set to `All`, all dry run stages will be processed. This indicates that modifications should not be persisted.
title: VolumeAttachment — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the `propagationPolicy` parameter in the DELETE /apis/storage.k8s.io/v1/volumeattachments endpoint?
response: The `propagationPolicy` parameter determines whether and how garbage collection will be performed. It can be set to 'Orphan', 'Background', or 'Foreground'. Either this field or `orphanDependents` may be set, but not both.
title: VolumeAttachment — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the eviction-pressure-transition-period flag in Kubernetes node-pressure eviction?
response: The eviction-pressure-transition-period flag controls how long the kubelet must wait before transitioning a node condition to a different state, helping to protect against oscillation.
title: Node-pressure Eviction — Node condition oscillation

instruction: What is the default value of the eviction-pressure-transition-period flag?
response: The default value of the eviction-pressure-transition-period flag is 5m (5 minutes).
title: Node-pressure Eviction — Node condition oscillation

instruction: Why do nodes sometimes oscillate above and below soft eviction thresholds?
response: Nodes oscillate above and below soft eviction thresholds without holding for the defined grace periods, causing the reported node condition to constantly switch between true and false.
title: Node-pressure Eviction — Node condition oscillation

instruction: What ClusterRole is defined to allow binding-related updates for dynamic resource allocation?
response: The ClusterRole defined is named 'dra-binding-updater'.
title: Harden Dynamic Resource Allocation in Your Cluster — Grant scheduler and allocation-controller permissions

instruction: What resources and verbs are allowed for the 'resourceclaims/status' and 'resourceclaims/binding' resources in the 'resource.k8s.io' API group?
response: For 'resourceclaims/status', the allowed verbs are 'get', 'patch', and 'update'. For 'resourceclaims/binding', the allowed verbs are 'patch' and 'update'.
title: Harden Dynamic Resource Allocation in Your Cluster — Grant scheduler and allocation-controller permissions

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/rolebindings/{name} request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/rolebindings/{name} request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is a server-defined value that clients may only use with identical query parameters. If the continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: RoleBinding — getWatch

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/rolebindings/{name} request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection, followed by a synthetic 'Bookmark' event.
title: RoleBinding — getWatch

instruction: What is the purpose of the kubelet's image credential provider plugin mechanism?
response: The kubelet's image credential provider plugin mechanism allows the kubelet to dynamically retrieve credentials for a container image registry using exec plugins, enabling features like short-lived credentials and avoiding storing static credentials on disk.
title: Configure a kubelet image credential provider

instruction: When might you want to use the kubelet's image credential provider plugin mechanism?
response: You may want to use this capability if API calls to a cloud provider service are required to retrieve authentication information for a registry, credentials have short expiration times and requesting new credentials frequently is required, or storing registry credentials on disk or in imagePullSecrets is not acceptable.
title: Configure a kubelet image credential provider

instruction: How do the kubelet and the exec plugin communicate?
response: The kubelet and the exec plugin communicate through stdio (stdin, stdout, and stderr) using Kubernetes versioned APIs.
title: Configure a kubelet image credential provider

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'gracePeriodSeconds' parameter in the ServiceAccount deletion request?
response: The 'gracePeriodSeconds' parameter specifies the duration in seconds before the ServiceAccount object should be deleted. A non-negative integer value can be provided, with zero indicating immediate deletion. If set to nil, the default grace period for the specified type will be used.
title: ServiceAccount — deleteDelete

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'orphanDependents' to true in the ServiceAccount deletion request?
response: Setting 'orphanDependents' to true will cause the dependent objects of the ServiceAccount to be orphaned. This field is deprecated and its usage is discouraged in favor of 'propagationPolicy'.
title: ServiceAccount — deleteDelete

instruction: What are the possible values for the 'propagationPolicy' field in the ServiceAccount deletion request?
response: The 'propagationPolicy' field can take one of the following values: 'Orphan', 'Background', or 'Foreground'. These values determine how garbage collection will be performed on the ServiceAccount's dependents.
title: ServiceAccount — deleteDelete

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential' parameter in the ServiceAccount deletion request?
response: The 'ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential' parameter, when set to true, allows for an unsafe deletion of the ServiceAccount resource if the normal deletion flow fails due to a corrupt object error. This option should be used with caution as it may potentially break the cluster.
title: ServiceAccount — deleteDelete

instruction: What is the purpose of the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-subnets annotation in Kubernetes?
response: The service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-subnets annotation is used to configure a load balancer and determine in which AWS availability zones to deploy the managed load balancing service.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-subnets (beta)

instruction: What are the acceptable formats for the value of the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-subnets annotation?
response: The value is either a comma separated list of subnet names, or a comma separated list of subnet IDs.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-subnets (beta)

instruction: How would you specify multiple subnets using the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-subnets annotation?
response: You would specify multiple subnets by providing a comma-separated list of subnet names or IDs, for example: "private-a,private-b"
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-subnets (beta)

instruction: Which subresource allows updating container resources in a pod?
response: The 'resize' subresource allows container resources (spec.containers[*].resources) to be updated.
title: Pods — Pod subresources

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'ephemeralContainers' subresource in a pod?
response: The 'ephemeralContainers' subresource allows ephemeral containers to be added to a Pod.
title: Pods — Pod subresources

instruction: Who typically uses the 'status' subresource to update a pod's status?
response: The 'status' subresource is typically only used by the Kubelet and other system controllers.
title: Pods — Pod subresources

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'binding' subresource in a pod?
response: The 'binding' subresource allows setting the pod's spec.nodeName via a Binding request, typically used by the scheduler.
title: Pods — Pod subresources

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in a GET /apis/flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1/watch/flowschemas/{name} request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: FlowSchema — getWatch

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in a GET /apis/flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1/watch/flowschemas/{name} request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. The server may reject a continue value it does not recognize. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token.
title: FlowSchema — getWatch

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` in a GET /apis/flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1/watch/flowschemas/{name} request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent.
title: FlowSchema — getWatch

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersionMatch` field in a GET /apis/flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1/watch/flowschemas/{name} request?
response: The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: FlowSchema — getWatch

instruction: What are the factors kubelet considers when determining the order to evict pods?
response: The kubelet uses the following parameters to determine the pod eviction order: whether the pod's resource usage exceeds requests, pod priority, and the pod's resource usage relative to requests.
title: Node-pressure Eviction — Pod selection for kubelet eviction

instruction: How does kubelet rank and evict pods when the node is under resource pressure?
response: kubelet ranks and evicts pods in the following order: BestEffort or Burstable pods where the usage exceeds requests (evicted based on their Priority and then by how much their usage level exceeds the request), and then Guaranteed pods and Burstable pods where the usage is less than requests (evicted last, based on their Priority).
title: Node-pressure Eviction — Pod selection for kubelet eviction

instruction: What is the eviction policy for Guaranteed pods?
response: Guaranteed pods are guaranteed only when requests and limits are specified for all the containers and they are equal. These pods will never be evicted because of another pod's resource consumption.
title: Node-pressure Eviction — Pod selection for kubelet eviction

instruction: How does kubelet sort pods for eviction when nodefs or imagefs triggers evictions?
response: If nodefs triggers evictions, the kubelet sorts pods based on their total disk usage (local volumes + logs and a writable layer of all containers). If imagefs triggers evictions, the kubelet sorts pods based on the writable layer usage of all containers or the storage of images rank.
title: Node-pressure Eviction — Pod selection for kubelet eviction

instruction: What is the purpose of the `pretty` parameter in the DELETE /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1beta2/devicetaintrules request?
response: The `pretty` parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If set to `true`, the output will be pretty-printed, but this is not the default behavior unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool like curl or wget.
title: DeviceTaintRule — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: How does the `continue` parameter work in the DELETE /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1beta2/devicetaintrules request?
response: The `continue` parameter should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is a server-defined value that clients may use to retrieve the next set of results. If the continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error. Clients may only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters.
title: DeviceTaintRule — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting `dryRun` to `All` in the DELETE /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1beta2/devicetaintrules request?
response: When `dryRun` is set to `All`, all dry run stages will be processed. This means that the request will be validated and tested without actually persisting any modifications.
title: DeviceTaintRule — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the schema of the .spec.template field in a DaemonSet?
response: The .spec.template field in a DaemonSet has the same schema as a Pod, except it is nested and does not have an apiVersion or kind.
title: DaemonSet — Pod Template

instruction: What is the required RestartPolicy for a Pod Template in a DaemonSet?
response: A Pod Template in a DaemonSet must have a RestartPolicy equal to Always, or be unspecified, which defaults to Always.
title: DaemonSet — Pod Template

instruction: What additional requirement does a Pod template in a DaemonSet have compared to a regular Pod?
response: In addition to required fields for a Pod, a Pod template in a DaemonSet has to specify appropriate labels.
title: DaemonSet — Pod Template

instruction: What are the conditions under which the kubelet sets the PodReadyToStartContainers condition to False?
response: The kubelet sets the PodReadyToStartContainers condition to False when it detects a Pod does not have a runtime sandbox with networking configured. This occurs in the following scenarios: early in the lifecycle of the Pod before sandbox setup, or later in the lifecycle if the Pod sandbox has been destroyed due to node reboot, or Pod sandbox virtual machine rebooting for container runtimes that use virtual machines for isolation.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Pod readiness to start containers

instruction: When does the kubelet set the PodReadyToStartContainers condition to True?
response: The kubelet sets the PodReadyToStartContainers condition to True after sandbox creation, network configuration, volume mounting, and (if requested) dynamic resource allocation are complete.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Pod readiness to start containers

instruction: What happens after the PodReadyToStartContainers condition is set to True?
response: Image pulling and container creation occur after the PodReadyToStartContainers condition is set to True.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Pod readiness to start containers

instruction: How does the presence of init containers affect the setting of the Initialized condition?
response: For a Pod with init containers, the kubelet sets the Initialized condition to True after the init containers have successfully completed. For a Pod without init containers, the kubelet sets the Initialized condition to True before sandbox creation and network configuration starts.
title: Pod Lifecycle — Pod readiness to start containers

instruction: What happens to exec probes during a container runtime restart?
response: Exec probes fail for the duration of the restart because the kubelet cannot run commands inside containers. With a short timeout and failure threshold, a failing liveness probe can cause a container to be restarted, and a failing readiness probe can cause the Pod to flap out of the Ready state.
title: What Happens After A Node Restart — Impact of a container runtime restart

instruction: How does a container runtime restart affect the node's readiness and scheduling of new Pods?
response: The node is reported as NotReady by the kubelet, which blocks scheduling of new Pods onto the node.
title: What Happens After A Node Restart — Impact of a container runtime restart

instruction: What happens to an init container that was executing when the container runtime restarts?
response: Its execution state can be lost, in which case the init container runs again.
title: What Happens After A Node Restart — Impact of a container runtime restart

instruction: Under what conditions can interrupting an operation during a container runtime restart lead to inconsistent state?
response: Interrupting an operation at a precise moment, such as an image pull or sandbox creation during a CNI or NRI call, can leave state inconsistent. This can result in issues like inconsistent image layers or a sandbox in an inconsistent state with partial CNI initialization and possible resource leaks.
title: What Happens After A Node Restart — Impact of a container runtime restart

instruction: What type of matches are supported for the host field in an Ingress resource?
response: Precise matches and wildcard matches are supported. Precise matches require an exact match of the HTTP host header with the host field, while wildcard matches require the HTTP host header to be equal to the suffix of the wildcard rule.
title: Ingress — Hostname wildcards

instruction: How does a wildcard match work for the host field in an Ingress resource?
response: A wildcard match requires the HTTP host header to be equal to the suffix of the wildcard rule. For example, a wildcard rule '*.foo.com' would match 'bar.foo.com' but not 'baz.bar.foo.com' or 'foo.com'.
title: Ingress — Hostname wildcards

instruction: Can a wildcard host rule in an Ingress resource match a domain with multiple levels?
response: No, a wildcard host rule in an Ingress resource only covers a single DNS label. For example, '*.foo.com' matches 'bar.foo.com' but not 'baz.bar.foo.com'.
title: Ingress — Hostname wildcards

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/resourceclaimtemplates request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: ResourceClaimTemplate — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/resourceclaimtemplates request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is server-defined and clients may only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: ResourceClaimTemplate — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1/watch/namespaces/{namespace}/resourceclaimtemplates request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent.
title: ResourceClaimTemplate — getWatch List

instruction: How does RBAC refer to resources in the Kubernetes API?
response: RBAC refers to resources using exactly the same name that appears in the URL for the relevant API endpoint.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Referring to resources

instruction: How do you represent a subresource, such as the logs for a Pod, in an RBAC role?
response: To represent a subresource in an RBAC role, use a slash (/) to delimit the resource and subresource. For example, 'pods/log'.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Referring to resources

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'resourceNames' list in an RBAC role?
response: The 'resourceNames' list is used to restrict requests to individual instances of a resource. When specified, requests can be restricted to individual instances of a resource.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Referring to resources

instruction: How do you use the wildcard '*' symbol in an RBAC role to refer to all resources, apiGroups, or verbs?
response: You can use the wildcard '*' symbol to refer to all such objects. For example, to allow access to perform any current and future action on all current and future resources in a specific API group, you can set 'resources' to '*' and 'verbs' to '*'.
title: Using RBAC Authorization — Referring to resources

instruction: What is the purpose of the `propagationPolicy` field in the DELETE collection API?
response: The `propagationPolicy` field determines whether and how garbage collection will be performed. It can be set to 'Orphan', 'Background', or 'Foreground' to control the deletion of dependents.
title: LeaseCandidate — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting `ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential` to `true` in the DELETE collection API?
response: If set to `true`, it will trigger an unsafe deletion of the resource in case the normal deletion flow fails with a corrupt object error. This may potentially break the cluster if the workload associated with the resource being unsafe-deleted relies on normal deletion flow.
title: LeaseCandidate — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What happens to a PersistentVolumeClaim if a matching PersistentVolume does not exist?
response: The claim will remain unbound indefinitely.
title: Persistent Volumes — Binding

instruction: Can a PersistentVolumeClaim be bound to a PersistentVolume that has more capacity than requested?
response: Yes, the user will always get at least what they asked for, but the volume may be in excess of what was requested.
title: Persistent Volumes — Binding

instruction: What type of binding is a PVC to PV binding?
response: A one-to-one mapping, using a ClaimRef which is a bi-directional binding between the PersistentVolume and the PersistentVolumeClaim.
title: Persistent Volumes — Binding

instruction: What is the purpose of the `pretty` parameter in the DELETE /apis/flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1/flowschemas endpoint?
response: The `pretty` parameter is used to pretty-print the output. If set to `true`, the output will be pretty-printed, but this defaults to `false` unless the user-agent indicates a browser or command-line HTTP tool like curl or wget.
title: FlowSchema — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: How does the `continue` parameter work when retrieving more results from the server?
response: The `continue` option should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It is a server-defined value that clients may only use with identical query parameters, except for the value of `continue`. If the specified `continue` value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error.
title: FlowSchema — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting `dryRun` to `All` in the DELETE /apis/flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1/flowschemas endpoint?
response: When `dryRun` is set to `All`, all dry run stages will be processed. This indicates that modifications should not be persisted, and an invalid or unrecognized dryRun directive will result in an error response.
title: FlowSchema — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the `propagationPolicy` field when deleting a FlowSchema?
response: The `propagationPolicy` field determines whether and how garbage collection will be performed. It can be set to 'Orphan', 'Background', or 'Foreground', and either this field or `orphanDependents` may be set, but not both.
title: FlowSchema — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the --windows-priorityclass flag in the kubelet?
response: The --windows-priorityclass flag allows setting the scheduling priority of the kubelet process on Windows, enabling it to get more CPU time slices compared to other processes.
title: Resource Management for Windows nodes — CPU management

instruction: What is the recommended value for the --windows-priorityclass flag to prevent Pods from starving the kubelet of CPU cycles?
response: The recommended value is ABOVE_NORMAL_PRIORITY_CLASS or above.
title: Resource Management for Windows nodes — CPU management

instruction: Can Windows guarantee a minimum amount of CPU time for processes?
response: No, Windows can limit the amount of CPU time allocated for different processes but cannot guarantee a minimum amount of CPU time.
title: Resource Management for Windows nodes — CPU management

instruction: What is the purpose of the CSIDriver object in Kubernetes?
response: The CSIDriver object captures information about a Container Storage Interface (CSI) volume driver deployed on the cluster. Kubernetes attach detach controller uses this object to determine whether attach is required, and Kubelet uses this object to determine whether pod information needs to be passed on mount.
title: CSIDriver

instruction: What is the significance of the metadata.name field in a CSIDriver object?
response: The metadata.name field indicates the name of the CSI driver that this object refers to; it MUST be the same name returned by the CSI GetPluginName() call for that driver. The driver name must be 63 characters or less, beginning and ending with an alphanumeric character ([a-z0-9A-Z]) with dashes (-), dots (.), and alphanumerics between.
title: CSIDriver

instruction: Are CSIDriver objects namespaced in Kubernetes?
response: No, CSIDriver objects are non-namespaced.
title: CSIDriver

instruction: What is the recommended Kubernetes API object for applications requiring rolling update functionality?
response: Deployment is recommended if you want the rolling update functionality, because they are declarative, server-side, and have additional features.
title: ReplicationController — Deployment (Recommended)

instruction: Why is it recommended to use a ReplicationController even for applications that require only a single pod?
response: A ReplicationController replaces pods that are deleted or terminated for any reason, such as in the case of node failure or disruptive node maintenance. It acts like a process supervisor, but for multiple pods across multiple nodes.
title: ReplicationController — Deployment (Recommended)

instruction: How does a ReplicationController handle local container restarts?
response: A ReplicationController delegates local container restarts to some agent on the node, such as the kubelet.
title: ReplicationController — Deployment (Recommended)

instruction: How are changes to a ConfigMap mounted as a Volume on a Pod made available?
response: Changes to a ConfigMap mounted as a Volume on a Pod are available seamlessly after the subsequent kubelet sync.
title: Updating Configuration via a ConfigMap — Summary

instruction: What happens when a ConfigMap is marked as immutable?
response: Once a ConfigMap is marked as immutable, it is not possible to revert this change, make any change to the contents of the data or binaryData field, or make the ConfigMap mutable again.
title: Updating Configuration via a ConfigMap — Summary

instruction: What options are available if a ConfigMap needs to be changed after being marked as immutable?
response: You can delete and recreate the ConfigMap, or you can make a new different ConfigMap.
title: Updating Configuration via a ConfigMap — Summary

instruction: What happens to running containers and their Pods when a ConfigMap is deleted?
response: Running containers and their Pods maintain a mount point to any volume that referenced that existing ConfigMap.
title: Updating Configuration via a ConfigMap — Summary

instruction: What are the required fields of the .spec section in a Deployment?
response: The .spec.template and .spec.selector are the only required fields of the .spec section in a Deployment.
title: Deployments — Pod Template

instruction: What is the schema of the .spec.template field in a Deployment?
response: The .spec.template is a Pod template and has exactly the same schema as a Pod, except it is nested and does not have an apiVersion or kind.
title: Deployments — Pod Template

instruction: What restart policy is allowed for a Pod template in a Deployment?
response: Only a .spec.template.spec.restartPolicy equal to Always is allowed, which is the default if not specified.
title: Deployments — Pod Template

instruction: What is the purpose of the node.cloudprovider.kubernetes.io/uninitialized taint?
response: The node.cloudprovider.kubernetes.io/uninitialized taint is used to mark a Node as unusable when kubelet is started with an external cloud provider. It prevents scheduling of pods on the Node until a controller from the cloud-controller-manager initializes the Node and removes the taint.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — node.cloudprovider.kubernetes.io/uninitialized

instruction: When is the node.cloudprovider.kubernetes.io/uninitialized taint removed?
response: The node.cloudprovider.kubernetes.io/uninitialized taint is removed when a controller from the cloud-controller-manager initializes the Node.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — node.cloudprovider.kubernetes.io/uninitialized

instruction: What effect does the node.cloudprovider.kubernetes.io/uninitialized taint have on pod scheduling?
response: The node.cloudprovider.kubernetes.io/uninitialized taint with effect "NoSchedule" prevents pods from being scheduled on the Node until the taint is removed.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — node.cloudprovider.kubernetes.io/uninitialized

instruction: What is the primary function of the kubelet's sync loop?
response: The kubelet's sync loop periodically reconciles the desired state of Pods (defined by a Pod spec) with the actual state of the running containers on a node.
title: Kubelet Sync Loop

instruction: How does the kubelet communicate with a container runtime to create and manage containers?
response: The kubelet uses the Container Runtime Interface (CRI) to instruct a container runtime (like containerd or CRI-O) to create a 'pod sandbox' and then create/start the individual containers defined in the Pod spec.
title: Kubelet Sync Loop

instruction: What is the role of PLEG in the kubelet's operation?
response: PLEG (Pod Lifecycle Event Generator) periodically polls the runtime for the state of all containers and generates events that wake up the Sync Loop to update the Pod status when containers start, stop, or fail.
title: Kubelet Sync Loop

instruction: Why might there be a delay between the status of a Pod seen in the API and its actual state on the node?
response: Because PLEG uses a polling mechanism to generate events, there might be a slight delay between the status seen in the API (e.g., via 'kubectl get pod') and the instant reality on the node.
title: Kubelet Sync Loop

instruction: How do I disable encryption at rest for secrets in my Kubernetes cluster?
response: To disable encryption at rest, place the 'identity' provider as the first entry in your encryption configuration file. This involves adding an 'identity' provider before any existing encryption providers, such as 'aescbc', in the EncryptionConfiguration file.
title: Decrypt Confidential Data that is Already Encrypted at Rest — Configure the API server to decrypt objects

instruction: What changes need to be made to the EncryptionConfiguration file to decrypt objects that are already encrypted at rest?
response: You need to add the 'identity' provider as the first entry in the 'providers' list. For example, add '- identity: {}' before the existing encryption provider, such as 'aescbc'. The 'identity' provider should be specified with an empty configuration object '{}'.
title: Decrypt Confidential Data that is Already Encrypted at Rest — Configure the API server to decrypt objects

instruction: What action is required after updating the EncryptionConfiguration file to decrypt objects at rest?
response: After updating the EncryptionConfiguration file, you need to restart the kube-apiserver Pod on the node for the changes to take effect.
title: Decrypt Confidential Data that is Already Encrypted at Rest — Configure the API server to decrypt objects

instruction: What is the purpose of the `continue` option in the ReplicationController delete collection API?
response: The continue option should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It allows clients to retrieve the next set of results from a previous query with identical query parameters.
title: ReplicationController — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting `orphanDependents` to `true` in the ReplicationController delete collection API?
response: If `orphanDependents` is set to `true`, the dependent objects will be orphaned. This field is deprecated and replaced by `propagationPolicy`.
title: ReplicationController — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What are the possible values for the `propagationPolicy` field in the ReplicationController delete collection API?
response: The possible values for `propagationPolicy` are: 'Orphan' - orphan the dependents; 'Background' - allow the garbage collector to delete the dependents in the background; 'Foreground' - a cascading policy that deletes all dependents in the foreground.
title: ReplicationController — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the `gracePeriodSeconds` field in the ReplicationController delete collection API?
response: The `gracePeriodSeconds` field specifies the duration in seconds before the object should be deleted. A value of zero indicates delete immediately.
title: ReplicationController — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of a ResourceQuota object in Kubernetes?
response: A ResourceQuota object is used to restrict the number of objects of a particular type that can be created in a namespace.
title: Configure Quotas for API Objects

instruction: What are some prerequisites for configuring quotas for API objects in Kubernetes?
response: You need to have a Kubernetes cluster and the kubectl command-line tool must be configured to communicate with your cluster. It is recommended to run this on a cluster with at least two nodes that are not acting as control plane hosts.
title: Configure Quotas for API Objects

instruction: How can you verify the version of your Kubernetes cluster?
response: You can verify the version of your Kubernetes cluster by entering 'kubectl version'.
title: Configure Quotas for API Objects

instruction: What are some projects that support running Kubernetes node components as a non-root user?
response: Some projects that support running Kubernetes node components as a non-root user include rootlesscontaine.rs, kind with Rootless Docker, Usernetes, and K3s with rootless mode.
title: Running Kubernetes Node Components as a Non-root User — See Also

instruction: Where can I find more information about Kubelet-in-UserNS, also known as Rootless mode?
response: You can find more information about Kubelet-in-UserNS, also known as Rootless mode, in KEP-2033.
title: Running Kubernetes Node Components as a Non-root User — See Also

instruction: Who is responsible for third-party products or projects mentioned on the Kubernetes documentation pages?
response: The Kubernetes project authors are not responsible for third-party products or projects mentioned on the documentation pages. Responsibility lies with the respective projects themselves, as per the CNCF website guidelines.
title: Running Kubernetes Node Components as a Non-root User — See Also

instruction: How can I make the Redis leader highly available and meet traffic demands?
response: You can add a few Redis followers, or replicas.
title: Example: Deploying PHP Guestbook application with Redis — Set up Redis followers

instruction: How do users request dynamically provisioned storage in a PersistentVolumeClaim?
response: Users request dynamically provisioned storage by including a storage class in their PersistentVolumeClaim using the storageClassName field.
title: Dynamic Volume Provisioning — Using Dynamic Provisioning

instruction: What is the correct way to specify a storage class in a PersistentVolumeClaim in Kubernetes v1.9 and later?
response: In Kubernetes v1.9 and later, users should use the storageClassName field of the PersistentVolumeClaim object to specify a storage class. The value of this field must match the name of a StorageClass configured by the administrator.
title: Dynamic Volume Provisioning — Using Dynamic Provisioning

instruction: What happens to the dynamically provisioned volume when the PersistentVolumeClaim is deleted?
response: When the PersistentVolumeClaim is deleted, the dynamically provisioned volume is destroyed.
title: Dynamic Volume Provisioning — Using Dynamic Provisioning

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'failureThreshold' field in a Kubernetes probe?
response: The 'failureThreshold' field specifies the minimum consecutive failures for the probe to be considered failed after having succeeded. It defaults to 3 and has a minimum value of 1.
title: Pod — Probe

instruction: How is the 'initialDelaySeconds' field used in a Kubernetes probe?
response: The 'initialDelaySeconds' field specifies the number of seconds after the container has started before liveness probes are initiated. This allows the container to start up and become ready before the probe begins checking its health.
title: Pod — Probe

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'terminationGracePeriodSeconds' in a Kubernetes probe?
response: Setting 'terminationGracePeriodSeconds' in a Kubernetes probe overrides the pod's terminationGracePeriodSeconds value and specifies the duration in seconds the pod needs to terminate gracefully upon probe failure. The value must be a non-negative integer and is used to give the pod time to clean up before being forcibly terminated.
title: Pod — Probe

instruction: What are the possible probe actions that can be performed against a container in Kubernetes?
response: The possible probe actions are: Exec (execute a command in the container), GRPC (perform a GRPC HealthCheckRequest), HTTPGet (perform an HTTP GET request), TCPSocket (establish a connection to a TCP port).
title: Pod — Probe

instruction: What is the purpose of the system:masters group in Kubernetes?
response: The system:masters group is a break-glass, super user group that can bypass the authorization layer of Kubernetes, such as RBAC.
title: PKI certificates and requirements — Configure certificates for user accounts

instruction: How does kubeadm generate administrator certificates in kubeconfig files?
response: kubeadm generates two separate administrator certificates in kubeconfig files. One is in admin.conf with Subject: O = kubeadm:cluster-admins, CN = kubernetes-admin, and another is in super-admin.conf with Subject: O = system:masters, CN = kubernetes-super-admin.
title: PKI certificates and requirements — Configure certificates for user accounts

instruction: What commands are used to configure a kubeconfig file for a user account?
response: The commands used are: KUBECONFIG=<filename> kubectl config set-cluster default-cluster --server=https://<host ip>:6443 --certificate-authority <path-to-kubernetes-ca> --embed-certs, KUBECONFIG=<filename> kubectl config set-credentials <credential-name> --client-key <path-to-key>.pem --client-certificate <path-to-cert>.pem --embed-certs, KUBECONFIG=<filename> kubectl config set-context default-system --cluster default-cluster --user <credential-name>, and KUBECONFIG=<filename> kubectl config use-context default-system.
title: PKI certificates and requirements — Configure certificates for user accounts

instruction: What is the purpose of the admin.conf, super-admin.conf, kubelet.conf, controller-manager.conf, and scheduler.conf files?
response: These files are used to configure administrator user for the cluster, super administrator user for the cluster, kubelet, kube-controller-manager, and kube-scheduler respectively.
title: PKI certificates and requirements — Configure certificates for user accounts

instruction: What is the purpose of the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-backend-protocol annotation in a Kubernetes Service?
response: The cloud controller manager integration with AWS elastic load balancing configures the load balancer listener based on the value of this annotation.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-backend-protocol (beta)

instruction: What is the effect of setting service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-connection-draining-enabled to "false" on an AWS load balancer?
response: The load balancer's connection draining setting is configured based on the value of this annotation, and setting it to "false" disables connection draining.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-backend-protocol (beta)

instruction: On which Kubernetes object is the service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-backend-protocol annotation used?
response: This annotation is used on a Service.
title: Well-Known Labels, Annotations and Taints — service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-backend-protocol (beta)

instruction: How can I check if swap memory is enabled on my system?
response: Run 'sudo swapon --show'. If there is no output, then swap memory is already disabled.
title: Running Kubelet in Standalone Mode — Swap configuration

instruction: How can I make swap memory disabled persist across reboots?
response: Make sure swap is disabled in either '/etc/fstab' or 'systemd.swap', depending on how it was configured on your system.
title: Running Kubelet in Standalone Mode — Swap configuration

instruction: What is the purpose of the `continue` option when deleting a collection of EndpointSlices?
response: The continue option should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It allows clients to retrieve the next set of results from a previous query with identical query parameters.
title: EndpointSlice — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting `dryRun` to `All` when deleting a collection of EndpointSlices?
response: When `dryRun` is set to `All`, all dry run stages will be processed, and modifications will not be persisted.
title: EndpointSlice — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the difference between `orphanDependents` and `propagationPolicy` when deleting a collection of EndpointSlices?
response: Either `orphanDependents` or `propagationPolicy` may be set, but not both. `orphanDependents` determines whether dependent objects should be orphaned, while `propagationPolicy` determines how garbage collection will be performed.
title: EndpointSlice — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersion` field when deleting a collection of EndpointSlices?
response: The `resourceVersion` field sets a constraint on what resource versions a request may be served from. It ensures that the request is served from a specific resource version.
title: EndpointSlice — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: How can I retrieve information about a failed pod?
response: You can check the terminationMessagePath field in the Container, retrieve logs, and review Pod status and Pod phase. Additionally, you can learn about container states.
title: Determine the Reason for Pod Failure — What's next

instruction: What are some possible reasons for a pod failure related to image retrieval?
response: One possible reason for pod failure related to image retrieval is ImagePullBackOff.
title: Determine the Reason for Pod Failure — What's next

instruction: Where can I report a problem or suggest an improvement for Kubernetes documentation?
response: You can open an issue in the GitHub Repository to report a problem or suggest an improvement.
title: Determine the Reason for Pod Failure — What's next

instruction: How can you manually create a service account in the current namespace?
response: You can manually create a service account in the current namespace using the command `kubectl create serviceaccount (NAME)`. For example, `kubectl create serviceaccount jenkins` creates a service account named 'jenkins' in the current namespace.
title: Authenticating — Service account tokens

instruction: What is the format of the username that service accounts use to authenticate?
response: Service accounts authenticate with the username in the format `system:serviceaccount:(NAMESPACE):(SERVICEACCOUNT)`. They are also assigned to the groups `system:serviceaccounts` and `system:serviceaccounts:(NAMESPACE)`.
title: Authenticating — Service account tokens

instruction: How can you manually create a token for a service account?
response: You can manually create an associated token for a service account using the command `kubectl create token (SERVICEACCOUNT)`. For example, `kubectl create token jenkins` creates a token for the 'jenkins' service account. The created token is a signed JSON Web Token (JWT).
title: Authenticating — Service account tokens

instruction: Can service account bearer tokens be used outside the cluster?
response: Yes, service account bearer tokens are perfectly valid to use outside the cluster and can be used to create identities for long standing jobs that wish to talk to the Kubernetes API.
title: Authenticating — Service account tokens

instruction: What is the purpose of the `allowWatchBookmarks` field in the GET /apis/apiregistration.k8s.io/v1/watch/apiservices request?
response: The `allowWatchBookmarks` field requests watch events with type 'BOOKMARK'. Servers that do not implement bookmarks may ignore this flag and bookmarks are sent at the server's discretion.
title: APIService — getWatch List

instruction: How does the `continue` field work in the GET /apis/apiregistration.k8s.io/v1/watch/apiservices request?
response: The `continue` field should be set when retrieving more results from the server. The server may reject a continue value it does not recognize. If the specified continue value is no longer valid, the server will respond with a 410 ResourceExpired error together with a continue token.
title: APIService — getWatch List

instruction: What is the effect of setting `sendInitialEvents=true` and `watch=true` in the GET /apis/apiregistration.k8s.io/v1/watch/apiservices request?
response: When `sendInitialEvents=true` is set together with `watch=true`, the watch stream will begin with synthetic events to produce the current state of objects in the collection. Once all such events have been sent, a synthetic 'Bookmark' event will be sent.
title: APIService — getWatch List

instruction: What is the purpose of the `resourceVersionMatch` field in the GET /apis/apiregistration.k8s.io/v1/watch/apiservices request?
response: The `resourceVersionMatch` field determines how `resourceVersion` is applied to list calls. It is highly recommended that `resourceVersionMatch` be set for list calls where `resourceVersion` is set.
title: APIService — getWatch List

instruction: At what levels are Pressure Stall Information (PSI) metrics collected in Kubernetes?
response: PSI metrics are collected at the node, pod, and container levels.
title: Understand Pressure Stall Information (PSI) Metrics

instruction: How are PSI metrics exposed in Kubernetes?
response: PSI metrics are exposed through two sources: the kubelet's Summary API, which provides PSI data at the node, pod, and container level, and the /metrics/cadvisor endpoint on the kubelet, which exposes PSI metrics in the Prometheus format.
title: Understand Pressure Stall Information (PSI) Metrics

instruction: What is the purpose of the 'continue' option in the DELETE collection API for HorizontalPodAutoscaler?
response: The continue option should be set when retrieving more results from the server. It allows clients to retrieve the next set of results from a previous query with identical query parameters.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the effect of setting 'dryRun' to 'All' in the DELETE collection API for HorizontalPodAutoscaler?
response: When 'dryRun' is set to 'All', all dry run stages will be processed, and modifications will not be persisted.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What is the difference between 'propagationPolicy' values 'Orphan', 'Background', and 'Foreground' in the DELETE collection API for HorizontalPodAutoscaler?
response: The 'propagationPolicy' values determine how garbage collection will be performed. 'Orphan' orphans the dependents, 'Background' allows the garbage collector to delete dependents in the background, and 'Foreground' deletes all dependents in the foreground.
title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler — deleteDelete Collection

instruction: What are the possible values for the persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy field in a PersistentVolumeSpec?
response: The possible values for the persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy field are "Delete", "Recycle", and "Retain". "Delete" means the volume will be deleted from Kubernetes on release from its claim. "Recycle" means the volume will be recycled back into the pool of unbound persistent volumes on release from its claim. "Retain" means the volume will be left in its current phase (Released) for manual reclamation by the administrator.
title: PersistentVolume — PersistentVolumeSpec

instruction: What is the purpose of the claimRef field in a PersistentVolumeSpec?
response: The claimRef field is part of a bi-directional binding between PersistentVolume and PersistentVolumeClaim. It is expected to be non-nil when bound. claim.VolumeName is the authoritative bind between PV and PVC.
title: PersistentVolume — PersistentVolumeSpec

instruction: What is the significance of the accessModes field in a PersistentVolumeSpec?
response: The accessModes field contains all ways the volume can be mounted. It provides more information on how the volume can be used, for example, read-only or read-write.
title: PersistentVolume — PersistentVolumeSpec

instruction: Which volume plugins are deprecated in PersistentVolumeSpec and what are their recommended replacements?
response: The following volume plugins are deprecated: AWSElasticBlockStore (replaced by ebs.csi.aws.com CSI driver), AzureDisk (replaced by disk.csi.azure.com CSI driver), AzureFile (replaced by file.csi.azure.com CSI driver), CephFS, Cinder (replaced by cinder.csi.openstack.org CSI driver), FlexVolume (replaced by CSIDriver), Flocker, GCEPersistentDisk (replaced by pd.csi.storage.gke.io CSI driver), Glusterfs. They are either no longer supported or redirected to CSI drivers.
title: PersistentVolume — PersistentVolumeSpec

instruction: What are the required permissions for the kubelet on the configuration file?
response: The kubelet must have read permissions on the file.
title: Set Kubelet Parameters Via A Configuration File — Create the config file

instruction: What is the format of the kubelet configuration file?
response: The configuration file must be a JSON or YAML representation of the parameters in the KubeletConfiguration struct.
title: Set Kubelet Parameters Via A Configuration File — Create the config file

instruction: Under what conditions will the kubelet evict Pods according to the provided configuration file example?
response: The kubelet will evict Pods under the following conditions: when the node's available memory drops below 100MiB, when the node's main filesystem's available space is less than 10%, when the image filesystem's available space is less than 15%, and when more than 95% of the node's main filesystem's inodes are in use.
title: Set Kubelet Parameters Via A Configuration File — Create the config file

instruction: What is the purpose of the imagefs filesystem in the kubelet configuration?
response: The imagefs is an optional filesystem that container runtimes use to store container images and container writable layers.
title: Set Kubelet Parameters Via A Configuration File — Create the config file

instruction: How can I configure the kubelet that kubeadm will start with a custom configuration?
response: You can pass a custom KubeletConfiguration API object using a configuration file with the kubeadm command, for example: kubeadm ... --config some-config-file.yaml.
title: Configuring each kubelet in your cluster using kubeadm — Configure kubelets using kubeadm

instruction: How can I view the default values for the KubeletConfiguration structure?
response: You can use the command kubeadm config print init-defaults --component-configs KubeletConfiguration to see all the default values for this structure.
title: Configuring each kubelet in your cluster using kubeadm — Configure kubelets using kubeadm

instruction: Is it possible to apply instance-specific patches to the base KubeletConfiguration?
response: Yes, it is possible to apply instance-specific patches over the base KubeletConfiguration. For more details, refer to Customizing the kubelet.
title: Configuring each kubelet in your cluster using kubeadm — Configure kubelets using kubeadm

instruction: What happens to a Pod's serviceAccountName if it is not set when the ServiceAccount admission controller is active?
response: The admission controller sets the name of the ServiceAccount for the incoming Pod to 'default'.
title: Managing Service Accounts — ServiceAccount admission controller

instruction: What action does the ServiceAccount admission controller take if the ServiceAccount referenced by a Pod does not exist?
response: The admission controller rejects the incoming Pod. This check applies even for the 'default' ServiceAccount.
title: Managing Service Accounts — ServiceAccount admission controller

instruction: Under what conditions does the ServiceAccount admission controller add an extra volume and a volumeMount to a Pod's containers?
response: The admission controller adds an extra volume containing a token for API access and a volumeMount to each container in the Pod, provided that neither the ServiceAccount's automountServiceAccountToken field nor the Pod's automountServiceAccountToken field is set to false. The volume is mounted at /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount for Linux containers and at the equivalent path for Windows nodes.
title: Managing Service Accounts — ServiceAccount admission controller

instruction: What else does the ServiceAccount admission controller add to a Pod if it doesn't already contain any imagePullSecrets?
response: The admission controller adds imagePullSecrets to the Pod, copying them from the ServiceAccount.
title: Managing Service Accounts — ServiceAccount admission controller

instruction: What happens if the path provided for a HostPath volume is a symlink?
response: The symlink will be followed to the real path.
title: PersistentVolume — HostPathVolumeSource

instruction: What is the default type for a HostPath volume if not specified?
response: The default type is an empty string, which is for backwards compatibility.
title: PersistentVolume — HostPathVolumeSource

instruction: What type should be used for a HostPath volume if a directory should be created if it does not exist?
response: The type should be set to "DirectoryOrCreate". If nothing exists at the given path, an empty directory will be created there as needed with file mode 0755, having the same group and ownership with Kubelet.
title: PersistentVolume — HostPathVolumeSource

instruction: Can HostPath volumes support ownership management or SELinux relabeling?
response: No, Host path volumes do not support ownership management or SELinux relabeling.
title: PersistentVolume — HostPathVolumeSource

instruction: How can I restrict which nodes a Persistent Volume (PV) can be accessed from?
response: You can specify node affinity in the `.spec` of a PV to define constraints that limit what nodes the volume can be accessed from.
